topics.title: Properties and Nature of Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties and Nature of Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fluid? — The Formal KTU Definition

A **fluid** is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows)** under the action of an **applied shear stress** (or tangential force), no matter how small that stress is. In the formal language of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPOT416, Module 1), a fluid is defined as:

> A fluid is a material which deforms **continuously** under the action of a **shearing force**, however small the force may be, and does not recover its original shape after the force is removed.

This single property — the inability to sustain a static shear stress — is the **defining mechanical characteristic** that separates a fluid from a solid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honey-on-a-Spoon Intuition

Imagine you place a small dollop of **honey** on a flat plate, and next to it you place a small cube of **rubber**. Now tilt the plate slightly:

* The **rubber cube** slides down a little and then **stops** — it resists the shear stress elastically.
* The **honey**, in contrast, **keeps flowing, layer by layer, indefinitely** — it cannot resist the shear at all; it only resists the *rate* of deformation.

That continuous, unstoppable, layer-upon-layer sliding is exactly what we mean by **fluid flow**. The faster the layers slide, the greater the **resistance (viscosity)** the fluid offers.

### 1.3 The Two Broad Families of Fluids

Fluids are broadly classified (as per KTU Module 1 expectations) into:

1. **Liquids** — Have a definite (nearly constant) volume, take the shape of their container, and possess a free surface when unconfined. Example: **water, oil, mercury**.
2. **Gases** — Have neither definite shape nor definite volume; they expand to fill the entire container. Example: **air, oxygen, nitrogen**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT416 / Module 1):** Students are expected to be fluent with the **continuum hypothesis** and the **fluid properties** — specifically density, specific weight, specific gravity, viscosity, compressibility, bulk modulus, surface tension, capillarity, and vapour pressure. Memorize the **SI units** and the **symbol conventions** since they appear in nearly every numerical problem.

### 1.4 The Continuum Hypothesis

At the molecular level, fluids are **discrete particles separated by empty space**. However, engineering problems deal with **macroscopic volumes** that contain an enormous number of molecules. To simplify the analysis, we adopt the **Continuum Hypothesis**:

> A fluid is treated as a **continuous, infinitely divisible substance** in which physical properties (density, pressure, velocity) are defined at every point in space and vary smoothly.

This hypothesis **fails** only in extreme cases — for example, in **rarefied gas dynamics** at very high altitudes, in **nanofluidics**, or in **vacuum systems** of extreme low pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KEEP MEMORIZED):**
> * Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard value used in KTU numericals).
> * Density of water at $4^\circ \text{C}$, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
> * Specific weight of water, $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.
> * Dynamic viscosity of water at $20^\circ \text{C}$, $\mu_{\text{water}} \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.
> * Kinematic viscosity of water at $20^\circ \text{C}$, $\nu_{\text{water}} \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

---

### 1.5 Pre-Concept Recap — Properties at a Glance

| Property | Symbol | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass density | $\rho$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Mass per unit volume |
| Specific weight | $\gamma$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | Weight per unit volume |
| Specific gravity | $S$ (or $G$) | dimensionless | Ratio of fluid density to water density |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$ | Resistance to deformation |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | $\mu / \rho$ |
| Pressure | $p$ | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$ | Normal force per unit area |
| Bulk modulus | $K$ | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$ | Compressibility resistance |
| Surface tension | $\sigma$ | $\text{N/m}$ | Force per unit length on free surface |
| Capillarity (height) | $h$ | $\text{m}$ | Rise/fall in a narrow tube |
| Vapour pressure | $p_v$ | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$ | Pressure at which fluid vaporizes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mass Density ($\rho$)

* **Definition:** Mass per unit volume of a fluid.
* **Mathematical Form:** $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$
* **Why it matters:** Density governs inertia, weight, and forces in any fluid system. It is the **most fundamental intensive property** of a fluid.

## 2.2 Specific Volume ($v$) and Specific Weight ($\gamma$)

* **Specific volume** is volume per unit mass: $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ (units: $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$).
* **Specific weight** is weight per unit volume: $\gamma = \rho \, g$ (units: $\text{N/m}^3$).
* **Real-world utility:** In **hydraulic engineering**, $\gamma$ is used to compute the hydrostatic pressure on submerged surfaces; in **aerospace**, $\gamma$ of air is used to find lift-related forces.

## 2.3 Specific Gravity ($S$)

* **Definition:** Ratio of the density (or specific weight) of a fluid to the density (or specific weight) of **water at $4^\circ \text{C}$**.
* **Formula:** $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{fluid}}}{\gamma_{\text{water}}}$
* **Important:** $S$ is **dimensionless** and **numerically identical** whether you use $\rho$ or $\gamma$.

## 2.4 Viscosity — The Heart of Fluid Mechanics

Viscosity is the property that quantifies a fluid's **resistance to deformation** under shear. It is the most important transport property.

### 2.4.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity

Consider a fluid layer between two parallel plates, the top plate moving with velocity $U$ relative to the bottom (stationary) plate. The fluid sticks to both surfaces (**no-slip condition**). The velocity varies **linearly** across the gap.

$$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$

Where:
* $\tau$ = shear stress ($\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$)
* $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$)
* $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow ($\text{s}^{-1}$)

### 2.4.2 Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) vs. Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)

* **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ — measures resistance to shear. Symbol $\mu$, unit $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.
* **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu$ — dynamic viscosity divided by density: $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$. Unit $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. It is the **diffusivity of momentum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Frequently Asked:** *Why is $\nu$ called "kinematic" viscosity?*
> Because its units ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$) involve only **kinematic** quantities (length and time) and **no force**. It represents the rate at which momentum diffuses through the fluid.

### 2.4.3 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

* **Newtonian fluid** — obeys Newton's law with $\mu$ **constant** (independent of shear rate). Examples: **water, air, glycerin, oils, gasoline**.
* **Non-Newtonian fluid** — apparent viscosity varies with shear rate.
  * **Pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning): paints, blood, ketchup.
  * **Dilatant** (shear-thickening): starch suspensions, wet sand.
  * **Bingham plastic**: toothpaste, drilling mud (need a yield stress to start flowing).
  * **Thixotropic / Rheopectic**: time-dependent behaviour.

## 2.5 Compressibility and Bulk Modulus

* **Compressibility ($\beta$)** is the fractional change in volume per unit change in pressure.
  $$ \beta = -\frac{1}{V} \frac{dV}{dp} $$
* **Bulk modulus ($K$)** is the **reciprocal** of compressibility.
  $$ K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -V \frac{dp}{dV} = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho} $$
* For **water** at standard conditions, $K \approx 2.2 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 2.2 \text{ GPa}$.
* For **air** (at atmospheric pressure and standard temperature), $K \approx 1.01 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ — i.e., air is roughly **20,000 times more compressible** than water.
* **Real-world utility:** Bulk modulus is critical in **water-hammer analysis**, in **hydraulic accumulator design**, and in **acoustic** problems (speed of sound $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$).

## 2.6 Surface Tension ($\sigma$)

* **Definition:** Surface tension is the **tensile force per unit length** acting along any line on a free liquid surface. It arises from the **cohesive molecular forces** at the interface.
* **Mathematical form:** $\sigma = F / L$ (units: $\text{N/m}$).
* **Energy interpretation:** It is also the **surface energy per unit area** ($\text{J/m}^2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Bubble and droplet relations (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
> * Pressure difference across a **soap bubble** (two surfaces): $\Delta p = \dfrac{8 \sigma}{D}$
> * Pressure difference across a **liquid droplet** (one surface): $\Delta p = \dfrac{4 \sigma}{D}$
> * Pressure difference across a **hollow bubble** is **double** that of a droplet of the same diameter.

## 2.7 Capillarity

* **Capillarity** is the phenomenon of liquid **rising (or falling)** in a small-bore tube due to surface tension and contact angle.
* **Jurin's Law** for capillary rise:
  $$ h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos \theta}{\rho g d} $$
  Where $d$ is the tube diameter, $\theta$ is the contact angle, $\sigma$ is surface tension, $\rho$ is density, and $g$ is gravity.
* If $\theta < 90^\circ$ (e.g., water on clean glass), $h > 0$ (rises).
* If $\theta > 90^\circ$ (e.g., mercury on glass), $h < 0$ (depresses).

## 2.8 Vapour Pressure

* **Vapour pressure** is the pressure at which a liquid **boils** at a given temperature — i.e., the pressure at which the liquid and its vapour are in **equilibrium**.
* If the local pressure on a liquid drops below its vapour pressure, **cavitation** occurs (vapour bubbles form and collapse). This is destructive to **pump impellers**, **turbine blades**, and **propellers**.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** The table below summarizes **every formula** you must memorize for Module 1. No vertical pipes $\vert$ are used inside cells — only the LaTeX equivalents `\vert` / `\mid` are used to keep Markdown table syntax intact.

| Quantity | Formula | Unit | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass density | $\rho = m / V$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Intensive property |
| Specific weight | $\gamma = \rho \, g$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | Weight density |
| Specific gravity | $S = \rho / \rho_{\text{water}}$ | dimensionless | $S_{\text{water}} = 1$ |
| Specific volume | $v = 1 / \rho$ | $\text{m}^3 / \text{kg}$ | Reciprocal of density |
| Newton's shear stress | $\tau = \mu \, du/dy$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Defines $\mu$ |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | $\text{m}^2 / \text{s}$ | Momentum diffusivity |
| Compressibility | $\beta = -(1/V) \, dV/dp$ | $\text{m}^2 / \text{N}$ or $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Negative of fractional volume change |
| Bulk modulus | $K = -V \, dp/dV = \rho \, dp/d\rho$ | $\text{Pa}$ | $K = 1/\beta$ |
| Speed of sound in fluid | $c = \sqrt{K / \rho}$ | $\text{m} / \text{s}$ | Acoustic application |
| Surface tension pressure (droplet) | $\Delta p = 4 \sigma / D$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Single surface |
| Surface tension pressure (bubble) | $\Delta p = 8 \sigma / D$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Two surfaces |
| Capillary rise (Jurin's law) | $h = 4 \sigma \cos \theta / (\rho \, g \, d)$ | $\text{m}$ | $d$ is tube diameter |
| Vapour pressure | $p_v$ from tables | $\text{Pa}$ | Cavitation when $p_{\text{local}} \leq p_v$ |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Applications

* **Hydraulic machinery** (pumps, turbines) — design requires density, viscosity, and bulk modulus.
* **Aerospace engineering** — air density and viscosity are crucial for lift and drag computations.
* **Civil engineering** — capillary rise in soil causes **moisture migration** in building foundations.
* **Chemical engineering** — non-Newtonian behaviour governs flow in polymer processing, food industry, and biomedical flows.
* **Naval architecture** — surface tension affects small vessels, cavitation affects propellers and sonar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Specific Weight from Mass Density

**Starting Premise:** Weight is the force exerted by gravity on a mass. By Newton's second law, $W = m \, g$.

**Step 1.** Mass density is defined as:
$$ \rho = \frac{m}{V} $$

**Step 2.** Weight of the fluid element is:
$$ W = m \, g = \rho \, V \, g $$

**Step 3.** Divide both sides by volume $V$ to obtain the **weight per unit volume** (specific weight):
$$ \gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \rho \, g $$

**Conclusion:** $\gamma = \rho \, g$ is dimensionally consistent ($\text{N/m}^3 = \text{kg/m}^3 \times \text{m/s}^2$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity (Momentum-Flux Form)

**Starting Premise:** Consider two parallel fluid layers of equal area $A$, separated by a small distance $dy$. The upper layer moves with velocity $u + du$ relative to the lower layer, which moves at $u$.

**Step 1.** The rate of deformation (velocity gradient) is:
$$ \frac{du}{dy} $$

**Step 2.** By **Newton's postulate**, the shear stress is **directly proportional** to the velocity gradient:
$$ \tau \propto \frac{du}{dy} $$

**Step 3.** Introducing the constant of proportionality (dynamic viscosity $\mu$):
$$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$

**Step 4.** Force on the upper layer required to maintain the velocity difference:
$$ F = \tau \, A = \mu \, A \, \frac{du}{dy} $$

**Momentum-flux interpretation:** Newton's second law in differential form states that force equals the rate of change of momentum. The momentum flux across area $A$ due to the velocity gradient is $\rho A u (du/dy) \, dy$ per unit time. Equating leads to the momentum-diffusion form:
$$ \tau = \nu \, \frac{d}{dy}\!\left(\rho u\right) \approx \rho \nu \frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{(incompressible)} $$

Which is **mathematically equivalent** to $\tau = \mu \, du/dy$ since $\mu = \rho \nu$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bulk Modulus from Compressibility Definition

**Starting Premise:** Compressibility is defined as:
$$ \beta = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dp} $$

**Step 1.** The reciprocal of $\beta$ gives the **bulk modulus**:
$$ K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -V \frac{dp}{dV} $$

**Step 2.** Using the **mass conservation law** ($m = \rho V$ = constant for a closed element):
$$ m = \rho V \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{m}{\rho} $$

**Step 3.** Differentiate $V = m / \rho$ with respect to pressure $p$ (noting $m$ is constant):
$$ \frac{dV}{dp} = -\frac{m}{\rho^2} \frac{d\rho}{dp} = -\frac{V}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{dp} $$

**Step 4.** Substitute this $dV/dp$ back into the expression for $K$:
$$ K = -V \cdot \left(-\frac{V}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{dp}\right) = \frac{V^2}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{dp} \cdot \frac{1}{V} = V \cdot \frac{1}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{dp} \cdot \frac{1}{1} $$

Wait — let us be more careful. Substituting $dV/dp = -(V/\rho)(d\rho/dp)$ into $K = -V \, dV/dp$:

$$ K = -V \cdot \left(-\frac{V}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{dp}\right) = \frac{V^2}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{dp} $$

But since $V = m / \rho$, then $V^2 = m^2 / \rho^2$. Substituting:
$$ K = \frac{m^2}{\rho^3}\frac{d\rho}{dp} $$

Since $m/V = \rho$, we have $V = m/\rho$, so $V^2/\rho = m^2/\rho^3$. To simplify back to the field form, divide numerator and denominator by $V$:

$$ K = V \cdot \frac{1}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{dp} \cdot V \cdot \frac{1}{V} = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho} \quad \text{per unit volume basis} $$

**Step 5.** The clean field form is:
$$ \boxed{K = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho}} $$

**Physical interpretation:** Bulk modulus is the pressure increase required to cause a unit fractional increase in density. The higher the $K$, the stiffer (less compressible) the fluid.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

**Starting Premise:** A thin vertical tube of diameter $d$ is dipped into a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\sigma$. The liquid rises to a height $h$ inside the tube.

**Step 1.** The surface tension acts **tangentially** along the contact line. The vertical component of the upward pull is:
$$ F_{\text{up}} = \sigma \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \cos \theta $$

Where $\pi d$ is the contact perimeter and $\theta$ is the contact angle measured through the liquid.

**Step 2.** The weight of the liquid column of height $h$ supported is:
$$ W = \rho \, g \, A \, h = \rho \, g \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h $$

**Step 3.** At **static equilibrium**, the upward surface-tension force balances the downward weight of the column:
$$ \sigma \cdot \pi d \cdot \cos \theta = \rho \, g \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h $$

**Step 4.** Cancel $\pi$ from both sides and rearrange:
$$ h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos \theta}{\rho \, g \, d} $$

**Conclusion:** Jurin's law. Note that **capillary rise is inversely proportional to tube diameter** — the narrower the tube, the higher the rise.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Pressure Difference Across a Spherical Droplet (Young–Laplace Equation)

**Starting Premise:** For a spherical interface, the **Young–Laplace equation** states that the pressure jump across a curved interface is proportional to its mean curvature.

**Step 1.** For a sphere of radius $R$, the principal curvatures are equal: $1/R$ each, so the mean curvature is $2/R$.

**Step 2.** Young–Laplace equation:
$$ \Delta p = \sigma \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right) $$

**Step 3.** Substituting $R_1 = R_2 = R$:
$$ \Delta p = \frac{2 \sigma}{R} $$

**Step 4.** Since the diameter $D = 2R$, we have $R = D/2$. Substituting:
$$ \Delta p = \frac{2 \sigma}{D/2} = \frac{4 \sigma}{D} $$

**Step 5.** For a **bubble (two interfaces)**, multiply by 2:
$$ \Delta p_{\text{bubble}} = \frac{8 \sigma}{D} $$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Scenario | Surface(s) | $\Delta p$ Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Solid-liquid interface | None (rigid) | 0 |
| Liquid droplet in air | 1 spherical surface | $4 \sigma / D$ |
| Soap bubble in air | 2 spherical surfaces (inner + outer) | $8 \sigma / D$ |
| Solid spherical particle in liquid | 1 interface | $4 \sigma / D$ |
| Capillary meniscus | 1 nearly-spherical surface | $2 \sigma / R_{\text{meniscus}}$ |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A liquid has a specific gravity of **0.85** and dynamic viscosity of **$2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$**. Compute the kinematic viscosity.

**Given:**
* $S = 0.85$
* $\mu = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
* $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Compute density of liquid:**
$$ \rho = S \times \rho_{\text{water}} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Compute kinematic viscosity:**
$$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{850} = 2.941 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$

**Answer:** $\nu \approx 2.94 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Inter-relation of Fluid Properties

The following **Mermaid block-level architecture flow** maps how the various fluid properties inter-connect, which is the type of high-level structural map required in KTU Module 1 viva and theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID]:::root --> B[LIQUID]:::branch
    A --> C[GAS]:::branch
    B --> D[Mass Density rho]:::leaf
    B --> E[Specific Weight gamma]:::leaf
    B --> F[Specific Gravity S]:::leaf
    B --> G[Viscosity]:::leaf
    B --> H[Compressibility and Bulk Modulus]:::leaf
    B --> I[Surface Tension]:::leaf
    B --> J[Capillarity]:::leaf
    B --> K[Vapour Pressure]:::leaf
    G --> G1[Dynamic Viscosity mu]:::subleaf
    G --> G2[Kinematic Viscosity nu]:::subleaf
    G --> G3[Newtonian Fluid]:::subleaf
    G --> G4[Non-Newtonian Fluid]:::subleaf
    G4 --> G4a[Pseudoplastic]:::subleaf
    G4 --> G4b[Dilatant]:::subleaf
    G4 --> G4c[Bingham Plastic]:::subleaf
    H --> H1[Compressibility beta]:::subleaf
    H --> H2[Bulk Modulus K]:::subleaf
    I --> I1[Droplet Relation 4sigma by D]:::subleaf
    I --> I2[Bubble Relation 8sigma by D]:::subleaf
    J --> J1[Jurin Law h equals 4sigma cos theta by rho g d]:::subleaf
    K --> K1[Cavitation Threshold]:::subleaf

    classDef root fill:#1f3a68,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d1f3a,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#2e6fb5,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1c3f6b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#7eb0d5,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#1c3f6b,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef subleaf fill:#cce0ee,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#5a8baf,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Newton's Law of Viscosity (Velocity Profile Between Parallel Plates)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bottom Plate Stationary u=0]:::plate --> B[Newtonian Fluid Layer dy]:::fluid
    B --> C[Top Plate Moving Velocity U]:::plate
    D[Shear Stress tau acting on top plate]:::force --> C
    B --> E[Linear Velocity Profile du by dy]:::profile
    E --> F[Upper Layer Velocity u plus du]:::vec
    E --> G[Lower Layer Velocity u]:::vec
    H[Newton Equation tau equals mu du by dy]:::formula --> E

    classDef plate fill:#8a6d3b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3a2a14,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fluid fill:#3a8aaa,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1c4a64,stroke-width:2px
    classDef force fill:#a83232,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5a1414,stroke-width:2px
    classDef profile fill:#e0c060,color:#3a2a14,stroke:#a08520,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vec fill:#4caf50,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef formula fill:#ffffff,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#1c3f6b,stroke-width:1.5px
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Fluid Sample]:::root --> T{Does mu remain constant with shear rate?}:::decision
    T -- YES --> N[Newtonian Fluid]:::leaf
    T -- NO --> NN[Non-Newtonian Fluid]:::leaf
    NN --> N1[Pseudoplastic shear-thinning]:::sub
    NN --> N2[Dilatant shear-thickening]:::sub
    NN --> N3[Bingham Plastic yield stress]:::sub
    NN --> N4[Thixotropic time-dependent]:::sub
    NN --> N5[Rheopectic time-dependent]:::sub
    N --> N0a[Examples water air oils]:::ex
    N1 --> N1a[Examples paints blood ketchup]:::ex
    N2 --> N2a[Examples starch suspension wet sand]:::ex
    N3 --> N3a[Examples toothpaste drilling mud]:::ex

    classDef root fill:#1f3a68,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d1f3a,stroke-width:3px
    classDef decision fill:#e0c060,color:#3a2a14,stroke:#a08520,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#7eb0d5,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#1c3f6b,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef sub fill:#a0c8e0,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#1c3f6b,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef ex fill:#cce0ee,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#5a8baf,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Capillary Rise Tube

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Glass Tube dipped in liquid]:::tube --> B[Liquid Free Surface Outside Tube]:::surf
    A --> C[Capillary Meniscus Shape]:::men
    A --> D[Height of Rise h]:::height
    A --> E[Contact Angle theta]:::ang
    A --> F[Tube Diameter d]:::dia
    D --> G[Upward Surface Tension Force]:::force
    D --> H[Downward Weight of Liquid Column]:::wgt
    G --> I[Equilibrium gives Jurin Law]:::eq
    H --> I
    I --> J[h equals 4 sigma cos theta by rho g d]:::final

    classDef tube fill:#8a6d3b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3a2a14,stroke-width:2px
    classDef surf fill:#3a8aaa,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1c4a64,stroke-width:2px
    classDef men fill:#a0c8e0,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#1c3f6b,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef height fill:#e0c060,color:#3a2a14,stroke:#a08520,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef ang fill:#4caf50,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef dia fill:#a83232,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5a1414,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef force fill:#4caf50,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef wgt fill:#a83232,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5a1414,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef eq fill:#7eb0d5,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#1c3f6b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef final fill:#ffffff,color:#0d1f3a,stroke:#1c3f6b,stroke-width:2px
```
