# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the following terms:**
(a) **Dynamic viscosity** (1 mark)
(b) **Kinematic viscosity** (1 mark)
(c) **Bulk modulus** (1 mark)

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is defined as the shear stress required to produce a unit rate of shear deformation. By Newton's law of viscosity, $\tau = \mu \, (du/dy)$, hence $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$. Its SI unit is $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$.

(b) **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to mass density, $\nu = \mu / \rho$. Its SI unit is $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. It represents the **diffusivity of momentum**.

(c) **Bulk Modulus ($K$):** It is defined as the ratio of change in pressure to the corresponding volumetric strain, $K = -V \, (dp/dV)$. Its SI unit is $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$. It measures the **resistance of a fluid to uniform compression**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Shear stress vs. rate of shear | Linear, $\tau = \mu \, (du/dy)$ with **constant** $\mu$ | Non-linear, $\mu$ depends on $du/dy$ |
| Viscosity | **Constant** at given temperature | **Variable** (shear-dependent) |
| Examples | Water, Air, Glycerin, Kerosene | Blood, Paint, Toothpaste, Drilling mud |

Non-Newtonian fluids are further classified as **pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning, e.g., paints), **dilatant** (shear-thickening, e.g., starch slurry), and **Bingham plastics** (yield stress required, e.g., toothpaste).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Define **viscosity**. Explain Newton's law of viscosity and its significance. Derive the dimensions of dynamic viscosity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **flat plate of area $1.5 \text{ m}^2$** is pulled horizontally over a thin film of oil **$1.0 \text{ mm}$ thick** at a velocity of **$0.4 \text{ m/s}$**. The dynamic viscosity of oil is **$0.9 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$**. Compute:
(i) The shear stress on the oil film.
(ii) The force required to maintain the motion.
(iii) The power dissipated in shearing the oil.
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Definition:** Viscosity is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion of its layers. It is a measure of the internal friction of a fluid.

**Newton's Law of Viscosity:** For a Newtonian fluid in laminar flow, the shear stress $\tau$ between two adjacent fluid layers is proportional to the rate of shear deformation (velocity gradient) normal to the flow direction.

$$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$

**Significance:**
* Viscosity is the only fluid property that causes **energy loss** (head loss) during flow.
* It governs the **boundary layer** behaviour, the **Reynolds number**, and the **drag** on submerged bodies.
* It is responsible for the **no-slip condition** at solid walls.

**Dimensional derivation of $\mu$:**
$$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} = \frac{F/A}{u/y} = \frac{F \cdot y}{A \cdot u} $$

Using primary dimensions $[F] = \text{MLT}^{-2}$, $[A] = \text{L}^2$, $[u] = \text{LT}^{-1}$, $[y] = \text{L}$:
$$ [\mu] = \frac{(\text{MLT}^{-2}) \cdot \text{L}}{(\text{L}^2) \cdot (\text{LT}^{-1})} = \frac{\text{ML}^{2}\text{T}^{-2}}{\text{L}^{3}\text{T}^{-1}} = \text{ML}^{-1}\text{T}^{-1} $$

Hence dimensions of $\mu$ are $[\text{M L}^{-1} \text{T}^{-1}]$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
* $A = 1.5 \text{ m}^2$
* $dy = 1.0 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
* $u = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$
* $\mu = 0.9 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$

**(i) Shear stress:**
$$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.9 \times \frac{0.4}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.9 \times 400 = 360 \text{ N/m}^2 $$

**(ii) Force required:**
$$ F = \tau \times A = 360 \times 1.5 = 540 \text{ N} $$

**(iii) Power dissipated:**
$$ P = F \times u = 540 \times 0.4 = 216 \text{ W} $$

**Final Answers:** $\tau = 360 \text{ Pa}$, $F = 540 \text{ N}$, $P = 216 \text{ W}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Define and explain the terms:
(i) **Surface tension** and **(ii) Capillarity**.
Derive the formula for the **capillary rise** of a liquid in a small-bore tube. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A glass tube of **$1.5 \text{ mm}$ diameter** is immersed in **water at $20^\circ \text{C}$**. Compute the capillary rise. Take $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 0^\circ$ (clean glass), $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**(i) Surface tension ($\sigma$):** Surface tension is defined as the **tensile force per unit length** acting on an imaginary line drawn on a free liquid surface. It arises due to **cohesive forces** between liquid molecules, which pull surface molecules inwards to minimize surface area. Numerically:
$$ \sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad (\text{units: N/m}) $$

Alternatively, it is the **excess surface energy per unit area** ($\text{J/m}^2$).

**(ii) Capillarity:** Capillarity is the phenomenon of **rise or fall of a liquid** in a small-bore tube (capillary tube) due to the combined effect of surface tension and the wettability of the tube material.

**Derivation of capillary rise (Jurin's Law):**

Consider a capillary tube of internal diameter $d$ dipped in a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\sigma$. The liquid wets the tube and rises to a height $h$ above the free surface outside. The contact angle is $\theta$ (measured through the liquid).

**Step 1 — Upward force due to surface tension:** Surface tension acts along the contact perimeter $\pi d$ and has a vertical component $\cos \theta$ per unit length. Hence:
$$ F_{\text{up}} = \sigma \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \cos \theta $$

**Step 2 — Downward weight of liquid column:** Volume of the cylindrical column is $(\pi d^2/4) h$. Its weight is:
$$ W = \rho \, g \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h $$

**Step 3 — Static equilibrium:** Equating $F_{\text{up}} = W$:
$$ \sigma \, \pi d \, \cos \theta = \rho \, g \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h $$

**Step 4 — Solving for $h$:**
$$ h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos \theta}{\rho \, g \, d} $$

This is **Jurin's Law**. The rise is inversely proportional to the tube diameter and vanishes when $\theta = 90^\circ$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
* $d = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
* $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$
* $\theta = 0^\circ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 1$
* $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Substituting into Jurin's law:**
$$ h = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}} $$

**Numerator:** $4 \times 0.0728 = 0.2912$

**Denominator:** $1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 14.715$

**Computing $h$:**
$$ h = \frac{0.2912}{14.715} = 0.01979 \text{ m} = 19.79 \text{ mm} $$

**Final Answer:** $h \approx 19.8 \text{ mm} \approx 1.98 \text{ cm}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU University Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up units of viscosity** — $\mu$ is in $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ (or $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$) while $\nu$ is in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. A student writing $\nu$ in $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ gets **zero** for the unit.
> 2. **Forgetting $g$ in specific weight** — Writing $\gamma = \rho$ instead of $\gamma = \rho g$ is a guaranteed 1-mark cut. Examiners are strict.
> 3. **Differentiating droplet vs. bubble** — A common KTU trap: $\Delta p = 4\sigma/D$ is for a **droplet (single surface)**; for a **bubble (two surfaces)**, it is $8\sigma/D$. Students often write $4\sigma/D$ for both.
> 4. **Capillary rise formula misuse** — The $4$ in Jurin's law comes from the area $\pi d^2/4$ of the cylinder. Some students incorrectly use $\pi r^2$ alone, leading to wrong numerical answers.
> 5. **Sign convention in bulk modulus** — The negative sign in $K = -V \, dV/dp$ is often dropped, giving a negative value, which is **physically meaningless** ($K$ must always be positive).
> 6. **Forgetting the no-slip condition** — In viscosity problems, the layer next to the wall is assumed to have the wall's velocity (zero for a stationary wall). Missing this gives wrong velocity gradient $du/dy$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Definition of a fluid** — substance that deforms continuously under shear, however small.
* **Continuum hypothesis** — fluid treated as a continuous medium; valid in most engineering problems.
* **Mass density** $\rho = m/V$ ($\text{kg/m}^3$); **Specific weight** $\gamma = \rho g$ ($\text{N/m}^3$); **Specific gravity** $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ (dimensionless).
* **Newton's law of viscosity** $\tau = \mu \, du/dy$; $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is kinematic viscosity.
* **Newtonian fluids** have constant $\mu$; **Non-Newtonian fluids** (pseudoplastic, dilatant, Bingham) have apparent viscosity dependent on shear rate.
* **Compressibility** $\beta = -(1/V)(dV/dp)$; **Bulk modulus** $K = 1/\beta = \rho \, dp/d\rho$.
* **Surface tension** is force per unit length on a free surface (N/m). **Droplet** $\Delta p = 4\sigma/D$; **Bubble** $\Delta p = 8\sigma/D$.
* **Capillary rise** (Jurin's law): $h = 4\sigma \cos \theta / (\rho g d)$.
* **Vapour pressure** $p_v$ — the pressure at which a liquid boils; **cavitation** occurs when local pressure falls below $p_v$.
* **SI units to remember:** $\rho \to \text{kg/m}^3$, $\gamma \to \text{N/m}^3$, $\mu \to \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\nu \to \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $\sigma \to \text{N/m}$, $K \to \text{Pa}$.
* **Standard reference values:** $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\sigma_{\text{water}} \approx 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$ at $20^\circ \text{C}$.
* **Continuum assumption fails** only at extremely low pressures (high altitudes, vacuum) or nanoscale flows.
* **The four most frequent Module 1 questions** in KTU exams are: (1) Newton's law derivation, (2) viscosity numerical on parallel plates, (3) Jurin's law derivation, (4) bulk modulus and surface-tension relations. Be prepared to draw the diagrams and write units clearly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ideal Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Ideal Fluid** is defined as a hypothetical, incompressible, non-viscous fluid in which there is **no internal friction** (zero viscosity) and **no resistance to shear deformation**. It is a theoretical construct that obeys the fundamental laws of conservation of mass, momentum, and energy without any energy dissipation due to viscous effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Exact):** An ideal fluid is a fluid that possesses a definite density, is incompressible, has zero viscosity, and its velocity is uniform over every cross-section perpendicular to its direction of flow. Surface tension effects are also considered negligible.

### 1.2 Key Properties of an Ideal Fluid

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Incompressibility** | Density ($\rho$) remains constant regardless of pressure variations |
| **Zero Viscosity** | No internal friction between fluid layers; no shear stress |
| **No Surface Tension** | Cohesive forces at the free surface are ignored |
| **Steady Flow** | Velocity at any point does not change with time |
| **Irrotational Flow** | Fluid particles do not rotate about their own axes |
| **Frictionless Flow** | No energy loss due to friction between fluid and boundary |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a perfectly smooth, frictionless glass channel through which water flows. There is no resistance between water layers, no "stickiness" along the walls, and no eddies. The water slides effortlessly as if composed of countless perfectly lubricated layers. This mental picture represents an **ideal fluid**.

A simpler analogy: think of **honey versus air**. Honey has high viscosity (resistance to flow), while air behaves very close to an ideal fluid because its viscosity is extremely low ($\mu \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s/m²). However, *no real fluid is truly ideal* — this concept serves as a mathematical simplification that yields accurate results in many engineering applications.

### 1.4 Why Study Ideal Fluids in KTU?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The study of ideal fluids forms the foundation for understanding **Bernoulli's Equation**, the **Continuity Equation**, and **streamline analysis**, which are the cornerstones of fluid mechanics and frequently appear in KTU University Examinations (weightage: 15–20% of Module 1 marks).

### 1.5 Real Fluid vs. Ideal Fluid — A Comparison

| Parameter | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity | **Zero** | Non-zero (finite) |
| Compressibility | Incompressible | May be compressible |
| Surface Tension | Neglected | Present |
| Shear Stress | Zero (at rest and in motion) | Present during motion |
| Energy Losses | **No losses** | Losses due to friction |
| Existence | Hypothetical | Physically existent |

### 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of water (standard):** $\rho = 1000$ **kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
- **Atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ **kPa**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C:** $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ **N·s/m²**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions Governing Ideal Fluid Flow

The mathematical treatment of ideal fluid flow rests upon five core assumptions. Each assumption simplifies the governing equations by removing terms that are mathematically difficult to handle.

- **Assumption 1: Incompressibility**
  - **Why:** Ensures that density $\rho$ is constant throughout the flow domain.
  - **How:** This allows us to take $\rho$ outside derivatives and integrals, simplifying the continuity equation dramatically.
  - **Limitation:** Valid only for Mach numbers $M < 0.3$.

- **Assumption 2: Zero Viscosity (Inviscid Flow)**
  - **Why:** Eliminates the viscous stress tensor from the Navier–Stokes equations.
  - **How:** Reduces the equation of motion to the **Euler equation**, which is analytically tractable.
  - **Limitation:** Cannot predict boundary layer behavior or drag forces.

- **Assumption 3: Steady Flow**
  - **Why:** All flow properties (velocity, pressure, density) are time-independent.
  - **How:** $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$; the local acceleration term vanishes.
  - **Limitation:** Excludes unsteady phenomena like vortex shedding.

- **Assumption 4: Irrotational Flow**
  - **Why:** The curl of velocity is zero ($\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$).
  - **How:** Allows introduction of a velocity potential function $\phi$ where $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$.
  - **Limitation:** Excludes flows with vorticity (e.g., rotating tanks).

- **Assumption 5: No Slip at Boundaries (Modified for Ideal Fluids)**
  - **Why:** For ideal fluids, the no-slip condition is **replaced by the slip condition** (zero shear stress at the wall).
  - **How:** The fluid can "slip" along solid boundaries without resistance.

### 2.2 The Equation of Motion for Ideal Fluids (Euler's Equation)

Euler's equation is the cornerstone governing equation for ideal fluid flow. It is derived by applying Newton's second law to a fluid element, neglecting viscous terms.

$$\boxed{\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}}$$

For a static fluid, the equation reduces to the **hydrostatic pressure equation**:
$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the most frequently referenced content in KTU 2024 ESE papers. Memorize these formulas thoroughly.**

| Formula | Expression | Application / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Euler's Equation (vector form)** | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$ | General equation of motion for ideal fluid |
| **Euler's Equation (z-direction)** | $\rho \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} - \rho g$ | Flow under gravity |
| **Hydrostatic Pressure** | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Pressure at depth in static fluid |
| **Continuity Equation (1D)** | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Mass conservation in pipe flow |
| **Continuity Equation (3D)** | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | General mass conservation |
| **Continuity (Incompressible)** | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | For constant density flows |
| **Bernoulli's Equation** | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ | Energy conservation along a streamline |
| **Bernoulli (SI units)** | $P + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$ | Energy per unit volume form |
| **Velocity Potential** | $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ | Irrotational flow definition |
| **Stream Function** | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \; v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | 2D incompressible flow |
| **Cauchy Condition (Irrotational)** | $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ | Rotation-free flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Alert (KTU Board Convention):**
> - Pressure $P$: **N/m²** or **Pa**
> - Velocity $V$: **m/s**
> - Density $\rho$: **kg/m³**
> - Head terms ($V^2/2g$, $z$, $P/\rho g$): **meters of fluid**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ideal fluid model is extensively used in:

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Preliminary design of aircraft wings (lift calculations) before accounting for viscous corrections.
- **Hydraulic Engineering:** Design of spillways, weirs, and open channel flow where viscous effects are minor.
- **Pipe Flow Analysis:** Initial sizing of water distribution networks (corrections applied later using Darcy–Weisbach equation).
- **Turbomachinery:** Velocity triangle analysis in pumps and turbines assumes inviscid flow in the rotor passages.
- **Meteorology:** Large-scale atmospheric flow patterns where viscosity is negligible compared to inertial forces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (1D, Incompressible)

This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU 2024 ESE papers. Pay careful attention to every step.

**Step 1: Setup the Control Volume**

Consider a stream tube (or pipe) with two cross-sections $A_1$ and $A_2$ at distances $x_1$ and $x_2$, respectively. The fluid enters at section 1 with velocity $V_1$ and exits at section 2 with velocity $V_2$.

**Step 2: Mass Flow Rate at Inlet**

The mass of fluid entering the control volume per unit time at section 1 is:
$$\dot{m}_1 = \rho_1 A_1 V_1$$

Where:
- $\rho_1$ = density of fluid at section 1
- $A_1$ = cross-sectional area at section 1
- $V_1$ = velocity of fluid at section 1

**Step 3: Mass Flow Rate at Outlet**

Similarly, the mass of fluid leaving the control volume per unit time at section 2 is:
$$\dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

**Step 4: Apply Conservation of Mass**

For steady flow (no accumulation within the control volume):
$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$$

Therefore:
$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

**Step 5: Apply Incompressibility Condition**

For an ideal (incompressible) fluid: $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho = \text{constant}$.

Dividing both sides by $\rho$:
$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}$$

This is the **Continuity Equation for 1D incompressible flow**, stated as:
$$\boxed{A V = Q = \text{constant}}$$

Where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate in m³/s.

### 3.2 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation

This derivation is the **14-mark centerpiece** of Module 1 in KTU examinations. Master every step.

**Step 1: Write Euler's Equation in Component Form**

For a steady flow (local acceleration = 0), Euler's equation in the x-direction is:
$$\rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}$$

**Step 2: Separate Variables**

Multiply both sides by $ds$ (infinitesimal distance along streamline):
$$\rho V \, dV = -dP$$

**Step 3: Integrate Along a Streamline**

Integrating from point 1 to point 2 along a streamline:
$$\int_1^2 \rho V \, dV = -\int_1^2 dP$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the Integrals**

For constant density (incompressible):
$$\rho \int_1^2 V \, dV = -[P_2 - P_1]$$

$$\frac{\rho V^2}{2} \bigg|_1^2 = -(P_2 - P_1)$$

**Step 5: Apply Gravity (z-direction)**

Including the gravitational body force term, Euler's equation in the s-direction becomes:
$$\rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

Multiplying by $ds$ and integrating from 1 to 2:
$$\int_1^2 \rho V \, dV + \int_1^2 dP + \int_1^2 \rho g \, dz = 0$$

**Step 6: Final Integration**

Evaluating each integral:
$$\frac{\rho V_2^2}{2} - \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2} + (P_2 - P_1) + \rho g (z_2 - z_1) = 0$$

Rearranging:
$$P_1 + \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2} + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{\rho V_2^2}{2} + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 7: Statement of Bernoulli's Equation**

$$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = \text{constant along a streamline}}$$

Or in head form (dividing by $g$):
$$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}}$$

Where each term represents:
- $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ = **Pressure head** (m)
- $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ = **Velocity head** (m)
- $z$ = **Elevation head** (m)

### 3.3 Derivation of Hydrostatic Pressure Equation

**Step 1: Consider a Static Fluid Column**

For a static fluid, $V = 0$ and there is no acceleration.

**Step 2: Apply Euler's Equation in z-direction**

The equation reduces to:
$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

**Step 3: Integrate with Boundary Condition**

Integrating from the free surface (where $z = 0$, $P = P_0$) to depth $h$ (where $z = -h$):
$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\rho g \int_0^{-h} dz$$

**Step 4: Final Result**

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

This is the fundamental equation for hydrostatic pressure distribution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ideal Fluid Flow Architecture (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ideal Fluid System] --> B[Governing Assumptions]
    A --> C[Governing Equations]
    A --> D[Engineering Applications]
    
    B --> B1[Incompressibility rho constant]
    B --> B2[Zero Viscosity mu equals 0]
    B --> B3[Steady Flow dV by dt equals 0]
    B --> B4[Irrotational Flow curl V equals 0]
    
    C --> C1[Euler Equation]
    C --> C2[Continuity Equation]
    C --> C3[Bernoulli Equation]
    
    C1 --> C1a[Vector Form]
    C1 --> C1b[Component Form]
    C1 --> C1c[Static Form]
    
    C2 --> C2a[1D Form A1V1 equals A2V2]
    C2 --> C2b[3D Form divergence rho V equals 0]
    
    C3 --> C3a[Energy Form P plus half rho V squared]
    C3 --> C3b[Head Form P by rho g plus V squared by 2g]
    
    D --> D1[Aerospace Wing Analysis]
    D --> D2[Pipe Flow Sizing]
    D --> D3[Open Channel Hydraulics]
    D --> D4[Turbomachinery Design]
```

### 4.2 Bernoulli's Equation — Energy Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pressure Energy] -->|Converts to| KE[Kinetic Energy]
    KE -->|Converts to| PE[Potential Energy]
    PE -->|Converts to| P
    P --> Sum[Total Energy Constant]
    KE --> Sum
    PE --> Sum
    
    Sum -->|At all points along streamline| E[Energy Conservation]
```

### 4.3 Streamline Behavior Schematic (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Inlet Section A1 V1 P1] -->|Continuity| F2[Narrow Section A2 V2 P2]
    F2 -->|Continuity| F3[Outlet Section A3 V3 P3]
    
    F1 -.Bernoulli.-> F2
    F2 -.Bernoulli.-> F3
    
    F2 --> Note1[Area decreases: Velocity increases]
    F2 --> Note2[By Bernoulli: Pressure decreases]
    
    Note1 --> App[Application: Venturi Meter, Airfoil, Nozzle]
    Note2 --> App
```

### 4.4 Ideal vs. Real Fluid Comparison (Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Fluid Flow Problem] --> Q1{Is viscosity important?}
    
    Q1 -->|No| Ideal[Use Ideal Fluid Model]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Real[Use Real Fluid Model with Navier Stokes]
    
    Ideal --> I1[Euler Equation]
    Ideal --> I2[Bernoulli Equation]
    Ideal --> I3[Continuity Equation]
    
    Real --> R1[Reynolds Number Analysis]
    Real --> R2[Boundary Layer Theory]
    Real --> R3[Darcy Weisbach Friction Losses]
    
    I1 --> Out[Engineering Solution]
    I2 --> Out
    I3 --> Out
    R1 --> Out
    R2 --> Out
    R3 --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above block diagrams represent the **functional architecture** of ideal fluid analysis. For physical drawings (streamlines, flow nets, etc.), refer to your KTU prescribed textbook for visual schematics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an ideal fluid. List any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** An ideal fluid is a hypothetical fluid which is **incompressible**, has **zero viscosity**, possesses a **definite density**, and offers **no resistance** to shear stress or deformation.

**Properties (1.5 Marks – 0.375 each):**
1. **Incompressible:** Density remains constant under all conditions of pressure and temperature.
2. **Zero viscosity:** No internal friction exists between adjacent fluid layers.
3. **No surface tension:** Cohesive forces at the free surface are neglected.
4. **No shear stress:** Fluid offers no resistance to tangential forces, even during motion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the continuity equation for 1D steady incompressible flow and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (2 Marks):** For a 1D, steady, incompressible flow:
$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant (Volumetric flow rate, } Q)$$

**Physical Significance (1 Mark):** The continuity equation represents the **conservation of mass** for an incompressible fluid. It states that the volumetric flow rate remains constant along a streamline, meaning that if the cross-sectional area of a pipe decreases, the velocity must increase proportionally, and vice versa.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Question A**

**(a)** State and derive the **Continuity Equation** for a 1D steady incompressible flow along a stream tube. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ideal fluid flows through a pipe of diameter **20 cm** at section 1 with a velocity of **3 m/s**. If the pipe contracts to a diameter of **10 cm** at section 2, find:
   (i) The velocity at section 2
   (ii) The mass flow rate (Take $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

- Setup of control volume with two cross-sections $A_1$ and $A_2$: **(1 Mark)**
- Expression for mass flow rate at inlet: $\dot{m}_1 = \rho_1 A_1 V_1$: **(1.5 Marks)**
- Expression for mass flow rate at outlet: $\dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$: **(1.5 Marks)**
- Application of conservation of mass: $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$: **(1 Mark)**
- Application of incompressibility ($\rho_1 = \rho_2$): **(1 Mark)**
- Final result: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$: **(1 Mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 20$ cm $= 0.20$ m, therefore $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_1^2$
- $V_1 = 3$ m/s
- $D_2 = 10$ cm $= 0.10$ m, therefore $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_2^2$
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Calculate Areas (2 Marks)**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.01 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply Continuity Equation to Find $V_2$ (3 Marks)**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.03142 \times 3}{0.007854} = \frac{0.09426}{0.007854}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 12 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Mass Flow Rate (2 Marks)**

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_1 V_1 = 1000 \times 0.03142 \times 3$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 94.26 \text{ kg/s}}$$

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Question B**

**(a)** Starting from Euler's equation of motion, derive **Bernoulli's Equation** for steady, incompressible, irrotational flow along a streamline. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water flows through a horizontal pipe that narrows from a diameter of **15 cm** at section A to **7.5 cm** at section B. The pressure at section A is **80 kPa** and the velocity is **2 m/s**. Calculate:
   (i) The velocity at section B
   (ii) The pressure at section B **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

- Statement of Euler's equation for ideal fluid: **(1 Mark)**
$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$$

- Assumptions clearly listed: **(1.5 Marks)**
  - Steady flow
  - Incompressible
  - Irrotational
  - Inviscid
  - Along a streamline

- Component form along streamline: **(1 Mark)**
$$\rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

- Separation of variables and integration: **(2 Marks)**
- Final result with each term explained: **(1.5 Marks)**
$$P_1 + \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2} + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{\rho V_2^2}{2} + \rho g z_2$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $D_A = 15$ cm $= 0.15$ m, $D_B = 7.5$ cm $= 0.075$ m
- $P_A = 80$ kPa $= 80{,}000$ Pa
- $V_A = 2$ m/s
- Horizontal pipe: $z_A = z_B$

**Step 1: Calculate Velocity at B using Continuity (3 Marks)**

$$A_A V_A = A_B V_B$$

$$V_B = V_A \times \left(\frac{D_A}{D_B}\right)^2 = 2 \times \left(\frac{0.15}{0.075}\right)^2 = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation (3 Marks)**

Since the pipe is horizontal, $z_A = z_B$:
$$P_A + \frac{\rho V_A^2}{2} = P_B + \frac{\rho V_B^2}{2}$$

$$P_B = P_A + \frac{\rho}{2}(V_A^2 - V_B^2)$$

$$P_B = 80{,}000 + \frac{1000}{2}(2^2 - 8^2)$$

$$P_B = 80{,}000 + 500 \times (4 - 64) = 80{,}000 + 500 \times (-60)$$

$$P_B = 80{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_B = 50 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 3: Result (1 Mark)**

The pressure at section B is **50 kPa**, which is less than at section A because the velocity has increased (consistent with Bernoulli's principle).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid These to Score Full Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always write units with every numerical answer. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units.
> 2. **Skipping Assumptions:** In Bernoulli's derivation, listing assumptions is **mandatory** for full 1.5 marks. Examiners specifically look for: steady, incompressible, inviscid, irrotational.
> 3. **Sign Errors in Hydrostatic:** When using $P = P_0 + \rho g h$, ensure $h$ is measured **downward from the free surface**. A sign error here costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 4. **Mixing Head Form with Energy Form:** Do not mix $P/\rho g$ form with $P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ form in the same equation. Choose one and stick to it.
> 5. **Area Calculation Mistakes:** Always calculate $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ carefully. Diameter in cm must be converted to m **before** squaring.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ideal Fluids (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Ideal fluid = incompressible + inviscid + zero surface tension. **(Must-know)**
- **Assumptions:** Steady, incompressible, irrotational, inviscid, no surface tension. **(List in every derivation)**
- **Euler's Equation:** $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$ — the master equation of motion for ideal fluids.
- **Continuity Equation (1D):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ — conserves mass; applicable only to incompressible, steady, 1D flow.
- **Bernoulli's Equation:** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ — three heads: pressure, velocity, elevation.
- **Bernoulli Assumptions:** Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no shaft work, no heat transfer.
- **Energy Per Unit Volume Form:** $P + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$ (in Pa or N/m²).
- **Energy Per Unit Weight Form:** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ (in meters of fluid).
- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — pressure increases linearly with depth.
- **Velocity Potential:** $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ — valid for irrotational flow only.
- **Stream Function:** $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \; v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ — for 2D incompressible flow.
- **Continuity (3D):** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ for incompressible flow.
- **Cauchy Condition:** $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ defines irrotational flow.
- **Real Fluid Differences:** Viscosity, surface tension, compressibility, and friction losses exist in real fluids.
- **Standard Values to Memorize:** $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.
- **Key Applications:** Venturi meter, pitot tube, orifice meter, airfoil lift, siphon, spillway design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 ESE, prioritize mastering the derivations of **Continuity Equation** and **Bernoulli's Equation**. These two derivations account for approximately **60–70%** of the Module 1 marks. Practice at least 5 numerical problems based on Bernoulli's equation applied to pipes, nozzles, and Venturi meters before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Real Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Real Fluid?

A **Real Fluid** is a fluid that possesses **viscosity** — an internal resistance to deformation (flow). Unlike an *ideal fluid* (which is inviscid and incompressible in the theoretical sense), a real fluid experiences continuous shear stress whenever it is in motion, and this stress is proportional to the rate of change of velocity across the flow (the velocity gradient).

Formally, in KTU terminology:

> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A real fluid is a fluid in which shear stress exists whenever the fluid is in motion, and this shear stress is related to the rate of angular deformation (shear strain rate) by a proportionality constant called the **coefficient of dynamic viscosity (μ)**.

Mathematically, this is captured by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at a point in the fluid (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (N·s/m² or Pa·s)
- $\frac{dv}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow (s⁻¹)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, *real fluids* are introduced as a direct counter-concept to the *ideal fluid* studied earlier. The central property distinguishing the two is **viscosity**. Every fluid you will encounter in practical engineering — water, oil, air, blood, honey — is a *real fluid*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honey vs. Water Test 🍯💧

Imagine you tilt two identical glasses — one filled with **water** and the other with **honey**:

- **Water** flows quickly, smoothly, and almost effortlessly. It has *low viscosity*.
- **Honey** flows slowly, sluggishly, resisting the motion. It has *high viscosity*.

Both are real fluids. The difference lies in the **magnitude of internal molecular friction** that resists layers of fluid sliding over each other.

> **Engineering Intuition:** Viscosity is essentially the **"internal stickiness"** of a fluid. Molecules in a moving fluid layer pull and drag adjacent layers along — this molecular interaction is what we measure as viscosity.

A more formal analogy: think of a **deck of cards**. When you push the top card, only that card moves (low friction = low viscosity). Now imagine gluing the cards slightly together — pushing the top card drags the second, the third, and so on. That "dragging" of adjacent layers is precisely the *viscous effect* in a real fluid.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following viscosity-related quantities are **must-know** for KTU 2024 examinations:

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Typical Value (Water, 20°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | N·s/m² (Pa·s) | Poise (P) | $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | m²/s | Stokes (St) | $1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | N/m² (Pa) | dyne/cm² | — |

**Conversion to remember:**

$$1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s} = 0.1 \text{ N·s/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Viscosities?** Dynamic viscosity (μ) measures the fluid's *internal resistance to shear*. Kinematic viscosity (ν) divides this by density (ρ) to give a measure of *resistance to flow under gravity*. Kinematic viscosity is especially useful in **Reynolds number** and similar dimensionless flow analyses.

---

## 1.4 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation

Sir Isaac Newton's hypothesis (1687) stated that the shear stress in a fluid is **directly proportional to the rate of shear strain** (velocity gradient):

$$\boxed{\tau \propto \frac{dv}{dy} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}}$$

The constant of proportionality $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity**. Fluids that strictly obey this linear relationship are called **Newtonian fluids**.

### Derivation Insight (Geometric View):

Consider two parallel plates, distance $dy$ apart, with the top plate moving at velocity $dv$ relative to the bottom:

- The fluid layer in contact with the bottom plate is **stationary** (no-slip condition).
- The fluid layer in contact with the top plate moves at velocity $dv$.
- The fluid in between is in **laminar shear**.

The shear strain rate is:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} \quad (\text{units: s}^{-1})$$

And the shear stress opposing motion is:

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$

This single equation is the **gateway** to almost all viscous flow analysis in Fluid Mechanics.

---

## 1.5 Categories of Real Fluids

Real fluids are classified into two broad categories based on their **stress-strain-rate behaviour**:

### A. Newtonian Fluids
Fluids that obey Newton's law of viscosity **exactly** — linear $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ relationship passing through origin.

**Examples:** Water, air, gasoline, alcohol, mercury, most simple hydrocarbons.

### B. Non-Newtonian Fluids
Fluids that **do not obey** Newton's law — the relationship between $\tau$ and $\dot{\gamma}$ is non-linear, or there exists a yield stress, or the behaviour is time-dependent.

**Examples:** Blood, ketchup, paint, toothpaste, mud, printer ink, polymer melts.

The detailed taxonomy of non-Newtonian fluids is given in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Be prepared to **sketch and identify** the rheological curves (τ vs. γ̇) for each type of fluid. These are frequently asked as 3-mark or 6-mark questions.

---

## 1.6 Variation of Viscosity with Temperature

Viscosity is **strongly temperature-dependent**, but the nature of dependence differs between liquids and gases:

### For Liquids (e.g., water, oil):
$$\boxed{\mu \text{ DECREASES as temperature INCREASES}}$$

Liquids become *runny* when heated. Example: heating honey makes it flow more easily.

### For Gases (e.g., air, nitrogen):
$$\boxed{\mu \text{ INCREASES as temperature INCREASES}}$$

Gases become *thicker* when heated. This is because gas viscosity arises from molecular momentum transfer, which increases with thermal agitation.

### Effect of Pressure:
- **Liquids:** Viscosity is *largely insensitive* to pressure (except at very high pressures).
- **Gases:** Viscosity is *independent* of pressure (within moderate ranges) — this is a famous result derived from kinetic theory.

### Empirical Formulas:

For **liquids** (Sutherland's equation simplified for liquids is rarely used; instead):

$$\mu_T = \mu_{20} \cdot e^{\left[b\left(\frac{1}{T+273} - \frac{1}{293}\right)\right]}$$

For **gases** (Sutherland's formula):

$$\boxed{\mu = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}}$$

Where:
- $\mu_0$ = Viscosity at reference temperature $T_0$
- $S$ = Sutherland's constant (gas-specific, e.g., 110.4 K for air)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state "viscosity decreases with temperature" without specifying **liquid or gas**. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this imprecision. Always state the fluid phase!

---

## 1.7 Summary Box — Quick Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Fluids at a Glance:**
> - Real fluids **have viscosity**; ideal fluids do not.
> - **Newton's law:** $\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$
> - **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ has units of Pa·s.
> - **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ has units of m²/s.
> - Viscosity of liquids **decreases** with temperature; of gases **increases**.
> - Real fluids are **Newtonian** or **Non-Newtonian** (further sub-classified).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🟡 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Treatment of Newton's Law of Viscosity

### 2.1.1 The Concept of Shear Strain Rate

In a fluid element undergoing shear deformation, consider a small rectangular fluid element. If the bottom face is fixed and the top face is displaced by $dl$ in time $dt$, then:

- Shear strain: $\gamma = \dfrac{dl}{dy}$
- Shear strain rate: $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d\gamma}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{dy}\dfrac{dl}{dt} = \dfrac{dv}{dy}$

This is the foundational kinematic relationship:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

### 2.1.2 The Constitutive Equation

Combining the proportionality and the constant of proportionality:

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}}$$

This is the **constitutive equation** for a Newtonian fluid — a rheological equation of state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of μ:** It is the shear stress required to produce a unit velocity gradient in the fluid. A high-μ fluid requires a large force to deform it; a low-μ fluid deforms easily.

---

## 2.2 The No-Slip Condition

A critical consequence of viscosity is the **no-slip condition** at solid boundaries:

> *"The fluid layer immediately in contact with a solid boundary has the same velocity as that boundary."*

- At a **stationary wall**: fluid velocity = 0
- At a **moving wall** (e.g., rotating shaft): fluid velocity = wall velocity

This condition is the origin of the velocity gradient $\frac{dv}{dy}$ in viscous flow and is the reason boundary layers form.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Real Fluids — Comprehensive Taxonomy

Real fluids are classified based on the **rheological behaviour** — the relationship between shear stress $\tau$ and shear strain rate $\dot{\gamma}$:

### A. Newtonian Fluids
Linear relationship passing through origin.
$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$
**Examples:** Water, air, alcohol, glycerine, kerosene.

### B. Non-Newtonian Fluids
Non-linear or time-dependent relationship. Five principal sub-categories:

#### B.1 Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning)
- **Behaviour:** Viscosity **decreases** with increasing shear rate.
- **Curve:** Concave-down, passes through origin.
- **Equation:** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ where $n < 1$.
- **Examples:** Blood, paint, ketchup, polymer solutions, paper pulp.

#### B.2 Dilatant (Shear-Thickening)
- **Behaviour:** Viscosity **increases** with increasing shear rate.
- **Curve:** Concave-up, passes through origin.
- **Equation:** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ where $n > 1$.
- **Examples:** Suspensions of starch, wet sand, corn starch in water ("oobleck"), high-concentration slurries.

#### B.3 Bingham Plastic
- **Behaviour:** Behaves as a rigid solid until shear stress exceeds a **yield stress** $\tau_y$, then flows linearly.
- **Curve:** Linear but offset from origin.
- **Equation:** $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ for $\tau > \tau_y$.
- **Examples:** Toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise, sewage sludge.

#### B.4 Thixotropic (Time-Dependent Shear-Thinning)
- **Behaviour:** Viscosity **decreases with time** under constant shear, then **recovers gradually** when shear is removed.
- **Examples:** Gelatin, honey, some greases, bentonite clay gels.

#### B.5 Rheopectic (Time-Dependent Shear-Thickening)
- **Behaviour:** Viscosity **increases with time** under constant shear.
- **Examples:** Gypsum paste, some printer inks, suspensions that "set" under agitation.

#### B.6 Viscoelastic
- **Behaviour:** Exhibits both viscous (flow) and elastic (recoil) characteristics.
- **Examples:** Silly putty, polymer melts, dough, biological fluids like synovial fluid.

---

## 2.4 Apparent Viscosity Concept (for Non-Newtonian Fluids)

For non-Newtonian fluids, the **dynamic viscosity is not constant**. An "effective" or "apparent" viscosity is defined as:

$$\boxed{\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}}$$

This apparent viscosity varies with the shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids but remains constant for Newtonian fluids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Apparent viscosity is not a fluid property** — it is a *flow-condition-dependent* quantity. This distinction is frequently tested in KTU exams.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas to memorize for Module 1 — Real Fluids (KTU 2024):**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Pa |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s |
| 3 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Shear strain rate | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\mu_a = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity (non-Newtonian) | Pa·s |
| 5 | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham plastic equation | Pa |
| 6 | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Power-law fluid (pseudo/dilatant) | Pa |
| 7 | $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ | CGS to SI conversion | — |
| 8 | $\mu_{liquid} \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$ | Liquid viscosity trend | — |
| 9 | $\mu_{gas} \uparrow$ as $T \uparrow$ | Gas viscosity trend | — |
| 10 | $1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ | Stokes to m²/s conversion | — |

> ⚠️ **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or "such-that" symbols use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve table integrity.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The study of real fluids is foundational to numerous engineering applications:

| Engineering Field | Practical Application |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Pipe flow design, water supply systems, dam spillway hydraulics |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Lubrication theory (journal bearings), hydraulic machinery |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Mixing, pumping of slurries, polymer processing |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Blood flow in arteries, drug delivery microfluidics |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Boundary layer analysis, atmospheric flight |
| **Petroleum Engineering** | Drilling mud rheology, oil pipeline transport |

> [!NOTE]
> **The takeaway:** Every time a fluid moves in a pipe, around an aerofoil, through a bearing, or inside a human artery, **viscosity governs the dynamics**. The simple equation $\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$ is the doorway to a vast engineering universe.

---

## 2.7 Analytical Hierarchy — How Viscosity Enters All Flow Problems

A structured "mind-map" of how the real-fluid concept propagates through fluid mechanics:

1. **Newton's Law of Viscosity** ($\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$) →
2. **Velocity Profile** (parabolic in laminar pipe flow, derived by integrating $\tau$ balance) →
3. **Pressure Drop** (Hagen-Poiseuille equation) →
4. **Reynolds Number** ($Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$) →
5. **Flow Regime** (Laminar vs. Turbulent) →
6. **Friction Factor** (Darcy-Weisbach equation) →
7. **Energy Losses** (in pipelines, fittings, valves)

This progression is why **real fluids are introduced first** in Module 1 — they are the prerequisite for nearly every subsequent module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔵 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Given:**
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has units $\text{N·s/m}^2$
- Density $\rho$ has units $\text{kg/m}^3$

**To find:** Derive the units of kinematic viscosity $\nu$.

**Step 1:** Write the definition:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute SI units:

$$\nu = \frac{\text{N·s/m}^2}{\text{kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 3:** Recall that $1 \text{ N} = 1 \text{ kg·m/s}^2$:

$$\nu = \frac{(\text{kg·m/s}^2) \cdot \text{s} / \text{m}^2}{\text{kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify numerator:

$$\nu = \frac{\text{kg·m·s}}{\text{s}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2 \cdot (\text{kg/m}^3)} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s·m}} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

$$\boxed{[\nu] = \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Step 5 (CGS equivalent):** The unit "Stokes" (St) is defined as 1 cm²/s.

$$1 \text{ St} = 1 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

> **Physical meaning:** Kinematic viscosity represents the *diffusivity of momentum* — it tells us how rapidly momentum spreads through a fluid under viscous action.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shear Stress on a Plate Moving Over a Viscous Fluid

**Setup:** A flat plate of area $A$ moves at constant velocity $U$ over a thin film of fluid of thickness $h$. The plate below is stationary. The fluid obeys Newton's law of viscosity.

**Step 1 — Velocity profile assumption (linear):**

For a thin film with no-slip at both surfaces, the velocity varies linearly:

$$v(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

Where $y$ is measured from the stationary bottom plate.

**Step 2 — Compute velocity gradient:**

$$\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's law of viscosity:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy} = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total viscous force on the plate:**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \frac{\mu A U}{h}$$

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu A U}{h}}$$

> **Insight:** This is the foundational equation of **lubrication theory**. It tells us that to *reduce* the viscous drag, we either:
> - Use a low-viscosity fluid (small $\mu$),
> - Reduce contact area (small $A$),
> - Move slower (small $U$), or
> - Increase the film thickness (large $h$).
>
> This is exactly why **engine oils with viscosity additives (multi-grade oils)** are formulated — to balance fuel economy (low μ) with bearing protection (high h).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Viscous Flow in a Pipe)

**Setup:** A Newtonian fluid flows steadily in a fully-developed laminar regime through a circular pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, under an applied pressure drop $\Delta P$.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$:**

Pressure force pushing the element forward:

$$F_p = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$$

Viscous (shear) force acting on the lateral surface of the cylinder:

$$F_\tau = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium (steady flow → forces balance):**

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

**Step 3 — Solve for shear stress:**

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's law of viscosity:**

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{dv}{dr}$$

Note: the negative sign is because velocity *decreases* as $r$ *increases* (from centreline to wall).

**Step 5 — Equate and rearrange:**

$$-\mu \frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

$$dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot r \, dr$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from $r = 0$ (centreline, $v = v_{max}$) to radius $r$ (velocity $v$):**

$$\int_{v_{max}}^{v} dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{0}^{r} r \, dr$$

$$v - v_{max} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{r^2}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Apply boundary condition:** At $r = R$, $v = 0$ (no-slip at wall):

$$0 - v_{max} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back into the velocity expression:**

$$v(r) = v_{max} \left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

$$\boxed{v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)}$$

This is a **parabolic velocity profile** — the hallmark of laminar viscous flow in a circular pipe.

**Step 9 — Compute volumetric flow rate by integrating over the cross-section:**

$$Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_0^R \left(R^2 r - r^3\right) dr$$

**Step 10 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[R^2 \cdot \frac{r^2}{2}\Big|_0^R - \frac{r^4}{4}\Big|_0^R\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P \cdot R^4}{8\mu L}}$$

This is the famous **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation** is the canonical result of real-fluid theory applied to pipe flow. It is highly tested in KTU end-semester exams, often as a 7-mark derivation problem.

**Step 11 — Express in terms of pipe diameter $D = 2R$:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P \cdot D^4}{128 \mu L}$$

**Step 12 — Average velocity:**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L} = \frac{v_{max}}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The average velocity is **half** the maximum (centreline) velocity for laminar flow — a frequently tested fact.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power-Law Fluid — Apparent Viscosity for Pseudoplastic/Dilatant Fluids

**Setup:** A non-Newtonian fluid follows the power-law (Ostwald-de Waele) model:

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$$

**Step 1 — Apparent viscosity definition:**

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{K \dot{\gamma}^n}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the exponent $n$:**

- If $n = 1$: $\mu_a = K = \mu$ → Newtonian fluid (K is dynamic viscosity).
- If $n < 1$: $\mu_a$ **decreases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ → **Pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning).
- If $n > 1$: $\mu_a$ **increases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ → **Dilatant** (shear-thickening).

**Step 3 — Consistency index $K$ and flow behaviour index $n$:**

- $K$ has units of $\text{Pa·s}^n$ (depends on $n$).
- $n$ is dimensionless.

> **Engineering note:** Polymer processing (extrusion, injection moulding) relies heavily on the power-law model. Tubes of toothpaste and ketchup bottles work precisely *because* these fluids are pseudoplastic — they flow under squeezing shear, then stay put on the brush/plate.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A fluid with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.5 \text{ Pa·s}$ and density $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ flows between two parallel plates separated by $h = 5 \text{ mm}$. The top plate moves at $U = 0.2 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate:
(a) The shear stress on the fluid.
(b) The kinematic viscosity of the fluid.
(c) The shear force per unit area if the plate is $1 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m}$.

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.5 \text{ Pa·s}$
- $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $h = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$
- $U = 0.2 \text{ m/s}$

**Part (a) — Shear stress:**

Velocity gradient (linear profile):

$$\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.2}{0.005} = 40 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Shear stress:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy} = 0.5 \times 40 = 20 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 20 \text{ Pa} = 20 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

**Part (b) — Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.5}{850} = 5.882 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 5.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Part (c) — Total shear force:**

Area:

$$A = 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$$

Force:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F = 10 \text{ N}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Fluid Type from τ vs. γ̇ Data

| Fluid Type | $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ Shape | Yield Stress $\tau_y$ | $n$ Value (power-law) | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | Linear through origin | None | $n = 1$ | Water, air |
| Pseudoplastic | Concave-down, through origin | None | $n < 1$ | Blood, paint, ketchup |
| Dilatant | Concave-up, through origin | None | $n > 1$ | Starch slurry, wet sand |
| Bingham plastic | Linear, offset from origin | $\tau_y > 0$ | $n = 1$ (after yield) | Toothpaste, drilling mud |
| Thixotropic | Time-decreasing viscosity (hysteresis loop) | None/variable | Time-dependent | Gelatin, bentonite gels |
| Rheopectic | Time-increasing viscosity (hysteresis loop) | None/variable | Time-dependent | Gypsum paste |

# 🔴 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **Define the following terms with respect to real fluids:**
> (i) Dynamic viscosity
> (ii) Kinematic viscosity
> (iii) Shear stress

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** It is defined as the shear stress required to produce a unit velocity gradient in a fluid. It is a measure of the fluid's internal resistance to deformation. Its SI unit is **N·s/m² or Pa·s**.

**(ii) Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Its SI unit is **m²/s**. It represents the *diffusivity of momentum* and is used in dimensionless groups like Reynolds number.

**(iii) Shear Stress (τ):** It is the tangential force per unit area acting on a fluid layer due to viscosity. For a Newtonian fluid:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$$

Its SI unit is **N/m² or Pa**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> **Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Obeys Newton's law of viscosity ($\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$) | Does not obey Newton's law |
| $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ relationship | Linear, passes through origin | Non-linear or time-dependent |
| Viscosity | Constant (independent of shear rate) | Variable (depends on shear rate or time) |
| Examples | Water, air, kerosene, alcohol, glycerine | Blood, paint, ketchup, toothpaste, mud |
| Yield Stress | None | May or may not be present |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 1 Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. With the help of a neat sketch, describe a simple experiment to determine the viscosity of a fluid. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two parallel plates 5 mm apart are moving with a relative velocity of 0.5 m/s. The space between them is filled with a fluid of dynamic viscosity 0.9 N·s/m². Determine:
>   (i) The shear stress in the fluid.
>   (ii) The shear force per square metre of the plate.
>   (iii) The kinematic viscosity if the fluid density is 950 kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Newton's Law of Viscosity & Experiment (7 Marks):**

**Statement:** Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) at that point, and the constant of proportionality is called the **dynamic viscosity (μ)**.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$$

**Explanation:**

- The law holds for **Newtonian fluids** only.
- $\mu$ is constant for a given fluid at a given temperature and pressure.
- For non-Newtonian fluids, the linear proportionality fails.

**Experiment — Falling Sphere (or Falling Ball) Viscometer:**

A simple setup uses a small sphere falling through the fluid under gravity. The sphere reaches a constant **terminal velocity** $V_t$ when the gravitational force balances buoyancy and viscous drag.

By **Stokes' Law**, the drag force on a sphere of radius $r$ falling at terminal velocity is:

$$F_d = 6\pi \mu r V_t$$

**Force balance at terminal velocity:**

$$\text{Weight} - \text{Buoyancy} = \text{Viscous drag}$$

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g = 6\pi \mu r V_t$$

Solving for viscosity:

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{9 V_t}}$$

**Procedure:** Drop a steel ball of known radius $r$ and density $\rho_s$ into a tall column of fluid. Measure the terminal velocity by timing its fall between two marked points. Compute $\mu$ from the formula above.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Plate separation $h = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$
- Relative velocity $U = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.9 \text{ N·s/m}^2$
- Density $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Compute velocity gradient:**

Assuming linear velocity profile (Couette flow):

$$\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.5}{0.005} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute shear stress:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy} = 0.9 \times 100 = 90 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 90 \text{ Pa}}$$

**Step 3 — Shear force per square metre:**

The shear force per unit area is exactly the shear stress:

$$F/A = \tau = 90 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{F/A = 90 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.9}{950}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 9.474 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** Students often write $\nu$ in cm²/s or in Stokes, but forget to convert units. Always verify that the final answer is in **m²/s** for SI compliance, or convert appropriately to CGS units.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** Do **NOT** forget the **no-slip condition** at both plates. The velocity gradient is $U/h$ only because the velocity profile is linear. If the profile is parabolic (e.g., pressure-driven flow), the gradient varies with $y$.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE OPTION*
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the following types of non-Newtonian fluids:
>   (i) Pseudoplastic fluid
>   (ii) Dilatant fluid
>   (iii) Bingham plastic fluid **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A liquid of viscosity $0.05 \text{ Pa·s}$ and density $900 \text{ kg/m}^3$ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $50 \text{ mm}$ and length $10 \text{ m}$ under a pressure head of $2 \text{ m}$ of the liquid. Assuming laminar flow, calculate:
>   (i) The maximum velocity at the centreline.
>   (ii) The average velocity.
>   (iii) The discharge in litres per second. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question Choice B

**Part (a) — Non-Newtonian Fluid Types (7 Marks):**

**(i) Pseudoplastic Fluid (Shear-Thinning):**
- Viscosity **decreases** as shear rate increases.
- The $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ curve is **concave-down** and passes through the origin.
- Power-law: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ where $n < 1$.
- **Examples:** Blood, paint, ketchup, polymer solutions, paper pulp.

**(ii) Dilatant Fluid (Shear-Thickening):**
- Viscosity **increases** as shear rate increases.
- The $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ curve is **concave-up** and passes through the origin.
- Power-law: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ where $n > 1$.
- **Examples:** Suspensions of starch in water, wet sand, corn starch slurry.

**(iii) Bingham Plastic Fluid:**
- Behaves as a **rigid solid** until shear stress exceeds the **yield stress** $\tau_y$.
- Once yielded, the fluid flows linearly with apparent viscosity $\mu_p$.
- Equation: $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ for $\tau > \tau_y$.
- The $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ curve is a **straight line with non-zero intercept** on the $\tau$-axis.
- **Examples:** Toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise, sewage sludge.

**Sketch (textual):**

```
Pseudoplastic:                Dilatant:                  Bingham Plastic:
  tau                            tau                        tau
  |   .                          |                          |        /
  |  .                           |                         /|       /
  | .                            |                       /  |      /
  |/                             |                     /    |     /
  +------- gamma_dot             +---------- gamma_dot tau_y|    /
                                                          +------ gamma_dot
   (concave down)               (concave up)                (linear offset)
```

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.05 \text{ Pa·s}$
- $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, hence $R = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $L = 10 \text{ m}$
- Pressure head $h_p = 2 \text{ m}$ of liquid

**Step 1 — Compute pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P = \rho g h_p = 900 \times 9.81 \times 2 = 17{,}658 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum (centreline) velocity from Hagen-Poiseuille theory:**

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{17{,}658 \times (0.025)^2}{4 \times 0.05 \times 10}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{17{,}658 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{11.036}{2.0}$$

$$\boxed{v_{max} \approx 5.518 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Average velocity (laminar flow):**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{v_{max}}{2} = \frac{5.518}{2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{avg} \approx 2.759 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Discharge:**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = A \cdot V_{avg} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.759$$

$$Q = 5.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.418 \text{ L/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 5.42 \text{ L/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #3:** Always verify **laminar flow assumption** by computing $Re = \rho V_{avg} D / \mu$ and ensuring $Re < 2300$. If $Re > 2300$, the Hagen-Poiseuille equation is **invalid** and the problem must be solved using turbulent-flow correlations.
>
> **Check for this problem:**
>
> $$Re = \frac{900 \times 2.759 \times 0.05}{0.05} \approx 2483$$
>
> Hmm — this is **just above 2300**, borderline turbulent. In a strict KTU exam, students should state this caveat in their answer to demonstrate engineering rigour. In practice, the problem assumes laminar flow, so the answer above is accepted.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #4:** Many students forget to convert the **pressure head** (in metres of liquid) to actual pressure in **Pascals**. Always use $\Delta P = \rho g h$ before substituting into the Hagen-Poiseuille equation.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Fluids — High-Density Quick Recap (Save for last-minute revision):**

### Core Concept
- ✅ Real fluids **have viscosity**; ideal fluids do not.
- ✅ Viscosity = **internal resistance to shear deformation**.
- ✅ Newton's law: $\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$.

### Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **Dynamic viscosity (μ):** Pa·s — shear stress per unit velocity gradient.
- ✅ **Kinematic viscosity (ν):** m²/s — dynamic viscosity ÷ density.
- ✅ **No-slip condition:** Fluid at solid boundary = boundary velocity.
- ✅ **Shear strain rate:** $\dot{\gamma} = dv/dy$ in simple shear.

### Classifications
- ✅ **Newtonian** — linear τ-γ̇, constant μ (water, air).
- ✅ **Pseudoplastic** — shear-thinning, $n < 1$ (blood, paint).
- ✅ **Dilatant** — shear-thickening, $n > 1$ (starch slurry).
- ✅ **Bingham plastic** — yield stress $\tau_y$ then linear flow (toothpaste, mud).
- ✅ **Thixotropic** — time-decreasing viscosity (gelatin).
- ✅ **Rheopectic** — time-increasing viscosity (gypsum paste).
- ✅ **Viscoelastic** — viscous + elastic recoil (silly putty, dough).

### Critical Trends to Remember
- ✅ **Liquid μ DECREASES with T↑** (molecular cohesion breaks down).
- ✅ **Gas μ INCREASES with T↑** (momentum transfer intensifies).
- ✅ **Pressure** has minimal effect on liquid μ; gas μ is independent of P (moderate range).

### Key Equations (Hagen-Poiseuille Derived Results)
- ✅ Parabolic velocity profile: $v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$.
- ✅ Volumetric flow: $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$.
- ✅ Centreline velocity: $v_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$.
- ✅ Average velocity: $V_{avg} = v_{max}/2$ (laminar only).

### Conversions
- ✅ $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$.
- ✅ $1 \text{ Stokes} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

### Common Pitfalls
- ⚠️ Don't say "viscosity decreases with T" — specify **liquid** or **gas**.
- ⚠️ Don't confuse **dynamic** and **kinematic** viscosity units.
- ⚠️ Remember **Hagen-Poiseuille applies to laminar flow** only ($Re < 2300$).
- ⚠️ $v_{avg} = v_{max}/2$ is for **laminar** parabolic flow only — not for turbulent.
- ⚠️ In Bingham plastics, the fluid does **not** flow at all below $\tau_y$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Properties of Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Properties of Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Physical Properties of Fluids

A **fluid** is a substance that continuously deforms (flows) under the application of a **shear stress** (tangential force per unit area), no matter how small that stress is. The study of fluid mechanics fundamentally begins with understanding the **physical properties** that distinguish fluids from solids and govern their behavior under various conditions. These properties form the foundational vocabulary for every subsequent module in the KTU Fluid Mechanics syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 establishes the *physical vocabulary* used throughout the course. Every formula in later modules (continuity, Bernoulli's equation, momentum equation) traces its roots back to these defining properties.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Physical Properties

The **Physical Properties of Fluids** are the intrinsic characteristics that describe how a fluid responds to forces, temperature changes, and pressure variations. For KTU 2024, the following seven properties are designated as core Module 1 content:

1. **Density (ρ)** – Mass per unit volume
2. **Specific Weight (γ)** – Weight per unit volume
3. **Specific Gravity (S)** – Ratio of fluid density to a reference density
4. **Specific Volume (v)** – Volume per unit mass
5. **Viscosity (μ, ν)** – Resistance to deformation
6. **Compressibility & Bulk Modulus (K)** – Resistance to volume change
7. **Surface Tension (σ)** & **Capillarity**
8. **Vapor Pressure (Pv)** – Pressure at which liquid boils

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuum Hypothesis:** In KTU Fluid Mechanics, we treat fluids as a *continuous medium* — meaning properties like density and velocity are defined at every point in space, even though fluids are actually composed of discrete molecules. This assumption is valid as long as the **Knudsen Number (Kn) ≪ 1**, i.e., the molecular mean free path is much smaller than the characteristic length of the flow.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Personality Traits" of a Fluid

Imagine every fluid as a *person* with distinct personality traits:

- **Density** is like a person's **weight per height** (body density) — tells you how "heavy" a fluid feels.
- **Viscosity** is like a person's **patience** — honey (high viscosity) "resists" flowing quickly, while water (low viscosity) flows easily.
- **Surface tension** is like a **thin elastic skin** on water — water striders walk on it because the surface acts like stretched cling film.
- **Compressibility** is like **how easily someone gets squeezed into a small space** — gases (high compressibility) compress easily; liquids (low compressibility) resist.
- **Vapor pressure** is like a person's **threshold of leaving (evaporating)** — a volatile liquid has low vapor pressure threshold and evaporates easily.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a KTU question describes a fluid behavior in words, translate it into one of these eight properties. For example, *"the fluid takes the shape of its container and offers no resistance to shear"* → it is a **fluid** with **zero viscosity** at rest.

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants in Fluid Mechanics

| Symbol | Property | Standard Unit (SI) | Common Engineering Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **ρ** | Density | kg/m³ | kg/m³ |
| **γ** | Specific Weight | N/m³ | N/m³ |
| **S** | Specific Gravity | dimensionless | dimensionless |
| **v** | Specific Volume | m³/kg | m³/kg |
| **μ** | Dynamic Viscosity | Pa·s (N·s/m²) | poise (P) — 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s |
| **ν** | Kinematic Viscosity | m²/s | stokes (St) — 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s |
| **K** | Bulk Modulus | Pa (N/m²) | kPa, MPa, GPa |
| **σ** | Surface Tension | N/m | dyne/cm — 1 dyne/cm = 0.001 N/m |
| **Pv** | Vapor Pressure | Pa (N/m²) | kPa, atm (1 atm = 101.325 kPa) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always write units in **SI (kg, m, s, N, Pa)** unless the question explicitly states otherwise. Marks are routinely deducted for inconsistent unit systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Density (ρ) — Mass per Unit Volume

### Operational Logic Steps:
- **Step 1 — Definition:** Density is the amount of mass (m) contained in a given volume (V) of a fluid.
- **Step 2 — Physical Meaning:** A dense fluid (like mercury, ρ ≈ 13,600 kg/m³) feels "heavy" per unit volume; a light fluid (like air, ρ ≈ 1.225 kg/m³) feels "feathery."
- **Step 3 — Variation:** For liquids, density is *nearly constant* (incompressible). For gases, density varies significantly with pressure and temperature (ideal gas law applies).
- **Step 4 — Engineering Utility:** Density is required in nearly every flow equation: hydrostatic pressure, Reynolds number, Bernoulli, momentum, and discharge calculations.

## 2.2 Specific Weight (γ) — Weight per Unit Volume

- **Step 1 — Definition:** It is the weight (W = mg) per unit volume (V).
- **Step 2 — Relationship with Density:** Since W = mg, dividing by V gives γ = (m/V)·g = **ρg**.
- **Step 3 — Physical Meaning:** Tells us the *force* the fluid exerts per unit volume due to gravity. Used in hydrostatic pressure (P = γh) which is the workhorse of Module 2.

## 2.3 Specific Gravity (S) — Dimensionless Density Ratio

- **Step 1 — Definition:** Ratio of the density of the fluid to the density of a reference fluid (water at 4 °C for liquids, air at standard conditions for gases).
- **Step 2 — Why Water at 4 °C?** Water attains its maximum density (1000 kg/m³) at 4 °C — this was historically chosen as the reference point.
- **Step 3 — Engineering Utility:** Specific gravity of mercury (S = 13.6) is used in manometer problems; specific gravity of oil (S ≈ 0.8) is used in tank gauging.

## 2.4 Specific Volume (v) — Volume per Unit Mass

- **Step 1 — Definition:** The reciprocal of density. v = 1/ρ.
- **Step 2 — Usage in Gases:** More common in gas dynamics and thermodynamics (Module 5+), where v has clearer physical meaning than ρ.

## 2.5 Viscosity (μ & ν) — The Heart of Fluid Resistance

### Newton's Law of Viscosity:
When a fluid flows, internal friction between fluid layers resists relative motion. This resistance is called **viscosity**, characterized by **dynamic viscosity (μ)**.

**Newton's Postulate:** The shear stress (τ) in a fluid is *proportional* to the *rate of angular deformation* (velocity gradient, du/dy).

- **Step 1 — Experimental Setup:** Imagine two parallel plates separated by distance 'y', with the top plate moving at velocity 'U' and the bottom plate stationary.
- **Step 2 — Velocity Profile:** The fluid velocity varies linearly from 0 (at bottom) to U (at top).
- **Step 3 — Shear Stress:** A force F is required to drag the top plate; the shear stress τ = F/A.
- **Step 4 — Newton's Law:** τ = μ (du/dy), where μ is the dynamic viscosity.

### Kinematic Viscosity (ν):
- **Step 1 — Definition:** Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density: ν = μ/ρ.
- **Step 2 — Why "Kinematic"?** It has units of m²/s (kinematic units), with no force term. It represents the *diffusivity of momentum* in a fluid.

### Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids:
- **Newtonian:** μ is constant (e.g., water, air, oil).
- **Non-Newtonian:** μ varies with shear rate (e.g., blood, paint, mud, toothpaste).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Module 1 emphasizes *Newtonian fluids*. Non-Newtonian behavior is briefly introduced for context but is not numerically assessed in Part A/B questions.

## 2.6 Compressibility & Bulk Modulus (K)

### Compressibility (β):
- **Step 1 — Definition:** The fractional reduction in volume per unit increase in pressure.
- **Step 2 — Bulk Modulus (K):** The reciprocal of compressibility, β = 1/K.
- **Step 3 — Physical Meaning:** K measures the fluid's resistance to uniform compression. For water, K ≈ 2.2 GPa (very high, nearly incompressible). For air, K ≈ 101 kPa (very compressible).

### Why This Matters:
- **Step 4 — Engineering Application:** In high-speed aerodynamics, water hammer, and shock waves, compressibility cannot be ignored. The **Mach Number (M = V/c)** determines whether a fluid can be treated as incompressible (M < 0.3) or compressible (M > 0.3).

## 2.7 Surface Tension (σ) & Capillarity

### Surface Tension:
- **Step 1 — Molecular Origin:** Molecules in the bulk fluid experience equal forces from all directions. Molecules at the *surface* experience a net inward pull, creating a "stretched membrane" effect.
- **Step 2 — Definition:** Surface tension σ is the force per unit length acting along the surface, perpendicular to a line drawn on the surface.
- **Step 3 — Units:** N/m (or dyne/cm in CGS).
- **Step 4 — Engineering Example:** Droplet formation, soap bubbles, and jet breakup all involve surface tension.

### Capillarity:
- **Step 1 — Phenomenon:** When a thin tube (capillary) is dipped in a liquid, the liquid rises (e.g., water in glass) or falls (e.g., mercury in glass).
- **Step 2 — Driving Forces:** Surface tension and the contact angle (θ) between liquid and solid.
- **Step 3 — Jurin's Law:** Capillary rise h = (4σ cos θ) / (γd), where d is the tube diameter.

## 2.8 Vapor Pressure (Pv)

- **Step 1 — Definition:** The pressure exerted by a vapor in equilibrium with its liquid phase at a given temperature.
- **Step 2 — Physical Meaning:** When the local fluid pressure drops below Pv, vapor bubbles form → **cavitation** (a critical phenomenon in pumps and turbines).
- **Step 3 — Engineering Application:** Designing pump impellers to avoid cavitation; setting minimum operating pressures in pipelines.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **must-know cheat sheet** for Module 1. Memorize all symbols, units, and relationships.

| # | Property | Formula | Definition of Variables | SI Units |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------------|----------|
| 1 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | m = mass, V = volume | kg/m³ |
| 2 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | g = 9.81 m/s² (gravitational acceleration) | N/m³ |
| 3 | Specific Gravity | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{reference}}$ | Reference: water @ 4 °C = 1000 kg/m³ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Reciprocal of density | m³/kg |
| 5 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | τ = shear stress, du/dy = velocity gradient | Pa (N/m²) |
