| 6 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | Measured by viscometer | Pa·s |
| 7 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density | m²/s |
| 8 | Compressibility | $\beta = \dfrac{-dV/V}{dP}$ | Fractional volume change per unit pressure | m²/N (= 1/Pa) |
| 9 | Bulk Modulus | $K = \dfrac{1}{\beta} = \dfrac{-dP}{dV/V}$ | Resistance to compression | Pa (N/m²) |
| 10 | Surface Tension (force) | $F = \sigma L$ | F = force on line, L = length of line | N/m |
| 11 | Capillary Rise | $h = \dfrac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\gamma d}$ | θ = contact angle, d = tube diameter | m |
| 12 | Vapor Pressure | $P_v$ | Equilibrium pressure at given T | Pa |
| 13 | Pressure-Velocity Relation | $P = \gamma h = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth h | Pa |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Viscosity and compressibility dictate aircraft skin-friction drag and shock-wave formation.
- **Civil Engineering:** Specific weight and surface tension govern dam design, spillway flow, and concrete curing.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Vapor pressure and cavitation limits determine pump selection and pipe network design.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Specific gravity and viscosity determine settling rates, pipeline pressure drops, and reactor mixing.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood viscosity (non-Newtonian) and capillary rise govern IV flow and micro-fluidic devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Newton's Law of Viscosity from the No-Slip Condition

### Setup:
Consider two infinite parallel plates separated by a small distance 'h'. The bottom plate is fixed, and the top plate moves with a constant velocity 'U' under the application of a tangential force 'F'. The space between the plates is filled with a fluid.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \quad \text{Experimental observation shows a linear velocity profile:} \\
&\quad u(y) = \left(\frac{U}{h}\right) y \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \quad \text{The velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) is constant:} \\
&\quad \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \quad \text{Newton's postulate: shear stress } \tau \text{ is proportional to velocity gradient:} \\
&\quad \tau \propto \frac{du}{dy} \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \quad \text{Introducing the constant of proportionality } \mu \text{ (dynamic viscosity):} \\
&\quad \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \quad \text{Substituting the linear profile, the shear stress on either plate becomes:} \\
&\quad \tau = \mu \frac{U}{h} \\
&\textbf{Step 6:} \quad \text{The force on the top plate of area A required to maintain velocity U:} \\
&\quad F = \tau A = \mu A \frac{U}{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Physical Interpretation:
- The **no-slip condition** (fluid in contact with a solid surface has the same velocity as the surface) is essential.
- For **μ to be a true fluid property**, τ must depend only on du/dy, not on time or position. Fluids obeying this are called **Newtonian fluids**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \quad \text{Start with definitions of dynamic and kinematic viscosity:} \\
&\quad \mu = \text{dynamic viscosity} \quad [\text{Pa·s}] \\
&\quad \rho = \text{fluid density} \quad [\text{kg/m}^3] \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \quad \text{Kinematic viscosity is defined as the ratio:} \\
&\quad \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \quad \text{Unit verification:} \\
&\quad [\nu] = \frac{[\text{Pa·s}]}{[\text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{[\text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{s}]}{[\text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{[\text{kg/(m·s}^2)] \cdot \text{s}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \quad \text{Special-case numerical example:} \\
&\quad \text{For water at 20 °C: } \rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3, \mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} \\
&\quad \nu = \frac{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}{998} = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 1.004 \text{ cSt (centistokes)} \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \quad \text{Engineering takeaway: kinematic viscosity is essentially "momentum diffusivity."}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bulk Modulus from Compressibility

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \quad \text{Compressibility } \beta \text{ is defined as the fractional decrease in volume per unit increase in pressure:} \\
&\quad \beta = -\frac{dV/V}{dP} = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dP} \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \quad \text{The negative sign ensures } \beta \text{ is positive (since dV < 0 when dP > 0).} \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \quad \text{Bulk modulus is defined as the reciprocal of compressibility:} \\
&\quad K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -\frac{dP}{dV/V} = -V\frac{dP}{dV} \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \quad \text{For a finite (rather than differential) pressure change, the average bulk modulus is:} \\
&\quad K_{avg} = -\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V / V} \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \quad \text{Numerical example for water: applying } \Delta P = 1 \text{ MPa causes } \Delta V/V \approx -4.55 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\quad K = -\frac{1 \times 10^{6}}{-4.55 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.2 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} = 2.2 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \quad \text{A thin glass tube of diameter } d \text{ is dipped in a liquid. Surface tension pulls the liquid up.} \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \quad \text{Force due to surface tension along the contact line of length } \pi d:} \\
&\quad F_{\sigma} = \sigma \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \cos\theta \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \quad \text{Vertical component of this force (upward):} \\
&\quad F_{\sigma, v} = \pi d \sigma \cos\theta \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \quad \text{Weight of the liquid column raised to height h:} \\
&\quad W = \rho g \cdot V = \rho g \left(\frac{\pi d^2}{4} h\right) \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \quad \text{At equilibrium, upward force = downward weight:} \\
&\quad \pi d \sigma \cos\theta = \rho g \frac{\pi d^2}{4} h \\
&\textbf{Step 6:} \quad \text{Solving for h:} \\
&\quad h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d} = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\gamma d} \\
&\textbf{Step 7:} \quad \text{For water-glass } (\theta \approx 0, \cos\theta = 1), \sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}, d = 1 \text{ mm} = 0.001 \text{ m}, \gamma = 9790 \text{ N/m}^3: \\
&\quad h = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{9790 \times 0.001} = 0.0297 \text{ m} \approx 30 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(validates the formula)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Hydrostatic Pressure Variation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \quad \text{Consider a static fluid element of height } dz \text{ and horizontal area } A. \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \quad \text{Force balance in the vertical direction:} \\
&\quad P \cdot A - (P + dP) \cdot A - \rho g \cdot A \cdot dz = 0 \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \quad \text{Simplify:} \\
&\quad -A \, dP - \rho g A \, dz = 0 \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \quad \text{Isolate dP/dz:} \\
&\quad \frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \quad \text{For an incompressible fluid (constant } \rho\text{), integrate from point 1 to point 2:} \\
&\quad \int_{P_1}^{P_2} dP = -\rho g \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz \\
&\quad P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1) \\
&\textbf{Step 6:} \quad \text{If point 1 is the free surface (} P_1 = P_{atm}, z_1 = 0\text{) and point 2 is at depth h:} \\
&\quad P - P_{atm} = \rho g h \quad \text{or} \quad P_{gauge} = \gamma h
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Property Inter-Relationships

| From Property | To Property | Conversion Formula | Comment |
|---------------|-------------|--------------------|---------|
| ρ | γ | γ = ρg | Use g = 9.81 m/s² (KTU convention) |
| ρ | S | S = ρ/ρ_water | Reference: water at 4 °C |
| γ | S | S = γ/γ_water | Quicker for manometer problems |
| ρ | v | v = 1/ρ | Used in gas dynamics |
| μ | ν | ν = μ/ρ | Use to compare fluids of different densities |
| β | K | K = 1/β | K is more commonly tabulated |
| σ (N/m) | σ (dyne/cm) | 1 N/m = 1000 dyne/cm | CGS conversion |
| μ (Pa·s) | μ (poise) | 1 Pa·s = 10 poise | 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s (water at 20 °C ≈ 1 cP) |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A liquid has a kinematic viscosity of $4 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s and a specific gravity of 0.85. Calculate (a) the dynamic viscosity in Pa·s, and (b) the specific weight in N/m³.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Given:} \\
&\nu = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \\
&S = 0.85 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&\textbf{Part (a) — Dynamic Viscosity:} \\
&\mu = \nu \cdot \rho = (4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 850 = 0.034 \text{ Pa·s} \\
&\textbf{Part (b) — Specific Weight:} \\
&\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 850 \times 9.81 = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3 \\
&\boxed{\mu = 0.034 \text{ Pa·s}, \quad \gamma = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Physical Properties of Fluids

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[PHYSICAL PROPERTIES OF FLUIDS] --> nodeB[Rheological Properties]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Thermodynamic Properties]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Interfacial Properties]

    nodeB --> nodeE[Viscosity]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Dynamic Viscosity mu]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Kinematic Viscosity nu]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Newtonian Fluids]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Non Newtonian Fluids]

    nodeC --> nodeJ[Density rho]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Specific Weight gamma]
    nodeC --> nodeL[Specific Gravity S]
    nodeC --> nodeM[Specific Volume v]
    nodeC --> nodeN[Compressibility beta]
    nodeC --> nodeO[Bulk Modulus K]
    nodeC --> nodeP[Vapor Pressure Pv]

    nodeD --> nodeQ[Surface Tension sigma]
    nodeD --> nodeR[Capillarity]
    nodeD --> nodeS[Contact Angle theta]

    nodeF -.-> nodeG
    nodeJ -.-> nodeK
    nodeJ -.-> nodeL
    nodeN -.-> nodeO
    nodeQ -.-> nodeR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top with the central concept, then trace the branches to see how each property is categorized. The dotted arrows (`.->`) indicate mathematical relationships (e.g., ν = μ/ρ).

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Newton’s Law of Viscosity Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Top Plate Moves at Velocity U] --> step2[Fluid in Contact Moves at U]
    step2 --> step3[No Slip at Bottom Plate]
    step3 --> step4[Linear Velocity Profile Forms]
    step4 --> step5[Velocity Gradient du by dy]
    step5 --> step6[Shear Stress tau Appears]
    step6 --> step7[tau equals mu times du by dy]
    step7 --> step8[Newtonian Fluid Behavior]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capillary Rise Force Balance

```mermaid
graph TD
    cap1[Glass Tube in Liquid] --> cap2[Surface Tension Pulls Liquid Up]
    cap2 --> cap3[Force F sigma equals pi d sigma cos theta]
    cap3 --> cap4[Weight of Column Pulls Down]
    cap4 --> cap5[Weight W equals rho g pi d squared by 4 times h]
    cap5 --> cap6[Equilibrium F sigma equals W]
    cap6 --> cap7[Capillary Rise h equals 4 sigma cos theta by gamma d]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Compressibility vs. Bulk Modulus Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    c1[Pressure Increase dP] --> c2[Volume Decrease dV]
    c2 --> c3[Compressibility beta equals minus dV by V divided by dP]
    c3 --> c4[Bulk Modulus K equals 1 over beta]
    c4 --> c5[For Water K equals 2.2 GPa]
    c4 --> c6[For Air K equals 101 kPa]
    c5 --> c7[Treated as Incompressible]
    c6 --> c8[Treated as Compressible]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a Block Diagram instead of a physical drawing?** A Mermaid graph cannot render curved menisci or sheared fluid layers with high fidelity. The block-diagram representation abstracts the *physical concept* into **functional steps** (cause → effect → property) which is the cognitive structure examiners reward in KTU answers.

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Continuum Hypothesis Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    con1[Flow Problem Encountered] --> con2{Knudsen Number Kn}
    con2 --> con3[Kn much less than 1]
    con2 --> con4[Kn greater than 0.1]
    con3 --> con5[Continuum Hypothesis Valid]
    con5 --> con6[Treat Fluid as Continuous]
    con6 --> con7[Apply Navier Stokes Equations]
    con4 --> con8[Need Molecular Approach]
    con8 --> con9[Use Boltzmann Equation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A consists of 5–10 short questions (2–3 marks) covering direct recall and one-line derivations. Model answers should be 3–5 sentences or a single formula with a brief explanation.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — Definition of Dynamic Viscosity
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q:** Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units and the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

Dynamic viscosity (μ) is the constant of proportionality in **Newton's law of viscosity** that relates shear stress (τ) in a fluid to the velocity gradient (du/dy) perpendicular to the flow direction. Its SI unit is the **pascal-second (Pa·s)**. Kinematic viscosity (ν) is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density, ν = μ/ρ, and has SI units of **m²/s**. Kinematic viscosity represents the *diffusivity of momentum* in the fluid, while dynamic viscosity quantifies the *internal friction*.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — Distinction Between Density and Specific Weight
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

**Q:** Differentiate between density, specific weight, and specific gravity of a fluid.

**Model Answer:**

- **Density (ρ)** is the mass per unit volume of a fluid (kg/m³).
- **Specific weight (γ)** is the weight per unit volume (N/m³) and is related to density by γ = ρg.
- **Specific gravity (S)** is the *dimensionless* ratio of the fluid's density to that of a reference fluid (water at 4 °C for liquids).

For mercury: ρ = 13,600 kg/m³, γ = 13,600 × 9.81 = 133,416 N/m³, S = 13.6.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 7 + 7 = 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part must have: (i) a labeled diagram, (ii) a step-by-step solution, and (iii) a boxed final answer with units.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Newton's Law of Viscosity & Capillary Rise

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Question A]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A 5-mm-thick layer of oil (μ = 0.5 Pa·s) is sandwiched between two parallel plates, the top one moving at 1.2 m/s. Calculate the shear stress on the top plate. If the plate area is 0.25 m², find the force required to maintain this motion.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A clean glass tube of 1.5 mm diameter is dipped in water at 20 °C. Find the capillary rise. (Given: σ = 0.0728 N/m, θ = 0° for clean water-glass, ρ = 998 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².)

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Given:} \\
&\mu = 0.5 \text{ Pa·s} \\
&h = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m} \\
&U = 1.2 \text{ m/s} \\
&A = 0.25 \text{ m}^2 \\
&\textbf{Step 1: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity:} \\
&\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \frac{U}{h} \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Substitute values:} \\
&\tau = 0.5 \times \frac{1.2}{0.005} = 0.5 \times 240 = 120 \text{ N/m}^2 = 120 \text{ Pa} \\
&\textbf{Step 3: Force on the plate:} \\
&F = \tau \cdot A = 120 \times 0.25 = 30 \text{ N} \\
&\boxed{\tau = 120 \text{ Pa}, \quad F = 30 \text{ N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Q (b):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Given:} \\
&d = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m} \\
&\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m} \\
&\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1 \\
&\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
&\textbf{Step 1: Use Jurin's Law for capillary rise:} \\
&h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d} \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Substitute values:} \\
&h = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{998 \times 9.81 \times 0.0015} \\
&h = \frac{0.2912}{14.686} \\
&h = 0.01983 \text{ m} \approx 19.83 \text{ mm} \\
&\boxed{h \approx 19.83 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — Compressibility & Specific Weight

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, Question B]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A liquid has a bulk modulus of 2.4 GPa. Find the pressure required to reduce its volume by 1.5%.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A tank contains 0.6 m³ of a liquid with specific gravity 1.25. Calculate its mass, weight, and specific weight.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Given:} \\
&K = 2.4 \text{ GPa} = 2.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \\
&\frac{\Delta V}{V} = -1.5\% = -0.015 \quad \text{(negative sign indicates volume reduction)} \\
&\textbf{Step 1: Use the definition of bulk modulus:} \\
&K = -\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V / V} \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Solve for } \Delta P: \\
&\Delta P = -K \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{V} = -(2.4 \times 10^9) \times (-0.015) \\
&\Delta P = 3.6 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 36 \text{ MPa} \\
&\boxed{\Delta P = 36 \text{ MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Q (b):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Given:} \\
&V = 0.6 \text{ m}^3 \\
&S = 1.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = 1.25 \times 1000 = 1250 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&\textbf{Step 1: Mass:} \\
&m = \rho V = 1250 \times 0.6 = 750 \text{ kg} \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Weight:} \\
&W = m g = 750 \times 9.81 = 7357.5 \text{ N} \approx 7.36 \text{ kN} \\
&\textbf{Step 3: Specific Weight:} \\
&\gamma = \rho g = 1250 \times 9.81 = 12,262.5 \text{ N/m}^3 \\
&\boxed{m = 750 \text{ kg}, \quad W = 7.36 \text{ kN}, \quad \gamma = 12,262.5 \text{ N/m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing 1.2 × 10⁻³ instead of 1.2 × 10⁶ when the value is large. Always express in scientific notation with units.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in bulk modulus:** The negative sign in K = −V(dP/dV) is *mathematically essential* to keep K positive. Drop it and you get a negative bulk modulus — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using g = 10 m/s² instead of 9.81 m/s²:** KTU convention is **9.81 m/s²**. Using 10 is "computationally convenient" but **not standard** in KTU valuation keys unless explicitly stated.
> 4. **Confusing specific weight with density:** These are *different* physical quantities. Density is in kg/m³; specific weight is in N/m³. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** Part B questions **require** a labeled diagram (capillary tube, parallel plates, etc.). Omitting it costs 1–2 marks per sub-question.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state that the fluid is *Newtonian*, *incompressible* (where applicable), and the flow is *laminar* when using Newton's law of viscosity.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 1, Physical Properties of Fluids:**

- [ ] **Density (ρ):** ρ = m/V; SI unit kg/m³. Water at 4 °C has ρ = 1000 kg/m³.
- [ ] **Specific Weight (γ):** γ = ρg; SI unit N/m³. For water, γ = 9810 N/m³.
- [ ] **Specific Gravity (S):** Dimensionless. S = ρ/ρ_water. Mercury has S = 13.6.
- [ ] **Specific Volume (v):** v = 1/ρ; SI unit m³/kg.
- [ ] **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** τ = μ(du/dy); μ is dynamic viscosity in Pa·s.
- [ ] **Kinematic Viscosity:** ν = μ/ρ; SI unit m²/s. For water at 20 °C, ν ≈ 10⁻⁶ m²/s = 1 cSt.
- [ ] **Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian:** Water/air/oil are Newtonian; blood, paint, mud are non-Newtonian.
- [ ] **Compressibility (β):** β = −(1/V)(dV/dP); units 1/Pa.
- [ ] **Bulk Modulus (K):** K = 1/β. For water, K ≈ 2.2 GPa (nearly incompressible).
- [ ] **Mach Number Criterion:** M < 0.3 → incompressible; M > 0.3 → compressible.
- [ ] **Surface Tension (σ):** Force per unit length along a surface, SI unit N/m. Water at 20 °C: σ ≈ 0.0728 N/m.
- [ ] **Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law):** h = 4σ cos θ / (γd). For water-glass, θ ≈ 0, so cos θ = 1.
- [ ] **Vapor Pressure (Pv):** Pressure at which liquid boils. When local P drops below Pv → **cavitation**.
- [ ] **No-Slip Condition:** Fluid in contact with a solid surface has the same velocity as that surface. This is the cornerstone of boundary layer theory.
- [ ] **Continuum Hypothesis:** Valid when Kn ≪ 1. Allows us to treat fluid properties as continuous functions of space and time.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic Pressure:** P = γh = ρgh. This is the basis of all manometer problems in Module 2.
- [ ] **Common Conversions to Memorize:**
  - 1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s
  - 1 stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s
  - 1 dyne/cm = 0.001 N/m
  - 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 101,325 Pa
- [ ] **Standard Reference Conditions:** Water at 4 °C (ρ = 1000 kg/m³), standard gravity g = 9.81 m/s².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Every Module 1 property you study here will resurface in **continuity equations, Bernoulli's equation, Reynolds number, boundary layer theory, and dimensional analysis**. Mastering these foundational properties is not optional — it is the *sine qua non* of fluid mechanics. Practice the numerical examples, memorize the formula sheet, and visualize each property through the Mermaid diagrams provided.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Density ($\rho$)** is defined as the **mass per unit volume** of a fluid substance. It is a fundamental intrinsic property that quantifies how tightly matter is packed within a given spatial envelope. Mathematically, it is expressed as the limiting ratio of mass to volume as the volume shrinks to a point (continuum approach):

$$\rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to \delta V} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V}$$

where the elemental volume $\delta V$ is assumed to be mathematically infinitesimal yet physically large enough to contain a statistically meaningful number of molecules (satisfying the continuum hypothesis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Density is a **scalar point function** of space and time in general, i.e., $\rho = \rho(x, y, z, t)$. For a homogeneous fluid in static equilibrium, it reduces to a constant scalar.

The **SI Unit** of density is **kilogram per cubic meter ($\text{kg/m}^3$)**, while in CGS units, it is expressed as $\text{g/cm}^3$ or $\text{g/cc}$. The dimensional formula is $\left[ M L^{-3} \right]$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Packed Suitcase"**
>
> Imagine you are packing two suitcases of the **exact same size** (volume). In the first suitcase, you carefully fold lightweight cotton t-shirts; in the second, you stack heavy denim jeans and books. Both suitcases occupy identical space, but the second one is far heavier because the *amount of matter packed into the same space* is greater.
>
> **Density is simply "how much stuff is squeezed into a given space."** A kilogram of feathers and a kilogram of iron have the **same mass** but vastly different **densities** because the feathers occupy a much larger volume.

### 1.3 Related Fundamental Fluid Properties (KTU Module 1 Cluster)

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Key Distinction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density** | $\rho$ | Mass per unit volume | Independent of gravity |
| **Specific Weight** | $\gamma$ | Weight per unit volume ($\gamma = \rho g$) | Depends on local gravity |
| **Specific Gravity** | $S$ (or $SG$) | Ratio of fluid density to water density at 4 °C | Dimensionless quantity |
| **Specific Volume** | $v$ | Volume per unit mass ($v = 1/\rho$) | Reciprocal of density |
| **Bulk Modulus** | $K$ | Resistance to compression | Related to compressibility |

### 1.4 Standard Reference Values (Memorize for KTU Board Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Density of Water:** $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **at 4 °C** (maximum density of water).
> **Standard Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
> **Standard Density of Air (at 15 °C, 1 atm):** $\rho_{\text{air}} \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
> **Mercury Density:** $\rho_{\text{Hg}} \approx 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

These constants form the foundation of nearly every numerical problem in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Breaking Down the Concept

The concept of density in fluid mechanics operates on three hierarchical levels of understanding:

**Step 1 — The Continuum Premise**
A fluid is treated as a continuous medium rather than a collection of discrete molecules. This allows us to assign a *unique density value* to every point in the fluid domain, making calculus-based analysis possible.

**Step 2 — The Local vs. Global Distinction**
* **Local (Point) Density:** $\rho = \rho(x,y,z,t)$ — varies from point to point in non-uniform or compressible flow.
* **Average (Bulk) Density:** $\bar{\rho} = \dfrac{m_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$ — used when fluid composition varies (e.g., mixtures).

**Step 3 — The Incompressibility Simplification**
For liquids under moderate pressure changes, density is assumed **constant** ($\rho = \text{constant}$). This is the **incompressible flow assumption** and is the default in most KTU Module 1 problems.

### 2.2 Why and How Density Governs Fluid Behavior

* **Why it matters:** Density determines the **inertia** of a fluid element. Newton's second law for fluids ($\sum F = ma$) when expressed per unit volume yields $\sum f = \rho a$, where $\rho$ multiplies acceleration.
* **How it manifests:**
  * In **buoyancy** (Archimedes' principle): $\rho$ decides whether an object floats or sinks.
  * In **manometry**: Pressure at depth $h$ is $P = \rho g h$.
  * In **flow rate problems**: Mass flow $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ and weight flow $\dot{W} = \gamma A V$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the definitive formula reference for the topic "Density" in KTU 2024 Module 1 of Fluid Mechanics. Commit these to active memory.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use Case | SI Unit | Typical KTU Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Basic density definition | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | 2–3 |
| 2 | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | Specific weight from density | $\text{N/m}^3$ | 3 |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at 4°C}}}$ | Specific gravity (dimensionless) | — | 3–7 |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Specific volume | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | 2 |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate in pipe | $\text{kg/s}$ | 7 |
| 6 | $\dot{W} = \gamma A V$ | Weight flow rate in pipe | $\text{N/s}$ | 7 |
| 7 | $\rho_{\text{mixture}} = \dfrac{\sum m_i}{\sum V_i}$ | Density of immiscible mixture | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | 7 |
| 8 | $S_{\text{mixture}} = \dfrac{\sum S_i V_i}{\sum V_i}$ | Specific gravity of liquid mixture (vol. basis) | — | 7 |
| 9 | $S_{\text{mixture}} = \dfrac{\sum (m_i / S_i)}{\sum (m_i / S_i / \rho_w)}$ | Specific gravity by mass basis | — | 7 |
| 10 | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ | Ideal gas density (universal gas law) | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | 7 |
| 11 | $\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T}$ | Ideal gas density using molar mass | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | 7 |

Where $R_u = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ is the **Universal Gas Constant** and $R = R_u / M$ is the **Specific Gas Constant** for a given gas.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Civil Engineering:** Density of concrete ($\approx 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$) governs dead load calculations for dams, bridges, and high-rise buildings.
* **Mechanical/Aerospace:** Air density variation with altitude directly affects aircraft lift, jet engine thrust, and wind-turbine power output.
* **Chemical Engineering:** Density is essential in **petroleum refining** (API gravity is inversely derived from specific gravity) and in **pipeline transport** of crude oil, LPG, and natural gas.
* **Hydraulic Engineering:** Density differences drive **stratified flow** in reservoirs and **thermocline** formation in lakes, which directly affect water-quality modeling.

### 2.5 Critical Conceptual Distinctions (Frequently Confused in Exams)

> [!WARNING]
> **Density ($\rho$) vs. Specific Weight ($\gamma$):**
> $\gamma$ depends on $g$ (location on Earth), but $\rho$ does **not**. On the Moon, mass and density are identical to Earth, but specific weight drops to roughly $1/6$th.
>
> **Specific Gravity ($S$) vs. Density ($\rho$):**
> $S$ is **dimensionless**; $\rho$ has units. Never write "specific gravity = 0.85 kg/m³" — it is a pure number.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Specific Weight and Density

Starting from Newton's second law applied to a fluid element of mass $m$ subject to gravitational pull:

**Step 1 — Write weight as a force.**

$$W = m \cdot g$$

where $W$ is weight in Newtons, $m$ is mass in kg, and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by the volume $V$ occupied by the fluid element.**

$$\frac{W}{V} = \frac{m \cdot g}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the left-hand side as specific weight $\gamma$ and rearrange the right-hand side.**

$$\gamma = \left( \frac{m}{V} \right) \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $m/V$ is the definition of density $\rho$.**

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

This elegant relation shows that specific weight is simply **density scaled by gravitational acceleration**. It is dimensionally consistent: $\left[ M L^{-3} \right] \cdot \left[ L T^{-2} \right] = \left[ M L^{-2} T^{-2} \right] = \text{N/m}^3$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Density of an Ideal Gas

**Step 1 — Start with the universal (ideal) gas law.**

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R_u \cdot T$$

where $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa), $V$ = volume (m³), $n$ = number of moles, $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), and $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

**Step 2 — Convert moles to mass using molar mass $M$ (kg/kmol).**

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $n = m/M$ into the gas law.**

$$P \cdot V = \frac{m}{M} \cdot R_u \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the mass-to-volume ratio on one side.**

$$\frac{m}{V} = \frac{P \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize $m/V$ as density $\rho$.**

$$\rho = \frac{P \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T}$$

This is the **standard ideal-gas density equation** used in compressible flow and atmospheric calculations. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Density of a Mixture of Immiscible Liquids (Volume Basis)

Consider $k$ immiscible liquids with specific gravities $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k$ and corresponding volumes $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k$.

**Step 1 — Total mass of the mixture.**

$$m_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \rho_i V_i = \rho_w \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i V_i$$

where $\rho_w$ is the density of water and we used $\rho_i = S_i \cdot \rho_w$.

**Step 2 — Total volume of the mixture (since liquids are immiscible, volumes are additive).**

$$V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} V_i$$

**Step 3 — Specific gravity of the mixture.**

$$S_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{mixture}}}{\rho_w} = \frac{m_{\text{total}} / V_{\text{total}}}{\rho_w} = \frac{\rho_w \sum S_i V_i}{\rho_w \sum V_i}$$

**Step 4 — Final clean form.**

$$S_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i V_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} V_i}$$

This is the **volume-weighted average of specific gravities**. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternate form (mass basis):** If specific gravities are given along with masses, use $S_{\text{mixture}} = \dfrac{\sum m_i}{\sum (m_i / S_i)}$. This is a common KTU trick question.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Module 1 Style)

> **Problem:** A vessel contains 0.4 m³ of glycerin ($S = 1.26$) and 0.6 m³ of oil ($S = 0.85$). Find (a) the specific gravity of the mixture, and (b) the specific weight of the mixture in $\text{kN/m}^3$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$S_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{S_1 V_1 + S_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2} = \frac{(1.26)(0.4) + (0.85)(0.6)}{0.4 + 0.6}$$

$$S_{\text{mixture}} = \frac{0.504 + 0.510}{1.0} = \frac{1.014}{1.0} = 1.014$$

**Part (b):**

$$\gamma_{\text{mixture}} = \rho_{\text{mixture}} \cdot g = S_{\text{mixture}} \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$$

$$\gamma_{\text{mixture}} = 1.014 \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\gamma_{\text{mixture}} = 9947.34 \text{ N/m}^3 \approx 9.947 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Final Answers:** $S_{\text{mixture}} = 1.014$ (dimensionless), $\gamma_{\text{mixture}} \approx 9.95 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Density Property Hierarchy

The following diagram maps the logical interconnections between density, specific weight, specific gravity, and specific volume, illustrating how each property flows from the fundamental definition of density.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Fundamental Quantity: Mass m (kg)"]
    node2["Fundamental Quantity: Volume V (m^3)"]
    nodeA["DENSITY rho = m / V"]
    node3["SQUARE BRACKET kg per m^3"]
    nodeB["SPECIFIC WEIGHT gamma = rho times g"]
    node4["SQUARE BRACKET N per m^3"]
    nodeC["SPECIFIC GRAVITY S = rho_fluid / rho_water"]
    node5["SQUARE BRACKET dimensionless"]
    nodeD["SPECIFIC VOLUME v = 1 / rho"]
    node6["SQUARE BRACKET m^3 per kg"]
    nodeE["MASS FLOW RATE m_dot = rho A V"]
    nodeF["WEIGHT FLOW RATE W_dot = gamma A V"]
    nodeG["IDEAL GAS DENSITY rho = P M / Ru T"]

    node1 --> nodeA
    node2 --> nodeA
    nodeA --> node3
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> node4
    nodeC --> node5
    nodeD --> node6
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

### 4.2 Density Variation Decision Flowchart (For Problem-Solving)

This block diagram helps KTU students select the correct density formula based on the problem context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Identify the Fluid Type"]
    q1{"Is it an Ideal Gas?"}
    q2{"Is it a Single Liquid?"}
    q3{"Is it a Liquid Mixture?"}

    p1["Use Ideal Gas Law: rho = P M over R_u T"]
    p2["Given rho directly OR S then rho = S times rho_w"]
    p3a["Given volumes of each liquid"]
    p3b["Given masses of each liquid"]
    p4a["S_mix = SUM S_i V_i over SUM V_i"]
    p4b["S_mix = SUM m_i over SUM of m_i over S_i"]
    p5["Compute Specific Weight: gamma = rho g"]
    p6["Use rho in subsequent equations"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|YES| p1
    q1 -->|NO| q2
    q2 -->|YES| p2
    q2 -->|NO| q3
    q3 -->|Volumes| p3a
    q3 -->|Masses| p3b
    p3a --> p4a
    p3b --> p4b
    p1 --> p5
    p2 --> p5
    p4a --> p5
    p4b --> p5
    p5 --> p6
```

### 4.3 Standard Reference Density Cross-Reference Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refWater["WATER at 4 C: rho = 1000 kg/m^3, S = 1.00"]
    refAir["AIR at 15 C, 1 atm: rho = 1.225 kg/m^3, S = 0.001225"]
    refMercury["MERCURY at 20 C: rho = 13600 kg/m^3, S = 13.6"]
    refSeawater["SEA WATER: rho = 1025 kg/m^3, S = 1.025"]
    refGlycerin["GLYCERIN: rho = 1260 kg/m^3, S = 1.26"]
    refOil["TYPICAL OIL: rho = 850 to 900 kg/m^3, S = 0.85 to 0.90"]

    refWater --- refAir
    refWater --- refMercury
    refWater --- refSeawater
    refWater --- refGlycerin
    refWater --- refOil
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize **water, mercury, air, glycerin, and seawater** specific gravities — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 1 numerical problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the following fluid properties and state their SI units:
(a) Density
(b) Specific Weight
(c) Specific Gravity

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Density ($\rho$):** The mass per unit volume of a fluid, given by $\rho = m/V$. **SI Unit:** $\text{kg/m}^3$.

(b) **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** The weight per unit volume of a fluid, given by $\gamma = \rho \cdot g = W/V$. **SI Unit:** $\text{N/m}^3$.

(c) **Specific Gravity ($S$):** The ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of pure water at 4 °C, given by $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water at 4°C}}$. **It is a dimensionless quantity** (no units).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
Distinguish between density and specific weight. Why does a fluid have the same density on Earth and on the Moon, but different specific weights?

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Density ($\rho$) | Specific Weight ($\gamma$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Mass per unit volume | Weight per unit volume |
| Depends on $g$? | **No** | **Yes** |
| SI Unit | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| Formula | $\rho = m/V$ | $\gamma = \rho g$ |

The density of a fluid depends only on the **amount of matter packed into a unit volume** (a property of the material itself), which is unaffected by gravitational field strength. However, specific weight depends on the **local gravitational acceleration $g$**. Since the Moon's gravity is approximately $1/6$th of Earth's, the specific weight on the Moon is also $1/6$th of its Earth value, while the density remains unchanged.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A storage tank contains 0.8 m³ of a liquid with specific gravity 1.15 and 0.5 m³ of another liquid with specific gravity 0.92. The liquids are immiscible. Find:
(i) The total mass of the mixture.
(ii) The specific gravity of the mixture.
(iii) The specific weight of the mixture in $\text{kN/m}^3$.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the density of an ideal gas in terms of pressure, molar mass, universal gas constant, and absolute temperature. An ideal gas is stored at a pressure of 200 kPa and temperature of 60 °C. If the molar mass of the gas is 28 g/mol, calculate its density. Take $R_u = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Given:** $V_1 = 0.8 \text{ m}^3$, $S_1 = 1.15$; $V_2 = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$, $S_2 = 0.92$; $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Total mass:**

Mass of liquid 1: $m_1 = S_1 \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_1 = 1.15 \times 1000 \times 0.8 = 920 \text{ kg}$

Mass of liquid 2: $m_2 = S_2 \cdot \rho_w \cdot V_2 = 0.92 \times 1000 \times 0.5 = 460 \text{ kg}$

Total mass: $m_{\text{total}} = 920 + 460 = 1380 \text{ kg}$

**(ii) Specific gravity of the mixture (volume basis):**

$$S_{\text{mix}} = \frac{S_1 V_1 + S_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2} = \frac{(1.15)(0.8) + (0.92)(0.5)}{0.8 + 0.5}$$

$$S_{\text{mix}} = \frac{0.920 + 0.460}{1.3} = \frac{1.380}{1.3} = 1.0615$$

**(iii) Specific weight:**

$$\gamma_{\text{mix}} = S_{\text{mix}} \cdot \rho_w \cdot g = 1.0615 \times 1000 \times 9.81$$

$$\gamma_{\text{mix}} = 10{,}413.7 \text{ N/m}^3 \approx 10.41 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Derivation:** Starting from the ideal gas law $PV = n R_u T$, with $n = m/M$:

$$\frac{m}{V} = \frac{P \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{P \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $P = 200 \text{ kPa} = 200{,}000 \text{ Pa}$, $T = 60 + 273 = 333 \text{ K}$, $M = 28 \text{ g/mol} = 0.028 \text{ kg/mol}$.

$$\rho = \frac{(200{,}000)(0.028)}{(8.314)(333)} = \frac{5600}{2768.56} \approx 2.023 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Answer:** $\rho \approx 2.02 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Define density and specific gravity. A hydraulic oil has a specific gravity of 0.86. Calculate (i) its density, (ii) its specific weight in $\text{N/m}^3$, and (iii) the mass of 3.5 m³ of this oil.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pipe of diameter 15 cm carries oil (specific gravity 0.88) with a mean velocity of 2.5 m/s. Find the (i) mass flow rate and (ii) weight flow rate of the oil through the pipe. If the same volume flow rate is achieved with glycerin (specific gravity 1.26), what is the new mass flow rate?

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

Given: $S = 0.86$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $V = 3.5 \text{ m}^3$.

**(i) Density:**

$$\rho = S \cdot \rho_w = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(ii) Specific weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 860 \times 9.81 = 8436.6 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**(iii) Mass of 3.5 m³:**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 860 \times 3.5 = 3010 \text{ kg}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

Given: $D = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $V = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$, $S_{\text{oil}} = 0.88$, $S_{\text{glycerin}} = 1.26$.

**Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Volume flow rate:**

$$Q = A \cdot V = 0.01767 \times 2.5 = 0.04418 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(i) Mass flow rate of oil:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{oil}} = \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot Q = 880 \times 0.04418 = 38.88 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(ii) Weight flow rate of oil:**

$$\dot{W}_{\text{oil}} = \gamma_{\text{oil}} \cdot Q = 880 \times 9.81 \times 0.04418 = 381.4 \text{ N/s}$$

**New mass flow rate with glycerin (same $Q$):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{glycerin}} = 1260 \times 0.04418 = 55.67 \text{ kg/s}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** A classic mistake is using diameter in cm without converting to meters, or temperature in °C without converting to Kelvin. Always double-check unit consistency before substituting into formulas.
> 2. **Mixing up mixture formulas:** If specific gravities and *masses* are given, use the mass-based formula $S_{\text{mix}} = \sum m_i \,/\, \sum (m_i/S_i)$. If *volumes* are given, use $S_{\text{mix}} = \sum S_i V_i \,/\, \sum V_i$. Mixing these up costs **3–4 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Forgetting to state "dimensionless" for specific gravity:** A common deduction is failing to mention that $S$ has no units. One mark gone.
> 4. **Not specifying reference temperature for water:** Always write "density of water at 4 °C" — vague answers lose a mark.
> 5. **Sign and direction errors in pressure calculations:** When asked to find pressure at depth, use $P = \rho g h$ with absolute depth (not relative).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — Density Module**
>
> - **Definition:** $\rho = m/V$ — mass per unit volume; SI unit $\text{kg/m}^3$.
> - **Standard reference:** $\rho_{\text{water at 4°C}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
> - **Specific Weight:** $\gamma = \rho g$ — depends on $g$; SI unit $\text{N/m}^3$.
> - **Specific Gravity:** $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ — **dimensionless**.
> - **Specific Volume:** $v = 1/\rho$ — SI unit $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$.
> - **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho A V$.
> - **Weight flow rate:** $\dot{W} = \gamma A V$.
> - **Ideal gas density:** $\rho = P M / (R_u T)$ — always use **absolute** $P$ and $T$ (Kelvin).
> - **Mixture specific gravity (volume basis):** $S_{\text{mix}} = \sum S_i V_i / \sum V_i$.
> - **Mixture specific gravity (mass basis):** $S_{\text{mix}} = \sum m_i / \sum (m_i / S_i)$.
> - **Density is independent of $g$**; specific weight is **not**.
> - **Density of water is maximum at 4 °C** (unique anomaly of water — exam favorite!).
> - **Mercury:** $S = 13.6$ — memorize for manometer problems.
> - **Air:** $\rho \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at standard sea-level conditions.
> - **Continuum assumption** is implicit in the definition of $\rho$ as a point function.
> - **Compressibility of liquids is negligible** under ordinary conditions → $\rho = \text{constant}$ for most KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscosity** is defined as the **property of a fluid that offers resistance to deformation (shear) under the action of an applied shear stress.** It is a measure of the internal friction within a fluid, arising due to cohesive forces and molecular momentum transfer between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities.

Mathematically, viscosity quantifies the ratio of the **shear stress (τ)** to the **rate of shear strain (du/dy)** in a flowing fluid. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, viscosity forms the foundational concept of Module 1, linking kinematics to real fluid behavior, since all real fluids exhibit some degree of viscosity (unlike the idealized **inviscid** or **frictionless** fluid assumed in Euler's equations).

The two principal forms of viscosity studied are:

| Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity** | **μ** (mu) | Direct measure of internal fluid friction |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | **ν** (nu) | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Honey vs. Water**
> Imagine pouring **honey** and **water** from two identical glasses on a tilted table. Honey flows sluggishly, almost reluctantly, while water glides off quickly. The reason is **viscosity**. Honey has a high resistance to flow because its molecules cling tightly to one another (strong **cohesive forces**), while water's molecules slip past each other with much less resistance. Viscosity is, quite literally, the "thickness" or "stickiness" of a fluid, although technically the resistance to flow.

A more rigorous mechanical analogy is to imagine a **deck of cards** on a table. If you push the top card horizontally, the card below it moves a little, the next moves a tiny bit, and so on. The relative sliding between layers is analogous to the **shear** experienced by fluid layers, and viscosity is the "friction" between these layers.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key physical constants and benchmark values:
> - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** **μ ≈ 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** (or N·s/m²)
> - **Dynamic viscosity of air at 20 °C:** **μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** **ν ≈ 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - **Kinematic viscosity of air at 20 °C:** **ν ≈ 1.51 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**
> - **Glycerine (high viscosity reference):** **μ ≈ 1.5 Pa·s** at 20 °C

These values are critical for KTU numerical problems where temperature-dependent viscosity is often involved.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity

Issac Newton's pioneering hypothesis (1687) states that for a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress between two moving fluid layers is **directly proportional** to the velocity gradient normal to the direction of flow. This is one of the most frequently tested topics in the KTU Board examination.

**Logical Step-by-Step Breakdown:**

1. **Shear Stress Origin**: When fluid flows over a solid surface, the layer in contact with the surface (boundary layer) has zero velocity (no-slip condition), while the layer furthest from the surface moves with the maximum (free-stream) velocity.
2. **Velocity Gradient Formation**: This creates a continuous variation in velocity from zero at the wall to the maximum at the bulk flow, defined as the **velocity gradient** $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ (units: s⁻¹).
3. **Internal Molecular Friction**: Adjacent layers moving at different velocities exchange momentum through molecular collisions and cohesive forces, generating a tangential **shear stress** τ on each layer.
4. **Newton's Postulate**: The shear stress is linearly proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

5. **Introducing the Proportionality Constant**: The constant of proportionality is the **dynamic viscosity (μ)**.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

6. **Physical Interpretation**: A fluid with a large value of μ requires a large shear stress to maintain a given velocity gradient — it is "thick" or viscous. A small μ indicates a "thin," easily flowing fluid.

## 2.2 Classification of Fluids by Viscosity Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **Newtonian Fluids:** Fluids that obey Newton's law of viscosity (linear τ vs. du/dy relationship). Examples: water, air, gasoline, alcohol, mercury, glycerine.
>
> **Non-Newtonian Fluids:** Fluids where the relationship between τ and du/dy is non-linear or time-dependent. Examples: blood, paint, ketchup, printer ink, mud, toothpaste, polymer melts.

Non-Newtonian fluids are further classified into:
- **Bingham Plastics** (e.g., toothpaste, drilling mud) — behave as a solid until yield stress is exceeded.
- **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning)** (e.g., blood, paint) — viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate.
- **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening)** (e.g., starch suspensions, wet sand) — viscosity increases with shear rate.
- **Thixotropic** (e.g., yogurt, gel) — viscosity decreases over time under constant shear.
- **Rheopectic** (e.g., gypsum paste) — viscosity increases over time under constant shear.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** required for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 numerical and theoretical questions on Viscosity. The pipe notation has been replaced with `\vert` for LaTeX safety.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | SI Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity (defining μ) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\left( \dfrac{du}{dy} \right)}$ | Definition of dynamic viscosity | Pa·s (N·s/m²) |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity (ratio of μ to density) | m²/s |
| 4 | $[M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-1}]$ | Dimensions of dynamic viscosity μ | — |
| 5 | $[L^{2}T^{-1}]$ | Dimensions of kinematic viscosity ν | — |
| 6 | $\mu_{rel} = \dfrac{\mu_{fluid}}{\mu_{water}}$ | Relative viscosity (dimensionless) | — |
| 7 | $\mu_{poise} = \dfrac{1}{10}\,\text{Pa·s}$ | Conversion: 1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s | — |
| 8 | $\nu_{stoke} = 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ | Conversion: 1 stoke = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s | — |
| 9 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dV}{dy}$ for a flat plate | Shear stress on a plate moving with velocity V at distance y | Pa |
| 10 | $F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous force on area A with velocity gradient | N |

## 2.4 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

> [!NOTE]
> **For Liquids:** Viscosity **decreases** with increasing temperature. The molecules gain kinetic energy, weakening cohesive (intermolecular) bonds. Empirical formula (Andrade's equation):
> $$\mu = A \cdot e^{B/T}$$
> where A and B are fluid-specific constants and T is absolute temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Gases:** Viscosity **increases** with increasing temperature. Higher temperature means faster molecular motion and more frequent momentum-exchanging collisions. Sutherland's formula:
> $$\mu = \mu_{0} \left( \dfrac{T}{T_{0}} \right)^{3/2} \dfrac{T_{0} + S}{T + S}$$
> where S is Sutherland's constant (≈ 110 K for air).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic & Lubricant Design:** Viscosity governs film thickness in journal bearings, engine lubrication, and hydraulic system efficiency.
- **Pipeline Pumping Power:** Pressure drop and pump power (P = Q·Δp) depend on μ; the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta p\, r^{4}}{8 \mu L}$ for laminar flow uses μ directly.
- **Meteorology & Oceanography:** Viscosity of air and seawater influences wind boundary layers, oceanic currents, and weather modeling.
- **Bioprocess Engineering:** Viscosity of blood affects circulation; viscosity of fermentation broths impacts mixing and mass transfer.
- **Paint & Coatings Industry:** Shear-thinning behavior ensures easy brushing and drip-free film formation.
- **Petroleum Engineering:** Viscosity of crude oil governs pipeline transport and Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dimensional Analysis of Dynamic Viscosity (μ)

This derivation is **extremely high-yield** — KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive or verify the dimensions of μ and ν.

**Given:** Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$

**Step 1: Recall the dimensions of each term**

The shear stress τ has dimensions of pressure (force per area):

$$[\tau] = \frac{[F]}{[A]} = \frac{MLT^{-2}}{L^{2}} = M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-2}$$

The velocity gradient $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ has dimensions of inverse time:

$$\left[ \frac{du}{dy} \right] = \frac{LT^{-1}}{L} = T^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange Newton's law to solve for μ**

$$\mu = \tau \cdot \frac{dy}{du} = \frac{\tau}{\left( \dfrac{du}{dy} \right)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the dimensions**

Substituting the dimensions identified in Step 1:

$$[\mu] = \frac{[\tau]}{\left[ \dfrac{du}{dy} \right]}$$

$$[\mu] = \frac{M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-2}}{T^{-1}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the powers of T**

When dividing by $T^{-1}$, the exponent is subtracted: $-2 - (-1) = -1$:

$$[\mu] = M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-2-(-1)} = M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Final Result**

$$\boxed{[\mu] = [M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-1}]}$$

This matches the dimensional formula for dynamic viscosity in the SI system.

**Step 6: SI Units of μ**

Since $[\mu] = M L^{-1} T^{-1}$, in SI units this becomes:

$$[\mu] = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{m}^{2}} = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dimensional Analysis of Kinematic Viscosity (ν)

**Given:** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$

**Step 1: Dimensions of dynamic viscosity μ**

From Derivation 1 above:

$$[\mu] = M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Dimensions of fluid density ρ**

Density is mass per unit volume:

$$[\rho] = \frac{[M]}{[L^{3}]} = M^{1}L^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the definition of ν**

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]}$$

**Step 4: Perform dimensional division**

Substitute the dimensional expressions for μ and ρ:

$$[\nu] = \frac{M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-1}}{M^{1}L^{-3}}$$

**Step 5: Apply the laws of exponents**

The M terms cancel: $M^{1-1} = M^{0} = 1$. For L: $-1 - (-3) = -1 + 3 = 2$. For T: $-1 - 0 = -1$. Therefore:

$$[\nu] = M^{0}L^{2}T^{-1}$$

**Step 6: Final Result**

$$\boxed{[\nu] = [L^{2}T^{-1}]}$$

The SI units are $\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$, and the CGS unit is the **stoke** (1 stoke = $10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Newton's Law from Shear Force on a Moving Plate (KTU Classic Problem)

A flat plate of area **A** is separated from a fixed plate by a fluid film of thickness **y**. The plate is moved with a constant velocity **V** by a force **F**. Derive Newton's law of viscosity.

**Step 1: Define the experimental setup**

Consider a thin fluid film of uniform thickness **y** between a fixed lower plate and an upper plate of area **A** that is pulled horizontally with velocity **V**.

**Step 2: Apply the no-slip boundary condition**

The fluid layer touching the moving plate has velocity V, and the layer touching the fixed plate has velocity 0. This creates a linear velocity distribution.

**Step 3: Express the velocity gradient**

Since the velocity changes uniformly from 0 to V over the thickness y, the velocity gradient is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{V - 0}{y} = \frac{V}{y}$$

**Step 4: Empirical observation**

Experiments (conducted by Newton) show that the force F required to move the plate is:

(i) Directly proportional to the area A: $F \propto A$

(ii) Directly proportional to the velocity V: $F \propto V$

(iii) Inversely proportional to the film thickness y: $F \propto \dfrac{1}{y}$

Combining all three proportionalities:

$$F \propto A \cdot \frac{V}{y}$$

**Step 5: Introduce the constant of proportionality**

$$F = \mu A \cdot \frac{V}{y}$$

The proportionality constant μ is the dynamic viscosity.

**Step 6: Express the shear stress**

Shear stress is force per unit area:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \mu \cdot \frac{V}{y}$$

Recognizing that $\dfrac{V}{y} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ (the velocity gradient):

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}}$$

This is Newton's law of viscosity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A fluid of dynamic viscosity 0.9 N·s/m² flows between two parallel plates 1.2 m long and 0.8 m wide, separated by a gap of 8 mm. The upper plate moves at 1.5 m/s while the lower plate is stationary. Calculate: (a) the shear stress on the plates, (b) the viscous force resisting motion, and (c) the velocity gradient in the fluid.

**Given Data:**

$$\mu = 0.9\,\text{N·s/m}^{2}, \quad L = 1.2\,\text{m}, \quad B = 0.8\,\text{m}, \quad y = 8\,\text{mm} = 0.008\,\text{m}, \quad V = 1.5\,\text{m/s}$$

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Shear stress on the plates**

Using Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

The velocity gradient is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{V}{y} = \frac{1.5}{0.008} = 187.5\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

Substituting into Newton's law:

$$\tau = 0.9 \times 187.5 = 168.75\,\text{N/m}^{2}$$

**Part (b): Viscous force resisting motion**

The plate area is:

$$A = L \times B = 1.2 \times 0.8 = 0.96\,\text{m}^{2}$$

The viscous (shear) force is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 168.75 \times 0.96 = 162.0\,\text{N}$$

**Part (c): Velocity gradient**

From Step (a):

$$\frac{du}{dy} = 187.5\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Final Answers (boxed for KTU exam):**

$$\boxed{\tau = 168.75\,\text{N/m}^{2}, \quad F = 162.0\,\text{N}, \quad \frac{du}{dy} = 187.5\,\text{s}^{-1}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Viscosity Concept Map

The following Mermaid diagram maps the hierarchical structure of the viscosity topic as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus. ## 4.2 Schematic Process Flow: Newton's Law Derivation Pathway

This flow chart visualizes the logical sequence of how Newton's law of viscosity emerges from first principles — useful for KTU short-answer and derivation questions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[START FIXED AND MOVING PLATE] --> S2[FILL FLUID FILM THICKNESS Y]
    S2 --> S3[APPLY NO SLIP CONDITION]
    S3 --> S4[OBSERVE LINEAR VELOCITY PROFILE]
    S4 --> S5[DEFINE VELOCITY GRADIENT V BY Y]
    S5 --> S6[FORCE F PROPORTIONAL A V AND INVERSE Y]
    S6 --> S7[INTRODUCE CONSTANT MU]
    S7 --> S8[TAU EQUALS MU DU BY DY]
    S8 --> S9[END NEWTONS LAW VERIFIED]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Schematic: Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[FLUID UNDER SHEAR] --> N2{OBEYS NEWTONS LAW LINEAR}
    N2 --> N3[YES NEWTONIAN FLUID]
    N2 --> N4[NO NON NEWTONIAN FLUID]
    N3 --> N5[EXAMPLES WATER AIR GLYCERINE]
    N4 --> N6{STRESS STRAIN RELATION}
    N6 --> N7[PSEUDOPLASTIC BLOOD PAINT]
    N6 --> N8[DILATANT STARCH SLURRY]
    N6 --> N9[BINGHAM TOOTHPASTE]
    N6 --> N10[THIXOTROPIC YOGURT]
    N6 --> N11[RHEOPECTIC GYPSUM PASTE]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Viscosity in Engineering Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    E1[ENGINEERING DOMAIN] --> E2[MECHANICAL LUBRICATION]
    E1 --> E3[CIVIL PIPELINE HYDRAULICS]
    E1 --> E4[CHEMICAL REACTOR MIXING]
    E1 --> E5[BIOMEDICAL BLOOD FLOW]
    E1 --> E6[AEROSPACE BOUNDARY LAYER]

    E2 --> F1[JOURNAL BEARING FILM]
    E3 --> F2[HAGEN POISEUILLE LAW]
    E4 --> F3[REYNOLDS NUMBER CHECK]
    E5 --> F4[APPARENT VISCOSITY MODEL]
    F2 --> G1[PRESSURE DROP DELTA P]
    F3 --> G2[FLOW REGIME LAMINAR OR TURBULENT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the following terms with their SI units and dimensions:**
(a) Dynamic viscosity
(b) Kinematic viscosity

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Dynamic Viscosity (μ):**
Dynamic viscosity is defined as the tangential shear stress required to produce a unit rate of shear deformation in a fluid. It is the proportionality constant in Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$.

- **SI Unit:** $\text{N·s/m}^{2}$ or $\text{Pa·s}$
- **CGS Unit:** poise (1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s)
- **Dimensions:** $[M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-1}]$

**(b) Kinematic Viscosity (ν):**
Kinematic viscosity is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid: $\nu = \mu / \rho$. The term "kinematic" arises because momentum diffusivity in a fluid is governed primarily by this quantity, with no external force units involved.

- **SI Unit:** $\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$
- **CGS Unit:** stoke (1 stoke = $10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$)
- **Dimensions:** $[L^{2}T^{-1}]$

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State Newton's law of viscosity. Explain why it is applicable only to Newtonian fluids.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain, i.e., the velocity gradient $(du/dy)$ normal to the direction of flow.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where μ is the dynamic viscosity of the fluid (a constant for a given fluid and temperature).

**Applicability to Newtonian Fluids:**
Newton's law of viscosity is applicable **only to Newtonian fluids** because, for such fluids, the relationship between τ and $du/dy$ is **linear**, and μ remains **constant** (independent of the shear rate). For **non-Newtonian fluids** (such as blood, paint, or polymer solutions), the viscosity is a function of the shear rate, so μ is not constant and the simple linear relationship breaks down. For non-Newtonian fluids, the quantity $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ is called the **apparent viscosity** and varies with shear rate.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Derive Newton's law of viscosity. Explain the concept of a velocity gradient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat plate of area $1.0\,\text{m}^{2}$ is pulled with a velocity of $0.5\,\text{m/s}$ through a fluid film of thickness $5\,\text{mm}$ on a fixed plate. The dynamic viscosity of the fluid is $0.85\,\text{Pa·s}$. Calculate: (i) the shear stress, (ii) the viscous drag force, and (iii) the kinematic viscosity if the fluid density is $920\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

**Step 1: Experimental Setup**
Consider a fluid film of uniform thickness *y* sandwiched between a stationary lower plate and a movable upper plate of area *A*. A tangential force *F* is applied to the upper plate, moving it with a uniform velocity *V*.

**Step 2: No-Slip Boundary Condition**
The fluid layer in contact with the moving plate acquires velocity *V* (no-slip), while the layer at the fixed plate has zero velocity. A linear velocity profile is established across the gap.

**Step 3: Velocity Gradient**
The rate of change of velocity with distance from the fixed plate is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{V - 0}{y - 0} = \frac{V}{y}$$

This is the **velocity gradient** (units: s⁻¹), representing the rate of shear deformation.

**Step 4: Experimental Observations**

The force F is observed to be:
- Directly proportional to the plate area A: $F \propto A$
- Directly proportional to the velocity V: $F \propto V$
- Inversely proportional to the gap thickness y: $F \propto \dfrac{1}{y}$

Combining:

$$F \propto A \cdot \frac{V}{y}$$

**Step 5: Introduce Proportionality Constant**
A constant of proportionality μ (dynamic viscosity) is introduced:

$$F = \mu A \frac{V}{y}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Shear Stress Equation**
Dividing both sides by the area A and noting $V/y = du/dy$:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. The constant μ is the dynamic viscosity, which physically represents the fluid's internal resistance to flow.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$A = 1.0\,\text{m}^{2}, \quad V = 0.5\,\text{m/s}, \quad y = 5\,\text{mm} = 0.005\,\text{m}, \quad \mu = 0.85\,\text{Pa·s}, \quad \rho = 920\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$$

**(i) Shear Stress:**

Velocity gradient:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{V}{y} = \frac{0.5}{0.005} = 100\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

Shear stress:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.85 \times 100 = 85\,\text{Pa}$$

**(ii) Viscous Drag Force:**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 85 \times 1.0 = 85\,\text{N}$$

**(iii) Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.85}{920} = 9.239 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\tau = 85\,\text{Pa}, \quad F = 85\,\text{N}, \quad \nu = 9.24 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Distinguish between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with suitable examples and rheological diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of diameter $120\,\text{mm}$ rotates at $600\,\text{rpm}$ inside a bearing of internal diameter $120.5\,\text{mm}$ filled with oil of dynamic viscosity $0.18\,\text{N·s/m}^{2}$. Calculate: (i) the linear velocity of the shaft, (ii) the velocity gradient in the oil film, and (iii) the viscous torque required to overcome friction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids:**

| Property | Newtonian Fluids | Non-Newtonian Fluids |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Obey Newton's law of viscosity ($\tau = \mu\, du/dy$) | Do not obey Newton's law of viscosity |
| Viscosity Behavior | μ is constant (independent of shear rate) | μ varies with shear rate (apparent viscosity) |
| Stress-Strain Plot | A straight line through origin | Curved or non-linear plot |
| Examples | Water, air, gasoline, glycerine, alcohol, mercury | Blood, paint, ketchup, toothpaste, drilling mud, starch slurry |

**Rheological Diagrams (Conceptual Description):**
- **Newtonian:** A straight line through the origin on a τ vs. $du/dy$ plot, with slope = μ.
- **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning):** Curve concave-down, slope decreases (e.g., blood, paint).
- **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening):** Curve concave-up, slope increases (e.g., starch suspension).
- **Bingham Plastic:** Horizontal line at a yield stress τ₀ before fluid begins to flow (e.g., toothpaste, drilling mud).
- **Thixotropic/Rheopectic:** Time-dependent curves showing decreasing or increasing viscosity with time at constant shear.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$d = 120\,\text{mm} = 0.12\,\text{m}, \quad N = 600\,\text{rpm}, \quad D = 120.5\,\text{mm} = 0.1205\,\text{m}, \quad \mu = 0.18\,\text{N·s/m}^{2}$$

**(i) Linear Velocity of the Shaft:**

First, compute the angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 600}{60} = 20\pi \approx 62.832\,\text{rad/s}$$

The linear (tangential) velocity at the shaft surface is:

$$V = \omega \times r = \omega \times \frac{d}{2} = 62.832 \times 0.06 = 3.7699\,\text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Velocity Gradient in the Oil Film:**

The thickness of the oil film is:

$$y = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{0.1205 - 0.1200}{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$$

The velocity gradient is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{V}{y} = \frac{3.7699}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 15{,}079.6\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Viscous Torque:**

The shear stress on the shaft surface is:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.18 \times 15{,}079.6 = 2714.33\,\text{N/m}^{2}$$

The shear force on the shaft surface is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A_{shaft} = \tau \cdot (\pi d L)$$

For unit length (L = 1 m):

$$F = 2714.33 \times \pi \times 0.12 \times 1 = 1022.84\,\text{N/m}$$

The torque per unit length is:

$$T = F \times r = 1022.84 \times 0.06 = 61.37\,\text{N·m/m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{V \approx 3.77\,\text{m/s}, \quad \frac{du}{dy} \approx 15{,}080\,\text{s}^{-1}, \quad T \approx 61.37\,\text{N·m/m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Viscosity Problems (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** KTU examiners commonly test whether students correctly convert mm to m (e.g., 8 mm = 0.008 m, not 8). Forgetting this single conversion can lose **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing dynamic and kinematic viscosity:** Students frequently mix up μ and ν. Always check units: μ is in Pa·s (N·s/m²), ν is in m²/s.
> 3. **Wrong dimensions of kinematic viscosity:** A common mistake is writing $[\nu] = [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$ (which is μ's dimension). The correct dimension is $[L^{2}T^{-1}]$ — it has **no mass term**.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s:** Angular velocity in viscous-torque problems must be in rad/s. Using 600 directly without the $2\pi/60$ factor loses the full sub-part.
> 5. **Plotting errors for non-Newtonian fluids:** In 7-mark theory questions, students often write definitions but fail to draw the **rheological diagram** (τ vs. du/dy curve). Examiners expect a sketch — it is worth **2–3 marks** on its own.
> 6. **Sign convention for shear stress:** Always indicate the direction (e.g., the fixed plate experiences a drag force opposite to the motion of the moving plate). Examiners reward clarity of physical interpretation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Viscosity: High-Density Revision Summary**
>
> - **Definition:** Viscosity = internal fluid resistance to shear deformation.
> - **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$ — single most important equation.
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** Units = Pa·s; Dimensions = $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$.
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** $\nu = \mu / \rho$; Units = m²/s; Dimensions = $[L^{2} T^{-1}]$.
> - **Newtonian Fluids:** Constant μ; examples: water, air, glycerine.
> - **Non-Newtonian Fluids:** Variable μ; examples: blood, paint, ketchup, toothpaste.
> - **No-Slip Condition:** Fluid in contact with a solid surface has the same velocity as the surface.
> - **Velocity Gradient:** $du/dy$ in s⁻¹ — rate of shear strain.
> - **Temperature Effect:** μ **decreases** with T for liquids; μ **increases** with T for gases.
> - **Unit Conversions to Memorize:**
>   * 1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s
>   * 1 stoke = $10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$
>   * 1 centipoise = $10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$ (water at 20°C ≈ 1 cP)
> - **Rheological Diagram:** Always draw a straight line (Newtonian) vs. curved line (Non-Newtonian) for full marks.
> - **Engineering Importance:** Lubrication, pipeline flow, blood flow, weather, mixing.
> - **Key Common Formulas:** $F = \mu A (du/dy)$, $\nu = \mu / \rho$, $T = \tau \cdot A \cdot r$ (for journal bearings).
> - **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Most Module 1 questions test (a) definitions + units + dimensions (3 marks), and (b) numerical problems involving shear stress, force, or kinematic viscosity (7 marks). Practice dimensional derivations thoroughly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Viscosity on Temperature and Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Viscosity on Temperature and Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Viscosity ($\mu$)** is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion of its layers. It arises due to internal molecular friction (cohesion and momentum transfer) between fluid layers moving at different velocities.

Mathematically, by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain), measured in s⁻¹

The **kinematic viscosity** is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

with units of **m²/s** (also expressed in **Stokes (St)** where 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s, and **centistokes (cSt)** = 10⁻⁶ m²/s).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine sliding a heavy cardboard box across a floor versus sliding it across a tray of honey. The honey "sticks" to the box and resists motion more than the dry floor. That "stickiness" is viscosity.

Now think about how this stickiness changes:
- **Honey heated** → becomes runnier → viscosity **decreases**.
- **Air heated** → the molecules move faster and exchange more momentum between layers → viscosity **increases**.

This opposite behavior between liquids and gases is the central idea of this topic. **Pressure** acts like squeezing the fluid — generally thickens liquids (liquids are nearly incompressible, but pressurizing them forces molecules closer) while leaving gases nearly unaffected at moderate pressures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Liquids (e.g., water, oil, honey):** Viscosity $\mu$ **decreases** with temperature; $\mu$ **increases** slightly with pressure.
> - **Gases (e.g., air, steam, nitrogen):** Viscosity $\mu$ **increases** with temperature; $\mu$ is **largely independent** of pressure at moderate pressures.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Values (Bold Constants)

| Fluid | Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s) | Kinematic Viscosity $\nu$ (m²/s) |
|---|---|---|
| Water at 20 °C | **1.002 × 10⁻³** | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶** |
| Air at 20 °C | **1.81 × 10⁻⁵** | **1.51 × 10⁻⁵** |
| Mercury at 20 °C | **1.55 × 10⁻³** | **1.14 × 10⁻⁷** |
| SAE 30 Oil at 20 °C | **0.29** | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Liquids and Gases Behave Differently

The mechanism of viscosity in liquids vs. gases is fundamentally different:

**For Liquids:**
- Viscosity is caused mainly by **cohesive (intermolecular) forces** between closely packed molecules.
- As temperature rises, molecules gain energy → cohesive bonds weaken → layers slide more easily → **$\mu$ decreases with $T$**.

**For Gases:**
- Viscosity is caused by **momentum transfer** between molecules moving randomly between layers (Kinetic Theory of Gases).
- As temperature rises, molecular random motion increases → more momentum is transferred across layers → **$\mu$ increases with $T$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this contrast** — it is the most frequently asked Part A question in KTU ESE.

## 2.2 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity of Liquids

### (a) Empirical Linear Relation (approximate)

$$\mu_T = \mu_0 \left[1 - \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

where $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of viscosity (per °C), and subscript 0 refers to a reference state.

### (b) More Accurate Exponential Form

$$\mu_T = \mu_0 \cdot 10^{\, A\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where:
- $T$ and $T_0$ are in **Kelvin**
- $A$ = constant depending on the liquid

### (c) Standard Lubricating-Oil Equation (KTU Favorite)

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\log_{10}\mu\right) = A - B \log_{10} T$$

This is a **double-logarithmic relation** used widely in petroleum/lubrication engineering (ASTM Chart).

## 2.3 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity of Gases

### Sutherland's Formula (Most Important)

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

where $S$ = **Sutherland's constant** (for air, $S \approx 110.4$ K).

### Simplified Power Law (for moderate temperature ranges)

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{n}, \quad n \approx 0.7 \text{ for air}$$

## 2.4 Effect of Pressure on Viscosity

### For Liquids
$$\mu_p = \mu_0 \, e^{\beta P}$$

where $\beta$ = compressibility-viscosity coefficient. For most lubricating oils, viscosity roughly **doubles for every 3000–4000 kPa** of pressure increase.

### For Gases
- Below ~1 MPa (≈ 10 atm): viscosity is **essentially independent of pressure** (ideal-gas behavior).
- Above ~1 MPa: viscosity **increases with pressure** according to:

$$\mu_p = \mu_1 \left[1 + \frac{C}{(T/T_c)^{3}}\left(\frac{P}{P_c}\right)\right]$$

where $P_c$, $T_c$ are the **critical pressure** and **critical temperature**, and $C$ is a constant.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions in KTU ESE Module 1.**

| # | Relation | Applies To | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu\,(du/dy)$ | All Newtonian fluids | Pa, Pa·s, s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | All fluids | m²/s |
| 3 | $\mu_T = \mu_0 [1 - \alpha(T - T_0)]$ | Liquids (approximate) | Pa·s, °C⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\mu_T = \mu_0 \cdot 10^{A(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Liquids (accurate) | Pa·s, K |
| 5 | $\mu/\mu_0 = (T/T_0)^{3/2}(T_0 + S)/(T + S)$ | Gases (Sutherland) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\mu/\mu_0 = (T/T_0)^{n},\; n \approx 0.7$ | Gases (power law) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\mu_p = \mu_0 e^{\beta P}$ | Liquids (pressure) | Pa·s, Pa⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\log_{10}(\log_{10}\mu) = A - B\log_{10}T$ | Lubricating oils | — |
| 9 | $\mu_p = \mu_1[1 + C(P/P_c)/(T/T_c)^{3}]$ | Gases (high pressure) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> In all temperature formulas, **always use absolute temperature (Kelvin)**. Using °C is a common KTU valuation pitfall.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic system design:** Viscosity changes with temperature affect pump efficiency, bearing lubrication, and pipe-flow pressure drops.
- **Aerospace / HVAC:** Gas viscosity variation with temperature is critical for re-entry thermal analysis, jet-engine design, and ventilation duct sizing.
- **Engine lubrication:** Oil viscosity index (VI) and ASTM viscosity-temperature charts dictate oil grade selection (SAE 10W, 20W, 30…).
- **Polymer processing & petroleum refining:** Viscosity-temperature-pressure relationships govern pipeline transport and injection molding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sutherland's Formula from Kinetic Theory of Gases

Starting from the **Chapman-Enskog kinetic theory**, the dynamic viscosity of an ideal gas is:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{c} \lambda$$

where $\bar{c}$ = mean molecular speed, $\lambda$ = mean free path. Now:

$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}, \quad \lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 P}$$

Substituting $\rho = m n$ and combining:

$$\mu = \frac{2}{3\pi^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{m k_B T}}{\pi d^2}$$

This gives $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$ — accurate only at moderate temperatures. Sutherland's correction replaces the constant molecular diameter assumption with a temperature-dependent effective diameter:

$$d_{\text{eff}} = d\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{T}\right)^{-1/2}$$

where $S$ = Sutherland's constant. Substituting back and simplifying:

$$\mu = C \cdot \frac{T^{3/2}}{T + S}$$

Dividing two such states $(T_0, \mu_0)$ and $(T, \mu)$:

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Sutherland's Formula

**Problem:** The viscosity of air at 27 °C is $1.846 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Using Sutherland's formula with $S = 110.4$ K, find the viscosity at 127 °C.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:
$$T_0 = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$$
$$T = 127 + 273 = 400 \text{ K}$$

Step 2 — Apply Sutherland's equation:
$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the temperature ratio:
$$\left(\frac{400}{300}\right)^{3/2} = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{3/2} = 1.5396$$

Step 4 — Evaluate the Sutherland correction:
$$\frac{300 + 110.4}{400 + 110.4} = \frac{410.4}{510.4} = 0.8041$$

Step 5 — Combine the factors:
$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = 1.5396 \times 0.8041 = 1.2380$$

Step 6 — Solve for $\mu$:
