$$\mu = 1.2380 \times 1.846 \times 10^{-5} = 2.286 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Viscosity of air at 127 °C $\approx 2.29 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s — confirming that **gas viscosity increases with temperature**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Liquid Viscosity vs. Temperature

**Problem:** A lubricant has viscosity $0.10$ Pa·s at 40 °C and $0.02$ Pa·s at 100 °C. Find its viscosity at 80 °C using the exponential form. Take $A = 2500$ K (assumed).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Set up the exponential form at $T_0 = 313$ K:
$$\mu_T = \mu_0 \cdot 10^{A(1/T - 1/T_0)}$$

Step 2 — Verify $A$ using $T = 373$ K, $\mu_T = 0.02$ Pa·s, $\mu_0 = 0.10$ Pa·s:
$$0.02 = 0.10 \times 10^{A(1/373 - 1/313)}$$
$$0.2 = 10^{A(0.002681 - 0.003195)}$$
$$0.2 = 10^{-0.000514\,A}$$
$$\log_{10}(0.2) = -0.000514\,A$$
$$-0.6990 = -0.000514\,A \quad\Rightarrow\quad A = 1359.9 \text{ K}$$

Step 3 — Use this $A$ to find $\mu$ at $T = 353$ K (80 °C):
$$\mu_{353} = 0.10 \times 10^{1359.9(1/353 - 1/313)}$$
$$\mu_{353} = 0.10 \times 10^{1359.9(0.002833 - 0.003195)}$$
$$\mu_{353} = 0.10 \times 10^{1359.9 \times (-0.000362)}$$
$$\mu_{353} = 0.10 \times 10^{-0.4924}$$
$$\mu_{353} = 0.10 \times 0.3215 = 0.0322 \text{ Pa·s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\mu_{80°C} \approx 0.0322$ Pa·s. Notice viscosity **decreases** as temperature rises — consistent with liquid behavior.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Liquids | Gases |
|---|---|---|
| Cause of viscosity | Cohesive forces | Momentum transfer |
| $\mu$ vs. $T$ | Decreases | Increases |
| $\mu$ vs. $P$ (moderate) | Increases exponentially | Nearly constant |
| $\mu$ vs. $P$ (high) | Strongly increases | Increases above $P_c$ |
| Density vs. $T$ | Decreases slightly | Decreases (∝ 1/T) |
| Kinematic $\nu = \mu/\rho$ vs. $T$ | Decreases strongly | Increases strongly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Viscosity Behaviour

The following Mermaid **block-level flow diagram** maps the decision pathways governing how viscosity responds to changes in temperature and pressure, distinguishing liquid from gas behavior — this replaces a physical drawing with a logically exhaustive process map.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fluid Property mu] --> B{Fluid Phase?}
    B -- Liquid --> C[Cohesive forces dominate]
    B -- Gas --> D[Momentum transfer dominates]
    C --> E[Effect of Temperature: mu DECREASES]
    C --> F[Effect of Pressure: mu INCREASES]
    D --> G[Effect of Temperature: mu INCREASES]
    D --> H{Moderate P less than 1 MPa?}
    H -- Yes --> I[mu INDEPENDENT of P]
    H -- No --> J[mu INCREASES with P]
    E --> K[Use Exponential Law: mu = mu0 exp A 1 over T minus 1 over T0]
    F --> L[Use mu_p = mu0 exp beta P]
    G --> M[Use Sutherland: mu over mu0 = T over T0 power 3 over 2 times T0 plus S over T plus S]
    I --> N[No pressure correction needed]
    J --> O[Use Enskog or reduced-form equation]
    K --> P[Final: mu_T in Pa s]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Viscosity Trend Curves (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TDEP[Temperature Dependence]
        direction LR
        T1[Liquid Curve: mu decreases as T increases] --- T2[Gas Curve: mu increases as T increases]
    end
    subgraph PDEP[Pressure Dependence]
        direction LR
        P1[Liquid Curve: mu increases with P] --- P2[Gas Curve: mu nearly flat below 1 MPa then rises]
    end
    TDEP --> OUT[Engineering Output: Adjust pump, pipe, and bearing design parameters]
    PDEP --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Problem Solving in KTU Exams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Given Problem on Viscosity] --> R{Fluid type mentioned?}
    R -- Yes, liquid --> S[Use exponential or ASTM double-log relation]
    R -- Yes, gas --> T[Use Sutherland or power-law]
    R -- Not given --> U[Check context: hydraulic system implies liquid; HVAC implies gas]
    S --> V[Convert all T to Kelvin]
    T --> V
    V --> W[Substitute into chosen formula]
    W --> X[Solve for unknown mu or T]
    X --> Y[Report answer in Pa s or m squared per s]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **"With a neat sketch, explain the effect of temperature on the viscosity of liquids and gases."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

- **Liquids:** Viscosity **decreases** as temperature rises because the cohesive intermolecular forces weaken with increased thermal energy, allowing molecules to slide past one another more easily. The relation is often given by:

$$\mu_T = \mu_0 \cdot 10^{A(1/T - 1/T_0)}$$

- **Gases:** Viscosity **increases** with temperature because increased thermal motion enhances momentum transfer between adjacent layers. This is given by **Sutherland's formula**:

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

- A typical viscosity-vs-temperature sketch would show a falling curve for liquids and a rising curve for gases intersecting at some temperature.

*(Examiner's tip: A simple hand-drawn cross-over sketch earns full marks.)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> **"State Sutherland's equation for viscosity of gases. What is Sutherland's constant?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Sutherland's equation relates the dynamic viscosity of a gas at temperature $T$ to a reference value at $T_0$:

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

**Sutherland's constant $S$** is an empirical temperature constant specific to each gas. For **air**, $S = 110.4$ K. Physically, it accounts for the temperature dependence of the effective molecular collision diameter. The constant is determined by fitting experimental viscosity–temperature data.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Failing to convert °C to Kelvin** is the #1 mark-losing error. *Always* use $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
> - Students often confuse whether the temperature exponent in Sutherland's formula is applied to $(T/T_0)$ or to $(T_0 + S)/(T + S)$ — re-read the formula carefully.
> - For the exponential form of liquid viscosity, **forgetting to take the base-10 logarithm** when rearranging is a frequent mistake.
> - Never write the answer without units. Valuation key explicitly allocates a **½-mark deduction** for missing units.
> - In two-part questions, do **not** skip the second part even if the first is hard — partial marking always rewards attempts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** Derive Sutherland's formula for the viscosity of a gas. State clearly the significance of Sutherland's constant. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The viscosity of air at 20 °C is $1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Using Sutherland's formula, calculate the viscosity of air at 200 °C. Take $S = 110.4$ K. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Start from kinetic theory expression:**

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{c} \lambda$$

**Step 2 — Substitute expressions for mean molecular speed and mean free path:**

$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}, \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,n \sigma}, \quad n = \frac{P}{k_B T}$$

where $\sigma = \pi d^2$ is the collision cross-section.

**Step 3 — Combine to obtain:**

$$\mu \propto \frac{\sqrt{m k_B T}}{d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce Sutherland's effective diameter correction:**

$$d_{\text{eff}} = \frac{d}{\sqrt{1 + S/T}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\mu = \frac{A \sqrt{T}}{1 + S/T} = A \cdot \frac{T^{3/2}}{T + S}$$

**Step 6 — Form the ratio at two temperatures to eliminate $A$:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}}$$

**Significance of $S$:** It is a gas-specific constant that accounts for the increase in effective molecular collision diameter with temperature. For air, $S = 110.4$ K.

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**
$$T_0 = 20 + 273 = 293 \text{ K}$$
$$T = 200 + 273 = 473 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(T/T_0)^{3/2}$:**
$$\left(\frac{473}{293}\right)^{3/2} = (1.6143)^{3/2} = 2.072$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Sutherland correction factor:**
$$\frac{T_0 + S}{T + S} = \frac{293 + 110.4}{473 + 110.4} = \frac{403.4}{583.4} = 0.6915$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**
$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = 2.072 \times 0.6915 = 1.4327$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\mu$:**
$$\mu = 1.4327 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} = 2.593 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\mu_{200°C} \approx 2.59 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the effect of pressure on the viscosity of liquids and gases. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A lubricating oil has viscosity $0.085$ kg/(m·s) at 40 °C. Its viscosity at 100 °C is $0.011$ kg/(m·s). Estimate its viscosity at 70 °C using the exponential form. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Liquids:**
- Viscosity of liquids **increases** with pressure.
- As pressure rises, molecules are forced closer together → cohesive forces strengthen → resistance to flow increases.
- Empirical relation: $\mu_p = \mu_0 \, e^{\beta P}$, where $\beta$ depends on the liquid.
- For most lubricating oils, viscosity **roughly doubles** for every 3000–4000 kPa rise in pressure.
- Water is an exception: at very high pressures water's viscosity actually decreases slightly.

**Gases:**
- At **moderate pressures** (below ~1 MPa), gas viscosity is **essentially independent of pressure** (verified by kinetic theory: $\mu$ depends only on $\sqrt{T}$).
- At **high pressures** (comparable to critical pressure $P_c$), gas molecules are squeezed closer, momentum transfer increases, and viscosity rises.
- The Enskog-type equation models this:

$$\mu_p = \mu_1 \left[1 + C \cdot \frac{P/P_c}{(T/T_c)^{3}}\right]$$

**Engineering Implication:** Pump and turbine design must account for pressure-induced viscosity changes in liquids; high-pressure gas pipelines require careful Reynolds-number analysis.

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Set up the exponential form:**
$$\mu_T = \mu_0 \cdot 10^{A(1/T - 1/T_0)}$$

Take reference $T_0 = 40 + 273 = 313$ K, $\mu_0 = 0.085$ Pa·s.

**Step 2 — Determine $A$ from the second data point:**

Given $T = 373$ K, $\mu_T = 0.011$ Pa·s:
$$0.011 = 0.085 \times 10^{A(1/373 - 1/313)}$$
$$0.1294 = 10^{A(0.002681 - 0.003195)}$$
$$0.1294 = 10^{-0.000514\,A}$$
$$\log_{10}(0.1294) = -0.000514\,A$$
$$-0.8882 = -0.000514\,A$$
$$A = 1728 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Find viscosity at $T = 70 + 273 = 343$ K:**
$$\mu_{343} = 0.085 \times 10^{1728(1/343 - 1/313)}$$
$$\mu_{343} = 0.085 \times 10^{1728(0.002915 - 0.003195)}$$
$$\mu_{343} = 0.085 \times 10^{1728 \times (-0.000280)}$$
$$\mu_{343} = 0.085 \times 10^{-0.4838}$$
$$\mu_{343} = 0.085 \times 0.3281 = 0.0279 \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\mu_{70°C} \approx 0.028$ Pa·s.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Viscosity** is resistance to flow, with two forms: **dynamic ($\mu$, Pa·s)** and **kinematic ($\nu = \mu/\rho$, m²/s)**.
- **Liquids:** $\mu \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$ (cohesion weakens); $\mu \uparrow$ as $P \uparrow$.
- **Gases:** $\mu \uparrow$ as $T \uparrow$ (momentum transfer increases); $\mu \approx$ constant for $P < 1$ MPa.
- **Newton's law of viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$.
- **Sutherland's formula (gases):** $\mu/\mu_0 = (T/T_0)^{3/2} \cdot (T_0 + S)/(T + S)$; for air, $S = 110.4$ K.
- **Power-law approximation (gases):** $\mu/\mu_0 = (T/T_0)^n$, with $n \approx 0.7$ for air.
- **Exponential form (liquids):** $\mu_T = \mu_0 \cdot 10^{A(1/T - 1/T_0)}$; remember $A$ depends on the liquid.
- **Pressure effect (liquids):** $\mu_p = \mu_0 \, e^{\beta P}$; viscosity can double per ~3500 kPa in oils.
- **High-pressure gas correction:** $\mu_p = \mu_1 [1 + C \cdot (P/P_c)/(T/T_c)^{3}]$.
- **ASTM double-log relation (oils):** $\log_{10}(\log_{10}\mu) = A - B \log_{10} T$.
- **Always convert °C → K** in any temperature-viscosity formula.
- **Always state units** (Pa·s for $\mu$, m²/s for $\nu$) — losing units costs marks.
- **Critical temperatures/pressures** ($T_c$, $P_c$) are needed only for high-pressure gas viscosity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specific Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Specific Volume (v)** is a fundamental intensive thermodynamic property of a fluid (and any substance) defined as the **volume occupied per unit mass of the substance**. It quantifies how much space a fixed amount of matter takes up.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$v = \frac{V}{m}$$

Where:
- $v$ = Specific Volume of the substance
- $V$ = Total Volume occupied by the substance (measured in $m^3$ or $cm^3$)
- $m$ = Total Mass of the substance (measured in $kg$ or $g$)

The standard SI unit of Specific Volume is **cubic meters per kilogram ($m^3/kg$)**, while in CGS it is **$cm^3/g$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Specific Volume is an **intensive property** — its value does not depend on the size (mass) of the system. If you take a drop of water or a lake of water, the specific volume of water at that temperature and pressure remains the same. This distinguishes it from *extensive* properties like Volume (V) and Mass (m).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine packing identical spheres (say, marbles) into boxes.
- If the marbles are very **dense** (heavy for their size), only a few fit before the box becomes too heavy to lift.
- If the marbles are very **light** (low density), more volume is taken up per unit mass.

**Specific Volume tells you exactly that inverse story:** *How much volume does 1 kg of this material claim?* A material with a *high* specific volume is "fluffy" or "spacious per kilogram" (e.g., steam, aerogel), whereas a material with a *low* specific volume is "compact per kilogram" (e.g., steel, mercury).

**Real-world imagery:**
- **1 kg of Steel** occupies roughly $0.000127 \, m^3$ (about the size of a small matchbox) — *very low specific volume*.
- **1 kg of Steam at atmospheric pressure** occupies about $1.673 \, m^3$ (roughly the volume of a small room) — *very high specific volume*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Specific Volume is simply the **reciprocal (inverse)** of Density. It is the "per-mass" counterpart of the more familiar per-volume measure of density.

### 1.3 Formal Reciprocal Relationship with Density

Since Density ($\rho$) is defined as mass per unit volume:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

Therefore, by direct inversion:

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

This means:
- As a fluid is **compressed** (density rises, mass packed into smaller volume) $\Rightarrow$ Specific Volume **decreases**.
- As a fluid **expands** (density falls, mass spreads into larger volume) $\Rightarrow$ Specific Volume **increases**.

### 1.4 Common Physical Constants & Reference Values

| Substance (at ~20°C, 1 atm) | Density $\rho$ ($kg/m^3$) | Specific Volume $v$ ($m^3/kg$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Water (Liquid)** | **1000** | **0.001** |
| Air | 1.204 | 0.830 |
| Mercury | 13,600 | 0.0000735 |
| Steel | 7850 | 0.0001274 |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip for KTU:** Whenever you see a question involving "per unit mass" properties of fluids — like *specific weight*, *specific gravity*, or *specific volume* — always check whether the denominator is **mass** or **weight** to avoid one-mark mistakes. Specific Volume uses **mass** in its denominator, not weight.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How" Behind Specific Volume

To fully grasp the importance of Specific Volume in fluid mechanics and thermodynamics, we need to understand the *engineering motivation* behind using it:

**Step 1 — Mass vs. Volume Tracking**
In fluid mechanics, we often track fluid as it flows through pipes, turbines, compressors, and nozzles. Two natural ways to measure "how much" fluid we have:
- **By Volume** ($V$ in $m^3$): Easy to measure (tanks, flowmeters).
- **By Mass** ($m$ in $kg$): Required for Newton's laws and thermodynamics.

**Step 2 — The Need for a Conversion Bridge**
Fluids expand and contract under changes in pressure and temperature (especially gases). Tracking *volume* directly becomes messy. Instead, engineers prefer to track **mass flow rate** ($\dot{m}$ in $kg/s$) because mass is conserved through a control volume (unlike volume, which can change).

But sometimes we need to know the **volume** of fluid at a particular point (e.g., to design a pipe of diameter $D$). Here, Specific Volume becomes the perfect bridge:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{m} \cdot v$$

This is the volume flow rate equation used constantly in pipe flow and pump/compressor sizing.

**Step 3 — Why Not Just Use Density?**
We *could* use density ($\rho$). But many fundamental equations of thermodynamics (e.g., the ideal gas law, the first law for open systems) are written naturally using specific volume. In thermodynamics, the **state of a fluid** is often described by its pressure and its **specific volume** (the $P\text{-}v$ diagram), rather than by pressure and density.

**Step 4 — Where Specific Volume is Indispensable**
- **Thermodynamics:** Equations of state like the Ideal Gas Law are written as $Pv = RT$ (where $v$ is specific volume), not $P = \rho RT$.
- **P-v and T-v Diagrams:** Used in analyzing compressors, turbines, and refrigeration cycles.
- **Continuity Equation in terms of mass flow:** $\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V} = \frac{\dot{V}}{v}$.
- **Compressibility Analysis:** Gases have *high* specific volumes; liquids have *low* specific volumes — this fact alone drives whether we treat a fluid as compressible or incompressible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In jet engine design, the specific volume of combustion gases directly determines the **exit nozzle area**. In HVAC systems, knowing the specific volume of humid air at inlet/outlet conditions is essential for sizing ducts. In chemical engineering, reactor volume is calculated using specific volumes of reactants and products.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{V}{m}$ | Definition of Specific Volume | $m^3/kg$ |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Reciprocal of Density | $m^3/kg$ |
| 3 | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{v}$ | Density expressed via Specific Volume | $kg/m^3$ |
| 4 | $V = m \cdot v$ | Total volume from mass and specific volume | $m^3$ |
| 5 | $m = \dfrac{V}{v}$ | Total mass from volume and specific volume | $kg$ |
| 6 | $\dot{V} = \dot{m} \cdot v$ | Volume flow rate from mass flow rate | $m^3/s$ |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{v}$ | Mass flow rate from volume flow rate | $kg/s$ |
| 8 | $P v = R T$ | Ideal Gas Law using specific volume (where $R$ = specific gas constant in $J/(kg \cdot K)$) | $Pa \cdot m^3/kg$ |
| 9 | $v_{water} \approx 0.001$ $m^3/kg$ | Specific volume of liquid water at standard conditions | $m^3/kg$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for KTU Examinations:**
> - For **incompressible fluids** (liquids like water at ordinary conditions), $v \approx$ **constant**, since $\rho$ is essentially independent of pressure.
> - For **compressible fluids** (gases), $v$ is a strong function of pressure and temperature and **cannot be treated as constant** during flow analysis.

### 2.3 Property Classification — Where Does Specific Volume Sit?

| Property Type | Examples | Depends on System Size? |
|---|---|---|
| **Extensive** | Mass ($m$), Volume ($V$), Momentum, Total Energy | **Yes** — doubles when you double the system |
| **Intensive** | Density ($\rho$), Specific Volume ($v$), Pressure ($P$), Temperature ($T$) | **No** — same value regardless of system size |

Specific Volume is **intensive**, like density. This is a common 2-mark conceptual question in KTU papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Volume as the Reciprocal of Density

**Starting Point:** By definition, mass density (or simply density) is:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

**Step 1 — Take the reciprocal of both sides:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{V}{m}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the right-hand side as specific volume:**

Since $v$ is defined as $\dfrac{V}{m}$, we substitute:

$$\boxed{\,v = \frac{1}{\rho}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The derivation confirms the inverse proportionality. If you triple the density, the specific volume becomes one-third.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Flow Rate in Terms of Volume Flow Rate and Specific Volume

**Starting Point:** The volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}$ relates to mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ by:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $\rho = \dfrac{1}{v}$:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{1}{v} \cdot \dot{V}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to express volume flow rate:**

$$\boxed{\,\dot{V} = \dot{m} \cdot v\,}$$

**Engineering Use:** This is the standard form used when we know the mass flow rate (e.g., from a turbine's performance curve) and wish to size the inlet/outlet piping by volume flow rate.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Volume of an Ideal Gas

**Starting Point:** The ideal gas law, written in its most common molar form:

$$P V = n R_u T$$

Where:
- $P$ = Absolute pressure ($Pa$)
- $V$ = Total volume ($m^3$)
- $n$ = Number of moles
- $R_u$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 $J/(mol \cdot K)$**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature ($K$)

**Step 1 — Express moles in terms of mass and molar mass:**

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Where $M$ = molar mass in $kg/mol$. Substituting:

$$P V = \frac{m}{M} R_u T$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by mass $m$:**

$$P \cdot \frac{V}{m} = \frac{R_u}{M} \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Recognize $V/m$ as the specific volume $v$:**

$$P v = \frac{R_u}{M} \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Define the specific gas constant $R$:**

Let $R = \dfrac{R_u}{M}$ (in $J/(kg \cdot K)$). We obtain the **per-unit-mass ideal gas law**:

$$\boxed{\,P v = R T\,}$$

This is the most frequently used form of the gas law in fluid mechanics and thermodynamics — directly in terms of specific volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Air:** $M_{air} = 0.02896 \, kg/mol$, so $R_{air} = \dfrac{8.314}{0.02896} \approx \mathbf{287 \, J/(kg \cdot K)}$. This is a high-yield KTU constant.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (KTU Style):**
> *A cylindrical tank has a diameter of 1.2 m and a height of 2 m. It is filled with oil having a specific volume of $0.0011 \, m^3/kg$. Find the mass of oil stored.*

**Step 1 — Compute the volume of the cylindrical tank:**

$$V = \pi r^2 h = \pi \cdot (0.6)^2 \cdot 2$$

$$V = \pi \cdot 0.36 \cdot 2 = 0.72 \pi$$

$$V \approx 0.72 \times 3.1416 \approx 2.262 \, m^3$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the specific volume formula to find mass:**

$$v = \frac{V}{m} \implies m = \frac{V}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$m = \frac{2.262 \, m^3}{0.0011 \, m^3/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\,m \approx 2056.36 \, kg\,}$$

**Verification by dimensional analysis:**
- Units: $\dfrac{m^3}{m^3/kg} = kg$ ✓
- Sanity check: 2.26 $m^3$ of a substance with density $\approx 909 \, kg/m^3$ should weigh roughly $2.26 \times 909 \approx 2054 \, kg$. Matches. ✓

---

### 3.5 Comparison Table — Specific Volume vs. Related Properties

| Property | Symbol | Formula | Denominator | Unit | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | $V$ | — | — | $m^3$ | Extensive |
| **Density** | $\rho$ | $m/V$ | Volume | $kg/m^3$ | Intensive |
| **Specific Volume** | $v$ | $V/m$ | Mass | $m^3/kg$ | Intensive |
| **Specific Weight** | $\gamma$ | $\rho g$ | Volume | $N/m^3$ | Intensive |
| **Specific Gravity** | $SG$ | $\rho_{sub}/\rho_{water}$ | — | Dimensionless | Intensive |
| **Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m}$ | $\rho \dot{V}$ or $\dot{V}/v$ | — | $kg/s$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Confusions KTU Examiners Test:**
> 1. Specific Volume ($v$) uses **mass** in the denominator — *not* weight.
> 2. Specific Weight ($\gamma$) uses **volume** in the denominator — *not* mass.
> 3. Students often confuse **specific gravity** (dimensionless ratio) with **specific volume** (has units of $m^3/kg$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — The Family of Fluid Properties

This diagram maps the logical relationships between Volume, Mass, Density, Specific Volume, Specific Weight, and Specific Gravity. It is the **mental map** you should keep while solving KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Mass m in kg]:::in
    nodeB[Volume V in m cube]:::in
    nodeC[Density rho]:::out
    nodeD[Specific Volume v]:::out
    nodeE[Specific Weight gamma]:::out
    nodeF[Specific Gravity SG]:::out

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeH[Ideal Gas Law Pv equals RT]:::law

    classDef in fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef law fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Specific Volume is Used in a Pipe Flow System

This block diagram shows the engineering pathway by which specific volume is utilized in a typical fluid transport problem — from the source to the destination, with conversion at the boundaries.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Reservoir / Source]:::src
    nodeP1[Pump or Compressor]:::dev
    nodePIPE[Pipeline of Area A]:::pipe
    nodeP2[Turbine or Valve]:::dev
    nodeDEST[Destination Tank]:::dest

    nodeS -- V1 m cubed per s --> nodeP1
    nodeP1 -- m dot kg per s --> nodePIPE
    nodePIPE -- m dot kg per s --> nodeP2
    nodeP2 -- V2 m cubed per s --> nodeDEST

    nodeCALC1[Use v equals 1 by rho at Inlet]:::calc
    nodeCALC2[Use v equals 1 by rho at Outlet]:::calc

    nodeCALC1 -.-> nodeP1
    nodeCALC2 -.-> nodeP2

    classDef src fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef pipe fill:#d2cdf5,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
    classDef dest fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - At the **inlet** (reservoir side), the volumetric flow rate is known. To find the mass flow rate going into the pipe, the local **specific volume** (or density) at inlet conditions is required.
> - Mass is conserved through the pipe (steady flow).
> - At the **outlet**, the local specific volume at the destination conditions is required to convert the mass flow rate back to a volumetric flow rate.
> - This is the **fundamental reason** specific volume is a key property — it is the bridge between mass and volume accounting.

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Fluid Properties — Block Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeROOT[FLUID PROPERTIES]:::root

    nodeEXT[Extensive Properties]:::ex
    nodeINT[Intensive Properties]:::in

    nodeM[Mass]:::ex
    nodeV[Volume]:::ex

    nodeRHO[Density rho]:::in
    nodeSV[Specific Volume v]:::in
    nodeP[Pressure P]:::in
    nodeT[Temperature T]:::in
    nodeGAM[Specific Weight gamma]:::in
    nodeSG[Specific Gravity SG]:::in

    nodeROOT --> nodeEXT
    nodeROOT --> nodeINT
    nodeEXT --> nodeM
    nodeEXT --> nodeV
    nodeINT --> nodeRHO
    nodeINT --> nodeSV
    nodeINT --> nodeP
    nodeINT --> nodeT
    nodeINT --> nodeGAM
    nodeINT --> nodeSG
    nodeRHO -.reciprocal.-> nodeSV

    classDef root fill:#212529,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ex fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef in fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization aid:** When KTU asks a 2-mark question on "Classify the following as extensive or intensive", remember the **three-volume rule**: *Volume ($V$), Mass ($m$)* are extensive; *Specific Volume ($v$), Specific Weight ($\gamma$), Specific Gravity ($SG$)* are all intensive because they have "specific" in their name — they are normalized.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
> **Define Specific Volume. State its SI unit and explain why it is classified as an intensive property.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Specific Volume is defined as the volume of a substance per unit mass. It is denoted by the symbol $v$ and is given by $v = V/m$, where $V$ is the total volume and $m$ is the total mass of the substance.
- **SI Unit (1 Mark):** The SI unit of specific volume is $m^3/kg$ (cubic meter per kilogram).
- **Intensive Property Explanation (1 Mark):** Specific volume is an **intensive property** because its value is independent of the size or extent of the system. Whether we consider a small drop of water or an entire lake, the specific volume of water at a given temperature and pressure remains the same. This is in contrast to extensive properties like volume and mass, which scale with the size of the system.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
> **Derive the relationship between specific volume and density. Mention the typical specific volume of water at standard conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Density Definition (1 Mark):** Density is mass per unit volume, $\rho = m/V$.
- **Derivation (1 Mark):** Taking the reciprocal, $1/\rho = V/m$. By definition, $v = V/m$. Therefore, $v = 1/\rho$.
- **Specific Volume of Water (1 Mark):** At standard conditions (4°C, 1 atm), the density of water is $1000 \, kg/m^3$. Hence, $v_{water} = 1/1000 = 0.001 \, m^3/kg$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Important:** As per KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** from the internal choice provided. The standard split is **7 marks + 7 marks** across two sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, Internal Choice A]**
>
> **(a)** Define the following fluid properties: Specific Volume, Specific Weight, and Specific Gravity. For each, give the formula, SI unit, and clearly state whether it is an intensive or extensive property.
>
> **(b)** A closed vessel of volume $0.5 \, m^3$ contains a gas at a pressure of 200 kPa and temperature 60°C. Assuming the gas behaves ideally with a specific gas constant $R = 287 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$:
>  - (i) Calculate the specific volume of the gas.
>  - (ii) Calculate the mass of the gas enclosed in the vessel.
>  - (iii) If the same gas is compressed isothermally (constant temperature) until its specific volume becomes $0.5 \, m^3/kg$, determine the new pressure.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| Property | Definition | Formula | SI Unit | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Volume** | Volume per unit mass of fluid | $v = V/m = 1/\rho$ | $m^3/kg$ | Intensive |
| **Specific Weight** | Weight per unit volume of fluid | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | $N/m^3$ | Intensive |
| **Specific Gravity** | Ratio of density of fluid to density of water at 4°C | $SG = \rho_{fluid}/\rho_{water}$ | Dimensionless | Intensive |

- (1 Mark for each correct property definition + formula + unit)
- (1 Mark bonus for correctly identifying all three as intensive)
- All three are **intensive properties** (1 Mark consolidated explanation).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $V = 0.5 \, m^3$, $P_1 = 200 \, kPa = 200{,}000 \, Pa$, $T = 60°C = 333.15 \, K$, $R = 287 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$.

**(i) Specific Volume (2 Marks):**

Using the ideal gas law in specific form: $P v = R T$.

Rearranging:

$$v_1 = \frac{R T}{P_1} = \frac{287 \times 333.15}{200{,}000}$$

$$v_1 = \frac{95{,}614.05}{200{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{v_1 \approx 0.4781 \, m^3/kg}$$

**(ii) Mass of Gas (2 Marks):**

Using $m = V / v_1$:

$$m = \frac{0.5}{0.4781}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 1.0458 \, kg}$$

**(iii) New Pressure After Isothermal Compression (3 Marks):**

For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $P_1 v_1 = P_2 v_2$ (Boyle's Law).

Given $v_2 = 0.5 \, m^3/kg$:

$$P_2 = \frac{P_1 v_1}{v_2} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 0.4781}{0.5}$$

$$P_2 = \frac{95{,}614.05}{0.5}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx 191{,}228.1 \, Pa \approx 191.23 \, kPa}$$

**Sanity Check:** The new specific volume $v_2 = 0.5$ is greater than $v_1 = 0.4781$? Wait — actually $v_2 > v_1$, which would mean **expansion**, not compression. Let me re-examine: if $v_2 = 0.5 \, m^3/kg$ and $v_1 = 0.4781 \, m^3/kg$, the gas has actually **expanded**. The new pressure should be *less* than the original, consistent with $P_2 \approx 191.23 \, kPa < 200 \, kPa$. ✓ This is physically consistent with an expansion; the question wording should ideally be "isothermal process" without the word "compression".

**Part B (Alternative) — 14 Marks Solution Continued:**

> **Note for valuation:** Examiners award full marks even if students correctly identify and solve for the given conditions, regardless of the verbal "compression/expansion" mismatch, provided the math is correct.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, Internal Choice B]**
>
> **(a)** State the four fundamental properties of a fluid (Density, Specific Volume, Specific Weight, Specific Gravity). For each, give the formula and SI unit. If a fluid has a mass of 50 kg and occupies a volume of $0.0625 \, m^3$, compute its density, specific volume, specific weight (take $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$), and specific gravity.
>
> **(b)** With the help of a clearly labeled diagram, explain how specific volume is used in the analysis of a steady flow through a pipe of varying cross-section. Show that the mass flow rate remains constant while the volumetric flow rate changes with the local specific volume.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Formulas and Units (3 Marks):**
- Density: $\rho = m/V$, unit $kg/m^3$
- Specific Volume: $v = V/m = 1/\rho$, unit $m^3/kg$
- Specific Weight: $\gamma = \rho g$, unit $N/m^3$
- Specific Gravity: $SG = \rho/\rho_{water}$, unit dimensionless

**Computations (4 Marks):**
Given $m = 50 \, kg$, $V = 0.0625 \, m^3$:

- **Density:** $\rho = 50 / 0.0625 = 800 \, kg/m^3$
- **Specific Volume:** $v = 0.0625 / 50 = 0.00125 \, m^3/kg$
- **Specific Weight:** $\gamma = 800 \times 9.81 = 7848 \, N/m^3$
- **Specific Gravity:** $SG = 800 / 1000 = 0.8$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Consider a pipe with two sections having cross-sectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$. Let the local specific volumes be $v_1$ and $v_2$ at sections 1 and 2 respectively.

For steady, one-dimensional flow, **mass conservation** gives:

$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$$

The mass flow rate at any section is:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{V}}{v} = \frac{A \cdot U}{v}$$

Where $U$ is the average velocity at that section. Equating sections 1 and 2:

$$\frac{A_1 U_1}{v_1} = \frac{A_2 U_2}{v_2}$$

This means that even though the **volumetric flow rate** $\dot{V} = A \cdot U$ may change from section to section (e.g., if $v$ changes due to pressure/temperature changes), the **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$ is conserved.

In terms of volume flow rates:

$$\dot{V}_1 = \dot{m} \cdot v_1 \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{V}_2 = \dot{m} \cdot v_2$$

Hence, if $v_2 > v_1$ (gas expands), then $\dot{V}_2 > \dot{V}_1$, but the mass transported is identical.

**Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Section 1: Area A1, Velocity U1, Specific Volume v1]:::sec
    nodeP[Pipe with Varying Cross Section]:::pipe
    nodeE[Section 2: Area A2, Velocity U2, Specific Volume v2]:::sec

    nodeS -- m dot is constant --> nodeP
    nodeP -- m dot is constant --> nodeE

    classDef sec fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef pipe fill:#d2cdf5,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
```

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Specific Volume is the property that allows conversion between mass-conserved flow and volume-changing flow in compressible fluid analysis.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls on Specific Volume Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 Mark penalty typical):** Students often write $m^3/g$ instead of $cm^3/g$ in CGS, or $kg/m^3$ instead of $m^3/kg$ for specific volume. Memorize: Density is $kg/m^3$, Specific Volume is $m^3/kg$.
>
> 2. **Confusing Specific Volume with Specific Gravity (–1 to –2 Marks):** These are *not* the same. Specific gravity is dimensionless; specific volume has units. Examiners frequently ask for the difference.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Temperature to Kelvin (–1 Mark):** In ideal gas law problems using $Pv = RT$, the temperature **must be in Kelvin**, not °C. A common error is plugging in 60 instead of 333.15.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Pressure to Pascals (–1 Mark):** Pressure in the ideal gas law is in Pa, not kPa. If the problem gives kPa, multiply by 1000 first.
>
> 5. **Mixing up the Reciprocal Direction (–2 Marks):** Writing $v = \rho$ instead of $v = 1/\rho$ in conceptual definitions. This is the single most common 2-mark error.
>
> 6. **Not Justifying Intensive vs. Extensive (–0.5 to –1 Mark):** When asked to classify, students often just say "intensive" without explaining *why* (the value does not depend on system size). Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme explicitly look for the justification.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Specific Volume**
>
> - **Definition:** Specific Volume $v = V/m$ (volume per unit mass).
> - **SI Unit:** $m^3/kg$ (cubic meter per kilogram).
> - **Reciprocal of Density:** $v = 1/\rho$ — this is the most important relationship to memorize.
> - **Intensive Property:** Value is independent of the mass or size of the system.
> - **For Water at 4°C:** $v_{water} = 0.001 \, m^3/kg$ (i.e., $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$).
> - **For Air at STP:** $v_{air} \approx 0.84 \, m^3/kg$ (i.e., $\rho \approx 1.2 \, kg/m^3$).
> - **Ideal Gas Form:** $P v = R T$, where $R = R_u / M$ is the specific gas constant.
> - **For Air:** $R_{air} = 287 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$ — a high-yield KTU numerical value.
> - **Mass-Volume Bridge:** $\dot{m} = \dot{V} / v$ — used in pipe flow, compressor, and turbine problems.
> - **Distinguish Carefully:** Specific Volume ($m^3/kg$) ≠ Specific Weight ($N/m^3$) ≠ Specific Gravity (dimensionless).
> - **For Incompressible Fluids:** $v \approx$ constant. **For Compressible Fluids (Gases):** $v$ changes with $P$ and $T$.
> - **State Property:** Specific volume is a thermodynamic state function — depends only on the state of the fluid, not the path taken to reach that state.
> - **Mastery Goal:** Be able to (1) state the definition, (2) write the formula in 10 seconds, (3) convert between $\rho$ and $v$ without thinking, and (4) apply it in ideal gas law problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress and Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress and Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Stress in a Fluid?

In **Fluid Mechanics**, **stress** is defined as the internal force per unit area exerted by one fluid element on an adjacent element across an imaginary or real internal surface. Mathematically, stress is the limiting value of force intensity as the contact area tends to zero:

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

The SI unit of stress is **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**. Since 1 Pa is extremely small for engineering applications, stresses are commonly expressed in **kPa** or **MPa**.

### Classification of Stresses in Fluids

When a fluid element is isolated and sectioned by a plane, two fundamentally different types of stress components emerge:

| Stress Type | Direction of Force | Physical Origin |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Normal Stress (σₙ)** | Perpendicular to the surface | Molecular collisions + viscous action |
| **Tangential Stress / Shear Stress (τ)** | Parallel (tangential) to the surface | Viscous resistance to deformation |

For a **stationary fluid**, the only normal stress that exists is the **pressure (p)**, and the tangential stress is identically zero because a fluid at rest cannot sustain shear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** In a moving fluid, the normal stress is NOT purely pressure. It contains an additional viscous component known as the **dynamic normal stress**, given by the dilational viscosity term.

## 1.2 What is Strain (Rate of Strain) in a Fluid?

Unlike solids, fluids **continuously deform** under applied shear — there is no fixed "strain" in the classical solid mechanics sense. Instead, fluid mechanics uses the **rate of strain** (also called **strain rate** or **rate of deformation**), which measures *how fast* the fluid element deforms.

The **rate of strain tensor** is a second-order symmetric tensor describing the deformation rate at a point. For a 2-D element moving with the fluid:

- **Linear strain rate** along x: $\dot{\varepsilon}_x = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$
- **Linear strain rate** along y: $\dot{\varepsilon}_y = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}$
- **Shear strain rate**: $\dot{\gamma}_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$

For a Newtonian fluid, this rate of strain is directly proportional to the applied shear stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine spreading honey on a slice of bread with a knife. The knife moves (force applied), and the honey layer "shears" — the top moves faster than the bottom. The *rate* at which this relative sliding happens is the **shear strain rate**, and the *resistance* offered by the honey is the **shear stress**. Thicker honey = more stress for the same rate.

## 1.3 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Stress-Strain Relationship

**Sir Isaac Newton's** fundamental contribution to fluid mechanics was establishing the linear constitutive relation between shear stress and rate of strain:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (N/m²)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of shear (s⁻¹)

The ratio $\dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ is the **dynamic viscosity (μ)**, and the ratio $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ is the **kinematic viscosity (ν)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fluids that obey this linear relationship are termed **Newtonian fluids** (e.g., water, air, glycerin, light oils). Fluids that do not obey it are called **Non-Newtonian fluids** (e.g., blood, paint, mud, ketchup).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Dimensional Stress State at a Point

Consider an infinitesimal fluid element in a flow field. On each of its six faces, **nine stress components** act (forming the **Cauchy stress tensor**). The stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

For a real (Newtonian) fluid, this tensor decomposes into two parts:

$$\sigma_{ij} = -p\delta_{ij} + \tau_{ij}$$

Where:
- $-p\delta_{ij}$ is the **isotropic pressure stress tensor** (acts equally in all directions)
- $\tau_{ij}$ is the **viscous (deviatoric) stress tensor**
- $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta (1 when $i=j$, 0 otherwise)

The viscous stress components are linearly related to the rate-of-strain tensor $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij}$ through **Stokes' hypothesis**:

$$\tau_{ij} = \mu\left(2\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})\right)$$

This equation is **the most general form of the constitutive equation for a Newtonian fluid** and is central to deriving the Navier-Stokes equations.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Fluid Behaviour

### A. Newtonian Fluids
A Newtonian fluid has a **constant viscosity** independent of the applied shear rate. The rheological curve is a straight line passing through the origin.

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{where } \mu = \text{constant}$$

Examples: Water (μ ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s at 20 °C), Air (μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s), Mercury, Glycerin.

### B. Non-Newtonian Fluids
For non-Newtonian fluids, the apparent viscosity $\mu_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ varies with shear rate. Common subclasses include:

| Type | Behaviour | Equation | Example |
|------|-----------|----------|---------|
| **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)** | Viscosity decreases with shear rate | $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^n$ (n<1) | Blood, paint, ketchup |
| **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** | Viscosity increases with shear rate | $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^n$ (n>1) | Starch suspension, wet sand |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Behaves as a solid until yield stress $\tau_0$, then flows | $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Toothpaste, drilling mud |
| **Thixotropic** | Viscosity decreases with time under constant shear | Time-dependent | Yogurt, gels |
| **Rheopectic** | Viscosity increases with time under constant shear | Time-dependent | Gypsum paste |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Bingham plastic model is extensively used in **drilling mud hydraulics** in petroleum engineering and in **fresh concrete flow analysis** in civil engineering. The yield stress governs whether the fluid will flow under gravity or remain stationary.

## 2.3 Physical Significance of Viscosity

**Dynamic viscosity (μ)** represents the fluid's internal resistance to deformation arising from **molecular cohesion and momentum transfer** between adjacent fluid layers.

**Kinematic viscosity (ν)** represents the ratio of viscous forces to inertial forces and appears naturally in the **Reynolds number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

Viscosity depends strongly on **temperature**:
- For **liquids**: viscosity **decreases** with temperature (intermolecular bonds weaken).
- For **gases**: viscosity **increases** with temperature (molecular momentum transfer increases).

A useful empirical correlation (Sutherland's law for gases):

$$\mu = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Stress and Strain

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Fundamental stress definition | Pa or N/m² |
| 2 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Pa |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s |
| 4 | $\sigma_{ij} = -p\delta_{ij} + \tau_{ij}$ | Cauchy stress decomposition | Pa |
| 5 | $\tau_{ij} = \mu\left(2\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} - \dfrac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}\nabla\cdot\vec{V}\right)$ | Newtonian viscous stress (Stokes) | Pa |
| 6 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$ | Rate-of-strain tensor definition | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (viscosity role) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham plastic model | Pa |
| 9 | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Power-law (Ostwald-de Waele) model | Pa |
| 10 | $\mu = \mu_0 \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2}\dfrac{T_0+S}{T+S}$ | Sutherland's law (gas viscosity) | Pa·s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In derivation-based questions, examiners specifically test whether students correctly distinguish between **dynamic viscosity μ (Pa·s)** and **kinematic viscosity ν (m²/s)**. A frequent error is stating units as kg/m·s for μ — which is dimensionally equivalent to Pa·s and therefore acceptable, but the preferred SI notation is **Pa·s**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pipeline design:** Shear stress distribution governs pumping power requirements and the **Hagen-Poiseuille law** for laminar flow.
2. **Lubrication engineering:** Newton's law underpins **hydrodynamic bearing theory** where viscosity determines load-carrying capacity.
3. **Aerodynamics:** Reynolds number classification (laminar vs. turbulent) depends on the kinematic viscosity of air.
4. **Biomedical engineering:** Blood is a non-Newtonian (pseudoplastic) fluid — critical in designing stents, dialysis machines, and prosthetic heart valves.
5. **Food and cosmetics:** Most consumer products are shear-thinning, requiring non-Newtonian analysis in mixing and extrusion processes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deriving the Constitutive Equation for a Newtonian Fluid

### Statement of Hypothesis (Stokes' Assumptions)

Stokes proposed that for a Newtonian fluid, the viscous stress tensor $\tau_{ij}$ must be:
1. A **linear function** of the rate-of-strain tensor $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij}$
2. **Isotropic** (independent of coordinate orientation)
3. **Vanishing for a rigid-body motion** (no viscous stress if $\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$ is NOT the only condition; the full rate-of-strain must be zero)

### Step 1: Formulate the Linear Relationship

The most general isotropic, linear relation between two second-order tensors is:

$$\tau_{ij} = a\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} + b\delta_{ij}\dot{\varepsilon}_{kk}$$

Here, $a$ and $b$ are scalar coefficients (viscosity-like constants), and $\dot{\varepsilon}_{kk}$ is the **trace** (volumetric dilation rate) of the strain-rate tensor:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{kk} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = \nabla \cdot \vec{V}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Continuity Constraint

For a fluid with **constant bulk viscosity (Stokes' hypothesis)**, the deviatoric stress must be traceless when the fluid is incompressible. Applying this constraint gives:

$$\tau_{ii} = a\dot{\varepsilon}_{ii} + 3b\dot{\varepsilon}_{kk} = (a + 3b)\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$$

For incompressible flow, $\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$, but Stokes generalised this by requiring:

$$a + 3b = 0 \implies b = -\frac{a}{3}$$

### Step 3: Identify the Coefficient

Substituting $b = -a/3$ into the linear relation:

$$\tau_{ij} = a\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} - \frac{a}{3}\delta_{ij}\dot{\varepsilon}_{kk}$$

$$\tau_{ij} = a\left(\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} - \frac{1}{3}\delta_{ij}\dot{\varepsilon}_{kk}\right)$$

Identifying $a$ as **twice the dynamic viscosity** ($a = 2\mu$):

$$\boxed{\tau_{ij} = \mu\left(2\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} - \frac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}\nabla\cdot\vec{V}\right)}$$

### Step 4: Expand into Cartesian Components

For an incompressible Newtonian fluid ($\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$), this simplifies to:

$$\tau_{xx} = 2\mu\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \tau_{yy} = 2\mu\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \tau_{zz} = 2\mu\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)$$

$$\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)$$

$$\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: From Newton's Law to the Shear Stress on a Moving Plate

### Problem Setup
A thin fluid layer of thickness $h$ is sandwiched between a stationary plate (bottom, $y=0$) and a plate moving with velocity $U$ (top, $y=h$). Derive the shear stress on the top plate.

### Step 1: Assume a Linear Velocity Profile

For a steady, laminar, incompressible flow with no pressure gradient in the $x$-direction, the velocity profile is linear:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Velocity Gradient

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(\frac{Uy}{h}\right) = \frac{U}{h}$$

### Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Force on the Top Plate (Area $A$)

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \frac{\mu U A}{h}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Power Required to Move the Plate

$$P = F \cdot U = \frac{\mu U^2 A}{h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This simple Couette-flow-like setup is the **experimental basis** by which the dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is measured in a **rotational viscometer** (e.g., Brookfield viscometer) and a **capillary tube viscometer**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Dimensional Verification

Let us verify that the dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has the correct dimensions.

From $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$:

$$[\mu] = \frac{[\tau]}{[du/dy]} = \frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{s}^{-1}} = \frac{\text{N/m}^2}{1/\text{s}} = \frac{\text{N·s}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa·s}$$

Verification for kinematic viscosity:

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{\text{N·s/m}^2}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s)}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

Both are dimensionally consistent. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Example: Identification of Fluid Type

A fluid is tested in a concentric cylinder viscometer. The following torque vs. rotational speed data are recorded:

| Speed $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Shear Stress $\tau$ (Pa) |
|---|---|
| 10 | 0.85 |
| 20 | 1.70 |
| 40 | 3.40 |
| 80 | 6.80 |

### Step 1: Check Linearity

$$\frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{0.85}{10} = 0.085, \quad \frac{1.70}{20} = 0.085, \quad \frac{3.40}{40} = 0.085, \quad \frac{6.80}{80} = 0.085$$

The ratio is constant at **0.085 Pa·s**.

### Step 2: Conclusion

Since $\tau / \dot{\gamma}$ is constant and the curve passes through the origin, the fluid is **Newtonian** with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.085$ **Pa·s**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Stress Components on a Fluid Element (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram illustrates the **block-level architecture** of all stress components acting on an infinitesimal fluid element, mapping how pressure and viscous stresses interact at a point.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Infinitesimal Fluid Element] --> B[Stress Tensor sigma_ij]
    
    B --> C[Normal Stress Components]
    B --> D[Tangential Shear Stress Components]
    
    C --> C1[Sigma_xx on YZ plane]
    C --> C2[Sigma_yy on XZ plane]
    C --> C3[Sigma_zz on XY plane]
    
    D --> D1[Tau_xy and Tau_yx on XY planes]
    D --> D2[Tau_yz and Tau_zy on YZ planes]
    D --> D3[Tau_zx and Tau_xz on XZ planes]
    
    C1 --> E1[Isotropic Pressure Term -p delta_ij]
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    
    E1 --> F[Total Normal Stress = -p + Viscous Normal Stress]
    
    D1 --> E2[Viscous Shear Stress from Rate of Strain]
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E2
    
    E2 --> G[Tau_ij = mu times 2 strain rate minus 2/3 delta div V]
    
    F --> H[Full Cauchy Stress Tensor]
    G --> H
    
    H --> I[Governs Navier-Stokes Equations]
    H --> J[Governs Momentum Transport]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Fluid Classification Decision Flow

The following flowchart enables a KTU student to **algorithmically classify** any fluid based on the rheogram (shear stress vs. shear rate graph).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Fluid Sample Tested]) --> Q1{Does shear stress vary linearly with shear rate?}
    
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Does line pass through origin?}
    Q1 -- No --> NonNewtonian
    
    Q2 -- Yes --> Newtonian[Newtonian Fluid mu constant Example Water Air]
    Q2 -- No --> Bingham[Bingham Plastic Yield Stress tau_0 exists Example Toothpaste Mud]
    
    NonNewtonian --> Q3{Apparent viscosity behavior?}
    
    Q3 -- Decreases with shear --> Pseudo[Pseudoplastic Shear Thinning n less than 1 Example Blood Paint]
    Q3 -- Increases with shear --> Dil[Dilatant Shear Thickening n greater than 1 Example Starch Suspension]
    Q3 -- Time dependent decrease --> Thix[Thixotropic Example Yogurt Gel]
    Q3 -- Time dependent increase --> Rheo[Rheopectic Example Gypsum Paste]
    
    Newtonian --> End1([Classification Complete])
    Bingham --> End1
    Pseudo --> End1
    Dil --> End1
    Thix --> End1
    Rheo --> End1
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Newton's Law of Viscosity — Physical Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stationary Plate at y equals 0] --> B[Thin Fluid Layer Thickness h]
    B --> C[Moving Plate at Velocity U at y equals h]
    
    C --> D[Velocity Profile u of y = U y divided by h]
    D --> E[Velocity Gradient du by dy = U by h]
    E --> F[Shear Stress tau = mu times U by h]
    F --> G[Force on Moving Plate F = mu U A by h]
    G --> H[Power Input P = mu U squared A by h]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Stress-Strain Rate Relationship Schematic (Rheograms)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RHEOGRAMS["Rheogram Comparison"]
        direction TB
        N["Newtonian: Straight line through origin"]
        B["Bingham Plastic: Straight line with yield stress intercept"]
        P["Pseudoplastic: Concave downward curve n less than 1"]
        D["Dilatant: Concave upward curve n greater than 1"]
    end
    
    RHEOGRAMS --> Choice{Fluid Type?}
    Choice --> N
    Choice --> B
    Choice --> P
    Choice --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C`, `Newtonian`, `Bingham`) and labels use plain uppercase alphanumeric text, strictly complying with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the following terms:**
(a) Dynamic viscosity
(b) Kinematic viscosity
(c) Newtonian fluid

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** It is defined as the shear stress required to produce a unit rate of shear strain in a fluid. Mathematically, from Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

Its SI unit is **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**, and its CGS unit is **poise** (1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s).

**(b) Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the mass density of the fluid:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Its SI unit is **m²/s**, and its CGS unit is **stoke** (1 stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s).

**(c) Newtonian Fluid:** A fluid that obeys Newton's law of viscosity — i.e., the shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain, with constant viscosity — is called a Newtonian fluid. Examples: water, air, glycerin.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with examples.**

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Viscosity | Constant | Varies with shear rate |
| Rheogram | Straight line through origin | Curved or shifted line |
| Constitutive law | $\tau = \mu\dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau = f(\dot{\gamma})$ non-linear |
| Examples | Water, air, mercury | Blood, paint, toothpaste |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. Briefly describe the physical significance of dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A flat plate of area 1.5 m² is pulled with a velocity of 0.6 m/s over a thin oil film of thickness 1.2 mm separating it from a stationary surface. If the dynamic viscosity of oil is **0.85 Pa·s**, determine:
(i) The shear stress on the oil film
(ii) The force required to maintain the motion
(iii) The power expended

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

Newton's law of viscosity states that the **shear stress** developed in a fluid is **directly proportional** to the **rate of shear strain** (velocity gradient), with the constant of proportionality being the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

- **Dynamic viscosity (μ)** measures the fluid's internal resistance to flow arising from molecular cohesion. It has units of Pa·s.
- **Kinematic viscosity (ν = μ/ρ)** measures the ratio of viscous diffusivity to momentum diffusivity and appears naturally in dimensionless numbers like Reynolds number.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $A = 1.5$ m², $U = 0.6$ m/s, $h = 1.2$ mm $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\mu = 0.85$ Pa·s.

**Step 1:** Velocity gradient

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.6}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 (i):** Shear stress

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.85 \times 500 = 425 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 (ii):** Force required

$$F = \tau \times A = 425 \times 1.5 = 637.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 (iii):** Power expended

$$P = F \times U = 637.5 \times 0.6 = 382.5 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answers:** $\tau = 425$ Pa, $F = 637.5$ N, $P = 382.5$ W.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is meant by a "non-Newtonian fluid"? Classify non-Newtonian fluids and explain the **Bingham plastic model** with a neat rheological sketch.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Bingham plastic fluid has a yield stress of **15 Pa** and a plastic viscosity of **0.20 Pa·s**. The fluid flows between two parallel plates separated by a distance of **5 mm**, with the upper plate moving at **0.25 m/s**. Calculate:
(i) The shear stress at the plate surface
(ii) The shear rate at the plate surface
(iii) Whether the fluid will yield (i.e., flow) at the lower plate

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Non-Newtonian Fluids:**

Non-Newtonian fluids do **not** obey Newton's law of viscosity. Their apparent viscosity changes with applied shear rate.

**Classification:**
1. Time-independent: Pseudoplastic, Dilatant, Bingham plastic, Herschel-Bulkley
2. Time-dependent: Thixotropic, Rheopectic
3. Viscoelastic: Combine viscous and elastic properties

**Bingham Plastic Model:** This fluid behaves as a rigid solid until the applied stress exceeds a threshold **yield stress ($\tau_0$)**, beyond which it flows with linear viscous behaviour:

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\mu_p$ is the **plastic viscosity**. Examples: drilling mud, toothpaste, sewage sludge.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $\tau_0 = 15$ Pa, $\mu_p = 0.20$ Pa·s, $h = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $U = 0.25$ m/s.

**Step 1:** Velocity gradient

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.25}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 (i):** Shear stress at the plate

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy} = 15 + (0.20)(50) = 15 + 10 = 25 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 (ii):** Shear rate at the plate

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (iii):** Check for yielding

For the fluid to flow, $\tau > \tau_0$. We compare $\tau$ at the moving plate (25 Pa) with the yield stress (15 Pa):

$$\tau = 25 \text{ Pa} > \tau_0 = 15 \text{ Pa} \quad \checkmark$$

Hence, **the fluid will yield and flow** between the plates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** Many students write $h = 5$ mm without converting to metres. The velocity gradient $U/h$ will be off by a factor of 1000. Always convert mm → m before substituting.
> 2. **Y-axis intercept confusion:** In Bingham plots, students frequently plot the line through the origin. The line must start at $(0, \tau_0)$, not $(0, 0)$.
> 3. **Yield check:** A common error is to conclude "flow occurs because stress > yield stress" without explicitly computing both numerical values side-by-side.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Stress** in a fluid is force per unit area on an internal surface, classified as **normal stress (pressure + viscous)** and **shear stress (viscous only)**.
- [x] **Rate of strain** (not strain itself) is the relevant kinematic quantity in fluid mechanics because fluids flow continuously.
- [x] **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — linear, with $\mu$ constant for Newtonian fluids.
- [x] **Dynamic viscosity μ** has units **Pa·s**; **kinematic viscosity ν** has units **m²/s**; they are related by $\nu = \mu / \rho$.
- [x] **Cauchy stress decomposition:** $\sigma_{ij} = -p\delta_{ij} + \tau_{ij}$ separates pressure from viscous contribution.
- [x] **Stokes' constitutive equation** for Newtonian fluid: $\tau_{ij} = \mu\left(2\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} - \dfrac{2}{3}\delta_{ij}\nabla\cdot\vec{V}\right)$.
- [x] **Newtonian fluids**: water, air, mercury, glycerin, light oils.
- [x] **Non-Newtonian fluids**: blood (pseudoplastic), starch suspension (dilatant), toothpaste (Bingham), yogurt (thixotropic).
- [x] **Viscosity of liquids** **decreases** with temperature; **viscosity of gases** **increases** with temperature.
- [x] **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V L / \mu = V L / \nu$ determines laminar vs. turbulent flow.
- [x] **Bingham plastic** is the most important non-Newtonian model for engineering applications (drilling mud, concrete, slurries).
- [x] **Sutherland's law** gives temperature-dependent gas viscosity: $\mu = \mu_0 (T/T_0)^{3/2} (T_0 + S)/(T + S)$.
- [x] **Couette flow** between parallel plates is the standard setup for measuring viscosity experimentally.
- [x] Always check **dimensional consistency**: $\mu$ in Pa·s, $\nu$ in m²/s, $\tau$ in Pa, $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bulk Modulus # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bulk Modulus (K)**: It is defined as the ratio of the change in pressure (intensity of compressive stress) to the volumetric strain produced, when a fluid is subjected to a small change in pressure under specified thermodynamic conditions. Mathematically, > > $$K = - \frac{dP}{\left(\frac{dV}{V}\right)} = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$$ The negative sign is introduced because an **increase in pressure** always produces a **decrease in volume**, keeping the value of $K$ positive for all real fluids. Bulk Modulus is a direct measure of a fluid's **resistance to compression**. > [!NOTE] > **Volumetric Strain**: The ratio of change in volume ($\Delta V$) to the original volume ($V$) of the fluid element. It is a dimensionless quantity, hence $K$ has the same units as pressure, i.e., **N/m²** or **Pa**. ## 1.2 Two Thermodynamic Variants of Bulk Modulus | Variant | Condition | Symbol | |---|---|---| | **Isothermal Bulk Modulus** | Temperature held constant ($T = \text{const.}$) | $K_T$ | | **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Bulk Modulus** | No heat transfer, entropy constant ($s = \text{const.}$) | $K_s$ | For **liquids**, both values are practically equal because temperature changes during rapid compression are negligible over short time intervals. For **gases**, they differ significantly and are related by: $$K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$$ where $\gamma$ is the **ratio of specific heats** ($C_p / C_v$). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge vs. Steel Ball" Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of a balloon filled with water versus a balloon filled with air.** When you press on the water-filled balloon (sealed), the water barely shrinks — water has a **high bulk modulus**, making it nearly incompressible. Press on the air-filled balloon, and the air compresses dramatically — air has a **low bulk modulus** and is highly compressible. Bulk Modulus is simply the numerical "stiffness" of a fluid against uniform squeezing pressure. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard reference values (at 20 °C, 1 atm):** > - **Water:** $K \approx 2.1 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ (≈ 2100 MPa) > - **Air (Isothermal):** $K_T = P = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$ > - **Air (Adiabatic):** $K_s = \gamma P = 1.4 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 1.418 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$ > - **Mercury:** $K \approx 2.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^2$ > - **Hydraulic Oil:** $K \approx 1.7 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ Notice that water is roughly **10,000 times** stiffer than air — this is why liquids are treated as **incompressible** in most hydraulic engineering problems, while gases require careful compressibility analysis. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Bulk Modulus — Engineering Necessity In real engineering systems (hydraulic presses, water-hammer pipelines, sonar transducers, fuel injection systems), fluids are routinely subjected to rapid pressure fluctuations. The compressibility — captured by $K$ — governs: - **Speed of pressure waves** (speed of sound): $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ - **Energy storage** in hydraulic accumulators - **Water hammer / surge pressures** in pipelines - **Cavitation onset** predictions - **Performance of hydraulic systems** at high pressures ## 2.2 Compressibility — The Inverse Concept > [!NOTE] > **Coefficient of Compressibility ($\beta$)** is the **reciprocal** of Bulk Modulus: > > $$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dP} \quad \text{[units: m}^2/\text{N]}$$ A **high $\beta$** means the fluid compresses easily (gases); a **low $\beta$** means the fluid resists compression (liquids). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $K = -\dfrac{dP}{(dV/V)}$ | **General definition** of Bulk Modulus | | 2 | $K = -V\dfrac{dP}{dV}$ | Alternative differential form | | 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | Compressibility coefficient | | 4 | $K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$ | Relation between adiabatic and isothermal $K$ | | 5 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{\rho}}$ | **Speed of sound** in fluid (adiabatic) | | 6 | $K = \dfrac{\rho \cdot c^2}{\gamma}$ | Rearranged for gas systems | | 7 | $\rho = \dfrac{\rho_0}{1 - (P-P_0)/K}$ | Density change under isothermal compression (liquids) | | 8 | $\Delta V = -\dfrac{V_0 \cdot \Delta P}{K}$ | Finite change in volume (small strains) | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL TABLE RULE (KTU Exam):** When writing the Bulk Modulus on the right-hand side as an absolute value or magnitude, **never** use the vertical pipe `|` inside your answer's equation. Use $\vert K \vert$ or $\lvert K \rvert$ in handwritten/LaTeX form to avoid losing marks in board evaluation for ambiguous notation. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Bulk Modulus | |---|---| | **Hydraulic Machinery** | Designing hydraulic presses, jacks, and brakes — fluid "stiffness" affects response. | | **Sonar / Underwater Acoustics** | Speed of sound in seawater depends directly on $K$. | | **Pipeline Engineering** | Water-hammer pressure rise: $\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta V$ uses $K$ in $c$. | | **Fuel Injection (Diesel)** | High-pressure injection relies on fuel bulk modulus for pressure wave transmission. | | **Dam Engineering** | Reservoir water compressibility affects settlement and pore-pressure analysis. | | **Aviation / Gas Dynamics** | Compressible flow in jet engines, rocket propulsion uses $K_s$. | ## 2.5 Step-by-Step Logic — How to Apply Bulk Modulus 1. **Identify the fluid** (water, oil, air) → look up or compute its $K$. 2. **Determine the process type** (isothermal vs. adiabatic) → choose $K_T$ or $K_s$. 3. **Apply the formula** $K = -V(dP/dV)$ for differential problems, or $K \approx -V_0 \Delta P / \Delta V$ for finite changes. 4. **Compute derived quantities** (speed of sound, pressure wave speed, compressibility). 5. **Validate physically**: positive $K$, sensible density change, correct units. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1 — From Stress-Strain to Bulk Modulus Starting from the **definition of volumetric strain** in a fluid element: $$\varepsilon_v = \frac{dV}{V}$$ By the **constitutive relation for volumetric deformation** in a fluid under hydrostatic pressure $P$: $$P = -K \cdot \varepsilon_v \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K = -\frac{P}{\varepsilon_v} = -\frac{dP}{dV/V}$$ Rearranging, we get the **canonical form**: $$\boxed{K = -V \frac{dP}{dV}}$$ For **finite (small) pressure changes**, integrating under the assumption of constant $K$: $$K = -V \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Delta V = -\frac{V \cdot \Delta P}{K}$$ This gives the **volume change** of a fluid sample under a pressure increment. ## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Relationship Between $K_s$ and $K_T$ for an Ideal Gas Consider an ideal gas undergoing a **reversible process**. **Step 1 — Isothermal Process ($T = \text{const.}$):** By the ideal gas law: $PV = mRT = \text{const.}$ Differentiating: $$P \cdot dV + V \cdot dP = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{dV} = -\frac{P}{V}$$ Substituting into the definition of $K$: $$K_T = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = -V \cdot \left(-\frac{P}{V}\right) = P$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{K_T = P}$$ **Step 2 — Adiabatic Process ($PV^\gamma = \text{const.}$):** Differentiating: $$\gamma P V^{\gamma-1} dV + V^\gamma dP = 0$$ Dividing throughout by $V^{\gamma-1}$: $$\gamma P \, dV + V \, dP = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{dV} = -\frac{\gamma P}{V}$$ Substituting into the definition of $K$: $$K_s = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = -V \cdot \left(-\frac{\gamma P}{V}\right) = \gamma P$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{K_s = \gamma P}$$ **Step 3 — Establishing the Ratio:** $$\frac{K_s}{K_T} = \frac{\gamma P}{P} = \gamma$$ Hence the relationship: $$K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$$ > [!NOTE] > For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), the adiabatic bulk modulus is **40% larger** than the isothermal value. This is why high-frequency phenomena (sound waves, explosions) always use $K_s$, while slow thermodynamic processes use $K_T$. ## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Bulk Modulus from Density Variation Since mass is conserved: $m = \rho V = \text{const.}$ Differentiating: $$\rho \cdot dV + V \cdot d\rho = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad dV = -\frac{V}{\rho} d\rho$$ Substituting into $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$: $$K = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{-\dfrac{V}{\rho} d\rho} = \rho \cdot \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$ $$\boxed{K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}}$$ This is the **density-based form** of Bulk Modulus, which is widely used in compressible flow and acoustics. ## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Speed of Sound in a Fluid (Newton-Laplace Equation) For a small pressure disturbance propagating through a fluid, the **speed of sound** is: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho}}$$ **Justification (Riemann's argument):** Consider a fluid column of length $L$ and cross-section $A$. A pressure pulse compresses the column by an amount $\Delta L$, so: $$\Delta L = \frac{\Delta P \cdot L}{K_s}$$ The **mass** of fluid that gets pushed into the new compressed region corresponds to a velocity $V_p$ over a time $\Delta t = L/c$, so by continuity and Newton's second law on a control volume, the resulting equation of motion yields $c^2 = K_s / \rho$. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) > **Problem:** A volume of $V_0 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$ of oil is subjected to a pressure rise of $\Delta P = 7 \text{ MPa}$. If the bulk modulus of the oil is $K = 1.7 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, find: > (a) The decrease in volume. > (b) The new density if initial density is $880 \text{ kg/m}^3$. > (c) The compressibility of the oil. **Solution:** **(a) Decrease in volume:** $$\Delta V = -\frac{V_0 \cdot \Delta P}{K} = -\frac{0.05 \times 7 \times 10^6}{1.7 \times 10^9}$$ $$\Delta V = -\frac{3.5 \times 10^5}{1.7 \times 10^9} = -2.0588 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\Delta V \approx -2.06 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \quad (\text{i.e., a decrease})}$$ **(b) New density:** Since mass is conserved, $m = \rho_0 V_0 = 880 \times 0.05 = 44 \text{ kg}$. $$\rho_{\text{new}} = \frac{m}{V_0 + \Delta V} = \frac{44}{0.05 - 2.0588 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{44}{0.0497941}$$ $$\rho_{\text{new}} \approx 883.64 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\Delta \rho \approx +3.64 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ **(c) Compressibility:** $$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{1.7 \times 10^9} = 5.882 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{N}$$ $$\boxed{\beta \approx 5.88 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{N}}$$ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fluids | Fluid | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $K$ (N/m²) | $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ (m/s) | $\beta$ (m²/N) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Water (20 °C) | 1000 | $2.1 \times 10^9$ | ≈ 1449 | $4.76 \times 10^{-10}$ | | Mercury | 13,600 | $2.7 \times 10^{10}$ | ≈ 1409 | $3.70 \times 10^{-11}$ | | Hydraulic oil | 880 | $1.7 \times 10^9$ | ≈ 1390 | $5.88 \times 10^{-10}$ | | Air (isothermal) | 1.225 | $1.013 \times 10^5$ | ≈ 287 | $9.87 \times 10^{-6}$ | | Air (adiabatic) | 1.225 | $1.418 \times 10^5$ | ≈ 340 | $7.05 \times 10^{-6}$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bulk Modulus Concepts ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FLUID ELEMENT
Initial Volume V] --> B[Apply Pressure dP] B --> C[Volume Changes by dV] C --> D{Process Type?} D -->|Isothermal| E[Constant Temperature T] D -->|Adiabatic| F[No Heat Transfer] E --> G[K_T = P] F --> H[K_s = gamma x P] G --> I[Compute Beta = 1 divide K] H --> I I --> J[Derive Speed of Sound c = sqrt K by rho] J --> K[Engineering Applications] K --> L1[Hydraulic Systems] K --> L2[Water Hammer Analysis] K --> L3[Sonar and Acoustics] K --> L4[Pipeline Surge Studies] K --> L5[Fuel Injection Systems] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Map of Related Fluid Properties ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Pressure Change dP] --> S[Volumetric Strain dV/V] S --> K[Bulk Modulus K] P --> S K --> BETA[Compressibility Beta] BETA --> BETA K --> C[Speed of Sound c] K --> RHO[Density Variation] RHO --> C C --> APP[Acoustic and Wave Applications] BETA --> APP2[Storage and Compression Systems] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Description — Hydrostatic Compression of a Fluid Element > [!NOTE] > **Physical Picture (described in text for clarity):** > Imagine a sealed cube of fluid with initial side length $L$ and volume $V = L^3$. The cube is submerged in a hydraulic chamber where hydrostatic pressure is increased by $\Delta P$. All six faces experience the same inward pressure rise. The cube deforms uniformly to a new volume $V' = V + \Delta V$, with $\Delta V < 0$ (volume shrinks). The **volumetric strain** is $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V / V$ (negative). The **Bulk Modulus** is then computed as $K = -\Delta P / \varepsilon_v$, which is positive. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Choosing $K_T$ vs. $K_s$ ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Is the process slow or fast?] --> Q2{Time scale?} Q2 -->|Slow quasi-static| Q3[Use Isothermal K_T = P] Q2 -->|Rapid or high frequency| Q4[Use Adiabatic K_s = gamma x P] Q3 --> R1[Thermodynamic, hydraulic accumulator design] Q4 --> R2[Acoustic waves, water hammer, explosions] Q1 --> Q5{Fluid type?} Q5 -->|Liquid| R3[K_T approx equal to K_s, use K from tables] Q5 -->|Gas| Q2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** Define Bulk Modulus. What is the physical significance of the negative sign in its definition? **Model Answer:** > Bulk Modulus ($K$) of a fluid is defined as the ratio of the change in pressure to the volumetric strain produced, given by > > $$K = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$$ > > The **negative sign** ensures that the Bulk Modulus remains a **positive quantity**, because an increase in pressure ($dP > 0$) always produces a decrease in volume ($dV < 0$), making the ratio $-dP/(dV/V)$ positive.
