Physically, it represents the fluid's **resistance to uniform compression**. **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark definition, 1 mark equation, 1 mark explanation of negative sign]** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** State the relationship between isothermal and adiabatic bulk moduli. Why is $K_s$ used in the speed of sound equation? **Model Answer:** > The relationship is > > $$K_s = \gamma \cdot K_T$$ > > where $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ is the ratio of specific heats. The **speed of sound** involves $K_s$ because sound waves are **rapid, high-frequency disturbances** that occur too quickly for heat exchange with the surroundings — the process is essentially **adiabatic (isentropic)**, not isothermal. > > $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho}}$$ **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark relation, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark justification for $K_s$]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between the isothermal bulk modulus ($K_T$) and the adiabatic bulk modulus ($K_s$) for an ideal gas, clearly stating all thermodynamic assumptions. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A hydraulic system contains $0.08 \text{ m}^3$ of oil with bulk modulus $K = 1.8 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ and density $\rho = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$. When the system pressure rises suddenly by $5.5 \text{ MPa}$, calculate: - (i) The change in volume of the oil. - (ii) The new density of the oil. - (iii) The speed of sound in the oil at this condition. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution (Question A):** **(a) Derivation of $K_s = \gamma K_T$:** We start with the ideal gas equation: $PV = mRT$. **For an isothermal process** ($T = \text{const.}$), differentiating $PV = \text{const.}$: $$P \, dV + V \, dP = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{dV} = -\frac{P}{V}$$ Therefore: $$K_T = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = -V \cdot \left(-\frac{P}{V}\right) = P \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{K_T = P}$$ **For an adiabatic process** ($PV^\gamma = \text{const.}$), differentiating: $$\gamma P V^{\gamma - 1} dV + V^\gamma dP = 0$$ Dividing by $V^{\gamma - 1}$: $$\gamma P \, dV + V \, dP = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{dV} = -\frac{\gamma P}{V}$$ Therefore: $$K_s = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = \gamma P \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{K_s = \gamma P}$$ **Taking the ratio:** $$\frac{K_s}{K_T} = \frac{\gamma P}{P} = \gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{K_s = \gamma K_T}$$ **Assumptions:** - Ideal gas behavior ($PV = mRT$). - Reversible processes. - Constant specific heats (constant $\gamma$). - Negligible change in $\gamma$ over the pressure range. **(b) Numerical solution:** **Given:** $V_0 = 0.08 \text{ m}^3$, $K = 1.8 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $\rho_0 = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\Delta P = 5.5 \text{ MPa} = 5.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$. **(i) Change in volume:** $$\Delta V = -\frac{V_0 \cdot \Delta P}{K} = -\frac{0.08 \times 5.5 \times 10^6}{1.8 \times 10^9}$$ $$\Delta V = -\frac{4.4 \times 10^5}{1.8 \times 10^9} = -2.444 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\Delta V \approx -2.44 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \text{ (decrease)}}$$ **(ii) New density:** Mass is conserved: $m = \rho_0 V_0 = 870 \times 0.08 = 69.6 \text{ kg}$. $$V_{\text{new}} = V_0 + \Delta V = 0.08 - 2.444 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0797556 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\rho_{\text{new}} = \frac{m}{V_{\text{new}}} = \frac{69.6}{0.0797556} = 872.66 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\rho_{\text{new}} \approx 872.66 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ **(iii) Speed of sound:** $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho_{\text{new}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.8 \times 10^9}{872.66}} = \sqrt{2.0627 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{c \approx 1436.2 \text{ m/s}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the concept of compressibility and bulk modulus. Show that $K = \rho (dP/d\rho)$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** At a depth of 5 km in the ocean, the pressure is approximately $50 \text{ MPa}$. If seawater has a bulk modulus of $K = 2.3 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ and density $\rho_0 = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at the surface, determine the density of seawater at this depth. Also find the velocity of sound at this depth. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution (Question B):** **(a) Concept of compressibility and bulk modulus derivation:** **Compressibility ($\beta$)** is defined as the fractional change in volume per unit change in pressure: $$\beta = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dP} \quad \text{[units: m}^2/\text{N]}$$ **Bulk Modulus ($K$)** is its reciprocal: $$K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -V\frac{dP}{dV}$$ **Derivation of $K = \rho (dP/d\rho)$:** Since the mass of the fluid element is conserved: $$m = \rho V = \text{const.}$$ Differentiating both sides: $$\rho \, dV + V \, d\rho = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad dV = -\frac{V}{\rho} d\rho$$ Substituting this into $K = -V(dP/dV)$: $$K = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{-\dfrac{V}{\rho} d\rho} = \rho \cdot \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$ $$\boxed{K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}}$$ **(b) Numerical solution:** **Given:** $P_0 \approx 0.1013 \text{ MPa}$ (surface), $P = 50 \text{ MPa}$, $K = 2.3 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $\rho_0 = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **Using $K = \rho \, (dP/d\rho)$ and assuming $K$ is constant over the pressure range:** $$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{dP}{K}$$ Integrating from surface to depth: $$\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{K}$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\rho}{\rho_0}\right) = \frac{P - P_0}{K} = \frac{(50 - 0.1013) \times 10^6}{2.3 \times 10^9}$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\rho}{\rho_0}\right) = \frac{49.899 \times 10^6}{2.3 \times 10^9} = 0.021695$$ $$\frac{\rho}{\rho_0} = e^{0.021695} = 1.02193$$ $$\rho = 1025 \times 1.02193 = 1047.48 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\rho \approx 1047.5 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ **Velocity of sound:** $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.3 \times 10^9}{1047.48}} = \sqrt{2.196 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{c \approx 1481.9 \text{ m/s}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Bulk Modulus Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $K = -V(dP/dV)$: This yields a negative $K$ and **loses 1 full mark** in the definition step. > 2. **Confusing $K_T$ with $K_s$**: For sound/rapid processes, you MUST use $K_s = \gamma P$, not $K_T = P$. Wrong choice = **–2 marks**. > 3. **Unit mismatch**: Bulk Modulus in GPa vs. MPa vs. N/m². Always convert to a common unit (N/m²) before computation. **1 mark penalty** for not converting. > 4. **Not conserving mass** in density-update problems: Use $m = \rho_0 V_0 = \rho_{\text{new}} V_{\text{new}}$, NOT the approximate formula $\Delta \rho \approx \rho_0 \Delta P / K$ unless explicitly told. **–2 marks** for the wrong formula. > 5. **Pipes in equations**: Writing $\lvert K \rvert$ or $\vert K \vert$ in your answer sheet (when magnitudes are needed) without parentheses causes valuation ambiguity. **0.5 mark deduction** for notational sloppiness. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Bulk Modulus** > > - **Definition:** $K = -V (dP/dV)$ — the negative sign keeps $K$ positive. > - **Units:** $K$ has units of pressure (Pa, N/m², MPa, GPa). $\beta = 1/K$ has units of m²/N. > - **For an ideal gas:** $K_T = P$ and $K_s = \gamma P$. Always state the process! > - **Speed of sound:** $c = \sqrt{K_s / \rho}$ — uses the **adiabatic** Bulk Modulus. > - **Density form:** $K = \rho \, (dP/d\rho)$ — useful in compressible flow problems. > - **Volume change:** $\Delta V = -V_0 \, \Delta P / K$ — valid for small $\Delta P$ and constant $K$. > - **Mass conservation:** $m = \rho V = \text{const.}$ — always use this to find new density. > - **Liquids:** $K$ is very large (water ≈ 2.1 GPa), so liquids are **nearly incompressible**. > - **Gases:** $K$ is small (air ≈ 0.1 MPa) and process-dependent. > - **Engineering use:** Hydraulic systems, water hammer, sonar, fuel injection, pipeline surge. > - **KTU pitfall:** Forgot the negative sign → lost mark. Wrong process ($T$ vs. $s$) → lost marks. Unit mismatch → lost mark. > - **Tip for exams:** Always start by **identifying the process** (isothermal/adiabatic), then **list given data with units**, then **substitute** into the appropriate formula.

topics.title: Specific Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specific Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Specific Weight** (also known as **weight density** or **specific gravity weight**) is defined as the **weight of a fluid per unit volume**. It quantifies the heaviness of a fluid system under the influence of gravitational acceleration.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{mg}{V} = \rho \, g$$

Where:
- $\gamma$ = Specific Weight of the fluid
- $W$ = Weight of the fluid (in **N**)
- $V$ = Volume of the fluid (in **m³**)
- $m$ = Mass of the fluid (in **kg**)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (in **kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (in **m/s²**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Specific weight is a **fundamental fluid property** that bridges the gap between mass-based properties ($\rho$) and force-based properties (weight). It is one of the most frequently used properties in fluid statics, manometry, and buoyancy problems.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two identical buckets — one filled with **water** and the other with **mercury**. Even though both buckets have the **same volume**, the mercury bucket is **much heavier** to lift. Why? Because the *weight packed into every cubic meter* is different. That "weight per unit volume" is exactly what **specific weight** ($\gamma$) measures.

- **Water**: ~9810 N fits in 1 m³
- **Mercury**: ~133,100 N fits in the same 1 m³

So mercury has a much higher specific weight — you feel the difference when carrying it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Density ($\rho$)** = mass per unit volume → "How much stuff is in there?"
> - **Specific Weight ($\gamma$)** = weight per unit volume → "How heavy does each unit of volume feel on Earth?"
> They differ by a factor of $g$ ($9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ on Earth's surface).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) | **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Specific weight of water at 4°C | **9810 N/m³** (or 9.81 kN/m³) | N/m³ |
| Density of water at 4°C | **1000 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Standard sea-level air | **11.8 N/m³** | N/m³ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Underlying Logical Structure

The specific weight emerges naturally from Newtonian mechanics when we consider a fluid parcel under gravity. The derivation follows a clear causal chain:

- **Step 1 — Mass Foundation:** Every fluid parcel of volume $V$ contains a mass $m$ determined by its density: $m = \rho V$.
- **Step 2 — Gravitational Force Application:** Under Earth's gravity, this mass experiences a downward force equal to its weight: $W = mg$.
- **Step 3 — Normalization by Volume:** To get a *property* (intensive quantity independent of sample size), we divide weight by volume: $\gamma = W/V$.
- **Step 4 — Combining Steps:** Substituting Step 1 into Step 2 and then into Step 3 gives: $\gamma = \rho g$.

**The "Why" Behind It:**
- Density alone cannot predict how fluids behave under gravity-based phenomena (hydrostatic pressure, buoyancy, manometer readings). Specific weight, which already *contains* $g$, is the natural property for such problems.
- **The "How":** Whenever you see a fluid in a gravitational field producing pressure or force, $\gamma$ is the operative property — not $\rho$.

### Variation with Physical Conditions

- **Temperature:** As temperature rises, fluids (especially gases, but also liquids) expand → density drops → specific weight decreases.
- **Pressure:** For **liquids** (incompressible), $\rho$ and $\gamma$ are nearly constant with pressure. For **gases** (compressible), they vary significantly with pressure.
- **Location:** Since $g$ varies slightly with latitude and altitude (≈ 9.78 to 9.83 m/s² on Earth), $\gamma$ also varies — though for most engineering problems at KTU level, we use **g = 9.81 m/s²** as a constant.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight from density and gravity | N/m³ |
| 2 | $\gamma = W/V$ | Specific weight from weight and volume | N/m³ |
| 3 | $\rho = \gamma / g$ | Density from specific weight | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $W = \gamma V$ | Total weight from specific weight and volume | N |
| 5 | $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$ | Standard value for water at 4°C | N/m³ |
| 6 | $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ | Standard gravity (KTU convention) | m/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Specific weight is the **master property** in the hydrostatic pressure equation $P = \gamma h$, which underpins the design of dams, water tanks, barometers, manometers, and submarine hulls. Mastery of $\gamma$ is non-negotiable for KTU fluid mechanics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: From First Principles — Deriving $\gamma = \rho g$

**Starting Point:** Consider a fluid sample of volume $V$ resting on a weighing scale under Earth's gravity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Mass of the fluid sample} \quad &\rightarrow \quad m = \rho \, V \\
\text{Step 2: Weight of the fluid sample} \quad &\rightarrow \quad W = m \, g \\
\text{Step 3: Substitute Step 1 into Step 2} \quad &\rightarrow \quad W = (\rho V) \, g \\
\text{Step 4: Solve for specific weight} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{(\rho V) g}{V} \\
\text{Step 5: Cancel common V} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \boxed{\gamma = \rho \, g}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the fundamental relationship.

---

### Derivation 2: Deriving the Specific Weight of Water at Standard Conditions

We start with the known density of water at **4 °C**: $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and standard gravity $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{water} &= \rho_{water} \times g \\
&= 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2 \\
&= 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the value **memorized** for KTU problems unless otherwise specified.

---

### Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A tank contains **0.5 m³** of an oil with a mass of **440 kg**. Calculate the specific weight, density, and total weight of the oil. Take $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

**Given:**
- Volume: $V = 0.5 \, \text{m}^3$
- Mass: $m = 440 \, \text{kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Density:} \quad \rho &= \frac{m}{V} = \frac{440 \, \text{kg}}{0.5 \, \text{m}^3} \\
\rho &= 880 \, \text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(b) Specific Weight:} \quad \gamma &= \rho \, g = 880 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2 \\
\gamma &= 8632.8 \, \text{N/m}^3 \approx 8.63 \, \text{kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(c) Total Weight:} \quad W &= m \, g = 440 \, \text{kg} \times 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2 \\
W &= 4316.4 \, \text{N} \approx 4.32 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using $W = \gamma V$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \gamma \times V = 8632.8 \, \text{N/m}^3 \times 0.5 \, \text{m}^3 \\
W &= 4316.4 \, \text{N} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods agree — confirming the consistency of the derived relations.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Specific Weight of Common Fluids (at 20 °C, 1 atm)

| Fluid | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Specific Weight $\gamma$ (N/m³) | State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water (4 °C) | 1000 | 9810 | Liquid |
| Sea Water | 1025 | 10055 | Liquid |
| Mercury | 13,600 | 133,416 | Liquid |
| Air (STP) | 1.204 | 11.81 | Gas |
| Glycerin | 1260 | 12,360 | Liquid |
| Engine Oil | 917 | 8996 | Liquid |
| Ethyl Alcohol | 789 | 7740 | Liquid |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Conceptual Map of Specific Weight in the Fluid Property Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID PROPERTIES]:::root --> B[INTENSIVE PROPERTIES]
    A --> C[EXTENSIVE PROPERTIES]
    B --> D[MASS-BASED rho]
    B --> E[WEIGHT-BASED gamma]
    C --> F[Mass m kg]
    C --> G[Weight W N]
    C --> H[Volume V m3]
    D --> D1[rho equals m per V]
    E --> E1[gamma equals W per V]
    D1 --> I[gamma equals rho times g]
    H --> I
    D --> I
    E1 --> J[Applications P equals gamma h]
    J --> J1[Hydrostatic Pressure]
    J --> J2[Manometry]
    J --> J3[Buoyancy Force]
    J --> J4[Dam Design]
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef default fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
```

### Diagram 2: Functional Flow — How Specific Weight is Computed in Engineering Practice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[STEP 1 IDENTIFY FLUID]:::step --> A2[STEP 2 READ DENSITY rho FROM TABLES OR MEASURE]
    A2 --> A3[STEP 3 APPLY g equals 9.81 m per s2]
    A3 --> A4[STEP 4 COMPUTE gamma equals rho times g]
    A4 --> A5[STEP 5 USE gamma IN HYDROSTATIC EQUATIONS]
    A5 --> A6[STEP 6 OBTAIN PRESSURE OR FORCE]
    A6 --> A7[STEP 7 DESIGN OR VERIFY ENGINEERING SYSTEM]:::end
    classDef step fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

### Diagram 3: Visual Analogy — Two Buckets, Same Volume, Different Weights

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[BUCKET 1 WATER]:::w --> C1[Volume equals 1 m3]
    B2[BUCKET 2 MERCURY]:::m --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Water mass equals 1000 kg]
    C1 --> D2[Mercury mass equals 13600 kg]
    D1 --> E1[Water gamma equals 9810 N per m3]
    D2 --> E2[Mercury gamma equals 133416 N per m3]
    E1 --> F1[FEELS LIGHT]
    E2 --> F2[FEELS VERY HEAVY]
    classDef w fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef m fill:#d1d5db,stroke:#374151,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Each node in the Mermaid diagrams above uses clean, alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., `A1`, `B1`) prefixed with letters to comply with Mermaid parser safeguards. Labels are pure uppercase text with no embedded markdown formatting, ensuring clean rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define Specific Weight. State its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
Specific weight is defined as the weight of a fluid per unit volume. It is denoted by the symbol $\gamma$ (gamma) and is expressed as:

$$
\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \rho \, g
$$

Where $\rho$ is the mass density and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity.
**SI Unit:** N/m³ (Newton per cubic meter).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**The specific weight of a certain liquid is 7.85 kN/m³. Find its density. Take $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= 7.85 \, \text{kN/m}^3 = 7850 \, \text{N/m}^3 \\
\rho &= \frac{\gamma}{g} = \frac{7850 \, \text{N/m}^3}{9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2} \\
\rho &= 800.20 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \approx 800 \, \text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** Define the terms: Specific Weight, Mass Density, and Specific Gravity. Explain the relationship between them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Mass Density ($\rho$):** The mass of a fluid per unit volume. $\rho = m/V$. Unit: kg/m³.
- **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** The weight of a fluid per unit volume. $\gamma = W/V = \rho g$. Unit: N/m³.
- **Specific Gravity (S):** The ratio of the specific weight (or density) of a fluid to that of water at a standard reference temperature (usually 4 °C). $S = \gamma_{fluid} / \gamma_{water} = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water}$. It is dimensionless.

**Relationship:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \rho \, g \\
S &= \frac{\gamma_{fluid}}{\gamma_{water}} = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}} \\
\rho_{fluid} &= S \times \rho_{water} = S \times 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \\
\gamma_{fluid} &= S \times \gamma_{water} = S \times 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** A vessel contains 0.6 m³ of glycerin, which has a specific gravity of 1.26. Determine: **(i)** Specific weight, **(ii)** Mass, **(iii)** Weight of glycerin. Take $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:** $V = 0.6 \, \text{m}^3$, $S = 1.26$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Specific Weight:} \quad \gamma &= S \times \gamma_{water} = 1.26 \times 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3 \\
\gamma &= 12,360.6 \, \text{N/m}^3 \approx 12.36 \, \text{kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii) Mass:} \quad \rho &= S \times \rho_{water} = 1.26 \times 1000 = 1260 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \\
m &= \rho \times V = 1260 \times 0.6 = 756 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(iii) Weight:} \quad W &= \gamma \times V = 12,360.6 \times 0.6 \\
W &= 7416.36 \, \text{N} \approx 7.42 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for specific weight ($\gamma$) starting from its fundamental definition. Explain how it differs from mass density. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting with a fluid of mass $m$ and volume $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad m &= \rho V \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad W &= mg \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad W &= (\rho V)g \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad \gamma &= \frac{W}{V} = \frac{\rho V g}{V} = \rho g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Difference from mass density:**
- $\rho$ depends only on the fluid's composition, temperature, and pressure.
- $\gamma$ depends on $\rho$ *and* the local gravitational field $g$.
- Therefore, $\gamma$ varies slightly with location on Earth, while $\rho$ does not (in a fixed thermodynamic state).
- $\rho$ is a "mass property"; $\gamma$ is a "force property" (already includes gravity).

**(b)** The specific weight of sea water is $10,050 \, \text{N/m}^3$ at a certain location. If $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, find: **(i)** Mass density, **(ii)** Specific gravity, **(iii)** Weight of 2.5 m³ of this sea water. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:** $\gamma_{sw} = 10,050 \, \text{N/m}^3$, $V = 2.5 \, \text{m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Mass Density:} \quad \rho_{sw} &= \frac{\gamma_{sw}}{g} = \frac{10,050}{9.81} \\
\rho_{sw} &\approx 1024.46 \, \text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii) Specific Gravity:} \quad S &= \frac{\gamma_{sw}}{\gamma_{water}} = \frac{10,050}{9810} \\
S &\approx 1.0244
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(iii) Weight of 2.5 m}^3\text{:} \quad W &= \gamma_{sw} \times V = 10,050 \times 2.5 \\
W &= 25,125 \, \text{N} \approx 25.13 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Specific weight is often given in kN/m³ in problem statements; convert to N/m³ before substituting into formulas.
> 2. **Confusing $\rho$ and $\gamma$:** Students frequently use density where specific weight is required (especially in the $P = \gamma h$ formula). **Always check what the question asks for.**
> 3. **Mixing specific gravity with specific weight:** Specific gravity is **dimensionless**; specific weight has units of N/m³. Do NOT use $S$ in place of $\gamma$.
> 4. **Using $g = 9.8$ or $g = 10$ inconsistently:** Stick to $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ unless the problem specifies otherwise — this is the KTU convention.
> 5. **Rounding too early:** Carry at least 3 significant figures throughout the calculation to avoid losing marks for a wrong final answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition (Must Memorize):** Specific weight $\gamma$ = weight per unit volume = $\rho g$.
- **Standard Value (Must Memorize):** $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3 = 9.81 \, \text{kN/m}^3$ at 4 °C.
- **Standard Gravity (KTU Convention):** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.
- **SI Unit:** N/m³. **FPS Unit:** lb/ft³.
- **Key Conversion:** $\rho = \gamma / g$ and $W = \gamma V$.
- **Temperature Effect:** $\gamma$ decreases as temperature increases (for most fluids).
- **Pressure Effect:** Negligible for liquids, significant for gases.
- **Specific Gravity Connection:** $S = \gamma_{fluid} / \gamma_{water} = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water}$ — **dimensionless**.
- **Application Anchor:** Specific weight is the **direct input** to the hydrostatic pressure equation $P = \gamma h$ — its mastery unlocks all of fluid statics.
- **Distinguish Carefully:** $\rho$ (mass per volume) vs. $\gamma$ (weight per volume) — they are **not the same**; they differ by factor $g$.
- **Master the Triangle:** $\gamma \leftrightarrow \rho$ (via $g$), $\gamma \leftrightarrow W$ (via $V$), $\rho \leftrightarrow m$ (via $V$).
- **Common Exam Trap:** When given a fluid's specific weight and asked for its density, **always divide by $g$**, not multiply.
- **Quick Sanity Check:** Water's specific weight (9810 N/m³) should feel "familiar" — if your numerical answer is wildly different (e.g., 98 N/m³ or 98,100 N/m³), re-check your arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relative Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Fundamentals of Fluid Properties

## 1. Relative Density (Specific Gravity)

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Relative Density (RD)** — also universally referred to as **Specific Gravity (SG)** — is a **dimensionless scalar quantity** that expresses the ratio between the **density of a given fluid substance** and the **density of a standard reference substance** under specified conditions of temperature and pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Relative Density (S) of a fluid is defined as the ratio of the density of the fluid ($\rho_{fluid}$) to the density of a standard fluid ($\rho_{standard}$).
>
> $$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{standard}}$$

The standard reference fluids conventionally adopted in KTU board examinations are:

| Fluid Type | Standard Reference Substance | Reference Density |
|------------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| **Liquids** | **Pure water at 4 °C** | **1000 kg/m³** |
| **Gases** | **Air at standard conditions (15 °C, 1 atm)** | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| **Heavy liquids** | **Mercury at 20 °C** | **13,600 kg/m³** |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Comparison Scale" of Fluids:**
> Imagine two identical glass jars sitting on a weighing scale. Jar A is filled with engine oil, and Jar B is filled with water. The scale shows the oil jar weighs **0.88 times** as much as the water jar. That "0.88" is precisely the **Relative Density of engine oil**.
>
> In plain English: *Relative Density tells you "how heavy a fluid is compared to an equal volume of water."*

**Why 4 °C water?**
At exactly **4 °C (277.15 K)**, pure water achieves its **maximum density** of **1000 kg/m³** (or **1 g/cm³**). This makes it the universal benchmark because it is:
- A reproducible, naturally occurring standard
- Easily measured in any laboratory
- Independent of geographic location

---

### 1.3 Physical Interpretation

- If **RD > 1** → The fluid is **denser (heavier)** than water (e.g., mercury, seawater, glycerol).
- If **RD < 1** → The fluid is **lighter** than water (e.g., petrol, kerosene, oil).
- If **RD = 1** → The fluid has the **same density** as water at 4 °C.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the Key Benchmark:** The Relative Density of **mercury = 13.6**. This single value appears in nearly every KTU numerical involving manometers and barometers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Theoretical Framework & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Concept

The fundamental RD relationship cascades into several derived fluid properties. The logical dependency is:

1. **Density ($\rho$)** → intrinsic mass per unit volume
2. **Specific Weight ($\gamma$)** → weight per unit volume
3. **Specific Volume ($v$)** → volume per unit mass
4. **Relative Density ($S$)** → density expressed as a ratio

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Board Examinations**

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Standard Reference |
|---|----------|---------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Relative Density** | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | **Dimensionless** | $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | **Density from RD** | $\rho_{fluid} = S \times \rho_{water}$ | kg/m³ | at 4 °C |
| 3 | **Specific Weight** | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g = S \cdot \gamma_{water}$ | N/m³ | $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ |
| 4 | **Specific Volume** | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{S \cdot \rho_{water}}$ | m³/kg | — |
| 5 | **RD of Mercury** | $S_{Hg} = 13.6$ | Dimensionless | at 20 °C |
| 6 | **RD in CGS units** | $S = \dfrac{\text{Specific gravity in g/cc}}{\text{Reference}}$ | — | $\rho_{water} = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$ |
| 7 | **Temperature correction** | $\rho_T = \dfrac{\rho_0}{1 + \beta (T - T_0)}$ | kg/m³ | $\beta$ = volumetric expansion |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Notation Trap:** Some textbooks use $\gamma_w$ for specific weight of water. In KTU answer scripts, **always write out full units** (N/m³) at least once to earn full methodology marks.

---

## 2.3 Density of Water at Various Temperatures

| Temperature (°C) | Density (kg/m³) | RD (using 4 °C as reference) |
|------------------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| **0** | 999.84 | 0.9998 |
| **4** | **1000.00** | **1.0000** (Maximum) |
| 20 | 998.20 | 0.9982 |
| 25 | 997.04 | 0.9970 |
| 100 | 958.40 | 0.9584 |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Relative Density

1. **Hydrometer Design:** Used by battery technicians, brewers, and petroleum engineers to instantly measure fluid concentration.
2. **Buoyancy Calculations:** Archimedes' Principle uses RD to predict whether an object will float.
3. **Manometry:** RD of mercury enables conversion of pressure head in mmHg to Pascal.
4. **Pipe Flow & Pump Selection:** Determines required pumping power ($\rho$ directly enters Bernoulli's equation).
5. **Shipbuilding & Naval Architecture:** RD of seawater (~1.025) directly affects buoyancy and deadweight tonnage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Derivations, Relationships & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Specific Weight from Relative Density

**Starting Point:** The specific weight of a fluid is defined as the weight per unit volume.

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{m \cdot g}{V}$$

Since density $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$, we substitute:

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

Now, applying the definition of Relative Density $\rho = S \cdot \rho_w$:

$$\gamma_{fluid} = (S \cdot \rho_w) \cdot g = S \cdot (\rho_w \cdot g)$$

Recognizing that $\gamma_w = \rho_w \cdot g$ is the specific weight of water:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{fluid} = S \cdot \gamma_w}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Volume from Relative Density

Specific volume $v$ is the reciprocal of density:

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho_{fluid}} = \frac{1}{S \cdot \rho_{water}}$$

Expressing this with units:

$$v = \frac{1}{S \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3} \quad \text{[in m}^3\text{/kg]}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstrations

### Numerical 1: Mass Computation from Relative Density

> **Problem:** A storage tank has a volume of **2.5 m³** filled completely with a fluid having Relative Density **0.85**. Compute the **(a)** mass and **(b)** weight of the fluid contained. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Given:**
- Volume $V = 2.5 \text{ m}^3$
- Relative Density $S = 0.85$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Solution:**

**(a) Mass of the fluid:**

Density of the fluid:
$$\rho_{fluid} = S \cdot \rho_{water}$$
$$\rho_{fluid} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Mass of fluid in the tank:
$$m = \rho_{fluid} \cdot V$$
$$m = 850 \times 2.5 = 2125 \text{ kg}$$

**(b) Weight of the fluid:**
$$W = m \cdot g$$
$$W = 2125 \times 9.81 = 20,846.25 \text{ N} \approx 20.85 \text{ kN}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** For 3-mark short problems, always state **Given Data → Formula → Substitution → Final Answer with Units** to secure full methodology marks.

---

### Numerical 2: Specific Weight from RD of Mercury

> **Problem:** Determine the specific weight and density of mercury at 20 °C, given $S_{Hg} = 13.6$.

**Solution:**

$$\rho_{Hg} = S_{Hg} \cdot \rho_w = 13.6 \times 1000 = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\gamma_{Hg} = S_{Hg} \cdot \gamma_w = 13.6 \times 9810$$
$$\gamma_{Hg} = 133{,}416 \text{ N/m}^3 = 133.416 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

This is why a 1-meter column of mercury exerts a pressure of **133.4 kPa** — a foundational result used in barometric problems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Fluid | Relative Density | Density (kg/m³) | Specific Weight (kN/m³) | Floatation Behavior in Water |
|-------|------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Air (at 15 °C) | 0.001225 | 1.225 | 0.0120 | Rises rapidly |
| Petrol | 0.72 | 720 | 7.07 | Floats |
| Kerosene | 0.78 | 780 | 7.65 | Floats |
| Pure Water (4 °C) | 1.000 | 1000 | 9.81 | Neutral |
| Seawater | 1.025 | 1025 | 10.05 | Submerges slightly |
| Glycerine | 1.26 | 1260 | 12.36 | Sinks |
| Mercury | 13.6 | 13,600 | 133.42 | Sinks rapidly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Schematics & Functional Architecture

## 4.1 Mermaid Concept Map: Relative Density and Its Cascading Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STANDARD REFERENCE\nrho water = 1000 kg per m3\nat 4 degree C"]
    nodeB["RELATIVE DENSITY S\nDimensionless Ratio"]
    nodeC["DENSITY of fluid\nrho = S x rho water\nUnit: kg per m3"]
    nodeD["SPECIFIC WEIGHT\ngamma = rho x g\nUnit: N per m3"]
    nodeE["SPECIFIC VOLUME\nv = 1 by rho\nUnit: m3 per kg"]
    nodeF["MASS in Volume V\nm = rho x V\nUnit: kg"]
    nodeG["WEIGHT in Volume V\nW = m x g\nUnit: Newton"]
    nodeH["BUOYANCY FORCE\nFb = rho fluid x g x V\nUnit: Newton"]
    nodeI["HYDROMETER READING\nDirect measurement of S"]
    nodeJ["MANOMETER HEAD\nh = P by gamma"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeJ
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Engineering Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input["INPUT STAGE"]
        stepA["Measured Fluid Sample\nVolume V known"]
        stepB["Reference: Pure Water\nat 4 degree C"]
    end

    subgraph process["PROCESSING STAGE"]
        stepC["Compute Mass Ratio\nm fluid by m water"]
        stepD["Apply Definition\nS = rho fluid by rho water"]
        stepE["Multiply by 9810\ngamma = S x gamma water"]
    end

    subgraph output["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        stepF["Report S Dimensionless"]
        stepG["Report gamma in N per m3"]
        stepH["Decide Floatation\nor Sinking Behavior"]
    end

    stepA --> stepC
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepD --> stepF
    stepE --> stepG
    stepF --> stepH
    stepG --> stepH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Diagram 4.1** shows how RD acts as the "central hub" from which all other derived fluid properties branch.
> - **Diagram 4.2** illustrates the laboratory-to-application workflow used in fluid mechanics labs at KTU-affiliated colleges.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Relative Density. Why is water at 4 °C chosen as the reference fluid?**

**Model Answer:**
Relative Density (S) of a fluid is the ratio of its density to the density of pure water at 4 °C.

$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water @ 4°C}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity.

**Why 4 °C?** Pure water attains its **maximum density (1000 kg/m³)** at 4 °C, providing a reproducible, naturally available standard independent of geographic location. This makes it the universal benchmark for fluid property measurements.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`
**A liquid has a Relative Density of 0.78. Calculate its specific weight and specific volume. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\gamma = S \cdot \gamma_w = 0.78 \times 9810 = 7651.8 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$v = \frac{1}{S \cdot \rho_w} = \frac{1}{0.78 \times 1000} = 1.282 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following fluid properties with their SI units: **(i)** Density, **(ii)** Specific Weight, **(iii)** Specific Volume, **(iv)** Relative Density.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An oil of Relative Density **0.82** is stored in a cylindrical tank of diameter **1.2 m** and height **3 m**. Find the **(i)** mass, **(ii)** weight, and **(iii)** pressure at the base of the tank due to the oil alone.

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Definitions [7 Marks — split as 1.75 per definition]:**

1. **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume of a fluid. $\rho = m/V$. Unit: **kg/m³**.
2. **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Weight per unit volume. $\gamma = \rho g$. Unit: **N/m³**.
3. **Specific Volume ($v$):** Volume per unit mass. $v = 1/\rho$. Unit: **m³/kg**.
4. **Relative Density ($S$):** Ratio of fluid density to that of water at 4 °C. **Dimensionless.**

**(b) Numerical [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $S = 0.82$, Diameter $D = 1.2 \text{ m}$, Height $H = 3 \text{ m}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Volume of tank:
$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (1.2)^2 \times 3$$

$$V = 0.7854 \times 1.44 \times 3 = 3.393 \text{ m}^3$$

Density of oil:
$$\rho_{oil} = S \cdot \rho_w = 0.82 \times 1000 = 820 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(i) Mass of oil:**
$$m = \rho \cdot V = 820 \times 3.393 = 2782.26 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Weight of oil:**
$$W = m \cdot g = 2782.26 \times 9.81 = 27{,}294.0 \text{ N} \approx 27.29 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Pressure at base:**
$$P = \gamma_{oil} \cdot H = (S \cdot \gamma_w) \cdot H$$
$$P = (0.82 \times 9810) \times 3$$
$$P = 8044.2 \times 3 = 24{,}132.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 24.13 \text{ kPa}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of Relative Density in fluid mechanics with at least **four engineering applications**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A U-tube manometer uses mercury ($S = 13.6$) to measure the pressure of water flowing in a pipe. If the manometer deflection is **250 mm**, calculate the gauge pressure at the pipe centerline.

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Engineering Significance [7 Marks — 4 applications × 1.5 marks + 1 mark intro]:**

1. **Hydrometer Calibration:** RD allows instant, portable measurement of fluid concentration (e.g., battery acid, antifreeze).
2. **Buoyancy Determination:** RD directly determines whether a ship will float, computed via Archimedes' principle.
3. **Manometric Pressure Measurement:** RD of mercury enables conversion of column heights to pressure in Pascals.
4. **Pump and Pipeline Design:** RD dictates required head and power, since $\rho$ appears in the energy equation.

**(b) Numerical [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $S_{Hg} = 13.6$, $S_{water} = 1.0$, $h = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$.

For a manometer measuring water pressure using mercury:

$$P_{gauge} = h \cdot (S_{Hg} - S_{water}) \cdot \gamma_w$$

Substituting:
$$P_{gauge} = 0.25 \times (13.6 - 1.0) \times 9810$$

$$P_{gauge} = 0.25 \times 12.6 \times 9810$$

$$P_{gauge} = 30{,}901.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 30.9 \text{ kPa}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always write SI units in the final answer line. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks** per missing unit.
> 2. **Using RD as Density:** Students often confuse RD (dimensionless) with $\rho$ (kg/m³). Always multiply by $\rho_w$ before substitution.
> 3. **Reference Temperature:** If a problem does not specify temperature, **assume water at 4 °C** with $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
> 4. **Manometer Difference:** In differential manometers, use $(S_{manometer} - S_{fluid})$, **not** $S_{manometer}$ alone.
> 5. **Significant Figures:** Maintain at least **3 significant figures** in the final numerical answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Relative Density**

- **Definition:** $S = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water}$ — dimensionless ratio.
- **Reference Benchmark:** Water at **4 °C** with $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.
- **Critical Conversion:** Always convert RD to actual $\rho$ before substituting into force/mass equations.
- **Specific Weight:** $\gamma_{fluid} = S \times 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ (memorize this shortcut).
- **Specific Volume:** $v = 1/(S \times 1000) \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$.
- **Mercury Memorization:** $S_{Hg} = 13.6$ — used universally in manometer/barometer problems.
- **Floatation Rule:** If $S > 1$, fluid sinks in water; if $S < 1$, fluid floats.
- **Temperature Dependency:** RD decreases slightly with rising temperature due to thermal expansion (governed by coefficient $\beta$).
- **Manometer Pressure Formula:** $P = h \cdot (S_{Hg} - S_{fluid}) \cdot \gamma_w$.
- **Always** state given data, formula, substitution, and final answer with units in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surface Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Tension** is a fundamental physical property of liquid surfaces wherein the surface of a liquid behaves like a thin, elastic membrane under tension due to the **cohesive intermolecular forces** acting between liquid molecules at the interface. Quantitatively, surface tension is defined as the force per unit length acting on an imaginary line drawn on the liquid surface, the force being perpendicular to the line and lying in the plane of the surface.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L}$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Surface tension (N/m)
- $F$ = Force acting tangentially on the surface (N)
- $L$ = Length of the imaginary line over which the force acts (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT416 - Module 1):** Surface tension is treated as a **molecular phenomenon** arising out of cohesion-adhesion imbalance at interfaces. Students must clearly distinguish between **surface tension** (force per unit length) and **surface energy** (work done per unit area), and recognize that numerically both possess identical units and magnitude.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a stretched rubber sheet lying flat on a table. If you try to pull it apart from the centre, you feel a resisting force trying to keep the sheet intact. A liquid surface behaves **exactly like this stretched elastic sheet**, except that the "stretching" is caused not by external mechanical pull but by the **attractive forces** between the molecules of the liquid.

- **Inside the bulk of a liquid:** Every molecule is pulled equally in *all directions* by surrounding molecules. The **net force** on it is **zero**.
- **At the surface:** A molecule has neighbours only on one side (below) and air molecules (very weak attraction) on the other. So the surface molecules experience a **net inward pull**, packing tightly and forming a "skin."

This "skin" is what allows **water striders to walk on water**, **soap bubbles to form perfect spheres**, and **a steel needle to float on water** (until it pierces the surface tension).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant:** For pure water at **20°C**, the surface tension is approximately **0.0728 N/m** (or 72.8 mN/m). For mercury, it is about **0.485 N/m**, owing to the very strong metallic bonding.

## 1.3 Cohesion vs. Adhesion (Foundational Forces)

| Property | Cohesion | Adhesion |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Attractive force between molecules of the **same** substance | Attractive force between molecules of **different** substances |
| **Example** | Water-Water molecules clinging together | Water-Glass molecules clinging at the meniscus |
| **Resultant Effect** | Tends to **minimize** surface area (spherical droplets) | Tends to **spread** liquid on a solid surface |
| **Dominance Test** | If cohesion > adhesion → liquid forms droplets (Mercury on glass) | If adhesion > cohesion → liquid wets the surface (Water on glass) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** Meniscus shape (concave vs. convex) is **directly determined** by the relative magnitude of cohesive and adhesive forces. Water in a glass tube forms a **concave meniscus** because adhesion > cohesion. Mercury forms a **convex meniscus** because cohesion > adhesion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Molecular Theory of Surface Tension

The molecular theory explains surface tension by considering the unbalanced forces experienced by molecules at the surface:

1. **Molecules in the Bulk:** Each molecule in the interior of the liquid is surrounded on all sides by other identical molecules. The **time-averaged cohesive forces** acting on it are equal in magnitude and uniformly distributed in all directions. Therefore, the **resultant force is zero**, and the molecule is in a state of minimum potential energy.

2. **Molecules near the Surface (within ~$10^{-9}$ m):** A molecule approaching the surface begins to lose neighbours above it (since gas molecules are far apart and exert negligible attractive force). It experiences a **net inward pull** towards the bulk of the liquid.

3. **Molecules exactly at the Surface:** A molecule at the free surface has only sideways and downward neighbours. The **net force is directed into the bulk (downward)**, perpendicular to the surface.

4. **Consequence — Energy Imbalance:** To bring a molecule from the bulk to the surface, **work must be done against this net inward force**. This work is stored as **potential energy**, called **surface energy**.

5. **Equilibrium Condition:** Since every physical system tends toward minimum potential energy, the liquid surface tends to **contract to the smallest possible area** for a given volume. This is why free droplets assume a **spherical shape** (sphere has minimum surface area per unit volume).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Surface Energy Numerically Equal to Surface Tension?**
> Work done to increase surface area by $\Delta A$ = $W = \sigma \cdot \Delta A$
> Energy stored per unit area = $\sigma \cdot \Delta A / \Delta A = \sigma$ (J/m²)
> Thus, surface tension $\sigma$ in **N/m** is numerically identical to surface energy in **J/m²**.

## 2.2 Pressure Difference Across Curved Liquid Surfaces (Laplace's Law)

When a liquid surface is curved, the surface tension produces a **pressure difference** between the inside and outside of the curved surface. This is fundamentally important for bubbles, droplets, and capillary rise.

### Case 1: Spherical Droplet (Single Air-Liquid Interface)

For a spherical liquid droplet of radius $R$ in air:

$$\Delta p = p_{inside} - p_{outside} = \frac{2\sigma}{R}$$

### Case 2: Soap Bubble (Two Air-Liquid Interfaces, i.e., Thin Film)

A soap bubble has an inner and outer surface, both contributing to surface tension:

$$\Delta p = \frac{4\sigma}{R}$$

### Case 3: Liquid Jet (Cylindrical Curvature, One Interface)

For a cylindrical liquid jet of radius $R$:

$$\Delta p = \frac{\sigma}{R}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $\Delta p = 2\sigma/R$ for soap bubbles. Remember: a soap bubble has **two surfaces** (inner and outer film), each contributing $\sigma$, so the total pressure jump is **twice** that of a droplet of the same radius.

## 2.3 Capillarity (Capillary Action)

Capillarity is the phenomenon of **rise or fall of a liquid** in a narrow tube (capillary) due to the combined effects of **surface tension** and the **wetting characteristic** of the liquid-solid pair.

### Capillary Rise Formula

For a capillary tube of inner diameter $D$ (or radius $r$) dipped in a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\sigma$, the **equilibrium height** $h$ of liquid column is:

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g D}$$

Equivalently, in terms of radius $r = D/2$:

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = Angle of contact (between liquid and solid surface)
- $\rho$ = Density of liquid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $D$ = Diameter of capillary tube (m)
- $r$ = Radius of capillary tube (m)

### Sign Convention for $\cos\theta$:
- **Wetting liquid** (e.g., water-glass): $\theta < 90°$, so $\cos\theta > 0$, **liquid rises** ($h$ positive).
- **Non-wetting liquid** (e.g., mercury-glass): $\theta > 90°$, so $\cos\theta < 0$, **liquid falls** ($h$ negative → depression).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Tension Definition | $\sigma = F/L$ | Force per unit length, N/m |
| 2 | Surface Energy | $W = \sigma \cdot \Delta A$ | Work to increase surface area, J |
| 3 | Capillary Rise (diameter form) | $h = 4\sigma\cos\theta/(\rho g D)$ | $D$ = tube diameter |
| 4 | Capillary Rise (radius form) | $h = 2\sigma\cos\theta/(\rho g r)$ | $r$ = tube radius |
| 5 | Pressure in Spherical Droplet | $\Delta p = 2\sigma/R$ | Single interface |
| 6 | Pressure in Soap Bubble | $\Delta p = 4\sigma/R$ | Double interface |
| 7 | Pressure in Liquid Jet | $\Delta p = \sigma/R$ | Cylindrical, single |
| 8 | Force on Contact Length | $F = \sigma \cdot L$ | Tangential force on wetted perimeter |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Surface tension governs many critical engineering phenomena:

- **Hydraulic & Civil Engineering:** Capillary rise causes **moisture rising in building foundations** (dampness in walls), affecting material selection and damp-proofing design.
- **Petroleum Engineering:** Capillary pressure determines **oil recovery efficiency** in porous reservoir rocks.
- **Biomedical:** Surfactants (e.g., lung surfactants) reduce surface tension in alveoli, preventing collapse during breathing.
- **Detergents & Emulsions:** Soap reduces water's surface tension, allowing it to spread and wet fabrics/grease.
- **Raindrop Formation:** Surface tension maintains the spherical shape of small droplets in clouds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Pressure Difference Across a Spherical Droplet (Laplace's Law)

### Setup:
Consider a spherical liquid droplet of radius $R$ in equilibrium with its surrounding vapour. The pressure inside the droplet is $p_i$ and the pressure outside is $p_o$, such that $\Delta p = p_i - p_o$.

### Step 1: Imaginary Bisection
Mentally cut the spherical droplet into **two identical hemispheres** by an imaginary horizontal plane passing through the equator.

### Step 2: Identify Forces Acting on the Upper Hemisphere

**Force A — Pressure Force (acting outward on the cut surface):**
The projected area of the cut surface is the area of a great circle: $A = \pi R^2$.
Therefore, the net outward pressure force is:

$$F_{pressure} = \Delta p \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Force B — Surface Tension Force (acting inward along the circumference):**
Surface tension acts tangentially along the entire perimeter of the cut circle. The total length of this perimeter is $L = 2\pi R$. The surface tension force pulls the two hemispheres together:

$$F_{tension} = \sigma \cdot L = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R$$

### Step 3: Equilibrium Condition
For the hemisphere to be in static equilibrium, the outward pressure force must equal the inward surface tension force:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi R^2 = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\Delta p$
Divide both sides by $\pi R^2$:

$$\Delta p = \frac{2\sigma}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Smaller droplets have **higher internal pressure**. This is why very fine mist droplets evaporate faster (high internal pressure pushes molecules out). It is also the reason **bubbles get larger** when they merge — combined radius is smaller than the sum of individual radii (geometrically), reducing internal pressure imbalance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Capillary Rise Formula

### Setup:
A glass capillary tube of radius $r$ is dipped into a liquid that wets the tube (e.g., water). The liquid rises to a height $h$ inside the tube.

### Step 1: Force Balance — Vertical Equilibrium
Three vertical forces act on the **column of liquid** inside the capillary tube:

1. **Upward Force due to Surface Tension ($F_{\sigma}$):**
The liquid surface touches the tube along its circumference ($L = 2\pi r$). Surface tension $\sigma$ acts along this perimeter, pulling the liquid column **upward** at an angle $\theta$ to the vertical (where $\theta$ is the contact angle).

   The vertical component of this force is:

$$F_{\sigma, vertical} = \sigma \cdot \cos\theta \cdot 2\pi r$$

2. **Downward Force due to Weight of Liquid Column ($F_{weight}$):**

$$F_{weight} = m \cdot g = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g = \rho \cdot (\pi r^2 h) \cdot g$$

3. **Pressure at the Bottom:** Equal on both sides (atmospheric), so it cancels out.

### Step 2: Equating Upward and Downward Forces
At equilibrium, the upward surface tension force equals the downward weight of the raised liquid column:

$$\sigma \cdot \cos\theta \cdot 2\pi r = \rho \cdot \pi r^2 h \cdot g$$

### Step 3: Solving for $h$
Divide both sides by $\pi r$:

$$\sigma \cdot \cos\theta \cdot 2 = \rho \cdot r \cdot h \cdot g$$

Solve for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

Since the diameter $D = 2r$, we can equivalently write:

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g D}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** Capillary rise is **inversely proportional to tube radius**. If the tube radius is halved, the rise doubles. This is why very narrow tubes (like wicks in oil lamps) produce significant capillary action.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Problem:** A clean glass capillary tube of diameter **2.5 mm** is dipped in water at **20°C**. Calculate the capillary rise. Given: $\sigma_{water} = 0.0728$ N/m, $\theta = 0°$ (water-glass is fully wetting), $\rho_{water} = 998$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the given quantities:
- $D = 2.5$ mm $= 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\sigma = 0.0728$ N/m
- $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$
- $\rho = 998$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 2:** Apply the capillary rise formula:

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g D}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$h = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{998 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the numerator:

$$4 \times 0.0728 = 0.2912 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the denominator:

$$998 \times 9.81 = 9790.38$$

$$9790.38 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 24.476$$

**Step 6:** Compute $h$:

$$h = \frac{0.2912}{24.476} = 0.01190 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to mm for clarity:

$$h = 11.90 \text{ mm} \approx 11.9 \text{ mm}$$

### Final Answer:
**The capillary rise is approximately 11.9 mm.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Consistency Check:** In SI units, $\sigma$ is in N/m $\equiv$ J/m², $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s², $D$ in m. The resulting $h$ will be in **m**. Always verify before submitting the answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Molecular Force Distribution at the Liquid Surface

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the difference in net intermolecular force experienced by a molecule in the bulk versus at the surface.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Bulk Liquid Molecule] --> B[Equal Cohesive Pull From All Sides]
    B --> C[Net Intermolecular Force = ZERO]
    D[Surface Liquid Molecule] --> E[Strong Downward Pull From Below]
    E --> F[Negligible Pull From Air Above]
    F --> G[Net Force Directed INWARD Into Bulk]
    G --> H[Surface Energy Stored Due To Unbalanced Pull]
    H --> I[Surface Minimizes Area To Reduce Energy]
    I --> J[Droplet Tends To Become Spherical]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Capillary Rise Mechanism

This diagram traces the step-by-step physical mechanism by which a liquid column rises inside a capillary tube dipped in a wetting liquid.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Capillary Tube Dipped In Wetting Liquid] --> P2[Adhesion Exceeds Cohesion At Tube Walls]
    P2 --> P3[Liquid Wets Inner Wall And Forms Concave Meniscus]
    P3 --> P4[Surface Tension Acts Along Contact Perimeter 2 pi r]
    P4 --> P5[Vertical Component Of Surface Tension Pulls Liquid Upward]
    P5 --> P6[Weight Of Rising Column Acts Downward]
    P6 --> P7[Equilibrium Reached When Upward Force = Downward Weight]
    P7 --> P8[Steady Capillary Rise h Achieved]
    P8 --> P9[h Equals 4 Sigma Cos Theta Over rho g D]
```

## 4.3 Functional Comparison: Droplet vs. Soap Bubble vs. Liquid Jet

This block diagram contrasts the pressure-jump equations across three different curvature geometries — a frequently tested KTU comparison topic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0[Curved Liquid Surface Configurations] --> R1[Spherical Liquid Droplet]
    R0 --> R2[Soap Bubble Thin Film]
    R0 --> R3[Cylindrical Liquid Jet]
    R1 --> S1[Single Air Liquid Interface]
    S1 --> S2[Pressure Jump Delta P Equals 2 Sigma Over R]
    R2 --> T1[Two Air Liquid Interfaces Inner And Outer]
    T1 --> T2[Pressure Jump Delta P Equals 4 Sigma Over R]
    R3 --> U1[Single Cylindrical Interface]
    U1 --> U2[Pressure Jump Delta P Equals Sigma Over R]
```

## 4.4 Decision Block: Wetting vs. Non-Wetting Behavior

A logic-flow diagram used to determine whether a given liquid-solid pair will exhibit capillary rise or capillary depression.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q0[Liquid Solid Pair Encountered] --> Q1{Compare Adhesion And Cohesion}
    Q1 --> Q2[Adhesion Greater Than Cohesion]
    Q1 --> Q3[Cohesion Greater Than Adhesion]
    Q2 --> Q4[Contact Angle Theta Less Than 90 Degrees]
    Q4 --> Q5[Concave Meniscus Forms]
    Q5 --> Q6[Cos Theta Positive]
    Q6 --> Q7[Capillary Rise Occurs Example Water In Glass]
    Q3 --> Q8[Contact Angle Theta Greater Than 90 Degrees]
    Q8 --> Q9[Convex Meniscus Forms]
    Q9 --> Q10[Cos Theta Negative]
    Q10 --> Q11[Capillary Depression Occurs Example Mercury In Glass]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each block in the diagrams above represents a discrete physical state or condition. The arrows indicate causal transition. Students should map these flows onto their conceptual understanding of surface tension physics before attempting numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define surface tension. Explain the phenomenon of surface tension on the basis of molecular theory.**

**Model Answer:**

Surface tension is defined as the **tensile force per unit length** acting on an imaginary line drawn on a liquid surface, the force being tangential to the surface and perpendicular to the line. It is denoted by $\sigma$ and has units of **N/m**.

According to molecular theory:

- Molecules in the **bulk of the liquid** are surrounded on all sides by identical molecules and experience equal cohesive forces in all directions, giving a **net force of zero**.
- Molecules at the **free surface** lack neighbours above them (only gas molecules, with negligible attraction). Hence, they experience a **net inward (downward) pull**.
- Work must be done to bring a molecule from the bulk to the surface. This work is stored as **surface energy**.
- To minimize this surface energy, the liquid surface contracts to the **smallest possible area** for a given volume — explaining the spherical shape of droplets and the rise of liquids in capillaries.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad [\text{N/m}]$$

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between cohesion and adhesion. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Cohesion | Adhesion |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Force of attraction between molecules of the **same** substance | Force of attraction between molecules of **different** substances |
| **Example** | Water molecules clinging together inside a water droplet | Water molecules clinging to a glass surface at the meniscus |
| **Effect on Meniscus** | Promotes convex meniscus (if dominant) | Promotes concave meniscus (if dominant) |

**Practical Examples:**
- **Cohesion:** Mercury droplets on a glass surface bead up into spheres.
- **Adhesion:** Water spreads on a clean glass plate.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**a) (7 Marks)** Derive an expression for the capillary rise of a liquid in a narrow tube in terms of surface tension, contact angle, density, and tube radius.

**b) (7 Marks)** A glass capillary tube of diameter **1.5 mm** is dipped in water at **20°C**. Given $\sigma = 0.0728$ N/m, $\theta = 0°$, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, and $g = 9.81$ m/s², calculate:
- (i) The capillary rise in mm.
- (ii) The capillary rise if the same tube is used with mercury (take $\theta = 130°$, $\sigma_{Hg} = 0.485$ N/m, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³).

### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider a capillary tube of inner radius $r$ dipped into a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\sigma$, forming a contact angle $\theta$.

**Step 2:** The liquid rises to a height $h$ inside the tube due to the upward pull of surface tension.

**Step 3:** Surface tension acts along the perimeter of contact between liquid and tube, which equals $L = 2\pi r$. The vertical component (upward) is:

$$F_{up} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 4:** The weight of the liquid column of height $h$ inside the tube acts downward:

$$F_{down} = (\text{Mass}) \cdot g = (\rho \cdot \pi r^2 h) \cdot g$$

**Step 5:** At equilibrium, the upward and downward forces are equal:

$$\sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta = \rho \cdot \pi r^2 h \cdot g$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

Or equivalently (using diameter $D = 2r$):

$$\boxed{h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g D}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Part (b)(i): Capillary Rise of Water**

Given:
- $D = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\sigma = 0.0728$ N/m
- $\theta = 0°$, so $\cos\theta = 1$
- $\rho_{water} = 998$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Substituting:

$$h = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{998 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 0.0728 = 0.2912$

Denominator: $998 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 14.6855$

$$h = \frac{0.2912}{14.6855} = 0.01983 \text{ m} = 19.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Capillary rise of water ≈ 19.83 mm**

---

**Part (b)(ii): Capillary Depression of Mercury**

Given:
- $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\sigma_{Hg} = 0.485$ N/m
- $\theta = 130°$, so $\cos 130° = -0.6428$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Substituting:

$$h = \frac{4 \times 0.485 \times (-0.6428)}{13600 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 0.485 \times (-0.6428) = -1.2470$

Denominator: $13600 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 200.124$

$$h = \frac{-1.2470}{200.124} = -0.006231 \text{ m} = -6.23 \text{ mm}$$

**Capillary depression of mercury ≈ 6.23 mm (negative sign indicates depression)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**a) (7 Marks)** Explain the phenomenon of capillarity with a neat diagram. Define the **angle of contact** and explain how its value determines whether a liquid wets or does not wet a solid surface.

**b) (7 Marks)** Two soap bubbles of radii **3 cm** and **5 cm** are in contact with each other. Calculate the common radius of curvature at the contact interface and the pressure difference between the inside of the smaller bubble and the larger bubble. Take $\sigma_{soap} = 0.025$ N/m.

### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Capillarity and Contact Angle:**

**Definition of Capillarity:**
Capillarity is the phenomenon of **rise or fall of a liquid** in a narrow tube (or porous medium) due to the combined effects of surface tension and the wetting characteristics between the liquid and the solid surface.

**Angle of Contact ($\theta$):**
The angle of contact is defined as the angle measured **inside the liquid** between the **tangent to the liquid surface** at the point of contact and the **solid surface**, measured through the liquid.

**Three Cases:**

| Case | Condition | $\theta$ | Meniscus | Capillary Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wetting** (e.g., water-glass) | Adhesion > Cohesion | $\theta < 90°$ | Concave | **Rise** |
| **Non-Wetting** (e.g., mercury-glass) | Cohesion > Adhesion | $\theta > 90°$ | Convex | **Depression** |
| **Neutral** (e.g., water-paraffin) | Adhesion = Cohesion | $\theta = 90°$ | Flat | **No rise or fall** |

**Explanation:** The contact angle is determined by the balance of three interfacial tensions at the three-phase contact line (solid-liquid, liquid-gas, solid-gas). Young's equation governs this:

$$\sigma_{sg} = \sigma_{sl} + \sigma_{lg} \cos\theta$$

Where subscripts s, l, g refer to solid, liquid, and gas respectively.

When the **liquid-solid adhesion** dominates, $\sigma_{sl}$ is reduced, forcing $\cos\theta$ to be positive and large, resulting in $\theta < 90°$ (wetting case).

---

**Part (b) — Soap Bubble Interface Analysis:**

**Given:**
- Radius of bubble 1: $R_1 = 3$ cm $= 0.03$ m
- Radius of bubble 2: $R_2 = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
- Surface tension of soap film: $\sigma = 0.025$ N/m

**Step 1: Pressure inside Smaller Bubble ($p_1$):**

$$p_1 = p_{atm} + \frac{4\sigma}{R_1} = p_{atm} + \frac{4 \times 0.025}{0.03}$$

$$p_1 - p_{atm} = \frac{0.1}{0.03} = 3.333 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Pressure inside Larger Bubble ($p_2$):**

$$p_2 = p_{atm} + \frac{4\sigma}{R_2} = p_{atm} + \frac{4 \times 0.025}{0.05}$$

$$p_2 - p_{atm} = \frac{0.1}{0.05} = 2.000 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Pressure Difference between Smaller and Larger Bubble:**

$$\Delta p = p_1 - p_2 = 3.333 - 2.000 = 1.333 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Common Radius of Curvature at the Interface ($r$):**
For two soap bubbles in contact, the common interface is a spherical surface with radius $r$ such that the pressure inside the smaller bubble is balanced by the pressure in the larger bubble plus the surface tension contribution of the interface:

$$\frac{4\sigma}{R_1} - \frac{4\sigma}{R_2} = \frac{4\sigma}{r}$$

$$\frac{1}{r} = \frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2} = \frac{1}{0.03} - \frac{1}{0.05}$$

$$\frac{1}{r} = \frac{5 - 3}{0.15} = \frac{2}{0.15} = 13.333 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$r = \frac{1}{13.333} = 0.075 \text{ m} = 7.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Pressure difference** = $1.333$ N/m² (Pa) [smaller bubble has higher pressure]
- **Common interface radius** = $7.5$ cm

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always use diameter OR radius consistently.** Mixing $D$ and $r$ in the capillary rise formula is the #1 cause of incorrect numerical answers. The formulas are $h = 4\sigma\cos\theta/(\rho g D)$ AND $h = 2\sigma\cos\theta/(\rho g r)$ — pick one and stick with it.
> 2. **For soap bubbles, always use $4\sigma/R$, not $2\sigma/R$.** A soap bubble has **two** liquid-air interfaces.
> 3. **Negative $h$ means depression, not negative capillary rise.** Do not discard the negative sign; it conveys crucial physical information (mercury case).
> 4. **Always include units in the final answer.** KTU examiners frequently deduct 0.5 marks for missing or incorrect units.
> 5. **State assumptions clearly:** Assume room temperature (often 20°C), pure liquid, clean glass tube, and equilibrium condition unless stated otherwise.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Surface Tension Definition:** Force per unit length, $\sigma = F/L$, units **N/m**, also equal to **surface energy** in J/m².
- **Molecular Origin:** Net inward pull on surface molecules due to unbalanced cohesive forces.
- **Cohesion vs. Adhesion:** Cohesion = same substance; Adhesion = different substances.
- **Wetting Criterion:** Adhesion > Cohesion → wetting (concave meniscus); Cohesion > Adhesion → non-wetting (convex meniscus).
- **Capillary Rise Formula:** $h = 4\sigma\cos\theta/(\rho g D)$ (diameter form).
- **Capillary Depression:** Occurs when $\theta > 90°$ (mercury-glass typical).
- **Laplace's Pressure Equation:**
  - Spherical droplet: $\Delta p = 2\sigma/R$
  - Soap bubble: $\Delta p = 4\sigma/R$
  - Cylindrical jet: $\Delta p = \sigma/R$
- **Inversely Proportional Relationships:** $h \propto 1/D$ and $\Delta p \propto 1/R$.
- **Standard Reference Values:** Water at 20°C: $\sigma \approx 0.0728$ N/m; Mercury at 20°C: $\sigma \approx 0.485$ N/m; Soap solution: $\sigma \approx 0.025$ N/m.
- **Young's Equation:** $\sigma_{sg} = \sigma_{sl} + \sigma_{lg} \cos\theta$ — governs the contact angle at the three-phase line.
- **Spherical Droplet Shape:** Surface tension drives minimization of surface area; sphere has minimum surface area per unit volume.
- **Real-World Relevance:** Detergents, oil recovery, lung surfactants, damp-proofing, capillary wicks, drop formation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compressibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Compressibility** is defined as the property of a fluid by virtue of which its volume (or density) changes under the action of an external pressure. It quantifies the *reduction in volume per unit original volume per unit increase in pressure*, measured under specified thermodynamic conditions (isothermal or isothermal/adiabatic).

Mathematically, the **coefficient of compressibility** ($\beta$ or $\kappa$) is expressed as:

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V} \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_{T \text{ or } S}$$

The negative sign is introduced to make $\beta$ a **positive quantity**, since an increase in pressure ($dP > 0$) causes a decrease in volume ($dV < 0$).

The reciprocal of compressibility is known as the **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$)**:

$$K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -V \left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)$$

In terms of density ($\rho$):

$$K = \rho \left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$) and the **Speed of Sound** relationship are *high-priority* topics in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for Fluid Mechanics. Mastery of these is essential for Part B 14-mark derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Units of Bulk Modulus:** $K$ has the same units as pressure, i.e., $\text{N/m}^2$ or **Pa (Pascal)** in SI, or $\text{kgf/cm}^2$ in the metric system. The coefficient of compressibility $\beta$ has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$ or $\text{Pa}^{-1}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are squeezing two different substances with your hand:

| Substance | Behaviour Under Squeeze | Compressibility Class |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Steel sponge (dense foam)** | Barely changes shape | Low $\beta$, High $K$ |
| **Empty balloon (filled with air)** | Noticeably reduces in volume | High $\beta$, Low $K$ |
| **Water in a syringe** | Plunger barely moves | Very Low $\beta$, Very High $K$ |

**Real-World Intuition:**

- **Water** has a Bulk Modulus of approximately **$2.2 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$** (≈ 220,000 atm). This means you would need ~220,000 atmospheres of pressure to reduce water's volume by 1%. Hence, water is treated as **incompressible** in most engineering applications.
- **Air** at sea-level conditions has a Bulk Modulus of only **$1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$** (1 atm). Air is roughly **20,000 times more compressible** than water, which is why we treat gases as **compressible fluids**.

**Engineering Analogy – The Diving Balloon:**
If a balloon filled with air is taken from the ocean surface to great depth, the immense hydrostatic pressure (~1 atm per 10 m) will visibly shrink the balloon. The same balloon filled with water will show no perceptible change in size, regardless of depth. This is **compressibility in action**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in Fluid Mechanics?** Compressibility dictates whether we can use the simplified **incompressible Bernoulli's equation** or the more complex **compressible flow equations** (e.g., for high-speed gas dynamics, aerodynamics, gas pipelines, and steam turbines).

---

## 1.3 Types of Compressibility

Depending on the thermodynamic process during compression:

1. **Isothermal Compressibility ($\beta_T$)** – Compression at **constant temperature** (slow process, heat exchanged with surroundings). Relevant for liquids in long-duration processes.

$$\beta_T = -\frac{1}{V} \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_{T}$$

2. **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compressibility ($\beta_S$)** – Compression at **constant entropy** (rapid process, no heat exchange). Relevant for **sound waves** and high-speed compressible flow.

$$\beta_S = -\frac{1}{V} \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_{S}$$

For ideal gases: $\beta_T = \gamma \cdot \beta_S$ (where $\gamma = C_p / C_v$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Mechanism

A fluid's compressibility originates from the **intermolecular spacing** within its structure:

- **Liquids**: Molecules are tightly packed (similar to solids). Strong intermolecular repulsive forces resist compression → **Low $\beta$, High $K$**.
- **Gases**: Molecules are far apart (mean free path ~100 nm at STP). Weak intermolecular forces → **High $\beta$, Low $K$**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

### Step 1: Quantify Volume Change with Pressure
When a fluid element of volume $V$ is subjected to a differential pressure change $dP$, the corresponding volume change is $dV$. The fractional volume change per unit pressure is:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = -\beta \, dP$$

### Step 2: Convert to Density Form
Since mass is conserved ($m = \rho V = \text{const}$), differentiating $\rho V = m$:

$$V \, d\rho + \rho \, dV = 0 \implies dV = -\frac{V}{\rho} d\rho$$

Substituting into the compressibility definition:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{\rho} \left( \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial P} \right)$$

### Step 3: Establish Bulk Modulus Identity
Since $K = 1/\beta$:

$$K = \rho \left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho} \right)$$

### Step 4: Apply Ideal Gas Law for Gases
For an ideal gas, $P = \rho R T$ (where $R$ is the specific gas constant).

- **Isothermal** ($T$ = const): $K_T = P$
- **Adiabatic** ($PV^\gamma$ = const, $\gamma = C_p/C_v$): $K_S = \gamma P$

### Step 5: Speed of Sound Link
Newton–Laplace equation gives the speed of sound ($c$) in any fluid:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_S}{\rho}}$$

For an ideal gas under adiabatic conditions:

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

For air at 20°C: $c \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table for last-minute revision. Every formula here has appeared in past KTU ESE papers.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Applicable To | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | Coefficient of Compressibility | $\beta = -\dfrac{1}{V} \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)$ | All fluids | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Bulk Modulus (general) | $K = \rho \left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \rho} \right)$ | All fluids | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 3 | Fractional Volume Change | $\dfrac{dV}{V} = -\beta \, dP$ | All fluids | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Fractional Density Change | $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \beta \, dP$ | All fluids | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Isothermal Bulk Modulus (ideal gas) | $K_T = P$ | Ideal gas | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 6 | Adiabatic Bulk Modulus (ideal gas) | $K_S = \gamma P$ | Ideal gas | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 7 | Speed of Sound in Fluid | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_S}{\rho}}$ | All fluids | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 8 | Speed of Sound in Ideal Gas | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Ideal gas | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 9 | Mach Number | $M = \dfrac{V}{c}$ | Compressible flow | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Relation between $\beta_T$ and $\beta_S$ | $\beta_T = \gamma \beta_S$ | Ideal gas | – |
| 11 | Pressure change from depth (water) | $dP = \rho g \, dh$ | Static liquids | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 12 | Volume change for given $\Delta P$ | $\Delta V = \beta V \Delta P$ | All fluids | $\text{m}^3$ |
