# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bulk Modulus in Terms of Density

**Starting Point:** The general definition of compressibility:

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V} \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)$$

**Step 1:** For a fluid element of fixed mass $m$, we have the fundamental continuity relation:

$$\rho V = m = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to pressure $P$, holding mass constant:

$$V \left( \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial P} \right) + \rho \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right) = -\frac{V}{\rho} \left( \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial P} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute this into the compressibility equation:

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V} \left[ -\frac{V}{\rho} \left( \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial P} \right) \right] = \frac{1}{\rho} \left( \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial P} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Invert to get Bulk Modulus:

$$K = \frac{1}{\beta} = \rho \left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho} \right)$$

> **Conclusion:** $K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ (where the differential can be isothermal or adiabatic depending on the process).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bulk Modulus of an Ideal Gas (Isothermal & Adiabatic)

### Case A: Isothermal Process

**Starting Point:** Ideal Gas Equation: $PV = mRT$ (with $T$ = constant).

For unit mass: $P/\rho = RT = \text{constant}$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate $P = \rho R T$ with respect to $\rho$ at constant $T$:

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = RT$$

**Step 2:** Use the Bulk Modulus formula:

$$K_T = \rho \cdot \frac{dP}{d\rho} = \rho \cdot (RT) = (\rho R) T = P$$

**Final Result (Isothermal):**

$$\boxed{K_T = P}$$

---

### Case B: Adiabatic (Isentropic) Process

**Starting Point:** Adiabatic relation for an ideal gas: $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$, where $\gamma = C_p / C_v$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate $PV^\gamma = C$ implicitly:

$$V^\gamma \, dP + \gamma P V^{\gamma - 1} \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2:** Divide throughout by $V^{\gamma - 1}$:

$$V \, dP + \gamma P \, dV = 0$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$-\frac{1}{V} \frac{dV}{dP} = \frac{1}{\gamma P}$$

**Step 4:** The LHS is precisely the adiabatic compressibility:

$$\beta_S = \frac{1}{\gamma P}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the adiabatic bulk modulus is:

$$\boxed{K_S = \gamma P}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed of Sound in a Fluid (Newton–Laplace Equation)

**Starting Point:** Consider a sound wave as a small pressure disturbance propagating through a fluid. The disturbance is so rapid that the process is **adiabatic**, so we use $K_S$.

**Step 1:** Newton's formula for wave speed in an elastic medium:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\text{Elasticity}}{\text{Density}}}$$

**Step 2:** Replace elasticity with the **adiabatic bulk modulus**:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_S}{\rho}}$$

**Step 3:** For an ideal gas, substitute $K_S = \gamma P$:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4:** Use the ideal gas law $P/\rho = RT$:

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

**Step 5:** This is the well-known **Newton–Laplace equation** for the speed of sound in a perfect gas.

**Numerical Example:** For air at $T = 300 \text{ K}$ (≈ 27°C), with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$:

$$c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 300} = \sqrt{120{,}540} \approx 347.2 \text{ m/s}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relation Between $\beta_T$ and $\beta_S$ for an Ideal Gas

**Step 1:** From the equations of state:
- Isothermal: $K_T = P \implies \beta_T = 1/P$
- Adiabatic: $K_S = \gamma P \implies \beta_S = 1/(\gamma P)$

**Step 2:** Divide:

$$\frac{\beta_T}{\beta_S} = \frac{1/P}{1/(\gamma P)} = \gamma$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\beta_T = \gamma \beta_S \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad K_S = \gamma K_T}$$

> **Physical Insight:** Since $\gamma > 1$ for all real gases, $\beta_T > \beta_S$ — a gas is *easier* to compress slowly (isothermal, heat leaves the system) than rapidly (adiabatic, heat is trapped).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Fractional Density Increase in Ocean Water with Depth

**Step 1:** Hydrostatic relation: $dP = \rho g \, dh$

**Step 2:** Density change: $d\rho = \rho \beta_T \, dP = \rho \beta_T (\rho g \, dh)$

**Step 3:** Separate variables and integrate from surface ($h = 0$, $\rho = \rho_0$) to depth $h$ ($\rho = \rho$):

$$\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{\rho^2} = \beta_T g \int_0^h dh$$

$$-\frac{1}{\rho} + \frac{1}{\rho_0} = \beta_T g h$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\rho$:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{\rho_0} - \beta_T g h$$

$$\boxed{\rho(h) = \frac{\rho_0}{1 - \rho_0 \beta_T g h}}$$

**Numerical Estimate:** For seawater ($\rho_0 = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\beta_T \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-10} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$):

At depth $h = 1000 \text{ m}$:

$$\rho_0 \beta_T g h = 1025 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-10} \times 9.81 \times 1000 \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\rho(1000) \approx \frac{1025}{1 - 0.0044} \approx 1029.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

A density increase of only **0.44%** at 1 km depth — confirming water's near-incompressibility.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define the terms: (i) Coefficient of compressibility, (ii) Bulk Modulus of Elasticity. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Coefficient of Compressibility ($\beta$):** It is defined as the **reduction in volume per unit original volume per unit increase in pressure**, expressed as:

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V} \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)$$

It has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$ or $\text{Pa}^{-1}$.

(ii) **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$):** It is defined as the **ratio of volumetric stress to volumetric strain** within the elastic limit:

$$K = -\frac{dP}{(dV/V)} = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$$

**Relationship:**

$$K = \frac{1}{\beta} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \beta = \frac{1}{K}$$

**[3 Marks: 1 mark each for two definitions + 1 mark for relationship]**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**For an ideal gas, show that the adiabatic bulk modulus is $\gamma$ times the isothermal bulk modulus.**

**Model Answer:**

For an ideal gas, $P = \rho R T$.

- **Isothermal process** ($T$ = const): Differentiating $P = \rho R T$ with respect to $\rho$ gives $dP/d\rho = RT$, hence:

$$K_T = \rho \cdot \frac{dP}{d\rho} = \rho \cdot RT = P$$

- **Adiabatic process** ($PV^\gamma$ = const): Differentiating yields $V \, dP + \gamma P \, dV = 0$, from which $K_S = \gamma P$.

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{K_S}{K_T} = \frac{\gamma P}{P} = \gamma$$

$$\therefore K_S = \gamma K_T \quad \blacksquare$$

**[3 Marks: 1 mark for $K_T$ derivation, 1 mark for $K_S$ derivation, 1 mark for ratio]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries two full 14-mark questions, and the student must attempt **one** (internal choice). Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity of a fluid in terms of density and pressure**. Explain how it differs for **isothermal** and **adiabatic** processes for an ideal gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The velocity of sound in a fluid is given by $c = \sqrt{K_S / \rho}$. A submarine is submerged in seawater (bulk modulus $K = 2.2 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$, density $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Calculate:

  (i) The velocity of sound in seawater.
  (ii) The Mach number of the submarine if it travels at $36 \text{ km/h}$.
  (iii) The percentage increase in density of seawater at a depth of 2000 m, given the coefficient of compressibility $\beta_T = 4.4 \times 10^{-10} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Bulk Modulus $K$ is defined as the ratio of pressure increase to the **fractional volume decrease**:

$$K = -V \left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Convert to density form using $\rho V = m = \text{const}$:**

Differentiating $\rho V = m$:

$$V \, d\rho + \rho \, dV = 0 \implies dV = -\frac{V}{\rho} d\rho$$

Substituting back:

$$K = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{-(V/\rho) \, d\rho} = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

$$\boxed{K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}}$$

**Step 3 — Isothermal case (ideal gas, $T$ = const):**
From $P = \rho R T$:

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = RT \implies K_T = \rho R T = P$$

**Step 4 — Adiabatic case (ideal gas, $PV^\gamma$ = const):**
Differentiating $PV^\gamma = C$:

$$V^\gamma \, dP + \gamma P V^{\gamma-1} \, dV = 0 \implies dP = -\gamma P \frac{dV}{V}$$

Thus:

$$K_S = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = \gamma P$$

**Step 5 — Key Distinction:**
Since $\gamma > 1$, we have $K_S > K_T$, meaning a gas is **stiffer** against rapid (adiabatic) compression than slow (isothermal) compression. This is because rapid compression traps heat, raising internal pressure.

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for $K_T$, 2 marks for $K_S$, 1 mark for distinction]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Velocity of sound in seawater:**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_S}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.2 \times 10^9}{1025}}$$

$$c = \sqrt{2.146 \times 10^6} \approx 1464.9 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{c \approx 1465 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Mach number of the submarine:**

Convert speed: $V = 36 \text{ km/h} = 36 \times \dfrac{1000}{3600} = 10 \text{ m/s}$

$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{10}{1464.9} \approx 6.83 \times 10^{-3} = 0.00683$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 0.00683 \text{ (highly subsonic)}}$$

**(iii) Percentage density increase at $h = 2000$ m:**

Pressure at 2000 m depth: $P = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2000 = 2.011 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$

Density change:

$$d\rho = \rho \cdot \beta_T \cdot dP = 1025 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-10} \times 2.011 \times 10^7$$

$$d\rho = 1025 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-3} = 9.07 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Percentage increase:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \frac{9.07}{1025} \times 100 \approx 0.885\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Density increase} \approx 0.885\%}$$

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 3 marks (iii) with full substitution]**

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2021, Modified]

**(a)** Starting from the **Newton–Laplace equation**, derive the expression for the **velocity of sound in a perfect gas**. Show that for air at 27°C, the speed of sound is approximately 347 m/s. **(Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$.)** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic system uses oil (bulk modulus $K = 1.7 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$, initial volume $V_0 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$). If the system pressure suddenly increases by $5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$:

  (i) Find the **change in volume** of the oil.
  (ii) Calculate the **change in density** if the original density is $880 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
  (iii) Comment on whether the oil can be treated as incompressible in this scenario. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Newton–Laplace Equation:**

The speed of a pressure (sound) wave in a fluid medium is given by the ratio of the square root of the medium's elasticity to its inertia (density). For a rapid disturbance, the relevant elasticity is the **adiabatic bulk modulus** $K_S$:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_S}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to a Perfect Gas (Adiabatic):**

For an ideal gas, the adiabatic bulk modulus is:

$$K_S = \gamma P$$

Substituting:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 3 — Use the Ideal Gas Law:**

From $P = \rho R T$:

$$\frac{P}{\rho} = R T$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation for Air at $T = 300 \text{ K}$ (27°C):**

$$c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 300}$$

$$c = \sqrt{120{,}540}$$

$$c \approx 347.2 \text{ m/s} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering Note:** Sound travels ~4.3× faster in water than in air because $K_S / \rho$ is much larger for water (high $K_S$, moderate $\rho$).

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks for Newton–Laplace, 2 marks for ideal gas substitution, 2 marks for ideal gas law, 1 mark for numerical calculation]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Change in volume:**

From $\beta = 1/K$ and $\beta = -dV / (V \, dP)$:

$$\Delta V = \beta V_0 \Delta P = \frac{V_0 \Delta P}{K}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{0.05 \times 5 \times 10^6}{1.7 \times 10^9}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{1.7 \times 10^9} = 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V \approx 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 147 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**(ii) Change in density:**

Using $\Delta \rho / \rho = \beta \Delta P = \Delta P / K$:

$$\Delta \rho = \rho_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{K} = 880 \times \frac{5 \times 10^6}{1.7 \times 10^9}$$

$$\Delta \rho = 880 \times 2.941 \times 10^{-3} = 2.588 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \rho \approx 2.59 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

New density: $\rho = 880 + 2.59 = 882.59 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (a change of **0.294%**).

**(iii) Compressibility assessment:**

Since the fractional volume/density change is only ~0.3% under a substantial 50 atm pressure surge, the oil is **highly incompressible for practical engineering purposes**. However, in high-frequency dynamic applications (e.g., hydraulic shock/hammer analysis), this small compressibility becomes critical, as it stores significant energy and propagates pressure waves. Hence, the assumption of incompressibility is **valid for steady-state design** but **invalid for transient/dynamic analysis**.

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 3 marks (iii)]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in compressibility definition: $dV$ is negative, so the negative sign ensures $\beta > 0$. Omitting it costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Confusing $K_T$ and $K_S$**: Many students write $K = P$ without specifying whether the process is isothermal or adiabatic. Always state the process explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing $\gamma$ values**: Air = 1.4; CO₂ = 1.3; Helium = 1.67. Wrong $\gamma$ → wrong answer.
> 4. **Unit errors in Mach number**: $M$ is dimensionless. Always convert $V$ to m/s when $c$ is in m/s.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment" sub-part** in 7-mark problems: KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for engineering interpretation/justification. Don't just compute — comment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Compressibility** = fractional volume decrease per unit pressure increase ($\beta$).
- ✅ **Bulk Modulus** = reciprocal of compressibility ($K = 1/\beta$); has units of pressure (Pa).
- ✅ **Density form:** $K = \rho \left( \dfrac{dP}{d\rho} \right)$ — high-yield KTU formula.
- ✅ **Isothermal Bulk Modulus (ideal gas):** $K_T = P$.
- ✅ **Adiabatic Bulk Modulus (ideal gas):** $K_S = \gamma P$.
- ✅ **Relation:** $K_S = \gamma K_T$ and $\beta_T = \gamma \beta_S$.
- ✅ **Newton–Laplace:** $c = \sqrt{K_S / \rho} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ for ideal gas.
- ✅ **Mach Number:** $M = V / c$. $M < 1$ subsonic; $M = 1$ sonic; $M > 1$ supersonic.
- ✅ **Water** at 20°C: $K \approx 2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa → treated as **incompressible**.
- ✅ **Air** at STP: $K \approx 1.01 \times 10^5$ Pa → **highly compressible**.
- ✅ **Density variation with depth:** $\rho(h) = \rho_0 / (1 - \rho_0 \beta_T g h)$.
- ✅ **Remember the negative sign** in the compressibility definition — a classic 1-mark trap.
- ✅ **Sound waves use $K_S$, not $K_T$** — because the compression is rapid (adiabatic).
- ✅ **For ideal gases**, $\gamma = C_p / C_v$: Air = 1.4, CO₂ = 1.3, Monoatomic = 1.67.
- ✅ **Use SI units** throughout: Pa, m³, kg, m/s — KTU examiners are strict on unit consistency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capillarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Capillarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capillarity (Capillary Action)** is the phenomenon of **spontaneous rise or fall of a liquid** inside a narrow tube (called a *capillary tube*) — or within any confined geometry such as parallel plates or annular gaps — when the tube is brought in contact with a free liquid surface. This effect arises from the combined influence of **surface tension** at the liquid–air interface and the **wettability** of the solid surface, quantified by the **contact angle (θ)**.

Mathematically, the equilibrium height of the liquid column is governed by the **Jurin's Law** (a direct consequence of the force balance between the vertical component of surface tension and the gravitational weight of the displaced column):

$$h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, R}$$

where every symbol carries a precise physical meaning, summarized in the formula sheet of Section 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Paper Towel Effect"**
> Imagine dipping the corner of a **paper towel** into a shallow bowl of water. Within seconds, the water *climbs upward* into the paper, defying gravity. This is capillarity in action. The narrow gaps between the cellulose fibres act like thousands of tiny capillary tubes. Because the water "likes" the paper (adhesion > cohesion), it wets the fibres and is pulled up.
> Conversely, if you dip a glass tube into **mercury**, the mercury level **falls below** the free surface. Mercury does not "like" glass (cohesion > adhesion), so the surface tension pulls the liquid *downward*.
> **The thumb rule:** *Wetting liquid → rise; Non-wetting liquid → fall.*

### 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Examples

| Phenomenon | Engineering / Natural Observation |
|------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Oil rising in lamp wicks** | Traditional oil lamps rely on capillary rise through cotton wicks. |
| **Water transport in plants (xylem)** | Trees transport water from roots to leaves via microscopic capillary channels. |
| **Soil moisture retention** | Capillarity governs how groundwater rises in soil pores. |
| **Ink absorption in blotting paper** | Narrow pores draw ink upward. |
| **Mercury falling in a thermometer glass** | Classic demonstration of capillary depression. |
| **Damp rising in building walls** | Rising damp in masonry is an unwanted capillary effect. |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two most important surface-tension reference values (at **20 °C** and **1 atm**) for KTU numerical problems are:
> - **Surface tension of water in contact with air: σ ≈ 0.0728 N/m** (or **72.8 × 10⁻³ N/m**)
> - **Surface tension of mercury in contact with air: σ ≈ 0.510 N/m** (or **510 × 10⁻³ N/m**)
> - **Standard acceleration due to gravity: g = 9.81 m/s²** (use **9.81 m/s²** in all KTU numericals unless specified otherwise)
> - **Contact angle of water on clean glass: θ ≈ 0°** (perfectly wetting)
> - **Contact angle of mercury on clean glass: θ ≈ 140°** (non-wetting, hence depression)

### 1.5 Glossary of Foundational Terms

- **Surface Tension (σ):** The tensile force per unit length acting along any line drawn on a liquid surface, in N/m. It arises due to cohesive forces between liquid molecules.
- **Contact Angle (θ):** The angle measured *through the liquid* between the solid surface and the tangent to the liquid–air interface at the point of contact.
- **Adhesion:** The attractive force between molecules of *different* substances (e.g., water and glass).
- **Cohesion:** The attractive force between molecules of the *same* substance (e.g., water–water).
- **Meniscus:** The curved free surface of the liquid inside the capillary tube. **Concave** meniscus → wetting; **Convex** meniscus → non-wetting.
- **Wetting Fluid:** Fluid whose adhesive forces exceed cohesive forces (θ < 90°) — e.g., water on glass.
- **Non-Wetting Fluid:** Fluid whose cohesive forces exceed adhesive forces (θ > 90°) — e.g., mercury on glass.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions That Govern the Direction of Capillary Action

The *sign* of the rise (h) is dictated entirely by **cos θ**:

1. **If θ < 90° (wetting fluid, e.g., water on clean glass):** cos θ > 0 ⇒ h > 0 → **capillary rise (concave meniscus).**
2. **If θ = 90° (neutral wetting):** cos θ = 0 ⇒ h = 0 → **no rise, no fall.**
3. **If θ > 90° (non-wetting fluid, e.g., mercury on glass):** cos θ < 0 ⇒ h < 0 → **capillary depression (convex meniscus).**

### 2.2 Force-Balance Logic (Why and How)

The derivation is essentially a **two-force equilibrium** between:

- **Upward driving force:** The vertical component of surface tension acting along the wetted contact perimeter of the tube.
- **Downward resisting force:** The weight of the liquid column lifted (or pushed down) inside the tube.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the equation:** Surface tension is a *line force* (force per unit length of contact line). Weight is a *body force* acting on the *volume* of the lifted column. Equating these two gives rise height as a balance between **interfacial energetics** (numerator) and **gravitational pull** (denominator). This is why smaller tubes produce *taller* capillary columns — the contact line (and hence the surface-tension force) scales linearly with radius, while the weight scales as the square of the radius.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these expressions — they are the **backbone of any KTU 2024 question on Capillarity**.

| # | Geometry | Rise / Fall Formula | Equivalent Diameter Form | Notes |
|---|----------|---------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Cylindrical Capillary Tube** | $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, R}$ | $h = \dfrac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, d}$ | R = radius, d = diameter |
| 2 | **Capillary Rise between Two Parallel Plates** (separation a) | $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, a}$ | — | Two free surfaces (top + bottom) |
| 3 | **Capillary Rise in Annular Space** (outer R, inner r) | $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, (R - r)}$ | — | Gap = (R − r) |
| 4 | **Time to Reach Equilibrium Capillary Rise** | $t = \dfrac{128 \, \mu \, h \, L}{\pi \, \rho \, g \, d^{4}}$ | (Falling-head form, Lucas–Washburn) | L = tube length, μ = viscosity |
| 5 | **Jurin's Law — General Form** | $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, R}$ | — | For perfectly clean glass–water: θ = 0° ⇒ cos θ = 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Vigilance (KTU Examiner's Mark-Deduction Hotspot):**
> - σ in **N/m** (≡ kg/s²)
> - ρ in **kg/m³**
> - g in **m/s²**
> - R, d, a, h in **metres (m)**
> - μ (dynamic viscosity) in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**
> - **t** in **seconds (s)**
>
> Convert **mm → m**, **cm → m**, **diameter → radius** *before* plugging into the formula. A wrong unit conversion is the **#1 reason for losing 1–2 marks** in a 3-mark sub-question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic & Civil Engineering:** Predicting moisture migration in soils and concrete pores.
- **Petroleum Engineering:** Capillary pressure is critical in oil-recovery displacement curves.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow in capillaries of the circulatory system.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Designing wicking filters, chromatography columns, and absorbents.
- **Civil/Geotechnical Engineering:** Capillary rise influences *effective stress* in unsaturated soils, hence bearing capacity and frost heave.
- **Printing & Textile Engineering:** Ink penetration, dye wicking in fabrics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conventions used in all derivations:**
> - σ = surface tension of the liquid (N/m)
> - θ = contact angle between liquid and solid (measured *through* the liquid)
> - ρ = mass density of the liquid (kg/m³)
> - g = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
> - All lengths (R, d, a, h) are in **metres**.
> - The liquid is assumed to be in **static equilibrium** (no acceleration).
> - The free surface far from the tube is taken as the **datum (h = 0)**.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capillary Rise in a Vertical Cylindrical Tube (Jurin's Law)

**Physical Setup:** A glass capillary tube of inner radius **R** is dipped into a reservoir of a wetting liquid (e.g., water). The liquid rises inside the tube to an equilibrium height **h** above the free surface of the reservoir.

**Free-Body Diagram:** Consider the cylindrical column of liquid of height **h** inside the tube as a free body. The following forces act on it:

1. **Upward force due to surface tension** $F_{\sigma}$, acting along the wetted contact perimeter $2\pi R$.
2. **Downward weight of the liquid column** $W$, equal to mass × gravity.
3. **Atmospheric pressure** acts on the free surface outside (and on the meniscus inside) — it cancels out, so we may ignore it for the purpose of the force balance.
4. **Pressure on the curved meniscus** is balanced by the surface-tension reaction (this is implicit in the force balance below).

**Step 1: Magnitude of the upward surface-tension force**

The surface tension σ acts along the entire contact perimeter, which is the inner circumference of the tube at the meniscus. This perimeter is $2\pi R$. However, only the **vertical component** of σ supports the column. If the meniscus makes an angle θ with the tube wall, then the vertical component is $\sigma \cos\theta$.

$$F_{\sigma} = \sigma \cos\theta \times (\text{contact length}) = \sigma \cos\theta \times 2\pi R$$

**Step 2: Magnitude of the downward weight of the liquid column**

The cross-sectional area of the column is $\pi R^{2}$, the height is h, and the liquid density is ρ.

$$W = (\text{Volume}) \times \rho \times g = (\pi R^{2} \, h) \times \rho \times g$$

**Step 3: Apply static equilibrium (Upward force = Downward force)**

$$2\pi R \, \sigma \cos\theta = \pi R^{2} \, h \, \rho \, g$$

**Step 4: Solve for h**

Divide both sides by $\pi R$:

$$2 \sigma \cos\theta = R \, h \, \rho \, g$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, R}\,}$$

**Step 5: Alternative form using diameter d = 2R**

Substitute $R = d/2$:

$$h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, (d/2)} = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The height $h$ is **inversely proportional to the tube radius** — halving the tube radius *doubles* the capillary rise. This is the basis of the famous "*thin wicks work better than thick ones*" principle used in oil lamps.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capillary Rise between Two Vertical Parallel Plates

**Physical Setup:** Two long vertical parallel plates, each of length **L** (into the page) and separated by a small gap **a**, are dipped into a wetting liquid. The liquid rises between the plates.

**Step 1: Identify the contact lines**

The liquid wets *both* plates. Therefore, there are **two contact lines** — one on the inner face of each plate — each of length **L**.

Total contact length = $2 L$.

**Step 2: Vertical component of the surface-tension force**

$$F_{\sigma} = \sigma \cos\theta \times 2L = 2 L \sigma \cos\theta$$

**Step 3: Weight of the liquid column between the plates**

Cross-sectional area between the plates = $L \times a$ (length × gap).
Volume = $L \times a \times h$.

$$W = L \, a \, h \, \rho \, g$$

**Step 4: Equate the two forces**

$$2 L \sigma \cos\theta = L \, a \, h \, \rho \, g$$

Cancel **L** from both sides:

$$2 \sigma \cos\theta = a \, h \, \rho \, g$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, a}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the formula has a "2" for two plates but a different structure for tubes:** In the parallel-plate case, the contact length is *linear* in L (the plate length), and the column cross-section is *linear* in L. The L cancels, leaving the rise height independent of the plate length. In the cylindrical case, the contact length is *linear* in R, but the cross-section is *quadratic* in R, so R doesn't fully cancel.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capillary Rise in an Annular Space

**Physical Setup:** A small inner tube of outer radius **r** is placed concentrically inside a larger outer tube of inner radius **R**. The annular gap of width $(R - r)$ is dipped into a wetting liquid.

**Step 1: Identify the two contact lines**

The liquid wets *both* surfaces:
- Outer surface of the inner tube: contact length $= 2 \pi r$.
- Inner surface of the outer tube: contact length $= 2 \pi R$.

Total contact length:

$$L_{total} = 2 \pi r + 2 \pi R = 2 \pi (r + R)$$

**Step 2: Upward surface-tension force**

$$F_{\sigma} = 2 \pi (R + r) \, \sigma \cos\theta$$

**Step 3: Weight of the liquid in the annular space**

Cross-sectional area of the annular region:

$$A = \pi R^{2} - \pi r^{2} = \pi (R^{2} - r^{2}) = \pi (R - r)(R + r)$$

Volume of the liquid column = $A \times h$.

$$W = \pi (R - r)(R + r) \, h \, \rho \, g$$

**Step 4: Equate forces**

$$2 \pi (R + r) \, \sigma \cos\theta = \pi (R - r)(R + r) \, h \, \rho \, g$$

**Step 5: Cancel $\pi (R + r)$ from both sides**

$$2 \sigma \cos\theta = (R - r) \, h \, \rho \, g$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, (R - r)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Only the **gap** $(R - r)$ determines the rise, not the absolute radii. A small gap (tightly fitted inner tube) gives a very large capillary rise — this is the principle behind the **scientific wick** and **thermosyphon heat pipes**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem:** A clean glass capillary tube of internal diameter **2 mm** is dipped into water at **20 °C**. Take σ = 0.0728 N/m, θ = 0°, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s². Find the capillary rise.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $d = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ ⇒ $R = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$
- $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

Apply Jurin's Law:

$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho \, g \, d}$$

$$h = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.2912}{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{\,h \approx 0.01484 \text{ m} = 14.84 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** A 2 mm glass tube produces a capillary rise of nearly **15 mm** in water — a value that should always be remembered for sanity-checking KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The physical geometry of capillary rise involves curved menisci and 3-D meniscus shapes that are difficult to render in ASCII/Mermaid. Below is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps the *interactions* between the physical variables governing capillarity — the kind of conceptual map KTU examiners expect in design-style or derivation-style answers.

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Variables Governing Capillary Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Capillarity Phenomenon] --> B[Liquid Property Inputs]
    A --> C[Solid Geometry Inputs]
    A --> D[Liquid-Solid Interface Property]
    A --> E[Environmental Inputs]
    A --> F[Output Response h]
    
    B --> B1[Surface Tension sigma N/m]
    B --> B2[Liquid Density rho kg per m3]
    B --> B3[Dynamic Viscosity mu Pa s]
    
    C --> C1[Cylindrical Tube radius R]
    C --> C2[Parallel Plates gap a]
    C --> C3[Annular Space outer R inner r]
    
    D --> D1[Contact Angle theta degrees]
    D1 --> D1a[theta less than 90 wetting]
    D1 --> D1b[theta greater than 90 non wetting]
    D1 --> D1c[theta equals 90 neutral]
    
    E --> E1[Gravity g 9.81 m per s2]
    E --> E2[Temperature T 20 deg C]
    E --> E3[Atmospheric Pressure P atm]
    
    F --> F1[Capillary Rise h positive]
    F --> F2[Capillary Fall h negative]
    F --> F3[No Change h equals 0]
    
    B1 --> J1[Jurins Law Solver]
    B2 --> J1
    C1 --> J1
    C2 --> J2[Parallel Plate Solver]
    C3 --> J3[Annular Gap Solver]
    D1 --> J1
    D1 --> J2
    D1 --> J3
    E1 --> J1
    E1 --> J2
    E1 --> J3
    
    J1 --> F
    J2 --> F
    J3 --> F
```

### 4.2 Capillary Action Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Liquid meets Solid Surface] --> Q1{Compare Adhesion vs Cohesion}
    
    Q1 -->|Adhesion greater than Cohesion| W1[Wetting Behaviour]
    Q1 -->|Cohesion greater than Adhesion| NW1[Non Wetting Behaviour]
    Q1 -->|Equal| N0[Neutral Behaviour h equals 0]
    
    W1 --> W2[Contact angle theta less than 90]
    W2 --> W3[Concave Meniscus]
    W3 --> W4[Capillary Rise h positive]
    W4 --> W5[Formula h equals 2 sigma cos theta over rho g R]
    
    NW1 --> NW2[Contact angle theta greater than 90]
    NW2 --> NW3[Convex Meniscus]
    NW3 --> NW4[Capillary Depression h negative]
    NW4 --> NW5[Formula h equals 2 sigma cos theta over rho g R cos theta negative]
    
    W5 --> End[Output Equilibrium Height h]
    NW5 --> End
    N0 --> End
```

### 4.3 Text-Based Schematic — Capillary Rise

```
   Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm)
   |                  |
   |  ↓               |  ↓
   |  ↓ Surface       |  ↓
   |  ↓ Tension       |  ↓
   |                  |
   |==================|  ← Free surface of reservoir
   |                  |       (datum h = 0)
   |        \\\       |
   |         \\\      |  
   |          \\\     |    ↑ 
   |           \\\    |    |  h (capillary rise)
   |       ↑    \\\   |    |  
   |   F_σ ↑    ///   |    ↓
   |       ↑    ///   |
   |________↑___///___|  ← Bottom of capillary tube
   |                  |
   |  Weight W of     |
   |  liquid column   |
   |  = πR²hρg        |
   |__________________|
   
   Force balance:  F_σ = W
   2πRσcosθ  =  πR²hρg
   ⇒  h = 2σcosθ / (ρgR)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]** *(3 marks)*
> **Define capillarity. What determines whether a liquid will rise or fall in a capillary tube?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Capillarity** is the phenomenon of *spontaneous rise or fall of a liquid* in a narrow tube (or in narrow spaces between solid surfaces) due to the combined effect of **surface tension** of the liquid and the **wettability** of the solid surface, characterized by the **contact angle θ**.

The direction of motion is decided by the **relative magnitude of adhesive and cohesive forces**, captured by the contact angle:

$$h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$$

- If $\theta < 90°$ (adhesion > cohesion, e.g., **water on clean glass**), $\cos\theta > 0$ and the liquid **rises** with a **concave meniscus**.
- If $\theta > 90°$ (cohesion > adhesion, e.g., **mercury on glass**), $\cos\theta < 0$ and the liquid **falls** with a **convex meniscus**.
- If $\theta = 90°$, there is **no rise and no fall**.

> **Marking Scheme (KTU 2024):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for contact-angle condition, 1 mark for one correct example.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *(3 marks)*
> **Two clean glass capillary tubes of diameters 1 mm and 2 mm are dipped into water. Which tube will show a higher capillary rise, and by how much (in ratio)? Take σ = 0.0728 N/m, θ = 0°, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

From Jurin's Law:

$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

The capillary rise is **inversely proportional to the tube diameter d**.

$$\frac{h_1}{h_2} = \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{2 \text{ mm}}{1 \text{ mm}} = 2$$

Therefore, the **1 mm tube produces twice the rise** of the 2 mm tube.

Numerically (for verification):
$h_1 = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.02968 \text{ m} = 29.68 \text{ mm}$
$h_2 = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.01484 \text{ m} = 14.84 \text{ mm}$

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the ratio, 1 mark for the numerical verification.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the **capillary rise of a liquid in a vertical cylindrical tube** of radius R. State clearly the assumptions used in the derivation. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A glass capillary tube of internal diameter **1.5 mm** is dipped into water at 20 °C. The contact angle is 0°. Calculate:
>   (i) the **capillary rise** in mm, and
>   (ii) the **magnitude of the surface tension force** supporting the column.
>   Given: σ = 0.0728 N/m, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s². *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions (1 mark):*
1. The liquid is Newtonian and at static equilibrium.
2. The contact angle θ is uniform along the contact line.
3. The meniscus is spherical (valid for very small tubes where R ≪ capillary length).
4. The reservoir is large, so its level is unaffected.
5. Temperature is uniform.

*Derivation (6 marks):* Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes.

Consider the equilibrium of the liquid column of height h inside the capillary tube. The forces are:

- Upward surface-tension force: $F_{\sigma} = 2\pi R \, \sigma \cos\theta$
- Downward weight of column: $W = \pi R^{2} h \rho g$

Equating:

$$2\pi R \, \sigma \cos\theta = \pi R^{2} h \rho g$$

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R} = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}\,}$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given:
- $d = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Capillary rise h (3 marks):**

$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d} = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.2912}{14.715} = 0.01979 \text{ m} \approx 19.79 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Surface-tension force (4 marks):**

The vertical surface-tension force is:

$$F_{\sigma} = 2\pi R \, \sigma \cos\theta = \pi d \, \sigma \cos\theta$$

$$F_{\sigma} = \pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0728 \times 1$$

$$F_{\sigma} = 3.4307 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N} \approx 0.343 \text{ mN}$$

**Verification:** Weight of column = $\pi R^{2} h \rho g = \pi \times (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 0.01979 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \approx 3.43 \times 10^{-4}$ N. ✔ The forces balance.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **capillary rise of a liquid between two vertical parallel plates** separated by a distance *a*. Show that the result differs from the cylindrical case by a factor of **2**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Two long vertical parallel plates, each of width 50 mm (into the page), are separated by a gap of **1.2 mm** and dipped into water at 20 °C. Take σ = 0.0728 N/m, θ = 0°, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s². Compute:
>   (i) the **capillary rise h** in mm, and
>   (ii) the **vertical force per unit width** that the surface tension exerts on each plate. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes.

*Step 1:* The liquid wets both plates → two contact lines, each of length L (into the page). Total contact length = $2L$.

*Step 2:* Upward surface-tension force = $2L \, \sigma \cos\theta$.

*Step 3:* Weight of liquid column between the plates = $L \cdot a \cdot h \cdot \rho g$.

*Step 4:* Equate:

$$2 L \sigma \cos\theta = L \, a \, h \rho g$$

*Step 5:* Cancel L and solve:

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g a}\,}$$

**Comparison with cylindrical case:**

Cylindrical: $h_{cyl} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R} = \dfrac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$.

Parallel plates: $h_{plates} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g a}$.

If we set the gap $a = R$ (i.e., compare a tube of radius R with plates of gap R), the rise in the plates is **half** the rise in the tube. Conversely, to get the same rise, $a$ must be $2R$. *(2 marks for the comparison and physical insight.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $a = 1.2 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 0°$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(i) Capillary rise (3 marks):**

$$h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g a} = \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.1456}{11.772} = 0.01237 \text{ m} \approx 12.37 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Force per unit width on each plate (4 marks):**

Surface tension acts along the *single* contact line of one plate (length = 1 m for per-unit-width calculation). The vertical component of surface-tension force on one plate is:

$$F_{per\,m} = \sigma \cos\theta \times 1 = 0.0728 \times 1 \times 1 = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Hotspots in Capillarity Questions — Avoid These!**
> 1. **Unit-Conversion Error:** Students often forget to convert mm → m. A 1 mm radius treated as 1 m will yield a *microscopic* rise. **Always convert first, plug in second.**
> 2. **Forgetting cos θ in the formula:** Writing $h = 2\sigma/(\rho g R)$ without $\cos\theta$ loses **1 mark** instantly. The contact angle is *always* part of the answer unless θ = 0° is given.
> 3. **Sign Convention:** Capillary *depression* (mercury) has **negative h**. Do not write a negative magnitude as positive; mention the **direction (depression)** explicitly.
> 4. **Diameter vs Radius Confusion:** Jurin's Law is sometimes written with R, sometimes with d. The d-form has a factor of 4, not 2. Mixing the two is a classic slip.
> 5. **Atmospheric-Pressure Cancellation:** Students sometimes try to "include" atmospheric pressure and get confused. The pressures on the *concave* side of the meniscus are different on the two sides, but this is *already accounted for* in the surface-tension term. Do not double-count.
> 6. **Wrong Meniscus Type:** Mentioning the *shape* of the meniscus (concave/convex) in the descriptive part earns ½ to 1 extra mark.
> 7. **Mixing Parallel-Plate Formula with Cylindrical Formula:** These are *separate* formulas. The parallel-plate formula is **not** obtained by replacing R with a/2 in the cylindrical one (it would give a different factor).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Capillarity (Module 1, PEPOT416)**

- **Definition:** Capillarity = rise/fall of liquid in narrow confined spaces, driven by surface tension × contact-angle effects.
- **Jurin's Law (cylindrical tube):** $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R} = \dfrac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$.
- **Parallel plates (gap a):** $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g a}$.
- **Annular gap (R, r):** $h = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g (R - r)}$ — *only the gap matters*.
- **Sign Rule:** θ < 90° ⇒ **rise** (wetting, e.g., water–glass, θ = 0°); θ > 90° ⇒ **depression** (non-wetting, e.g., mercury–glass, θ ≈ 140°); θ = 90° ⇒ no change.
- **Reference values:** σ_water ≈ **0.0728 N/m**; σ_mercury ≈ **0.510 N/m**; g = **9.81 m/s²**.
- **Inverse relationship:** h ∝ 1/R or 1/d or 1/a — smaller geometry → larger rise.
- **Meniscus rule:** Concave meniscus ⇔ wetting ⇔ rise. Convex meniscus ⇔ non-wetting ⇔ depression.
- **Force balance idea:** Surface-tension force (along contact line) balances the weight of the lifted column. *This is the conceptual heart of every derivation.*
- **Time-dependent rise (Lucas–Washburn):** $t = \dfrac{128 \mu h L}{\pi \rho g d^{4}}$ — useful for wick-design problems.
- **Common real-world examples:** Oil-lamp wicks, plant xylem, rising damp in walls, blotting paper, mercury thermometers, blood flow in capillaries.
- **Unit hygiene:** Always convert mm → m, d → R where required, and σ → N/m. Keep ρ in kg/m³ and g in m/s².
- **Examiner loves:** A clean free-body diagram, the assumption list, the force-balance table, and the final boxed answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheology of Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rheology of Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rheology?

**Rheology** is the branch of **continuum mechanics** dedicated to the study of the **flow and deformation of matter**, particularly the complex behavior of materials that cannot be described by classical fluid mechanics or elasticity alone. The term is derived from the Greek word *rheos*, meaning "flow" or "stream," and was coined by **Eugene C. Bingham** in 1920.

In the context of fluid mechanics, rheology classifies fluids based on how they resist deformation (flow) under an applied **shear stress** ($\tau$). The simplest, ideal fluids follow a single linear rule (Newton's Law of Viscosity), but real engineering fluids often deviate from this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (Syllabus Highlight):**
> *Rheology is the science of deformation and flow of matter. It describes the interrelation between force, deformation, and time. In fluid mechanics, rheological behavior categorizes fluids based on the relationship between shear stress ($\tau$) and rate of shear strain / velocity gradient ($du/dy$).*

## 1.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation

For an **ideal (Newtonian) fluid**, the shear stress at any point in a flowing fluid is proportional to the rate of deformation (velocity gradient) at that point.

The mathematical form is:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at a point (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (or absolute) viscosity of the fluid (in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient or rate of shear strain $\dot{\gamma}$ (in **s⁻¹**)

For kinematic viscosity:
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Where $\rho$ is the mass density (in **kg/m³**) and $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity (in **m²/s**).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Honey vs. Water Intuition

Imagine you have two jars — one filled with **water** and one with **honey**.

- **Water** is a Newtonian fluid. If you tilt the jar, it flows immediately. The harder you shake it (more shear), the more it resists proportionally. Its internal "friction" is constant.
- **Honey** is more complex. It resists flow until you apply a certain minimum force. Once flowing, it might pour easily. Some fluids like **ketchup** actually become *thinner* (easier to flow) when shaken — these are shear-thinning fluids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Three Personalities of Fluids":**
> - **Newtonian (The Predictable Worker):** Works exactly as told. The more you push, the more it resists — linearly. (Water, air, thin oils)
> - **Shear-Thinning (The Lazy Worker):** Becomes easier to push once you start pushing. (Ketchup, blood, paint)
> - **Shear-Thickening (The Stubborn Worker):** Resists more as you push harder. (Cornstarch slurry, wet sand)
> - **Bingham Plastic (The Reluctant Worker):** Won't work at all until a minimum effort is applied. (Toothpaste, drilling mud)

## 1.4 Why is Rheology Important in Engineering?

- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete, cement slurries, and bitumen behave as non-Newtonian fluids during pumping.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Polymer solutions, food processing (chocolate, sauces).
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow in arteries is non-Newtonian, especially in narrow vessels.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Lubricants can become non-Newtonian under high pressure (boundary lubrication regime).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Universal Rheological Properties

Every fluid's response to shear stress is determined by these five properties:

1. **Elasticity** — The ability to return to original shape.
2. **Viscosity** — Resistance to flow.
3. **Plasticity** — Behavior as a solid until a yield stress is exceeded.
4. **Strength** — Maximum stress before permanent deformation.
5. **Time-Dependence** — Whether the response changes with duration of applied stress.

## 2.2 Comprehensive Classification of Fluids

The rheological behavior is best understood through the **$\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ plot** (rheogram).

### A. Time-Independent Fluids (Properties depend only on current shear rate)

| **Fluid Type** | **Behavior Description** | **Example Fluids** | **Rheogram Shape** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | Linear, passes through origin, $\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$ | Water, Air, Glycerin, Thin Oils | Straight line through origin |
| Pseudo-plastic (Shear-thinning) | Viscosity decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$, no yield stress | Blood, Paint, Polymer solutions, Fruit juice | Concave-down curve through origin |
| Dilatant (Shear-thickening) | Viscosity increases with $\dot{\gamma}$, no yield stress | Cornstarch in water, Wet sand, Sugar solutions | Concave-up curve through origin |
| Ideal Bingham Plastic | Linear after yield stress $\tau_y$ | Toothpaste, Drilling mud, Sewage sludge | Straight line, intercept on $\tau$-axis |
| Casson Fluid | Non-linear after yield stress $\tau_y$ (square root) | Blood, Chocolate, Fruit purees | Square-root curve from $\tau_y$ |
| Herschel-Bulkley | Non-linear (power-law) after yield stress $\tau_y$ | Coal slurries, certain muds | Power-law curve from $\tau_y$ |

### B. Time-Dependent Fluids (Properties depend on duration of shear)

| **Fluid Type** | **Behavior Description** | **Example Fluids** |
|---|---|---|
| Thixotropic | Apparent viscosity decreases with time of shearing, then recovers at rest | Paint, Printing ink, Gelatin |
| Rheopectic | Apparent viscosity increases with time of shearing | Gypsum paste, Some lubricants |
| Viscoelastic | Combines viscous flow and elastic recovery (partial rebound) | Egg white, Bitumen, Polymer melts, Dough |

## 2.3 Mathematical Models of Non-Newtonian Fluids

### A. Power-Law Model (Ostwald-de Waele Equation)

Used for pseudo-plastic and dilatant fluids:

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n = K \dot{\gamma}^n$$

Where:
- $K$ = Flow consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = Flow behavior index (dimensionless)
  - $n = 1$ → Newtonian
  - $n < 1$ → Pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning)
  - $n > 1$ → Dilatant (shear-thickening)

The **apparent viscosity** is:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

### B. Bingham Plastic Model

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau_y$ = Yield stress (Pa)
- $\mu_p$ = Plastic viscosity (Pa·s)

### C. Casson Fluid Model

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \frac{du}{dy}}$$

Where $\mu_\infty$ is the viscosity at infinite shear rate.

### D. Herschel-Bulkley Model (Generalized Bingham)

$$\tau = \tau_y + K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$

This is the most general three-parameter model, combining yield stress, consistency, and power-law behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** For examination questions, you are most likely to be tested on the Power-Law model, Bingham Plastic model, and their applications to fluid flow problems (e.g., friction factor in pipes, velocity profiles).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau$: Pa, $\mu$: Pa·s, $\frac{du}{dy}$: s⁻¹ | Newtonian fluid shear stress |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s | Ratio of viscous to inertial forces |
| Power-Law Model | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $K$: Pa·sⁿ, $n$: dimensionless | Pseudo-plastic / dilatant fluids |
| Apparent Viscosity | $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Pa·s | Effective viscosity at a given $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| Bingham Plastic Model | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_y$: Pa, $\mu_p$: Pa·s | Viscoplastic fluids with linear behavior |
| Casson Fluid Model | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ | Pa½ and Pa½·s½ | Blood, chocolate, food suspensions |
| Herschel-Bulkley Model | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Pa | Generalized non-Newtonian fluids with yield |
| Plastic Viscosity Relation | $\mu_p = \frac{\tau - \tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Pa·s | Slope of Bingham rheogram above yield |
| Reynolds Number (General) | $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime identification |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pipelines for Heavy Oils & Slurries:** The Bingham plastic model determines the minimum pressure gradient required to *initiate* flow — crucial for pumping crude oil in cold climates.
2. **Blood Flow Modeling:** The Casson model is used in medical simulations of blood flow in the heart and arteries.
3. **3D Printing / Additive Manufacturing:** Polymer melts used in FDM printing follow power-law behavior; the printability and extruder design depend on $K$ and $n$.
4. **Drilling Operations in Petroleum Engineering:** Drilling muds are designed as Bingham plastics to carry rock cuttings to the surface while remaining stable when flow stops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Profile of a Power-Law Fluid in a Circular Pipe

> **Objective:** Derive the velocity distribution $u(r)$ for a power-law fluid flowing through a circular pipe of radius $R$ under a constant pressure gradient $\frac{dp}{dx}$.

### Step 1: Start with the Equilibrium Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element

Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $dx$ inside the pipe. Forces acting on it are:
- Pressure force on the left face: $P \cdot \pi r^2$
- Pressure force on the right face: $(P + dP) \cdot \pi r^2$
- Shear force on the curved surface: $\tau \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dx$

For steady, fully-developed flow in the $x$-direction:

$$\sum F_x = 0$$

$$P \pi r^2 - (P + dP) \pi r^2 - \tau (2\pi r) dx = 0$$

### Step 2: Simplify the Pressure Terms

$$- dP \cdot \pi r^2 - \tau (2\pi r) dx = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Shear Stress $\tau$

Rearranging for $\tau$:

$$\tau = -\frac{r}{2} \frac{dP}{dx}$$

> **Note:** The negative sign in $\frac{dP}{dx}$ indicates that pressure decreases in the direction of flow, so $\frac{dP}{dx}$ is negative, making $\tau$ positive.

Let $G = -\frac{dP}{dx}$ (a positive pressure gradient). Then:

$$\tau = \frac{r \cdot G}{2}$$

At the wall ($r = R$), the shear stress is maximum:

$$\tau_w = \frac{R \cdot G}{2}$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Power-Law Model

For a power-law fluid: $\tau = K \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)^n$

Note: $\frac{du}{dr}$ is negative (velocity decreases with $r$ from the center), so we write $-\frac{du}{dr} = \left(\frac{-du}{dr}\right)$ for the magnitude.

Equating the two expressions for $\tau$:

$$K \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)^n = \frac{r \cdot G}{2}$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Velocity Gradient

$$\left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)^n = \frac{r \cdot G}{2K}$$

$$-\frac{du}{dr} = \left(\frac{r \cdot G}{2K}\right)^{1/n}$$

### Step 6: Integrate to Find Velocity

Separate variables and integrate from $r$ to $R$ (where $u = 0$ at the wall due to no-slip condition):

$$\int_{u}^{0} du = -\int_{r}^{R} \left(\frac{r \cdot G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} dr$$

$$-u = -\int_{r}^{R} \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} r^{1/n} dr$$

$$u = \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \int_{r}^{R} r^{1/n} dr$$

### Step 7: Evaluate the Integral

$$u = \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \left[ \frac{r^{1/n + 1}}{\frac{1}{n} + 1} \right]_{r}^{R}$$

Let $m = \frac{1}{n} + 1 = \frac{n+1}{n}$. Then:

$$u = \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{1}{m} \left[ R^m - r^m \right]$$

### Step 8: Final Velocity Profile Equation

$$\boxed{u(r) = \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \left[ R^{\frac{n+1}{n}} - r^{\frac{n+1}{n}} \right]}$$

> **Verification for Newtonian case ($n = 1$, $K = \mu$):**
> The equation reduces to the familiar Hagen-Poiseuille parabolic profile, $u = \frac{G}{4\mu}(R^2 - r^2)$, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Velocity and Mean Velocity for a Power-Law Fluid

### Step 9: Maximum Velocity ($u_{max}$) at $r = 0$

$$u_{max} = \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \cdot R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}$$

### Step 10: Mean Velocity ($\bar{u}$ or $V$)

The mean velocity is given by:

$$V = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2}$$

where the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Substituting the expression for $u(r)$:

$$Q = 2\pi \int_{0}^{R} \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \left[ R^{\frac{n+1}{n}} - r^{\frac{n+1}{n}} \right] r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{2\pi n}{n+1} \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \left[ R^{\frac{n+1}{n}} \int_{0}^{R} r \, dr - \int_{0}^{R} r^{\frac{2n+1}{n}} dr \right]$$

Evaluating the integrals:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi n}{n+1} \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \left[ R^{\frac{n+1}{n}} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} - \frac{R^{\frac{3n+1}{n}}}{\frac{3n+1}{n}} \right]$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$Q = \frac{\pi n}{3n+1} \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} R^{\frac{3n+1}{n}}$$

### Step 11: Mean Velocity

$$V = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{n}{3n+1} \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}$$

### Step 12: Ratio of Mean to Maximum Velocity

$$\frac{V}{u_{max}} = \frac{n}{3n+1} \div \frac{n}{n+1} = \frac{n+1}{3n+1}$$

> **KTU High-Yield Result:** For a Newtonian fluid ($n = 1$), $\frac{V}{u_{max}} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$ (well-known parabolic profile). For pseudo-plastic fluids ($n < 1$), the profile becomes more "plug-like" and the ratio approaches 1. This is a frequent exam question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Friction Factor for a Power-Law Fluid (Fanning's Friction Factor)

### Step 13: Wall Shear Stress Recap

From Step 3: $\tau_w = \frac{RG}{2}$, where $G = -\frac{dP}{dx}$

### Step 14: Express $G$ in terms of Mean Velocity $V$

From Step 11: $V = \frac{n}{3n+1} \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}$

Rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} = \frac{V(3n+1)}{n R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}}$$

### Step 15: Substitute into $\tau_w$

$$\tau_w = \frac{R}{2} G$$

Solving for $G$ from the rearrangement:

$$G = 2K \left[ \frac{V(3n+1)}{n R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}} \right]^n$$

$$\tau_w = R \cdot K \left[ \frac{V(3n+1)}{n R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}} \right]^n$$

### Step 16: Fanning Friction Factor Definition

$$f = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2}$$

### Step 17: Substitute $\tau_w$

$$f = \frac{2K \left[ \frac{V(3n+1)}{n R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}} \right]^n}{\rho V^2}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{2K}{\rho V^{2-n}} \cdot \left(\frac{3n+1}{n}\right)^n \cdot R^{-\left(\frac{n+1}{n}\right)n + 1}}$$

> **Simplified General Form (Fanning):**
> $$f = \frac{16}{Re_{gen}}$$
> where the **generalized Reynolds number** for a power-law fluid is:
> $$Re_{gen} = \frac{D^n V^{2-n} \rho}{8^{n-1} K \left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n}$$

> **Note:** This generalized Reynolds number is critical in KTU problems on non-Newtonian flow in pipes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Shear Stress for a Casson Fluid in a Pipe

### Step 18: Casson Model Setup

The Casson equation is:
$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \frac{du}{dy}}$$

### Step 19: Squaring Both Sides

$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_\infty \frac{du}{dy} + 2\sqrt{\tau_y \mu_\infty \frac{du}{dy}}$$

### Step 20: Recognize a "Plug Flow" Region

In a pipe of radius $R$ with a yield stress $\tau_y$, the fluid flows as a **rigid plug** (zero shear) in the central core where $\tau < \tau_y$. The plug radius $r_p$ is found by setting $\tau = \tau_y$:

$$\tau_y = \frac{r_p G}{2} \implies r_p = \frac{2\tau_y}{G}$$

### Step 21: Volumetric Flow Rate

The total flow rate $Q$ in a Casson fluid pipe is:

$$Q = \pi r_p^2 V_p + \int_{r_p}^{R} 2\pi r \cdot u(r) \, dr$$

> **Note:** $V_p$ is the plug velocity (constant in the central region) and the integral accounts for the sheared annular region.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Fluid Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID CLASSIFICATION]:::main --> B[Time-Independent Fluids]
    A --> C[Time-Dependent Fluids]
    A --> D[Ideal Viscoelastic Fluids]

    B --> B1[Newtonian]
    B --> B2[Non-Newtonian]
    B2 --> B2a[Pseudo-plastic n less than 1]
    B2 --> B2b[Dilatant n greater than 1]
    B2 --> B2c[Bingham Plastic has yield]
    B2 --> B2d[Non-Newtonian Plastic]

    C --> C1[Thixotropic viscosity drops with time]
    C --> C2[Rheopectic viscosity rises with time]

    D --> D1[Maxwell Fluid]
    D --> D2[Kelvin-Voigt Fluid]

    B2c --> B2c1[Ideal Bingham Linear above yield]
    B2c --> B2c2[Generalized Bingham Power Law above yield]
    B2c --> B2c3[Casson Fluid Square root above yield]

    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#d6e9f5,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Rheogram Block: Shear Stress vs Shear Rate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXES[COORDINATE SYSTEM]
        X[Shear Rate gamma dot on X axis]
        Y[Shear Stress tau on Y axis]
    end

    O[Origin]:::origin --> N[Newtonian Straight line through origin]
    O --> P[Pseudo-plastic Concave down from origin]
    O --> D[Dilatant Concave up from origin]
    O --> B[Bingham Plastic Straight line from yield stress]
    O --> H[Herschel Bulkley Curved line from yield stress]
    O --> C[Casson Fluid Square root curve from yield stress]

    N:::fluidA
    P:::fluidB
    D:::fluidC
    B:::fluidD
    H:::fluidE
    C:::fluidF

    classDef origin fill:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fluidA fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000
    classDef fluidB fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000
    classDef fluidC fill:#ffa500,stroke:#000
    classDef fluidD fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000
    classDef fluidE fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#000
    classDef fluidF fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Rheological Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Shear Stress tau]:::input --> M{Model Type}:::decision
    M -->|Newtonian| F1[tau equals mu times gamma dot]:::model1
    M -->|Power Law| F2[tau equals K times gamma dot to power n]:::model2
    M -->|Bingham Plastic| F3[tau equals tau_y plus mu_p times gamma dot]:::model3
    M -->|Casson| F4[sqrt tau equals sqrt tau_y plus sqrt mu_inf times gamma dot]:::model4
    M -->|Herschel Bulkley| F5[tau equals tau_y plus K times gamma dot to power n]:::model5

    F1 --> R1[Result Straight Line]:::result
    F2 --> R2[Result Concave Curve]:::result
    F3 --> R3[Result Shifted Line]:::result
    F4 --> R4[Result Square Root Curve]:::result
    F5 --> R5[Result Shifted Power Curve]:::result

    R1 --> A1[Used for Water Air Thin Oils]:::app
    R2 --> A2[Used for Blood Paint Polymers]:::app
    R3 --> A3[Used for Toothpaste Drilling Mud]:::app
    R4 --> A4[Used for Chocolate Food Suspensions]:::app
    R5 --> A5[Used for Coal Slurries Heavy Mud]:::app

```

## 4.4 Time-Dependent Behavior Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Time Dependent Non Newtonian Fluids]:::title --> TH[Thixotropic]:::type1
    T --> RH[Rheopectic]:::type2
    T --> VE[Viscoelastic]:::type3

    TH --> TH1[Structure breaks down with shearing time]:::desc1
    TH --> TH2[Apparent viscosity decreases]:::desc1
    TH --> TH3[Recovers at rest]:::desc1

    RH --> RH1[Structure builds up with shearing time]:::desc2
    RH --> RH2[Apparent viscosity increases]:::desc2

    VE --> VE1[Combines viscous and elastic effects]:::desc3
    VE --> VE2[Shows partial rebound after stress removed]:::desc3
    VE --> VE3[Modeled by Maxwell or Kelvin Voigt elements]:::desc3
```
