# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Rheology. Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with two examples each.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Rheology** is the branch of physics/engineering that studies the **flow and deformation of matter**, particularly the relationship between shear stress ($\tau$), shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$), and time. It deals with materials whose behavior cannot be described by classical Newtonian mechanics alone.

| **Feature** | **Newtonian Fluid** | **Non-Newtonian Fluid** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$ (linear, $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$) | $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is non-linear or time-dependent |
| Viscosity | Constant (independent of $\dot{\gamma}$) | Variable (depends on $\dot{\gamma}$ or time) |
| Rheogram | Straight line through origin | Curve (concave/convex) or shifted line |
| Examples | Water, Air, Glycerin, Kerosene, Thin oils | Blood, Paint, Ketchup, Drilling mud, Polymer solutions |

**Examples Required:**
- **Newtonian:** Water, air
- **Non-Newtonian:** Blood (pseudo-plastic), Toothpaste (Bingham plastic)

---

### Question 2: Define Apparent Viscosity. How does it vary for pseudo-plastic and dilatant fluids?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Apparent Viscosity ($\mu_a$)** is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear rate at a given condition for a non-Newtonian fluid:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

It represents the **effective viscosity** of the fluid at a particular shear rate and is **not a constant** (unlike Newtonian $\mu$).

**Variation:**
- **Pseudo-plastic fluids ($n < 1$):** $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Since $n - 1 < 0$, $\mu_a$ **decreases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases. *(Shear-thinning behavior)*
- **Dilatant fluids ($n > 1$):** $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Since $n - 1 > 0$, $\mu_a$ **increases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases. *(Shear-thickening behavior)*

For Newtonian fluids, $n = 1$, so $\mu_a = K = \mu$ (constant).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Convention:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question carries 14 marks split into 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A: Power-Law Fluid in a Circular Pipe
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]`

A power-law fluid with flow behavior index $n = 0.5$ and consistency index $K = 0.4$ Pa·sⁿ is flowing through a horizontal circular pipe of diameter $D = 0.1$ m and length $L = 50$ m. The pressure drop across the pipe length is $\Delta P = 200$ kPa. Density of fluid is $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the velocity profile for a power-law fluid in a circular pipe and hence find the maximum velocity and mean velocity for the given data.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (Recap from Section 3):**

Starting from the force balance on a cylindrical element:
$$\tau = \frac{r G}{2}$$

For a power-law fluid:
$$\tau = K \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)^n$$

Equating and integrating from $r$ to $R$ with no-slip $u(R) = 0$:

$$u(r) = \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \left[ R^{\frac{n+1}{n}} - r^{\frac{n+1}{n}} \right]$$

**Maximum velocity at $r = 0$:**

$$u_{max} = \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \cdot R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}$$

**Mean velocity:**

$$V = \frac{n}{3n+1} \left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n} R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $n = 0.5$, $K = 0.4$ Pa·sⁿ, $D = 0.1$ m → $R = 0.05$ m, $L = 50$ m, $\Delta P = 200,000$ Pa.

Pressure gradient:
$$G = -\frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{200,000}{50} = 4000 \text{ Pa/m}$$

Compute $\frac{G}{2K}$:
$$\frac{G}{2K} = \frac{4000}{2 \times 0.4} = 5000 \text{ s⁻¹·m⁻¹}$$

Compute $\left(\frac{G}{2K}\right)^{1/n}$:
$$\left(5000\right)^{1/0.5} = (5000)^2 = 25,000,000 = 2.5 \times 10^7$$

Compute $R^{\frac{n+1}{n}}$:
$$\frac{n+1}{n} = \frac{1.5}{0.5} = 3 \implies R^3 = (0.05)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Compute $\frac{n}{n+1}$:
$$\frac{0.5}{1.5} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Maximum velocity:**
$$u_{max} = 2.5 \times 10^7 \times \frac{1}{3} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^7 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{3}$$

$$u_{max} = \frac{3125}{3} \approx 1041.67 \text{ m/s}$$

**Mean velocity:**
$$V = \frac{n}{3n+1} \cdot \text{common factor} = \frac{0.5}{3(0.5)+1} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^7 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V = \frac{0.5}{2.5} \times 3125 = 0.2 \times 3125 = 625 \text{ m/s}$$

**Ratio check:** $V/u_{max} = (n+1)/(3n+1) = 1.5/2.5 = 0.6$ ✓

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the volumetric flow rate and the wall shear stress for the fluid.

**Model Solution:**

**Volumetric Flow Rate:**
$$Q = \pi R^2 V = \pi (0.05)^2 \times 625 = \pi \times 0.0025 \times 625$$
$$Q = 4.909 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Wall Shear Stress:**
$$\tau_w = \frac{RG}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 4000}{2} = 100 \text{ Pa}$$

**Generalized Reynolds Number (to verify flow regime):**
$$Re_{gen} = \frac{D^n V^{2-n} \rho}{8^{n-1} K \left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n}$$

$$Re_{gen} = \frac{(0.1)^{0.5} (625)^{1.5} (1000)}{8^{-0.5} (0.4) \left(\frac{2.5}{2}\right)^{0.5}}$$

$$Re_{gen} = \frac{0.3162 \times 15625 \times 1000}{0.3536 \times 0.4 \times 1.118}$$

$$Re_{gen} = \frac{4.94 \times 10^6}{0.158} \approx 3.13 \times 10^7$$

This very high value indicates **highly turbulent flow** for the given parameters.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B: Bingham Plastic Fluid Analysis
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]`

A Bingham plastic fluid with yield stress $\tau_y = 15$ Pa and plastic viscosity $\mu_p = 0.05$ Pa·s is flowing through a pipe of radius $R = 0.05$ m under a pressure gradient $G = 3000$ Pa/m. The fluid density is $\rho = 1200$ kg/m³.

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the velocity profile for a Bingham plastic fluid. Find the radius of the plug core and the plug velocity.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

For a Bingham plastic:
$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right) \quad \text{when } \tau > \tau_y$$
$$u = \text{constant (plug)} \quad \text{when } \tau \leq \tau_y$$

Equating with $\tau = rG/2$:
$$\frac{rG}{2} = \tau_y + \mu_p \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)$$

**Plug Radius $r_p$:** Set $\tau = \tau_y$:
$$r_p = \frac{2\tau_y}{G} = \frac{2 \times 15}{3000} = 0.01 \text{ m}$$

**Sheared Region ($r_p \leq r \leq R$):**
$$-\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{1}{\mu_p}\left(\frac{rG}{2} - \tau_y\right)$$

Integrating from $r$ to $R$ (where $u(R) = 0$):
$$u(r) = \frac{1}{\mu_p}\left[\frac{G}{4}(R^2 - r^2) - \tau_y(R - r)\right]$$

**Plug Velocity** (at $r = r_p$):
$$u_p = \frac{1}{\mu_p}\left[\frac{G}{4}(R^2 - r_p^2) - \tau_y(R - r_p)\right]$$

$$u_p = \frac{1}{0.05}\left[\frac{3000}{4}(0.0025 - 0.0001) - 15(0.05 - 0.01)\right]$$

$$u_p = 20 \left[750 \times 0.0024 - 15 \times 0.04\right]$$

$$u_p = 20 \left[1.8 - 0.6\right] = 20 \times 1.2 = 24 \text{ m/s}$$

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the volumetric flow rate, mean velocity, and the wall shear stress. Comment on the flow regime.

**Model Solution:**

**Volumetric Flow Rate:**
$$Q = \pi r_p^2 u_p + \int_{r_p}^{R} 2\pi r \cdot u(r) \, dr$$

The general formula for Bingham plastic flow rate is:
$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 G}{8\mu_p}\left[1 - \frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{r_p}{R}\right) + \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{r_p}{R}\right)^4\right]$$

Substituting values:
$$\frac{r_p}{R} = \frac{0.01}{0.05} = 0.2$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi (0.05)^4 (3000)}{8 \times 0.05}\left[1 - \frac{4}{3}(0.2) + \frac{1}{3}(0.2)^4\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 3000}{0.4}\left[1 - 0.2667 + 0.000533\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{0.0589}{0.4} \times 0.7338 = 0.1473 \times 0.7338$$

$$Q \approx 0.108 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Mean Velocity:**
$$V = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{0.108}{\pi (0.05)^2} = \frac{0.108}{0.00785} \approx 13.76 \text{ m/s}$$

**Wall Shear Stress:**
$$\tau_w = \frac{RG}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 3000}{2} = 75 \text{ Pa}$$

Since $\tau_w = 75$ Pa $\gg$ $\tau_y = 15$ Pa, the flow is well-established in the sheared region.

**Flow Regime Check (Critical Yield Number / Bingham Number):**
$$Bi = \frac{\tau_y D}{\mu_p V} = \frac{15 \times 0.1}{0.05 \times 13.76} = \frac{1.5}{0.688} \approx 2.18$$

A Bingham number of ~2.18 indicates significant yield-stress effects; the flow can be classified as **laminar viscoplastic flow**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always check whether $K$ is given in **Pa·sⁿ** or **Pa·sⁿ·m** for power-law fluids. Wrong units lead to full-mark deductions.
> 2. **Plug Region Error:** In Bingham plastic and Herschel-Bulkley fluids, students often forget to compute the **plug radius** $r_p$ and the **plug velocity** — these are MANDATORY sub-steps.
> 3. **Sign of Pressure Gradient:** $\frac{dP}{dx}$ is negative in flow direction. Using $G = -\frac{dP}{dx}$ as a positive quantity is the correct approach.
> 4. **Power-Law Exponent Confusion:** $n < 1$ is pseudo-plastic, $n > 1$ is dilatant. Mixing these up is the most common conceptual error.
> 5. **Apparent Viscosity:** Examiners often ask the value of apparent viscosity at a specific shear rate. Don't confuse it with Newtonian $\mu$.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Rheology of Fluids:**

- ✅ **Rheology** = study of flow & deformation; coined by **Eugene Bingham** in 1920.
- ✅ **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$; valid **only for Newtonian fluids**.
- ✅ **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ in **m²/s**.
- ✅ **Newtonian fluids** give a **straight-line rheogram through origin**.
- ✅ **Pseudo-plastic** (shear-thinning): $n < 1$, e.g., blood, paint, polymers.
- ✅ **Dilatant** (shear-thickening): $n > 1$, e.g., cornstarch + water, wet sand.
- ✅ **Bingham plastic:** has a **yield stress** $\tau_y$, behaves as solid until $\tau_y$ is exceeded. Examples: toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge.
- ✅ **Power-law model:** $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^n$; apparent viscosity $\mu_a = K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.
- ✅ **Casson fluid:** $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$; used for blood, chocolate.
- ✅ **Herschel-Bulkley:** most general; $\tau = \tau_y + K\dot{\gamma}^n$ (combines yield + power-law).
- ✅ **Thixotropic:** viscosity **decreases with time** of shearing; recovers at rest (paint, ink).
- ✅ **Rheopectic:** viscosity **increases with time** of shearing (gypsum paste).
- ✅ **Viscoelastic:** partial elastic recovery (bitumen, polymer melts, dough).
- ✅ **Velocity ratio:** For power-law fluid, $V/u_{max} = (n+1)/(3n+1)$. For $n = 1$, this equals **0.5** (parabolic).
- ✅ **Plug flow region** exists in yield-stress fluids (Bingham, Casson, Herschel-Bulkley) with plug radius $r_p = 2\tau_y / G$.
- ✅ **Generalized Reynolds number** for power-law fluid: $Re_{gen} = \frac{D^n V^{2-n}\rho}{8^{n-1} K \left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n}$.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress** in pipe flow: $\tau_w = RG/2$ (for any fluid in laminar pipe flow).
- ✅ **Apparent viscosity** is **not a fluid property** — it depends on the operating shear rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Hooks (Exam Day Quick Recall):**
> - *Pseudo-plastic = Pastries, Polymers, Paints* (all shear-thinning)
> - *Dilatant = Dense, Doughy, Dry* (shear-thickening)
> - *Bingham = Bitumen, Butter, Bentonite* (yield stress)
> - *Casson = Chocolates, Coagulants* (square-root)
> - *Thixotropic = Time-remembers* (gets thinner with time)
> - *Rheopectic = Rises* (gets thicker with time)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheological Classification of Fluids: Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rheological Classification of Fluids: Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Rheology? **Rheology** is the branch of continuum mechanics that studies the **deformation and flow of matter**, particularly the relationship between **applied shear stress (τ)**, **shear rate (γ̇)**, and **time**. The word is derived from the Greek word *rheo*, meaning "to flow." In simpler terms, rheology answers the question: > *"When you push or pull on a fluid, how does it respond — does it flow like water, stretch like syrup, or behave like a soft solid?"* ## 1.2 Definition of Fluid Classification (KTU Syllabus Definition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition — Rheological Classification of Fluids** > The rheological classification of fluids is a systematic categorization of fluids based on their **constitutive behavior**, i.e., the mathematical relationship between the **shear stress (τ)** acting on the fluid and the resulting **rate of shear strain (du/dy)**. Fluids are classified into: > 1. **Newtonian Fluids** — fluids that obey Newton's Law of Viscosity with a **constant coefficient of viscosity (μ)** independent of shear rate. > 2. **Non-Newtonian Fluids** — fluids that do **not** obey Newton's Law of Viscosity; their viscosity (apparent viscosity) is a function of shear rate and/or time. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have two glasses — one with **water** and one with **honey**. - If you stir both with a spoon at the same speed, the **water flows easily**, and the **honey resists strongly**. The honey's resistance changes depending on how fast you stir. - **Water** = a **Newtonian fluid** (its viscosity is a fixed number). - **Honey** = a **Non-Newtonian fluid** (its apparent viscosity changes with how hard you push). - **Toothpaste** is even weirder — it doesn't move at all until you squeeze hard enough (it has a *yield stress*). This idea of "how does the fluid respond when you push it?" is the **core of rheology**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Engineering Constants / Metrics for this Topic:** > - **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** Units: **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s** (SI) or **Poiseuille (Pl)** in CGS. > - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Units: **m²/s** or **Stokes (St)** in CGS. > - **Shear Stress (τ):** Units: **N/m²** or **Pa**. > - **Shear Rate (γ̇ = du/dy):** Units: **s⁻¹** or **1/s**. > - **Standard gravity g = 9.81 m/s²** (used in many derivations). > - **Water at 20°C:** μ ≈ **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**, ρ ≈ **998 kg/m³**. --- ## 1.3 The Two Master Categories | Category | Behaviour of Viscosity | Constitutive Equation | Common Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Newtonian** | Constant (μ = constant) | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Water, air, gasoline, alcohol, thin oils | | **Non-Newtonian** | Variable (depends on γ̇ and/or time) | Many forms (see §2) | Blood, paint, ketchup, toothpaste, mud, polymer melts | --- ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Fluid Mechanics > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Relevance (KTU Board Examination Focus)** > - The **design of pipelines, pumps, and mixing equipment** for industries like food processing, pharmaceuticals, oil & gas, cosmetics, and biopolymers **depends on accurately classifying the working fluid.** > - Wrong assumption of Newtonian behaviour (e.g., modelling blood as water) leads to **erroneous pressure drop calculations** — a frequently asked KTU question. > - This topic is the **foundation** for understanding laminar/turbulent flow, boundary layers, and CFD modelling later in the course. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation For a fluid in motion, consider two parallel layers separated by an infinitesimal distance $dy$. The upper layer moves with velocity $u + du$ relative to the lower layer at velocity $u$. The **internal friction** between these layers is quantified by the shear stress $\tau$. **Newton's postulate (1687):** > The shear stress in a fluid is **directly proportional** to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient), and the constant of proportionality is the **dynamic viscosity μ**. $$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa) - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) - $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient / Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) --- ## 2.2 Newtonian Fluids — Detailed Characteristics ### Definition (Formal) > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Newtonian fluid** is one whose viscosity $\mu$ depends **only on temperature and pressure** and is **independent of the shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}$. The shear stress vs. shear rate plot is a **straight line passing through the origin** with slope $\mu$. ### Properties of Newtonian Fluids - The plot of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a **perfect straight line through origin**. - The viscosity $\mu$ is **constant** for a given temperature and pressure. - Only **one rheological parameter (μ)** is needed to fully characterize the fluid. - At rest, the fluid cannot sustain shear stress (it deforms continuously). ### Examples of Newtonian Fluids - **Gases:** Air, nitrogen, oxygen, steam (at low pressures). - **Liquids:** Water, alcohol, gasoline, kerosene, mercury, light machine oils. --- ## 2.3 Non-Newtonian Fluids — Detailed Classification Non-Newtonian fluids do **not** obey $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$ with constant $\mu$. They are classified into **three major families** based on the dependency of viscosity on shear rate and time. ### 2.3.1 Time-Independent (Shear-Rate-Dependent) Fluids These fluids' apparent viscosity depends only on the **instantaneous shear rate**, not on the duration of shearing. #### (a) Bingham Plastic Fluids $$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ - $\tau_y$ = **Yield stress** (the minimum stress needed to initiate flow). - $\mu_p$ = Plastic viscosity (constant slope after yielding). - **Behaviour:** Acts like a solid until $\tau_y$ is exceeded; then flows like a Newtonian fluid. - **Examples:** Toothpaste, mayonnaise, mud, drilling mud, sewage sludge, chocolate. - **Graph:** Straight line that **does not pass through origin**; intercept on $\tau$-axis is $\tau_y$. #### (b) Pseudoplastic Fluids (Shear-Thinning) $$\tau = K \left( \frac{du}{dy} \right)^{n}, \quad 0 < n < 1$$ - $K$ = Consistency index (Pa·sⁿ). - $n$ = Flow behaviour index (dimensionless). - **Behaviour:** Apparent viscosity **decreases** as shear rate increases (it "thins out"). - **Examples:** Blood, paint, ketchup, nail polish, polymer solutions, paper pulp. - **Graph:** Curve passing through origin, **concave down** (slope decreases). - **Apparent Viscosity:** $\mu_a = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$, which decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases. #### (c) Dilatant Fluids (Shear-Thickening) $$\tau = K \left( \frac{du}{dy} \right)^{n}, \quad n > 1$$ - Same form as pseudoplastic, but $n > 1$. - **Behaviour:** Apparent viscosity **increases** as shear rate increases. - **Examples:** Suspensions of starch in water, wet sand, sugar in water, corn starch slurry. - **Graph:** Curve passing through origin, **concave up** (slope increases). > [!NOTE] > **Ostwald-de Waele Power Law (Generalized):** > The power-law model $\tau = K(\dot{\gamma})^n$ unifies pseudoplastic ($n < 1$), Newtonian ($n = 1$, $K = \mu$), and dilatant ($n > 1$) fluids. This is a **high-yield KTU formula**. ### 2.3.2 Time-Dependent Fluids #### (a) Thixotropic Fluids - **Apparent viscosity decreases with time** under constant shear rate. - The fluid "remembers" its rest state; structure breaks down with shearing and rebuilds when at rest. - **Examples:** Yogurt, gels, printer's ink, some paints, clay suspensions. #### (b) Rheopectic Fluids - **Apparent viscosity increases with time** under constant shear rate. - Rare in practice. - **Examples:** Some gypsum pastes, certain lubricants under specific conditions. ### 2.3.3 Viscoelastic Fluids - Exhibit **both viscous and elastic properties**. - Part of the deformation is recovered elastically when the stress is removed. - **Examples:** Polymer melts, bread dough, bitumen, slime, biological fluids like synovial fluid. ### 2.3.4 Special Case: Ideal/Bingham & Herschel-Bulkley Models - **Herschel-Bulkley Model** (combines yield stress and power-law behaviour): $$\tau = \tau_y + K \left( \frac{du}{dy} \right)^{n}, \quad \text{for } \tau > \tau_y$$ - When $n = 1$, this reduces to the **Bingham plastic model**. - When $\tau_y = 0$, this reduces to the **Power-law model**. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Rheological Classification of Fluids** | # | Fluid Type | Constitutive Equation | Key Parameter(s) | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Newtonian** | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | $\mu$ (constant) | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ | | 2 | **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_y, \mu_p$ | $\text{Pa}, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ | | 3 | **Pseudoplastic (Power Law)** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | $K, n$ (with $0 < n < 1$) | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{n}, -$ | | 4 | **Dilatant (Power Law)** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | $K, n$ (with $n > 1$) | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{n}, -$ | | 5 | **Herschel–Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | $\tau_y, K, n$ | $\text{Pa}, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{n}, -$ | | 6 | **Apparent Viscosity (general)** | $\mu_a = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | — | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ | | 7 | **Power-Law Apparent Viscosity** | $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | $K, n$ | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ | | 8 | **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\mu, \rho$ | $\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ | | 9 | **Newtonian Condition** | $n = 1, \, K = \mu$ | — | — | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **NOT** use the vertical bar symbol $\vert$ inside table cells. Use $\backslash \text{vert}$ or $\backslash \text{mid}$ in LaTeX instead. The above table is already correctly formatted. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Fluid Type Used | |---|---|---| | **Food Processing** | Pumping ketchup, chocolate, sauces | Pseudoplastic / Bingham | | **Oil & Gas** | Drilling mud circulation in wells | Bingham plastic | | **Biomedical** | Blood flow modelling in arteries | Pseudoplastic | | **Cosmetics** | Toothpaste, lotions, creams | Bingham / Pseudoplastic | | **Paints & Coatings** | Spray application, brush stroke | Pseudoplastic / Thixotropic | | **Cement / Concrete** | Pumping, pouring | Bingham plastic | | **Polymers** | Extrusion, moulding | Pseudoplastic / Viscoelastic | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's Postulate to Power-Law Generalization ### Step 1 — Start with Newton's Law of Viscosity For two infinitesimally close fluid layers, the internal friction per unit area (shear stress) is proportional to the velocity gradient normal to the flow: $$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ This equation implies that $\mu$ is a **constant of proportionality** (i.e., independent of $\dot{\gamma}$). ### Step 2 — Generalize the Linear Relationship For non-Newtonian fluids, the linear relationship is replaced by a **general functional dependence**: $$\tau = f\left(\frac{du}{dy}\right) = f(\dot{\gamma})$$ The simplest nonlinear extension is the **Ostwald–de Waele power law**, which assumes the shear stress is a power function of the shear rate: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$ ### Step 3 — Reduce the Power Law to Special Cases This is the most important KTU-style derivation. By varying the exponent $n$, we recover all classical fluid types: **Case A: $n = 1$ and $K = \mu$** Substituting into the power-law equation: $$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,1} = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity** — i.e., **Newtonian fluid**. **Case B: $0 < n < 1$ (Pseudoplastic)** When $n < 1$, the apparent viscosity is: $$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$ Since $n - 1 < 0$, an **increase in $\dot{\gamma}$ decreases $\mu_a$** → **shear-thinning**. **Case C: $n > 1$ (Dilatant)** The same apparent viscosity expression $\mu_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ now has $n - 1 > 0$, so an **increase in $\dot{\gamma}$ increases $\mu_a$** → **shear-thickening**. **Case D: $n = 0$ (Hypothetical rigid solid)** If $n = 0$, then $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,0} = K$ (a constant), independent of $\dot{\gamma}$. ### Step 4 — Generalize Further: Add Yield Stress (Herschel–Bulkley) For fluids that **resist flow until a critical stress is applied**, we add a yield stress term: $$\tau = \tau_y + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$ Reducing this: - If $n = 1$, $K = \mu_p$ → reduces to the **Bingham plastic** model. - If $\tau_y = 0$ → reduces to the **power-law** model. - If both $n = 1$ and $\tau_y = 0$ → reduces to **Newtonian**. This **nested reduction** is a frequently tested concept in KTU exams. --- ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Apparent Viscosity of a Pseudoplastic Fluid > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (Model KTU Numerical):** > A pseudoplastic fluid obeys the power-law $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$, with $K = 12 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{n}$ and $n = 0.6$. Compute the apparent viscosity at shear rates of (a) $\dot{\gamma}_1 = 10 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ and (b) $\dot{\gamma}_2 = 1000 \, \text{s}^{-1}$. Comment on the result. ### Solution **Step 1 — Apparent viscosity formula:** $$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$ **Step 2 — Compute at $\dot{\gamma}_1 = 10 \, \text{s}^{-1}$:** $$\mu_{a1} = 12 \cdot (10)^{0.6 - 1} = 12 \cdot (10)^{-0.4}$$ Evaluating $(10)^{-0.4}$: $$(10)^{-0.4} = \frac{1}{10^{0.4}} = \frac{1}{2.5119} \approx 0.3981$$ Therefore: $$\mu_{a1} \approx 12 \times 0.3981 \approx 4.778 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute at $\dot{\gamma}_2 = 1000 \, \text{s}^{-1}$:** $$\mu_{a2} = 12 \cdot (1000)^{0.6 - 1} = 12 \cdot (1000)^{-0.4}$$ Evaluating $(1000)^{-0.4}$: $$(1000)^{-0.4} = \frac{1}{1000^{0.4}} = \frac{1}{15.849} \approx 0.0631$$ Therefore: $$\mu_{a2} \approx 12 \times 0.0631 \approx 0.757 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Comment:** $$\frac{\mu_{a1}}{\mu_{a2}} = \frac{4.778}{0.757} \approx 6.31$$ The apparent viscosity **decreased by ~6.3 times** when the shear rate increased by 100 times. This confirms the **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behaviour — characteristic of fluids like blood and paint. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identification of Fluid from a Given Data Set A common KTU question pattern: given pairs of $(\dot{\gamma}, \tau)$ values, identify the fluid type and compute parameters. **Procedure:** 1. Compute the slope $\dfrac{\Delta \tau}{\Delta \dot{\gamma}}$ for each interval. 2. **If the slope is constant** → fluid is **Newtonian** (slope = $\mu$). 3. **If the plot is linear with a non-zero intercept on the $\tau$-axis** → **Bingham plastic** (intercept = $\tau_y$, slope = $\mu_p$). 4. **If the plot is curved with slope decreasing as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases** → **Pseudoplastic** → use $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$, take logarithms, fit a straight line in $\log \tau$–$\log \dot{\gamma}$ space to extract $K$ and $n$. 5. **If the plot is curved with slope increasing as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases** → **Dilatant** → same procedure as pseudoplastic but with $n > 1$. **Logarithmic Linearization (High-Yield Skill):** Take $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$. Apply $\log_{10}$ to both sides: $$\log_{10} \tau = \log_{10} K + n \cdot \log_{10} \dot{\gamma}$$ This is of the form $Y = a + bX$, where: - $Y = \log_{10} \tau$ - $X = \log_{10} \dot{\gamma}$ - Intercept $a = \log_{10} K$ → $K = 10^{a}$ - Slope $b = n$ **Exhaustive Numerical Illustration:** Suppose a fluid's data in a KTU problem is: | $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | $\tau$ (Pa) | |---|---| | 1 | 5 | | 10 | 25 | | 100 | 125 | Taking logarithms: | $\log_{10} \dot{\gamma}$ | $\log_{10} \tau$ | |---|---| | 0 | 0.699 | | 1 | 1.398 | | 2 | 2.097 | The slope is $\dfrac{2.097 - 0.699}{2 - 0} = \dfrac{1.398}{2} = 0.699$, so $n \approx 0.7$. The intercept is $\log_{10} K = 0.699$ → $K = 10^{0.699} \approx 5.0$. So the fluid is **pseudoplastic** with $\tau = 5 \dot{\gamma}^{0.7}$ (where $K$ has units $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{0.7}$). --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Why the Slope Method Fails for Non-Newtonian Fluids For a Newtonian fluid, the slope of the $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ line is constant: $$\frac{d\tau}{d\dot{\gamma}} = \mu = \text{constant}$$ For a power-law fluid $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$: $$\frac{d\tau}{d\dot{\gamma}} = n K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$ This derivative is **not constant** — it depends on $\dot{\gamma}$. Hence, the **"slope" of the $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ curve at any point is the local (differential) viscosity** $\mu_{diff} = nK\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$, while the **"slope of the line from origin to any point" is the apparent viscosity** $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Their ratio is precisely the flow behaviour index $n$: $$\frac{\mu_{diff}}{\mu_a} = n$$ This relation is sometimes tested in KTU viva questions. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fluid Sample] --> B{Rheological Test
Apply Shear Rate} B --> C{Does tau equal
mu times dot-gamma
with constant mu?} C -- YES --> D[NEWTONIAN FLUID
tau = mu times dot-gamma] C -- NO --> E[NON-NEWTONIAN FLUID] E --> F{Time-Dependent
Behaviour?} F -- NO --> G[Time-Independent] F -- YES --> H[Time-Dependent] G --> I{Is there a
yield stress tau-y?} I -- YES --> J{Yield then constant
slope after yield?} J -- YES --> K[BINGHAM PLASTIC
tau = tau-y + mu-p times dot-gamma] J -- NO --> L[HERSCHEL-BULKLEY
tau = tau-y + K times dot-gamma to the n] I -- NO --> M{Power law n
greater than 1?} M -- YES --> N[PSEUDOPLASTIC
Shear Thinning
0 less than n less than 1] M -- NO --> O[DILATANT
Shear Thickening
n greater than 1] H --> P{Viscosity changes
with time at constant shear?} P -- DECREASES --> Q[THIXOTROPIC] P -- INCREASES --> R[RHEOPECTIC] E --> S[Viscoelastic behaviour
Partial elastic recovery] style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style K fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914 style L fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4500 style N fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style O fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style Q fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082 style R fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000 style S fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#2F4F4F ``` --- ## 4.2 Shear Stress vs. Shear Rate Behavioural Curves ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Origin] --> B[Shear Rate axis] A --> C[Shear Stress axis] subgraph TIME_INDEPENDENT_BEHAVIOUR D[Newtonian: Straight line
through origin, slope mu] E[Bingham: Straight line
offset by yield stress tau-y] F[Pseudoplastic: Curved concave down
slope decreases with gamma] G[Dilatant: Curved concave up
slope increases with gamma] end D -. Example: Water .-> D E -. Example: Toothpaste .-> E F -. Example: Blood, Paint .-> F G -. Example: Starch Slurry .-> G style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914 style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Block Diagram of Rheological Analysis Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Step A: Fluid Sampling] --> stepB[Step B: Viscometer Calibration] stepB --> stepC[Step C: Apply Controlled Shear Rates] stepC --> stepD[Step D: Record Shear Stress Data] stepD --> stepE[Step E: Plot tau vs gamma-d dot] stepE --> stepF{Is the Plot
Linear Through Origin?} stepF -- YES --> stepG[Conclude Newtonian
Slope gives mu] stepF -- NO --> stepH{Is there a
non-zero intercept?} stepH -- YES --> stepI[Conclude Yield-Stress Fluid
Bingham or Herschel Bulkley] stepH -- NO --> stepJ[Conclude Power Law Fluid] stepJ --> stepK{Use log log plot
to find n and K} stepK --> stepL{Is n less than 1?} stepL -- YES --> stepM[Pseudoplastic] stepL -- NO --> stepN[Dilatant] stepI --> stepO[Extract tau-y and mu-p
or fit Herschel Bulkley] stepG --> stepP[Final Fluid Characterization Report] stepM --> stepP stepN --> stepP stepO --> stepP style stepA fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000 style stepB fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000 style stepC fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000 style stepD fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000 style stepE fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000 style stepF fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B style stepG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style stepH fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B style stepI fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914 style stepJ fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B style stepK fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B style stepL fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B style stepM fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style stepN fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style stepO fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4500 style stepP fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Define a Newtonian fluid. Give two examples and state the constitutive equation relating shear stress and shear rate.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 Mark):** A Newtonian fluid is one in which the shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient), with the constant of proportionality being the dynamic viscosity, which depends only on temperature and pressure (not on shear rate). > > **Constitutive Equation (1 Mark):** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ > > **Examples (1 Mark):** Water, air, alcohol, gasoline, light oils, mercury. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Q: Differentiate between a pseudoplastic and a dilatant fluid with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > | Feature | Pseudoplastic | Dilatant | > |---|---|---| > | **Power-law index $n$** | $0 < n < 1$ | $n > 1$ | > | **Apparent viscosity** | Decreases with shear rate (shear-thinning) | Increases with shear rate (shear-thickening) | > | **Curve shape** | Concave downward | Concave upward | > | **Example** | Blood, paint, ketchup, polymer solutions | Wet sand, corn starch slurry, sugar suspension | > > **(1 mark)** each for the table content, **(1 mark)** for the example and concluding statement. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1] **Q: (a) Classify fluids rheologically with a neat sketch of shear stress vs. shear rate for each type. (7 Marks)** **(b) A fluid follows the power-law $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ with $K = 4.5 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{n}$ and $n = 0.55$. Compute the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ and $\dot{\gamma} = 5000 \, \text{s}^{-1}$. Identify the fluid type. (7 Marks)** #### (a) Solution — Rheological Classification with Sketches (7 Marks) **Step 1 (1 Mark): Newtonian Fluid** - Equation: $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ - $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ plot: **straight line through the origin** with slope $\mu$. - Example: Water, air. **Step 2 (1 Mark): Bingham Plastic** - Equation: $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ - $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ plot: **straight line with intercept $\tau_y$ on the $\tau$-axis**. - Example: Toothpaste, drilling mud. **Step 3 (1 Mark): Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning)** - Equation: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $0 < n < 1$. - $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ plot: **concave down** through origin (slope decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$). - Example: Blood, paint, ketchup. **Step 4 (1 Mark): Dilatant (Shear-Thickening)** - Equation: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n > 1$. - $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ plot: **concave up** through origin (slope increases with $\dot{\gamma}$). - Example: Starch suspension, wet sand. **Step 5 (1 Mark): Viscoelastic** - Partial elastic recovery after stress removal. - Example: Polymer melts, bread dough. **Step 6 (2 Marks): Sketches** (All six fluid types should be plotted on a single $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ axis showing the origin, with proper labels and identification of intercepts and curve shapes.) #### (b) Solution — Numerical on Power-Law Fluid (7 Marks) **Given:** $K = 4.5 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{n}$, $n = 0.55$, $\dot{\gamma}_1 = 50 \, \text{s}^{-1}$, $\dot{\gamma}_2 = 5000 \, \text{s}^{-1}$. **Step 1 (1 Mark) — Identify the fluid type:** Since $n = 0.55 < 1$, the fluid is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**. **Step 2 (1 Mark) — Apparent viscosity formula:** $$\mu_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 4.5 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{0.55-1} = 4.5 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{-0.45}$$ **Step 3 (2 Marks) — At $\dot{\gamma}_1 = 50 \, \text{s}^{-1}$:** $$\mu_{a1} = 4.5 \cdot (50)^{-0.45}$$ Computing $(50)^{-0.45}$: $$(50)^{-0.45} = \frac{1}{50^{0.45}} = \frac{1}{5.696} \approx 0.1756$$ Therefore: $$\mu_{a1} = 4.5 \times 0.1756 \approx 0.790 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$ **Step 4 (2 Marks) — At $\dot{\gamma}_2 = 5000 \, \text{s}^{-1}$:** $$\mu_{a2} = 4.5 \cdot (5000)^{-0.45}$$ Computing $(5000)^{-0.45}$: $$(5000)^{-0.45} = \frac{1}{5000^{0.45}} = \frac{1}{36.92} \approx 0.02708$$ Therefore: $$\mu_{a2} = 4.5 \times 0.02708 \approx 0.122 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$ **Step 5 (1 Mark) — Comment:** $$\frac{\mu_{a1}}{\mu_{a2}} = \frac{0.790}{0.122} \approx 6.48$$ A 100-fold increase in shear rate (from 50 to 5000 $\text{s}^{-1}$) caused the apparent viscosity to drop by a factor of ~6.5, confirming the **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** nature of the fluid. --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative] **Q: (a) Explain with neat sketches the difference between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids. Discuss time-independent and time-dependent non-Newtonian fluids in detail. (7 Marks)** **(b) A Bingham plastic fluid has a yield stress $\tau_y = 12 \, \text{Pa}$ and a plastic viscosity $\mu_p = 0.25 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$. Determine the shear stress when the velocity gradient is $40 \, \text{s}^{-1}$. Will the fluid flow if the applied shear stress is $10 \, \text{Pa}$? Justify. (7 Marks)** #### (a) Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1 (1 Mark) — Newtonian Fluids:** Fluids that obey Newton's law of viscosity $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$ with $\mu$ constant. They exhibit a linear $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ relationship through the origin. **Step 2 (1 Mark) — Non-Newtonian Fluids:** Fluids that do not obey Newton's law; their apparent viscosity varies with shear rate and/or time. Their $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ plot is nonlinear or does not pass through the origin. **Step 3 (3 Marks) — Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluids:** - **Bingham Plastics** — require a yield stress $\tau_y$ to initiate flow; afterwards behave like Newtonian fluids with constant plastic viscosity $\mu_p$. Equation: $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$. Examples: toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise. - **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning)** — viscosity decreases with shear rate. Equation: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $0 < n < 1$. Examples: blood, paint, ketchup. - **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening)** — viscosity increases with shear rate. Equation: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n > 1$. Examples: starch suspension, wet sand. **Step 4 (2 Marks) — Time-Dependent Non-Newtonian Fluids:** - **Thixotropic Fluids:** Apparent viscosity decreases with time under constant shear (structure breaks down with shearing). Example: yogurt, gels, printer's ink. - **Rheopectic Fluids:** Apparent viscosity increases with time under constant shear (rare). Example: gypsum pastes, certain lubricants. #### (b) Solution — Numerical on Bingham Plastic (7 Marks) **Given:** $\tau_y = 12 \, \text{Pa}$, $\mu_p = 0.25 \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$, $\dot{\gamma} = 40 \, \text{s}^{-1}$. **Step 1 (1 Mark) — Bingham plastic equation:** $$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ **Step 2 (2 Marks) — Substituting values:** $$\tau = 12 + (0.25)(40)$$ **Step 3 (2 Marks) — Computing:** $$\tau = 12 + 10 = 22 \, \text{Pa}$$ **Step 4 (2 Marks) — Will the fluid flow at $\tau_{applied} = 10 \, \text{Pa}$?** For a Bingham plastic, flow initiates only when: $$\tau_{applied} \geq \tau_y$$ Since $10 \, \text{Pa} < 12 \, \text{Pa} = \tau_y$, the **fluid will NOT flow**. It will behave like a solid and only deform elastically until the yield stress is exceeded. **Final Answer:** $\tau = 22 \, \text{Pa}$ at $\dot{\gamma} = 40 \, \text{s}^{-1}$; the fluid will **not flow** at an applied stress of $10 \, \text{Pa}$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid** > 1. **Forgetting units of $K$:** For power-law fluids, $K$ has units of $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{n}$, **not** $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$. Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** if units are written wrongly. > 2. **Confusing $n$ with $\mu$:** Always remember — for power-law fluids, the **flow behaviour index $n$ is dimensionless**, while $K$ carries the stress-time unit. Do not equate $K$ with viscosity directly. > 3. **Misclassifying Bingham plastics as Newtonian:** If the $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ plot is linear but **not** through the origin, it is a Bingham plastic, not Newtonian. > 4. **Sign errors in $n - 1$:** For pseudoplastic, $n - 1 < 0$, so $\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ is in the denominator effectively. Be careful with the algebra. > 5. **Forgetting to take logarithms:** When asked to determine $K$ and $n$ from data, students often try to fit $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ directly. The **log-log linearization** is the correct method. > 6. **Confusing thixotropy and rheopexy:** Thixotropy = viscosity *decreases* with time; Rheopexy = viscosity *increases* with time. > 7. **Plotting through origin for yield-stress fluids:** The $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ line for Bingham plastic **does NOT** pass through the origin — it intersects the $\tau$-axis at $\tau_y$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** > > ✅ **Rheology** is the study of flow and deformation of matter; it classifies fluids based on their **shear stress vs. shear rate** relationship. > > ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — valid only for **Newtonian fluids**. > > ✅ **Newtonian fluids** have constant $\mu$; plot is a straight line through origin. Examples: water, air, alcohol, gasoline, light oils. > > ✅ **Non-Newtonian fluids** do not obey Newton's law; their apparent viscosity $\mu_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ is variable. > > ✅ **Bingham Plastic:** $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ — needs yield stress. Example: toothpaste, drilling mud. > > ✅ **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning):** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $0 < n < 1$. Viscosity decreases with shear. Example: blood, paint, ketchup. > > ✅ **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening):** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n > 1$. Viscosity increases with shear. Example: starch slurry, wet sand. > > ✅ **Thixotropic:** Viscosity **decreases with time** at constant shear rate. Example: yogurt, gels. > > ✅ **Rheopectic:** Viscosity **increases with time** at constant shear rate. Example: gypsum paste. > > ✅ **Viscoelastic:** Exhibits both viscous and elastic (recoverable) deformation. Example: polymer melts, dough. > > ✅ **Herschel–Bulkley Model:** $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ — general model; reduces to Bingham (n=1), Power-law (τ_y=0), or Newtonian (both). > > ✅ **Apparent Viscosity** of power-law fluid: $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. > > ✅ **Log-log linearization** is the standard method to determine $K$ and $n$ from experimental data. > > ✅ **Master reduction:** Power law with $n = 1$ and $K = \mu$ → Newtonian. > > ✅ **Units to remember:** $\mu$ in $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$, $K$ in $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{n}$, $\tau$ in $\text{Pa}$, $\dot{\gamma}$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$, $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$. > > ✅ **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. > > ✅ **Real-world relevance:** Correct fluid classification is critical for pipeline design (oil & gas), pumps (food industry), and biomedical flow modelling (blood flow in arteries). > > ✅ **Differential vs. Apparent Viscosity ratio** for power-law fluid: $\mu_{diff} / \mu_a = n$. ---

topics.title: Newton's Law of Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton's Law of Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Newton's Law of Viscosity (KTU Board Definition)**
> *"The shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a real fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain, which is the velocity gradient ($\dfrac{du}{dy}$), perpendicular to the direction of flow. The constant of proportionality is called the **dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** of the fluid."*

Mathematically, the law is expressed as:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress in the fluid (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow (in **s⁻¹**)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ is also termed the **Rate of Shear Strain** ($\dot{\gamma}$)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Deck of Cards" Model

Imagine a **deck of playing cards** resting flat on a table, with a coin placed on the top card.

- If you push the top card sideways, it slides over the second card.
- The second card moves slightly (dragged by friction), but slower than the first.
- The third card moves even slower, and so on, until the bottom card (in contact with the table) is essentially **stationary**.

This produces a **layered, telescoping motion** in which each layer moves relative to its neighbour. The frictional "stickiness" between layers is what we call **viscosity**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Takeaway**
> A fluid with **higher $\mu$** transmits more friction from one layer to the next, so it resists motion more (e.g., honey). A fluid with **lower $\mu$** flows more freely (e.g., water, air).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Benchmarks

> [!NOTE] **Standard Reference Values (Must Memorize for KTU Viva)**
> - Dynamic viscosity of **water at 20 °C**: $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ **Pa·s**
> - Dynamic viscosity of **air at 20 °C**: $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ **Pa·s**
> - Kinematic viscosity of **water at 20 °C**: $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ **m²/s**
> - **1 Poise (CGS unit)** = 0.1 **Pa·s** $\Rightarrow$ water = 1.002 centipoise (cP)

## 1.4 Newton's Postulate — The Underlying Hypothesis

Isaac Newton proposed that the **resistance** offered by a fluid to deformation is proportional to the **rate of deformation** itself, not to the deformation magnitude. This is the **Newtonian hypothesis** and forms the bedrock of classical fluid mechanics.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Fluids obeying $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ are called *Newtonian Fluids*.**
> Fluids that **do not** obey this linear relationship are called *Non-Newtonian Fluids* (e.g., blood, ketchup, paint, mud).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Decomposition of Newton's Law

To master this concept, break it into four logical sub-pieces:

### Step 1 — Establish the Shear Stress Concept
Shear stress ($\tau$) is the **tangential force per unit area** acting on a fluid layer due to the relative motion of an adjacent layer.

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} \quad [\text{N/m}^2 \text{ or Pa}]$$

### Step 2 — Define the Velocity Gradient
Consider two fluid layers separated by an infinitesimal distance $dy$. The upper layer moves at velocity $u + du$, and the lower layer moves at velocity $u$.

$$\text{Velocity Gradient} = \frac{du}{dy} \quad [\text{s}^{-1}]$$

### Step 3 — Apply Newton's Hypothesis
The resistance (shear stress) is **linearly proportional** to the rate of shear strain:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the Proportionality Constant
The constant $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity) emerges naturally:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Ready Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables — never the bare pipe `|`.**

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **SI Units** | **CGS Units** | **FPPS Units** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | N/m² (Pa) | dyne/cm² | lbf/ft² |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{(du/dy)}$ | N·s/m² (Pa·s) | Poise (P) | lbf·s/ft² |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s | Stokes (St) | ft²/s |
| Rate of Shear Strain | $\dot{\gamma}$ | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | s⁻¹ | s⁻¹ | s⁻¹ |
| Specific Weight | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | N/m³ | dyne/cm³ | lbf/ft³ |

> [!NOTE] **Conversion Traps to Avoid in KTU Exams**
> - $1$ Poise = $0.1$ Pa·s
> - $1$ Stokes = $10^{-4}$ m²/s
> - $1$ centipoise (cP) = $0.001$ Pa·s
> - $1$ mPa·s = $1$ cP

## 2.3 Why This Law Matters in Engineering

The Newton's law of viscosity is the **constitutive equation** of fluid mechanics — the equation that distinguishes a fluid's mechanical response from a solid's. Without it, we cannot:

- Derive the **Navier-Stokes equations** (governing equations of fluid flow)
- Solve **pipe flow problems** (Hagen-Poiseuille, Darcy-Weisbach)
- Analyze **boundary layer theory** (Prandtl)
- Design **lubrication systems** (hydrodynamic bearings)
- Model **blood flow** in biomedical engineering

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - **Hydraulic machinery** design (viscosity loss = power loss).
> - **Pipeline transport** of crude oil (high $\mu$ ⇒ high pumping cost).
> - **Lubricant selection** for bearings (Sommerfeld number depends on $\mu$).
> - **Atmospheric science** (viscosity of air governs wind shear).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law from Molecular Momentum Transfer

Consider a fluid in laminar flow between two parallel plates. A fluid layer at height $y$ has mean velocity $u$. A layer at $y + dy$ has mean velocity $u + du$.

> **Assumption:** Fluid molecules exhibit a random zigzag motion superimposed on the bulk flow. Molecules crossing from a faster layer to a slower layer bring in excess momentum; molecules crossing in the opposite direction bring in deficit momentum.

Let $N$ = number of molecules crossing per unit area per unit time in either direction.

Momentum transferred per unit area per unit time (which is the **shear stress**):

$$\tau = \frac{\text{Momentum difference}}{\text{Time} \times \text{Area}}$$

Molecule from upper layer carries mass $m$ at velocity $u + du$, molecule from lower layer carries mass $m$ at velocity $u$.

Net momentum flux in the $y$-direction:

$$\tau = N \cdot m \cdot (u + du) - N \cdot m \cdot u$$

Simplifying the difference:

$$\tau = N \cdot m \cdot du$$

Recognizing that $du$ over a small layer $dy$ gives the velocity gradient, and grouping the molecular constants into a single coefficient $\mu$:

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}}$$

This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity** rigorously derived from kinetic theory.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

By definition, kinematic viscosity is the dynamic viscosity divided by the mass density of the fluid.

> **Given:** $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $\rho$ = mass density

Define kinematic viscosity:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Solving for $\mu$:

$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$$

Substitute into Newton's law:

$$\tau = \rho \cdot \nu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Dimensional check:

- $\rho$ has units **kg/m³**
- $\nu$ has units **m²/s**
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ has units **s⁻¹**
- Product: $\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\text{s}} = \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}^2} = \dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2}$ ✓

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Shear Stress Calculation

> **Problem:** A fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s is contained between two parallel plates **10 mm** apart. The upper plate moves at **1.5 m/s** and the lower plate is stationary. Calculate the shear stress on the lower plate and the kinematic viscosity if the fluid density is **900 kg/m³**.

**Given Data:**
- $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s
- Plate separation $dy = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m
- Upper plate velocity $u = 1.5$ m/s
- Lower plate velocity $= 0$ m/s
- Density $\rho = 900$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Compute velocity gradient:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1.5 - 0}{0.01 - 0} = 150 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Law:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.05 \times 150 = 7.5 \; \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.05}{900} = 5.556 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:** $\tau = 7.5$ Pa, $\nu = 5.556 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Force on a Moving Plate

> **Problem:** A flat plate of area **0.5 m²** is dragged at **0.3 m/s** on a thin oil film of thickness **0.4 mm**. The dynamic viscosity of oil is **0.082 N·s/m²**. Find the force required to maintain the motion.

**Given Data:**
- $A = 0.5$ m²
- $V = 0.3$ m/s
- $dy = 0.4$ mm $= 0.0004$ m
- $\mu = 0.082$ N·s/m²

**Step 1 — Velocity gradient:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{0.3}{0.0004} = 750 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.082 \times 750 = 61.5 \; \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Force:**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 61.5 \times 0.5 = 30.75 \; \text{N}$$

**Final Answer:** $F = 30.75$ N

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Fluids

> [!NOTE] **Use `\vert` (vertical bar) carefully — this matrix uses the LaTeX `\vert` macro only inside math mode for absolute value, never in the table delimiter pipes.**

| **Property** | **Newtonian Fluid** | **Non-Newtonian Fluid** | **Examples (Newtonian)** | **Examples (Non-Newtonian)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Relation $\tau$ vs $du/dy$ | Linear | Non-linear | Water, air, gasoline | Blood, ketchup, mud, paint |
| Viscosity | Constant | Variable with $\dot{\gamma}$ | Mercury | Slurries, polymer solutions |
| Behaviour at rest | No yield stress | May have yield stress | Ethanol | Toothpaste, grease |
| Rheological plot | Straight line through origin | Curved / threshold | Glycerine (approx.) | Corn starch + water |
| KTU Exam Treat-ment | $\tau = \mu \cdot du/dy$ | $\tau = \eta \cdot (du/dy)^n$ or Bingham model | — | — |

For Non-Newtonian fluids, the apparent viscosity $\eta_{app}$ is a function of shear rate, and the **Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) model** is commonly used:

$$\tau = K \left( \frac{du}{dy} \right)^n$$

Where $K$ is the consistency index and $n$ is the flow behaviour index ($n < 1$ for pseudoplastic, $n > 1$ for dilatant).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Newton's Law of Viscosity Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Fluid in Motion] --> B{Velocity Gradient Exists?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply Newtons Hypothesis]
    B -- No --> D[Hydrostatic Equilibrium]
    C --> E[Shear Stress Proportional to Rate of Shear Strain]
    E --> F[Constant of Proportionality = Dynamic Viscosity mu]
    F --> G[Equation tau equals mu times du by dy]
    G --> H{Is mu Constant?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Newtonian Fluid]
    H -- No --> J[Non Newtonian Fluid]
    I --> I1[Water]
    I --> I2[Air]
    I --> I3[Glycerine]
    J --> J1[Pseudoplastic]
    J --> J2[Dilatant]
    J --> J3[Bingham Plastic]
    G --> K[Divide by Density]
    K --> L[Kinematic Viscosity nu equals mu by rho]
    L --> M[Used in Reynolds Number]
    M --> N[Flow Regime Classification]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Shear Stress in a Couette-Type Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Lower Stationary Plate velocity equals 0] --> F1[Fluid Layer 1]
    F1 --> F2[Fluid Layer 2]
    F2 --> F3[Fluid Layer 3]
    F3 --> F4[Fluid Layer 4]
    F4 --> P2[Upper Moving Plate velocity equals U]
    F1 -. shear stress tau1 .-> P1
    F2 -. shear stress tau2 .-> F1
    F3 -. shear stress tau3 .-> F2
    F4 -. shear stress tau4 .-> F3
    P2 -. applied force F .-> F4
    P1 --> G[Velocity varies linearly from 0 to U]
    P2 --> G
    G --> H[Velocity gradient du by dy equals U by h]
    H --> I[tau equals mu times U by h]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Effect of Temperature on Viscosity] --> B{Fluid Type}
    B -- Liquid --> C[Viscosity DECREASES with Temperature]
    C --> C1[Reduced cohesive forces between molecules]
    C1 --> C2[Example: Engine oil SAE 10W]
    B -- Gas --> D[Viscosity INCREASES with Temperature]
    D --> D1[Increased molecular momentum transfer]
    D1 --> D2[Example: Air, Steam, Combustion gases]
    C --> E[Formula often used: mu equals A times 10 power B by T]
    D --> F[Formula often used: mu proportional to T raised to 0.7]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Velocity Profile in Linear Shear Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Shear Flow between Two Parallel Plates] --> M1[Lower plate at y equals 0 velocity equals 0]
    M --> M2[Upper plate at y equals h velocity equals U]
    M1 --> V1[Velocity at y equals 0 is 0]
    M2 --> V2[Velocity at y equals h is U]
    V1 --> P[Linear Velocity Profile]
    V2 --> P
    P --> S[Slope du by dy equals U by h]
    S --> T[Shear Stress tau equals mu times U by h]
    T --> R[Power Dissipation per unit volume equals tau times du by dy]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Q1. State Newton's law of viscosity. Mention the SI and CGS units of dynamic viscosity.** `[3 Marks]`

> **Model Answer:**
> Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a real fluid is directly proportional to the velocity gradient ($\dfrac{du}{dy}$) normal to the direction of flow.
> Mathematically: $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$
> - **SI unit of $\mu$:** N·s/m² (or Pa·s)
> - **CGS unit of $\mu$:** Poise (P), where 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s
> - **Dynamic viscosity** is a measure of the fluid's internal resistance to flow. *[3 Marks: 1 for statement + 1 for equation + 1 for units]*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. Differentiate between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity.** `[3 Marks]`

> **Model Answer:**
> | Basis | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Definition | Resistance to deformation | Resistance to flow under gravity |
> | Formula | $\mu = \tau \cdot \dfrac{dy}{du}$ | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ |
> | SI unit | N·s/m² | m²/s |
> | CGS unit | Poise | Stokes |
> | Depends on | Fluid's molecular structure | Both $\mu$ and $\rho$ |
> - Relation: $\nu = \mu / \rho$ or $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ *[3 Marks]*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. A fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s is contained between two parallel plates 12 mm apart. The upper plate moves with a velocity of 1.2 m/s and the lower plate is stationary. Calculate:
- (i) The shear stress on the lower plate.
- (ii) The kinematic viscosity of the fluid if its density is 850 kg/m³. `[7 Marks]`

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Statement:** Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress in a real fluid is directly proportional to the velocity gradient normal to the flow direction.
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$
> where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity. `[2 Marks]`
>
> **Given Data:** $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s, $dy = 12$ mm = 0.012 m, $u_{top} = 1.2$ m/s, $u_{bottom} = 0$, $\rho = 850$ kg/m³
>
> **(i) Velocity gradient and shear stress:**
> $$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1.2 - 0}{0.012} = 100 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$
> $$\tau = 0.05 \times 100 = 5 \; \text{N/m}^2 \; \text{(Pa)} \; \mathbf{[3 \; Marks]}$$
>
> **(ii) Kinematic viscosity:**
> $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.05}{850} = 5.882 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s} \; \mathbf{[2 \; Marks]}$$

**(b)** Differentiate clearly between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with suitable examples. Show the rheological classification diagram for shear stress vs shear rate behaviour. `[7 Marks]`

> **Model Solution:**
>
> | Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | $\tau$–$du/dy$ relation | Linear | Non-linear |
> | Viscosity | Constant | Variable (shear-dependent) |
> | Examples | Water, air, gasoline, glycerine | Blood, ketchup, mud, paint, slurry |
> | Equation | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau = K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n$ |
> | Yield stress | None | May exist (Bingham plastic) |
> *[2 Marks for tabular differentiation]*
>
> **Rheological Classification:** *(Draw or describe the plot)*
> - Newtonian: Straight line through origin (slope = $\mu$)
> - Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning): $n < 1$, curve flattens (e.g., blood, paint)
> - Dilatant (shear-thickening): $n > 1$, curve steepens (e.g., corn starch slurry)
> - Bingham plastic: Linear but with yield stress (e.g., toothpaste, drilling mud) *[3 Marks for classification and description]*
>
> **Additional Explanation:** Power-law model $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ where $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = flow behaviour index. *[2 Marks]*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Define viscosity. Explain in detail the effect of temperature on the viscosity of liquids and gases. Why is this difference critical in engineering applications such as lubrication and gas turbine design? `[7 Marks]`

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Definition:** Viscosity is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the deformation (shear) caused by tangential (shear) stress. By Newton's law: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$. `[1 Mark]`
>
> **Effect on Liquids:** Viscosity of liquids **decreases** with increase in temperature.
> - As temperature rises, the cohesive/adhesive forces between molecules weaken.
> - Molecules can slide past each other more easily, reducing internal friction.
> - Example: Engine oil becomes thinner as it warms up. *[2 Marks]*
>
> **Effect on Gases:** Viscosity of gases **increases** with increase in temperature.
> - Increased temperature → higher molecular random motion → greater momentum exchange between layers.
> - Example: Hot air has higher viscosity than cold air. *[2 Marks]*
>
> **Engineering Significance:**
> - **Lubricant design (Liquids):** Multi-grade oils (SAE 10W-30) maintain viscosity across temperature ranges, ensuring proper lubrication during cold start and hot operation.
> - **Gas turbines / Aerodynamics (Gases):** Hot gas viscosity in turbine blades affects convective heat transfer and aerodynamic losses. Blade cooling systems must account for viscosity variation. *[2 Marks]*

**(b)** A flat plate of area 0.4 m² is dragged across a thin oil film of thickness 0.5 mm at a velocity of 0.25 m/s. The dynamic viscosity of the oil is 0.09 N·s/m² and its specific gravity is 0.86. Calculate:
- (i) The force required to drag the plate.
- (ii) The kinematic viscosity of the oil.
- (iii) The rate of shear strain in the oil film. `[7 Marks]`

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Given:** $A = 0.4$ m², $V = 0.25$ m/s, $dy = 0.5$ mm = 0.0005 m, $\mu = 0.09$ N·s/m², $SG = 0.86$ so $\rho = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860$ kg/m³
>
> **(i) Velocity gradient:**
> $$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{0.25}{0.0005} = 500 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.09 \times 500 = 45 \; \text{N/m}^2$$
> $$F = \tau \cdot A = 45 \times 0.4 = \mathbf{18 \; \text{N}} \; \mathbf{[3 \; Marks]}$$
>
> **(ii) Kinematic viscosity:**
> $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.09}{860} = 1.047 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s} \; \mathbf{[2 \; Marks]}$$
>
> **(iii) Rate of shear strain:**
> $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy} = \mathbf{500 \; \text{s}^{-1}} \; \mathbf{[2 \; Marks]}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Zones — KTU Examiner Reports**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert mm to m in plate separation (10 mm = 0.01 m). *Cost: up to 1 mark lost.*
> 2. **Misidentifying Density:** In kinematic viscosity problems, using $1000$ kg/m³ (water) instead of the given fluid density. *Cost: full marks lost for the part.*
> 3. **Confusing $\mu$ and $\nu$:** Writing $\nu = \mu \cdot \rho$ instead of $\nu = \mu / \rho$. *Cost: 1 mark in definition.*
> 4. **Forgetting $\mu$ is constant** when discussing Newtonian fluids — many students incorrectly write $\mu$ as a function of $du/dy$ even for Newtonian cases.
> 5. **Using $\dot{\gamma}$ for shear strain instead of shear strain rate** — a common conceptual error in Part A definitions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before KTU Exam**
> - **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$ — must be on the tip of your tongue.
> - **SI units:** $\mu$ → Pa·s; $\nu$ → m²/s.
> - **CGS trap:** 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s; 1 Stokes = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.
> - **Water reference values:** $\mu \approx 10^{-3}$ Pa·s; $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ → $\nu \approx 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
> - **Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian:** Newtonian = straight line, constant $\mu$; Non-Newtonian = curved or threshold.
> - **Temperature effect:** Liquids $\mu \downarrow$ as T ↑; Gases $\mu \uparrow$ as T ↑.
> - **Power-law model** (for non-Newtonian): $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ with $n < 1$ (pseudoplastic), $n > 1$ (dilatant).
> - **Most-asked numerics:** Plate-on-oil-film, pipe-flow Couette, kinematic viscosity conversion.
> - **Rheology sketch** (stress vs rate of strain): 4 curves — Newtonian, Pseudoplastic, Dilatant, Bingham — must be drawable from memory.
> - **Final formula to remember:** $\boxed{\mu = \rho \cdot \nu}$ and $\boxed{\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}}$

**End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Newton's Law of Viscosity.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classifications of Non-Newtonian Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classifications of Non-Newtonian Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity (Recap):**
> For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress $\tau$ at any point in a fluid flow is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) normal to the direction of flow.
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$
> where $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (constant) and $\frac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient.

A **Non-Newtonian fluid** is defined as a fluid for which the relationship between the shear stress $\tau$ and the rate of shear strain $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ is **non-linear**, and the proportionality constant (apparent viscosity) is **not constant** — it depends on the shear rate, the duration of shearing, or the previous flow history.

Formally,
$$\tau = \eta_{apparent}(\dot{\gamma}) \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$
where the apparent viscosity $\eta_{apparent}$ is a function of shear rate, time, or both.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are stirring a cup of water versus stirring a cup of tomato ketchup.
- **Water** is easy to stir fast or slow — the resistance stays the same. (Newtonian).
- **Ketchup** feels very thick when you start stirring slowly, but as you stir harder, it suddenly becomes thin and flows easily. The "thickness" (viscosity) changed with how hard you pushed. That is non-Newtonian behavior.

Think of non-Newtonian fluids as fluids that **"change their mind"** about viscosity depending on how you are forcing them to move.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study Non-Newtonian fluids?** Many engineering materials used in chemical, food, petroleum, polymer, cosmetic, bio-medical, and civil engineering (concrete, slurries, drilling muds, blood flow in arteries) are non-Newtonian. Treating them as Newtonian leads to grossly inaccurate design of pipes, pumps, mixers, and processing equipment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification Tree

Non-Newtonian fluids are broadly classified into **three major categories** based on how their apparent viscosity responds to shear rate and time:

1. **Time-Independent (Purely Viscous / Generalized Newtonian) Fluids** — the shear rate at any instant depends *only* on the current shear stress.
2. **Time-Dependent Fluids** — the shear rate depends on the *duration* of applied shear.
3. **Viscoelastic Fluids** — exhibit both viscous (liquid-like) and elastic (solid-like) recovery.

### 2.2 Sub-Classification Detail

#### A. Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluids

**(i) Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning) Fluid**

- Apparent viscosity **decreases** with increasing shear rate.
- Most common non-Newtonian type in industry.
- **Common examples:** blood, milk, paint, ketchup, fruit juice, polymer melts, paper pulp, nail polish.
- **Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) model:**
$$\tau = K \left(\dot{\gamma}\right)^n$$
where $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ), $n$ = flow behaviour index. For pseudoplastic, $0 < n < 1$.
- Apparent viscosity:
$$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

**(ii) Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) Fluid**

- Apparent viscosity **increases** with increasing shear rate.
- **Common examples:** corn-starch slurry in water, wet beach sand, concentrated sugar solution, ceramic suspensions.
- **Power-law model:** $\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^n$ with $n > 1$.
- At rest, particles are closely packed with liquid filling voids; under shear, the structure expands (dilates) and the liquid is no longer sufficient to lubricate, causing resistance to increase.

**(iii) Bingham Plastic Fluid**

- Behaves as a **rigid solid** below a critical (yield) shear stress $\tau_y$.
- Once $\tau > \tau_y$, it flows with a **linear** shear-stress–shear-rate relationship.
- **Common examples:** toothpaste, mayonnaise, drilling mud, sewage sludge, chocolate.
- **Model:**
$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$$
where $\tau_y$ = yield stress, $\mu_p$ = plastic viscosity (constant slope).

**(iv) Generalised Bingham Plastic (Herschel–Bulkley Fluid)**

- Combines a yield stress with power-law behaviour after yielding.
- **Model:**
$$\tau = \tau_y + K (\dot{\gamma})^n$$
- For $n = 1$ → reduces to Bingham Plastic.
- For $\tau_y = 0$, $n < 1$ → reduces to Pseudoplastic.

**(v) Casson Fluid**

- A special yield-stress fluid used to model **blood** and chocolate.
- **Model:**
$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_c \dot{\gamma}}$$

#### B. Time-Dependent Non-Newtonian Fluids

**(i) Thixotropic Fluid**

- Apparent viscosity **decreases with time** of shearing at constant shear rate.
- Structure breaks down gradually; regains viscosity when shear is removed (time-dependent recovery).
- **Examples:** yogurt, printing ink, gels, castor oil, gypsum paste.

**(ii) Rheopectic Fluid**

- Apparent viscosity **increases with time** of shearing at constant shear rate.
- Less common; structure builds up under prolonged shear.
- **Examples:** certain lubricants, fresh cement paste (initial set).

#### C. Viscoelastic Fluids

- Show both **viscous flow** (energy dissipated as heat) and **elastic recovery** (energy stored and recovered).
- After deformation, they partially return to original shape.
- **Examples:** egg white, bread dough, polymer melts, saliva, synovial fluid, slime.
- **Modelling approaches:**
  - **Maxwell model** — series spring and dashpot.
  - **Kelvin–Voigt model** — parallel spring and dashpot.
  - **Standard Linear Solid model** — combination of both.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Non-Newtonian Application |
|---|---|
| Petroleum Engineering | Drilling mud (Bingham plastic) — must suspend cuttings when flow stops |
| Bio-medical | Blood (Casson/Pseudoplastic) — governs flow through arteries and capillaries |
| Food Processing | Ketchup, chocolate, dairy — pipe flow design requires shear-thinning data |
| Polymer Processing | Polymer melts (Pseudoplastic) — die swell and extrusion analysis |
| Cosmetics | Toothpaste, lotions — yield stress determines squeeze behaviour from tubes |
| Civil Engineering | Concrete, cement slurry — Bingham/Herschel–Bulkley modelling for pumping |
| Defense | Body armour using shear-thickening fluids (STFs) — liquid hardens on impact |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Table for Non-Newtonian Fluids**

| Fluid Type | Rheological Equation | Key Parameter(s) | Restriction / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian (baseline) | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | $\mu$ (constant) | $n = 1$ |
| Pseudoplastic | $\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^n$ | $K,\, n$ | $0 < n < 1$ |
| Dilatant | $\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^n$ | $K,\, n$ | $n > 1$ |
| Bingham Plastic | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_y,\, \mu_p$ | Yield present |
| Herschel–Bulkley | $\tau = \tau_y + K (\dot{\gamma})^n$ | $\tau_y,\, K,\, n$ | Most general |
| Casson Fluid | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_c \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau_y,\, \mu_c$ | Blood, chocolate |
| Apparent Viscosity | $\eta_{app} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | — | Function of $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| Thixotropic | $\eta_{app} \downarrow$ with $t$ at constant $\dot{\gamma}$ | — | Time-dependent |
| Rheopectic | $\eta_{app} \uparrow$ with $t$ at constant $\dot{\gamma}$ | — | Time-dependent |
| Viscoelastic | Maxwell / Kelvin–Voigt | $G,\, \mu$ | Partial elastic recoil |

### 2.5 Core Conceptual Logic — "Why and How"

- **Why do fluids deviate from Newton's Law?** Because their microstructure (long polymer chains, suspended particles, flocs) responds to applied shear — orienting, stretching, breaking, or rebuilding.
- **How to identify the type experimentally?** Plot $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ on linear axes:
  - Straight line through origin → Newtonian.
  - Curve concave to $\dot{\gamma}$ axis → Pseudoplastic.
  - Curve convex to $\dot{\gamma}$ axis → Dilatant.
  - Straight line not through origin (intercepts $\tau$-axis) → Bingham Plastic.
  - Hysteresis loop in time plots → Thixotropic or Rheopectic.
  - Rod-climbing / recoil on sudden stop → Viscoelastic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Apparent Viscosity for a Power-Law Fluid

The Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) equation is taken as the starting constitutive equation:
$$\tau = K \left(\dot{\gamma}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 1 — Definition of apparent viscosity.**
By definition, apparent viscosity is the ratio of shear stress to shear rate at any instant:
$$\eta_{apparent} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the power-law expression.**
$$\eta_{apparent} = \frac{K (\dot{\gamma})^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent using index laws.**
$$\eta_{apparent} = K \, \dot{\gamma}^{\,n - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Behavioural interpretation.**
- If $n < 1$: the exponent $(n - 1)$ is negative, so increasing $\dot{\gamma}$ decreases $\eta_{apparent}$ → **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**.
- If $n > 1$: the exponent $(n - 1)$ is positive, so increasing $\dot{\gamma}$ increases $\eta_{apparent}$ → **dilatant (shear-thickening)**.
- If $n = 1$: $K = \mu$ (constant), and $\eta_{apparent} = \mu$ → **Newtonian limit**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Bingham Plastic Flow Curve

**Step 1 — Define the yield criterion.**
Below the yield stress $\tau_y$, the material does not deform continuously; it behaves as an elastic solid.
$$\tau < \tau_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma} = 0$$

**Step 2 — For stresses exceeding the yield value, deformation occurs linearly.**
$$\tau - \tau_y = \mu_p \, \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the standard form.**
$$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \, \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 4 — Slope interpretation.**
The slope of the $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ plot for $\tau > \tau_y$ is the plastic viscosity $\mu_p$. The intercept on the $\tau$-axis is the yield stress $\tau_y$.

### 3.3 Specialisation of the Herschel–Bulkley Model

**Step 1 — General equation.**
$$\tau = \tau_y + K (\dot{\gamma})^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Set $n = 1$.**
Then $(\dot{\gamma})^{1} = \dot{\gamma}$, giving:
$$\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}$$
Comparing with the Bingham form $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$, we identify $K = \mu_p$. Thus Herschel–Bulkley reduces to Bingham Plastic.

**Step 3 — Set $\tau_y = 0$ and $n < 1$.**
The yield stress disappears and the equation reduces to:
$$\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^{n}, \quad 0 < n < 1$$
This is the standard pseudoplastic power-law model.

**Step 4 — Set $\tau_y = 0$ and $n = 1$.**
$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \mu$$
The fluid reduces to Newtonian behaviour.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Comparison Table — All Non-Newtonian Categories**

| Property | Pseudoplastic | Dilatant | Bingham | Herschel–Bulkley | Casson | Thixotropic | Rheopectic | Viscoelastic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_y$ present? | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No | No |
| $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ shape | Curved (concave) | Curved (convex) | Linear (after yield) | Curved (after yield) | Square-root curve | Time-dependent loop | Time-dependent loop | Recoil on stop |
| $\eta_{app}$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ | Decreases | Increases | Constant (post-yield) | Decreases (if $n<1$) | Decreases | Decreases (with $t$) | Increases (with $t$) | Complex |
| Typical example | Blood, paint | Corn-starch slurry | Toothpaste | Drilling mud | Blood (alt.) | Yogurt | Gypsum paste | Egg white |
| Typical $n$ range | $0 < n < 1$ | $n > 1$ | $n = 1$ | $n \neq 1$ in general | — | — | — | — |
| Energy storage? | No | No | No | No | No | No | No | Yes |

### 3.5 Engineering Use-Case Derivation: Pressure Drop in Laminar Pipe Flow of a Power-Law Fluid

For fully-developed laminar flow of a power-law fluid in a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is given by the Rabinowitsch–Mooney equation:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{3}}{\dfrac{1}{n} + 3} \left(\frac{\tau_w}{K}\right)^{1/n}$$

where the wall shear stress is $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L}$.

**Step 1 — Solve for $\Delta P$.**
Rearranging the above equation:
$$\left(\frac{\tau_w}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{3}} \left(\frac{1}{n} + 3\right)$$

$$\tau_w = K \left[\frac{Q}{\pi R^{3}} \left(\frac{1}{n} + 3\right)\right]^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\tau_w = \Delta P R / (2 L)$.**
$$\frac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L} = K \left[\frac{Q}{\pi R^{3}} \left(\frac{1}{n} + 3\right)\right]^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $\Delta P$.**
$$\Delta P = \frac{2 L K}{R} \left[\frac{Q}{\pi R^{3}} \left(\frac{1}{n} + 3\right)\right]^{n}$$

This is the design equation used in practice for pumping pseudoplastic polymer solutions through pipes.

    q2 -- "Yes" --> typeBP["Bingham Plastic"]
