**Step 8 — Express in terms of depth $h$ (more intuitive form):**

Define the vertical depth **below** the reference surface as $h = z_0 - z$ (positive downward). Then $z - z_0 = -h$, and substituting:

$$P = P_0 - \rho g (-h) = P_0 + \rho g h$$

$$\boxed{\,P = P_0 + \rho g h\,}$$

This is the **Absolute Form of the Hydrostatic Law** — the equation most frequently tested in KTU university examinations.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Application of the Hydrostatic Law

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A submarine is operating at a depth of **250 m** in seawater. The density of seawater is $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and the atmospheric pressure at the surface is $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.3\ \text{kPa}$. Calculate the absolute pressure on the submarine hull and the gauge pressure experienced.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $h = 250\ \text{m}$
- $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- $P_0 = P_{\text{atm}} = 101.3 \times 10^3\ \text{Pa}$

**Step 1 — Compute gauge pressure:**

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = \rho g h$$

Substituting values:

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 250$$

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = 2{,}514{,}312.5\ \text{Pa}$$

$$P_{\text{gauge}} \approx 2514.3\ \text{kPa} \approx 2.51\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute absolute pressure:**

$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h$$

$$P_{\text{abs}} = 101.3 \times 10^3 + 2{,}514{,}312.5$$

$$P_{\text{abs}} = 2{,}615{,}612.5\ \text{Pa} \approx 2615.6\ \text{kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The gauge pressure is roughly **25 times** the atmospheric pressure, which is consistent with the depth being equivalent to ~250 atmospheres of water column. This is why submarine hulls are engineered as thick-walled pressure vessels.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of a Fluid Element (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture of forces** acting on an infinitesimal static fluid element. This is the schematic that supports the derivation in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Static Fluid Element] --> B[Vertical Force Balance]
    A --> C[Horizontal Force Balance]
    
    B --> D[Upward Force: P times dA on bottom face]
    B --> E[Downward Force: P + dP times dA on top face]
    B --> F[Body Force: Weight W equals rho times g times dV]
    
    D --> G[Net Vertical Force equals ZERO]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    
    G --> H[Simplify: dP equals minus rho g dz]
    H --> I[Differential Hydrostatic Law]
    
    C --> J[Lateral Pressures cancel by symmetry]
    J --> K[No horizontal pressure gradient]
    K --> L[Pressure is isotropic at a point]
    
    I --> M[Integrate: P equals P0 plus rho g h]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Absolute Hydrostatic Equation]
    
    N --> O[Engineering Applications]
    O --> P[Dam Design]
    O --> Q[Manometry]
    O --> R[Submarine Hulls]
    O --> S[Hydraulic Press]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The element experiences **three** distinct force groups — vertical pressure (top/bottom), body force (weight), and lateral pressure (which cancel).
- The vertical balance yields the **differential** form, while the lateral symmetry ensures pressure is **isotropic**.
- Integration produces the working engineering equation that powers the **four application domains** (dam, manometer, submarine, hydraulic press).

### 4.2 Pressure Variation with Depth (Conceptual Bar Chart)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Free Surface - P0] -->|h equals 0| B[Pressure equals P0]
    A -->|h equals h1| C[Pressure equals P0 plus gamma h1]
    A -->|h equals h2| D[Pressure equals P0 plus gamma h2]
    A -->|h equals h3| E[Pressure equals P0 plus gamma h3]
    
    B -.-> F[Linear Increase with Depth]
    C -.-> F
    D -.-> F
    E -.-> F
    
    F --> G[Slope equals gamma equals rho times g]
    G --> H[Denser fluid gives steeper slope]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **linearity** of the $P$–$h$ relationship is a direct consequence of $\rho$ and $g$ being constants for an incompressible fluid under uniform gravity. For compressible fluids (e.g., the atmosphere), the relationship becomes exponential.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State and explain the **Hydrostatic Law**. Mention the physical significance of the negative sign in the differential form $dP/dz = -\rho g$.

**Model Answer:**

The Hydrostatic Law states that the **rate of change of pressure with respect to vertical elevation in a static, incompressible fluid is equal to the negative of the product of the fluid's density and gravitational acceleration**. Mathematically:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

**Physical significance of the negative sign:** The sign is negative because pressure **decreases** as elevation $z$ increases. In other words, as we move **upward** in a static fluid, the pressure drops because there is less fluid weight above us. Equivalently, pressure **increases** with depth below a free surface.

*(3 marks — 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for physical significance of sign)*

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A **U-tube manometer** is connected to a pipe carrying water. The difference in mercury levels in the manometer is $50\ \text{mm}$. Calculate the pressure difference between the two points. Given: $\rho_{\text{Hg}} = 13600\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{\text{w}} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $h_{\text{Hg}} = 0.050\ \text{m}$, densities as above.

Applying the manometer equation (a direct application of the Hydrostatic Law in a connected fluid):

$$\Delta P = (\rho_{\text{Hg}} - \rho_{\text{w}}) \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{Hg}}$$

$$\Delta P = (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.050$$

$$\Delta P = 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.050$$

$$\Delta P = 6180.3\ \text{Pa} \approx 6.18\ \text{kPa}$$

*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer with unit)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A).** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **(a)** Derive the **Hydrostatic Law** $dP/dz = -\rho g$ from first principles by considering the equilibrium of a small fluid element. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vertical cylindrical tank of diameter $1.2\ \text{m}$ and height $3.0\ \text{m}$ is filled with oil of specific gravity $0.85$. The top of the tank is **sealed** and the air pressure above the oil is $50\ \text{kPa}$ (gauge). Calculate:
>  - (i) The pressure at the **bottom** of the tank.
>  - (ii) The total **hydrostatic force** on the base of the tank.
>  - (iii) The **gauge pressure** at a point $1.5\ \text{m}$ below the oil surface.
>
> Take $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. The fluid is **static** (no motion, hence no shear stress).
2. The fluid is **incompressible** ($\rho =$ constant).
3. The only body force is **gravity** acting in the vertical direction.
4. The fluid is a **continuum** (properties vary smoothly).

*Derivation:*

Consider an infinitesimal fluid element of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ with $z$ measured vertically upward.

| Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|
| Upward force on bottom face | $P \cdot dx \cdot dy$ |
| Downward force on top face | $(P + dP) \cdot dx \cdot dy$ |
| Weight (body force) | $\rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$ |

For static equilibrium, $\sum F_z = 0$:

$$P \cdot dx \cdot dy - (P + dP) \cdot dx \cdot dy - \rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$-dP \cdot dx \cdot dy - \rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = 0$$

Dividing by $dx \cdot dy$:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g \qquad \text{Q.E.D.}$$

*(Awarding scheme: 1 mark for figure, 2 marks for force balance, 2 marks for algebra, 1 mark for final equation, 1 mark for assumptions)*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.2\ \text{m}$ → $A = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (1.2)^2 / 4 = 1.131\ \text{m}^2$
- $H = 3.0\ \text{m}$
- $S = 0.85$ → $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $P_{\text{air,gauge}} = 50\ \text{kPa} = 50{,}000\ \text{Pa}$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

**(i) Pressure at the bottom of the tank:**

$$P_{\text{bottom}} = P_{\text{air}} + \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot g \cdot H$$

$$P_{\text{bottom}} = 50{,}000 + 850 \times 9.81 \times 3.0$$

$$P_{\text{bottom}} = 50{,}000 + 25{,}015.5 = 75{,}015.5\ \text{Pa}$$

$$P_{\text{bottom}} \approx 75.02\ \text{kPa (gauge)}$$

**(ii) Total hydrostatic force on the base:**

$$F = P_{\text{bottom}} \times A = 75{,}015.5 \times 1.131$$

$$F = 84{,}842.5\ \text{N} \approx 84.84\ \text{kN}$$

**(iii) Gauge pressure at 1.5 m below oil surface:**

$$P_{1.5} = P_{\text{air}} + \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot g \cdot 1.5$$

$$P_{1.5} = 50{,}000 + 850 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 = 50{,}000 + 12{,}507.75$$

$$P_{1.5} = 62{,}507.75\ \text{Pa} \approx 62.51\ \text{kPa (gauge)}$$

*(Awarding scheme: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks — includes formula, substitution, and unit)*

---

> **Q3(B).** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Starting from the differential form of the Hydrostatic Law, **integrate** to obtain the absolute form $P = P_0 + \rho g h$. Clearly state the boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **hydraulic press** has a small piston of diameter $50\ \text{mm}$ and a large piston of diameter $300\ \text{mm}$. A force of $400\ \text{N}$ is applied on the small piston. Calculate:
>  - (i) The pressure generated in the fluid.
>  - (ii) The force on the large piston.
>  - (iii) The mechanical advantage of the press.
>
> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Integration (7 marks):**

Starting from:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

Multiplying both sides by $dz$:

$$dP = -\rho g \cdot dz$$

Integrating between a reference point $(z_0, P_0)$ and an arbitrary point $(z, P)$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\rho g \int_{z_0}^{z} dz$$

$$P - P_0 = -\rho g (z - z_0)$$

**Boundary conditions:** At $z = z_0$ (free surface), $P = P_0$ (atmospheric pressure).

**Substitution for depth form:** Let $h = z_0 - z$ (depth below the free surface, positive downward). Then $z - z_0 = -h$:

$$P = P_0 - \rho g (-h) = P_0 + \rho g h$$

$$\boxed{\,P = P_0 + \rho g h\,}$$

*(Awarding scheme: 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for integration, 1 mark for boundary conditions, 2 marks for substitution to depth form, 1 mark for final box)*

**Part (b) — Hydraulic Press Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d_1 = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.050\ \text{m}$
- $d_2 = 300\ \text{mm} = 0.300\ \text{m}$
- $F_1 = 400\ \text{N}$

**Areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi d_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi d_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.300)^2}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2$$

**(i) Pressure generated:**

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{400}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$P = 203{,}718\ \text{Pa} \approx 203.7\ \text{kPa}$$

**(ii) Force on the large piston:**

By Pascal's transmission (which is a direct consequence of the Hydrostatic Law — pressure is the same at the same elevation throughout a connected static fluid):

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 203{,}718 \times 7.0686 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$F_2 = 14{,}400\ \text{N} \approx 14.4\ \text{kN}$$

**(iii) Mechanical advantage:**

$$MA = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{14{,}400}{400} = 36$$

Equivalently, $MA = (A_2 / A_1) = (d_2 / d_1)^2 = (300/50)^2 = 36$ ✓

*(Awarding scheme: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the surface pressure $P_0$** when the tank is sealed or pressurized. If $P_0 \neq P_{\text{atm}}$, the formula $P = \rho g h$ is **incomplete** — use the full $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency.** Always convert mm, cm to m before substituting into $\rho g h$. Mixed units are the most common source of wrong answers.
> 3. **Sign error on $dP/dz$.** Confusing the "upward positive" convention with the "depth positive downward" convention leads to flipped signs and wrong magnitudes.
> 4. **Forgetting the weight of the element** in derivation questions — if the body force term $\rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$ is missing, you lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 5. **Applying the law to moving fluids** — the Hydrostatic Law is valid **only for static fluids**. In a flowing fluid, additional dynamic (velocity-related) terms appear.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hydrostatic Law**

- [x] The **differential form** is $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ (with $z$ upward).
- [x] The **absolute form** is $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ (with $h$ depth downward).
- [x] The law applies **only to static fluids** — no motion, no shear, no acceleration.
- [x] Pressure is **isotropic** at a point (Pascal's Principle) — same in all directions.
- [x] The law is derived from the **vertical equilibrium** of an infinitesimal fluid element: $P\,dA - (P + dP)\,dA - \rho g\,dV = 0$.
- [x] $\gamma = \rho g$ is the **specific weight** — frequently used shorthand.
- [x] For **gauge pressure**, set $P_0 = 0$ and use $P_{\text{gauge}} = \rho g h$.
- [x] For **absolute pressure**, retain $P_0 = P_{\text{atm}} = 101.3\ \text{kPa}$ (at sea level).
- [x] Pressure difference between two points in a static fluid: $\Delta P = \rho g \Delta h$.
- [x] Engineering applications: **dams, manometers, hydraulic press, submarine hulls, tank design, barometers**.
- [x] **Horizontal pressure gradient is zero** in a static fluid — a frequently tested conceptual point.
- [x] The law is **independent of the shape of the container** — only vertical depth matters (the famous "hydrostatic paradox").

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Fluid Pressure Using Manometers and Its Different Classifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Fluid Pressure Using Manometers and Its Different Classifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Manometer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manometer**: A manometer is a fluid mechanics instrument used to measure the pressure of a fluid (liquid or gas) by balancing the unknown pressure against a column of a known reference fluid (typically mercury, water, or another liquid of known specific gravity) under the action of gravity. It operates strictly on the **Hydrostatic Equilibrium Principle**.

A manometer is essentially a **U-shaped or single-leg transparent tube** containing one or more fluids that indicate pressure based on the **height difference (manometric height)** of the liquid columns.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Liquid Balance"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**: Imagine two children of unequal weight sitting on a opposite ends of a seesaw. The heavier child pushes their end down until they find a balance point. Similarly, a manometer has two "pressures" pushing on a fluid — the **unknown pressure** we want to measure and a **known reference pressure** (often atmospheric). The fluid settles at a height where both pressures balance perfectly. By measuring how much higher (or lower) one column is than the other, we can mathematically recover the unknown pressure.

Think of a doctor's **sphygmomanometer** (blood pressure cuff) — it uses a column of mercury (or a digital equivalent) to measure blood pressure by balancing it against atmospheric pressure. That is a manometer in action!

### 1.3 Classification of Manometers (KTU Syllabus Map)

The KTU 2024 Scheme strictly classifies manometers into the following hierarchy:

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Tree (Memory Aid)**
> 1. **Simple Manometers** — measure pressure at a single point
>    - (a) Piezometer
>    - (b) U-Tube Manometer
>    - (c) Single Column Manometer (Vertical & Inclined)
> 2. **Differential Manometers** — measure pressure difference between two points
>    - (a) U-Tube Differential Manometer
>    - (b) Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer
> 3. **Micro-Manometers** — for measuring very small pressure differences with high precision

### 1.4 Standard Constants & Reference Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard KTU value)
- **Density of mercury (Hg) at 0°C**: $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of water at 4°C**: $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific gravity of mercury**: $S_{Hg} = 13.6$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure**: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principle — Hydrostatic Equilibrium

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theorem (Pascal's Law + Hydrostatics)**: In a static fluid, pressure at any point depends only on the **depth below the free surface** and the **density of the fluid**. The governing equation is:
> $$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$
> where $\rho$ = fluid density, $g$ = gravitational acceleration, $h$ = vertical height of fluid column above the point.

**Why this works**: Gravity pulls all fluid particles downward. To keep a fluid column in static equilibrium, an upward pressure difference must exactly balance the weight of the column. This is the foundation of all manometer derivations.

### 2.2 Classification Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1: Decide the measurement goal**
- Measure pressure at a **single point**? → Use **Simple Manometer**
- Measure pressure **difference** between two points? → Use **Differential Manometer**

**Step 2: Decide the magnitude of pressure**
- **High pressure (e.g., water pipelines)**? → Use **U-tube with mercury** (heavy liquid)
- **Moderate pressure (e.g., open tanks)**? → Use **Piezometer** (no heavy liquid)
- **Very low pressure / small difference**? → Use **Inclined / Micro-manometer** (amplifies small heights)
- **Differential pressure in liquids**? → Use **Inverted U-tube** (light liquid above heavy one)

**Step 3: Apply Hydrostatic Balance**
- Start at the unknown pressure point
- Travel down through the fluid: **add** $\rho g h$
- Travel up through the fluid: **subtract** $\rho g h$
- At the interface of two fluids: the pressure is **equal** (continuity condition)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Reference Table — Use this for all KTU numerical problems!**

| # | Manometer Type | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Piezometer** | $P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $h$ = height of liquid column above point A | Open tanks, low pressure liquids |
| 2 | **Simple U-tube** | $P_A + \rho_w g h_1 - \rho_m g h_2 = P_{atm}$ | $h_1$ = water height, $h_2$ = mercury deflection | Pipe pressure with mercury manometer |
| 3 | **Single Column (Vertical)** | $P_A = \rho_w g \left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right) h_2$ | $A_1$ = reservoir area, $A_2$ = tube area | More accurate than U-tube (lower deflection) |
| 4 | **Inclined Single Column** | $P_A = \rho_w g \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$ | $L$ = length along incline, $\theta$ = inclination angle | Very small gas pressures |
| 5 | **U-tube Differential** | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho) g h$ (simplified when levels equal) | $h$ = mercury deflection between two legs | Pressure drop in pipe flow |
| 6 | **Inverted U-tube Differential** | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_l - \rho_w) g h$ | $\rho_l$ = heavier manometric liquid | Differential pressure in **light fluids** (oils, gases) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Manometer Rule (Memorize This!)**
> $$\boxed{P_{\text{left}} + \sum (\rho g h)_{\text{down}} - \sum (\rho g h)_{\text{up}} = P_{\text{right}}}$$
> *Start at one end, add when going down through a fluid, subtract when going up, and equate to the pressure at the other end.*

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Piezometers** are used in **hydraulic engineering** to measure water depth in reservoirs.
- **U-tube mercury manometers** are standard in **HVAC systems** to measure gas pressure in ductwork.
- **Differential manometers** are critical in **flow measurement devices** like Venturi meters, orifice meters, and pitot-static tubes (will be covered in Module 2 of PEPOT416).
- **Inverted U-tube manometers** are used in **aircraft altimeters** and **oil pipeline systems**.
- **Inclined manometers** are employed in **wind tunnel testing** for low-pressure measurements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Piezometer Equation

**Setup**: A vertical transparent tube is connected to a vessel containing a liquid of density $\rho$. The liquid rises in the tube to a height $h$ above the point of connection (Point A).

**Step 1**: At the free surface of the liquid in the piezometer tube, pressure equals atmospheric pressure (assuming an open tube):
$$P_{\text{top}} = P_{atm}$$

**Step 2**: Apply the hydrostatic equation. As we go **down** from the free surface to point A through height $h$ of the same liquid, pressure **increases**:
$$P_A = P_{\text{top}} + \rho g h$$

**Step 3**: Substitute the atmospheric pressure (or take gauge pressure by setting $P_{atm} = 0$):
$$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho g h \quad \text{(Absolute Pressure)}$$
$$P_A = \rho g h \quad \text{(Gauge Pressure)}$$

$$\boxed{P_A = \rho g h}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Simple U-Tube Manometer

**Setup**: A U-tube containing mercury (density $\rho_m$) is connected to a pipe carrying water (density $\rho_w$). The mercury in the left leg is depressed by $h_2$ below the original level; the water column above the mercury in the left leg has height $h_1$. The right leg is open to atmosphere.

**Step 1**: Identify equal-pressure points. At the lowest mercury level (left side, where mercury meets water), the pressure equals the pressure at the same horizontal level in the right leg (mercury only).

**Step 2**: Write pressure at the mercury-water interface (left leg):
$$P_{\text{interface}} = P_A + \rho_w g h_1$$

**Step 3**: Write pressure at the same horizontal level in the right leg:
$$P_{\text{same level}} = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h_2$$

**Step 4**: Equate the two (continuity of pressure at same elevation in connected static fluid):
$$P_A + \rho_w g h_1 = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h_2$$

**Step 5**: Solve for $P_A$ (gauge pressure, set $P_{atm} = 0$):
$$P_A = \rho_m g h_2 - \rho_w g h_1$$

$$\boxed{P_A = g(\rho_m h_2 - \rho_w h_1)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: U-Tube Differential Manometer (Most Important for KTU)

**Setup**: Two points A and B in a pipeline (carrying fluid of density $\rho$) are connected to a U-tube containing a heavier manometric fluid (density $\rho_m$). The mercury in the right leg is higher than in the left leg by a height $h$.

**Step 1**: Identify the lower mercury level (in the left leg). Let's call this point "M" (mercury-water interface on A side).

**Step 2**: Pressure at point M from the A-side:
$$P_M = P_A + \rho g h_A$$
where $h_A$ is the vertical distance from A to the lower mercury level.

**Step 3**: Pressure at the same horizontal level in the right leg (right side of U-tube):
$$P_{\text{right, same level}} = P_B + \rho g h_B + \rho_m g h$$
where $h_B$ is the height from B to the lower mercury level on its side, and $h$ is the mercury deflection.

**Step 4**: Equate (continuity):
$$P_A + \rho g h_A = P_B + \rho g h_B + \rho_m g h$$

**Step 5**: Simplify. Note that $h_A - h_B = h$ (geometry of U-tube). Subtract $P_B$:
$$P_A - P_B = \rho g (h_B - h_A) + \rho_m g h = -\rho g h + \rho_m g h$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho) g h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case**: When the manometric fluid is mercury and the flowing fluid is water, the standard KTU formula becomes:
> $$P_A - P_B = (S_{Hg} - 1) \rho_w g h = 12.6 \cdot \rho_w g h$$
> where $S_{Hg} = 13.6$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer

**Setup**: An **inverted** U-tube contains a **lighter manometric fluid** (e.g., oil) above the heavier working fluid in the two legs. The two points A and B are at different elevations.

**Step 1**: Apply the manometer rule — start at A, go up through the working fluid (pressure **decreases**), pass through the lighter manometric fluid, then come down to B (pressure **increases**).

**Step 2**: The pressure at the lower manometric-fluid interface (in leg A) equals the pressure at the corresponding level in leg B.

**Step 3**: Working through the algebra (similar to above), the result is:
$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{\text{working}} - \rho_{\text{manometric}}) g h - \rho_{\text{working}} g (z_A - z_B)$$

For the simplified case where A and B are at the same elevation:
$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g h}$$
where $\rho_o$ is the density of the lighter (manometric) oil.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: A pipe carrying water has a U-tube manometer connected at two points A and B. The manometric fluid is mercury. The mercury level in the leg connected to A is 0.4 m below the pipe centerline, and in the leg connected to B is 0.7 m below. The mercury deflection is $h = 0.3$ m. Find the pressure difference $P_A - P_B$ if the points are 0.5 m apart vertically (A is higher).

**Solution**:
Given: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $h = 0.3$ m, elevation difference $z_A - z_B = 0.5$ m.

**Step 1**: Apply the general U-tube differential manometer equation:
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h - \rho_w g h - \rho_w g (z_A - z_B)$$

Wait — let's be careful with sign conventions. The mercury is deflected such that the leg connected to higher pressure is pushed down. Since mercury in A's leg is at -0.4 m and in B's leg is at -0.7 m, mercury in B is lower (0.3 m lower), so A has higher pressure.

**Step 2**: Correctly written:
$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_w) g h - \rho_w g (z_A - z_B)$$

**Step 3**: Substitute values:
$$P_A - P_B = (13600 - 1000)(9.81)(0.3) - (1000)(9.81)(0.5)$$

$$= (12600)(9.81)(0.3) - (1000)(9.81)(0.5)$$

$$= 37082.58 - 4905 = 32177.58 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4**: Final answer:
$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 32.18 \text{ kPa}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Manometer Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Manometer] --> B[Simple Manometer]
    A --> C[Differential Manometer]
    A --> D[Micro Manometer]
    B --> B1[Piezometer]
    B --> B2[U Tube Manometer]
    B --> B3[Single Column Manometer]
    B3 --> B3a[Vertical Type]
    B3 --> B3b[Inclined Type]
    C --> C1[U Tube Differential]
    C --> C2[Inverted U Tube Differential]
    D --> D1[Hook Gauge Type]
    D --> D2[Well Type Micromanometer]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Universal Manometer Problem-Solving Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Identify Two Pressure Points] --> S2[Choose Manometer Type]
    S2 --> S3[Identify All Fluid Columns]
    S3 --> S4[Choose Reference Level Equal Pressure Points]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Hydrostatic Equation from Point 1]
    S5 --> S6[Add rho g h when Going Down]
    S6 --> S7[Subtract rho g h when Going Up]
    S7 --> S8[Equate to Pressure at Point 2]
    S8 --> S9[Solve Algebraically for Unknown]
    S9 --> S10[Convert Units to kPa or N per m2]
    S10 --> END[Final Answer in SI Units]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a U-Tube Manometer System

```mermaid
graph LR
    PIPE[Pipe with Fluid] --> TAP1[Tap Point A]
    PIPE --> TAP2[Tap Point B]
    TAP1 --> LEG1[Left Leg of U Tube]
    TAP2 --> LEG2[Right Leg of U Tube]
    LEG1 --> HG1[Mercury Column Left]
    LEG2 --> HG2[Mercury Column Right]
    HG1 --> SCALE[Graduated Scale in mm]
    HG2 --> SCALE
    SCALE --> READ[Read Height Difference h]
    READ --> FORMULA[Apply P A minus P B equals rho m minus rho times g times h]
    FORMULA --> RESULT[Pressure Difference Output in Pa]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer

```mermaid
graph TB
    PT_A[Point A in Pipe] --> PIPE_A[Tube Going Up]
    PT_B[Point B in Pipe] --> PIPE_B[Tube Going Up]
    PIPE_A --> O1[Oil Interface Left]
    PIPE_B --> O2[Oil Interface Right]
    O1 --> OIL[Light Manometric Fluid Oil]
    O2 --> OIL
    OIL --> TOP[Top of Inverted U Tube Air]
    O1 -.->|height hA| REF[Reference Datum]
    O2 -.->|height hB| REF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Practical)**: In KTU lab exams, you will be asked to draw sketches of these manometers. Always label: (1) the unknown pressure point, (2) all fluid interfaces, (3) the reference datum (lowest level), (4) all heights ($h_1$, $h_2$, $h$, etc.), and (5) all densities ($\rho_w$, $\rho_m$, $\rho_o$).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> **Define a manometer. List the different types of manometers used for fluid pressure measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
A manometer is a device used to measure the pressure of a fluid by balancing it against the weight of a column of a reference fluid under gravity (1 mark). The types are:
1. Simple Manometers: Piezometer, U-tube manometer, Single column manometer (vertical and inclined) (1 mark)
2. Differential Manometers: U-tube differential manometer, Inverted U-tube differential manometer (1 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
> **State Pascal's law of fluid pressure and explain how it forms the basis of manometer operation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
Pascal's law states that the pressure applied to a confined static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions (1 mark). In a manometer, the pressure at the unknown point is transmitted through the manometric fluid (e.g., mercury) as a hydrostatic column (1 mark). By measuring the height difference of this column and applying $P = \rho g h$, the unknown pressure can be determined (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the pressure difference between two points in a pipeline using a **U-tube differential manometer** with mercury as the manometric fluid. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A U-tube manometer is connected to two points A and B in a pipe carrying oil (specific gravity = 0.85). The manometric fluid is mercury. The mercury levels in the two legs differ by 25 cm, and the point A is 30 cm above point B. Find the pressure difference $P_A - P_B$ in kPa. Take $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Q3(a)**:

**Given**: Two points A and B in a pipe carrying a fluid of density $\rho$. A U-tube containing mercury ($\rho_m$) is connected between them.

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Draw the schematic — show pipe, two connecting tubes, U-tube with mercury, and the deflection $h$.

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: Identify the reference datum. Take the lower mercury level as datum (left leg, where A connects). At this level, pressure is continuous across the U-tube.

**Step 3 (2 marks)**: Express pressure at the datum from the A-side:
$$P_{\text{datum}} = P_A + \rho g h_A$$
where $h_A$ is the vertical distance from A to the datum.

**Step 4 (2 marks)**: Express pressure at the same datum from the B-side:
$$P_{\text{datum}} = P_B + \rho g h_B + \rho_m g h$$
where $h_B$ is the distance from B to the datum, and $h = h_A - h_B$ is the mercury deflection.

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Equate and solve:
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h - \rho g (h_A - h_B) = (\rho_m - \rho) g h$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho) g h}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q3(b)**:

**Given**: $S_{\text{oil}} = 0.85$, so $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$; $h = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25$ m; elevation of A above B: $z_A - z_B = 0.30$ m; $\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Use the general formula:
$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h - \rho g h - \rho g (z_A - z_B)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: Substitute values:
$$P_A - P_B = (13600)(9.81)(0.25) - (850)(9.81)(0.25) - (850)(9.81)(0.30)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)**: Calculate each term:
- $(13600)(9.81)(0.25) = 33354$ Pa
- $(850)(9.81)(0.25) = 2084.625$ Pa
- $(850)(9.81)(0.30) = 2501.55$ Pa

**Step 4 (2 marks)**: Final answer:
$$P_A - P_B = 33354 - 2084.625 - 2501.55 = 28767.83 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 28.77 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Alternative Choice, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the working principle of a **piezometer** and an **inclined single column manometer**. State one advantage and one limitation of each. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A piezometer is used to measure the pressure of water at a point in a pipe. The water rises to a height of 1.8 m in the piezometer tube. Calculate the gauge pressure and absolute pressure at the point. Take atmospheric pressure = 101.3 kPa. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Q4(a)**:

**Piezometer (4 marks)**:
- **Working**: A piezometer is a simple vertical transparent tube open at the top, connected to a point in a vessel/pipe where pressure is to be measured. The liquid rises in the tube to a height $h$ where the hydrostatic pressure $\rho g h$ balances the unknown pressure.
- **Equation**: $P_A = \rho g h$
- **Advantage**: Simple, no need for any manometric fluid, direct reading.
- **Limitation**: Cannot measure high pressure (impractical for $h > 4$ m of water), cannot measure gas pressure, only suitable for liquids.

**Inclined Single Column Manometer (3 marks)**:
- **Working**: An inclined tube (inclination angle $\theta$) replaces the vertical tube of a single column manometer. This magnifies the small vertical deflection into a larger length $L$ along the incline.
- **Equation**: $P_A = \rho g L \sin\theta$
- **Advantage**: High sensitivity, suitable for measuring low gas pressures (e.g., in wind tunnels).
- **Limitation**: Requires calibration; not suitable for high pressures; sensitive to tube cleanliness.

#### **Model Solution for Q4(b)**:

**Given**: $h = 1.8$ m, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa.

**Step 1 (2 marks)**: Gauge pressure:
$$P_{gauge} = \rho_w g h = (1000)(9.81)(1.8) = 17658 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: Absolute pressure:
$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 101300 + 17658 = 118958 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)**: Convert to kPa:
$$P_{gauge} = 17.658 \text{ kPa} \approx 17.66 \text{ kPa}$$
$$P_{abs} = 118.958 \text{ kPa} \approx 118.96 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} \approx 17.66 \text{ kPa}, \quad P_{abs} \approx 118.96 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Based on Real Valuation Patterns)**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Elevation Term**: In differential manometers, students often forget the term $\rho g (z_A - z_B)$ when A and B are at different elevations. **Always check elevations!**
>
> 2. **Wrong Density Used**: Using $\rho_w$ instead of $\rho_m$ (mercury) or vice versa. **Memorize**: Heavy manometric fluid = mercury ($\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$); light manometric fluid = oil or water depending on context.
>
> 3. **Atmospheric Pressure Ignored**: In open-end manometers, forgetting that one end is open to atmosphere (and thus $P_{atm}$ enters the equation). If the problem says "gauge pressure," set $P_{atm} = 0$.
>
> 4. **Unit Mismatch**: Mixing cm and m. **KTU Standard**: Convert all lengths to **meters** before plugging in.
>
> 5. **Direction of Mercury Deflection**: Students often guess which leg has higher mercury. **Rule**: The leg connected to **higher pressure** will have its mercury **pushed down**.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Diagram**: KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** if no schematic diagram is drawn. Always include a labeled diagram!

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Manometer** = device to measure fluid pressure using hydrostatic balance of a liquid column.
- **Two main types**: **Simple Manometer** (single-point pressure) and **Differential Manometer** (pressure difference).
- **Simple Manometers** include **Piezometer**, **U-tube**, and **Single Column (vertical/inclined)**.
- **Differential Manometers** include **U-tube** and **Inverted U-tube**.
- **Core equation**: $P = \rho g h$.
- **U-tube differential manometer formula**: $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho) g h$ (when elevations equal).
- **Inverted U-tube formula**: $P_A - P_B = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g h$ (uses lighter manometric fluid).
- **Inclined manometer formula**: $P = \rho g L \sin\theta$ (magnifies small heights).
- **Always include a labeled diagram** in KTU exams (1–2 marks reserved).
- **Standard constants**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Direction rule**: Higher pressure → pushes manometric fluid **down** in its leg.
- **Conversion rule**: 1 kPa = 1000 Pa; 1 bar = 100 kPa; 1 m of water column ≈ 9.81 kPa.
- **Universal manometer trick**: Start at one point, add $\rho g h$ going down, subtract $\rho g h$ going up, equate at the other point.
- **Common KTU traps**: Elevation differences, sign conventions, atmospheric pressure, and density mix-ups.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problems Based on Manometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Problems Based on Manometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manometer:** A manometer is a **U-tube (or single-tube) fluid-filled device** that operates on the fundamental principle of **hydrostatic pressure equilibrium** to measure the pressure of a fluid (gas or liquid) at a given point, or the **pressure difference** between two points in a conduit/vessel. The unknown pressure is balanced against a column of a known manometric fluid (typically mercury, oil, or water).

**Classification of Manometers (as per KTU Fluid Mechanics syllabus):**

| Type | Operating Principle | Typical Fluid Used |
|------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Simple Manometer (Piezometer) | Direct vertical column of fluid | Liquid only (no gas) |
| U-Tube Single Fluid Manometer | Single manometric fluid in U-tube | Mercury / Water |
| Differential Manometer | Two manometric fluids, measures ΔP | Mercury / Lighter fluid |
| Inverted U-Tube Manometer | Air trapped at top, fluid below | Light liquids (oil) |
| Micromanometer | Inclined tube for amplified readings | Light fluids |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a straw inserted vertically into a juice glass — the juice rises inside the straw due to atmospheric pressure pushing the liquid down. The **height** the juice rises tells you exactly how much air pressure exists above it. A manometer does the **exact same thing** but uses a heavy fluid (mercury, Sg = **13.6**) so the column doesn't need to be very tall. By mathematically "walking" through the fluid from point A to point B and adding/subtracting pressures as we go down/up, we solve for the unknown pressure difference.

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constants

- Density of water, **ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³**
- Density of mercury, **ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³** (Specific gravity = **13.6**)
- Specific gravity of oil ≈ **0.7 – 0.9**
- Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Standard atmospheric pressure, **P_atm = 101.325 kPa**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Principle

The **fundamental operating principle** of every manometer is:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P$ = hydrostatic pressure (Pa or N/m²)
- $\rho$ = density of fluid column (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = vertical height of the fluid column (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rule (Manometer Pressure Balancing — "Go-Down-Go-Up Rule"):**
> When tracing a manometer from Point A to Point B:
> - **Going DOWN** in a fluid column → **ADD** ρgh (pressure increases)
> - **Going UP** in a fluid column → **SUBTRACT** ρgh (pressure decreases)
> - The two arms of the U-tube at the same elevation of the **manometric fluid** must have **equal pressure** (equilibrium).

### 2.2 The General Manometer Equation

For any multi-fluid manometer connecting points A and B:

$$P_A + \sum (\rho_i g h_i)_{\text{down}} - \sum (\rho_j g h_j)_{\text{up}} = P_B$$

Rearranged for pressure difference:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = \sum (\rho_j g h_j)_{\text{up}} - \sum (\rho_i g h_i)_{\text{down}}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Manometer Type | Pressure Difference Formula | Working Fluid Condition |
|----------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------|
| Piezometer (Simple) | $P_A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Single known liquid |
| U-Tube (single fluid) | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Above-mercury fluid is lighter |
| Differential U-Tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h + \text{(correction for height diff)}$ | Points A, B at different elevations |
| Inverted U-Tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_{\text{air}}) g h \approx \rho_f g h$ | Air column negligible |
| Inverted Differential | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_o) g h + \rho_f g (z_B - z_A)$ | Light oil above water |

### 2.4 Units & Conversions Used in KTU Valuation

- $1 \text{ m of water column} = 9.81 \text{ kPa}$
- $1 \text{ cm of Hg} = 133.32 \text{ Pa}$
- $1 \text{ m of Hg} = 13.6 \times 9.81 = 133.416 \text{ kPa}$
- Specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$ (units: N/m³)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Manometers are used in pipeline flow measurement, blood pressure devices (sphygmomanometer), boiler pressure gauges, barometers, Bourdon-tube calibration, and HVAC systems. Differential manometers find extensive use in measuring flow rates through orifice meters, venturi meters, and pitot tubes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the U-Tube Manometer Equation

Consider a simple U-tube manometer connected between two points A and B carrying water. Mercury (specific gravity **S_m = 13.6**) is the manometric fluid.

**Setting up the geometry:**
- Let the left limb of the U-tube contain water above mercury up to point A.
- The right limb contains water above mercury up to point B.
- Let $h$ be the difference in mercury levels between the two limbs (this is the manometer **deflection** or **reading**).
- Let point X be the lower mercury level (left) and point Y be the higher mercury level (right).

**Pressure at point X (from left side, going down):**

$$P_X = P_A + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

(Where $h_1$ = vertical height of water column above mercury on the left)

**Pressure at point Y (from right side, going down):**

$$P_Y = P_B + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_2$$

**Since X and Y are at the same elevation in the connected mercury fluid:**

$$P_X = P_Y$$

(Equilibrium condition for the manometric fluid)

**Substituting:**

$$P_A + \rho_w g h_1 = P_B + \rho_w g h_2$$

**Rearranging:**

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g (h_2 - h_1) = \rho_w g (h_2 - h_1)$$

Now, the height difference $h_2 - h_1$ can be related to the mercury deflection $h$ (where $h$ is the difference between the two mercury levels). If we define $h$ as the mercury column height difference, then:

$$h_2 - h_1 = \text{(adjustment for level differences)}$$

For the **standard case** where A and B are at the same elevation:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot h$$

This is because the water column on one side effectively replaces mercury of the same height, and the net "extra" pressure comes from the heavier mercury.

### 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Worked Example 1 (Simple U-Tube)

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
Two points A and B in a horizontal water pipe are connected by a U-tube manometer containing mercury. The points A and B are at the **same elevation**. The mercury level in the limb nearer to A is 150 mm **below** the centerline of the pipe, and the mercury level in the limb nearer to B is 150 mm **below** the centerline as well. Wait — this would be zero deflection. Let's revise:

> Two points A and B are connected by a U-tube mercury manometer. Point A is at the centerline of pipe carrying water, point B is at the centerline of another pipe carrying water. The mercury in the left limb (connected to A) is 100 mm below the centerline of A, and in the right limb (connected to B), the mercury level is 200 mm **below** the centerline of B. The fluid in the connecting tubes is water. Find the pressure difference $P_A - P_B$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Reference Datum:** Take the lower mercury level (left limb) as the datum for pressure equality.

**Step 2 — Trace from A to datum (going DOWN):**
Pressure at left datum = $P_A + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (0.100)$ — water column going down.

**Step 3 — Trace from B to the same datum (going DOWN through water, UP through mercury):**
Pressure at right datum = $P_B + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (0.200) - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot (0.100)$

Wait — we need to think about this more carefully. The mercury deflection $h$ = (height of right limb mercury) − (height of left limb mercury) = ?

Actually, let me set up cleanly:

Let the **left mercury level** be at height $z_L$ and the **right mercury level** be at height $z_R$. Let $h = z_R - z_L$ be the deflection (positive if right is higher).

For pressure equality at the **lower of the two mercury surfaces**:

$$P_{\text{lower}} = P_A + \rho_w g (z_A - z_L) \quad \text{(from A, going down through water)}$$

$$P_{\text{lower}} = P_B + \rho_w g (z_B - z_R) - \rho_{Hg} g (z_R - z_L)$$

Setting them equal:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g (z_B - z_R) - \rho_w g (z_A - z_L) + \rho_{Hg} g (z_R - z_L)$$

For A and B at same elevation ($z_A = z_B$), this simplifies to:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g (z_L - z_R) + \rho_{Hg} g (z_R - z_L) = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) g (z_R - z_L)$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_w) g h}$$

### 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example 2 (Differential Manometer — KTU Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
A differential manometer is connected at two points A and B of a horizontal pipe 600 mm apart carrying oil of specific gravity **0.85**. The manometer liquid is mercury. The deflection of mercury is **250 mm**. Find the pressure difference between A and B in kPa.

**Step 1 — Identify the Data:**

$$\rho_{\text{oil}} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 1000 = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$h = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Differential Manometer Formula (A and B at same level):**

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{\text{oil}}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$P_A - P_B = (13{,}600 - 850) \times 9.81 \times 0.25$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12{,}750 \times 9.81 \times 0.25$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12{,}750 \times 2.4525$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = 31{,}269.375 \text{ Pa} \approx 31.27 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification by Dimensional Check:**

$$[\rho][g][h] = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2} \cdot \text{m} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}^2} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa} \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Example 3 (Differential Manometer at Different Elevations — KTU Advanced)

**Problem Statement:**
Two points A (higher) and B (lower) in a pipe carrying water are separated by a vertical distance of **300 mm**, with A above B. A differential mercury manometer connected to A and B shows a deflection of **200 mm**, with the higher mercury level on the side connected to A. Find the pressure difference $P_A - P_B$.

**Step 1 — Identify Variables:**

Let $z_A - z_B = +0.300$ m (A is 0.300 m above B)
Mercury deflection $h = 0.200$ m, with right side (connected to B) higher. So $z_R - z_L = 0.200$ m.

**Step 2 — Pressure Equality at Lower Mercury Surface:**

Going from A down to left mercury surface (through water, height = $z_A - z_L$):

$$P_L = P_A + \rho_w g (z_A - z_L)$$

Going from B down to right mercury surface, then UP through mercury:

$$P_L = P_B + \rho_w g (z_B - z_R) + \rho_{Hg} g (z_R - z_L)$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Solve:**

$$P_A + \rho_w g (z_A - z_L) = P_B + \rho_w g (z_B - z_R) + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g (z_B - z_R) - \rho_w g (z_A - z_L) + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g [(z_B - z_R) - (z_A - z_L)] + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g (z_B - z_R - z_A + z_L) + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

Since $z_R - z_L = h$, we have $z_L = z_R - h$, so:

$$z_B - z_R - z_A + z_L = (z_B - z_A) - (z_R - z_L) = (z_B - z_A) - h$$

Therefore:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g [(z_B - z_A) - h] + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

$$P_A - P_B = -\rho_w g (z_A - z_B) - \rho_w g h + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

$$P_A - P_B = -\rho_w g (z_A - z_B) + (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) g h$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numbers:**

$z_A - z_B = 0.300$ m

$$P_A - P_B = -(1000)(9.81)(0.300) + (13{,}600 - 1000)(9.81)(0.200)$$

$$P_A - P_B = -2943 + (12{,}600)(9.81)(0.200)$$

$$P_A - P_B = -2943 + 24{,}721.2$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = 21{,}778.2 \text{ Pa} \approx 21.78 \text{ kPa}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Manometer Problem Templates

| Problem Type | Key Assumption | Critical Formula | KTU Marks Weight |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| Two points, same level, single manometric fluid | $z_A = z_B$ | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | 7 marks |
| Two points, different levels, single fluid | $z_A \neq z_B$ | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h + \rho_f g(z_B - z_A)$ | 14 marks |
| Multi-fluid manometer | Track each fluid layer | $\sum \rho g h$ along path | 14 marks |
| Inverted U-tube (light fluid) | Air column $\approx 0$ | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_o) g h$ | 7 marks |
| Micromanometer (inclined) | Read along slope $L$, angle $\theta$ | $h = L \sin \theta$ | 7 marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a U-Tube Manometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pipe Point A at Pressure P_A] --> B[Left Limb Tube]
    B --> C[Water Column Down]
    C --> D[Mercury Pool Left at Level Z_L]
    D --> E[Mercury Pool Right at Level Z_R]
    E --> F[Water Column Up]
    F --> G[Right Limb Tube]
    G --> H[Pipe Point B at Pressure P_B]
    E -.-> I[Deflection h = Z_R - Z_L]
    D -.-> I
    I --> J[Apply Hydrostatic Equilibrium P_X = P_Y]
    J --> K[Solve for P_A - P_B]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style H fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style I fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b
    style J fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style K fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow for Solving Manometer Problems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Points A and B and their elevations] --> S2[Step 2: Identify Manometric Fluid and its density]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Mark the Manometer Deflection h]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Choose Lower Mercury Surface as Datum]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Trace Pressure from A to Datum and from B to Datum]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Apply P_X equals P_Y Equilibrium]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Substitute Numerical Values and Solve for P_A minus P_B]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Convert Units to kPa and Report Answer]

    style S1 fill:#bbdefb
    style S4 fill:#ffccbc
    style S6 fill:#c8e6c9
    style S8 fill:#fff9c4
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block: Simple vs Differential vs Inverted Manometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SIMPLE["Simple Manometer Piezometer"]
        SIM1[Pressure Source P_A] --> SIM2[Vertical Glass Tube]
        SIM2 --> SIM3[Fluid Rises to Height h]
    end

    subgraph DIFF["Differential Manometer U-Tube"]
        DIF1[Pressure Source P_A Left Limb] --> DIF2[Manometric Fluid Mercury]
        DIF2 --> DIF3[Right Limb]
        DIF3 --> DIF4[Pressure Source P_B]
        DIF2 -.-> DIF5[Deflection h gives Delta P]
    end

    subgraph INV["Inverted U-Tube Manometer"]
        INV1[Pressure Source P_A Left Limb] --> INV2[Air Column Trapped]
        INV2 --> INV3[Light Manometric Fluid Oil]
        INV3 --> INV4[Right Limb]
        INV4 --> INV5[Pressure Source P_B]
    end

    style SIMPLE fill:#e1f5fe
    style DIFF fill:#fff3e0
    style INV fill:#f3e5f5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define a manometer. With a neat sketch, explain the principle of a differential U-tube manometer.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A manometer is a device used to measure fluid pressure by balancing it against a column of a known manometric fluid (Bansal, Cengel — standard references).
>
> **Principle:** It is based on the hydrostatic equation $P = \rho g h$. Two points at the same elevation in a continuous static fluid have equal pressure. The manometer is connected between two points A and B; the heavier manometric fluid (mercury, Sg = 13.6) settles at the bottom of the U-tube. The difference in mercury levels ($h$) is measured, and the pressure difference is calculated as $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$.
>
> *(Diagram: U-tube with A and B connected via water-filled tubes, mercury at the bottom, deflection h shown.)*
> **[1 Mark: Definition, 1 Mark: Principle, 1 Mark: Diagram/Equation]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**A simple U-tube manometer containing mercury is connected to a pipe in which oil of specific gravity 0.8 is flowing. The mercury deflection is 250 mm. Calculate the pressure at the pipe centerline in kPa. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Given: $S_{\text{oil}} = 0.8$, so $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 800$ kg/m³. $h = 0.25$ m. One end of the manometer is open to atmosphere, so $P_B = P_{\text{atm}}$ (gauge pressure = 0).
>
> $$P_A = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{\text{oil}}) \cdot g \cdot h$$
> $$P_A = (13{,}600 - 800) \times 9.81 \times 0.25$$
> $$P_A = 12{,}800 \times 9.81 \times 0.25$$
> $$P_A = 31{,}392 \text{ Pa} \approx 31.39 \text{ kPa}$$
> **[3 Marks: Correct formula, substitution, final answer]**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

## QUESTION A (14 Marks) — KTU Dec 2023 Pattern

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A differential mercury manometer is connected to two points A and B in a horizontal water pipeline. The manometer deflection is **180 mm**, with the mercury higher on the side of A. If the pressure at A is **150 kPa**, determine the pressure at B. Take Sg of mercury = **13.6**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the principle and working of an **inverted U-tube differential manometer**. List two advantages it has over a normal differential manometer.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**

$$\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
$$h = 0.180 \text{ m}, \quad P_A = 150 \text{ kPa} = 150{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Differential Manometer Formula (A and B at same level):**

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$P_A - P_B = (13{,}600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.180$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.180$$

$$P_A - P_B = 22{,}245.48 \text{ Pa} \approx 22.25 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P_B$:**

$$P_B = P_A - 22.25 = 150 - 22.25 = 127.75 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_B = 127.75 \text{ kPa}}$$

**[Marks Distribution: 2 — Formula, 3 — Substitution, 1 — Calculation, 1 — Final Answer with units]**

---

**(b) Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Principle of Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer:**

The inverted U-tube manometer is used to measure **pressure differences** in systems where the manometric fluid is **lighter than the fluid in the pipe** (e.g., measuring pressure difference in oil pipelines, gas flows, or when air contamination must be avoided).

**Construction:**
- The U-tube is inverted, with the **manometric fluid (lighter, e.g., oil) at the bottom of the inverted U**.
- The **space above the oil** in each limb is filled with the **pipe fluid (gas or air)** or simply air at atmospheric pressure.

**Working:**
- Two points A and B are connected to the two open ends (now pointing downward) of the inverted U-tube.
- The deflection $h$ of the oil column indicates the pressure difference.
- The governing equation is:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{\text{oil}} - \rho_{\text{pipe fluid}}) g h$$

For gas pipe, $\rho_{\text{pipe fluid}} \ll \rho_{\text{oil}}$, so:

$$P_A - P_B \approx \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Two Advantages:**
1. Suitable for measuring small pressure differences in gas flow systems because air column weight is negligible.
2. Avoids contamination of the pipe fluid since the manometric fluid (oil) is trapped at the bottom of the inverted loop.

**[Marks: 2 — Construction, 2 — Working, 1 — Equation, 2 — Advantages]**

---

## QUESTION B (14 Marks) — KTU May 2024 Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Two pressure taps at points A and B are taken from a horizontal pipe carrying oil of specific gravity **0.85**, separated by a vertical distance of **200 mm** (A above B). A U-tube mercury manometer is connected between A and B. The mercury deflection is **150 mm**, with the mercury higher on the side connected to B. Determine the pressure difference between A and B.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A simple piezometer is used to measure the pressure at the bottom of a tank containing oil of specific gravity **0.8**. The oil column rises to a height of **2.5 m** in the piezometer. Find the gauge pressure at the bottom of the tank in kPa. State **two limitations** of a piezometer.

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**

$$\rho_{\text{oil}} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
$$z_A - z_B = +0.200 \text{ m (A is above B)}$$
$$h = 0.150 \text{ m, with mercury higher on B side} \Rightarrow z_L - z_R = +0.150 \text{ m (left side lower)}$$

**Step 1 — Apply General Manometer Equation:**

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{\text{oil}}) \cdot g \cdot h + \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot g \cdot (z_B - z_A)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$P_A - P_B = (13{,}600 - 850)(9.81)(0.150) + (850)(9.81)(-0.200)$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12{,}750 \times 9.81 \times 0.150 + 850 \times 9.81 \times (-0.200)$$

$$P_A - P_B = 18{,}761.625 - 1{,}667.7$$

$$P_A - P_B = 17{,}093.925 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 17.09 \text{ kPa}}$$

**[Marks Distribution: 3 — Formula derivation, 2 — Substitution, 1 — Calculation, 1 — Final answer with units]**

---

**(b) Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Apply Piezometer Equation:**

$$P_A = \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$\rho_{\text{oil}} = 0.8 \times 1000 = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$P_A = 800 \times 9.81 \times 2.5$$

$$P_A = 19{,}620 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A = 19.62 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

**Two Limitations of a Piezometer:**

1. **Cannot measure gas pressure:** Since gases have negligible density, no measurable column rise occurs.
2. **Cannot measure high pressures:** For high pressures, the required column height becomes impractically large (e.g., 100 kPa water pressure needs a 10.2 m tall piezometer).

**[Marks: 3 — Formula + substitution, 1 — Final answer, 3 — Two limitations with brief explanation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 marks typical):** Many students forget that $z_B - z_A$ is **negative** when A is above B, leading to incorrect signs in the final answer.
> 2. **Unit Conversion (–1 mark):** Mercury deflection is often given in mm but must be converted to meters before substitution. Forgetting this causes a 1000x error.
> 3. **Datum Selection (–2 marks):** Always choose the **lower mercury surface** as the reference for pressure equality. Using the upper surface or pipe centerline without justification is penalized.
> 4. **Ignoring Pipe Fluid Density (–1 to –2 marks):** If the pipe carries oil/gas instead of water, students often use $\rho_w$ in their formula. Always use the **actual pipe fluid density**.
> 5. **Specific Gravity vs Density Confusion (–1 mark):** Remember $\rho = S \times 1000$ kg/m³. Specific gravity of mercury is 13.6, NOT its density.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Quick-Fire Revision Points for KTU Exam Day:**
>
> - [x] **Master the Go-Down-Go-Up Rule:** DOWN = ADD pressure; UP = SUBTRACT pressure.
> - [x] **Always take the lower mercury surface as datum** for pressure equality.
> - [x] **Standard formula for same-level points:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$.
> - [x] **Different-level points:** Add correction term $\rho_f g (z_B - z_A)$.
> - [x] **Mercury density = 13,600 kg/m³** (Sg = 13.6); **water density = 1000 kg/m³**.
> - [x] **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless KTU specifies otherwise (rarely 9.806 or 10).
> - [x] **Convert mm → m** before calculation (divide by 1000).
> - [x] **Final answer expected in kPa** for most KTU problems (divide Pa by 1000).
> - [x] **Piezometer** = single vertical tube; **U-tube** = two connected limbs; **Differential** = measures ΔP; **Inverted** = for light fluids.
> - [x] **Manometer equation is dimensionally consistent:** $[\rho][g][h] = \text{N/m}^2 = \text{Pa}$.
> - [x] **Pressure difference can be negative** — interpret the sign based on geometry (higher mercury level on the side with **lower pressure**).
> - [x] **Inverted U-tube** is used when manometric fluid is **lighter** than pipe fluid (oil in oil pipelines, air in gas systems).
> - [x] **Practice tracing pressure paths** step-by-step — this is the most-skipped step in KTU answer scripts and loses maximum marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Fluid Flow: Fluid Flow Classifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Course Code:** PEPOT416
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Fluid Flow: Fluid Flow Classifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Fluid Flow?

**Fluid flow** is the study of the motion of fluids (liquids and gases) and the forces acting on them. In engineering practice, fluid flow analysis is fundamental to the design of pipelines, channels, pumps, turbines, aircraft, hydraulic structures, and chemical process equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> Fluid flow is defined as the continuous deformation of a fluid body under the action of applied shear stresses. Mathematically, it is described by the velocity field $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$, where the velocity vector is a function of spatial coordinates and time.

## 1.2 Why Do We Classify Fluid Flow?

In real engineering problems, fluid behaviour varies enormously:

- **Tap water** flows quietly through a household pipe.
- **High-speed air** over an aircraft wing behaves very differently.
- **Blood** flowing in arteries is neither purely steady nor purely turbulent.

Because of this wide range of behaviour, engineers classify fluid flow into categories so that they can:

- Apply the **correct governing equations** (Euler's equation, Navier–Stokes equations, Bernoulli's equation).
- Select the **right analytical method** (integral vs differential control volume approach).
- Predict **frictional losses, energy transfer, and pressure distribution** accurately.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The River Story

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Intuition — The River Analogy**
> Imagine watching a river:
> - On a calm day, the water moves in **smooth, parallel layers** (like sheets of glass sliding over each other) — this is **laminar flow**.
> - In rapids, the water is **chaotic, with eddies and swirls** — this is **turbulent flow**.
> - A deep, slow section has water moving at nearly the **same speed everywhere** — this is **uniform flow**.
> - A section near a waterfall has water **accelerating** as it descends — this is **non-uniform (or varied) flow**.
> - If you watch a single leaf for an hour, its velocity does not change much — this is **steady flow**. If the dam upstream releases water periodically, the velocity at your point changes — this is **unsteady flow**.

This simple analogy is the foundation of all flow classification in fluid mechanics.

## 1.4 Primary Dimensions Governing Flow Classification

The classification of any fluid flow depends on three primary measurable quantities:

- **Velocity ($V$)** — measured in $\text{m/s}$
- **Density ($\rho$)** — measured in $\text{kg/m}^3$
- **Viscosity ($\mu$ or $\nu$)** — measured in $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$

From these, the two most important **dimensionless numbers** used in classification are:

- **Reynolds Number ($Re$)** — ratio of inertial to viscous forces
- **Mach Number ($M$)** — ratio of flow velocity to local speed of sound

Both of these will be derived rigorously in Section 3.

## 1.5 Overview of Flow Classification Schemes

| Basis of Classification | Categories |
|---|---|
| Time dependence | Steady / Unsteady |
| Spatial variation | Uniform / Non-uniform |
| Reynolds number | Laminar / Transitional / Turbulent |
| Mach number | Subsonic / Sonic / Supersonic / Hypersonic |
| Density change | Compressible / Incompressible |
| Rotation of fluid elements | Rotational / Irrotational |
| Number of spatial dimensions | 1-D / 2-D / 3-D |
| Flow geometry | Internal / External |
| Pathline behaviour | Streamline / Streakline / Pathline |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Question papers frequently ask students to "classify the following flows" or to "justify whether a given flow is steady/uniform/laminar/etc." Memorizing the criteria (and the threshold values for $Re$ and $M$) is essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification Based on Time Variation — Steady vs Unsteady Flow

### 2.1.1 Steady Flow

A flow is called **steady** if the fluid properties (velocity, pressure, density) at **every point in the flow field do not change with time**.

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Even though individual fluid particles are moving, the *pattern* of the flow (streamlines) remains unchanged from instant to instant. Think of a tap running at a fixed rate — the shape of the water jet is the same at every second.

### 2.1.2 Unsteady Flow

A flow is **unsteady** when fluid properties at any point **change with time**.

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} \neq 0$$

**Engineering Examples:**
- Flow during the startup of a pump
- Oscillating flow in the human vocal tract
- Surge waves in a penstock after sudden valve closure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-classification — Quasi-Steady Flow:** When the time-scale of property change is much larger than the transit time of fluid through the system, the flow is treated as *quasi-steady*. KTU examiners often test this nuance.

## 2.2 Classification Based on Space — Uniform vs Non-Uniform Flow

### 2.2.1 Uniform Flow

Velocity, pressure, and cross-section **do not change along the direction of flow** at a given instant.

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0 \quad \text{(along streamline)}$$

**Example:** Flow in a constant-diameter long pipe far from entrance effects.

### 2.2.2 Non-Uniform (Varied) Flow

Velocity **changes from point to point** along the flow direction.

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} \neq 0$$

**Example:** Flow through a converging nozzle, or a river approaching a waterfall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Steady ≠ Uniform, Unsteady ≠ Non-uniform.** A flow can be *steady and non-uniform* (e.g., flow in a tapered pipe at constant discharge) or *unsteady and uniform* (e.g., a long pipe after pump shutdown, where the velocity everywhere decays together).

## 2.3 Classification Based on Reynolds Number — Laminar vs Turbulent Flow

This is the **most important classification** in the entire module. It determines the internal structure of the flow.

### 2.3.1 Laminar Flow

- Fluid particles move in **smooth, parallel layers** (laminae) without mixing.
- Velocity is low; viscous forces dominate over inertial forces.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re < 2000$ (for pipe flow).

### 2.3.2 Transitional Flow

- Unstable, fluctuating between laminar and turbulent.
- $2000 < Re < 4000$.

### 2.3.3 Turbulent Flow

- Fluid particles move in **irregular, chaotic paths** with continuous mixing.
- Inertial forces dominate over viscous forces.
- $Re > 4000$.

**The Reynolds Number is defined as:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

where $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is the **kinematic viscosity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Osborne Reynolds (1883) Experiment:** Reynolds famously injected dye into a transparent pipe carrying water. At low velocities, the dye moved in a single straight line (laminar). Beyond a critical velocity, the dye diffused chaotically throughout the pipe (turbulent). This experiment is a KTU favourite for descriptive questions.

## 2.4 Classification Based on Mach Number — Compressibility Effects

The **Mach Number** quantifies compressibility:

$$M = \frac{V}{c}$$

where $c$ is the **local speed of sound** in the fluid:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

for a perfect gas, with $K$ = bulk modulus, $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats, $R$ = gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature.

| Mach Number Range | Classification | Density Change |
|---|---|---|
| $M < 0.3$ | **Incompressible** | Negligible ($< 2\%$) |
| $0.3 \leq M < 0.8$ | **Subsonic** | Small but non-negligible |
| $0.8 \leq M \leq 1.2$ | **Transonic** | Significant |
| $1.2 < M < 5.0$ | **Supersonic** | Large |
| $M > 5.0$ | **Hypersonic** | Very large |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** All liquid flows and all gas flows with $M < 0.3$ are treated as **incompressible**. Most civil and mechanical engineering problems (water supply, HVAC, low-speed aerodynamics) fall in this regime.

## 2.5 Classification Based on Rotation — Rotational vs Irrotational Flow

A flow is **irrotational** if every fluid element has **zero net angular velocity** about its own centre. Mathematically, the fluid particle does not spin.

- **Irrotational flow:** $\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{V} = 0$ (vorticity $\vec{\omega} = 0$)
- **Rotational flow:** $\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{V} \neq 0$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hint:** Solid-body rotation (like a rotating cylinder) and flow with viscous boundary layers are rotational. Ideal flow around a streamlined body (away from boundary layer) is irrotational. The concept of *velocity potential* ($\phi$, where $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$) applies only to irrotational flow.

## 2.6 Classification Based on Number of Dimensions

| Type | Velocity Components | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **1-D Flow** | $V = f(x)$ only | Flow in a long straight pipe |
| **2-D Flow** | $V = f(x, y)$ | Flow over a long cylinder (neglecting end effects) |
| **3-D Flow** | $V = f(x, y, z)$ | Flow around a finite wing, flow in a junction |

## 2.7 Classification Based on Flow Geometry

- **Internal Flow:** Fluid is bounded by solid surfaces on all sides. Examples: pipe flow, duct flow, flow in a nozzle.
- **External Flow:** Fluid is unbounded except for the body around which it flows. Examples: flow over a building, flow past an aircraft wing, wind over a bridge deck.

## 2.8 Pathline, Streakline, and Streamline

These three are *visualization tools* — not independent classifications, but often asked together.

- **Pathline:** The actual trajectory traced by a single fluid particle over time.
- **Streakline:** The locus of all particles that have previously passed through a given point (e.g., dye injected continuously at a fixed point).
- **Streamline:** A line that is everywhere tangent to the velocity field at a given instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For STEADY flow:** Pathlines, streaklines, and streamlines are **identical**.
> **For UNSTEADY flow:** They are **different**.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Flow Classification Used |
|---|---|
| Water supply networks | Steady, incompressible, turbulent, internal |
| Aircraft design | Compressible, subsonic/supersonic, external, 3-D |
| Hydraulic turbines | Rotational, unsteady during transient operation |
| Chemical reactors | Laminar/turbulent based on $Re$, incompressible |
| Blood flow modelling | Unsteady, pulsatile, transitional, non-Newtonian |
| Wind engineering on tall buildings | External, turbulent, incompressible (low $M$) |

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Threshold | Condition / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces |
| 2 | Reynolds Number (alt.) | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ |
| 3 | Laminar limit | $Re < 2000$ | — | Pipe flow laminar |
| 4 | Turbulent limit | $Re > 4000$ | — | Pipe flow turbulent |
| 5 | Mach Number | $M = \dfrac{V}{c}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of flow to sound speed |
| 6 | Speed of sound (gas) | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Perfect gas |
| 7 | Bulk modulus relation | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | General fluid |
| 8 | Vorticity | $\vec{\omega} = \vec{\nabla} \times \vec{V}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Zero ⇒ irrotational |
| 9 | Steady flow | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ | — | Time-invariant |
| 10 | Uniform flow | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0$ | — | Space-invariant |
| 11 | Incompressibility | $\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$ | — | Constant density |
| 12 | Velocity potential | $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Exists only for irrotational flow |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Students frequently lose marks by confusing *laminar* with *steady*, or *uniform* with *steady*. These are completely orthogonal classifications. Always justify your answer by **which variable is held constant** (time vs position).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dimensional Analysis of Reynolds Number

We want a dimensionless number that compares **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** in a flow.

**Step 1: Identify the relevant physical quantities.**

Inertial force per unit volume in a flow is proportional to $\rho V^2 / L$ (from Newton's second law on a fluid element of size $L$).

Viscous force per unit volume is proportional to $\mu V / L^2$ (from Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \, dV/dy \sim \mu V / L$).

**Step 2: Form the ratio.**

$$Re = \frac{\text{Inertial force per unit volume}}{\text{Viscous force per unit volume}}$$

$$Re = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2}$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V^2}{L} \cdot \frac{L^2}{\mu V} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

**Step 4: Express using kinematic viscosity $\nu$.**

Since $\nu = \mu / \rho$, we can also write:

$$Re = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

**Step 5: Confirm dimensions.**

$$[Re] = \frac{[\rho][V][L]}{[\mu]} = \frac{(\text{kg/m}^3)(\text{m/s})(\text{m})}{\text{kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}} = \frac{\text{kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}}{\text{kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> Reynolds number is **dimensionless**, which is why it can be used universally regardless of unit system (SI, CGS, Imperial).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mach Number from Speed of Sound

The speed of sound in a fluid is the speed at which a small pressure disturbance propagates.

**Step 1: Consider a small pressure disturbance $dp$ causing a density change $d\rho$.**

The compressibility of the fluid is characterized by its **bulk modulus** $K$:

$$K = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $dp$:**

$$dp = K \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 3: Apply continuity. A pressure wave moving at speed $c$ in a fluid of density $\rho$ creates a moving mass flux.**

For a control volume, the mass continuity across a wave front moving at $c$:

$$\rho c = (\rho + d\rho)(c - dV)$$

Ignoring second-order small quantities:

$$0 = \rho \, dV - c \, d\rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad dV = \frac{c}{\rho} d\rho$$

**Step 4: Apply momentum equation to a small fluid element accelerated by $dp$ across the wave front:**

$$dp = \rho c \, dV$$

**Step 5: Substitute $dV$ from Step 3 into Step 4:**

$$dp = \rho c \cdot \frac{c}{\rho} d\rho = c^2 d\rho$$

**Step 6: Compare with the bulk modulus relation $dp = K \, d\rho / \rho$:**

$$c^2 d\rho = \frac{K}{\rho} d\rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad c^2 = \frac{K}{\rho}$$

**Step 7: Take the square root:**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$

**Step 8: For a perfect gas, $K = \gamma p = \gamma \rho R T$, so:**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma p}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

**Step 9: Define Mach Number:**

$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Reynolds Number Criterion (Dimensional Reasoning)

We want to determine the critical velocity $V_c$ above which pipe flow becomes turbulent.

**Step 1: The transition is governed by three physical parameters:**

- Pipe diameter $D$
- Fluid density $\rho$
- Fluid viscosity $\mu$

**Step 2: By Buckingham's $\Pi$-theorem, a single dimensionless group is formed:**

$$\Pi = f(Re) = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

**Step 3: The transition occurs at a critical value $Re_c$, determined empirically by Reynolds (1883):**

$$Re_c = \frac{\rho V_c D}{\mu} \approx 2000 \text{ to } 2300$$

**Step 4: Therefore, the critical velocity is:**

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \, \mu}{\rho D} = \frac{Re_c \, \nu}{D}$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.** For water at 20 °C, $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, in a 25 mm diameter pipe:

$$V_c = \frac{2000 \times 1.004 \times 10^{-6}}{0.025} = 0.0803 \, \text{m/s}$$

Any mean velocity above 8 cm/s makes the flow turbulent.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Continuity Equation (1-D Steady)

This is foundational to flow classification because it links velocity and area.

**Step 1: Consider a streamtube of cross-section $A_1$ at section 1 and $A_2$ at section 2.**

Mass flow rate must be conserved for a steady flow with no sources/sinks:

$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$$

**Step 2: Express mass flow rate:**

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

**Step 3: For incompressible flow ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$):**

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q = \text{constant}}$$

**Step 4: Generalizing for compressible steady flow:**

$$\rho A V = \text{constant along streamtube}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for "How to Classify"

| Given Scenario | First Question to Ask | Classification Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Pipe flow, water, $V = 1$ m/s, $D = 50$ mm | Calculate $Re$ | $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ → if $> 4000$, **turbulent** |
| Aircraft at $V = 250$ m/s, $T = 288$ K | Calculate $M$ | $M = V / \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ → if $> 1$, **supersonic** |
| Water tap opened at constant rate | Does velocity change at a point? | $\partial V / \partial t = 0$ → **steady** |
| Tap opened slowly increasing | Does velocity change at a point? | $\partial V / \partial t \neq 0$ → **unsteady** |
