## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish clearly between the Eulerian and Lagrangian approaches to fluid flow description. State one practical advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Lagrangian Approach | Eulerian Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reference frame | Follows the fluid particle | Fixed at a spatial point |
| Independent variables | Initial labels $(a, b, c)$ and $t$ | Spatial coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and $t$ |
| Typical output | Trajectory $\vec{r}(a, b, c, t)$ | Field $f(x, y, z, t)$ |
| **Practical advantage** | Useful for analyzing the history of a specific particle (e.g., a pollutant tracer or blood corpuscle in a vessel) | Preferred in practice because it avoids tracking an astronomically large number of particles and naturally produces field quantities usable in differential equations like Navier–Stokes |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*With a neat expression, explain the physical significance of the material derivative $D/Dt$ for a scalar field $\phi(x, y, z, t)$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \left(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\right)\phi$$

The material derivative gives the **total time rate of change of a property as experienced by a moving fluid particle**. It combines two effects: (i) the **local rate of change** $\partial \phi / \partial t$ measured by a stationary observer (representing the unsteadiness of the flow at a fixed point), and (ii) the **convective rate of change** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\phi$ representing the change experienced by the particle as it moves through a spatially varying field. It is the **fundamental bridge** between Eulerian field data and Lagrangian particle history.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. Forgetting the convective term $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ in steady flow — students often drop it, but it is **non-zero unless the flow is uniform**.
> 2. Treating $\partial \vec{V} / \partial t$ and $D\vec{V} / Dt$ as the same quantity — they coincide **only for uniform flow**.
> 3. Substituting numerical values for $x, y, t$ **before** taking all partial derivatives — this leads to wrong answers because derivatives must be computed on the symbolic field first.
> 4. Confusing streamlines (Eulerian, instantaneous), pathlines (Lagrangian, history), and streaklines (Eulerian, dye experiment) — they are identical **only for steady flow**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 2, Choice A**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Module Test Paper, Model Question]**
> 
> The velocity field in an incompressible flow is given by:
> $$\vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = (x^2 + y^2)\hat{i} + (2xy)\hat{j} + 0 \cdot \hat{k} \;\; \text{m/s}$$
> 
> **(a)** Compute the acceleration field $\vec{a}(x, y, z, t)$ experienced by a fluid particle. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Hence find the acceleration of the fluid particle located at the point $(1 \text{ m}, 2 \text{ m})$ at $t = 3 \text{ s}$. Also determine the magnitude of the acceleration. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a) Acceleration field derivation.**

**Step 1 — Read off the velocity components.**
$$u = x^2 + y^2, \quad v = 2xy, \quad w = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the local (temporal) derivatives.**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required spatial derivatives.**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2x, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2y$$
$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2y, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2x$$

**Step 4 — Apply the material derivative formula.**
$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$
$$a_x = 0 + (x^2 + y^2)(2x) + (2xy)(2y)$$
$$a_x = 2x^3 + 2xy^2 + 4xy^2 = 2x^3 + 6xy^2$$

$$a_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}$$
$$a_y = 0 + (x^2 + y^2)(2y) + (2xy)(2x)$$
$$a_y = 2x^2y + 2y^3 + 4x^2y = 6x^2y + 2y^3$$

$$a_z = 0$$

**Step 5 — Acceleration field.**
$$\boxed{\vec{a}(x, y, t) = \left(2x^3 + 6xy^2\right)\hat{i} + \left(6x^2y + 2y^3\right)\hat{j} \;\; \text{m/s}^2}$$

**(b) Acceleration at the specified point.**

**Step 1 — Substitute $x = 1$, $y = 2$.**
$$a_x = 2(1)^3 + 6(1)(2)^2 = 2 + 24 = 26 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$a_y = 6(1)^2(2) + 2(2)^3 = 12 + 16 = 28 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Acceleration vector.**
$$\vec{a} = 26\hat{i} + 28\hat{j} \;\; \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude.**
$$\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{26^2 + 28^2} = \sqrt{676 + 784} = \sqrt{1460} \approx 38.21 \text{ m/s}^2$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 2, Choice B (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Module Test Paper, Alternative Question]**
> 
> **(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the expression for the **material derivative of a scalar field** $\phi(x, y, z, t)$ following a fluid particle. Clearly state each assumption and explain the role of the local and convective terms. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A 2-D Eulerian velocity field is given by $\vec{V} = (2x + y)\hat{i} + (x - y)\hat{j}$ m/s. Determine:
> (i) Whether the flow is **steady** or **unsteady**.
> (ii) Whether it is **uniform** or **non-uniform**.
> (iii) The **acceleration** of the particle currently at $(1, 1)$ m. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 for full step-by-step derivation).**

**Step 1 — Taylor expansion of $\phi$.**
Following a fluid particle, after time $dt$ the particle moves to $(x + u\,dt, y + v\,dt, z + w\,dt, t + dt)$:

$$\phi(t + dt) = \phi(x, y, z, t) + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}dt + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}u\,dt + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}v\,dt + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}w\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Divide by $dt$ and take the limit.**

$$\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3 — Vector form.**

$$\boxed{\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \left(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\right)\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Role of each term.**
- **Local term** $\partial \phi / \partial t$: rate of change at a fixed point due to time variation alone (zero for steady flow).
- **Convective term** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\phi$: rate of change due to the particle being transported by velocity $\vec{V}$ through a spatially varying field.

**(b) Evaluation.**

**Step 1 — Read velocity components.**
$$u = 2x + y, \quad v = x - y$$

**Step 2 — (i) Steadiness test.**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Flow is STEADY}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Uniformity test.**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2 \neq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Flow is NON-UNIFORM}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Acceleration at $(1, 1)$.**
Spatial derivatives:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -1$$

Velocity at $(1, 1)$:
$$u = 2(1) + 1 = 3 \text{ m/s}, \quad v = 1 - 1 = 0 \text{ m/s}$$

Acceleration components:
$$a_x = 0 + (3)(2) + (0)(1) = 6 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$a_y = 0 + (3)(1) + (0)(-1) = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{\vec{a} = 6\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} \;\; \text{m/s}^2}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **Lagrangian Approach:** Watch a single particle; independent variables are the labels $(a, b, c)$ and time $t$; output is the trajectory $\vec{r}(a, b, c, t)$.
- **Eulerian Approach:** Sit at a fixed point; independent variables are $(x, y, z)$ and $t$; output is the field $f(x, y, z, t)$.
- **Material derivative** (also called substantial, total, particle derivative) is the **rate following the fluid particle**:
  $$\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla$$
- The material derivative is **the bridge** that converts Eulerian field data into Lagrangian particle rates.
- **Local derivative** $\partial / \partial t$ = zero $\Leftrightarrow$ **steady flow**.
- **Convective derivative** $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla$ = zero $\Leftrightarrow$ **uniform flow**.
- Both terms vanish **only for steady + uniform flow**.
- **Acceleration field** for fluid flow is $D\vec{V}/Dt$ — the **inertia term** that appears in the Navier–Stokes equations.
- **Pathline** = trajectory of one particle (Lagrangian); **Streamline** = instantaneous tangent to velocity (Eulerian); **Streakline** = locus of dye particles passing through a point (Eulerian).
- In **steady flow**, pathline = streamline = streakline.
- The convective term $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ can be split using the identity:
  $$\left(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\right)\vec{V} = \nabla\left(\frac{\vert \vec{V} \vert^2}{2}\right) - \vec{V} \times \left(\nabla \times \vec{V}\right)$$
- Eulerian approach is preferred in modern fluid mechanics because the number of particles is astronomically large (Avogadro-scale).
- **Always differentiate symbolically first**, then substitute numerical values of $(x, y, z, t)$ — this is the single most common error in KTU valuation.
- **Units to remember:** Velocity [m/s], acceleration [m/s²], spatial coordinates [m], time [s].

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Fluid Mechanics**, the **Velocity Potential (φ)** is a **scalar function of spatial coordinates and time** such that the **velocity vector field** of the fluid flow is expressed as the **gradient** of this scalar function. Mathematically, for a 2D or 3D flow field, the velocity potential is defined as the function that satisfies:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$$

This means each component of the velocity vector equals the partial derivative of φ with respect to the corresponding spatial coordinate:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}, \quad w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> A velocity potential function **φ** can exist **ONLY for irrotational flow**, where the vorticity vector (curl of velocity) is identically zero: $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$. If flow has any rotation, φ **does not exist**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a hilly landscape (like the Western Ghats of Kerala). At every point, you can define an **elevation (height)** — a single number. Now, if you place a ball on this terrain, the ball naturally rolls in the direction of the **steepest descent**. The **velocity** of the ball is always perpendicular to the contour lines (lines of constant height) and is determined by the **gradient** of the height function.

In this analogy:
- **Height (h)** at every point $\rightarrow$ **Velocity Potential (φ)** at every point in fluid
- **Direction of ball's motion** $\rightarrow$ **Direction of fluid flow**
- **Steepest descent direction** $\rightarrow$ **Velocity vector** = $\nabla \phi$
- **Contour lines of constant height** $\rightarrow$ **Equipotential lines** (lines where $\phi$ = constant)
- **Ball's path perpendicular to contours** $\rightarrow$ **Streamlines perpendicular to equipotential lines**

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a ball moves in the direction of maximum rate of decrease of height, fluid particles move in the direction of maximum rate of decrease of velocity potential. The **velocity magnitude** equals the rate of change of φ along the streamline.

## 1.3 Physical Significance and Boundary Constants

- The velocity potential **φ** has units of **m²/s** (same as kinematic viscosity, but they are completely different concepts).
- For steady flow, $\phi = \phi(x, y, z)$; for unsteady flow, $\phi = \phi(x, y, z, t)$.
- **φ** is a continuous and differentiable scalar field in a simply connected region for irrotational flow.
- Equipotential lines (φ = constant) and streamlines (ψ = constant) form an **orthogonal curvilinear grid** in 2D flow.

> [!TIP]
> The stream function ψ (covered as a related topic) and velocity potential φ together form the **complex potential** $W = \phi + i\psi$, allowing powerful analogies with 2D potential theory. KTU students should remember that **both φ and ψ individually satisfy Laplace's equation** in irrotational, incompressible flow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Existence Condition — The Irrotationality Criterion

For a velocity potential φ to exist, the flow **must be irrotational**. The mathematical condition is that the curl of the velocity vector vanishes everywhere in the flow field:

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$$

Expanding the curl in Cartesian coordinates:

$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ u & v & w \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

This gives three scalar conditions:

- $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0$
- $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} = 0$
- $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$

When all three hold simultaneously, φ exists and the flow is **conservative** (work done is path-independent).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of Existence

1. **Start with a velocity field** $\vec{V} = (u, v, w)$.
2. **Compute the curl**: If $\nabla \times \vec{V} \neq 0$, no scalar potential exists.
3. **If curl is zero**, then there exists a function φ such that $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$.
4. **Integrate** any velocity component along its coordinate to recover φ (e.g., $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$ ⇒ $\phi = \int u \, dx$).
5. **Verify** that the integrated φ reproduces all velocity components consistently.

## 2.3 Laplace's Equation for φ

For an **incompressible** ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$) and **irrotational** flow:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{V} = \nabla \phi$$

Combining these:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \phi) = \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

In Cartesian coordinates, this expands to the **Laplace equation**:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any scalar function satisfying Laplace's equation is called a **harmonic function**. In 2D potential flow problems, solutions of $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ form the entire KTU ESE module of potential flow theory.

## 2.4 Cauchy–Riemann Equations and Orthogonality

For 2D flow, the stream function ψ and velocity potential φ satisfy the **Cauchy–Riemann equations**:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Proof of orthogonality** — The dot product of $\nabla \phi$ and $\nabla \psi$ is:

$$\nabla \phi \cdot \nabla \psi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = u(-v) + v(u) = 0$$

Since the dot product is zero, the **families of curves** φ = const and ψ = const intersect **at right angles**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:---------:|:------------|:----------------|:---------:|
| 1 | $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ | Velocity as gradient of potential | m/s |
| 2 | $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$ | x-component of velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $v = \partial \phi / \partial y$ | y-component of velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $w = \partial \phi / \partial z$ | z-component of velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ | Irrotationality condition | 1/s |
| 6 | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ | Laplace's equation for φ | m/s · (1/m²) = 1/(m·s) |
| 7 | $\partial \phi / \partial x = \partial \psi / \partial y$ | Cauchy–Riemann eq. (1) | m/s |
| 8 | $\partial \phi / \partial y = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ | Cauchy–Riemann eq. (2) | m/s |
| 9 | $V = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2} = \vert \nabla \phi \vert$ | Speed from potential gradient | m/s |
| 10 | $\phi_{\text{net}} = \phi_1 + \phi_2$ | Superposition principle | m²/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Engineering**: Computing pressure distribution over aircraft wings using $\phi$ to find $\nabla \phi$ and then Bernoulli's equation.
- **Hydrogeology**: Modeling groundwater flow through porous media (analogous to potential flow).
- **Turbomachinery**: Designing blade shapes for pumps and turbines by analyzing $\phi$ and ψ fields.
- **Meteorology**: Analyzing wind flow patterns where irrotational assumption holds in large-scale atmospheric motion.
- **Naval Architecture**: Hull form design for minimizing wave resistance using potential flow theory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving Laplace's Equation for φ

**Starting premise:** The flow is **incompressible** (continuity) and **irrotational** (potential exists).

**Step 1:** The continuity equation for incompressible flow in vector form:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Express velocity vector in terms of φ:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi \implies u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\hat{k}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into continuity:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \phi) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the Laplacian operator:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Logic:** Continuity demands zero net volume flux, while irrotationality forces velocity to be a gradient field. The only way both can hold is for the **divergence of the gradient** to vanish, which is precisely Laplace's equation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Computing φ for Uniform Flow

**Given:** A uniform flow with velocity $U$ in the x-direction, where $u = U$ and $v = 0$.

**Step 1:** Apply the definition of velocity components:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = U, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Integrate the first equation with respect to x:

$$\phi = \int U \, dx = Ux + f(y)$$

where $f(y)$ is an "integration function" of y alone.

**Step 3:** Differentiate φ with respect to y:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = f'(y)$$

**Step 4:** Set this equal to v = 0:

$$f'(y) = 0 \implies f(y) = C \text{ (constant)}$$

**Step 5:** Final expression for velocity potential of uniform flow:

$$\phi = Ux + C$$

By convention, the constant is dropped (since only gradients of φ matter physically), giving:

$$\phi = Ux$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Verifying the Source Flow Potential

**Given:** A 2D source of strength $m$ (volume flow rate per unit depth) at the origin, with radial velocity $V_r = m / (2\pi r)$ and $V_\theta = 0$.

**Step 1:** In polar coordinates, the velocity components are:

$$V_r = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = \frac{m}{2\pi r}, \quad V_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Integrate $V_r$ with respect to r:

$$\phi = \int \frac{m}{2\pi r} \, dr = \frac{m}{2\pi} \ln r + g(\theta)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to θ and apply $V_\theta = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \cdot g'(\theta) = 0 \implies g'(\theta) = 0 \implies g(\theta) = \text{const}$$

**Step 4:** Final potential for 2D source flow:

$$\phi = \frac{m}{2\pi} \ln r$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** Computing $\nabla^2 \phi$ in polar coordinates confirms it satisfies Laplace's equation (a topic tested in KTU ESE Module 2 problems). This shows the source flow is a valid potential flow solution.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stream Function from Velocity Potential

**Given:** φ for uniform flow $\phi = Ux$, derive ψ.

**Step 1:** Apply the Cauchy–Riemann equations:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = U, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Integrate $u = \partial \psi / \partial y = U$ with respect to y:

$$\psi = Uy + h(x)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to x:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = h'(x) = -v = 0 \implies h(x) = \text{const}$$

**Step 4:** Final stream function:

$$\psi = Uy$$

**Consistency check:** Streamlines are $Uy$ = const, i.e., $y$ = const, which are horizontal lines — perfectly matching the physical direction of uniform flow.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Elementary Potential Flows

| **Flow Type** | **Velocity Potential (φ)** | **Stream Function (ψ)** | **Velocity Components** |
|:--------------|:--------------------------|:------------------------|:-----------------------|
| Uniform flow (x-dir) | $Ux$ | $Uy$ | $u = U, v = 0$ |
| Uniform flow (y-dir) | $Vy$ | $-Vx$ | $u = 0, v = V$ |
| 2D Source/Sink | $\frac{m}{2\pi} \ln r$ | $\frac{m}{2\pi} \theta$ | $V_r = \frac{m}{2\pi r}, V_\theta = 0$ |
| 2D Vortex | $-\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi} \theta$ | $\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi} \ln r$ | $V_r = 0, V_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}$ |
| Doublet | $\frac{\mu \cos\theta}{r}$ | $\frac{\mu \sin\theta}{r}$ | Complex flow field |
| Flow past cylinder | $U(r + \frac{R^2}{r})\cos\theta$ | $U(r - \frac{R^2}{r})\sin\theta$ | Body with circulation |

> [!TIP]
> **Superposition principle:** Complex flows can be constructed by **adding velocity potentials** of elementary flows. For example, uniform flow + doublet = flow past a cylinder. This is a **favourite KTU ESE question** pattern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Velocity Field to Velocity Potential

The following block diagram illustrates the logical pathway connecting a given velocity field to the existence and form of the velocity potential function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Velocity Field V u,v,w] --> B[Compute Curl of V]
    B --> C{Is Curl of V equal to zero?}
    C -->|No| D[Velocity Potential DOES NOT EXIST]
    C -->|Yes| E[Velocity Potential EXISTS]
    E --> F[Integrate u with respect to x]
    F --> G[Integrate v with respect to y]
    G --> H[Integrate w with respect to z]
    H --> I{Cross-verify all three}
    I -->|Mismatch| J[Recompute and adjust constants]
    I -->|Match| K[Final Velocity Potential phi obtained]
    K --> L[Apply Continuity Equation]
    L --> M[Derive Laplace Equation for phi]
    M --> N[Use Cauchy Riemann with Stream Function]
    N --> O[Construct Equipotential Lines and Streamlines]
    O --> P[Orthogonal Curvilinear Grid Complete]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram: Role of φ in Potential Flow Analysis

The following diagram depicts the **functional architecture** showing how velocity potential feeds into the broader fluid dynamics analysis pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Boundary Conditions] --> A2[Flow Geometry]
    end
    subgraph POTENTIAL[Potential Computation]
        B1[Velocity Potential phi x,y,z] --> B2[Laplaces Equation for phi]
        B2 --> B3[Stream Function psi x,y,z]
        B1 --> B3
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Engineering Outputs]
        C1[Velocity Field V] --> C2[Pressure via Bernoulli]
        C2 --> C3[Force on Body]
        C1 --> C4[Flow Rate]
        B3 --> C1
    end
    A2 --> B1
    INPUT --> POTENTIAL
    POTENTIAL --> OUTPUT
```

## 4.3 Orthogonality Schematic: Equipotential Lines vs Streamlines

The following diagram illustrates the orthogonal relationship between equipotential lines and streamlines for a typical source-superimposed uniform flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equipotential Line phi equals constant 1] --> B[Intersection Point A1]
    C[Equipotential Line phi equals constant 2] --> D[Intersection Point A2]
    E[Streamline psi equals constant 1] --> B
    F[Streamline psi equals constant 2] --> D
    B --> G[Right Angle 90 degrees]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Orthogonal Curvilinear Grid]
    A --> H
    C --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway from the schematics:** In any potential flow problem, **knowing φ** is equivalent to **knowing the entire velocity field**, because the gradient operation is a one-to-one mapping in simply connected regions. This is the central reason why φ is so powerful in KTU ESE Module 2 problem-solving.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — *Define velocity potential function. State the condition for its existence.*

**Model Answer:**

The velocity potential φ is a scalar function of space and time such that the velocity vector equals its gradient: $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$. Its components are $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$, $v = \partial \phi / \partial y$, $w = \partial \phi / \partial z$.

**Condition for existence:** The flow must be **irrotational**, i.e., the curl of the velocity vector must be zero everywhere in the flow field:

$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$$

Specifically:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}$$

**(3 Marks — Full credit)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — *State Laplace's equation in terms of velocity potential and prove that it holds for an irrotational, incompressible flow.*

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For an incompressible, irrotational flow, the velocity potential φ satisfies:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

**Proof:**
For incompressible flow, continuity gives $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$. For irrotational flow, $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$. Substituting:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \phi) = 0 \implies \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

In Cartesian form:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

This is **Laplace's equation** for the velocity potential. **(3 Marks — Full credit)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** — *Velocity Potential and Its Properties*

**(a)** Derive the conditions under which a velocity potential function exists. Show that for an irrotational, incompressible flow, the velocity potential satisfies Laplace's equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the velocity field $\vec{V} = (3x^2 - 3y^2)\hat{i} + (6xy - 3y^2)\hat{j}$, determine whether a velocity potential exists. If yes, find φ and verify that it satisfies Laplace's equation. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1:** For a velocity potential φ to exist, the flow must be irrotational:

$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$$

In 2D Cartesian form:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$ and $v = \partial \phi / \partial y$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right)$$

This is automatically satisfied due to the **equality of mixed partial derivatives** (Clairaut's theorem), confirming φ exists.

**Step 3:** Apply the incompressibility condition $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** This is **Laplace's equation**, $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$. Any function φ satisfying this represents a valid potential flow. **(7 Marks — Full credit)**

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1:** Check irrotationality by computing the vorticity:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

Compute each term:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(6xy - 3y^2) = 6y$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x^2 - 3y^2) = -6y$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_z = 6y - (-6y) = 12y$$

**Step 2:** Since $\omega_z \neq 0$ in general, **the flow is NOT irrotational**. Therefore, **a velocity potential does NOT exist** for this velocity field.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students will attempt to find φ by integrating $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$ directly without first checking irrotationality. This wastes 4–5 marks in valuation. **Always check the curl FIRST** before integrating.

**Step 3 (Verification by attempting integration):** If we wrongly attempt $\phi = \int u \, dx$:

$$\phi = \int (3x^2 - 3y^2) \, dx = x^3 - 3xy^2 + f(y)$$

Then $v = \partial \phi / \partial y = -6xy + f'(y)$. Setting equal to the given $v$:

$$-6xy + f'(y) = 6xy - 3y^2 \implies f'(y) = 12xy - 3y^2$$

This depends on x — **a contradiction**, since $f(y)$ cannot depend on x. Hence, **no potential function exists**, confirming our irrotationality check. **(7 Marks — Full credit)**

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Internal Choice Alternative]** — *Elementary Potential Flows*

**(a)** Derive the velocity potential and stream function for a uniform flow of magnitude $U$ in the x-direction. Show that the equipotential lines and streamlines form an orthogonal system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 2D source of strength $m = 4\pi$ m²/s is located at the origin. Find the velocity potential and stream function. Determine the radial velocity at a point 2 m from the origin and the angle of the streamline passing through the point (3 m, 4 m). **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Step 1:** For uniform flow in x-direction, velocity components are:

$$u = U, \quad v = 0$$

**Step 2:** Using the definition of velocity potential:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = U \implies \phi = Ux + f(y)$$

Differentiating with respect to y:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = f'(y) = v = 0 \implies f(y) = C$$

Therefore, the velocity potential is:

$$\phi = Ux + C \quad \text{(constant usually dropped)} \quad \boxed{\phi = Ux}$$

**Step 3:** For the stream function, using Cauchy–Riemann:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = u = U \implies \psi = Uy + h(x)$$

Differentiating with respect to x:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = h'(x) = -v = 0 \implies h(x) = C$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\psi = Uy}$$

**Step 4: Orthogonality proof.** Compute gradients:

$$\nabla \phi = (U, 0), \quad \nabla \psi = (0, U)$$

Their dot product:

$$\nabla \phi \cdot \nabla \psi = (U)(0) + (0)(U) = 0$$

Since the dot product is zero, the families $\phi = \text{const}$ (vertical lines) and $\psi = \text{const}$ (horizontal lines) are **perpendicular to each other**. **(7 Marks — Full credit)**

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1:** For a 2D source of strength m, the standard results (derived in Section 3.3) are:

$$\phi = \frac{m}{2\pi} \ln r, \quad \psi = \frac{m}{2\pi} \theta$$

Substituting $m = 4\pi$ m²/s:

$$\phi = \frac{4\pi}{2\pi} \ln r = 2 \ln r \quad \text{(in m²/s)}$$

$$\psi = \frac{4\pi}{2\pi} \theta = 2\theta \quad \text{(in m²/s)}$$

**Step 2:** Radial velocity at $r = 2$ m:

$$V_r = \frac{m}{2\pi r} = \frac{4\pi}{2\pi \cdot 2} = 1 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Angle of streamline at point (3, 4):

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = 53.13^\circ$$

The streamline passing through this point has constant ψ value:

$$\psi = 2 \times 53.13^\circ \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 2 \times 0.9273 = 1.8546 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Verification:** Streamlines for a source are **radial lines** from the origin, confirming $\theta = 53.13°$ is constant for the entire radial line. **(7 Marks — Full credit)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Velocity Potential Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the irrotationality check** before integration: A 3–4 mark penalty is typical if students directly integrate without verifying $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$.
> 2. **Sign errors in Cauchy–Riemann equations**: Remember $v = \partial \phi / \partial y = -\partial \psi / \partial x$. Mixing up signs costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency**: The argument of logarithm (in source/sink potential) must be dimensionless. If the source strength has units m²/s, the logarithm argument is normalized: $\ln(r/r_0)$.
> 4. **Confusing stream function and potential**: In uniform flow along x-axis, $\phi = Ux$ (along flow) and $\psi = Uy$ (perpendicular to flow). This is a classic trap question.
> 5. **Missing the integration constant function $f(y)$**: Many students assume $\phi = \int u \, dx$ alone is enough. They forget to verify using the v-component.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (Core):** Velocity potential φ is a scalar function where $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$. It exists **only for irrotational flow** ($\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$).
- **Components:** $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$, $v = \partial \phi / \partial y$, $w = \partial \phi / \partial z$.
- **Laplace's Equation:** For incompressible + irrotational flow, $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$. This is the **governing PDE** for all potential flow problems.
- **Units:** Velocity potential has units of **m²/s**.
- **Equipotential lines** ($\phi$ = const) are perpendicular to **streamlines** ($\psi$ = const) at every point in the flow field.
- **Cauchy–Riemann equations** link φ and ψ: $\partial \phi / \partial x = \partial \psi / \partial y$ and $\partial \phi / \partial y = -\partial \psi / \partial x$.
- **Superposition principle:** Complex flows can be built by **adding velocity potentials** of elementary flows (uniform, source, sink, vortex, doublet).
- **Elementary potentials to memorize:**
  - Uniform flow: $\phi = Ux$
  - 2D source/sink: $\phi = (m/2\pi) \ln r$
  - 2D vortex: $\phi = -(\Gamma/2\pi) \theta$
- **Speed from potential:** $V = \vert \nabla \phi \vert = \sqrt{(\partial \phi / \partial x)^2 + (\partial \phi / \partial y)^2 + (\partial \phi / \partial z)^2}$
- **Practical check:** Always verify $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ **before** trying to find φ — this single step saves marks and prevents wasted effort.
- **Exam mantra:** "Irrotationality → φ exists → Laplace's equation → superposition → solution."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stream Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **two-dimensional, incompressible, steady fluid flow**, the **stream function** (denoted by the Greek letter $\psi$, "psi") is a **scalar function of space** $\psi(x, y)$ whose partial derivatives yield the velocity components of the flow. It is formally defined by the kinematic relations:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

where $u$ and $v$ are the velocity components along the $x$ and $y$ directions, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> A stream function is a function whose partial derivative with respect to a coordinate gives the velocity component perpendicular to that coordinate (with a sign convention). The existence of $\psi$ is **guaranteed for any 2D incompressible flow** because the continuity equation is automatically satisfied when $\psi$ is defined as above.

The **continuity equation** for a 2D incompressible flow is:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Substituting the definition of $\psi$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \, \partial x} = 0$$

This identity is **identically true** for any continuously differentiable scalar function $\psi$ (Clairaut–Schwarz theorem), confirming the existence of $\psi$ for 2D incompressible flow.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The River Map" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the stream function as a "topographic map" of a flowing river.**

Imagine standing on a high bridge over a wide, shallow river and looking down at the water surface. Even without seeing the actual water velocity arrows, you can describe the flow by drawing **isolines of equal "flow stage"** — curves connecting points that have the same "amount of fluid has passed" relative to a reference line.

- Each isoline $\psi(x, y) = \text{constant}$ is a **streamline** — the path a fluid particle follows.
- The **closer** two streamlines are drawn on the map, the **faster** the fluid is moving in that region (just like closely-spaced contour lines on a mountain map mean a steep slope).
- The **difference** $\Delta \psi$ between two streamlines physically represents the **volumetric flow rate per unit depth** crossing any curve joining the two streamlines.

So, $\psi$ does **not** represent a physical quantity flowing through space — it is a *mathematical bookkeeping device* that automatically satisfies conservation of mass (continuity) and whose gradient directions are everywhere tangent to the flow.

---

## 1.3 Physical Significance of $\psi$

| Aspect | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| **Geometric** | $\psi = \text{const}$ gives the family of **streamlines** of the flow. |
| **Kinematic** | $\partial \psi / \partial y = u$ and $-\partial \psi / \partial x = v$, so $\psi$ encodes the entire velocity field (for 2D incompressible flow). |
| **Volumetric** | The difference $\psi_2 - \psi_1$ between two streamlines equals the **volumetric flow rate per unit depth** across any curve joining them. |
| **Existence** | Guaranteed for 2D incompressible flow; not needed in 1D and is **not** generally defined in 3D (Stokes' stream function is a 3D analog in axisymmetric cases). |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** A common KTU question reads *"Show that the continuity equation is identically satisfied by the stream function."* The proof above (2 lines of substitution) is worth **3 full marks** — never skip it.

---

## 1.4 Stream Function vs Velocity Potential Function — A First Look

| Feature | Stream Function $\psi$ | Velocity Potential $\phi$ |
|---|---|---|
| Defined for | 2D incompressible flow (rotational or irrotational) | 2D incompressible **irrotational** flow only |
| Velocity relations | $u = \partial \psi / \partial y,\; v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ | $u = \partial \phi / \partial x,\; v = \partial \phi / \partial y$ |
| Constant surfaces are | **Streamlines** | **Equipotential lines** |
| Satisfies | Continuity (automatically) | Laplace equation ($\nabla^2 \phi = 0$) for irrotational flow |
| Streamlines & equipotentials | Always **mutually perpendicular** (for irrotational flow) | Same |

The detailed treatment of $\phi$ (velocity potential) and the complex potential $w = \phi + i\psi$ belongs to **Module 3 of KTU 2024 Scheme** and is referenced here only for completeness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation of the Stream Function Definition

We start from the **continuity equation** for a steady, 2D, incompressible flow:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

We wish to find a single scalar function $\psi(x, y)$ from which $u$ and $v$ can be obtained by differentiation. From multivariable calculus, if there exists a function $\psi$ such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{and} \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

then:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y}, \qquad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \, \partial x}$$

Substituting in continuity:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \, \partial x} \equiv 0 \quad \text{(Clairaut's identity — always true)}$$

Thus **any** twice-differentiable $\psi(x, y)$ automatically yields a 2D incompressible velocity field. Conversely, for **any** 2D incompressible flow, a stream function can always be constructed by integration.

---

## 2.2 Volumetric Flow Rate Between Two Streamlines

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Theorem:** The change in stream function between two streamlines equals the volumetric flow rate per unit depth crossing any curve joining them.

**Proof.** Consider two streamlines $\psi = \psi_1$ and $\psi = \psi_2$ joined by an arbitrary curve $C$. The volume flow rate per unit depth (outward normal) is:

$$q = \int_C (u \, dy - v \, dx)$$

Substituting $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$:

$$q = \int_C \left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \, dy + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \, dx\right) = \int_C d\psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

This is **path-independent** — a powerful property of the stream function. It also confirms that **no flow crosses a streamline**.

---

## 2.3 Stream Function for Elementary 2D Flows

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **four fundamental building blocks** of plane potential flow theory. KTU 2024 Scheme questions (14-mark problems) almost always involve superposing two or more of these.

### (a) Uniform Flow at an Angle

A uniform flow of velocity $U_\infty$ making an angle $\alpha$ with the $x$-axis:

$$u = U_\infty \cos\alpha, \qquad v = U_\infty \sin\alpha$$

Integrating the stream function definition:

$$\psi = U_\infty (y \cos\alpha - x \sin\alpha)$$

For flow **parallel to the $x$-axis** ($\alpha = 0$): $\quad \psi = U_\infty \, y$

For flow **parallel to the $y$-axis** ($\alpha = 90°$): $\quad \psi = -U_\infty \, x$

### (b) Source and Sink (Radial Flow)

A **source** of strength $Q$ (volume per unit time per unit depth) at the origin:

$$v_r = \frac{Q}{2\pi r}, \qquad v_\theta = 0$$

In Cartesian form, $u = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{x}{x^2 + y^2}$, $\; v = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{y}{x^2 + y^2}$.

The stream function:

$$\psi = \frac{Q}{2\pi} \arctan\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \frac{Q}{2\pi} \theta$$

A **sink** corresponds to $Q < 0$. Streamlines are radial lines $\theta = \text{const}$.

### (c) Free Vortex (Irrotational)

Tangential velocity: $\; v_\theta = \dfrac{K}{r}$, where $K$ is the **circulation strength** (positive $K$ → counter-clockwise rotation).

$$\psi = -K \ln r = -\frac{K}{2} \ln(x^2 + y^2)$$

Streamlines are concentric circles $r = \text{const}$.

### (d) Doublet (Source–Sink Pair)

A source and an equal sink brought infinitesimally close, with the product of strength and separation held constant (= moment $\mu$):

$$\psi = -\frac{\mu \, \sin\theta}{r} = -\frac{\mu \, y}{x^2 + y^2}$$

This is the **fundamental singularity used to model flow past a cylinder** when combined with uniform flow.

### (e) Flow Past a Circular Cylinder (Uniform + Doublet)

By **superposition**: $\psi = \psi_{\text{uniform}} + \psi_{\text{doublet}}$

$$\psi = U_\infty \left(r - \frac{R^2}{r}\right) \sin\theta$$

where $R$ is the cylinder radius. Streamline $\psi = 0$ is the cylinder surface $r = R$ plus the $x$-axis — this is the **non-penetrating boundary condition** automatically satisfied.

---

## 2.4 The Vorticity Connection

For a 2D flow, the **z-component of vorticity** is:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

Substituting the stream function derivatives:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) = -\left(\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

Therefore, the **stream function satisfies Poisson's equation**:

$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$$

For **irrotational flow** ($\omega_z = 0$):

$$\nabla^2 \psi = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace's equation)}$$

This single equation is the foundation of **potential flow theory**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master this table — it covers ≥ 80% of numerical and 3-mark KTU questions on stream function.

| # | Flow Type | Stream Function $\psi(x,y)$ | Velocity Magnitude | Streamline Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uniform flow (parallel to $x$) | $U_\infty \, y$ | $U_\infty$ | Horizontal lines |
| 2 | Uniform flow at angle $\alpha$ | $U_\infty (y\cos\alpha - x\sin\alpha)$ | $U_\infty$ | Parallel lines |
| 3 | Source / Sink (strength $\pm Q$) | $\dfrac{Q}{2\pi}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right)$ | $\dfrac{\lvert Q \rvert}{2\pi r}$ | Radial straight lines |
| 4 | Free vortex (strength $K$) | $-K \ln r$ | $\dfrac{\lvert K \rvert}{r}$ | Concentric circles |
| 5 | Doublet (moment $\mu$) | $-\dfrac{\mu \, y}{x^2 + y^2}$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{r^2}$ | Circles tangent to $x$-axis |
| 6 | Cylinder (radius $R$) in uniform flow | $U_\infty \!\left(r - \dfrac{R^2}{r}\right)\!\sin\theta$ | varies | $r=R$ is a streamline |
| 7 | Rotating cylinder (Rankine) | $\dfrac{1}{2}\omega R^2 \!\left(1 - \dfrac{R^2}{r^2}\right)$ | $\dfrac{\omega R^2}{r}$ | Logarithmic spirals |

**Key identities (memorize):**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

$$\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z \quad \text{(Poisson's equation)}$$

$$q_{\text{per unit depth}} = \Delta \psi \quad \text{(between two streamlines)}$$

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \psi \times \hat{k} \quad \text{(vector identity — velocity is normal to $\nabla\psi$)}$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The stream function is not just an academic construct — it is used in:

- **Aerodynamics** — Flow visualization around airfoils, lift estimation via Kutta–Joukowski theorem.
- **Hydrogeology** — Groundwater flow nets (flow lines $\equiv$ streamlines, equipotentials $\equiv \phi = $ const).
- **Meteorology / Oceanography** — Stream function plots of atmospheric circulation and ocean currents.
- **Civil engineering** — Seepage analysis under dams; flow nets in earth dams.
- **Turbomachinery** — Cascade flow analysis in blade passages.

> [!TIP]
> **The flow net** (used to estimate seepage beneath a dam) is constructed by drawing a family of streamlines and a perpendicular family of equipotentials — a direct engineering application of stream function theory.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Weightage Note (KTU 2024):** Direct derivations of stream functions for elementary flows carry **3–7 marks** each. Numerical problems involving superposition (e.g., source + uniform flow, vortex + uniform flow) carry **7–14 marks**. The following derivations are written in full, line-by-line detail.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Uniform Flow Parallel to the $x$-axis

**Given:** $u = U_\infty = $ constant, $v = 0$.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition.**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \implies \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = U_\infty$$

**Step 2 — Integrate with respect to $y$.**

$$\psi = U_\infty \, y + f(x)$$

where $f(x)$ is an arbitrary "constant of integration" depending only on $x$.

**Step 3 — Apply the second condition, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.**

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -f'(x) = 0 \implies f'(x) = 0 \implies f(x) = C$$

**Step 4 — Choose the constant $C = 0$** (the reference level for $\psi$ is arbitrary).

$$\boxed{\psi(x, y) = U_\infty \, y}$$

**Verification — streamlines:** $\psi = $ const $\Rightarrow y = $ const, i.e., **horizontal lines** — exactly the streamlines expected for uniform flow along the $x$-axis. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Source Flow from the Origin

**Given:** Radial velocity $v_r = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi r}$, with no swirl ($v_\theta = 0$).

**Step 1 — Convert to Cartesian components.**

$$u = v_r \cos\theta = \frac{Q}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{x}{r} = \frac{Q \, x}{2\pi (x^2 + y^2)}$$

$$v = v_r \sin\theta = \frac{Q}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{y}{r} = \frac{Q \, y}{2\pi (x^2 + y^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Use the stream function definition $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$.**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = \frac{Q \, x}{2\pi (x^2 + y^2)}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate with respect to $y$.** Treat $x$ as a constant during integration. Recall $\int \dfrac{dy}{x^2+y^2} = \dfrac{1}{x}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right)$:

$$\psi = \frac{Q}{2\pi} \arctan\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) + g(x)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the second condition $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.**

Differentiate $\psi$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = \frac{Q}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{1+(y/x)^2} \cdot \left(-\frac{y}{x^2}\right) + g'(x) = -\frac{Q \, y}{2\pi (x^2 + y^2)} + g'(x)$$

Imposing $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x = \dfrac{Q \, y}{2\pi (x^2 + y^2)}$:

$$\frac{Q \, y}{2\pi (x^2 + y^2)} - g'(x) = \frac{Q \, y}{2\pi (x^2 + y^2)} \implies g'(x) = 0 \implies g(x) = C$$

**Step 5 — Set $C = 0$ and write the final result:**

$$\boxed{\psi(r, \theta) = \frac{Q}{2\pi} \, \theta \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \psi = \frac{Q}{2\pi} \arctan\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}$$

**Verification — streamlines:** $\psi = $ const $\Rightarrow \theta = $ const $\Rightarrow$ **radial lines** from the origin. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Free Vortex (Irrotational)

**Given:** Tangential velocity $v_\theta = \dfrac{K}{r}$ (with $K$ the circulation constant).

**Step 1 — Write Cartesian velocity components.**

$$u = -v_\theta \sin\theta = -\frac{K}{r} \cdot \frac{y}{r} = -\frac{K \, y}{x^2 + y^2}$$

$$v = v_\theta \cos\theta = \frac{K}{r} \cdot \frac{x}{r} = \frac{K \, x}{x^2 + y^2}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$.**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = -\frac{K \, y}{x^2 + y^2}$$

Integrate w.r.t. $y$: $\int \dfrac{y \, dy}{x^2+y^2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+y^2)$:

$$\psi = -\frac{K}{2} \ln(x^2 + y^2) + h(x)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $x$ and impose $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -\frac{K}{2} \cdot \frac{2x}{x^2+y^2} + h'(x) = -\frac{K \, x}{x^2+y^2} + h'(x)$$

Setting $-\partial\psi/\partial x = v$:

$$\frac{K \, x}{x^2+y^2} - h'(x) = \frac{K \, x}{x^2+y^2} \implies h'(x) = 0 \implies h(x) = C$$

**Step 4 — Set $C = 0$ and express in polar form** using $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$:

$$\boxed{\psi(r, \theta) = -K \ln r}$$

**Streamlines:** $\psi = $ const $\Rightarrow r = $ const $\Rightarrow$ **concentric circles**. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stream Function for a Doublet

A doublet is formed by placing a source of strength $Q$ at $(0, +\epsilon)$ and a sink of strength $-Q$ at $(0, -\epsilon)$, then letting $\epsilon \to 0$ with the **moment** $\mu = 2Q\epsilon$ held constant.

**Step 1 — Write the combined stream function before the limit.**

$$\psi = \frac{Q}{2\pi} \arctan\!\left(\frac{y-\epsilon}{x}\right) - \frac{Q}{2\pi} \arctan\!\left(\frac{y+\epsilon}{x}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Use the identity** $\arctan A - \arctan B = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{A-B}{1+AB}\right)$ and Taylor-expand for small $\epsilon$.

The numerator simplifies to $\dfrac{-2\epsilon x}{x^2 + y^2}$ to leading order; the denominator to $1$ to leading order. So:

$$\psi \approx \frac{Q}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{-2\epsilon x}{x^2 + y^2} = -\frac{\mu}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{x}{x^2 + y^2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to polar coordinates** ($x = r\cos\theta$):

$$\boxed{\psi(r, \theta) = -\frac{\mu}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\cos\theta}{r}}$$

This is the canonical form used in the **superposition with uniform flow** to produce flow past a cylinder:

$$\psi_{\text{cylinder}} = U_\infty \, r \sin\theta - \frac{U_\infty R^2 \sin\theta}{r} = U_\infty \left(r - \frac{R^2}{r}\right) \sin\theta$$

The doublet strength $\mu = 2\pi U_\infty R^2$ ensures that $r = R$ is a streamline (no-penetration boundary).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Stokes' Stream Function (Axisymmetric 3D Flow) — Brief

For an **axisymmetric flow** in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ with swirl $v_\theta = 0$ (a meridional flow), the continuity equation is:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

This is satisfied identically by the **Stokes' stream function** $\Psi(r, z)$ defined by:

$$v_r = -\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial z}, \qquad v_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial r}$$

The volumetric flow rate through an axisymmetric surface bounded by a curve at $r = $ const equals $2\pi \Delta \Psi$ — the **2D analog of the planar result** $\Delta \psi$ per unit depth, scaled by the circumference $2\pi r$.

> [!TIP]
> Stokes' stream function is the 3D axisymmetric analog of the planar $\psi$ and is **highly probable** as a 7-mark question in KTU 2024 ESE.

---

## 3.6 Verification Table — All Elementary Flows

| Flow | $\partial \psi / \partial y$ gives $u$? | $-\partial \psi / \partial x$ gives $v$? | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Source | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (away from origin) |
| Free vortex | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (away from origin) |
| Doublet | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (away from origin) |
| Cylinder superposition | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (away from $r = R$) |

All elementary potential flows satisfy **Laplace's equation** in the flow domain — a hallmark of incompressible, irrotational flow.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — From Continuity to Stream Function

The following Mermaid diagram shows the logical flow of how the stream function arises from the continuity equation and connects to the velocity field, streamlines, and physical quantities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuity Equation in 2D] --> B[du/dx + dv/dy = 0]
    B --> C{Is flow 2D and incompressible?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Define stream function psi]
    D --> E1[u = partial psi / partial y]
    D --> E2[v = - partial psi / partial x]
    E1 --> F[Compute velocity field]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[psi = constant gives streamlines]
    F --> H[Delta psi = volumetric flow per unit depth]
    F --> I[Laplacian of psi = -vorticity]
    I --> I1[Irrotational: Laplacian psi = 0]
    I --> I2[Rotational: Poisson equation]
    G --> J[Plot flow visualization]
    H --> K[Engineering applications]
    I1 --> L[Superposition of elementary flows]
    L --> L1[Uniform]
    L --> L2[Source]
    L --> L3[Sink]
    L --> L4[Vortex]
    L --> L5[Doublet]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Streamlines of Elementary Flows

The diagram below maps the geometric pattern of streamlines produced by each elementary 2D flow, since streamlines are not representable by Mermaid in their true curve form, the block diagram conveys their geometric classification.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph E1[Uniform Flow]
        U1[Velocity U infinity along x]
        U2[Streamlines: horizontal lines]
        U3[psi = U infinity times y]
    end
    subgraph E2[Source Flow]
        S1[Strength Q positive at origin]
        S2[Streamlines: radial lines outward]
        S3[psi = Q theta by 2 pi]
    end
    subgraph E3[Free Vortex]
        V1[Strength K positive counter clockwise]
        V2[Streamlines: concentric circles]
        V3[psi = -K ln r]
    end
    subgraph E4[Doublet]
        D1[Moment mu at origin]
        D2[Streamlines: circles tangent to x axis]
        D3[psi = -mu sin theta by r]
    end
    E1 --> R[Superposition]
    E2 --> R
    E3 --> R
    E4 --> R
    R --> F1[Uniform plus Doublet equals Flow past Cylinder]
    R --> F2[Source plus Uniform equals Half Body]
    R --> F3[Source plus Sink plus Uniform equals Rankine Oval]
    R --> F4[Vortex plus Doublet plus Uniform equals Flow with Circulation]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Elementary Flow to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem type identified] --> Q{Geometry}
    Q -- Straight channel --> R1[Uniform flow psi equals U y]
    Q -- Point inflow outflow --> R2[Source or Sink psi equals Q theta by 2 pi]
    Q -- Circular motion --> R3[Free vortex psi equals -K ln r]
    Q -- Object in uniform flow --> R4[Use Doublet plus Uniform]
    Q -- Object with circulation --> R5[Use Doublet plus Vortex plus Uniform]
    R2 --> S1{Superpose with}
    R4 --> S2{Apply boundary}
    R5 --> S3{Apply Kutta condition}
    S1 -- Yes --> T1[Rankine half body or oval]
    S2 -- No penetration --> T2[Flow past cylinder]
    S3 -- Sharper edge --> T3[Flow past cylinder with lift]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[grad psi in x i plus grad psi in y j]

    D --> F[Minus grad psi by x in j direction]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define stream function. Show that the continuity equation for a 2D incompressible flow is identically satisfied by the stream function.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The stream function $\psi(x, y)$ for a 2D incompressible flow is defined by:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

Substituting in the 2D continuity equation $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y\,\partial x} = 0$$

This is identically true (Clairaut's theorem). **Hence continuity is automatically satisfied.** *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** What is the physical significance of the stream function? Prove that the difference in the values of the stream function between two streamlines equals the volumetric flow rate per unit depth between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The stream function $\psi$ has the following physical significance:

1. Lines of constant $\psi$ are **streamlines** of the flow.
2. The change in $\psi$ between two streamlines represents the **volumetric flow rate per unit depth** between them.

**Proof:** The flow rate per unit depth across an arbitrary curve $C$ joining two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ is:

$$q = \int_C (u\,dy - v\,dx) = \int_C \left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}dx\right) = \int_C d\psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

Hence the result. *(1 mark for significance, 2 marks for proof.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice (either/or). Sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The model answers below are written in the exact step-by-step format expected by KTU examiners.

---

### Question A — Choice 1

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Set A]`**

**(a)** Derive the stream function for **(i)** a uniform flow of velocity $U_\infty$ parallel to the $x$-axis, and **(ii)** a source of strength $Q$ at the origin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flow field is described by $\psi = 3x^2 y - y^3$. Determine: **(i)** the velocity components, **(ii)** whether the flow is rotational or irrotational, and **(iii)** the stream function value of the streamline passing through the point $(1, 2)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Uniform flow parallel to $x$-axis** (3 marks)

Given $u = U_\infty$, $v = 0$. From the definition:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = U_\infty \implies \psi = U_\infty \, y + f(x)$$

Then:

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -f'(x) = 0 \implies f(x) = C$$

Taking $C = 0$:

$$\boxed{\psi = U_\infty \, y}$$

Streamlines are horizontal lines $y = $ const, as expected.

**(ii) Source flow** (4 marks)

Radial velocity: $v_r = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi r}$, so:

$$u = \frac{Q \, x}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)}, \qquad v = \frac{Q \, y}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)}$$

Integrate $\partial\psi/\partial y = u$:

$$\psi = \int \frac{Q \, x}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)} dy = \frac{Q}{2\pi}\arctan\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) + g(x)$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $x$ and impose $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$:

$$\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x} = -\frac{Q \, y}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)} + g'(x) = -v \implies g'(x) = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\psi = \frac{Q}{2\pi}\arctan\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \frac{Q}{2\pi}\theta}$$

Streamlines are radial lines $\theta = $ const.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

Given: $\psi = 3x^2 y - y^3$.

**(i) Velocity components** (3 marks)

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x^2 - 3y^2$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -6xy$$

**(ii) Rotationality** (2 marks)

The $z$-component of vorticity:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -6y - (-6y) = 0$$

Hence the flow is **irrotational**.

(Alternatively, $\nabla^2 \psi = \partial^2\psi/\partial x^2 + \partial^2\psi/\partial y^2 = 6y - 6y = 0$, confirming irrotationality.)

**(iii) Stream function value at $(1, 2)$** (2 marks)

$$\psi(1, 2) = 3(1)^2(2) - (2)^3 = 6 - 8 = -2$$

The streamline passing through $(1, 2)$ is $\psi = -2$.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Set B]`**

**(a)** With neat mathematical steps, derive the stream function for a **free vortex** of strength $K$ at the origin. Show that the streamlines are concentric circles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The velocity field in a 2D incompressible flow is given by $u = 4xy$ and $v = 2x^2 - 2y^2$. Determine the stream function $\psi(x, y)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Given:** Tangential velocity $v_\theta = \dfrac{K}{r}$ (irrotational free vortex).

**Step 1:** Cartesian components:

$$u = -\frac{K \, y}{x^2+y^2}, \qquad v = \frac{K \, x}{x^2+y^2}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = -\frac{K \, y}{x^2+y^2} \implies \psi = -\frac{K}{2}\ln(x^2+y^2) + h(x)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate w.r.t. $x$:

$$\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x} = -\frac{K \, x}{x^2+y^2} + h'(x)$$

Set $-\partial\psi/\partial x = v = \dfrac{Kx}{x^2+y^2}$:

$$\frac{Kx}{x^2+y^2} - h'(x) = \frac{Kx}{x^2+y^2} \implies h'(x) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\psi = -K\ln r}$$

**Step 5 — Streamlines:** $\psi = $ const $\implies r = $ const. In Cartesian form, $x^2 + y^2 = $ const, which is the equation of a **circle centred at the origin**. Hence the streamlines are **concentric circles**. ✓ *(1 mark for derivation, 1 mark for streamline conclusion.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $u = 4xy$, $v = 2x^2 - 2y^2$.

**Step 1 — Verify continuity:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 4y + (-4y) = 0 \quad \text{✓ (incompressible)}$$

**Step 2 — Use $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$:**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 4xy \implies \psi = \int 4xy\,dy = 2xy^2 + f(x)$$

**Step 3 — Use $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ to determine $f(x)$:**

$$\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x} = 2y^2 + f'(x)$$

Setting $-\partial\psi/\partial x = v = 2x^2 - 2y^2$:

$$-(2y^2 + f'(x)) = 2x^2 - 2y^2 \implies -2y^2 - f'(x) = 2x^2 - 2y^2 \implies f'(x) = -2x^2$$

**Step 4 — Integrate $f'(x) = -2x^2$:**

$$f(x) = -\frac{2x^3}{3} + C$$

Taking $C = 0$:

$$\boxed{\psi(x, y) = 2xy^2 - \frac{2x^3}{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify** (optional, 1 mark):

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 4xy \quad \text{✓}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x} = -(2y^2 - 2x^2) = 2x^2 - 2y^2 \quad \text{✓}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in $v$:** Students frequently write $v = +\partial\psi/\partial x$ instead of $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. This single sign error **propagates through the entire problem** and can cost **3–5 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the integration constant $f(x)$:** When integrating $\partial\psi/\partial y$, you MUST add an arbitrary function of the *other* variable. Writing $\psi = U_\infty y$ without acknowledging $f(x) = 0$ loses **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong polar–Cartesian conversion:** A common error is writing $u = v_r \sin\theta$ instead of $u = v_r \cos\theta$. Remember: $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$, so $u$ is the projection on the $x$-axis (cosine component).
>
> 4. **Stating streamlines are streamlines without proof:** When asked "show that streamlines are concentric circles," examiners expect the algebraic step $\psi = $ const $\Rightarrow r = $ const $\Rightarrow x^2 + y^2 = $ const. A bare statement loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing $\psi$ with $\phi$:** Mixing up stream function with velocity potential. Always remember — $\psi$ is for streamlines (tangent to $\vec V$); $\phi$ is for equipotentials (perpendicular to $\vec V$).
>
> 6. **Forgetting to verify continuity:** In a derivation problem, write one line confirming $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$ to **secure a free 1 mark** that examiners often give.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** $\psi$ is a scalar such that $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.
- [x] **Existence:** Guaranteed for 2D, incompressible flow (continuity auto-satisfied).
- [x] **Streamlines:** $\psi(x, y) = $ constant are the streamlines of the flow.
- [x] **Flow rate:** $\Delta\psi$ between two streamlines = volumetric flow rate per unit depth.
- [x] **Poisson's equation:** $\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$. For irrotational flow, $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ (Laplace).
- [x] **Uniform flow** (parallel to $x$): $\psi = U_\infty y$.
- [x] **Source/Sink** (strength $Q$): $\psi = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi}\theta$ — radial streamlines.
- [x] **Free vortex** (strength $K$): $\psi = -K \ln r$ — circular streamlines.
- [x] **Doublet** (moment $\mu$): $\psi = -\dfrac{\mu \sin\theta}{r}$ — circles tangent to $x$-axis.
- [x] **Flow past cylinder** (radius $R$): $\psi = U_\infty \left(r - \dfrac{R^2}{r}\right)\sin\theta$.
- [x] **Stokes' stream function** (3D axisymmetric): $v_r = -\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial \Psi}{\partial z}$, $v_z = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial \Psi}{\partial r}$.
- [x] **Superposition principle:** $\psi_{\text{total}} = \psi_1 + \psi_2 + \psi_3 + \cdots$ for linear combinations of elementary flows.
- [x] **Mutual perpendicularity:** For irrotational flow, streamlines ($\psi$ = const) and equipotentials ($\phi$ = const) intersect at right angles.
- [x] **Vector identity:** $\vec V = \nabla\psi \times \hat{k}$ — velocity is normal to $\nabla\psi$ and lies in the $xy$-plane.
- [x] **Volume flow through 3D axisymmetric surface:** $Q = 2\pi \Delta \Psi$ (Stokes' analog of $\Delta \psi$).

> **End of Module 2 — Stream Function Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (PEPOT416) Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L3: Apply, L4: Analyze, L5: Evaluate cognitive levels).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circulation and Vorticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circulation and Vorticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Circulation (Γ)

**Circulation** is a scalar integral quantity that measures the *net rotational tendency* of a fluid along a closed curve. Mathematically, it is defined as the line integral of the velocity vector $\vec{V}$ taken around a closed contour $C$:

$$\Gamma = \oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

where $d\vec{l}$ is an infinitesimal vector tangent to the contour $C$, directed along the path of integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Circulation is the line integral of velocity tangent to a closed curve in a fluid flow field. The sign of $\Gamma$ follows the **Right-Hand Rule** (positive when the rotation is counter-clockwise).

## 1.2 Vorticity (ω⃗)

**Vorticity** is a *vector* quantity that describes the *local* (point-wise) rotational motion of a fluid element. It is mathematically defined as the **curl of the velocity vector field**:

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Irrotational Flow Condition:** A flow is said to be *irrotational* if the vorticity vector is identically zero at every point in the flow field, i.e., $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$. This does NOT mean the fluid is at rest — it means fluid elements are translating without spinning.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Circulation** | Walking around a roundabout in a circular path | "How much *flow* is following the closed loop?" (Global property) |
| **Vorticity** | Stirring tea with a spoon — watch a leaf spin | "Is a tiny fluid *element* spinning in place?" (Local property) |
| **Irrotational flow** | A perfectly slippery river current | Fluid moves in curves, but each drop is NOT rotating on its own axis |
| **Rotational flow** | Honey on a rotating turntable | Each fluid element rotates about its own axis *like a rigid body* |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** Circulation is a *macro* quantity (around a contour), Vorticity is a *micro* quantity (at a point). Stokes' theorem is the mathematical bridge that links them.

## 1.4 Physical Significance in Engineering

- **Aerospace:** Circulation around an airfoil directly determines **lift** via the Kutta-Joukowski theorem: $L' = \rho V_\infty \Gamma$.
- **Turbomachinery:** Vorticity governs the design of impellers, turbine blades, and pumps.
- **Meteorology:** Atmospheric vorticity drives cyclones, tornadoes, and ocean eddies.
- **Hydraulics:** Free vortex analysis is critical in designing spillways, vortex chambers, and pipe bends.

---

## 1.5 Velocity Components in Cylindrical Coordinates

For a 2-D flow in the $r$-$\theta$ plane, the velocity components are:

$$v_r = \frac{dr}{dt}, \quad v_\theta = r\frac{d\theta}{dt}, \quad v_z = 0$$

The element of arc length along a streamline is:

$$dl^2 = dr^2 + r^2 d\theta^2 + dz^2$$

---

## 1.6 Stream Function and Velocity Potential (Recap from Module 1)

- **Stream Function ($\psi$)** exists for 2-D incompressible flow: $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$, $v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$
- **Velocity Potential ($\phi$)** exists if flow is irrotational: $u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$, $v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$
- **Combined:** Both $\psi$ and $\phi$ exist *iff* the flow is 2-D, incompressible, AND irrotational (Laplace's equation governs both).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of Circulation

For a 2-D flow with velocity components $u$ in $x$-direction and $v$ in $y$-direction, the line integral around a closed curve $C$ is:

$$\Gamma = \oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \oint_{C} (u \, dx + v \, dy)$$

### Step-by-step logic:
1. **Resolve velocity** along $x$ and $y$ axes.
2. **Identify path direction** — counter-clockwise gives positive $\Gamma$.
3. **Integrate tangentially** along the closed contour.
4. **Sum contributions** from all four segments of the closed loop.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Vorticity

In Cartesian coordinates (3-D):

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ u & v & w \end{vmatrix}$$

This yields three components:

$$\omega_x = \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}, \quad \omega_y = \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}, \quad \omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

### For a 2-D flow in the $x$-$y$ plane (most common KTU case):

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Irrotationality Condition (2-D):** Flow is irrotational $\iff$ $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$. This is precisely the Cauchy-Riemann condition linking $\psi$ and $\phi$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Circulation | $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Scalar, signed by RHR |
| 2 | Vorticity (vector) | $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ | $1/\text{s}$ | Doubles angular velocity |
| 3 | 2-D Vorticity (z-comp.) | $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | $1/\text{s}$ | Most-used KTU form |
| 4 | Stokes' Theorem | $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{l} = \iint_A \vec{\omega}\cdot \hat{n}\, dA$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Links circulation & vorticity |
| 5 | Free vortex velocity | $v_\theta = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ | $C = \Gamma/(2\pi)$ |
| 6 | Free vortex pressure | $p = p_0 - \dfrac{\rho C^2}{2r^2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | $p$ decreases with $r$ |
| 7 | Forced vortex velocity | $v_\theta = r\omega$ | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) |
| 8 | Forced vortex pressure | $p = p_0 + \dfrac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | $p$ increases with $r^2$ |
| 9 | Combined vortex core | $v_\theta = r\omega$ for $r \leq a$ | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ | Forced inside |
| 10 | Combined vortex outer | $v_\theta = \dfrac{a^2 \omega}{r}$ for $r \geq a$ | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ | Free outside |
| 11 | Angular velocity of element | $\omega_{\text{element}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\vert \nabla \times \vec{V} \vert$ | rad/s | Half the vorticity magnitude |
| 12 | Stream function rate | $d\psi = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}dy$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Used in circ. calculation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** The absolute value symbol `\vert` is preferred. In tables and exam scripts, NEVER confuse $|\Gamma|$ (magnitude) with $\Gamma$ (signed scalar). KTU examiners *deduct 0.5 marks* for this.

---

## 2.4 Classification of Vortices

| Property | Free Vortex | Forced Vortex |
|---|---|---|
| **Vorticity** | Zero (irrotational) | Non-zero (rotational, $2\omega$) |
| **Velocity profile** | $v_\theta \propto 1/r$ (hyperbolic) | $v_\theta \propto r$ (linear) |
| **Pressure profile** | Decreases outward (vacuum possible at center) | Increases outward (rigid-body rotation) |
| **Examples** | Whirlpool, drain vortex, tornado outer region | Rotating cup of tea, centrifuge |
| **Streamlines** | Concentric circles | Concentric circles |
| **Energy** | Decreases with $r$ | Constant (kinetic energy per unit mass) |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Free vortex flow** models flow through *turbine runners* (Francis turbines) and *vortex chambers* in cyclone separators.
- **Forced vortex flow** describes fluid in a *centrifugal pump impeller* and *rotating cylindrical tanks* (used in dairy and chemical mixers).
- **Combined vortex** is a hybrid model used for *tornadoes, hurricanes, and draining bathtubs* (forced core + free periphery).
- **Circulation around an airfoil** (Kutta-Joukowski theorem) is the foundation of fixed-wing aircraft lift generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Vorticity in 2-D Cartesian Flow

**Given:** Velocity field $\vec{V} = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j}$ in 2-D (i.e., $w = 0$ and $\partial/\partial z = 0$).

**Step 1 — Write the curl operator determinant:**

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\[2pt] \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \\[2pt] u & v & w \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the determinant along the first row:**

$$\vec{\omega} = \hat{i}\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}\right) - \hat{j}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right) + \hat{k}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply 2-D assumptions** ($w = 0$, $\partial/\partial z = 0$):

$$\omega_x = 0 - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0$$

$$\omega_y = -\left(0 - 0\right) = 0$$

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\;\vec{\omega} = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)\hat{k}\;}$$

This is the **vorticity vector pointing out of the $x$-$y$ plane**, fully derived.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stokes' Theorem (Circulation = Vorticity × Area)

**Stokes' Theorem** states:

$$\oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \iint_{A} (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

**Proof outline (per KTU expected level):**

**Step 1 — Begin with Green's Theorem (2-D form of Stokes):**

$$\oint_{C} (P\,dx + Q\,dy) = \iint_{A} \left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial y}\right) dA$$

**Step 2 — Identify** $P = u(x,y)$ and $Q = v(x,y)$, the velocity components:

$$\oint_{C} (u\,dx + v\,dy) = \iint_{A} \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right) dA$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the LHS as Circulation $\Gamma$:**

$$\oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \Gamma$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the integrand on the RHS as Vorticity $\omega_z$:**

$$\iint_{A} \omega_z \, dA = \iint_{A} (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot \hat{k} \, dA$$

**Step 5 — Combine into Stokes' Theorem:**

$$\boxed{\;\Gamma = \iint_{A} \vec{\omega} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special case — Uniform Vorticity:** If $\omega$ is uniform over area $A$, then $\Gamma = \omega \cdot A$, or equivalently $\omega = \Gamma / A$. This is the most-tested KTU sub-case.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Free Vortex Flow Equations

**Assumptions:** Steady, incompressible, axisymmetric, irrotational, swirl-only flow ($v_r = 0$, $v_z = 0$, $v_\theta \neq 0$).

**Step 1 — Velocity potential exists** (irrotational flow), so:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Laplace's equation in cylindrical coordinates** (axisymmetric, no $v_r$, no $v_z$):

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial \theta^2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — For pure swirl** ($\phi = \phi(\theta)$ only), the radial term vanishes:

$$\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d^2 \phi}{d\theta^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = A\theta + B$$

**Step 4 — Compute velocity:**

$$v_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{d\phi}{d\theta} = \frac{A}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Define constant of integration** $C = A$:

$$\boxed{\;v_\theta = \frac{C}{r} = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\;}$$

since total circulation $\Gamma = \oint v_\theta \, dl = v_\theta \cdot 2\pi r = 2\pi C \;\Rightarrow\; C = \Gamma/(2\pi)$.

**Step 6 — Pressure distribution from Euler's equation** (radial equilibrium):

$$\frac{dp}{dr} = \frac{\rho v_\theta^2}{r} = \frac{\rho C^2}{r^3}$$

**Step 7 — Integrate with respect to $r$:**

$$p = -\frac{\rho C^2}{2r^2} + p_0$$
