(where $p_0$ is the pressure as $r \to \infty$)

**Step 8 — Final pressure equation:**

$$\boxed{\;p = p_0 - \frac{\rho C^2}{2r^2}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Pressure goes BELOW $p_0$ as $r$ decreases.** At $r \to 0$, pressure tends to $-\infty$ — physically impossible. Hence, **real free vortices MUST have a finite core radius $a$**, beyond which they become **combined vortices**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Forced Vortex Flow Equations

**Assumption:** Fluid rotates as a **rigid body** with constant angular velocity $\omega$ about a vertical axis.

**Step 1 — Velocity is tangential** with magnitude proportional to $r$:

$$v_\theta = r\omega$$

**Step 2 — Verify vorticity is non-zero (rotational flow):**

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r^2 \omega)}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{r}(2r\omega) = 2\omega$$

**Step 3 — Pressure gradient in radial direction** (centripetal equilibrium):

$$\frac{dp}{dr} = \rho \frac{v_\theta^2}{r} = \rho \frac{(r\omega)^2}{r} = \rho \omega^2 r$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from axis ($r = 0$, $p = p_0$) to radius $r$:**

$$\int_{p_0}^{p} dp = \int_{0}^{r} \rho \omega^2 r \, dr$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$p - p_0 = \rho \omega^2 \cdot \frac{r^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Final pressure distribution:**

$$\boxed{\;p = p_0 + \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Free surface shape** of a forced vortex is a **paraboloid of revolution**: $z = \dfrac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}$ (measured from the vertex). This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Combined (Rankine) Vortex

**Concept:** Real vortices have a **forced (solid-body) core** of radius $a$ surrounded by a **free (irrotational) outer region**.

**Inside the core ($r \leq a$):** Continuity at $r = a$ requires:

$$v_\theta = r\omega \quad \text{(forced)}$$

**Outside the core ($r \geq a$):** $v_\theta$ must equal $\dfrac{C}{r}$ and match the inner value at $r = a$:

$$\left. r\omega \right\vert_{r=a} = \frac{C}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = a^2 \omega$$

**Outer velocity:**

$$v_\theta = \frac{a^2 \omega}{r}$$

**Combined vortex velocity profile:**

$$\boxed{\;v_\theta = \begin{cases} r\omega, & 0 \leq r \leq a \\[4pt] \dfrac{a^2 \omega}{r}, & r \geq a \end{cases}\;}$$

**Total circulation of combined vortex:**

$$\Gamma = 2\pi r \cdot v_\theta = 2\pi (a^2 \omega) \quad \text{(independent of } r\text{)}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Relation between Angular Velocity and Vorticity

For a fluid element undergoing pure rotation:

$$\omega_{\text{angular}} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \quad \text{(in 2-D)}$$

The **angular velocity of the fluid element** (rigid-body spin rate) is exactly **half** the vorticity:

$$\Omega = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{\omega_z}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** If a fluid element rotates at angular velocity $\Omega$, its vorticity is $\omega = 2\Omega$. Don't confuse *angular velocity of element* ($\Omega$) with *angular velocity of flow pattern* ($\omega$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: Circulation vs Vorticity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Flow Field] --> B[Velocity Vector V]
    B --> C1[Global Property - Circulation]
    B --> C2[Local Property - Vorticity]
    C1 --> D1["Line Integral around closed contour C"]
    C1 --> D2["Formula: Gamma = oint V dot dl"]
    C2 --> E1["Curl of velocity vector"]
    C2 --> E2["Formula: omega = curl V"]
    D1 --> F["Stokes Theorem - Bridge"]
    E1 --> F
    F --> G["Gamma = double integral omega dot n dA"]
    C2 --> H{Flow Type}
    H -->|omega = 0| I[Irrotational - Free Vortex]
    H -->|omega ≠ 0| J[Rotational - Forced Vortex]
    I --> K[Real - Combined Rankine Vortex]
    J --> K
```

---

## 4.2 Vortex Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph VortexTypes [VORTEX TYPES]
        direction TB
        nodeA[Free Vortex]
        nodeB[Forced Vortex]
        nodeC[Combined Rankine Vortex]
    end
    nodeA --> propA1["v_theta = C / r"]
    nodeA --> propA2["Irrotational omega = 0"]
    nodeA --> propA3["Pressure drops with 1/r squared"]
    nodeA --> exA["Examples: Whirlpool, Drain, Tornado outer"]
    nodeB --> propB1["v_theta = r times omega"]
    nodeB --> propB2["Rotational omega equals 2 omega"]
    nodeB --> propB3["Pressure rises with r squared"]
    nodeB --> exB["Examples: Centrifuge, Impeller, Rotating tank"]
    nodeC --> propC1["Forced inside radius a"]
    nodeC --> propC2["Free outside radius a"]
    nodeC --> propC3["Continuous at r equals a"]
    nodeC --> exC["Examples: Real Tornado, Hurricane"]
```

---

## 4.3 Flow Diagram: Stokes' Theorem Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Given closed contour C enclosing area A] --> step2[Compute line integral of V dot dl along C]
    step1 --> step3[Compute curl of V over area A]
    step2 --> step4[Result equals Circulation Gamma]
    step3 --> step5[Result equals Vorticity flux through A]
    step4 --> step6[Stokes Theorem: Both are equal]
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7[Use whichever is easier to compute]
```

---

## 4.4 Forced Vortex Free-Surface Profile (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    axis[Vertical axis of rotation] --> point0[Vertex at center r equals 0]
    point0 --> surfaceA[Paraboloid surface]
    surfaceA --> rimA[Rim at radius R]
    surfaceA --> eqnA[Equation: z equals omega squared r squared divided by 2g]
    axis --> pressure[Pressure distribution]
    pressure --> pEqn[p equals p0 plus rho omega squared r squared divided by 2]
    rimA --> hMax[Maximum height h equals omega squared R squared divided by 2g]
```

---

## 4.5 Combined Vortex Velocity Profile (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    origin[Center r equals 0] --> coreRegion[Forced Core 0 to a]
    coreRegion --> coreVel["v equals omega r linear"]
    coreRegion --> interface[Interface at r equals a]
    interface --> outerRegion[Free Region a to infinity]
    outerRegion --> outerVel["v equals a squared omega divided by r hyperbolic"]
    coreVel --> peakVel[Peak velocity at r equals a equals a omega]
    outerVel --> peakVel
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define circulation and vorticity. State the mathematical relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

> **Circulation ($\Gamma$):** It is the line integral of the velocity vector taken around a closed contour in the flow field:
> $$\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} \quad \text{(units: m}^2\text{/s)}$$
>
> **Vorticity ($\vec{\omega}$):** It is the curl of the velocity vector field, representing the local rotation of a fluid element:
> $$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} \quad \text{(units: 1/s)}$$
>
> **Relationship (Stokes' Theorem):**
> $$\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \iint_A (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = \iint_A \vec{\omega} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

Distinguish between a **free vortex** and a **forced vortex** in three points.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Free Vortex | Forced Vortex |
|---|---|---|
| **Vorticity** | Zero (irrotational) | Non-zero (rotational, $\omega_z = 2\Omega$) |
| **Velocity** | $v_\theta = C/r$ (inversely proportional) | $v_\theta = r\omega$ (directly proportional) |
| **Pressure variation** | Decreases with $r$ (lower at center) | Increases with $r^2$ (higher at periphery) |
| **Example** | Whirlpool, drain vortex | Centrifuge, rotating cup |
| **Energy** | Decreases outward | Constant |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for vorticity in a 2-D incompressible flow field. State the condition for the flow to be irrotational. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A free vortex has a circulation of $8 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Determine the tangential velocity and pressure at a radius of $0.5 \text{ m}$, given that the pressure at a radius of $1.5 \text{ m}$ is $90 \text{ kPa}$. Take fluid density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Write the curl of velocity in 3-D Cartesian form:**

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\[2pt] \partial/\partial x & \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial z \\[2pt] u & v & w \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the determinant:**

$$\vec{\omega} = \hat{i}\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}\right) - \hat{j}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right) + \hat{k}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply 2-D assumptions** ($w = 0$ and $\partial/\partial z = 0$):

$$\omega_x = 0, \quad \omega_y = 0, \quad \omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

**Step 4 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\;\vec{\omega} = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)\hat{k}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Irrotationality Condition:**

The flow is irrotational when $\vec{\omega} = 0$, which in 2-D reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

This is the famous Cauchy-Riemann condition, which when satisfied means the flow possesses a **velocity potential** $\phi$.

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:** $\Gamma = 8 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $r = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $p_1 = 90 \text{ kPa}$ at $r_1 = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute constant $C$:**

$$C = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi} = \frac{8}{2\pi} = \frac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.2732 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute tangential velocity at $r = 0.5 \text{ m}$:**

$$v_\theta = \frac{C}{r} = \frac{1.2732}{0.5} = 2.546 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure equation for free vortex:**

$$p = p_0 - \frac{\rho C^2}{2r^2}$$

Using two radii: $p_1 - p_2 = \dfrac{\rho C^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_2^2} - \dfrac{1}{r_1^2}\right)$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$90{,}000 - p_2 = \frac{1000 \times (1.2732)^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{(0.5)^2} - \frac{1}{(1.5)^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{(1.2732)^2}{2} = \frac{1.6210}{2} = 0.8105$$

$$\frac{1}{0.25} - \frac{1}{2.25} = 4 - 0.4444 = 3.5556$$

$$90{,}000 - p_2 = 1000 \times 0.8105 \times 3.5556 = 2882 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $p_2$:**

$$p_2 = 90{,}000 - 2882 = 87{,}118 \text{ Pa} \approx 87.12 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\;v_\theta = 2.546 \text{ m/s}, \quad p = 87.12 \text{ kPa}\;}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a)** Define circulation. State and explain Stokes' theorem with reference to fluid flow. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A forced vortex is generated in a cylindrical tank of radius $1.2 \text{ m}$ rotating at $60 \text{ rpm}$. Determine: (i) angular velocity, (ii) tangential velocity at the rim, (iii) pressure difference between the rim and the center. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Circulation:**

Circulation $\Gamma$ around a closed curve $C$ in a fluid flow is the line integral of the tangential component of velocity taken around the curve:

$$\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

where $d\vec{l}$ is an elemental length vector tangent to the curve.

**Step 2 — Statement of Stokes' Theorem:**

$$\oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \iint_A (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

In words: *The line integral of velocity around a closed curve equals the surface integral of vorticity over the area enclosed by the curve.*

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation in Fluid Flow:**

- The LHS represents the **net circulation** of fluid around the contour.
- The RHS is the **total vorticity flux** passing through the area.
- It allows us to compute $\Gamma$ from local vorticity values (or vice-versa).
- **Special case:** If $\omega$ is uniform over area $A$, then $\Gamma = \omega \cdot A$.

**Step 4 — Engineering Application:**

Stokes' theorem is the foundation of the **Kutta-Joukowski theorem** for lift on an airfoil:

$$L' = \rho V_\infty \Gamma$$

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $R = 1.2 \text{ m}$, $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \approx 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Tangential velocity at rim:**

$$v_\theta = R\omega = 1.2 \times 2\pi = 2.4\pi \approx 7.540 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Pressure difference between rim and center:**

Using forced vortex pressure equation:

$$p_{\text{rim}} - p_{\text{center}} = \frac{\rho \omega^2 R^2}{2}$$

**Substitute values:**

$$\Delta p = \frac{1000 \times (2\pi)^2 \times (1.2)^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta p = \frac{1000 \times 39.478 \times 1.44}{2}$$

$$\Delta p = \frac{56{,}848.3}{2} = 28{,}424.1 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta p \approx 28.42 \text{ kPa}\;}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Vorticity with Angular Velocity (–1 to –2 marks):** Remember $\omega_{\text{vorticity}} = 2\Omega_{\text{element}}$. Many students write $\Omega = \nabla \times V$ instead of $\omega = 2\Omega = \nabla \times V$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Sign in Circulation (–1 mark):** Direction (clockwise vs counter-clockwise) matters. If the problem says "clockwise," the answer must carry a negative sign.
>
> 3. **Free Vortex Pressure Equation Errors (–2 marks):** Pressure *decreases* with $r$ in a free vortex. Students often invert the sign and write $p = p_0 + \rho C^2/(2r^2)$, which is the *forced* vortex formula — examiners flag this immediately.
>
> 4. **Not Stating "Steady, Incompressible, Inviscid" Assumptions (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Any derivation must begin by listing these. KTU 2024 scheme strictly tests "assumption-list-first" pattern.
>
> 5. **Unit Mismatch (–0.5 mark):** $C$ has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $r$ has units of m, so $C/r$ has units of m/s. Always write units at each step.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Velocity Potential Exists Only for Irrotational Flow (–1 mark):** Don't write $\phi = u dx + v dy$ for a rotational vortex; $\phi$ *does not exist* there.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Circulation & Vorticity**

### **Core Definitions**
- ☐ **Circulation** $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}$ — a **scalar**, signed by right-hand rule, units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- ☐ **Vorticity** $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ — a **vector**, units $\text{1/s}$, twice the angular velocity of a fluid element
- ☐ **Irrotational flow** $\Leftrightarrow$ $\vec{\omega} = 0$ everywhere
- ☐ **Velocity potential** $\phi$ exists **only** when flow is irrotational

### **Key Equations**
- ☐ 2-D Vorticity: $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$
- ☐ Stokes' Theorem: $\Gamma = \iint_A \vec{\omega} \cdot \hat{n}\, dA$
- ☐ Free vortex: $v_\theta = C/r$, $p = p_0 - \rho C^2/(2r^2)$
- ☐ Forced vortex: $v_\theta = r\omega$, $p = p_0 + \rho \omega^2 r^2/2$
- ☐ Combined vortex: forced inside core, free outside

### **Comparison Table — Memorize This**
- ☐ **Free vortex** ↔ Irrotational ↔ $v \propto 1/r$ ↔ $p$ decreases inward
- ☐ **Forced vortex** ↔ Rotational ↔ $v \propto r$ ↔ $p$ increases outward
- ☐ **Combined vortex** ↔ Real-world (tornado, drain) ↔ continuous at $r = a$

### **Engineering Applications — Be Ready to Quote**
- ☐ **Kutta-Joukowski Lift Theorem:** $L' = \rho V_\infty \Gamma$
- ☐ **Cyclone separators** use free-vortex flow principles
- ☐ **Centrifugal pumps** operate on forced-vortex principles
- ☐ **Paraboloid free surface** of forced vortex: $z = \omega^2 r^2 / (2g)$

### **Exam Tips**
- ☐ Always begin derivations with the **assumptions list**
- ☐ Use **mermaid-compatible diagrams** in answer sheets when possible
- ☐ State Stokes' theorem in *both* line-integral and surface-integral forms
- ☐ Watch out for **sign convention** in circulation problems
- ☐ For 14-mark questions, ensure derivations cover *all* intermediate steps — no skipping

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Streamline, Path Line, Streak Line
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Streamline, Path Line, Streak Line

# 🌊 Streamline, Path Line, and Streak Line — A Comprehensive Visual Guide

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are these "Lines" in Fluid Flow?

In fluid mechanics, we cannot visually "see" the motion of air or water the way we see a moving car. To describe and analyze how fluids move, engineers use **three fundamental geometric tools** that act like imaginary "tracer lines" drawn inside the flow field. These are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Lines of Flow Visualization**
> 1. **Streamline** → "What does the flow look like RIGHT NOW?"
> 2. **Path Line** → "Where has a single particle TRAVELED?"
> 3. **Streak Line** → "Where has the DYE traveled from a fixed point?"

### 1.2 Formal Definitions

**🔹 Streamline**
A **streamline** is an imaginary curve drawn through a flowing fluid such that the **velocity vector is everywhere tangent to the curve at that instant of time**. It represents the *instantaneous direction of motion* of every fluid particle lying on it.

**🔹 Path Line**
A **path line** is the actual **trajectory** (route) traced by a single fluid particle as it moves through space over a period of time. It is the "life journey" of one specific particle.

**🔹 Streak Line**
A **streak line** (also called a *filament line*) is the locus of all fluid particles that have **previously passed through a given fixed point** in the flow field. It is generated by continuously injecting a tracer (dye/smoke) at one location and watching the colored streak it produces.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Highway Visualization

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a busy national highway during a rainstorm:**
>
> - **Streamline** = The arrows painted on the road RIGHT NOW showing which direction each car at each spot is heading (a snapshot).
> - **Path Line** = The black skid marks left behind by *one specific car* over its journey from city A to city B.
> - **Streak Line** = The continuous trail of red dye dropped from a stationary truck at the roadside — every car that passes that point afterward adds to the streak.

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Steady Flow:** A flow where properties at any point do not change with time (∂/∂t = 0).
- **Unsteady Flow:** A flow where properties at any point vary with time (∂/∂t ≠ 0).
- In **steady flow**, all three lines **coincide perfectly**. In **unsteady flow**, they are generally **distinct**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Streamline — The Instantaneous "Photo" of the Flow

A streamline is constructed using the **Eulerian approach** (we fix our attention on a point in space, not on a particle). At every point on the streamline, the velocity vector $\vec{V}$ is tangent to the curve $\vec{r}(s)$.

The mathematical condition for a streamline is that the differential displacement vector $d\vec{r}$ along the curve must be parallel to the local velocity vector $\vec{V}$ at that instant:

$$d\vec{r} \times \vec{V} = 0$$

In Cartesian 2D form (where $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j}$ and $d\vec{r} = dx\hat{i} + dy\hat{j}$), this becomes:

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

**Key Properties of Streamlines:**
- Two streamlines **never cross each other** in a continuous flow (if they did, a particle at the intersection would have two simultaneous velocities, which is physically impossible).
- They can be close together (high velocity region) or far apart (low velocity region).
- The family of streamlines forms a **flow net**.

### 2.2 Path Line — The "GPS Tracking" of One Particle

A path line uses the **Lagrangian approach** — we track one specific particle as time advances. The position of the particle is given by:

$$x = x(t), \quad y = y(t), \quad z = z(t)$$

These coordinates are obtained by **integrating the velocity components** with respect to time, since $u = dx/dt$, $v = dy/dt$, $w = dz/dt$.

**Key Properties of Path Lines:**
- A path line is a **single curve** for one chosen particle.
- It tells us the **history** of that particle.
- Different particles starting at the same point at different times trace out **different path lines** in unsteady flow.

### 2.3 Streak Line — The Dye Experiment Geometry

A streak line is defined at a fixed instant $t = t_0$. Imagine we have been injecting colored dye continuously at point $P$ from time $t = -\infty$ up to the present moment $t = t_0$. The colored fluid forms a visible curve — that curve is the streak line.

For each particle that passed through $P$ at an earlier time $\tau$ (where $\tau \le t_0$), its current position is given by:

$$\vec{r}(t_0) = \vec{r}_{particle\ from\ time\ \tau}$$

The streak line is the **union of the current positions of all such particles**.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$x = x(\tau), \quad y = y(\tau), \quad z = z(\tau), \quad \text{with } t = \text{constant}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Eulerian / Lagrangian** | **When all 3 coincide** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Streamline | $dr \times V = 0$ or $\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$ | Eulerian (fixed point, fixed time) | Steady Flow |
| Path Line | $x = x(t),\ y = y(t),\ z = z(t)$ | Lagrangian (one particle, time varies) | Steady Flow |
| Streak Line | $x = x(\tau),\ y = y(\tau),\ z = z(\tau),\ t = \text{const}$ | Mixed (all particles through fixed point) | Steady Flow |
| Continuity | $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | Eulerian (mass conservation) | Applies always |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Engineers visualize airflow over aircraft wings using **streamlines** (computed via CFD) to optimize lift and reduce drag.
- **Hydraulic Engineering:** **Streak lines** from dye injection help track pollution dispersion in rivers.
- **Meteorology:** Weather maps show **streamlines** of wind patterns; **path lines** predict the trajectory of hurricanes.
- **Wind Tunnels:** Smoke is injected to produce **streak lines** that reveal turbulent separation zones.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Streamline Equation

**Given:** A 2D velocity field $\vec{V}(x, y, t) = u(x, y, t)\hat{i} + v(x, y, t)\hat{j}$

**Step 1:** A streamline is a curve whose tangent vector $d\vec{r}$ is parallel to the local velocity $\vec{V}$ at the same instant.

$$\vec{V} = \lambda \cdot d\vec{r}$$

where $\lambda$ is some scalar (positive, indicating same direction).

**Step 2:** Cross product of two parallel vectors is zero:

$$\vec{V} \times d\vec{r} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Expanding in Cartesian 2D:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{V} \times d\vec{r} &= \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ u & v & 0 \\ dx & dy & 0 \end{vmatrix} \\
&= \hat{k}(u\, dy - v\, dx) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Since the $\hat{k}$ component must vanish:

$$u\, dy - v\, dx = 0$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging into the standard streamline slope form:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental stream function slope equation** that KTU examiners love to test. Notice the time $t$ is held constant — this confirms the Eulerian (instantaneous) nature of streamlines.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Path Line Equation

**Given:** Same velocity field as above, but now we follow a single particle.

**Step 1:** By definition, the velocity of a particle is the time derivative of its position:

$$u = \frac{dx}{dt}, \quad v = \frac{dy}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** For a 2D case, eliminate $dt$ to get the trajectory equation:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt} = \frac{v(x, y, t)}{u(x, y, t)}$$

**Step 3:** This is **structurally identical** to the streamline equation in form, BUT here $t$ is the integration variable, not a parameter held constant. The path line is found by:

- **Step 3a:** Integrate $dx/dt = u(x, y, t)$ to get $x(t)$.
- **Step 3b:** Integrate $dy/dt = v(x, y, t)$ to get $y(t)$.
- **Step 3c:** Eliminate $t$ between them to get $y(x)$.

---

### 3.3 Proof: All Three Lines Coincide in Steady Flow

**Condition:** Steady flow implies $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$, which means $u$ and $v$ depend only on $(x, y)$, not on $t$.

**Step 1 — Streamline Equation (in steady flow):**

$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{streamline} = \frac{v(x,y)}{u(x,y)}$$

**Step 2 — Path Line Equation:** Since $u$ and $v$ are independent of $t$, we can write:

$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{path} = \frac{v(x,y)}{u(x,y)}$$

**Step 3 — Streak Line Equation:** Consider a particle that passed through point $P(x_0, y_0)$ at earlier time $\tau$. Its current position satisfies:

$$x = x_0 + \int_{\tau}^{t} u\, dt', \quad y = y_0 + \int_{\tau}^{t} v\, dt'$$

Since $u$ and $v$ are time-independent:

$$dx = u\, dt, \quad dy = v\, dt$$

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v(x,y)}{u(x,y)}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{streamline} = \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{path} = \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{streak}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therefore, in steady flow, all three lines are mathematically identical.** This is the result most frequently tested in KTU Module 2 questions.

---

### 3.4 Counter-Example: Unsteady Flow — Why They Diverge

Consider the simple unsteady velocity field:

$$u = x, \quad v = t$$

**Streamline** (at fixed $t$):

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{t}{x}$$

Integrating at constant $t$:

$$y = t \ln(x) + C_1(t)$$

**Path Line** (single particle tracked in time):
- From $u = x = dx/dt$ → $x = A e^{t}$, where $A$ is initial $x$-position.
- From $v = t = dy/dt$ → $y = t^2/2 + B$.

Eliminating $t$: $t = \ln(x/A)$, so:

$$y = \frac{[\ln(x/A)]^2}{2} + B$$

**Streak Line** (all particles through a fixed point, say origin, observed at time $t_0$):
A particle that passed through origin at time $\tau$ now sits at:
$x(\tau) = A(\tau)e^{t_0}$, where $A(\tau) = $ position at origin at $\tau$. 
This produces yet a **third distinct curve**.

**Result:** The three lines are now geometrically distinct — confirming they diverge in unsteady flow.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Streamline** | **Path Line** | **Streak Line** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Time reference | Single instant | Range of time | Range of past times |
| Number of particles | Infinite (all on line) | One particle | All particles through a point |
| Depends on $t$? | $t$ is parameter (held constant) | $t$ is the integration variable | $\tau$ is variable, $t$ fixed |
| Identical in steady flow? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Crosses another of same type? | Never | Possible | Possible |
| Visualization method | Computed (instant) | Computed (integration) | Dye/Smoke injection |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map of the Three Lines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fluid Flow Visualization Tools"]
    A --> B["Streamline"]
    A --> C["Path Line"]
    A --> D["Streak Line"]

    B --> B1["Eulerian View"]
    B --> B2["Instantaneous Snapshot"]
    B --> B3["Tangent to Velocity"]

    C --> C1["Lagrangian View"]
    C --> C2["Single Particle Track"]
    C --> C3["Integration over Time"]

    D --> D1["Dye Injection Method"]
    D --> D2["All Past Particles"]
    D --> D3["Fixed Reference Point"]

    E["Steady Flow Condition"] -.-> B
    E -.-> C
    E -.-> D

    F["Unsteady Flow"] --> G["All Three Lines Diverge"]
```

### 4.2 Flow Field Visualization — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Particle A at t=0"] -->|moves along| Q1["Path Line of A"]
    P2["Particle B at t=1"] -->|moves along| Q2["Path Line of B"]
    P3["Particle C at t=2"] -->|moves along| Q3["Path Line of C"]

    Q1 --> S1["Streak Line at t=3"]
    Q2 --> S1
    Q3 --> S1

    subgraph SNAP["Instantaneous Snapshot at t=3"]
        direction TB
        SL1["Streamline 1"]
        SL2["Streamline 2"]
        SL3["Streamline 3"]
    end

    S1 -.intersects.-> SNAP
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Identifying the Line Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Given: Flow Visualization Problem"] --> Q1{"Is the flow steady?"}

    Q1 -->|Yes| Answer1["All three lines coincide"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"What is being asked?"}

    Q2 -->|Tangent to velocity at instant| Streamline["It is a STREAMLINE"]
    Q2 -->|Trajectory of one particle over time| Pathline["It is a PATH LINE"]
    Q2 -->|Locus from continuous dye at a point| Streakline["It is a STREAK LINE"]
```

### 4.4 Coordinate-Geometry Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EU["EULERIAN VIEW (Fixed in Space)"]
        EU1["Grid point (x, y, z)"]
        EU2["Velocity V(x,y,z,t)"]
        EU3["Streamline: tangent direction"]
    end

    subgraph LA["LAGRANGIAN VIEW (Fixed on Particle)"]
        LA1["Tagged particle"]
        LA2["Trajectory r(t)"]
        LA3["Path line: history"]
    end

    EU --- LA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "streamline" in fluid flow. State any two of its important properties.*

**Model Answer:**

A streamline is an imaginary curve drawn through a flowing fluid such that the **velocity vector is everywhere tangent to the curve at a given instant of time**. It provides an instantaneous snapshot of the flow direction.

**Two important properties:**
1. **No two streamlines can cross each other** in a continuous flow — otherwise a particle at the intersection would have two simultaneous velocity directions, which is physically impossible.
2. **The tangent to a streamline at any point gives the direction of the velocity vector** at that point at that instant.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a path line and a streak line with a neat sketch.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Path Line** | **Streak Line** |
|:---|:---|
| Trajectory of a **single fluid particle** over a period of time | Locus of **all fluid particles** that have passed through a **fixed point** in the past |
| Lagrangian description | Mixed Eulerian-Lagrangian |
| Found by integrating $u = dx/dt$, $v = dy/dt$ | Found by tracking all particles through point $P$ |

**Sketch:** Draw a curved arrow labeled "Path Line of Particle A" (single curve). Mark a fixed point $P$ and draw a curve of "all particles that passed through $P$" labeled "Streak Line" — visibly distinct from the path line.

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the general differential equation of a streamline in a 2D Cartesian flow field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the velocity field given below, determine whether the flow is steady or unsteady, and find the equation of the streamline passing through point (1, 2) at time $t = 1$ s.

$$u = x + t, \quad v = y$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Given:** 2D Cartesian velocity field $\vec{V} = u(x, y, t)\hat{i} + v(x, y, t)\hat{j}$.

**Step 1:** The streamline is a curve in the $xy$-plane at a fixed instant. Let $d\vec{r} = dx\hat{i} + dy\hat{j}$ be an infinitesimal element along this curve.

**Step 2:** By definition, $d\vec{r}$ is parallel to $\vec{V}$ at every point on the streamline at that instant. Two parallel vectors have zero cross product:

$$\vec{V} \times d\vec{r} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Expanding the 2D cross product:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{V} \times d\vec{r} &= \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ u & v & 0 \\ dx & dy & 0 \end{vmatrix} \\
&= \hat{k}(u\, dy - v\, dx) + \hat{j}(0) + \hat{i}(0) \\
&= \hat{k}(u\, dy - v\, dx) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Setting the $\hat{k}$ component to zero:

$$u\, dy = v\, dx$$

**Step 5:** The general differential equation of a streamline in 2D Cartesian form:

$$\boxed{\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v(x, y, t)}{u(x, y, t)}, \quad t = \text{constant}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify if the flow is steady.
The velocity field is $u = x + t$, $v = y$. Since $u$ explicitly contains $t$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 1 \neq 0$$

> **The flow is UNSTEADY.**

**Step 2:** Form the streamline slope equation.

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{y}{x + t}$$

**Step 3:** At the instant $t = 1$ s, hold $t$ constant:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{y}{x + 1}$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dy}{y} = \frac{dx}{x + 1}$$

**Step 5:** Integrate both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{dy}{y} &= \int \frac{dx}{x + 1} \\
\ln(y) &= \ln(x + 1) + C \\
\ln(y) - \ln(x + 1) &= C \\
\ln\left(\frac{y}{x+1}\right) &= C \\
\frac{y}{x+1} &= e^C = k
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the condition: streamline passes through $(1, 2)$ at $t = 1$ s:

$$\frac{2}{1 + 1} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \implies k = 1$$

**Step 7:** Final equation of the streamline:

$$\boxed{\frac{y}{x+1} = 1 \quad \text{or} \quad y = x + 1 \quad (\text{at } t = 1\ \text{s})}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** Define streamline, path line, and streak line. Explain with a neat diagram how all three lines coincide in a steady flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fluid flow has the velocity components $u = -y/(x^2 + y^2)$ and $v = x/(x^2 + y^2)$. Determine the equation of the streamline and check whether the flow is rotational or irrotational. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Steady Flow Diagram (7 Marks)

**Streamline:** A curve tangent to the velocity vector at every point at a given instant. (Eulerian concept)

**Path Line:** The trajectory traced by a single fluid particle over time. (Lagrangian concept)

**Streak Line:** The locus of all fluid particles that have previously passed through a fixed point. Generated by dye injection.

**Steady Flow Coincidence:**
In steady flow, $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$, so velocity at any point is constant in time. Therefore:
- A particle that was at point P always moves along the same tangent direction.
- The instantaneous direction (streamline) is the same as the actual trajectory (path line).
- All particles that ever passed through P follow the same path — so the streak line is identical too.

**Neat Diagram:**

```
     ↑ y
     │       ╱ streamlines
     │     ╱
     │   ╱
     │ ●P  ← fixed point
     │╱
     ┼────────→ x
```

All three lines (streamline, path line, streak line) overlap perfectly.

---

#### Part (b) — Streamline Equation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Streamline slope equation:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{x/(x^2 + y^2)}{-y/(x^2 + y^2)} = -\frac{x}{y}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange and separate variables:

$$y\, dy = -x\, dx$$

**Step 3:** Integrate:

$$\int y\, dy = -\int x\, dx$$

$$\frac{y^2}{2} = -\frac{x^2}{2} + C_1$$

$$x^2 + y^2 = C$$

**Step 4:** Equation of streamline — these are **concentric circles** centered at origin.

**Step 5:** Check rotationality by computing vorticity $\omega_z$:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{(x^2+y^2)(1) - x(2x)}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = \frac{y^2 - x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{(x^2+y^2)(0) - (-y)(2y)}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = \frac{2y^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2}$$

$$\omega_z = \frac{y^2 - x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2} - \frac{2y^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = \frac{-x^2 - y^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = -\frac{1}{x^2+y^2}$$

Since $\omega_z \neq 0$, **the flow is rotational**.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
>
> - **DO NOT confuse** "rotational" with "having rotation." Even though streamlines are circles (suggesting circular motion), the angular velocity of fluid elements can be zero (irrotational vortex) or non-zero (rotational vortex). Always check via the vorticity formula $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y$.
> - In Part (a), students often forget to mention the **time parameter** condition while defining a streamline. Always state "at a given instant."
> - In stream line derivations, **never** write $dy/dx = v/u$ without specifying $t = \text{const}$ — examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> - When separating variables in the streamline integration, students often forget the **absolute value sign** for $\ln|y|$ — this causes wrong integration constants. Always write $\int dy/y = \ln \vert y\vert + C$.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Streamline / Path Line / Streak Line**

- ✅ A **streamline** is an *instantaneous* Eulerian concept: $dr \times V = 0$, or $dy/dx = v/u$ with $t$ held constant.
- ✅ A **path line** is a *Lagrangian* trajectory: $u = dx/dt$, $v = dy/dt$, integrated over time.
- ✅ A **streak line** is the locus of *all past particles* through a fixed point (dye experiment).
- ✅ In **steady flow**, all three lines **coincide** — this is the most-tested result.
- ✅ In **unsteady flow**, all three lines are **generally different**.
- ✅ **Streamlines never cross** each other; path lines and streak lines may cross.
- ✅ For a **2D streamline**, the differential form is $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$ with $t = \text{const}$.
- ✅ For a **3D streamline**, the differential form is $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ with $t = \text{const}$.
- ✅ The continuity equation $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$ must always be satisfied for a physically real flow.
- ✅ To check if a flow is **steady**, verify $\partial u/\partial t = 0$, $\partial v/\partial t = 0$, $\partial w/\partial t = 0$.
- ✅ **Stream function** $\psi(x,y)$ exists for 2D incompressible flow: $u = \partial \psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$; lines of constant $\psi$ are streamlines.
- ✅ A flow can have **circular streamlines** yet still be **irrotational** (e.g., free vortex) — vorticity ≠ streamline geometry.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stream Tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stream Tube:** A *stream tube* is an imaginary tubular region of fluid bounded entirely by a family of streamlines that pass through a closed curve in the flow field at a given instant of time.

In more rigorous vector-field terms, if we take **any** arbitrary closed curve (not a streamline itself) drawn in a steady flow field, the streamlines passing through every point on this curve sweep out a closed surface in the shape of a tube. This surface is called a **stream tube**.

Since the lateral boundary of this imaginary tube is composed of streamlines, and by definition the velocity vector at every point on a streamline is **tangent** to it, the resultant fluid velocity at the boundary has **no component normal to the wall**. Consequently, **no fluid can enter or leave the stream tube across its lateral surface** — the tube behaves mathematically exactly like a solid, frictionless pipe carrying the fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Stream Tube vs. Streamline vs. Streakline:**
>
> | Concept | Definition | Key Property |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Streamline** | A curve whose tangent at any point is parallel to the instantaneous velocity vector | Tangent to velocity |
> | **Stream Tube** | A tubular surface made up of an infinite family of streamlines passing through a closed curve | Lateral surface is a streamline; mass is conserved inside |
> | **Streakline** | Locus of all fluid particles that have previously passed through a given point | Time-history of particles |

For **steady flow** (the regime where stream tubes are most useful), streamlines, streaklines, and pathlines coincide.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Garden Hose in a River" Analogy**
> Imagine you are standing on the bank of a fast-flowing river. You drop a small, flexible **circular wire loop** (say, 10 cm in diameter) into the water at a single instant. The water particles that are inside the loop *at that exact moment* are tagged.
> As time progresses, the tagged particles move downstream, but they remain *enclosed* by the very streamlines that were originally passing through the loop's boundary. The flexible shape of this "packet" of water distorts — it can bulge outward where the river is slow and squeeze inward where the river is fast — but no river water from outside can leak in, and no tagged water can leak out.
> That imaginary, flexible, water-filled hose in the middle of the river **IS** a stream tube. The river is the entire flow field; the "hose" is the boundary formed by streamlines.

This analogy directly leads us to a vital physical consequence: **the mass of fluid trapped inside a stream tube is conserved** as it moves through the flow field. The fluid is "chained" inside by its own velocity field.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Stream-Tube Analysis:**
> - **Density (ρ)** — measured in **kg/m³** (for liquids ≈ 1000 kg/m³; for air ≈ 1.225 kg/m³)
> - **Velocity (V)** — measured in **m/s**
> - **Cross-sectional Area (dA)** — measured in **m²**
> - **Volumetric Flow Rate (Q)** — measured in **m³/s**
> - **Mass Flow Rate (ṁ)** — measured in **kg/s**
> - The fundamental continuity identity linking these is **ṁ = ρ · Q = ρ · V · A** (for uniform velocity at the section)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of a Stream Tube — Structural Breakdown

A stream tube, although imaginary, possesses mathematically precise and physically meaningful properties. We break them down in a logical sequence:

**Property 1: The lateral surface is a streamline at every point**
- *Why?* By construction, the tube is generated by drawing a streamline through *every* point of a closed loop. Hence, the lateral wall *is* a streamline.
- *How?* It follows that the velocity vector **V** at any point on the lateral wall is purely tangential to the wall — there is no normal component.

**Property 2: No fluid crosses the boundary (the "rigid-pipe" effect)**
- *Why?* A particle would need a velocity component perpendicular to the wall to leave the tube. Since the wall is a streamline, all velocity is parallel to it.
- *How?* Mathematically, $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ at the lateral surface, where $\hat{n}$ is the outward unit normal. Hence, the mass flux through the lateral surface is identically zero.

**Property 3: Mass inside a stream tube is conserved**
- *Why?* The conservation of mass (continuity principle) requires that mass can neither be created nor destroyed inside the tube, and since none can enter or leave through the boundary, the trapped mass must remain constant.
- *How?* This is the *foundation* from which the **Continuity Equation** is derived (see Section 3).

**Property 4: Cross-sectional shape can change along the tube length**
- *Why?* The boundary streamlines may converge or diverge depending on the local velocity field. Where the flow accelerates, streamlines converge and the cross-section shrinks; where the flow decelerates, streamlines diverge and the cross-section expands.
- *How?* This shape change is *not* arbitrary — it is dictated by the underlying velocity field, and the area–velocity relationship $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ must hold at every section.

**Property 5: A stream tube becomes infinitesimally thin (→ a single streamline) in the limit**
- *Why?* If we shrink the original closed curve down to a single point, the "tube" degenerates into a single streamline.
- *How?* This limiting behaviour is useful in analytical fluid mechanics because the *entire* flow field can be represented as an *infinite bundle of stream tubes*, each of which may be treated as a one-dimensional flow conduit.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Stream Tube Equations — Master These for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description / Name | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ | No-penetration condition on the lateral surface | All steady flows |
| 2 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Continuity for incompressible flow through a stream tube | Incompressible (ρ = const) |
| 3 | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Continuity for compressible flow | Compressible (variable ρ) |
| 4 | $Q = \int_A \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A}$ | Volumetric flow rate through a section | General |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{const.}$ | Mass flow rate is constant along a stream tube | Steady flow |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \frac{dA}{A} = -\frac{dV}{V} \cdot (1 - M^2)$ | Area–velocity relation including compressibility (M = Mach) | Compressible subsonic/supersonic |
| 7 | $dA \to 0 \implies \text{Tube} \to \text{Streamline}$ | Limiting form of a stream tube | Conceptual |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Rendering Tip:** In the markdown table above, every $\vert$ in a math expression has been written as `\vert` or wrapped cleanly. When typing answers in your KTU answer booklet, ensure you use the correct SI units (m, m², m/s, kg/s) — examiners often deduct ½ mark for missing units.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The stream tube is far more than a textbook abstraction. It is a *working tool* in numerous engineering disciplines:

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Wing-tip vortices and the *bound vortex* in lifting-line theory are modelled as a bundle of stream tubes, each carrying a portion of the lift.
- **Pipe & Duct Design:** Variable-area ducts (nozzles, diffusers) are designed by treating them as a single, gradually-changing stream tube.
- **Open-Channel Hydraulics:** The unit stream tube (or "stream filament") approach underpins the Saint-Venant equations of gradually-varied flow.
- **Turbomachinery:** The relative-velocity triangles in centrifugal pumps and turbines are derived by analysing flow through successive stream-tube cross-sections.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** Stream tubes are used as a *post-processing* visualisation tool to identify regions of high velocity, recirculation, or separation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Continuity Equation for an Incompressible Fluid Through a Stream Tube

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** To prove that, for a steady, incompressible flow, the product of cross-sectional area and average velocity is constant along a stream tube: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.

**Step 1 — Define a stream tube and choose two sections.**
Consider a stream tube in a steady flow field. Choose two cross-sections, normal to the streamlines, labelled Section 1 and Section 2, with areas $A_1$ and $A_2$ and average velocities $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively.

**Step 2 — Identify the control volume.**
The fluid trapped between Section 1 and Section 2, bounded laterally by streamlines, is our *control volume* (CV).

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of mass (the integral continuity equation).**
For a steady flow, the net mass flux out of the CV must equal the net mass flux in (the time-rate-of-change of mass inside is zero, since the flow is steady and the CV is bounded by streamlines that move with the fluid).

$$
\oint_{CS} \rho \, \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Decompose the closed-surface integral.**
The closed surface (CS) of the stream tube has three parts:
- (i) Section 1 (inlet), outward normal points *into* the flow → $\vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A} = -V_1 \, dA$
- (ii) Section 2 (outlet), outward normal points *out of* the flow → $\vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A} = +V_2 \, dA$
- (iii) Lateral surface: $\vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0$ (no penetration)

Substituting:

$$
\int_{A_1} \rho_1 (-V_1) \, dA \; + \; \int_{A_2} \rho_2 (+V_2) \, dA \; + \; 0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Recognise that average velocity can be factored out for uniform flow.**
For uniform (or average) velocity at each section:

$$
-\rho_1 A_1 V_1 + \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Impose the incompressibility condition.**
For an incompressible fluid, density is constant: $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$. The density cancels:

$$
\rho ( -A_1 V_1 + A_2 V_2 ) = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Conclude the final form of the continuity equation for a stream tube.**

$$
\boxed{\,A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant along the stream tube}\,}
$$

Or, equivalently, the volumetric flow rate is constant:

$$
Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate Constancy for a Compressible Fluid

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** To show that $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ is constant along a stream tube even when density varies (e.g., gas flow).

**Step 1 — Start from the same integral continuity statement:**
For steady flow, $\oint_{CS} \rho \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Evaluate each surface contribution:**

$$
-\int_{A_1} \rho_1 V_1 \, dA + \int_{A_2} \rho_2 V_2 \, dA + 0 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Factor averages and rearrange:**

$$
\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the left- and right-hand sides as the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stream Tube Concept at a Glance

| Aspect | Streamline | Stream Tube | Control Volume (General) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dimensionality** | 1-D curve | 3-D tubular region | Any arbitrary 3-D region |
| **Boundary** | N/A (a curve has no boundary in the flow sense) | Lateral surface is a streamline | Arbitrary — can cut streamlines |
| **Mass Flux Through Boundary** | N/A | **Zero** (lateral) | Generally non-zero |
| **Use** | Visualise local flow direction | Quantify mass/flow rate | Apply conservation laws generally |
| **Mathematical Identity** | $\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$ | $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{const.}$ | $\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV}\rho\,dV + \oint_{CS}\rho\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{A} = 0$ |

### 3.4 Limiting Behaviour: When a Stream Tube → Streamline

If we take a stream tube and let the cross-sectional area $\to 0$ (i.e., the original closed curve shrinks to a single point), the tube becomes a single streamline. This is the **stream filament** concept, and it underpins the method of characteristics used in 1-D gas dynamics.

$$
\lim_{dA \to 0} (\text{Stream Tube}) = \text{Streamline (Stream Filament)}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Stream Tube with Converging–Diverging Cross-Section

The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a stream tube, showing the inlet, the variable cross-section, the streamlines forming the lateral boundary, and the outlet. It also maps the key physical parameters (area, velocity, density) at each section.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Closed Curve C0 at Inlet] --> B[Section 1: Inlet Plane]
    B --> C{Stream Tube Lateral Boundary Made of Streamlines}
    C --> D[Section 2: Intermediate Cross Section]
    D --> E[Section 3: Outlet Plane]
    E --> F[Closed Curve CN at Outlet]
    C -. "V dot n = 0" .-> G[No Mass Flux Through Lateral Wall]
    B --> H[Area A1, Velocity V1, Density rho1]
    D --> I[Area A2, Velocity V2, Density rho2]
    E --> J[Area A3, Velocity V3, Density rho3]
    H --> K[Mass Flow Rate m dot = rho1 A1 V1]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Constant Along Stream Tube]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Conceptual Flow of Stream-Tube Analysis

This second diagram maps the *reasoning flow* a KTU student should follow when solving a stream-tube problem in the exam — from geometry recognition to the final equation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Identify Steady Flow Regime] --> S2[Draw or Visualize Stream Tube]
    S2 --> S3[Mark Inlet Section 1 and Outlet Section 2]
    S3 --> S4[Check Incompressibility of Fluid]
    S4 --> S5{Is rho constant?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Apply A1 V1 = A2 V2]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Apply rho1 A1 V1 = rho2 A2 V2]
    S6 --> S8[Compute Q or m dot]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[State Result with SI Units]
```

### 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (for Examiners/Students)

In a *physical* sense (which Mermaid cannot easily render), picture a tapering glass funnel with water flowing through it:
- The **top** of the funnel is a wide circular cross-section ($A_1$ large, $V_1$ small).
- The **bottom** is a narrow circular cross-section ($A_2$ small, $V_2$ large).
- The **slanted glass wall** of the funnel represents the stream-tube boundary (streamlines).
- Water inside the funnel cannot leak through the glass — analogous to fluid not crossing a streamline.
- The volume of water entering the top per second *must* equal the volume leaving the bottom per second — the **continuity principle** in action.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
> *Define a stream tube. State any two of its properties.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A stream tube is an imaginary tubular surface in a fluid flow field, formed by the locus of all streamlines passing through an arbitrary closed curve at a given instant.
> **Properties:**
> 1. The lateral boundary of a stream tube is a streamline, hence no fluid can cross it.
> 2. The mass flow rate is constant at every cross-section of the stream tube, provided the flow is steady.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> *Differentiate between a streamline and a stream tube.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Streamline | Stream Tube |
|---|---|
| A curve tangent to the velocity vector at every instant | A tubular region bounded by a family of streamlines |
| One-dimensional geometric object | Three-dimensional geometric region |
| Used to indicate flow direction locally | Used to apply continuity and quantify flow rate |

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Module Internal Choice Instruction (as per KTU 2024):** *Answer any one full question from this module. Each full question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a)** Define a stream tube. Using the stream-tube concept, derive the continuity equation for a **steady, incompressible** flow. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water flows steadily through a tapering pipe (treated as a stream tube). At Section 1, the diameter is $d_1 = 10$ cm and the average velocity is $V_1 = 2$ m/s. At Section 2, the diameter is $d_2 = 5$ cm. Determine:
(i) The average velocity at Section 2.
(ii) The volumetric flow rate through the pipe.
(iii) The mass flow rate of water (take $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Step 1 (Definition, 1 Mark):** A stream tube is an imaginary tube whose boundary is formed by streamlines passing through a closed curve; fluid cannot cross this boundary.
>
> **Step 2 (Assumptions, 1 Mark):**
> - Flow is **steady** ($\partial / \partial t = 0$).
> - Fluid is **incompressible** (ρ = constant).
> - Velocity is **uniform** over each cross-section (or we use *average* velocity).
>
> **Step 3 (Derivation, 5 Marks):** See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation, leading to $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q = \text{const.}$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

> **Given:**
> $d_1 = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$, $V_1 = 2 \text{ m/s}$, $d_2 = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
>
> **Compute cross-sectional areas:**
>
> $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
>
> $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
>
> **(i) Velocity at Section 2:** Apply continuity $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$:
>
> $$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 2}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.0 \text{ m/s}$$
>
> **(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**
>
> $$Q = A_1 V_1 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 = 1.5708 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 0.0157 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
>
> **(iii) Mass flow rate:**
>
> $$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.0157 = 15.708 \text{ kg/s} \approx 15.71 \text{ kg/s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the physical significance of a stream tube. State and prove the condition for mass conservation in a **compressible** flow along a stream tube. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Air ($\rho_1 = 1.2$ kg/m³) enters a duct of area $0.05$ m² at 30 m/s and leaves at Section 2 where the area is $0.02$ m² and density is $\rho_2 = 1.0$ kg/m³. Assuming steady flow, find:
(i) The exit velocity.
(ii) The mass flow rate.
(iii) The exit volumetric flow rate. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Physical Significance (2 Marks):** A stream tube physically isolates a "packet" of fluid that moves cohesively through the flow. It allows the engineer to apply 1-D conservation equations (mass, momentum, energy) to what is actually a 3-D flow. It is the basis of all control-volume analyses.
>
> **Proof of Mass Conservation (5 Marks):** Following the steps of Section 3.2 of these notes:
>
> $$\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \text{const.}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

> **Given:** $A_1 = 0.05$ m², $V_1 = 30$ m/s, $\rho_1 = 1.2$ kg/m³, $A_2 = 0.02$ m², $\rho_2 = 1.0$ kg/m³.
>
> **Mass flow rate is the same at both sections (since $\dot{m}$ is constant):**
>
> $$\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = 1.2 \times 0.05 \times 30 = 1.8 \text{ kg/s}$$
>
> **(i) Exit velocity:**
>
> $$V_2 = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_2 A_2} = \frac{1.8}{1.0 \times 0.02} = 90 \text{ m/s}$$
>
> **(ii) Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = 1.8$ kg/s (computed above).
>
> **(iii) Exit volumetric flow rate:**
>
> $$Q_2 = A_2 V_2 = 0.02 \times 90 = 1.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the units.** Many students lose ½ mark per sub-part by writing $V_2 = 8$ instead of $V_2 = 8$ m/s. Always include the SI unit.
> - **Confusing the area formula.** Use $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ for circular cross-sections, NOT $A = \pi d$ (that is circumference).
> - **Mixing up mass and volumetric flow rate.** A common slip is writing $\dot{m} = A V$ instead of $\dot{m} = \rho A V$. Examiners specifically test this distinction in the compressible-flow question.
> - **Forgetting assumptions in the derivation.** The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to *list* "steady flow" and "incompressible / constant density" as assumptions. Skipping this costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Not stating the no-penetration condition.** When defining a stream tube, you MUST mention that the lateral surface is a streamline, so the normal component of velocity is zero. This is the single most-lost 1-mark item.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A *stream tube* is an imaginary tube bounded by streamlines that pass through a closed curve. Fluid cannot cross the lateral surface.
- **No-penetration condition:** $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ on the lateral wall (this is the key mathematical property).
- **Mass conservation (general):** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ — valid for both compressible and incompressible steady flow.
- **Mass conservation (incompressible):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ — the simpler, more frequently tested form.
- **Limiting case:** As $dA \to 0$, a stream tube becomes a single streamline (stream filament).
- **Assumptions needed for the standard derivation:** (1) Steady flow, (2) Uniform velocity at each section (or use average velocity), (3) Incompressibility (for the simplified form).
- **Real-world use:** Underlies pipe-flow analysis, nozzle/diffuser design, lifting-line theory in aerodynamics, and CFD streamline visualisation.
- **Exam mantra:** Always include units, list assumptions, and state the no-penetration condition in your definition.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Fluid Mechanics**, the term **flow** refers to the continuous deformation or movement of fluid particles under the influence of applied forces (pressure, gravity, viscous, or surface tension forces). However, real-world fluid behavior exhibits enormous diversity — ranging from the slow, silky motion of honey to the chaotic, swirling motion of rapids.

**Classification of Flow** is the systematic categorization of fluid motion based on:
1. **Temporal variation** (time dependence)
2. **Spatial variation** (geometry dependence)
3. **Internal fluid behavior** (viscosity, density, rotation)
4. **Dimensionality** (number of spatial coordinates)
5. **Compressibility effects** (density changes)

Each classification provides a mathematical simplification that allows engineers to apply appropriate governing equations (Continuity, Navier-Stokes, Bernoulli, Euler) with reasonable accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 2, students must master the **Reynolds Number ($Re$)**, **critical Reynolds number ($Re_c$)**, and the distinction between **Laminar vs Turbulent flow** along with **Steady vs Unsteady** and **Uniform vs Non-uniform** classifications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing by a **fire hydrant** that has just been opened:

- If you watch the water at one fixed point, the velocity remains roughly the same every second → **Steady Flow**
- Now move your eyes along the pipe: the water speed is the same at the mouth as deep inside the pipe → **Uniform Flow**
- Open the hydrant gently: water glides in smooth parallel layers → **Laminar Flow** ($Re < 2000$)
- Open it fully: water sprays out chaotically with eddies and swirls → **Turbulent Flow** ($Re > 4000$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like traffic on a highway:**
> - **Laminar** = well-organized lanes, cars moving in sync
> - **Turbulent** = bumper-to-bumper chaos with random lane changes
> - **Steady** = traffic flow rate doesn't change over time
> - **Uniform** = traffic density is the same everywhere

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$)**: measured in **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**
- **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu = \mu/\rho$)**: measured in **m²/s**
- **Density ($\rho$)**: measured in **kg/m³** (water at 4°C = **1000 kg/m³**)
- **Reynolds Number ($Re$)**: dimensionless
- **Critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow**: $Re_c \approx \mathbf{2300}$ (laminar-turbulent transition)
- **Speed of Sound in air**: $c \approx \mathbf{343 \, m/s}$ at 20°C
- **Mach Number ($M$)**: dimensionless ratio $V/c$

## 1.4 Comprehensive Classification Tree

Fluid flow is classified along **eight principal axes**, summarized below:

| # | Classification Basis | Types |
|---|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | Time dependence | Steady / Unsteady |
| 2 | Space dependence | Uniform / Non-uniform |
| 3 | Viscosity | Laminar / Turbulent |
| 4 | Compressibility | Compressible / Incompressible |
| 5 | Rotation | Rotational / Irrotational |
| 6 | Dimensionality | 1-D / 2-D / 3-D |
| 7 | Continuity of properties | Ideal / Real (Viscous) |
| 8 | Specific energy | Subcritical / Critical / Supercritical |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Steady vs Unsteady Flow

**Steady Flow:** Fluid properties (velocity, pressure, density) at any fixed point in the flow field do **not change with time**.

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Unsteady Flow:** At least one property varies with time at a given location.

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} \neq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> A river in flood with rising water level is **unsteady**, even if the velocity profile appears stable.

**Why it matters:** Steady flow allows direct application of Bernoulli's equation and reduces the Navier-Stokes equations to ODEs.

---

### 2.1.2 Uniform vs Non-Uniform Flow

**Uniform Flow:** Velocity (in magnitude and direction) is the same at every point in the flow at a given instant.

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$$

**Non-Uniform Flow:** Velocity changes from point to point in the flow field.

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} \neq 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Steady flow can still be non-uniform (e.g., flow through a converging nozzle).
> Uniform flow is always steady if the fluid is incompressible.

---

### 2.1.3 Laminar vs Turbulent Flow (The Most Important Classification)

**Laminar Flow:**
- Fluid particles move in **well-defined, parallel layers (laminae)**
- No macroscopic mixing between layers
- Dominant viscous forces over inertial forces
- Smooth, predictable streamlines

**Turbulent Flow:**
- Fluid particles move in **irregular, chaotic paths**
- Continuous mixing; eddies, vortices, and fluctuations
- Dominant inertial forces over viscous forces
- Requires statistical (time-averaged) description

**The Reynolds Number** governs this transition:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $V$ = mean velocity (m/s)
- $D$ = characteristic length (pipe diameter, m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s)

**Flow Regimes for Pipe Flow:**
- $Re < 2000$: **Laminar**
- $2000 < Re < 4000$: **Transitional**
- $Re > 4000$: **Turbulent**

> [!NOTE]
> **Osborne Reynolds' Experiment (1883):** Dye injected into a glass pipe showed straight lines for low velocities (laminar) and chaotic dispersion at high velocities (turbulent).

---

### 2.1.4 Compressible vs Incompressible Flow

**Incompressible Flow:** Density remains essentially constant throughout the flow.

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} \approx 0$$

Valid when Mach Number $M < 0.3$.

**Compressible Flow:** Density changes significantly; pressure, temperature, and density are coupled by thermodynamics.

$$M = \frac{V}{c} > 0.3$$

where $c$ = speed of sound in the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All liquids are treated as incompressible. Gases flowing at $M < 0.3$ (e.g., wind, ventilation) are also treated as incompressible.

---

### 2.1.5 Rotational vs Irrotational Flow

**Rotational Flow:** Fluid particles rotate about their own axis while translating. Angular velocity $\vec{\omega} \neq 0$.

**Irrotational Flow:** Fluid particles translate without rotation; only deformation. Angular velocity $\vec{\omega} = 0$.

The angular velocity is computed as:

$$\vec{\omega} = \frac{1}{2} \nabla \times \vec{V} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} \right)\hat{i} + \frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} \right)\hat{j} + \frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)\hat{k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A free vortex (like a bathtub drain) is **rotational**. A free streamline past a body can be **irrotational** outside the boundary layer.

---

### 2.1.6 One, Two, and Three-Dimensional Flow

| Type | Velocity components | Example |
|------|---------------------|---------|
| **1-D** | One | Flow in a long straight pipe |
| **2-D** | Two | Flow over a long cylinder |
| **3-D** | Three | Flow around an aircraft wingtip |

In 1-D flow, all properties vary only along one axis (typically $x$): $V = V(x)$.

---

### 2.1.7 Ideal vs Real Flow

**Ideal Flow:** Inviscid (no viscosity) and incompressible; satisfies Euler's equation.

**Real Flow:** Viscous effects included; satisfies the full Navier-Stokes equation.

---

### 2.1.8 Subcritical, Critical, and Supercritical Flow (Open Channel)

Based on the **Froude Number**:

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gD}}$$

- $Fr < 1$: **Subcritical** (tranquil flow)
- $Fr = 1$: **Critical** flow
- $Fr > 1$: **Supercritical** (rapid/shooting flow)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L_c}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s |
| 3 | Mach Number | $M = \dfrac{V}{c}$ | $M < 0.3$ incompressible |
| 4 | Froude Number | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | $Fr = 1$ critical |
| 5 | Angular Velocity | $\vec{\omega} = \dfrac{1}{2}\nabla \times \vec{V}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Continuity (1-D) | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | m³/s |
| 7 | Steady condition | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$ | — |
| 8 | Uniform condition | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$ | — |
| 9 | Pipe Critical $Re_c$ | $Re_c \approx 2300$ | — |
| 10 | Continuity (mass) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the threshold values** — they appear frequently in KTU Part A and Part B questions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Aerospace Engineering:** Compressibility (Mach number) determines aircraft design and shock-wave analysis.
2. **Civil Engineering (Pipelines):** Reynolds number determines whether friction factor follows the **Hagen-Poiseuille** law (laminar) or the **Moody chart** (turbulent).
3. **Hydraulic Engineering:** Froude number classifies open-channel flow for designing spillways and channels.
4. **Turbomachinery:** Rotational flow concepts are essential in pumps, turbines, and centrifugal compressors.
5. **Meteorology:** Atmospheric flow is studied using rotational/irrotational classification to model cyclones.
6. **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow in arteries ($Re \approx 1000$) is in the transitional regime — critical for diagnosing arterial diseases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deriving the Reynolds Number by Dimensional Analysis

The Reynolds number can be derived using the **Buckingham Pi Theorem** by considering the pressure drop $\Delta p$ per unit length as a function of the governing variables:

$$\Delta p / L = f(\rho, V, D, \mu)$$

**Step 1:** Identify the variables and their dimensions.

$$\Delta p/L: [M L^{-2} T^{-2}], \quad \rho: [M L^{-3}], \quad V: [L T^{-1}], \quad D: [L], \quad \mu: [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$$

**Step 2:** Number of variables = 5; Number of fundamental dimensions (M, L, T) = 3. So we expect $5 - 3 = 2$ dimensionless groups.

**Step 3:** Choose repeating variables: $\rho$, $V$, $D$.

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p / L}{\rho^a V^b D^c}$$

Applying dimensional homogeneity:

$$[M L^{-2} T^{-2}] = [M L^{-3}]^a [L T^{-1}]^b [L]^c$$

Equating exponents:
- M: $1 = a \Rightarrow a = 1$
- T: $-2 = -b \Rightarrow b = 2$
- L: $-2 = -3a + b + c \Rightarrow -2 = -3 + 2 + c \Rightarrow c = -1$

Therefore:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p / L}{\rho V^2 D^{-1}} = \frac{\Delta p \, D}{\rho V^2 L}$$

**Step 4:** For $\pi_2$, group $\mu$:

$$\pi_2 = \frac{\mu}{\rho^a V^b D^c}$$

$$[M L^{-1} T^{-1}] = [M L^{-3}]^a [L T^{-1}]^b [L]^c$$

Equating exponents:
- M: $1 = a$
- T: $-1 = -b \Rightarrow b = 1$
- L: $-1 = -3 + 1 + c \Rightarrow c = 1$

$$\pi_2 = \frac{\mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{1}{Re}$$

Thus, $\pi_2 = 1/Re$, confirming:

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Worked Examples

### Example 3.2.1: Reynolds Number for Water in a Pipe

**Given:** Water at 20°C flows through a 50 mm diameter pipe at an average velocity of 3 m/s.
**Properties at 20°C:** $\rho = 998 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

**Solution:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 3 \times 0.050}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{149.7}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 149{,}401$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **Turbulent**.

---

### Example 3.2.2: Velocity for Laminar-Turbulent Transition

**Given:** Oil with $\rho = 880 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.025 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ flows in a 100 mm diameter pipe.
**Find:** Maximum velocity for laminar flow.

**Solution:**
For laminar-turbulent transition, use $Re = 2300$:

$$V_{\text{critical}} = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho D} = \frac{2300 \times 0.025}{880 \times 0.100}$$

$$V_{\text{critical}} = \frac{57.5}{88} = 0.6534 \, \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, any velocity below **0.653 m/s** guarantees laminar flow.

---

### Example 3.2.3: Mach Number Classification

**Given:** Air at standard conditions ($\rho = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) flows past an aircraft at 250 m/s. Speed of sound $c = 343 \, \text{m/s}$.

**Solution:**

$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{250}{343} = 0.729$$

Since $M > 0.3$, the flow is classified as **Compressible** and compressibility effects cannot be ignored.

---

### Example 3.2.4: Froude Number for Open Channel

**Given:** Water flows in a rectangular channel 2 m wide at 1.5 m depth with mean velocity 2 m/s.

**Solution:**
Hydraulic depth $D = 1.5$ m.

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gD}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 1.5}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{14.715}} = \frac{2}{3.836} = 0.521$$

Since $Fr < 1$, the flow is **Subcritical**.

---

### Example 3.2.5: Irrotational Flow Check

**Given:** Velocity field $\vec{V} = (2xy)\hat{i} + (y^2 - x^2)\hat{j} + 0\hat{k}$.

**Solution:**
Compute $\omega_z$ (z-component of angular velocity):

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -2x, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2x$$

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}(-2x - 2x) = -2x$$

Since $\omega_z \neq 0$ (except at $x=0$), the flow is **Rotational**.

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Key Distinctions

| Parameter | Steady | Unsteady | Uniform | Non-uniform | Laminar | Turbulent |
|-----------|--------|----------|---------|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Time variation | No | Yes | — | — | — | — |
| Space variation | — | — | No | Yes | — | — |
| Particle motion | — | — | — | — | Smooth layers | Chaotic |
| Reynolds number | — | — | — | — | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Head loss relation | — | — | — | — | Linear in $V$ | ~$V^{1.75}$ to $V^2$ |
| Equation used | Euler/Bernoulli | Unsteady Bernoulli | 1-D continuity | 3-D Navier-Stokes | Poiseuille Law | Empirical correlations |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Comprehensive Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA([Fluid Flow]) --> ClassA{Time Dependent?}
    ClassA -->|Yes| Unsteady[Unsteady Flow]
    ClassA -->|No| Steady[Steady Flow]
    
    StartB([Fluid Flow]) --> ClassB{Space Dependent?}
    ClassB -->|Yes| NonUniform[Non-Uniform Flow]
    ClassB -->|No| Uniform[Uniform Flow]
    
    StartC([Fluid Flow]) --> ClassC{Re Number?}
    ClassC -->|< 2000| Lam[Laminar Flow]
    ClassC -->|2000-4000| Trans[Transitional Flow]
    ClassC -->|> 4000| Turb[Turbulent Flow]
    
    StartD([Fluid Flow]) --> ClassD{Mach Number?}
    ClassD -->|< 0.3| Incomp[Incompressible Flow]
    ClassD -->|>= 0.3| Comp[Compressible Flow]
    
    StartE([Fluid Flow]) --> ClassE{Rotational?}
    ClassE -->|Yes| Rot[Rotational Flow]
    ClassE -->|No| Irrot[Irrotational Flow]
    
    StartF([Fluid Flow]) --> ClassF{Dimensionality?}
    ClassF -->|One axis| OneD[1-D Flow]
    ClassF -->|Two axes| TwoD[2-D Flow]
    ClassF -->|Three axes| ThreeD[3-D Flow]
    
    StartG([Fluid Flow]) --> ClassG{Viscosity?}
    ClassG -->|Neglected| Ideal[Ideal Inviscid Flow]
    ClassG -->|Considered| Real[Real Viscous Flow]
```

## 4.2 Reynolds Experiment Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TankA[Constant Head Tank] --> PipeIn[Inlet Section]
    PipeIn --> TestPipe[Glass Test Pipe with Bell Mouth]
    TestPipe --> DyeInj[Dye Injection Needle]
    DyeInj --> ObsWin[Observation Window]
    ObsWin --> FlowPattern{Flow Pattern?}
    FlowPattern -->|Low V| LamLine[Straight Dye Line - Laminar]
    FlowPattern -->|High V| TurbMix[Chaotic Dye Dispersion - Turbulent]
    FlowPattern -->|Medium V| TransWave[Intermittent Wavy Line - Transitional]
    
    TankA --> Valve[Control Valve]
    Valve --> FlowMeter[Flow Measurement]
    FlowMeter --> TestPipe
```

## 4.3 Flow Regime Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Given: rho, V, D, mu] --> CalcRe[Calculate Re = rhoVD / mu]
    CalcRe --> CheckRe{Re Value?}
    CheckRe -->|< 2000| OutL[Output: Laminar]
    CheckRe -->|2000 to 4000| OutT[Output: Transitional]
    CheckRe -->|> 4000| OutTurb[Output: Turbulent]
    
    Input2[Given: V, c] --> CalcM[Calculate M = V/c]
    CalcM --> CheckM{M Value?}
    CheckM -->|< 0.3| OutI[Output: Incompressible]
    CheckM -->|>= 0.3| OutC[Output: Compressible]
    
    Input3[Given: V, g, D] --> CalcFr[Calculate Fr = V/sqrt gD]
    CalcFr --> CheckFr{Fr Value?}
    CheckFr -->|< 1| OutSub[Output: Subcritical]
    CheckFr -->|= 1| OutCr[Output: Critical]
    CheckFr -->|> 1| OutSup[Output: Supercritical]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Relationship Between Classifications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FluidFlow[Real Fluid Flow Problem] --> Step1[Step 1: Check Time Dependence]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Check Space Dependence]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Compute Reynolds Number]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Compute Mach Number]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Check Rotation via Curl of V]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Select Governing Equation]
    
    Step6 --> EqChoice{Equation?}
    EqChoice -->|Laminar Incompressible| NS1[Hagen-Poiseuille Law]
    EqChoice -->|Turbulent Incompressible| NS2[Navier-Stokes with Empirical friction]
    EqChoice -->|Compressible| NS3[Euler Equations with Thermodynamics]
    EqChoice -->|Irrotational Ideal| NS4[Velocity Potential and Laplace Equation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above is a **functional decision architecture** rather than a physical drawing. It maps the analytical workflow a KTU student must follow when classifying a flow before solving the problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Reynolds Number. What is its physical significance in classifying fluid flow?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Reynolds Number is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow, defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$
