<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Turbulence

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Turbulence** is a complex, irregular, and chaotic state of fluid motion characterized by random fluctuations of velocity, pressure, and other flow properties in both space and time. In turbulent flow, the fluid particles move in **eddies** of varying sizes (from macroscopic to microscopic Kolmogorov scales) superimposed on the mean flow, resulting in vigorous mixing, enhanced momentum transfer, and significantly higher energy losses compared to laminar flow.

The transition from laminar to turbulent flow is universally characterized by the dimensionless **Reynolds Number (Re)**, defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid $[\text{kg/m}^3]$
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow $[\text{m/s}]$
- $D$ = Diameter of the pipe $[\text{m}]$
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity $[\text{N·s/m}^2]$
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$

**Critical Reynolds Number** for pipe flow: $Re_{cr} \approx \mathbf{2300}$ (lower) and $Re_{cr} \approx \mathbf{4000}$ (upper).

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Behavior |
|-------------|----------------------|----------|
| Laminar | $Re < 2000$ | Smooth, parallel streamlines |
| Transitional | $2000 < Re < 4000$ | Unstable, intermittent bursts |
| Turbulent | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic, fluctuating, well-mixed |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Understanding:**
> Imagine a calm, straight highway where cars move in perfect, orderly lanes — this is **laminar flow**. Now picture the same highway during a thunderstorm with heavy spray, cars weaving unpredictably, and small whirlwinds (eddies) forming between vehicles — this is **turbulent flow**.

A more physical analogy: Think of pouring **honey** (high viscosity) slowly down a spoon — the stream is smooth and coherent (laminar). Now pour **water** fast from a tap — the stream breaks into chaotic, swirling eddies (turbulent). The difference is governed by the ratio of **inertial forces** (driving motion) to **viscous forces** (resisting motion), which is precisely the Reynolds Number.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Turbulence Metrics (Syllabus Highlights):**
> - **Reynolds Number (Re):** Dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces
> - **Critical Reynolds Number ($Re_c$):** 2300 (lower limit) for pipe flow
> - **Turbulent Intensity (I):** $I = \dfrac{u'}{U}$ (ratio of RMS fluctuating velocity to mean velocity)
> - **Mixing Length ($\ell$):** Analogous to mean free path in kinetic theory
> - **Kinematic Eddy Viscosity ($\epsilon$):** Property of flow, NOT fluid

## 1.4 Characteristics of Turbulent Flow

- **Irregularity:** Random fluctuations in velocity at every point
- **Diffusivity:** Rapid mixing and momentum transfer across streamlines
- **Rotationality:** Vorticity fluctuations; presence of eddies of all scales
- **Dissipative:** Continuous conversion of kinetic energy → heat via viscous dissipation
- **Three-dimensionality:** Fluctuations occur in all three spatial directions
- **Continuum:** Satisfies continuum equations despite chaos (at high Re)

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reynolds Decomposition of Velocity

The instantaneous velocity in turbulent flow is decomposed into a time-averaged (mean) component and a fluctuating component:

$$\vec{V} = \bar{\vec{V}} + \vec{v'}$$

where:
- $\bar{\vec{V}}$ = Time-averaged mean velocity (steady component)
- $\vec{v'}$ = Fluctuating component (time-average $\overline{v'} = 0$)

The time-averaged value is defined as:

$$\bar{V} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} V \, dt$$

## 2.2 Reynolds (Turbulent) Shear Stress — The Core Concept

By substituting the Reynolds decomposition into the Navier-Stokes equations and time-averaging, an **additional apparent shear stress** emerges beyond the viscous stress:

$$\tau_{total} = \mu \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} - \rho \overline{u'v'}$$

The second term is the **Reynolds Stress**:

$$\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Reynolds stress is **NOT** a true fluid property — it is a property of the **flow** itself. It arises from momentum transfer by turbulent eddies (analogous to molecular momentum transfer in kinetic theory, but on a much larger scale).

## 2.3 Prandtl's Mixing Length Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Concept:** A fluid particle retains its momentum for a characteristic distance $\ell$ (mixing length) before mixing with the surrounding fluid — analogous to the mean free path of molecules in kinetic theory.

Prandtl assumed that the fluctuating velocity $v'$ is of the same order as the difference in mean velocity over the mixing length:

$$v' \sim \ell \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right|$$

This leads to the famous **Prandtl Mixing Length Formula**:

$$\tau_t = \rho \ell^2 \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$$

Defining the **Eddy Viscosity** (kinematic):

$$\epsilon = \ell^2 \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right|$$

The total shear stress becomes:

$$\tau = (\mu + \rho \epsilon) \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$$

## 2.4 Velocity Distribution in Turbulent Flow (Smooth Pipes)

For turbulent flow near a smooth wall, Prandtl's universal velocity distribution (the "Law of the Wall") is:

$$\frac{u}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \frac{u_* y}{\nu} \right) + 5.5 \quad \text{[for smooth pipes]}$$

where $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0 / \rho}$ is the **shear (friction) velocity**, and $y$ is the distance from the wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the log-law profile:** The mean velocity varies **logarithmically** with distance from the wall, NOT parabolically (as in laminar flow). This is one of the most-tested results in KTU examinations.

## 2.5 Velocity Distribution in Turbulent Flow (Rough Pipes)

For fully rough turbulent flow, viscosity becomes negligible and:

$$\frac{u}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \frac{y}{k_s} \right) + 8.5 \quad \text{[for rough pipes]}$$

where $k_s$ is the equivalent sand roughness height.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| $Re = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless |
| $Re_{cr} = 2300$ | Critical Reynolds Number (pipe) | dimensionless |
| $u = \bar{u} + u'$ | Reynolds decomposition | m/s |
| $\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$ | Reynolds (turbulent) shear stress | Pa ($\text{N/m}^2$) |
| $\tau_{total} = \mu \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy} - \rho \overline{u'v'}$ | Total shear stress in turbulent flow | Pa |
| $\tau_t = \rho \ell^2 \left\vert \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right\vert \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$ | Prandtl mixing length formula | Pa |
| $\epsilon = \ell^2 \left\vert \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right\vert$ | Kinematic eddy viscosity | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\dfrac{u}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \dfrac{u_* y}{\nu} \right) + 5.5$ | Velocity distribution (smooth pipe) | dimensionless |
| $\dfrac{u}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \dfrac{y}{k_s} \right) + 8.5$ | Velocity distribution (rough pipe) | dimensionless |
| $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0 / \rho}$ | Shear (friction) velocity | m/s |
| $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Darcy-Weisbach equation | m |
| $\tau_0 = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{8}$ | Wall shear stress from friction factor | Pa |
| $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10}(Re\sqrt{f}) - 0.8$ | Colebrook-White equation (transition) | dimensionless |
| $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{k_s}{3.7D} \right) + 1.74$ | Karman equation for fully rough flow | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** In the formulas above, $f$ is the **Darcy friction factor** (4× the Fanning friction factor). This is the convention used in KTU 2024 scheme textbooks.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Pipeline Design:** Oil, water, and gas pipelines require accurate head-loss prediction using friction factors derived from turbulence theory
- **Aerodynamics:** Aircraft wing design relies on understanding turbulent boundary layers to control drag
- **Chemical Reactors:** Turbulent mixing enhances heat and mass transfer rates
- **Hydraulic Structures:** Spillways, weirs, and open channels operate predominantly in turbulent regime
- **Blood Flow Modeling:** Biomedical engineering applications in hemodynamics
- **Environmental Engineering:** Pollutant dispersion in rivers and atmosphere

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reynolds Stress from Navier-Stokes Equations

We start with the **incompressible Navier-Stokes equation** in the $x$-direction (assuming steady mean flow in $x$):

$$\rho \frac{Du}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x$$

Expanding the material derivative and substituting $u = \bar{u} + u'$:

$$\rho \frac{\partial (\bar{u} + u')}{\partial t} + \rho (\bar{u} + u') \frac{\partial (\bar{u} + u')}{\partial x} + \rho (\bar{v} + v') \frac{\partial (\bar{u} + u')}{\partial y} + \rho (\bar{w} + w') \frac{\partial (\bar{u} + u')}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 (\bar{u} + u') + \rho g_x$$

Taking the **time average** of this entire equation. The time average of linear terms gives the mean, and the time average of products of fluctuating terms (with zero mean) gives the **Reynolds stresses**:

$$\rho \frac{D\bar{u}}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 \bar{u} + \rho g_x + \left[ \rho \frac{\partial (-\overline{u'v'})}{\partial y} + \rho \frac{\partial (-\overline{u'w'})}{\partial z} \right]$$

The bracketed terms are the **divergence of Reynolds stress tensor**. The dominant component is:

$$\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$$

This term represents the **apparent additional shear stress** caused by turbulent mixing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** The Reynolds stress appears mathematically identical to a viscous stress $\mu (du/dy)$ but is **NOT** a fluid property. It represents momentum transport by eddies — a **flow property**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Prandtl Mixing Length Formula — Complete Steps

**Step 1: Physical Hypothesis (Prandtl, 1925)**

A fluid "lump" or particle moves a transverse distance $\ell$ (the mixing length) in the $y$-direction before mixing with the surrounding fluid, preserving its original $x$-momentum.

**Step 2: Express Fluctuating Velocity**

Before mixing, the particle's mean velocity was $\bar{u}(y)$. After moving to position $y + \ell$, the local mean velocity is $\bar{u}(y + \ell)$. The fluctuation observed is:

$$u' \approx \bar{u}(y + \ell) - \bar{u}(y)$$

**Step 3: Taylor Series Expansion**

$$\bar{u}(y + \ell) = \bar{u}(y) + \ell \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} + \frac{\ell^2}{2} \frac{d^2\bar{u}}{dy^2} + \ldots$$

Therefore:

$$u' \approx \ell \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \quad \text{(neglecting higher orders)}$$

**Step 4: Crosswise Fluctuation**

The transverse fluctuating velocity $v'$ is assumed to be of the same order as the longitudinal fluctuation $u'$:

$$v' \sim \ell \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right|$$

**Step 5: Reynolds Stress from Fluctuations**

The Reynolds stress is given by:

$$\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'} = \rho \ell^2 \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$$

**Step 6: Define Eddy Viscosity**

$$\boxed{\epsilon = \ell^2 \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right| \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_t = \rho \epsilon \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Velocity Distribution in Turbulent Flow (Smooth Pipes)

**Assumption:** For turbulent flow in a smooth pipe, Prandtl assumed that the mixing length varies linearly with distance from the wall:

$$\ell = \kappa y$$

where $\kappa$ is the **von Kármán constant** ($\kappa \approx 0.4$).

**Step 1: Total Shear Stress**

For fully developed turbulent flow, the total shear stress is approximately constant and equal to the wall shear stress $\tau_0$:

$$\tau \approx \tau_0 = \mu \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} + \rho \ell^2 \left( \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right)^2$$

**Step 2: For turbulent core (viscous sublayer negligible)**

$$\tau_0 = \rho \kappa^2 y^2 \left( \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right)^2$$

**Step 3: Solve for $d\bar{u}/dy$**

$$\frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} = \frac{1}{\kappa y} \sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho}} = \frac{u_*}{\kappa y}$$

**Step 4: Integrate from $y=0$ to $y=y$**

$$\int_{0}^{\bar{u}} d\bar{u} = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \int_{0}^{y} \frac{dy}{y}$$

This gives:

$$\bar{u} = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln(y) + C$$

**Step 5: Apply boundary condition (from experiments on smooth pipes)**

Using the experimentally fitted constant $C = u_*(2.5 \ln(u_*/\nu) + 5.5)$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\bar{u}}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \frac{u_* y}{\nu} \right) + 5.5}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Velocity Distribution in Rough Pipes

For fully rough turbulent flow, the viscous sublayer is **disrupted** by wall roughness, and the velocity profile depends only on roughness height $k_s$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\bar{u}}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \frac{y}{k_s} \right) + 8.5}$$

This profile is **independent of viscosity** — confirming the dominance of inertial (turbulent) effects.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow

| **Property** | **Laminar Flow** | **Turbulent Flow** |
|--------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Velocity profile | Parabolic | Flat (logarithmic) |
| Velocity at center | $V_{max} = 2V_{mean}$ | $V_{max} \approx 1.2 V_{mean}$ |
| Friction factor $f$ | $f = 64/Re$ | $f$ from Moody chart / Colebrook |
| Head loss | $h_f \propto V$ | $h_f \propto V^{1.75 \text{ to } 2.0}$ |
| Energy dissipation | Low | High |
| Mixing | Molecular diffusion only | Vigorous eddy mixing |
| Reynolds Number | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Shear stress | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau = (\mu + \rho\epsilon) \dfrac{du}{dy}$ |
| Velocity fluctuation | Zero ($u' = 0$) | Non-zero ($u' \neq 0$) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Turbulent Flow. What is the critical Reynolds number for pipe flow?** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Turbulent Flow Definition:** A flow regime in which fluid particles move in irregular, chaotic paths with random fluctuations superimposed on the mean motion, resulting in intense mixing and high energy dissipation. The instantaneous velocity at any point varies with time about a time-averaged mean value.

The instantaneous velocity is decomposed as:
$$u = \bar{u} + u'$$
where $\bar{u}$ is the time-averaged velocity and $u'$ is the fluctuating component.

**Critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow:** $Re_c = \mathbf{2300}$ (lower limit for transition).

- For $Re < 2000$: Laminar
- For $2000 < Re < 4000$: Transitional
- For $Re > 4000$: Turbulent

---

### **Question 2: What is Reynolds Stress? Why is it not a true fluid property?** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reynolds Stress** is the additional apparent shear stress that arises in turbulent flow due to momentum transfer by fluctuating eddies, defined mathematically as:
> $$\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$$

**Why NOT a true fluid property:**

1. Unlike dynamic viscosity $\mu$, Reynolds stress **depends on the flow conditions** (velocity, geometry, turbulence intensity)
2. It arises from the **mathematical time-averaging process** applied to the Navier-Stokes equations
3. It is proportional to **fluid density** $\rho$, not viscosity $\mu$
4. Its magnitude varies with position in the flow field (unlike constant $\mu$)
5. It vanishes identically in laminar flow

Hence, Reynolds stress is a **flow property**, not a thermodynamic/physical property of the fluid substance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: Derive Prandtl's Mixing Length Theory. Obtain the velocity distribution equation for turbulent flow in a smooth pipe.** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

#### **Part (a): Derive Prandtl's Mixing Length Formula [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Physical Hypothesis**

Prandtl hypothesized that a fluid lump moves a transverse distance $\ell$ (mixing length) before mixing with the surrounding fluid, retaining its original momentum.

**Step 2: Express Fluctuating Velocity**

The fluctuation in $x$-velocity is the difference in mean velocity over the mixing length:

$$u' = \bar{u}(y + \ell) - \bar{u}(y)$$

**Step 3: Taylor Series Expansion**

$$\bar{u}(y + \ell) = \bar{u}(y) + \ell \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} + \frac{\ell^2}{2!} \frac{d^2\bar{u}}{dy^2} + \ldots$$

Neglecting higher-order terms:

$$u' \approx \ell \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$$

**Step 4: Transverse Fluctuation**

The lateral fluctuating velocity $v'$ is of the same order:

$$v' \sim \ell \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right|$$

**Step 5: Substitute into Reynolds Stress**

$$\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'} = -\rho \overline{\ell \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \cdot \ell \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right|}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_t = \rho \ell^2 \left| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right| \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}}$$

**Step 6: Define Eddy Viscosity**

Defining $\eta = \rho \ell^2 \left| \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right|$ (eddy viscosity), we have $\tau_t = \eta \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$.

---

#### **Part (b): Derive Velocity Distribution for Smooth Pipe [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Assumption on Mixing Length**

For turbulent flow near a smooth wall:

$$\ell = \kappa y \quad \text{where} \quad \kappa = 0.4 \text{ (von Kármán constant)}$$

**Step 2: Constant Total Shear Stress**

For fully developed turbulent flow:

$$\tau_0 = \rho \kappa^2 y^2 \left( \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right)^2$$

**Step 3: Solve for Velocity Gradient**

$$\frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} = \frac{1}{\kappa y} \sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho}} = \frac{u_*}{\kappa y}$$

where $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho}$ is the **shear velocity**.

**Step 4: Integrate**

$$\int_0^{\bar{u}} d\bar{u} = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \int_0^y \frac{dy}{y} = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln y + C$$

**Step 5: Apply Experimental Boundary Condition**

For a smooth pipe, integrating the constant of motion using Nikuradse's experimental data:

$$\frac{\bar{u}}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \frac{u_* y}{\nu} \right) + 5.5$$

This is the **Prandtl Universal Velocity Distribution for Smooth Pipes**.

**Step 6: Key Observations**

- Velocity varies **logarithmically** with $y$ (not parabolically)
- The profile is much **flatter** than laminar (parabolic) profile
- The factor **2.5** = $1/\kappa$ where $\kappa = 0.4$

---

### **Question B: Explain Reynolds decomposition. Starting from the Navier-Stokes equation, derive the expression for Reynolds stress in turbulent flow.** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

#### **Part (a): Reynolds Decomposition and Mathematical Setup [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of Reynolds Decomposition**

Any instantaneous flow property is expressed as the sum of a time-averaged mean and a fluctuating component:

$$u = \bar{u} + u', \quad v = \bar{v} + v', \quad w = \bar{w} + w', \quad p = \bar{p} + p'$$

where the time average is defined as:

$$\bar{u} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} u(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2: Properties of Fluctuations**

$$\overline{u'} = 0, \quad \overline{v'} = 0, \quad \overline{w'} = 0$$

$$\overline{\bar{u} u'} = \bar{u} \cdot \overline{u'} = 0$$

**Step 3: Time-Averaging Rules**

$$\overline{\phi + \psi} = \bar{\phi} + \bar{\psi}$$
$$\overline{\bar{\phi} \psi'} = \bar{\phi} \cdot \overline{\psi'} = 0$$
$$\overline{\phi' \psi'} \neq 0 \quad \text{(generally)}$$

**Step 4: Navier-Stokes Equation in $x$-direction**

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x$$

**Step 5: Substitute Decomposition**

Replace all variables with $\phi = \bar{\phi} + \phi'$ and apply time averaging. The nonlinear convective terms produce products of fluctuations.

---

#### **Part (b): Derivation of Reynolds Stress [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Time-Average the $x$-momentum equation**

$$\rho \frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial t} + \rho \left( \bar{u} \frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial x} + \bar{v} \frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial y} + \bar{w} \frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 \bar{u} + \rho g_x - \rho \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u'u'}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \overline{u'v'}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \overline{u'w'}}{\partial z} \right)$$

**Step 2: Identify the Reynolds Stress Tensor**

The terms involving products of fluctuations are the **Reynolds stresses**:

$$\tau_{xx} = -\rho \overline{u'^2}, \quad \tau_{xy} = -\rho \overline{u'v'}, \quad \tau_{xz} = -\rho \overline{u'w'}$$

**Step 3: Dominant Shear Component**

For boundary layer-type flows where $\partial/\partial y$ dominates:

$$\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$$

**Step 4: Physical Interpretation**

This term represents **momentum flux across a plane** due to turbulent eddies:

- $v'$ = velocity carrying fluid across the plane
- $u'$ = $x$-momentum being transported

**Step 5: Final Result**

$$\boxed{\tau_{total} = \mu \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} - \rho \overline{u'v'}}$$

The first term is **viscous stress** (molecular), and the second is **Reynolds (turbulent) stress** (eddy transport). The negative sign appears because downward-moving fluid ($v' < 0$) typically carries higher $x$-momentum (so $u' > 0$), making $\overline{u'v'} < 0$ and $-\rho\overline{u'v'} > 0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often write $\tau_t = \rho \overline{u'v'}$ **without the negative sign**. The correct expression is $\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$. **Loses 1-2 marks.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing Reynolds Stress with Viscous Stress:** Reynolds stress is **NOT** a property of the fluid. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. State explicitly that $\tau_t$ depends on flow conditions, not on fluid composition.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Time-Averaging Step:** The Reynolds stress appears ONLY after time-averaging nonlinear terms. Students who skip the time-averaging step (or claim it appears "by definition") lose marks.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Constants in Velocity Distribution:** The constant **2.5** (= $1/\kappa$) and **5.5** / **8.5** are empirical — do NOT derive them from first principles. State "from experimental fitting" clearly.
> 
> 5. **Mixing Length vs. Mean Free Path:** Students confuse Prandtl's mixing length with molecular mean free path. While analogous, mixing length is **much larger** (mm to cm scale, not nm).
> 
> 6. **Units of Reynolds Stress:** Always state $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$. Students sometimes write units of viscosity by mistake.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting to specify smooth vs. rough pipe:** The velocity distribution formula depends on pipe type. Examiners WILL check this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Turbulence**

**Core Concepts:**
- Turbulence = irregular, chaotic fluid motion with random fluctuations
- Reynolds number $Re = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ governs flow regime
- $Re < 2000$ (Laminar), $Re > 4000$ (Turbulent), $2000 < Re < 4000$ (Transitional)
- Critical Reynolds number for pipes: $Re_c = 2300$

**Mathematical Tools:**
- Reynolds decomposition: $u = \bar{u} + u'$ with $\overline{u'} = 0$
- Time average: $\bar{u} = \dfrac{1}{T} \displaystyle\int_0^T u \, dt$
- Reynolds stress: $\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$ (NOT a fluid property)
- Total shear stress: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy} - \rho \overline{u'v'}$

**Prandtl Mixing Length Theory:**
- $\tau_t = \rho \ell^2 \left\vert \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right\vert \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$
- Eddy viscosity: $\epsilon = \ell^2 \left\vert \dfrac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right\vert$
- $\ell = \kappa y$ near walls (von Kármán $\kappa = 0.4$)

**Velocity Distribution:**
- Smooth pipe: $\dfrac{u}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \dfrac{u_* y}{\nu} \right) + 5.5$
- Rough pipe: $\dfrac{u}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left( \dfrac{y}{k_s} \right) + 8.5$
- Logarithmic profile — FLATTER than laminar parabolic profile
- Shear velocity: $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho}$

**Comparison Essentials:**
- Laminar: $V_{max} = 2V_{mean}$ (parabolic)
- Turbulent: $V_{max} \approx 1.2 V_{mean}$ (nearly flat)
- Friction factor laminar: $f = 64/Re$
- Friction factor turbulent: from Moody chart / Colebrook equation

**Frequently Asked Exam Topics:**
- Derivation of Reynolds stress (very high frequency)
- Derivation of Prandtl mixing length formula
- Velocity distribution in smooth AND rough pipes
- Difference between Reynolds stress and viscous stress
- Eddy viscosity concept
- Significance of critical Reynolds number
- Nikuradse's experiments (smooth, transitional, fully rough)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Stress** is defined as the additional apparent stress tensor that emerges in a turbulent fluid flow when the instantaneous Navier–Stokes equations are subjected to a **Reynolds time-averaging** procedure. Mathematically, it is the correlation between fluctuating velocity components, expressed as:
>
> $$\tau_{ij}^{R} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$$

In the simplest one-dimensional shear flow form, the **Reynolds shear stress** is given by:

$$\tau_{t} = -\rho \overline{u' v'}$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $u', v'$ are the fluctuating components of velocity in the stream-wise and transverse directions, respectively. The overbar denotes the **time-averaged** value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Significance**: Unlike the viscous stress (which arises from molecular momentum diffusion), Reynolds stress is a **macro-scale momentum transfer** caused by the random, chaotic motion of fluid parcels (eddies) characteristic of turbulence.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **busy pedestrian crosswalk at a traffic signal**:

- **Laminar flow analogy**: People walking in a disciplined, single-file line — everyone moves with a uniform velocity. No collisions, no side-to-side interactions. This is pure *viscous* flow.

- **Turbulent flow analogy**: When the signal turns green, pedestrians surge forward chaotically. People bump into each other, jostle sideways, and *transfer momentum* across lanes. A person walking fast in one lane "drags" or "pushes" the slower walker in the next lane, even though they never physically touched in the molecular sense.

This **lateral momentum transfer by turbulent eddies** is precisely what Reynolds stress represents. It is *not* a real surface force, but an **apparent stress** required to make the time-averaged equations mathematically consistent.

> [!TIP]
> **For exam writing**: Always remember — Reynolds stress is an *apparent* (not real viscous) stress that exists **only** in turbulent flow and vanishes identically in laminar flow because $u' = v' = 0$.

---

### 1.3 Key Notation & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $u, v, w$ | Instantaneous velocity components | m/s |
| $\overline{u}, \overline{v}, \overline{w}$ | Time-averaged velocity components | m/s |
| $u', v', w'$ | Fluctuating velocity components | m/s |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density (typically **1000 kg/m³** for water, **1.225 kg/m³** for air) | kg/m³ |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity | Pa·s |
| $\tau_{ij}^{R}$ | Reynolds stress tensor | Pa (N/m²) |
| $\varepsilon_{m}$ | Kinematic eddy viscosity | m²/s |

---

### 1.4 Prerequisites Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> Before diving deeper, ensure fluency with the following:
> 1. **Reynolds Decomposition**: $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$
> 2. **Time-Averaging Rules**: $\overline{\overline{u_i}} = \overline{u_i}$ and $\overline{u_i'} = 0$
> 3. **Product Rule**: $\overline{u_i v_j} = \overline{u_i}\overline{v_j} + \overline{u_i' v_j'}$
> 4. **Navier–Stokes Equations** for incompressible flow
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

#### **Step 1: Reynolds Decomposition**

The instantaneous velocity and pressure are split into mean and fluctuating parts:

$$u = \overline{u} + u' \quad ; \quad v = \overline{v} + v' \quad ; \quad p = \overline{p} + p'$$

By the definition of time averaging:

$$\overline{u'} = 0 \quad ; \quad \overline{v'} = 0 \quad ; \quad \overline{p'} = 0$$

---

#### **Step 2: Why Reynolds Stress Arises — The "Why" Behind It**

The Navier–Stokes momentum equation contains the **non-linear convective term** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$. When we substitute the decomposed velocity:

$$(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \big[(\overline{\vec{V}} + \vec{V'}) \cdot \nabla\big](\overline{\vec{V}} + \vec{V'})$$

Expanding and time-averging, **three types of terms** appear:

| Term | Result After Averaging | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $(\overline{\vec{V}} \cdot \nabla)\overline{\vec{V}}$ | Mean convection | Bulk flow transport |
| $(\overline{\vec{V}} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V'}$ | $\overline{(\overline{\vec{V}} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V'}} = 0$ | Vanishes (since $\overline{V'}=0$) | Mean advection of fluctuation |
| $(\vec{V'} \cdot \nabla)\overline{\vec{V}}$ | $\overline{(\vec{V'} \cdot \nabla)\overline{\vec{V}}} = 0$ | Vanishes | Fluctuation advecting mean |
| **$(\vec{V'} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V'}$** | $\overline{(\vec{V'} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V'}} \neq 0$ | **Reynolds Stress Term** | Eddy momentum transfer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The critical insight**: The non-linear product of two fluctuating quantities, when averaged, does **NOT** vanish. This is the mathematical root of the closure problem of turbulence.

---

#### **Step 3: Reynolds Stress Tensor Components**

For a 3D turbulent flow, the symmetric Reynolds stress tensor is:

$$\tau^{R} = \begin{bmatrix} -\rho \overline{u'^2} & -\rho \overline{u'v'} & -\rho \overline{u'w'} \\ -\rho \overline{u'v'} & -\rho \overline{v'^2} & -\rho \overline{v'w'} \\ -\rho \overline{u'w'} & -\rho \overline{v'w'} & -\rho \overline{w'^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

* **Normal stresses**: $-\rho \overline{u'^2}, -\rho \overline{v'^2}, -\rho \overline{w'^2}$ (always negative)
* **Shear stresses**: $-\rho \overline{u'v'}, -\rho \overline{u'w'}, -\rho \overline{v'w'}$

---

#### **Step 4: Boussinesq's Eddy Viscosity Hypothesis (1877)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Hypothesis**: Reynolds stress can be modelled analogously to viscous stress, using an "eddy viscosity" $\mu_t$:
>
> $$-\rho \overline{u'v'} = \mu_{t} \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y}$$

where $\mu_t$ is the **eddy (turbulent) viscosity**, NOT a fluid property but a **flow property** depending on turbulence intensity.

**Limitation**: Fails for flows with strong streamline curvature, separation, or strong pressure gradients.

---

#### **Step 5: Prandtl's Mixing Length Theory (1925)**

Prandtl postulated a "mixing length" $l_m$ analogous to the mean free path in kinetic theory. The fluctuating velocity is proportional to the mean velocity gradient over this distance:

$$|u'| \approx l_{m} \left| \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} \right|$$

Hence:

$$-\rho \overline{u'v'} = \rho l_{m}^{2} \left| \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} \right| \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y}$$

Near a solid wall: $l_m = \kappa y$, where $\kappa \approx 0.4$ is the **von Kármán constant**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Examination**:

| # | Formula | Description / Context | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $u = \overline{u} + u'$ | Reynolds Decomposition | m/s |
| 2 | $\tau_{ij}^{R} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | Reynolds Stress Tensor (general) | Pa |
| 3 | $\tau_{t} = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$ | Reynolds Shear Stress | Pa |
| 4 | $-\rho \overline{u'v'} = \mu_t \dfrac{d\overline{u}}{dy}$ | Boussinesq Eddy Viscosity Hypothesis | Pa |
| 5 | $\mu_{t} = \rho \varepsilon_{m}$ | Eddy Viscosity (relation) | Pa·s |
| 6 | $-\rho \overline{u'v'} = \rho l_{m}^{2} \left\vert \dfrac{d\overline{u}}{dy} \right\vert \dfrac{d\overline{u}}{dy}$ | Prandtl Mixing Length | Pa |
| 7 | $l_m = \kappa y$ | Mixing Length near wall ($\kappa = 0.4$) | m |
| 8 | $u^{+} = \dfrac{\overline{u}}{u_{*}}, \quad y^{+} = \dfrac{y u_{*}}{\nu}$ | Inner Wall Coordinates (dimensionless) | — |
| 9 | $u^{+} = \dfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y^{+}) + 5.0$ | Logarithmic Law of the Wall | — |
| 10 | $u^{+} = 8.74 (y^{+})^{1/7}$ | 1/7th Power Law (smooth pipe) | — |
| 11 | $\tau_{total} = \mu \dfrac{d\overline{u}}{dy} - \rho \overline{u'v'}$ | Total Shear Stress (viscous + Reynolds) | Pa |
| 12 | $\tau_{total} = (\mu + \mu_t) \dfrac{d\overline{u}}{dy}$ | Total Shear (eddy viscosity form) | Pa |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Aerospace**: Aircraft wing design — predicting boundary layer separation and stall requires accurate Reynolds stress modelling.
* **Piping Systems**: Friction factor in turbulent pipe flow ($f \propto \tau_w$) directly depends on Reynolds stress at the wall.
* **Hydraulic Engineering**: Open channel flows, spillways, and dam outflows are predominantly turbulent.
* **Chemical Reactors**: Mixing efficiency and heat/mass transfer rates depend on turbulent eddy transport.
* **Meteorology & Oceanography**: Atmospheric and oceanic circulation models rely on Reynolds stress parameterisations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Reynolds Stress Tensor from Navier–Stokes Equations

**Starting Equation**: The instantaneous x-momentum equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x$$

---

**Step 1**: Apply **Reynolds Decomposition** to all dependent variables:

$$u = \overline{u} + u' \quad ; \quad v = \overline{v} + v' \quad ; \quad w = \overline{w} + w' \quad ; \quad p = \overline{p} + p'$$

---

**Step 2**: Substitute into the x-momentum equation and apply the time-averaging operator $\overline{(\cdot)}$:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial t} + \overline{u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}} + \overline{v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}} + \overline{w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}} \right) = -\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 \overline{u} + \rho g_x$$

---

**Step 3**: Expand the convective term using Reynolds decomposition for the product $u \cdot u$:

$$\overline{u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}} = \overline{(\overline{u}+u') \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\overline{u}+u')}$$

$$= \overline{\overline{u}\frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial x} + \overline{u}\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x} + u'\frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial x} + u'\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x}}$$

---

**Step 4**: Apply time-averaging rules ($\overline{\overline{u}\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x}} = \overline{u} \cdot \overline{\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x}} = 0$ because $\overline{u'} = 0$):

$$\overline{u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}} = \overline{u}\frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial x} + \overline{u'\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x}}$$

Using continuity for incompressible flow, $\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v'}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w'}{\partial z} = 0$, the second term simplifies:

$$\overline{u'\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\overline{u'^2}$$

---

**Step 5**: Similarly expand $\overline{v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}}$:

$$\overline{v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}} = \overline{v}\frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} + \overline{v'\frac{\partial u'}{\partial y}} = \overline{v}\frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\overline{u'v'}$$

(The last identity uses continuity: $\overline{v'\frac{\partial u'}{\partial y}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\overline{u'v'} - \overline{u'\frac{\partial v'}{\partial y}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\overline{u'v'}$)

---

**Step 6**: Combine all terms back into the x-momentum equation:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial t} + \overline{u}\frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial x} + \overline{v}\frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} + \overline{w}\frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 \overline{u} + \rho g_x + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-\rho \overline{u'^2}) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-\rho \overline{u'v'}) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(-\rho \overline{u'w'})$$

---

**Step 7**: Identify the **Reynolds Stress Terms**:

$$\boxed{\rho \frac{D\overline{u}}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 \overline{u} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\rho \overline{u'^2}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\rho \overline{u'v'}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(-\rho \overline{u'w'}\right) + \rho g_x}$$

This is the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) Equation**. The three extra terms containing $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ are the **divergence of the Reynolds stress tensor** — mathematically, the gradient of apparent turbulent stresses.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Prandtl's Mixing Length Formula

**Premise**: Consider a fluid particle that travels a vertical distance $l_m$ (mixing length) before mixing with the surrounding fluid, transferring its x-momentum.

**Step 1**: The fluctuating velocity component $u'$ arises because the particle arrives at a new location with a velocity different from the local mean:

$$u' \approx l_{m} \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y}$$

---

**Step 2**: Similarly, the transverse fluctuation:

$$v' \approx u' \approx l_{m} \left| \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} \right|$$

---

**Step 3**: Hence, the product:

$$\overline{u'v'} = -l_{m}^{2} \left| \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} \right| \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y}$$

(The negative sign because, if $\partial \overline{u}/\partial y > 0$, a particle moving up — positive $v'$ — arrives with lower $u$, giving negative $u'$.)

---

**Step 4**: Multiply by $-\rho$:

$$-\rho \overline{u'v'} = \rho l_{m}^{2} \left| \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} \right| \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y}$$

Near a wall, $l_m = \kappa y$:

$$\boxed{\tau_{t} = -\rho \overline{u'v'} = \rho \kappa^{2} y^{2} \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u}}{\partial y} \right)^{2}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Total Shear Stress in Turbulent Pipe Flow

**Step 1**: Write total (viscous + turbulent) shear stress:

$$\tau_{total} = \mu \frac{d\overline{u}}{dy} - \rho \overline{u'v'}$$

---

**Step 2**: For fully-developed turbulent pipe flow, apply a **force balance** on a fluid cylinder of radius $r$:

$$\tau_{total}(r) \cdot 2\pi r L = \Delta p \cdot \pi r^2$$

$$\tau_{total}(r) = \frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L}$$

---

**Step 3**: At the wall ($r = R$):

$$\tau_{w} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot R}{2L}$$

---

**Step 4**: Combining the two relations:

$$\tau_{total}(r) = \tau_{w} \cdot \frac{r}{R} = \tau_{w} \left(1 - \frac{y}{R}\right)$$

(where $y = R - r$ is the distance from the wall)

---

**Step 5**: At the wall, the viscous component dominates because $y = 0$:

$$\tau_{w} = \mu \left. \frac{d\overline{u}}{dy} \right|_{y=0}$$

This yields the **law of the wall** and the **friction velocity** $u_{*} = \sqrt{\tau_{w}/\rho}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Feature | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Velocity fluctuations | Absent ($u' = v' = w' = 0$) | Present and random |
| Reynolds stress | $\tau_{ij}^{R} = 0$ | $\tau_{ij}^{R} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} \neq 0$ |
| Dominant stress | Viscous ($\mu \, d\overline{u}/dy$) | Reynolds ($-\rho \overline{u'v'}$) + viscous |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic ($u/U = 1 - (r/R)^2$) | Flatter (power law / log law) |
| Wall shear stress | $\tau_w = 8\mu U/D$ | $\tau_w = f \cdot \rho U^2 / 8$ |
| Mixing | Molecular diffusion only | Eddy diffusion dominates |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: Reynolds Decomposition Concept Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Instantaneous Velocity Field]:::startNode --> B[Reynolds Decomposition Operator]
    B --> C[Mean Component U Bar]
    B --> D[Fluctuating Component U Prime]

    F --> H[Correlation u prime v prime]

    J --> G
    G --> K[Closure Problem]

    N --> O

```

```mermaid
    subgraph TENSOR[Reynolds Stress Tensor Tau R]

    end

    NORMAL[Normal Stresses Diagonal Block]:::normalStyle

    T11 --> NORMAL

```

### 4.3 Diagram: Stress Partition in Turbulent Boundary Layer

```mermaid
graph TD
    FLOW[Turbulent Flow Over Flat Plate]:::flowNode
    FLOW --> VISC[Viscous Stress mu dU by dy]
    FLOW --> REYN[Reynolds Stress minus rho u prime v prime]

    VISC --> DOM_V[Dominant in Viscous Sublayer y plus less than 5]
    REYN --> DOM_R[Dominant in Log Layer y plus greater than 30]

    DOM_V --> LAW1[Linear Law of the Wall]
    DOM_R --> LAW2[Logarithmic Law of the Wall]

    LAW1 --> BUFF[Buffer Layer Transition Region]
    LAW2 --> BUFF

    BUFF --> TOTAL[Total Shear Stress mu plus mu t times dU by dy]

    TOTAL --> EWALL[Wall Shear Stress Tau Wall]
    EWALL --> FRIC[Friction Factor and Drag Prediction]

```

### 4.4 Diagram: Closure Problem Hierarchy

```mermaid
    RANS --> EXTRA[Extra Reynolds Stress Terms Appear]
    EXTRA --> CLOS[Closure Problem Six Unknown Correlations]
    CLOS --> L0[Zero Equation Algebraic Models]
    CLOS --> L1[One Equation Models]
    CLOS --> L2[Two Equation k epsilon Models]

    L0 --> BOUSS[Boussinesq Hypothesis mu t]
    L0 --> PRAND[Prandtl Mixing Length l m]
    L1 --> KEL[Kolmogorov k equation]
    L2 --> STD[Standard k epsilon by Launder and Spalding]

    BOUSS --> APPL[Industrial CFD Applications]
    PRAND --> APPL
    KEL --> APPL
    STD --> APPL

```
