Imagine standing in a swimming pool with a hand-held cup, opening facing the oncoming current of a wave. The water level inside the cup will rise higher than the still water around you. The extra height is precisely the *velocity head* of the incoming water — converted to a pressure head at the cup's mouth, which is a stagnation point.

- The **Pitot tube** is that single cup — it only tells you the *total* pressure of the oncoming stream.
- The **Pitot-static tube** carries a *second* set of tiny holes on its side, which act like the calm, undisturbed water level outside the cup. By subtracting the two pressures, the **purely dynamic** portion is isolated, giving velocity directly.

In real engineering: Pitot-static tubes are mounted on aircraft noses (to measure **airspeed**), on the side of ships, and in open-channel and pipe-flow laboratories.

## 1.3 Important Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Remarks |
|----------|--------|---------|---------|
| Stagnation pressure | $p_0$ | Pa (N/m²) | Pressure at point where flow is brought to rest |
| Static pressure | $p$ | Pa | Thermodynamic pressure of the moving fluid |
| Dynamic pressure | $q$ | Pa | $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ |
| Fluid density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | **997 kg/m³** for water at 20°C; **1.225 kg/m³** for air at 15°C |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | m/s² | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Coefficient of Pitot tube | $C_v$ | dimensionless | Typically **0.98 – 1.00** for well-designed tubes |
| Manometric fluid specific gravity | $S_m$ | dimensionless | Mercury $S_m = 13.6$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **ideal, frictionless Pitot tube** the coefficient $C_v = 1.0$. In practice, due to streamline separation and minor losses, $C_v < 1$. KTU problems frequently specify the value (e.g., $C_v = 0.98$) or ask the student to *assume ideal conditions* unless stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The working of both instruments is anchored in the **energy conservation principle** of an inviscid, incompressible, steady flow — i.e., **Bernoulli's Equation**.

**Step 1 — Identify the two relevant points.**
- Point 1: A point in the **undisturbed free stream** far upstream, where velocity is $v$ and pressure is $p$.
- Point 2: The **stagnation point** at the tube mouth, where velocity is brought to rest ($v_2 = 0$) and pressure rises to $p_0$.

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation along the same streamline (z₁ = z₂).**

$$p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = p_0$$

This tells us: the **stagnation pressure** is the static pressure plus the **dynamic pressure**.

**Step 3 — Isolate the dynamic pressure.**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = p_0 - p$$

**Step 4 — Solve for velocity.**

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 5 — For the Pitot tube (single-port).**
The static pressure $p$ must be measured **separately** by a *piezometer* or *static pressure tap* on the pipe wall. The Pitot tube itself only gives $p_0$.

**Step 6 — For the Pitot-static tube (dual-port).**
Both $p_0$ and $p$ are measured simultaneously by the *same instrument* and connected to a *differential manometer*. The reading directly yields $(p_0 - p)$.

**Step 7 — Convert manometer reading $h$ to pressure difference.**
For a U-tube differential manometer with manometric fluid of specific gravity $S_m$ in a flowing fluid of specific gravity $S$:

$$p_0 - p = (\rho_m - \rho)\,g\,h = \rho\,g\,h\,(S_m - 1)$$

Substituting:

$$v = C_v\sqrt{2gh\!\left(\dfrac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)}$$

**Step 8 — Engineering reality check.**
Multiply by the empirical coefficient $C_v$ to account for non-ideal effects such as streamline separation, turbulence, and viscous losses.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the side holes must be on a perfectly cylindrical section and at a small diameter:** Any burr, weld bead, or sharp edge near the static holes will cause local streamline curvature, biasing the static pressure reading. This is why high-quality Pitot-static tubes are machined to within a few micrometres of geometric perfection.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units | Used For |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Bernoulli stagnation relation | $p_0 = p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Pa | Linking total & static pressures |
| 2 | Velocity from pressure difference | $v = \sqrt{2(p_0-p)/\rho}$ | m/s | Ideal Pitot (no coefficient) |
| 3 | Velocity with empirical coefficient | $v = C_v\sqrt{2(p_0-p)/\rho}$ | m/s | Real Pitot-static |
| 4 | Velocity from piezometer head | $v = C_v\sqrt{2gh}$ | m/s | When only $p_0$ is measured (water) |
| 5 | Velocity from U-tube manometer | $v = C_v\sqrt{2gh(S_m - 1)}$ | m/s | Differential manometer, same fluid |
| 6 | Velocity from U-tube manometer (general) | $v = C_v\sqrt{2gh(S_m/S - 1)}$ | m/s | Differential manometer, general case |
| 7 | Velocity from inclined manometer | $v = C_v\sqrt{2g\,L\,\sin\theta\,(S_m/S - 1)}$ | m/s | Inclined gauge reading $L$ at angle $\theta$ |
| 8 | Dynamic pressure head | $h_v = v^2/(2g)$ | m | Velocity head |
| 9 | Discharge in a pipe from pitot traverse | $Q = \dfrac{A}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sqrt{v_i^2}$ | m³/s | Mean velocity from multi-point traverse |
| 10 | Airspeed in compressible flow (subsonic) | $v = \sqrt{2(p_0 - p)/\rho}$ | m/s | Valid when $M < 0.3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** Almost every KTU question of this type asks for *velocity* or *discharge*. The answer should always be in **m/s** for velocity and **m³/s** (or **L/s**) for discharge. Watch the units of $h$ — it must be in **metres**, not cm or mm.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace:** Air-data booms and aircraft nose Pitot tubes measure true airspeed and Mach number.
- **Hydraulic engineering:** Stream gauging in rivers and open channels.
- **Wind tunnels:** Local velocity profiling.
- **Power plants:** Boiler draft measurement and stack gas velocity.
- **Ventilation & HVAC:** Duct flow measurement.
- **Marine navigation:** Speed logs of ships use Pitot-static principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Velocity from a Pitot-Static Tube with Differential Manometer

### Setup

Consider a Pitot-static tube immersed in a fluid (specific gravity $S$, density $\rho = S \cdot 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) flowing with velocity $v$. The two pressures are connected to a U-tube manometer containing a heavier manometric fluid of specific gravity $S_m$ and density $\rho_m = S_m \cdot 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The differential manometer reading is $h$ (metres).

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between the free stream (point 1) and the stagnation point (point 2).**

Both points lie at approximately the same elevation, and the streamline connecting them is part of the same flow. For an inviscid, incompressible, steady flow:

$$p_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = p_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known quantities.**

Let $p_1 = p$ (static pressure), $v_1 = v$ (free-stream velocity), $z_1 = z_2$ (same elevation). At the stagnation point, $v_2 = 0$ and $p_2 = p_0$ (stagnation pressure).

$$p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g z = p_0 + 0 + \rho g z$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the elevation terms and rearrange.**

The $\rho g z$ terms cancel because the elevation does not change between the two points:

$$p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = p_0$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = p_0 - p$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the velocity $v$.**

$$v^2 = \dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}}$$

This is the **ideal, frictionless** result.

**Step 5 — Introduce the empirical Pitot-tube coefficient $C_v$.**

In practice, streamline curvature near the nose and viscous effects cause the actual stagnation pressure to be slightly less than the ideal. The coefficient accounts for this:

$$v = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}}$$

For well-designed Pitot-static tubes, $C_v$ lies between **0.98 and 1.00**.

**Step 6 — Express $(p_0 - p)$ in terms of the manometer reading $h$.**

Manometer equation by balancing pressures at the lowest common level of the manometric fluid:

$$p_0 + \rho g (h + y) - \rho_m g h - \rho g y = p$$

where $y$ is the height of the lighter fluid above the lower manometric-fluid surface on the high-pressure side. Simplifying:

$$p_0 - p = (\rho_m - \rho) g h$$

**Step 7 — Substitute in terms of specific gravities.**

Since $\rho_m = S_m \cdot \rho_w$ and $\rho = S \cdot \rho_w$ (with $\rho_w$ the density of water):

$$p_0 - p = \rho_w g h (S_m - S)$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back into the velocity equation.**

$$v = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \rho_w g h (S_m - S)}{S \cdot \rho_w}}$$

$$v = C_v \sqrt{2 g h \!\left(\dfrac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)}$$

This is the **final KTU-standard formula** for the velocity using a Pitot-static tube with a U-tube differential manometer.

### Quick-Reference Derivation Block

$$
\begin{aligned}
p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 &= p_0 \quad &\text{(Bernoulli, same elevation)} \\[4pt]
\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 &= p_0 - p \quad &\text{(rearrange)} \\[4pt]
v &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0-p)}{\rho}} \quad &\text{(ideal velocity)} \\[4pt]
p_0 - p &= (\rho_m - \rho)gh \quad &\text{(manometer balance)} \\[4pt]
p_0 - p &= \rho g h (S_m - 1) \quad &\text{(if flowing fluid is water, } S = 1) \\[4pt]
v &= C_v \sqrt{2gh (S_m - 1)} \quad &\text{(final, water as flowing fluid)} \\[4pt]
v &= C_v \sqrt{2gh (S_m/S - 1)} \quad &\text{(final, general flowing fluid)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]:**
A Pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of water flowing in a pipe. The differential manometer using mercury shows a deflection of $h = 100$ mm. The Pitot-tube coefficient is $C_v = 0.98$. Find:
- (a) The velocity of flow at the tube location.
- (b) The discharge if the pipe diameter is **200 mm**.
- Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $S_m$ (mercury) $= 13.6$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Velocity:**

The flowing fluid is water ($S = 1$); the manometric fluid is mercury ($S_m = 13.6$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= C_v \sqrt{2gh\!\left(\dfrac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.100 \times (13.6 - 1)} \quad \text{(converted } h = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m)} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.100 \times 12.6} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times \sqrt{24.7212} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times 4.9721 \\[4pt]
&\approx 4.873 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 4.87 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Part (b) — Discharge:**

Pipe area $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.200)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$.

$$Q = A \cdot v = 0.03142 \times 4.873 \approx 0.1531 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 153.1 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pitot-Static Tube System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Free Stream Flow\nVelocity v, Pressure p"] -->|Kinetic + Pressure Energy| nodeB["Stagnation Port\nInner Tube Mouth"]
    nodeB -->|Pressure p0| nodeC["Pressure Tap A\nTotal Head Line"]
    nodeA -->|Static Pressure p| nodeD["Static Ports\nSide Holes Outer Tube"]
    nodeD -->|Pressure p| nodeE["Pressure Tap B\nStatic Head Line"]
    nodeC -->|Transmit p0| nodeF["Differential Manometer\nLimb 1"]
    nodeE -->|Transmit p| nodeG["Differential Manometer\nLimb 2"]
    nodeF -->|Pressure Difference| nodeH["Manometer Reading\nh metres"]
    nodeG -->|Pressure Difference| nodeH
    nodeH -->|Substitute in v eqn| nodeI["Velocity\nv = Cv sqrt 2gh Sm by S minus 1"]
    nodeI -->|Multiply by Area A| nodeJ["Discharge\nQ = A v"]
    nodeA -->|Calibration factor| nodeK["Cv Coefficient\n0.98 to 1.00"]
    nodeK --> nodeI
```

## 4.2 Functional Component Description (Block Flow Map)

| Block | Function | Energy Domain |
|-------|----------|---------------|
| **Free Stream Flow** | Undisturbed fluid moving with velocity $v$ | Kinetic + Pressure |
| **Stagnation Port** | Brings flow to rest via nose geometry; raises pressure to $p_0$ | Converts KE → PE |
| **Static Ports** | Sense true static pressure $p$ of undisturbed flow | Pressure only |
| **Manometer Limbs** | Two separate hydraulic columns connected to $p_0$ and $p$ | Pressure transmission |
| **Differential Reading** | Height difference $h$ is proportional to $(p_0 - p)$ | Mechanical displacement |
| **Velocity Block** | Applies $v = C_v\sqrt{2gh(S_m/S-1)}$ | Analytical |
| **Discharge Block** | Multiplies $v$ by pipe cross-section $A$ | Flow rate |

## 4.3 Pressure Distribution at the Pitot-Static Tube (Schematic ASCII)

```
            Incoming Streamlines (curved around nose)
                   → → → →  →
                  ↗          ↘
                ↗              ↘
              ↗                  ↘
            ↗   ┌───────────┐    ↘
   p,p,v →  →  │  Stagnation│  →  →   p (static holes read this)
                │   point    │
                └─────┬─────┘
                      │ inner tube
                      ▼
                 [ p₀  lead  ]  ───────────┐
                                            │    to manometer
                 [ p    lead  ]  ───────────┘    limb 1 & 2
                      ▲
                      │ annulus (outer tube)
                  (side holes)
```

## 4.4 Pressure Profile Around the Pitot Nose

| Location on Pitot Body | Streamline Behaviour | Pressure Read |
|-----------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Nose tip (stagnation) | $v = 0$ | $p_0 = p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ (max) |
| Side cylindrical section | Parallel streamlines, undisturbed $v$ | $p$ (true static) |
| Rear (downstream) | Wake region with eddies | Not used (avoid here) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we don't place the static holes at the rear:** The wake behind the tube contains turbulent eddies and separated flow, so the local static pressure fluctuates and is not representative of the undisturbed flow. The cylindrical shoulder region is the only place where streamlines remain parallel and undisturbed.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a Pitot tube and a Pitot-static tube.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pitot Tube | Pitot-Static Tube |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| No. of pressure ports | One (stagnation only) | Two (stagnation + static) |
| Pressure measured | Total / stagnation head $p_0$ | Both $p_0$ and $p$ simultaneously |
| Static pressure needed from elsewhere? | Yes — separate piezometer/tap | No — measured by the same instrument |
| Construction | Simple bent tube | Concentric double tube with side holes |
| Accuracy | Lower (errors in $p$ reading) | Higher (differential reading more accurate) |
| Typical coefficient $C_v$ | ~0.98 | ~0.98 – 1.00 |

(3 marks: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark key differentiator)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State the principle on which a Pitot tube works. Derive the expression for the velocity of flow.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A Pitot tube works on the principle of **conversion of kinetic energy of a flowing fluid into pressure (potential) energy at a stagnation point**, governed by **Bernoulli's equation**.

**Derivation:**

Applying Bernoulli's equation between a free-stream point (1) and the stagnation point (2) at the same elevation:

$$p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = p_0$$

Rearranging for $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}}$$

With the coefficient $C_v$:

$$\boxed{v = C_v\sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}}}$$

(3 marks: 1 mark principle + 2 marks derivation)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A Pitot-static tube is inserted in a pipe carrying oil of specific gravity **0.85**. The differential manometer using mercury reads a deflection of **80 mm**. If the Pitot-tube coefficient is **0.98**, determine the velocity of oil at the location of the tube.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A pipe of diameter **150 mm** carries water. A Pitot tube placed at the centre of the pipe records a stagnation pressure of **35 kN/m²** while a static pressure tap on the pipe wall reads **29 kN/m²**. Calculate:
(i) The maximum (centre-line) velocity.
(ii) The average velocity, assuming the velocity profile follows the relation $v = v_{max}\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{1/7}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Given: $S = 0.85$, $S_m = 13.6$ (mercury), $h = 0.080$ m, $C_v = 0.98$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= C_v \sqrt{2gh\!\left(\dfrac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.080 \times \left(\dfrac{13.6}{0.85} - 1\right)} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.080 \times (16.0 - 1)} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.080 \times 15.0} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times \sqrt{23.544} \\[4pt]
&= 0.98 \times 4.852 \\[4pt]
&\approx 4.755 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 4.76 \text{ m/s}}$$

(7 marks: 2 marks formula identification + 3 marks substitution + 2 marks answer with units)

**Part (b):**

Given: $p_0 = 35{,}000$ Pa, $p = 29{,}000$ Pa, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_v = 1.0$ (assumed ideal).

**(i) Centre-line (maximum) velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{max} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times (35{,}000 - 29{,}000)}{1000}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 6{,}000}{1000}} = \sqrt{12} \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.464 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{v_{max} \approx 3.46 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Average velocity (1/7th power-law profile):**

For a power-law profile $v = v_{max}\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{1/n}$ with $n = 7$:

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{n+1}\left[(n+1) - \text{adjustment}\right]$$

The general result is:

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{n}{(n+1)(n+2)} \cdot 2 \cdot v_{max} \quad \text{(for the 1/n power law)}$$

For $n = 7$:

$$\dfrac{v_{avg}}{v_{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times 7}{(7+1)(7+2)} = \dfrac{14}{8 \times 9} = \dfrac{14}{72} = \dfrac{7}{36} \approx 0.1944$$

$$v_{avg} = 0.1944 \times 3.464 \approx 0.673 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{avg} \approx 0.67 \text{ m/s}}$$

(7 marks: 2 marks for v_max + 3 marks for power-law formula + 2 marks final answer)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a Pitot-static tube. State clearly the locations of the stagnation and static pressure sensing points.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Air flows through a duct at standard atmospheric conditions. A Pitot-static tube reads a differential pressure head of **20 mm of water**. Calculate the velocity of air. Take the density of air = **1.2 kg/m³**, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** *Construction and Working of Pitot-Static Tube*

**Construction (Sketch Description):**
A Pitot-static tube consists of two concentric metallic tubes forming a single instrument:
1. **Inner tube:** Open at the nose facing the flow. This opening is called the **stagnation port** or **impact port** and registers the stagnation pressure $p_0$.
2. **Outer tube:** Has a ring of **2 to 8 small static holes** drilled on its cylindrical surface, located about 5 to 10 tube diameters downstream of the nose tip. These are called the **static ports** and register the static pressure $p$.
3. The two tubes are connected at the rear to two separate pressure leads.
4. The leads are connected to a U-tube or inclined differential manometer.

**Working:**
- The fluid is brought to rest at the nose tip (stagnation point), so kinetic energy is converted to pressure.
- Static holes on the cylindrical shoulder sense the true static pressure of the undisturbed stream (where streamlines are parallel to the tube).
- The two pressures are taken to a manometer; the difference $(p_0 - p)$ is read as the manometer deflection $h$.
- Velocity is calculated from:

$$v = C_v\sqrt{2gh(S_m/S - 1)}$$

(7 marks: 2 marks sketch + 2 marks stagnation principle + 2 marks static port location + 1 mark formula)

**Part (b):**

Given: $h = 20$ mm of water = $0.020$ m. The "head" is already in terms of water, so for a U-tube using water as manometric fluid measuring air, the simplification is:

$$p_0 - p = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.020 = 196.2 \text{ Pa}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho_{air}}} \quad \text{(take } C_v = 1.0) \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 196.2}{1.2}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{327.0} \\[4pt]
&\approx 18.08 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 18.08 \text{ m/s}}$$

(7 marks: 2 marks pressure difference + 3 marks substitution + 2 marks final answer)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the coefficient $C_v$:** The question may give $C_v = 0.98$ — using 1.0 loses 1 mark. Always check whether the problem states "ideal" or "real".
> 2. **Unit conversion of $h$:** Students commonly leave $h$ in mm. $h$ **must be in metres** in the formula $v = C_v\sqrt{2gh(\cdot)}$. Missing this unit conversion = 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Wrong ratio $S_m/S$ vs $(S_m - S)/S$:** The correct expression is $(S_m/S - 1)$. Writing $S_m - S$ without dividing by $S$ is a frequent error.
> 4. **Misidentifying stagnation vs static points:** Remember: **stagnation = at the nose, static = on the cylindrical side, NEVER at the rear wake**.
> 5. **Discharge ≠ velocity:** If the question asks for discharge $Q$, do not stop at $v$. Multiply by area $A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pitot tube** = single-port stagnation sensor; needs separate static measurement.
- ✅ **Pitot-static tube** = dual-port (stagnation + static); used with a differential manometer.
- ✅ **Working principle:** Conversion of kinetic energy → pressure head at a stagnation point, governed by **Bernoulli's equation**.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $v = C_v \sqrt{2(p_0 - p)/\rho}$.
- ✅ **With manometer:** $v = C_v \sqrt{2gh(S_m/S - 1)}$.
- ✅ **Static holes** are placed on the **cylindrical shoulder**, *not* at the nose or the rear.
- ✅ $C_v \approx 0.98$ for a well-designed tube; $= 1.0$ only for ideal inviscid flow.
- ✅ Pitot-static measures **local point velocity**, not average velocity.
- ✅ To get average velocity, **integrate** the velocity profile (e.g., 1/7th power law, or log law for turbulent flow).
- ✅ Always convert $h$ to **metres**; always give $v$ in **m/s** and $Q$ in **m³/s**.
- ✅ Compressibility correction is **not** required if Mach number $M < 0.3$ (typical KTU assumption).
- ✅ Real-world use: aircraft airspeed sensors, wind tunnels, river gauging, HVAC ducts, ship speed logs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Rectangular and Triangular Notches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Rectangular and Triangular Notches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Notch? (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notch (KTU 2024 Definition):** A notch is a calibrated opening, made in a vertical wall of a tank or reservoir, through which a liquid (usually water) flows. It is essentially a **small, sharp-edged orifice open at the top**, used as a measuring device to determine the **volumetric discharge (flow rate)** of the liquid flowing through it.

**Key Terminology:**
- **Weir:** When a notch is installed in a large dam or open channel, it is often referred to as a **weir**. The terms "notch" and "weir" are used interchangeably in fluid mechanics, though "notch" typically refers to smaller lab installations, while "weir" refers to larger field installations.
- **Nappe / Vein:** The sheet of water flowing out through the notch is called the **nappe** or **vein**.
- **Head (H):** The vertical height of the free liquid surface above the **sill (crest)** of the notch is called the **head** (in **metres, m**).
- **Sill / Crest:** The bottom edge of the notch over which water flows.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a small rectangular window cut into the side of a water tank. As water fills the tank, it starts spilling out through this window. The higher the water rises above the bottom of the window, the faster the water gushes out. By simply measuring *how high* the water is above the bottom of the window, you can predict *how much* water is flowing out. This simple idea is the foundation of every notch. The **shape** of the window (rectangular or V-shaped) changes the math of how water exits, allowing engineers to design the perfect "flow meter" for any expected flow rate.

## 1.3 Types of Notches (KTU 2024 Classification)

Notches are classified based on the **shape of the opening** cut into the wall:

| S.No. | Type of Notch | Shape Description | Typical Use |
|-------|---------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Rectangular Notch** | A simple rectangular opening of width **L** | High discharge measurement |
| 2 | **Triangular (V-Notch) Notch** | A V-shaped opening with apex angle **θ** | Low / accurate discharge measurement |
| 3 | **Trapezoidal Notch** | Combination of rectangular + triangular | Wide range of discharges |
| 4 | **Stepped Notch** | Rectangular notch with steps | Used in sedimentation tanks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** This module concentrates on the **Rectangular Notch** and the **Triangular (V) Notch** — the two most common and analytically important types in board examinations.

## 1.4 Why Use a Notch? (Engineering Utility)

- Acts as a **simple, accurate flow-measuring device** in laboratories and irrigation canals.
- No external power source required — purely based on **gravitational physics**.
- **Discharge coefficient (Cd)** values are well-established empirically, giving reliable readings.
- Forms the conceptual basis for designing large-scale **weirs** in dams and barrages.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions Made During Notch Analysis (KTU Standard)

When deriving the discharge equations for notches, the following ideal assumptions are adopted:

1. The notch is **rectangular (sharp-edged)**, and the upstream face of the plate is vertical and smooth.
2. The **nappe is fully ventilated** — air freely enters the space below the nappe so that pressure under the nappe equals **atmospheric pressure**.
3. The **velocity of approach** (water approaching the notch) is **negligibly small** (i.e., taken as zero).
4. The flow is **steady, incompressible, and irrotational**.
5. The coefficient of discharge (**Cd**) accounts for all real-fluid losses (contraction, viscosity, turbulence).

## 2.2 Coefficients Associated with a Notch

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Typical Range (Rectangular) | Typical Range (Triangular) |
|--------|------|---------|------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Cd** | Coefficient of Discharge | Ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge | 0.61 – 0.65 | 0.58 – 0.62 |
| **Cv** | Coefficient of Velocity | Ratio of actual velocity to theoretical velocity | 0.95 – 0.99 | 0.95 – 0.99 |
| **Cc** | Coefficient of Contraction | Ratio of contracted nappe area to notch area | 0.62 – 0.65 | 0.60 – 0.65 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relation:** The three coefficients are related by the equation:
> $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$
> This is a **favourite 1-mark KTU question**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

### **A. Rectangular Notch**

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Theoretical velocity at depth **h** | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | m/s |
| Area of elementary strip | $dA = L \cdot dh$ | m² |
| Discharge through elementary strip | $dQ_{th} = L \cdot dh \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | m³/s |
| **Actual Discharge (Full Notch)** | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | m³/s |
| Discharge per unit width | $q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | m³/s per m |

> **Constant Term (Memorise!):** $\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} = 1.84 \, (\text{when } C_d = 0.62)$
> So, a simplified working formula: $Q = 1.84 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$ m³/s (with $C_d = 0.62$)

### **B. Triangular (V-Notch)**

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Width of elementary strip at depth **h** | $b = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2)$ | m |
| Area of elementary strip | $dA = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot dh$ | m² |
| **Actual Discharge (Full Notch)** | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | m³/s |
| Special case: **Right-angled V-notch** ($\theta = 90°$) | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | m³/s |

> **Constant Term (Memorise!):** $\dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} = 2.362 \, (\text{when } C_d = 0.60)$
> So, $Q = 2.362 \cdot H^{5/2}$ m³/s (right-angled V-notch, $C_d = 0.60$)

## 2.4 Comparison: Rectangular vs. Triangular Notch (Board Exam Favourite Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Examiner's Tip:** Comparison questions frequently appear as **3-mark short questions**. Memorise the following table.

| Property | Rectangular Notch | Triangular (V) Notch |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Shape | Rectangle of width **L** | V-shape with apex angle **θ** |
| Head exponent in Q-equation | **H raised to 3/2** | **H raised to 5/2** |
| Sensitivity to small heads | **Less sensitive** (exponent smaller) | **More sensitive** (better for low flows) |
| Best suited for | **Large** discharges | **Small** discharges |
| Coefficient of discharge (Cd) | ≈ 0.62 | ≈ 0.60 |
| Nappe contraction | **Less** (only side contractions) | **More** (all-around) |
| Construction | Simple, easy | Slightly more precise |
| Discharge expression | $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ | $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ |

## 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages

### **Rectangular Notch**
- ✅ Simple to construct, easy to analyse.
- ✅ Suitable for **large** discharge measurement.
- ❌ Less accurate at **low** heads (head exponent 3/2 makes small H give small Q).
- ❌ Requires correction for **end contractions** if notch width < tank width.

### **Triangular Notch**
- ✅ Highly accurate for **small** discharges (exponent 5/2 amplifies small heads).
- ✅ Self-cleaning — debris passes easily through the V-opening.
- ✅ No end-contraction correction needed.
- ❌ Slightly more complex to fabricate precisely.
- ❌ Not ideal for very high discharges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Discharge Through a Rectangular Notch

### **Step 1 — Setup the Geometry**

Consider a rectangular notch of width **L** cut into the vertical wall of a tank. Water flows through it with a head **H** above the sill (bottom edge of the notch).

### **Step 2 — Consider an Elementary Strip**

At a depth **h** below the free liquid surface, take an elementary horizontal strip of thickness **dh**. The strip lies at depth h, so the height of water above the strip is **h**.

The length of this strip = width of notch = **L**.

**Area of elementary strip:**
$$dA = L \cdot dh$$

### **Step 3 — Apply Torricelli's Equation**

The theoretical velocity of water through this strip (using Torricelli's theorem) is:
$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### **Step 4 — Write the Theoretical Discharge Through the Strip**

The theoretical discharge through the elementary strip is:
$$dQ_{th} = \text{Area} \times \text{Velocity} = L \cdot dh \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

### **Step 5 — Apply the Coefficient of Discharge**

The **actual** discharge is obtained by multiplying with Cd:
$$dQ = C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \sqrt{h} \cdot dh$$

### **Step 6 — Integrate Over the Full Head (from 0 to H)**

To get the total discharge, integrate from the free surface (h = 0) to the sill (h = H):

$$Q = C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H \sqrt{h} \, dh$$

**Solving the integral:**
$$\int_0^H \sqrt{h} \, dh = \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh = \left[\frac{h^{3/2}}{3/2}\right]_0^H = \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$

### **Step 7 — Final Rectangular Notch Formula**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

> **Units check:** Cd (dimensionless) × L (m) × √(2g) (m½/s) × H^(3/2) (m^(3/2)) = **m³/s** ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Discharge Through a Triangular (V) Notch

### **Step 1 — Setup the Geometry**

Consider a V-notch with apex angle **θ** (the angle at the bottom vertex of the V). Water flows through the V with head **H** above the apex (vertex).

### **Step 2 — Width of Elementary Strip at Depth h**

At a depth **h** below the free surface, the distance from the apex to the free surface is **(H − h)**. The half-width of the V at this level is:
$$\frac{b}{2} = (H - h) \tan(\theta/2)$$

So the full width of the elementary strip is:
$$b = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2)$$

### **Step 3 — Area of Elementary Strip**

The thickness of the strip is **dh**, so:
$$dA = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot dh$$

### **Step 4 — Theoretical Velocity at Depth h**

By Torricelli's theorem (height of water above strip = **h**):
$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### **Step 5 — Theoretical Discharge Through the Strip**

$$dQ_{th} = dA \cdot v = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dh$$

### **Step 6 — Apply Coefficient of Discharge and Integrate**

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H (H - h) \sqrt{h} \, dh$$

**Expanding the integrand:**
$$(H - h)\sqrt{h} = H \cdot h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}$$

### **Step 7 — Evaluate the Integral**

$$\int_0^H (H \cdot h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}) \, dh = H \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh - \int_0^H h^{3/2} \, dh$$

**First term:**
$$H \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh = H \cdot \left[\frac{2}{3} h^{3/2}\right]_0^H = \frac{2}{3} H^{5/2}$$

**Second term:**
$$\int_0^H h^{3/2} \, dh = \left[\frac{2}{5} h^{5/2}\right]_0^H = \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$

**Combining:**
$$\int_0^H (H - h) \sqrt{h} \, dh = \frac{2}{3} H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$

**Finding a common denominator (LCM = 15):**
$$= \frac{10 H^{5/2}}{15} - \frac{6 H^{5/2}}{15} = \frac{4 H^{5/2}}{15}$$

### **Step 8 — Final Triangular Notch Formula**

$$Q = 2 \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4 H^{5/2}}{15}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}}$$

> **Special case (Right-angled V-notch, θ = 90°):** tan(45°) = 1, so:
> $$Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 style]:**
A rectangular notch of width **0.8 m** is fitted in the side of a tank. The head of water over the notch is **200 mm**. If the coefficient of discharge is **0.62**, find the discharge in **litres/second**. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

### **Step 1 — List the Data**
- Width of notch, **L = 0.8 m**
- Head over notch, **H = 200 mm = 0.2 m**
- Coefficient of discharge, **Cd = 0.62**
- Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²**

### **Step 2 — Write the Formula**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

### **Step 3 — Compute Each Term**

$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$H^{3/2} = (0.2)^{3/2} = (0.2)^1 \times \sqrt{0.2} = 0.2 \times 0.4472 = 0.0894 \text{ m}^{3/2}$$

### **Step 4 — Substitute and Solve**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.8 \times 4.429 \times 0.0894$$

$$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 0.8 \times 4.429 \times 0.0894$$

$$Q = 0.1309 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### **Step 5 — Convert to Litres/Second**

$$Q = 0.1309 \times 1000 = \mathbf{130.9 \text{ L/s}}$$

> **Answer:** The discharge through the rectangular notch is **≈ 130.9 L/s**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Head-Discharge Relationship (V-Notch)

This matrix demonstrates **why a V-notch is preferred for low flows** — the exponent **5/2** magnifies small heads.

| Head H (m) | H^(3/2) (Rectangular factor) | H^(5/2) (Triangular factor) | Q_rect (m³/s) | Q_tri (m³/s) |
|------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 0.05       | 0.0112                        | 0.000559                     | 0.0137         | 0.00079       |
| 0.10       | 0.0316                        | 0.00316                      | 0.0387         | 0.00448       |
| 0.20       | 0.0894                        | 0.0179                       | 0.1095         | 0.0253        |
| 0.50       | 0.3536                        | 0.1768                       | 0.4331         | 0.2503        |
| 1.00       | 1.0000                        | 1.0000                       | 1.2250         | 1.4154        |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** For the same head, the rectangular notch discharges much more than the V-notch — but at very small heads, the V-notch still gives a **measurable, distinguishable** flow, while small errors in H can cause big errors in Q for a rectangular notch. Hence, **V-notch = precision, rectangular = range**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Notch Discharge Analysis

The following Mermaid **block diagram** illustrates the **functional flow of analysis** for a notch problem — from the physical setup, through theoretical modelling, to the final discharge equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Setup Tank with Notch of width L or angle theta] --> B[Define Head H Vertical distance from free surface to sill]
    B --> C[Choose Elementary Strip at depth h from free surface, thickness dh]
    C --> D[Apply Torricellis Theorem Velocity v = sqrt of 2gh]
    D --> E[Compute Strip Discharge dQ = Cd times Area times v]
    E --> F[Integrate from h = 0 to h = H]
    F --> G{Rectangular or Triangular Notch}
    G -- Rectangular --> H[Integrate L times sqrt h dh yields 2 by 3 times H to the 3 by 2]
    G -- Triangular --> I[Integrate 2 times H minus h times tan theta by 2 times sqrt h dh yields 4 by 15 times H to the 5 by 2]
    H --> J[Final Q = 2 by 3 Cd L sqrt 2g H to the 3 by 2]
    I --> K[Final Q = 8 by 15 Cd tan theta by 2 sqrt 2g H to the 5 by 2]
    J --> L[Discharge in m3 per second Convert to L per s if required]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Rectangular Notch (Conceptual Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Tank Reservoir] --> P2[Vertical Wall]
    P2 --> P3[Rectangular Notch Width L]
    P3 --> P4[Head H of water above sill]
    P3 --> P5[Nappe Sheet of water flowing out]
    P5 --> P6[Discharge Q m3 per second]
    P4 --> P7[Head exponent 3 by 2]
    P7 --> P6
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Triangular V-Notch (Conceptual Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Tank Reservoir] --> T2[Vertical Wall]
    T2 --> T3[V Notch with apex angle theta]
    T3 --> T4[Head H above apex vertex]
    T3 --> T5[Nappe V shaped sheet flowing out]
    T5 --> T6[Discharge Q m3 per second]
    T4 --> T7[Head exponent 5 by 2]
    T7 --> T6
```

## 4.4 Functional Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Notch Type] --> Q1{What is the expected flow range}
    Q1 -- High Discharge --> R1[Use Rectangular Notch Q proportional to H to the 3 by 2]
    Q1 -- Low Discharge --> R2[Use Triangular V Notch Q proportional to H to the 5 by 2]
    Q1 -- Wide Range --> R3[Use Trapezoidal Notch Rectangular plus 2 V notches]
    R1 --> Out[Measure Head H and Compute Q]
    R2 --> Out
    R3 --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagrams are designed to map the *logical flow of analysis* — students should reproduce this stepwise structure in their exam answers to score full marks on "explain with neat sketch" type questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a notch. What are the different types of notches used for discharge measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** A notch is a calibrated opening made in the vertical wall of a tank or reservoir through which liquid flows, used to measure the **volumetric discharge (flow rate)** of the liquid.

> **Types (2 Marks):** The commonly used notches are:
> 1. **Rectangular Notch** — a simple rectangular opening of width *L*; used for large discharges.
> 2. **Triangular (V) Notch** — a V-shaped opening with apex angle *θ*; used for small/accurate discharges.
> 3. **Trapezoidal Notch** — a combination of rectangular and triangular notches; used for wide discharge ranges.
> 4. **Stepped Notch** — a rectangular notch with steps; used in sedimentation and grit chambers.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
**Differentiate between a rectangular notch and a triangular notch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Rectangular Notch | Triangular Notch |
|-------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Has a rectangular opening of width *L* | Has a V-shaped opening with apex angle *θ* |
| 2 | Discharge ∝ $H^{3/2}$ | Discharge ∝ $H^{5/2}$ |
| 3 | Suitable for **large** discharge measurement | Suitable for **small/accurate** discharge measurement |
| 4 | Less sensitive to small changes in head | Highly sensitive to small changes in head |
| 5 | $C_d ≈ 0.62$ | $C_d ≈ 0.60$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice. You must attempt **any one** of the two given alternatives. Each question is split into **sub-parts (a) and (b)**, typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a **rectangular notch**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A rectangular notch of width **1.2 m** has a head of **300 mm** of water. If the coefficient of discharge is **0.62**, find the discharge in **litres per second**. Also find the discharge if a **triangular notch** of apex angle **60°** is used under the same head, with $C_d = 0.60$. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

> **Setup (1 Mark):** Consider a rectangular notch of width *L* fitted in the vertical wall of a tank. Let *H* be the head of water above the sill of the notch.

> **Elementary strip (2 Marks):** At a depth *h* below the free surface, consider an elementary horizontal strip of thickness *dh* and length *L*.
> $$dA = L \cdot dh$$

> **Apply Torricelli + Coefficient (2 Marks):** Theoretical velocity = $\sqrt{2gh}$. Actual discharge through strip:
> $$dQ = C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \sqrt{h} \cdot dh$$

> **Integration (2 Marks):**
> $$Q = C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh = C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$
> $$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- Rectangular notch: L = 1.2 m, H = 0.3 m, $C_d$ = 0.62
- Triangular notch: θ = 60°, H = 0.3 m, $C_d$ = 0.60
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Rectangular Notch Discharge (3 Marks):**

$$Q_R = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.3)^{3/2}$$

$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$H^{3/2} = (0.3)^{1.5} = 0.3 \times \sqrt{0.3} = 0.3 \times 0.5477 = 0.1643 \text{ m}^{3/2}$$

$$Q_R = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.429 \times 0.1643 = \mathbf{0.3446 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 344.6 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Triangular Notch Discharge (3 Marks):**

$$Q_T = \frac{8}{15} \times C_d \times \tan(\theta/2) \times \sqrt{2g} \times H^{5/2}$$

$$\tan(30°) = 0.5774$$

$$H^{5/2} = (0.3)^{2.5} = (0.3)^2 \times \sqrt{0.3} = 0.09 \times 0.5477 = 0.0493 \text{ m}^{5/2}$$

$$Q_T = 0.5333 \times 0.60 \times 0.5774 \times 4.429 \times 0.0493 = \mathbf{0.0411 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 41.1 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a **triangular (V) notch**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **right-angled V-notch** is used to measure the discharge in a channel. If the head of water over the notch is **250 mm** and the coefficient of discharge is **0.60**, find the discharge in **litres per second**. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

> **Setup (1 Mark):** Consider a V-notch with apex angle *θ* fitted in the vertical wall of a tank. Let *H* be the head above the apex (vertex).

> **Width of elementary strip (2 Marks):** At depth *h* below the free surface, the width of the elementary strip is:
> $$b = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2)$$

> **Discharge through strip (2 Marks):** With $dA = b \cdot dh$ and $v = \sqrt{2gh}$:
> $$dQ = C_d \cdot 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \sqrt{h} \cdot dh$$

> **Integration (2 Marks):**
> $$Q = 2 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H (H - h) \sqrt{h} \, dh$$
> $$= 2 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \left[ \frac{2}{3} H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2} \right] = 2 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4 H^{5/2}}{15}$$
> $$\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}}$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- Apex angle: θ = 90° (right-angled V-notch) → tan(45°) = 1
- Head: H = 0.25 m
- $C_d$ = 0.60
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Simplify the formula (1 Mark):**

For right-angled V-notch, tan(θ/2) = tan(45°) = 1:
$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \times C_d \times \sqrt{2g} \times H^{5/2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute constants (2 Marks):**

$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$H^{5/2} = (0.25)^{2.5} = (0.25)^2 \times \sqrt{0.25} = 0.0625 \times 0.5 = 0.03125 \text{ m}^{5/2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve (2 Marks):**

$$Q = 0.5333 \times 0.60 \times 4.429 \times 0.03125$$

$$Q = 0.0442 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/s (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{Q = 0.0442 \times 1000 = 44.2 \text{ L/s}}$$

> **Result:** The discharge through the right-angled V-notch is approximately **44.2 L/s**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks common):** Students forget to convert **mm to m**. A head of "250 mm" must be entered as **0.25 m** in the formula. Always check units first!
>
> 2. **Wrong Exponent (–3 marks fatal):** Mixing up $H^{3/2}$ (rectangular) with $H^{5/2}$ (triangular) is the most common error. **Mnemonic:** "Rec-3" and "Tri-5" — the notch with 3 letters in the abbreviation uses 3/2, the one with "Tri" (3 letters… okay, use the formula, not the mnemonic!) Better: **Rectangle has 4 sides → 3/2; Triangle has 3 sides → 5/2**. Just memorise the formula!
>
> 3. **Forgetting tan(θ/2) (–2 marks):** In the V-notch formula, students often write $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ even when the angle is **not 90°**. Always include the $\tan(\theta/2)$ term.
>
> 4. **Using g = 10 instead of 9.81 (–0.5 to –1 mark):** KTU 2024 strictly expects $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless stated otherwise. Using 9.81 m/s² shows rigour.
>
> 5. **No Sill/Apex Definition in derivation (–1 mark):** In the derivation, explicitly **state the limits of integration** (0 to H) and **define H** as the head above the sill (rectangular) or apex (triangular). Examiners reward this.
>
> 6. **Skipping the "Apply Cd" step (–1 mark):** The theoretical discharge is multiplied by $C_d$ to get the **actual** discharge. Skipping this step gives the wrong final numerical answer.
>
> 7. **Final answer in wrong unit (–1 mark):** If the question asks for "litres per second", convert m³/s × 1000 before writing the final answer. Don't stop at m³/s.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Master Definitions**
> - **Notch** = calibrated opening in tank wall for discharge measurement.
> - **Weir** = large-scale notch in dams/channels.
> - **Nappe** = sheet of water flowing through the notch.
> - **Sill / Crest** = bottom edge of the notch.

> ✅ **Core Rectangular Notch Formula**
> $$Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$

> ✅ **Core Triangular (V) Notch Formula**
> $$Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$
> For right-angled V-notch (θ = 90°): $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$

> ✅ **Coefficient Trio (Memorise the relationship)**
> $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$

> ✅ **Typical Cd Values**
> - Rectangular notch: $C_d \approx 0.62$
> - Triangular notch: $C_d \approx 0.60$

> ✅ **Head Exponent Quick Memory Aid**
> - **Rectangular → exponent 3/2** (Q grows slower with H)
> - **Triangular → exponent 5/2** (Q grows faster with H → better for small H)

> ✅ **Notch Selection Rule of Thumb**
> - **Large discharge / large head → Rectangular Notch**
> - **Small discharge / need accuracy → Triangular V-Notch**
> - **Wide range / general purpose → Trapezoidal Notch**

> ✅ **Standard Assumptions to State Before Any Derivation**
> 1. Sharp-edged notch, vertical wall, smooth upstream face.
> 2. Fully ventilated nappe (atmospheric pressure beneath).
> 3. Velocity of approach ≈ 0.
> 4. Steady, incompressible, irrotational flow.
> 5. Cd accounts for all real-flow losses.

> ✅ **Numerical Must-Do's (Before Every Calculation)**
> 1. Convert **all lengths to metres** (mm → m by ÷ 1000).
> 2. Use **g = 9.81 m/s²**.
> 3. Always multiply by **Cd** to get actual discharge.
> 4. Final answer: if asked in L/s, multiply m³/s by 1000.

> ✅ **Drawing Tips for the Board Exam**
> - Always draw a **vertical tank wall** with the notch and label **H**, **L** (or **θ**), and the **nappe**.
> - Show the **elementary strip** at depth *h* with thickness *dh* — this is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
> - Use an **arrow** to indicate the direction of flow through the nappe.

> ✅ **Comparison Table to Memorise (Favourite KTU 3-Mark Question)**
> - Rectangular vs. Triangular: head exponent, sensitivity, Cd, suitable discharge range — see **Section 2.4**.

> ✅ **Right-angled V-Notch Special Case**
> - When θ = 90°, tan(45°) = 1, so the formula simplifies to:
>   $$Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$
> - Numerical value of constant: $\frac{8}{15} \times 0.60 \times \sqrt{19.62} \approx 2.362$
> - So, $Q = 2.362 \, H^{5/2}$ m³/s — handy for quick estimates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Stress and Velocity Distribution in a Pipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Shear Stress and Velocity Distribution in a Pipe ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shear Stress Distribution in a Pipe** refers to the variation of viscous shear stress ($\tau$) across the cross-section of a circular pipe carrying a steady, incompressible, laminar flow. The shear stress varies **linearly** from zero at the centerline ($r = 0$) to a maximum value at the pipe wall ($r = R$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Velocity Distribution in a Pipe (Hagen-Poiseuille Flow)** is the variation of axial fluid velocity ($u$) across the pipe cross-section, which follows a **parabolic (quadratic) profile** for steady, fully-developed, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal pipe. Where: - $r$ = Radial distance from pipe centerline (in **m**) - $R$ = Pipe radius (in **m**) - $u$ = Local axial velocity at radius $r$ (in **m/s**) - $\tau$ = Local shear stress at radius $r$ (in **N/m²** or **Pa**) - $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop over length $L$ (in **Pa**) - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**) - $L$ = Length of pipe section (in **m**) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Honey Jar:** > Imagine squeezing honey out of a vertical tube. The honey layer touching the inner wall of the tube "sticks" to the wall (no-slip condition) and remains stationary. As you move toward the center of the tube, the honey moves faster and faster. The very center moves the fastest, while the outer layers drag behind due to viscous friction. This creates a **parabolic velocity profile** — slow at the walls, fastest in the middle, just like a river flows faster in the middle than near the banks. **Why does shear stress behave differently?** The shear stress is a measure of the **frictional drag** between adjacent fluid layers. At the centerline, the fluid layers are symmetric, so there is **no net shear** (τ = 0). As you move outward toward the wall, the layers have more relative motion with their neighbors, and the viscous friction increases. At the wall, the friction is maximum because the wall is stationary while the fluid is moving. > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway:** Shear stress is **ZERO at the center** and **MAXIMUM at the wall**. Velocity is **MAXIMUM at the center** and **ZERO at the wall**. They are **inverse in nature**. ### 1.3 Governing Assumptions (KTU Syllabus Highlights) 1. **Steady flow** — flow parameters do not change with time. 2. **Incompressible flow** — fluid density $\rho$ is constant. 3. **Laminar flow** — Reynolds number $Re < 2000$ (Newtonian flow regime). 4. **Fully developed flow** — velocity profile is constant along the pipe axis ($du/dx = 0$). 5. **Newtonian fluid** — shear stress proportional to velocity gradient ($\tau = \mu \, du/dr$). 6. **Horizontal pipe** — gravity effects perpendicular to flow are neglected (or pressure gradient includes hydrostatic effects). 7. **No-slip condition** — fluid velocity at the wall equals zero ($u = 0$ at $r = R$). 8. **Axisymmetric flow** — velocity depends only on radius $r$, not on angular position $\theta$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic **Step 1: Free-Body Force Balance on a Fluid Cylinder** Consider an imaginary coaxial cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$ inside the pipe. The forces acting on this fluid element along the flow direction are: - **Pressure force** pushing the cylinder forward: $\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$ - **Shear (viscous) force** resisting the motion at the cylindrical surface: $\tau \cdot 2\pi r \cdot L$ **Step 2: Equilibrium Condition** For steady, fully-developed flow with no acceleration, the net force on the fluid element must be zero (Newton's Second Law with zero inertia): $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r \cdot L$$ **Step 3: Solve for Shear Stress Distribution** Dividing both sides by $2\pi r L$: $$\tau = \frac{\Delta P}{2L} \cdot r$$ This is a **linear function of $r$** — confirming that shear stress increases linearly from the center to the wall. **Step 4: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity** For a Newtonian fluid: $$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$ The negative sign indicates that velocity *decreases* as $r$ *increases* (velocity gradient is negative). **Step 5: Equate and Integrate** Setting the two expressions for $\tau$ equal: $$-\mu \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2L} \cdot r$$ $$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot r$$ Integrating with respect to $r$: $$u = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \cdot r^2 + C$$ **Step 6: Apply Boundary Condition (No-Slip)** At $r = R$, $u = 0$: $$0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \cdot R^2 + C \implies C = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$ **Step 7: Final Velocity Distribution Equation** $$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$ This is the famous **parabolic velocity profile**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for solving KTU university exam problems on this topic. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Shear stress distribution | $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Linear in $r$ | | 2 | Maximum shear stress (at wall) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ | Pa | Occurs at $r = R$ | | 3 | Minimum shear stress (at center) | $\tau_{min} = 0$ | Pa | Occurs at $r = 0$ | | 4 | Velocity distribution | $u = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ | m/s | Parabolic profile | | 5 | Maximum velocity (at centerline) | $u_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$ | m/s | At $r = 0$ | | 6 | Mean (average) velocity | $V = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L}$ | m/s | $V = u_{max} / 2$ | | 7 | Hagen-Poiseuille Equation | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8\mu L V}{R^2}$ | Pa | Pressure drop form | | 8 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$ | m³/s | Q = A·V | | 9 | Pressure gradient relation | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{32 \mu V}{D^2}$ | Pa/m | In terms of diameter | | 10 | Momentum flux correction factor | $\beta = \dfrac{4}{3}$ | dimensionless | For laminar flow only | | 11 | Kinetic energy correction factor | $\alpha = 2$ | dimensionless | For laminar flow only | | 12 | Friction factor (Fanning) | $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ | dimensionless | Laminar flow in pipes | | 13 | Friction factor (Darcy) | $f_D = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | dimensionless | $f_D = 4f$ | | 14 | Wall shear stress (in terms of $V$) | $\tau_w = \dfrac{4\mu V}{R}$ | Pa | At $r = R$ | | 15 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | $Re < 2000$ for laminar | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL NOTATION ALERT:** In the table above, $\Delta P$ represents the **pressure drop** ($\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$) over the pipe length $L$. Do **NOT** confuse $\Delta P$ with dynamic pressure $\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$. Some textbooks write the pressure gradient as $-\dfrac{dP}{dx}$, which equals $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ for a horizontal pipe. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |-------|-------------| | **Petroleum Engineering** | Designing crude oil pipelines, calculating pumping power requirements | | **Pharmaceutical Industry** | Flow of syrups, blood-analog fluids through IV tubes | | **HVAC Systems** | Sizing chilled water and refrigerant lines in buildings | | **Biomedical Engineering** | Blood flow modeling in arteries (using modified Hagen-Poiseuille) | | **Chemical Engineering** | Reactor design, heat exchanger tube-side flow analysis | | **Hydraulics** | Laminar water flow in small-bore tubes and capillaries | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The $R^4$ dependence in the flow rate equation means that even a small **doubling of pipe radius** causes a **16-fold increase** in flow rate (for the same pressure drop). This is why even minor pipe diameter changes have massive hydraulic consequences. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Shear Stress Distribution **Starting Premise:** Consider a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ carrying a steady, fully-developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid. Let the pressure at section 1 be $P_1$ and at section 2 (distance $L$ downstream) be $P_2$, with $P_1 > P_2$. **Step 1 — Define a control volume:** Imagine a coaxial cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ (where $0 \leq r \leq R$) and length $L$, with its axis coincident with the pipe axis. **Step 2 — Identify the forces acting on the cylinder along the flow direction:** $$F_{pressure,1} = P_1 \cdot \pi r^2 \quad \text{(acts in the +x direction)}$$ $$F_{pressure,2} = P_2 \cdot \pi r^2 \quad \text{(acts in the -x direction)}$$ $$F_{shear} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L \quad \text{(acts in the -x direction, resisting motion)}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law (steady state ⇒ acceleration = 0):** $$\sum F_x = 0$$ $$P_1 \pi r^2 - P_2 \pi r^2 - \tau (2\pi r L) = 0$$ **Step 4 — Define the pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:** $$(P_1 - P_2) \pi r^2 = \tau (2\pi r L)$$ $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$ **Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $\tau$:** $$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2}{2\pi r L} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ $$\boxed{\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{2L} \cdot r}$$ **Step 6 — Determine the maximum shear stress at the wall ($r = R$):** $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \tau(R) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity Distribution (Parabolic Profile) **Step 1 — Start with Newton's Law of Viscosity for a Newtonian fluid:** $$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$ The negative sign indicates that velocity $u$ *decreases* as radius $r$ *increases* (since flow is fastest at the center and zero at the wall). **Step 2 — Equate this to the previously derived shear stress distribution:** $$-\mu \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ **Step 3 — Separate variables and rearrange:** $$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot r$$ $$du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot r \, dr$$ **Step 4 — Integrate both sides from the wall (where $u = 0$ at $r = R$) to an arbitrary radius $r$:** $$\int_0^{u} du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_R^{r} r \, dr$$ $$u - 0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\right]_R^{r}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the integral limits:** $$u = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(r^2 - R^2\right)$$ **Step 6 — Simplify (the negative sign flips the order of subtraction):** $$u = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$ $$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)}$$ **Step 7 — Maximum velocity at the centerline ($r = 0$):** $$u_{max} = u(0) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$ **Step 8 — Velocity at the wall ($r = R$):** $$u(R) = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (confirms no-slip condition)}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean (Average) Velocity and Hagen-Poiseuille Equation **Step 1 — Define mean velocity as the volumetric flow rate divided by cross-sectional area:** $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute volumetric flow rate by integrating the velocity profile over a ring element of area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$:** $$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the velocity distribution:** $$Q = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$ **Step 4 — Pull out the constants:** $$Q = \frac{2\pi \Delta P}{4\mu L} \int_0^R \left(R^2 r - r^3\right) dr$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the limits:** $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$ $$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}}$$ **Step 6 — Divide by cross-sectional area $A = \pi R^2$ to find mean velocity:** $$V = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4 / (8\mu L)}{\pi R^2} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L}$$ $$\boxed{V = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L} = \frac{u_{max}}{2}}$$ **Step 7 — Express as the canonical Hagen-Poiseuille equation (in terms of diameter $D = 2R$):** $$V = \frac{\Delta P (D/2)^2}{8\mu L} = \frac{\Delta P D^2}{32 \mu L}$$ Solving for $\Delta P$: $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The mean velocity is exactly **half** of the centerline maximum velocity. This 2:1 ratio is a unique signature of laminar parabolic flow. For turbulent flow, this ratio approaches ~0.8 due to the flatter (more uniform) velocity profile. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Wall Shear Stress in Terms of Mean Velocity **Step 1 — Start with the maximum shear stress at the wall:** $$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P R}{2L}$$ **Step 2 — Solve the mean velocity equation for $\Delta P / L$:** $$V = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L} \implies \frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8\mu V}{R^2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the wall shear stress equation:** $$\tau_w = \frac{R}{2} \cdot \frac{8\mu V}{R^2} = \frac{4\mu V}{R}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{4\mu V}{R} = \frac{8\mu V}{D}}$$ > [!TIP] > This formula is extremely useful in KTU problems because it directly relates the wall shear stress to the **mean velocity** $V$ and **dynamic viscosity** $\mu$, without requiring knowledge of the pressure drop. ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Quantity | Value at Center ($r=0$) | Value at Wall ($r=R$) | Profile Type | |----------|--------------------------|------------------------|--------------| | Velocity $u$ | $u_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$ | $0$ | Parabolic (concave down) | | Shear stress $\tau$ | $0$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P R}{2L}$ | Linear (zero at center) | | Velocity gradient $du/dr$ | $0$ | $-\dfrac{\Delta P R}{2\mu L}$ | Linear (zero at center) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pipe Flow Physics Map The following Mermaid block diagram maps the interactions between the physical variables governing laminar pipe flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pressure Drop dP over Length L] --> B[Drives Shear Stress in Fluid] A --> C[Drives Velocity Profile] D[Pipe Radius R] --> B D --> C E[Dynamic Viscosity mu] --> C E --> F[Wall Shear Stress tau_w] B --> F B --> G[Linear tau vs r Profile] C --> H[Parabolic u vs r Profile] F --> I[Friction Factor f] H --> J[Mean Velocity V] H --> K[Max Velocity u_max] K --> J J --> L[Volumetric Flow Q] D --> L J --> M[Reynolds Number Re] E --> M M --> N{Laminar or Turbulent} N -->|Re less than 2000| O[Hagen-Poiseuille Valid] N -->|Re greater than 2000| P[Empirical Correlations Needed] ``` --- ### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: From Pressure Drop to Velocity Profile ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Section 1: Pressure P1] -->|Pressure Drop dP| P2[Section 2: Pressure P2] P1 --> CYL[Coaxial Fluid Cylinder
Radius r, Length L] P2 --> CYL CYL -->|Force Balance| TAU[Shear Stress tau = dP times r divided by 2L] TAU -->|Linear Profile| TW[Wall tau_max] TAU -->|Newton Viscosity Law| INT[Integration with Boundary Condition] INT --> UVEL[Velocity u = dP over 4 mu L times R squared minus r squared] UVEL -->|Parabolic Profile| UC[Centerline u_max] UVEL -->|Integration over Area| Q[Volumetric Flow Q = pi dP R to the 4 divided by 8 mu L] UC --> V[Mean Velocity V = u_max divided by 2] V --> Q ``` --- ### 4.3 Profile Comparison Diagram: Shear Stress vs. Velocity ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PIPECROSS[Pipe Cross Section View] direction LR AXIS[Pipe Centerline at r = 0]:::center WALL[Pipe Wall at r = R]:::wall end subgraph TAU_PROFILE[Shear Stress Profile] direction LR T1[tau = 0 at center]:::zeroNode T2[tau increases linearly]:::midNode T3[tau = tau_max at wall]:::maxNode end subgraph VEL_PROFILE[Velocity Profile] direction LR V1[u = u_max at center]:::maxNode V2[u decreases parabolically]:::midNode V3[u = 0 at wall]:::zeroNode end PIPECROSS --> TAU_PROFILE PIPECROSS --> VEL_PROFILE classDef center fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef wall fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef zeroNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef midNode fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef maxNode fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** > - **Green nodes** (zero values): Shear stress at center = 0; velocity at wall = 0 > - **Red nodes** (maximum values): Shear stress at wall = maximum; velocity at center = maximum > - The two profiles are **mirror images** of each other across the radial axis. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **For steady, fully-developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe, derive the expression for the shear stress distribution. State the boundary conditions used.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (1 mark):** Consider a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ carrying steady, fully-developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid. Let the pressure drop over a length $L$ be $\Delta P$. **Free-body force balance (1 mark):** For a coaxial fluid cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$: $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$ **Resulting shear stress distribution (1 mark):** Solving for $\tau$: $$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ **Boundary conditions used:** (a) $\tau = 0$ at $r = 0$ (axisymmetry), (b) $\tau = \tau_{max}$ at $r = R$ (wall). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate clearly between the velocity distribution and shear stress distribution in fully-developed laminar pipe flow. Mention the location of their maximum and minimum values.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Shear Stress ($\tau$) | Velocity ($u$) | |---------|----------------------|----------------| | Profile shape | **Linear** with $r$ | **Parabolic** with $r$ | | Mathematical form | $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ | $u = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$ | | Maximum value | $\tau_{max}$ at **wall** ($r = R$) | $u_{max}$ at **center** ($r = 0$) | | Minimum value | $0$ at **center** ($r = 0$) | $0$ at **wall** ($r = R$) | | Physical reason | Friction with stationary wall | No-slip at wall, free flow at center | --- ### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Starting from a force balance on a fluid element, derive the **velocity distribution equation** for steady, fully-developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe of radius $R$. State clearly all assumptions and boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An oil with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.1$ N·s/m² and specific gravity $0.85$ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 50$ mm and length $L = 100$ m at a mean velocity of $V = 0.5$ m/s. Determine: - (i) The pressure drop $\Delta P$ over the pipe length. - (ii) The maximum velocity $u_{max}$ at the centerline. - (iii) The wall shear stress $\tau_w$. - (iv) The shear stress at a radial distance $r = 15$ mm from the centerline. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Steady, incompressible, laminar flow 2. Newtonian fluid (obeys $\tau = \mu \, du/dr$) 3. Fully developed flow ($du/dx = 0$) 4. Horizontal pipe (gravity acts perpendicular to flow) 5. Axisymmetric flow (no swirl) **Force balance on a coaxial cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$ (2 marks):** $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ **Apply Newton's law of viscosity (1 mark):** $$-\mu \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ **Separate variables and integrate (2 marks):** $$\int_0^u du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_R^r r \, dr$$ $$u = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$ **Boundary conditions (1 mark):** $u = 0$ at $r = R$ (no-slip); $u = u_{max}$ at $r = 0$ (axisymmetry). **Final equation:** $$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)}$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $\mu = 0.1$ N·s/m², $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m, $L = 100$ m, $V = 0.5$ m/s. **(i) Pressure drop $\Delta P$ (2 marks):** Using the Hagen-Poiseuille equation in terms of mean velocity: $$\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{32 \times 0.1 \times 100 \times 0.5}{(0.05)^2} = \frac{160}{0.0025} = 64{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P = 64 \text{ kPa}}$$ **(ii) Maximum velocity $u_{max}$ (2 marks):** For laminar flow, $u_{max} = 2V$: $$u_{max} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{u_{max} = 1.0 \text{ m/s}}$$ **(iii) Wall shear stress $\tau_w$ (1.5 marks):** $$\tau_w = \frac{4\mu V}{R} = \frac{4 \times 0.1 \times 0.5}{0.025} = \frac{0.2}{0.025} = 8.0 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_w = 8.0 \text{ N/m}^2}$$ **(iv) Shear stress at $r = 15$ mm (1.5 marks):** The pipe radius is $R = D/2 = 25$ mm. The ratio $r/R = 15/25 = 0.6$. $$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L} = \frac{64{,}000 \times 0.015}{2 \times 100} = \frac{960}{200} = 4.8 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\tau(15 \text{ mm}) = 4.8 \text{ N/m}^2}$$ **Verification:** $\tau_w = \tau(R) = \frac{64{,}000 \times 0.025}{200} = 8.0$ Pa ✓ (matches part iii) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for volumetric flow rate through a horizontal circular pipe under steady, laminar, fully-developed flow conditions. Show that the mean velocity is half the maximum velocity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 100$ mm carries water ($\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m², $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) with a pressure drop of $\Delta P = 5$ kPa over a length of $L = 50$ m. Calculate: - (i) The maximum velocity $u_{max}$ at the centerline. - (ii) The mean velocity $V$. - (iii) The volumetric flow rate $Q$. - (iv) The Reynolds number $Re$ and verify whether the flow is laminar. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Derivation of Hagen-Poiseuille (7 marks):** **Starting point (1 mark):** From the velocity distribution derived earlier: $$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$ **Volumetric flow rate through a differential ring of area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$ (2 marks):** $$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr$$ **Evaluate the integral (2 marks):** $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$ **Final Hagen-Poiseuille equation (1 mark):** $$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}}$$ **Mean velocity = half of max velocity (1 mark):** $$V = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L} = \frac{u_{max}}{2}$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $D = 0.1$ m, $R = 0.05$ m, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m², $\Delta P = 5000$ Pa, $L = 50$ m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. **(i) Maximum velocity $u_{max}$ (1.5 marks):** $$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L} = \frac{5000 \times (0.05)^2}{4 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 50} = \frac{5000 \times 0.0025}{0.2} = \frac{12.5}{0.2}$$ $$\boxed{u_{max} = 62.5 \text{ m/s}}$$ **(ii) Mean velocity $V$ (1.5 marks):** $$V = \frac{u_{max}}{2} = \frac{62.5}{2}$$ $$\boxed{V = 31.25 \text{ m/s}}$$ **(iii) Volumetric flow rate $Q$ (2 marks):** $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L} = \frac{\pi \times 5000 \times (0.05)^4}{8 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 50}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \times 5000 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.03125}{0.4} = \frac{0.09817}{0.4}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.2454 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$ **(iv) Reynolds number check (2 marks):** $$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 31.25 \times 0.1}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3125}{0.001} = 3{,}125{,}000$$ $$\boxed{Re = 3.125 \times 10^6 \gg 2000}$$ **Conclusion:** The flow is **highly turbulent**, not laminar. This means the assumed parabolic profile and Hagen-Poiseuille equation are **not valid** for this problem. The high Reynolds number is physically unrealistic for water flow in a 100 mm pipe at $\Delta P = 5$ kPa, suggesting the problem data is contrived or there is a calculation interpretation issue. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Note:** In a real exam, the Reynolds number is **always** checked at the end of such problems. If $Re > 2000$, the laminar flow assumption fails, and the Hagen-Poiseuille equations cannot be applied. This is a very common trick question in KTU papers to test whether students blindly apply formulas without verifying the flow regime. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Exams (Module 4 — Shear Stress & Velocity Distribution):** > > 1. **Forgetting the boundary conditions (–1 mark):** Always state both $u = 0$ at $r = R$ and $\tau = 0$ at $r = 0$ explicitly. Examiners specifically look for these. > > 2. **Sign error in Newton's law of viscosity (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing $\tau = +\mu \, du/dr$ instead of $\tau = -\mu \, du/dr$. The negative sign is *crucial* and reflects the physical fact that velocity decreases with increasing radius. > > 3. **Unit inconsistency (–0.5 mark):** Mixing mm and m in calculations. Always convert all dimensions to **SI units (m, Pa, m/s)** before substituting into formulas. > > 4. **Not verifying the laminar flow assumption (–1 mark):** Always calculate the Reynolds number $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ and confirm $Re < 2000$ for laminar flow. KTU examiners award marks for this check. > > 5. **Mean velocity formula confusion (–1 mark):** Remember that $V = u_{max} / 2$ **ONLY** for laminar flow. For turbulent flow, this ratio is approximately $0.8$ to $0.9$. > > 6. **Missing the $R^4$ dependence explanation:** When asked about pipe sizing, remember that flow rate scales with $R^4$, not $R^2$. This is a favorite conceptual question. > > 7. **Neglecting to state the assumptions (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always list the 7–8 assumptions at the start of your derivation. Examiners allocate marks for this. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Shear Stress & Velocity Distribution** - ✅ **Shear stress distribution:** $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ — **linear** in $r$ - ✅ **Maximum shear stress at the wall:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ — pipe walls experience the most friction - ✅ **Minimum shear stress at the center:** $\tau = 0$ — axisymmetric flow - ✅ **Velocity distribution:** $u = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ — **parabolic** profile - ✅ **Maximum velocity at the centerline:** $u_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$ - ✅ **Mean velocity:** $V = \dfrac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L} = \dfrac{u_{max}}{2}$ — half of $u_{max}$ for laminar flow - ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$ — flow rate scales with $R^4$ - ✅ **Wall shear stress in terms of $V$:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{4\mu V}{R} = \dfrac{8\mu V}{D}$ - ✅ **Friction factor (Darcy):** $f_D = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ for laminar flow - ✅ **Friction factor (Fanning):** $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ (Fanning = Darcy / 4) - ✅ **Kinetic energy correction factor:** $\alpha = 2$ for laminar parabolic flow - ✅ **Momentum flux correction factor:** $\beta = \dfrac{4}{3}$ for laminar parabolic flow - ✅ **Pressure gradient form:** $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{32 \mu V}{D^2}$ — in terms of diameter - ✅ **Laminar condition:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} < 2000$ - ✅ **No-slip condition:** $u = 0$ at $r = R$ (always include in derivations) - ✅ **Key assumption checklist:** Steady, incompressible, laminar, fully-developed, Newtonian, axisymmetric, horizontal pipe - ✅ **Physical insight:** Shear stress and velocity profiles are **inverse** to each other — one is maximum where the other is zero > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** KTU Module 4 questions almost always combine a **derivation (7 marks)** with a **numerical application (7 marks)**. Practice the force balance derivation until you can reproduce it in under 5 minutes. Memorize the key formulas in the cheat sheet above — they are the "highway" to solving numerical problems quickly.

topics.title: Law of Fluid Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Law of Fluid Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law of Fluid Friction (KTU 2024 Module 4 Definition):**
