> The Law of Fluid Friction describes the resistance offered by a fluid to motion when it flows over a surface or through a conduit. It quantifies the energy loss (head loss) due to the viscous shear forces acting at the fluid–solid interface. Mathematically, it is embodied in the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**, which states that the head loss due to friction is directly proportional to the length of the pipe, the square of the flow velocity, and a dimensionless friction coefficient, while being inversely proportional to the pipe diameter.

In other words, whenever a real (viscous) fluid flows through a pipe, energy is continuously lost as the fluid layers slide over one another and over the pipe wall. This mechanical energy is converted into heat (irreversible). The law governing this energy loss is what we call the **Law of Fluid Friction**.

## 1.2 Underlying Physical Phenomenon

Fluid friction arises from two complementary mechanisms:

1. **Internal Viscous Shear (Fluid–Fluid Friction):** The relative motion between adjacent fluid layers produces tangential shear stress. This is governed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:
   $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$
   where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $\frac{du}{dy}$ is the velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow.

2. **Wall Shear (Fluid–Solid Friction):** The no-slip condition at the pipe wall produces a wall shear stress $\tau_0$, which drags the fluid and reduces its velocity near the boundary.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Water Flowing Through a Garden Hose:**
> Imagine pushing water through a long garden hose. You instinctively notice three things:
> - A **longer hose** requires more pressure (more friction).
> - A **thinner hose** requires more pressure for the same flow (more friction).
> - **Faster flow** requires disproportionately more pressure (quadratic relationship).
>
> The Law of Fluid Friction formalizes these everyday observations into a single equation, allowing engineers to design pipelines, water-supply networks, and oil-transport systems with predictable pressure requirements.

## 1.4 Classification of Flow Regimes

The Law of Fluid Friction takes **two distinct mathematical forms** depending on the **Reynolds Number (Re)**:

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number | Friction Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar Flow** | Re < 2000 | Hagen–Poiseuille Law ($f = 64/Re$) |
| **Transitional Flow** | 2000 < Re < 4000 | Unstable; design uses safety factor |
| **Turbulent Flow** | Re > 4000 | Empirical laws (Blasius, Colebrook–White) |

The **Reynolds Number** is defined as:
$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

where $\rho$ = fluid density, $V$ = mean velocity, $D$ = pipe diameter, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, and $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity (Foundation)

For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress at any point is proportional to the rate of angular deformation (velocity gradient):

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹)

For water at **20 °C**: $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho \approx 998$ kg/m³.
For air at **20 °C**: $\mu \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.

## 2.2 Darcy–Weisbach Equation (Cornerstone)

The **head loss** $h_f$ due to friction in a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ carrying fluid at mean velocity $V$ is:

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

The corresponding **pressure loss** is:
$$\Delta P_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

where $f$ is the **Darcy friction factor** (dimensionless).

## 2.3 The Friction Factor $f$

The friction factor is **not a constant** — it depends on:
- **Reynolds Number** (Re) — flow regime effect
- **Relative Roughness** ($\varepsilon/D$) — pipe surface effect

### 2.3.1 For Laminar Flow (Re < 2000)
$$f = \frac{64}{Re}$$
*(Derived from first principles — no empirical constants required.)*

### 2.3.2 For Turbulent Flow (Re > 4000)
**Blasius Equation (smooth pipes, Re < 10⁵):**
$$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$$

**Colebrook–White Equation (transitionally rough turbulent flow):**
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the absolute roughness of the pipe wall.

## 2.4 Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow in Pipes)

For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, D^4}{128 \mu L}$$

$$V_{mean} = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L}$$

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\gamma D^2}$$

## 2.5 Velocity Distribution in Laminar Flow

The velocity profile across the pipe cross-section is **parabolic**:
$$u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]$$

where $u_{max} = 2 \cdot V_{mean}$ occurs at the centerline ($r = 0$), and $u = 0$ at the wall ($r = R$) due to the no-slip condition.

## 2.6 Shear Stress Distribution in Pipes

For **fully-developed laminar flow** through a horizontal pipe, applying a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$:

$$\tau \cdot (2\pi r L) = \Delta P \cdot (\pi r^2)$$

$$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

This is a **linear distribution** with maximum value at the wall:
$$\tau_0 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \, \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Newtonian fluids |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number | All flows |
| 3 | $h_f = f \, \dfrac{L}{D} \, \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach Equation | All pipe flows |
| 4 | $\Delta P_f = f \, \dfrac{L}{D} \, \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Friction Pressure Drop | All pipe flows |
| 5 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Friction Factor (Laminar) | Re < 2000 |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | Blasius Equation | Smooth pipes, 4000 < Re < 10⁵ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}} \right)$ | Colebrook–White | Turbulent, all roughness |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P \, D^4}{128 \mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille Discharge | Laminar |
| 9 | $V_{mean} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L}$ | Mean Velocity (Laminar) | Laminar |
| 10 | $u(r) = u_{max}\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$ | Parabolic Velocity Profile | Laminar |
| 11 | $\tau(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ | Shear Stress at radius $r$ | Laminar, fully developed |
| 12 | $\tau_0 = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$ | Wall Shear Stress | Laminar, fully developed |
| 13 | $h_L = K \, \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Minor Loss (form, fittings) | All flows |
| 14 | $L_e / D = K / f$ | Equivalent Length Ratio | All flows |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Law of Fluid Friction is the **backbone of pipeline design** in:
> - **Water supply networks** (municipal distribution, pumping stations)
> - **Oil & gas transmission pipelines** (long-distance, high-pressure)
> - **HVAC systems** (chilled water, steam distribution)
> - **Hydraulic machinery** (head loss in penstocks of hydroelectric plants)
> - **Chemical process industries** (flow through reactors, heat exchangers)
> - **Fire-fighting and irrigation systems**

A correct application of these friction laws determines the **pump power requirement**, the **pipe diameter selection**, and the **energy efficiency** of the entire system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy–Weisbach Equation from Wall Shear Stress

### Step 1 — Force Balance on Fluid in Pipe
Consider steady, fully-developed flow through a horizontal pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$. Take a control volume of fluid inside the pipe. The forces acting along the flow direction are:
- Pressure force at inlet: $P_1 \cdot A$
- Pressure force at outlet: $P_2 \cdot A$
- Wall shear force: $\tau_0 \cdot (\pi D L)$

For **uniform flow** (no acceleration), the net force is zero:

$$P_1 A - P_2 A - \tau_0 (\pi D L) = 0$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Cross-Sectional Area
Cross-sectional area: $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$. Substituting:

$$(P_1 - P_2) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D L$$

### Step 3 — Simplify to Wall Shear Stress
$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \frac{4 \tau_0 L}{D}$$

$$\tau_0 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L}$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Head Loss
The pressure drop can be written as $\Delta P = \rho g h_f$. Substituting:

$$\tau_0 = \frac{\rho g h_f \cdot D}{4 L}$$

$$h_f = \frac{4 \tau_0 L}{\rho g D}$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the Friction Factor
By definition, the Darcy friction factor is non-dimensionalised form of wall shear:

$$f = \frac{8 \tau_0}{\rho V^2}$$

Solving for $\tau_0$:
$$\tau_0 = \frac{f \rho V^2}{8}$$

### Step 6 — Final Form
Substitute $\tau_0$ from Step 5 into Step 4:

$$h_f = \frac{4 L}{\rho g D} \cdot \frac{f \rho V^2}{8}$$

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

$$\boxed{h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

This is the **Darcy–Weisbach equation** — the fundamental law of fluid friction in pipes.

## 3.2 Derivation of Friction Factor for Laminar Flow ($f = 64/Re$)

### Step 1 — Velocity Profile Assumption
For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid, the Navier–Stokes equations reduce to:

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = \mu \left( \frac{d^2 u}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{du}{dr} \right)$$

Since the pressure gradient $\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L}$ is constant for fully-developed flow:

$$-\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \mu \cdot \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{du}{dr} \right)$$

### Step 2 — First Integration
Multiply both sides by $\frac{r}{\mu}$ and integrate with respect to $r$:

$$\int \frac{1}{\mu}\!\left(-\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right) r \, dr = \int \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{du}{dr} \right) dr$$

$$-\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} r^2 + C_1 = r \frac{du}{dr}$$

### Step 3 — Second Integration
Divide by $r$ and integrate again:

$$-\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \frac{r^2}{2} + C_1 \ln r + C_2 = u(r)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions
**Condition 1:** Velocity is finite at the centerline $r = 0$. The term $C_1 \ln r$ becomes $-\infty$ unless $C_1 = 0$. So:
$$C_1 = 0$$

**Condition 2:** No-slip at the wall — $u = 0$ at $r = R$:
$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} + C_2$$

$$C_2 = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8 \mu L}$$

### Step 5 — Velocity Profile
$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$

This is a **parabolic profile** with maximum at $r = 0$:

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L} = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{16 \mu L}$$

### Step 6 — Mean Velocity
Integrate over the pipe cross-section:
$$V = \frac{1}{A} \int_A u \, dA = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$V = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$V = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L R^2} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr$$

$$V = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L R^2} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R$$

$$V = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L R^2} \left( \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} \right) = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L R^2} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{V = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L}}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille mean velocity equation**.

### Step 7 — Derive the Friction Factor
From the Darcy–Weisbach equation, $h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$ with $\Delta P = \rho g h_f$:

$$\Delta P = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

Equate with Hagen–Poiseuille: $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$:

$$f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

$$f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2} \cdot \frac{D}{L} \cdot \frac{2}{\rho V^2} = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D}$$

Since $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{64}{Re}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Discharge Equation

From Step 6 above, $V = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L}$, and discharge $Q = A \cdot V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot V$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, D^4}{128 \, \mu \, L}}$$

## 3.4 Shear Stress Distribution (Analytical)

Apply Newton's second law to a cylindrical element of fluid of radius $r$, length $L$, coaxial with the pipe. Forces along the axis:

- Pressure at left face: $P_1 \cdot \pi r^2$
- Pressure at right face: $P_2 \cdot \pi r^2$
- Shear on cylindrical surface: $\tau \cdot 2 \pi r L$

For equilibrium of the fluid core:
$$(P_1 - P_2) \pi r^2 = \tau (2 \pi r L)$$

$$\tau(r) = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) \cdot r}{2L} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

At the wall, $r = R = D/2$:
$$\tau_0 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$$

This **linear distribution** is unique to laminar flow; in turbulent flow the profile is much flatter near the centre and steeper near the wall.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Friction Factor Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Pipe Data] --> B[Compute Reynolds Number Re]
    B --> C{Re less than 2000?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Laminar Flow Regime]
    D --> E[Use f = 64 divided by Re]
    E --> F1[Compute Head Loss h_f]
    C -- No --> G{Re greater than 4000?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Turbulent Flow Regime]
    H --> I{Relative Roughness known?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Use Colebrook White Equation]
    I -- No --> K{Re less than 10^5?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Blasius: f = 0.316 divided by Re^0.25]
    K -- No --> M[Use Moody Chart or Swamee Jain]
    J --> F1
    L --> F1
    M --> F1
    F1 --> N[Apply Darcy Weisbach h_f = f L V^2 over 2gD]
    G -- No --> O[Transitional Flow Re 2000 to 4000]
    O --> P[Use conservative design with f from Moody chart at Re = 4000]
    P --> F1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mechanism of Fluid Friction

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Pressure Energy at Inlet] --> B[Fluid Flows Through Pipe]
    B --> C[Internal Viscous Shear]
    B --> D[Wall Shear due to No Slip]
    C --> E[Kinetic Energy Dissipated as Heat]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Reduced Pressure Energy at Outlet]
    F --> G[Head Loss h_f measured]
    G --> H[Darcy Weisbach Equation applied]
    H --> I[Friction Factor f determined from Re and Roughness]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation of Velocity and Shear Stress Profiles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pipe Wall r equals R] --> P2[Wall Shear maximum tau zero]
    P2 --> P3[Velocity u equals 0 at wall]
    P3 --> P4[Shear decreases linearly to zero at center]
    P4 --> P5[Velocity increases parabolically to u max at center]
    P5 --> P6[Parabolic profile u of r equals u max times 1 minus r by R squared]
    P6 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Engineering Pipeline System — Friction Loss Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Pump or Reservoir] --> S2[Straight Pipe Section L1]
    S2 --> S3[Entrance Loss K equals 0.5]
    S3 --> S4[Elbow 1 Loss K equals 0.9]
    S4 --> S5[Straight Pipe L2]
    S5 --> S6[Valve Loss K equals 0.3 to 10]
    S6 --> S7[Elbow 2 Loss K equals 0.9]
    S7 --> S8[Exit Loss K equals 1.0]
    S8 --> S9[Tank or Open Discharge]
    S2 --> N1[Friction Loss h_f1]
    S5 --> N2[Friction Loss h_f2]
    N1 --> T[Total Head Loss H_L = sum of h_f plus sum of h_minor]
    N2 --> T
    S3 --> M1[Minor Loss h_m1]
    S4 --> M2[Minor Loss h_m2]
    S6 --> M3[Minor Loss h_m3]
    S7 --> M4[Minor Loss h_m4]
    S8 --> M5[Minor Loss h_m5]
    M1 --> T
    M2 --> T
    M3 --> T
    M4 --> T
    M5 --> T
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
*State Newton's law of viscosity. What is the SI unit of dynamic viscosity?*

**Model Answer:**
Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress in a flowing fluid is directly proportional to the rate of change of velocity (velocity gradient) perpendicular to the direction of flow. Mathematically:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\tau$ = shear stress (N/m²), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $\frac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient (s⁻¹).

The **SI unit** of dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is **Pa·s** (Pascal-second) or **N·s/m²** or **kg/(m·s)**.

For water at 20 °C, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Define the Darcy friction factor. Write the expression for the friction factor in laminar flow through a circular pipe.*

**Model Answer:**
The **Darcy friction factor** $f$ is a dimensionless quantity that represents the resistance to flow caused by the wall shear stress in a pipe. It relates the wall shear stress to the dynamic pressure of the flow:

$$f = \frac{8 \tau_0}{\rho V^2}$$

For **laminar flow** (Re < 2000) through a circular pipe:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{64}{Re}}$$

This result is derived analytically from the Navier–Stokes equations without any empirical constants.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the Darcy–Weisbach equation for head loss due to friction in a pipe. Starting from the wall shear stress, show all intermediate steps clearly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 100 mm and length 200 m carries oil (kinematic viscosity $\nu = 0.4 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s, specific gravity = 0.85) at a velocity of 1.5 m/s. Determine:
   (i) The Reynolds number and state the flow regime.
   (ii) The Darcy friction factor.
   (iii) The head loss due to friction.
   (iv) The power required to maintain this flow. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider a horizontal pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ with fluid flowing steadily. Let $P_1$ and $P_2$ be pressures at inlet and outlet. Let $\tau_0$ be the wall shear stress.

Force balance on fluid column:
$$P_1 A - P_2 A - \tau_0 (\pi D L) = 0$$

With $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$:
$$(P_1 - P_2) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \pi D L$$

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \frac{4 \tau_0 L}{D}$$

Converting to head loss: $\Delta P = \rho g h_f$:
$$h_f = \frac{4 \tau_0 L}{\rho g D}$$

By definition of Darcy friction factor: $f = \dfrac{8 \tau_0}{\rho V^2}$, so $\tau_0 = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{8}$.

Substituting:
$$h_f = \frac{4 L}{\rho g D} \cdot \frac{f \rho V^2}{8} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

$$\boxed{h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- $L = 200$ m
- $\nu = 0.4 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s $= 4 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- Specific gravity $S = 0.85$ → $\rho = 850$ kg/m³
- $V = 1.5$ m/s

**(i) Reynolds Number:**
$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.1}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.15}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 3750$$

Since 2000 < Re < 4000, the flow is **transitional**.

**(ii) Friction Factor:**
For design safety, the friction factor is taken corresponding to the upper limit of transitional flow (or we conservatively use the turbulent regime). Using the laminar formula for a transition-zone design check:
$$f_{lam} = \frac{64}{3750} = 0.01707$$

Alternatively, for safe design at $Re = 4000$:
$$f_{safe} = \frac{0.316}{(4000)^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{7.952} \approx 0.0397$$

For a typical KTU exam, **$f = 0.0397$** (using Blasius, since flow is essentially on the verge of turbulence).

**(iii) Head Loss:**
$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.0397 \times \frac{200}{0.1} \times \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.0397 \times 2000 \times \frac{2.25}{19.62} = 0.0397 \times 2000 \times 0.1147$$

$$h_f = 9.11 \text{ m of oil}$$

**(iv) Power Required:**
$$P = \rho g Q h_f$$

Discharge: $Q = A V = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 \times 1.5 = 0.01178$ m³/s

$$P = 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.01178 \times 9.11$$

$$P = 884.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.885 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice for 14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam, Module 4]**

**(a)** For laminar flow through a circular pipe, derive the expression for the velocity profile and the mean velocity using the Navier–Stokes equations. Also show that $f = 64/Re$ for laminar flow. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A liquid of viscosity $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s and density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter 50 mm and length 30 m. If the pressure drop is 50 kPa, determine:
   (i) The maximum velocity at the centreline.
   (ii) The mean velocity.
   (iii) The discharge through the pipe.
   (iv) The wall shear stress.
   (Assume laminar flow and verify the same.) (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

For steady, laminar, fully-developed, incompressible flow through a horizontal pipe, the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates reduces to:

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = \mu \left( \frac{d^2 u}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{du}{dr} \right)$$

With $dP/dx = -\Delta P/L$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{du}{dr} \right) = -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$

Integrating once:
$$r \frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} r^2 + C_1$$

Integrating again:
$$u(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \cdot \frac{r^2}{2} + C_1 \ln r + C_2$$

Boundary conditions:
- $u$ finite at $r = 0$ → $C_1 = 0$
- $u = 0$ at $r = R$ → $C_2 = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8 \mu L}$

Velocity profile:
$$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\!\left(R^2 - r^2\right)}$$

Mean velocity (by integration):
$$V = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L}$$

Maximum velocity at $r = 0$:
$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{16 \mu L} = 2 V$$

For $f = 64/Re$, equating Hagen–Poiseuille with Darcy–Weisbach:
$$f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

$$f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{64}{Re}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $L = 30$ m
- $\Delta P = 50$ kPa $= 50{,}000$ Pa

**Verification of laminar flow** (compute Re after finding V):
We first find V from Hagen–Poiseuille (assumed laminar):

$$V = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L} = \frac{50{,}000 \times (0.05)^2}{32 \times 0.1 \times 30}$$

$$V = \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.0025}{96} = \frac{125}{96} = 1.302 \text{ m/s}$$

Reynolds number:
$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.302 \times 0.05}{0.1} = 651$$

Since **Re = 651 < 2000**, the flow is **laminar**. ✓ Assumption verified.

**(i) Maximum velocity:**
$$u_{max} = 2 V = 2 \times 1.302 = 2.604 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Mean velocity:**
$$V = 1.302 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Discharge:**
$$Q = A V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot V = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.05)^2 \times 1.302$$

$$Q = 0.001963 \times 1.302 = 2.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.556 \text{ L/s}$$

**(iv) Wall Shear Stress:**
$$\tau_0 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.05}{4 \times 30} = \frac{2500}{120}$$

$$\tau_0 = 20.83 \text{ N/m}^2$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams (Module 4 — Law of Fluid Friction):**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify laminar flow:** Many students directly apply $f = 64/Re$ without first computing Re. Always check the regime!
> 2. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m (e.g., $D = 100$ mm and $L = 200$ m). Convert **all dimensions to SI units (metres)** before substitution.
> 3. **Wrong viscosity:** Using dynamic viscosity $\mu$ in place of kinematic viscosity $\nu$ (or vice-versa) when computing Re. The relation is $Re = VD/\nu = \rho VD/\mu$.
> 4. **Confusing Darcy vs Fanning friction factor:** The two differ by a factor of 4. KTU textbooks (and most Indian engineering curricula) use the **Darcy** factor by default. Do not mix them.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps:** Examiners award marks for **each step** in derivations. A jump from the force balance straight to the final Darcy–Weisbach equation without showing $A = \pi D^2/4$ and the substitution of $\tau_0$ in terms of $f$ will lead to a **loss of 2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Neglecting the transitional regime:** If Re lies between 2000 and 4000, the safe design value of $f$ is computed at $Re = 4000$ (using Blasius or Moody chart). State this clearly.
> 7. **Power formula sign error:** Power $P = \rho g Q h_f$ requires the **specific weight** $\gamma = \rho g$, not the density $\rho$ alone, multiplied by $g$. Forgetting the factor $g$ is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Law of Fluid Friction**

- **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$; defines Newtonian fluid behaviour. Units of $\mu$: Pa·s or N·s/m².
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$.
  - $Re < 2000$ → Laminar.
  - $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ → Transitional.
  - $Re > 4000$ → Turbulent.
- **Darcy–Weisbach Equation:** $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ and $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$. Applicable to **all** flow regimes.
- **Laminar Friction Factor:** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ — **theoretical** (no empirical constants).
- **Turbulent Smooth Pipe (Re < 10⁵):** Blasius: $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$.
- **Turbulent General (Colebrook–White):** $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}} \right)$ — requires **iterative solution** or Moody's chart.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille Discharge:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P \, D^4}{128 \mu L}$ — laminar only.
- **Mean velocity (laminar):** $V = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L}$.
- **Velocity profile (laminar):** Parabolic: $u(r) = u_{max}\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$ with $u_{max} = 2V$.
- **Shear stress (laminar):** $\tau(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$, linear; max at wall: $\tau_0 = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$.
- **Wall Shear in terms of $f$:** $\tau_0 = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{8}$.
- **Minor losses:** $h_L = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$, where $K$ depends on fitting type (entrance, exit, valve, bend).
- **Equivalent length method:** $L_e = \dfrac{K D}{f}$ — converts a minor loss into an equivalent straight pipe length.
- **Moody's Diagram:** A graphical tool giving $f$ as a function of Re and $\varepsilon/D$ — the only chart you must know how to read for this module.
- **Power to overcome friction:** $P = \rho g Q h_f = \gamma Q h_f$ (watts).
- **Friction factor relationship:** Darcy = 4 × Fanning (do not confuse).
- **Darcy–Weisbach is valid for all regimes** — only the formula for $f$ changes with the regime.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Head Loss Due to Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Head Loss Due to Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Head Loss Due to Friction (Major Energy Loss):** The continuous and irreversible loss of mechanical energy (head) experienced by a real fluid as it flows through a uniform straight pipe, caused by the viscous shear stresses acting between adjacent fluid layers and between the fluid and the pipe wall. It is the dominant component of total head loss in long pipelines and is mathematically expressed by the **Darcy–Weisbach Equation**.

The total head loss in a pipe system is broadly classified into:

1. **Major Head Loss ($h_f$ or $h_L$):** Caused primarily by friction along the length of the pipe. It is the *continuous* loss occurring throughout the pipe length and is therefore the focus of this topic.
2. **Minor Head Losses ($h_m$):** Caused by pipe fittings, bends, valves, sudden expansions, sudden contractions, and entrance/exit effects. These are *localized* losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** For the B.Tech examination, students must be able to compute the friction head loss using the Darcy–Weisbach equation, identify the correct friction factor ($f$ or $\lambda$) based on the flow regime (laminar/turbulent), and apply the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for laminar flow.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake through a long, narrow straw. The longer the straw, the harder you have to suck. Why? Because the viscous milkshake continuously rubs against the inner wall of the straw and against itself, sapping your energy along the way. The same principle applies to water flowing through a long pipeline:

- The **fluid molecules in contact with the pipe wall** are stationary (no-slip condition). They drag the adjacent layers, which drag the next, and so on, creating a velocity gradient from wall to centerline.
- This **viscous shearing** continuously converts useful mechanical energy (pressure head + velocity head + elevation head) into **heat (thermal energy)**, which is permanently lost to the surroundings.
- The longer the pipe, the rougher the pipe, the faster the flow, and the more viscous the fluid — the **greater the friction head loss**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of head loss as a *tax* that the fluid must continuously pay to the pipe wall as it travels from the source to the destination. Engineers design pipelines to *minimize* this tax using smooth pipes, larger diameters, and optimal flow velocities (typically 1–2 m/s for water distribution).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters govern head loss due to friction:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of water (at 4 °C):** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic viscosity of water (at 20 °C):** $\mu_w = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water (at 20 °C):** $\nu_w = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Typical commercial pipe roughness ($\varepsilon$):** $0.045 \text{ mm (smooth steel)}$ to $0.15 \text{ mm (cast iron)}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Friction Loss Occur? — The Physics

The head loss due to friction is fundamentally a manifestation of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. Real (viscous) fluids obey the **no-slip condition** at the boundary, which forces a velocity gradient across the pipe cross-section. This velocity gradient produces shear stress, which does irreversible work against the flow, dissipating mechanical energy as heat.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Dissipation

1. **No-Slip Condition:** The fluid layer touching the pipe wall has zero velocity relative to the wall. This creates a velocity profile (parabolic for laminar, logarithmic for turbulent) across the cross-section.
2. **Shear Stress Generation:** The velocity gradient $\frac{du}{dy}$ produces viscous shear stress $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ (Newton's law of viscosity).
3. **Wall Shear Stress:** The shear stress at the pipe wall, $\tau_w$, exerts a retarding frictional force on the fluid column: $F_f = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L$.
4. **Energy Balance:** This frictional force performs negative work on the fluid, equal to $F_f \cdot V$ per unit time. This mechanical energy is converted to heat, manifesting as a drop in the **piezometric head** (or **total head**) along the pipe.
5. **Friction Factor Parameterization:** To make the calculation universal, the wall shear stress is non-dimensionalized into a **friction factor** $f$ (or $\lambda$), which encapsulates the combined effect of viscosity, turbulence, and wall roughness.

## 2.2 Classification of Flow Regimes (Reynolds Number)

The friction factor depends critically on the **Reynolds Number**, defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Friction Behavior |
|-------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 2000$ | $f$ depends only on $Re$ (smooth, predictable) |
| **Transitional** | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | Unstable; experimentally uncertain |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | $f$ depends on $Re$ **and** relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU numerical problems, the flow regime is *always* explicitly stated or implied. You must:
> 1. Compute $Re$ first.
> 2. Identify the regime.
> 3. Use the appropriate friction factor formula (Hagen–Poiseuille for laminar, Moody chart / Colebrook–White / empirical equations for turbulent).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION WARNING:** KTU textbooks (and the KTU 2024 scheme) commonly use either $f$ (Darcy friction factor, sometimes called $\lambda$) or $4f$ (Fanning friction factor). They differ by a factor of 4. **Always check which one your problem uses before solving.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Applicable Regime | Key Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Darcy–Weisbach Equation | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | All regimes | Uses Darcy friction factor $f$ |
| 2 | Darcy–Weisbach (Fanning form) | $h_f = \dfrac{4f L V^2}{2gD}$ | All regimes | Uses Fanning factor $4f$ (smaller by 4×) |
| 3 | Hagen–Poiseuille Equation | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$ | Laminar only | Derived analytically from Navier–Stokes |
| 4 | Laminar Darcy Factor | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | $Re < 2000$ | Independent of roughness |
| 5 | Laminar Fanning Factor | $4f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ | $Re < 2000$ | Same as Hagen–Poiseuille reformulated |
| 6 | Blasius Equation | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | $4000 < Re < 10^5$, smooth pipes | Empirical; smooth pipe only |
| 7 | Colebrook–White Equation | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | Fully turbulent, transition zone | Implicit (iterative); industry standard |
| 8 | Swamee–Jain (Explicit) | $f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$ | Turbulent | Explicit alternative to Colebrook–White |
| 9 | Chezy Formula | $V = C \sqrt{m i}$ | Turbulent (open channel/pipe) | $C$ = Chezy coefficient |
| 10 | Manning Formula (pipes) | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Turbulent | $n$ = Manning's roughness |
| 11 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ | All regimes | Determines flow regime |
| 12 | Relative Roughness | $\dfrac{\varepsilon}{D}$ | Turbulent | Ratio of wall roughness to pipe diameter |
| 13 | Friction Slope | $S_f = \dfrac{h_f}{L} = f \dfrac{V^2}{2gD}$ | All regimes | Energy grade line slope per unit length |
| 14 | Pressure Drop | $\Delta p = \rho g h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | All regimes | Useful when pressure loss is asked |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Roughness Values ($\varepsilon$ in mm) — Memorize for KTU:**
> - Riveted steel: 0.9 – 9.0
> - Concrete: 0.3 – 3.0
> - Cast iron: 0.25 – 0.8
> - Galvanized iron: 0.15
> - Commercial steel: 0.045
> - Drawn tubing (glass, copper, plastic): 0.0015 (smooth)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The head loss due to friction governs the design of virtually every pipeline-based system:

- **Water Supply Networks:** Municipal corporations must size pipes so that friction losses do not drop pressure below consumer requirements at the tap.
- **Hydraulic Power Plants:** Long penstocks carrying water to turbines are designed with minimum friction loss to maximize the available head at the turbine inlet.
- **Oil and Gas Pipelines:** Crude oil, natural gas, and refined petroleum products are pumped across hundreds of kilometers; friction losses determine the **pump station spacing** and **horsepower requirements**.
- **HVAC Systems:** Chilled water and condenser water loops in air-conditioning systems are sized using the Darcy–Weisbach equation to size circulation pumps.
- **Firefighting Networks:** Standpipe and sprinkler systems must deliver water at minimum pressures at the nozzle, requiring careful friction analysis.
- **Sewage and Drainage:** Gravity-driven sewer lines use the Manning equation (a friction formula) to set the slope needed for self-cleansing velocity.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy–Weisbach Equation (From Energy Balance)

**Objective:** Derive the head loss in a straight, uniform pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ carrying a steady flow at average velocity $V$.

**Starting Point:** Apply the steady-flow energy equation (modified Bernoulli) between section 1 (inlet) and section 2 (outlet) of a horizontal pipe (no elevation change, $z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

**Step 1 — Continuity (uniform pipe):**
Since the pipe has constant cross-sectional area, by the continuity equation $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, we have $V_1 = V_2 = V$. Therefore, the velocity head terms cancel:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + h_f$$

**Step 2 — Isolating the head loss:**
Solving for $h_f$:

$$h_f = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3 — Relating pressure drop to wall shear stress:**
The frictional resistance force exerted by the pipe wall on the fluid column over length $L$ is:

$$F_f = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L$$

where $\tau_w$ is the wall shear stress (N/m²) and $\pi D L$ is the wetted lateral surface area.

**Step 4 — Force balance on the fluid column:**
For steady flow, this frictional force must equal the net pressure force driving the flow (assuming horizontal pipe):

$$(p_1 - p_2) \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L$$

$$p_1 - p_2 = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Expressing in terms of head loss:**
Substituting back into the head loss equation:

$$h_f = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{\rho g D}$$

**Step 6 — Non-dimensionalizing the wall shear stress:**
Define the **Darcy friction factor** $f$ as:

$$f = \frac{8 \tau_w}{\rho V^2}$$

Solving for $\tau_w$:

$$\tau_w = \frac{f \rho V^2}{8}$$

**Step 7 — Final substitution:**
Substitute $\tau_w$ into the head loss expression:

$$h_f = \frac{4 L}{\rho g D} \cdot \frac{f \rho V^2}{8} = f \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

$$\boxed{h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

This is the celebrated **Darcy–Weisbach equation**, universally valid for both laminar and turbulent flow in a straight pipe.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow)

**Objective:** Derive an analytical expression for the head loss in fully-developed laminar flow in a horizontal circular pipe.

**Starting Point:** Apply the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates for steady, fully-developed, axisymmetric laminar flow in a horizontal pipe (gravity acts perpendicular to the flow direction):

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial u}{\partial r} \right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Pressure gradient is constant:**
For fully-developed flow, the velocity profile $u(r)$ does not change with $x$. Therefore, $\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$ must be a constant. Denote it as:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{p_2 - p_1}{L} = -\frac{\Delta p}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Ordinary differential equation in $r$:**

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{du}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{\Delta p}{L}$$

**Step 3 — First integration (multiply by $r$ and integrate):**

$$r \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L} r^2 + C_1$$

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary condition 1: Finite velocity at the centerline.**
At $r = 0$, $\frac{du}{dr}$ must be finite (otherwise the gradient would be singular). This forces $C_1 = 0$:

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L} r$$

**Step 5 — Second integration:**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4 \mu L} r^2 + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply boundary condition 2: No-slip at the wall.**
At $r = R = D/2$, $u = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{\Delta p}{4 \mu L} R^2 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{\Delta p R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

**Step 7 — Parabolic velocity profile (Hagen–Poiseuille profile):**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4 \mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right) = \frac{\Delta p}{4 \mu L} R^2 \left( 1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2} \right)$$

**Step 8 — Maximum velocity (at the centerline, $r = 0$):**

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta p R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

**Step 9 — Average velocity (integrate over cross-section):**

$$V = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2}{R^2} \int_0^R \frac{\Delta p}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

$$V = \frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L R^2} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr = \frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L R^2} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R$$

$$V = \frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L R^2} \left( \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} \right) = \frac{\Delta p}{2 \mu L R^2} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\Delta p R^2}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 10 — Express pressure drop:**

$$\Delta p = \frac{8 \mu L V}{R^2} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

**Step 11 — Convert to head loss:**

$$h_f = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho g} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$$

$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow head loss.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Laminar Friction Factor $f = 64/Re$

**Step 1 — Equate the two head loss expressions** (Darcy–Weisbach and Hagen–Poiseuille) for the same laminar flow:

$$f \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel common terms ($L$, $g$, $D$, $V$):**

$$\frac{f V}{2} = \frac{32 \mu}{\rho D}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $f$:**

$$f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize Reynolds number in the denominator:**
By definition, $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$, so $\frac{\mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{1}{Re}$:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{64}{Re}}$$

**Conclusion:** For laminar flow, the Darcy friction factor depends *only* on the Reynolds number and is *independent* of pipe roughness. This is why laminar flow calculations are so reliable and predictable.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Given Quantity | Flow Regime | Friction Factor Source | Final Formula to Use |
|----------------|-------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| $V$, $D$, $\rho$, $\mu$, $L$ | Laminar ($Re < 2000$) | $f = 64/Re$ | $h_f = \dfrac{64}{Re} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ |
| $V$, $D$, $\rho$, $\mu$, $L$ | Turbulent, smooth | Blasius: $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ | $h_f = \dfrac{0.316 L V^{1.75}}{D^{1.25} \cdot 2g \cdot Re^{0.25} \cdot \rho}$ etc. |
| $V$, $D$, $\varepsilon$, $L$ | Turbulent, rough | Moody chart or Colebrook–White | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ (iterative on $f$) |
| $Q$ (discharge), $D$, $L$, $\varepsilon$ | Turbulent | Moody chart or Swamee–Jain | Convert $Q \to V = Q/A$, then $h_f$ |
| $h_f$, $L$, $D$, $V$ | Any | Re-arrange Darcy–Weisbach | $f = \dfrac{2 g D h_f}{L V^2}$ (to find $f$, then read $\varepsilon$ from Moody chart) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Oil with kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ and specific gravity $0.86$ flows through a horizontal cast iron pipe of diameter $D = 150 \text{ mm}$ at a velocity of $2.5 \text{ m/s}$. The pipe length is $500 \text{ m}$. Compute the head loss due to friction.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.15}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.375}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2500$$

**Step 2 — Identify flow regime:**
$Re = 2500$ is in the **transitional** range $(2000 < Re < 4000)$. For KTU exam purposes, if not otherwise specified, we treat this as **turbulent** and use either Blasius or Moody chart.

**Step 3 — Choose friction factor (assume smooth pipe approximation with Blasius since $Re < 10^5$):**

$$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(2500)^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{7.07} \approx 0.0447$$

**Step 4 — Apply Darcy–Weisbach:**

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.0447 \times \frac{500}{0.15} \times \frac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.0447 \times 3333.33 \times \frac{6.25}{19.62} = 0.0447 \times 3333.33 \times 0.3185$$

$$\boxed{h_f \approx 47.45 \text{ m of oil}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (alternative using Hagen–Poiseuille for laminar limit):**
If we incorrectly treated this as laminar, $f = 64/2500 = 0.0256$, giving $h_f \approx 27.2$ m. The difference highlights why **regime identification is critical**.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Head Loss Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete analytical workflow a KTU student must follow when solving a "head loss due to friction" problem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given pipe geometry L, D, fluid properties rho, mu, nu, flow rate Q or velocity V] --> B[Compute mean velocity V = Q divided by A]
    B --> C[Compute Reynolds Number Re = V times D divided by nu]
    C --> D{Re less than 2000?}
    D -- YES --> E[Regime = LAMINAR]
    D -- NO --> F{Re between 2000 and 4000?}
    F -- YES --> G[Regime = TRANSITIONAL treat as turbulent in exam]
    F -- NO --> H[Regime = TURBULENT]
    E --> I[Use f = 64 divided by Re Darcy factor]
    G --> J[Use Blasius f = 0.316 divided by Re power 0.25 OR Moody chart OR Colebrook White]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Apply Darcy Weisbach h_f = f times L by D times V squared by 2g]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Convert h_f to pressure drop if needed Delta p = rho times g times h_f]
    L --> M[END: Report h_f in meters and Delta p in Pa or N per m squared]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Hagen–Poiseuille Parabolic Velocity Profile

The following diagram illustrates the parabolic velocity distribution that physically gives rise to the friction head loss in laminar pipe flow:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PipeCross["Circular Pipe Cross-Section (radius R = D/2)"]
        P1[Centerline: u = u_max] --- P2[Mid radius: u = u_max divided by 2] --- P3[Pipe wall: u = 0 NO SLIP]
    end
    PipeCross -. "Parabolic profile u of r = u_max times 1 minus r squared by R squared" .-> PipeCross
    Q1[Shear stress tau = mu times du by dr is MAXIMUM at wall] --> P3
    Q2[Friction force on fluid F_f = tau_w times pi times D times L acts LEFTWARD] --> P3
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: From Microscopic Shear to Macroscopic Head Loss

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[No-Slip Condition at Wall] --> B[Velocity Gradient du by dr]
    B --> C[Wall Shear Stress tau_w = mu times du by dr]
    C --> D[Friction Force F_f = tau_w times pi times D times L]
    D --> E[Pressure Drop Delta p = 4 tau_w L by D]
    E --> F[Head Loss h_f = Delta p by rho times g]
    F --> G[Darcy Weisbach h_f = f L by D times V squared by 2g]
    G --> H[For Laminar: f = 64 by Re]
    G --> I[For Turbulent: f from Moody chart, Colebrook White, or Blasius]
    H --> J[Final h_f value in meters]
    I --> J
```

## 4.4 Moody's Diagram — Conceptual Representation

Since the Moody diagram is a complex 2D plot that cannot be rendered directly in Mermaid, we represent its **functional regions** as a decision tree that guides the student to the right friction-factor source:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Find Re from V, D, nu] --> M2{Is Re less than 2000?}
    M2 -- YES --> M3[Laminar Zone: f = 64 by Re straight horizontal line in Moody chart]
    M2 -- NO --> M4{Is Re greater than 4000?}
    M4 -- YES --> M5{Is epsilon by D less than 0.0001?}
    M5 -- YES --> M6[Smooth Pipe Zone: use Blasius f = 0.316 by Re power 0.25]
    M5 -- NO --> M7[Transition Zone: use Colebrook White equation iteratively]
    M7 --> M8[Fully Rough Zone horizontal asymptote: f depends only on epsilon by D]
    M8 --> M9[Read friction factor from Moody chart or compute]
    M3 --> M9
    M6 --> M9
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following terms in the context of pipe flow friction:**
(a) Hydraulic gradient
(b) Energy gradient
(c) Friction factor

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Hydraulic Gradient (HGL):** The line joining the piezometric head ($\frac{p}{\rho g} + z$) at various points along the pipe, plotted as a function of distance. Its slope equals the friction slope $S_f = h_f / L$ for a uniform pipe.

(b) **Energy Gradient (EGL):** The line joining the total head ($\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z$) at various points along the pipe. In a uniform pipe, the EGL is parallel to and lies $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ above the HGL. The slope of the EGL equals the rate of head loss per unit length.

(c) **Friction Factor ($f$):** A dimensionless parameter that quantifies the resistance to flow offered by the pipe wall. Defined as $f = \frac{8 \tau_w}{\rho V^2}$, it incorporates the combined effect of fluid viscosity, flow turbulence, and wall roughness. For laminar flow, $f = 64/Re$ (independent of roughness); for turbulent flow, $f$ is read from the Moody chart or computed using Colebrook–White or empirical formulas.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between major and minor head losses in a pipe flow system. State the Darcy–Weisbach equation and explain the physical significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Major Head Loss ($h_f$) | Minor Head Loss ($h_m$) |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Cause** | Friction along the pipe length | Local disturbances (fittings, valves, bends) |
| **Location** | Distributed continuously | Localized at specific points |
| **Magnitude** | Dominant in long pipes | Usually small (hence the name) |
| **Formula** | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $h_m = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ (loss coefficient $K$) |
| **Depends on** | $L$, $D$, $f$, $V$ | Type and number of fittings |

The Darcy–Weisbach equation is:

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Physical significance of each term:**
- $h_f$ — head loss in meters (or fluid feet) of the flowing fluid.
- $f$ — Darcy friction factor, dimensionless; represents the resistance characteristics of the pipe wall and flow.
- $L$ — length of the pipe over which friction occurs (in m).
- $D$ — internal diameter of the pipe (in m).
- $V$ — mean flow velocity (in m/s).
- $g$ — acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 200 mm and length 1000 m carries water (kinematic viscosity $\nu = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$) at a velocity of 1.5 m/s. Take roughness $\varepsilon = 0.2$ mm. Determine the head loss due to friction using:
  (i) Darcy–Weisbach equation with friction factor from the Moody chart.
  (ii) The Hagen–Poiseuille equation (compare and discuss why it is *not* applicable here).
  **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the Darcy–Weisbach equation starting from the energy equation for a horizontal pipe. Clearly state all assumptions. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{VD}{\nu} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.2}{10^{-6}} = 3 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2 — Compute relative roughness:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.2 \text{ mm}}{200 \text{ mm}} = 0.001$$

**Step 3 — Identify flow regime:**
$Re = 3 \times 10^5 > 4000$ ⟹ **Turbulent flow**.

**Step 4 — Find friction factor using the Colebrook–White equation (analytical equivalent of Moody chart):**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}} \right)$$

Substituting values and iterating (or using Swamee–Jain explicit formula):

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\left( \frac{0.001}{3.7} + \frac{5.74}{(3 \times 10^5)^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2}$$

$$= \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\left( 2.703 \times 10^{-4} + 1.05 \times 10^{-4} \right) \right]^2}$$

$$= \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}(3.753 \times 10^{-4}) \right]^2} = \frac{0.25}{(-3.4257)^2} = \frac{0.25}{11.74} \approx 0.0213$$

So $f \approx 0.0213$.

**Step 5 — Apply Darcy–Weisbach:**

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.0213 \times \frac{1000}{0.2} \times \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.0213 \times 5000 \times \frac{2.25}{19.62} = 0.0213 \times 5000 \times 0.1147$$

$$\boxed{h_f \approx 12.21 \text{ m of water}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Hagen–Poiseuille (laminar equation) for comparison:**

$$h_f^{HP} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} = \frac{32 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 1.5}{1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.2)^2}$$

$$= \frac{48}{392.4} = 0.122 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Discussion:**
The Hagen–Poiseuille result ($0.122$ m) is **100× smaller** than the turbulent Darcy–Weisbach result ($12.21$ m). The Hagen–Poiseuille equation is **not applicable** here because:
- $Re = 3 \times 10^5 \gg 2000$ (flow is fully turbulent, not laminar).
- Laminar flow equations assume viscous-dominated flow, while turbulent flow has additional Reynolds stresses and roughness-induced momentum losses that dramatically increase head loss.
- The laminar friction factor $f = 64/Re = 2.13 \times 10^{-4}$ severely underestimates the true resistance.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. Key points to write in the exam:

1. Write the steady-flow energy equation between inlet (1) and outlet (2).
2. For a horizontal uniform pipe, $z_1 = z_2$ and $V_1 = V_2 = V$.
3. Show that $h_f = (p_1 - p_2)/(\rho g)$.
4. Force balance on the fluid column: $(p_1 - p_2) A = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L$.
5. Define friction factor: $f = 8\tau_w/(\rho V^2)$.
6. Substitute and simplify to obtain:

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Steady, incompressible flow.
- Uniform pipe diameter (constant cross-section).
- Fully-developed flow (velocity profile does not change with $x$).
- Single-phase fluid (no air entrainment).
- No minor losses or work done by pumps/turbines between sections 1 and 2.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** An oil of specific gravity 0.85 and dynamic viscosity $0.05 \text{ Pa·s}$ flows through a 100 mm diameter horizontal pipe at a rate of 8 liters per second. Determine:
  (i) The Reynolds number and flow regime.
  (ii) The head loss over a 200 m length.
  (iii) The shear stress at the pipe wall.
  **[7 marks]**

**(b)** What is the Moody chart? Explain its significance in determining the friction factor. How is the friction factor read from the Moody chart for:
  (i) Laminar flow.
  (ii) Smooth-pipe turbulent flow.
  (iii) Fully-rough turbulent flow.
  **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Convert flow rate:**

$$Q = 8 \text{ L/s} = 0.008 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute mean velocity:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.008}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.019 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — (i) Reynolds number and flow regime:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{850 \times 1.019 \times 0.1}{0.05} = \frac{86.62}{0.05} = 1732.4$$

Since $Re = 1732 < 2000$, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

**Step 5 — (ii) Head loss using Hagen–Poiseuille:**

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} = \frac{32 \times 0.05 \times 200 \times 1.019}{850 \times 9.81 \times (0.1)^2}$$

$$= \frac{325.96}{83.385} = 3.91 \text{ m of oil}$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Wall shear stress:**
Using the definition of friction factor: $\tau_w = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{8}$, where $f = 64/Re = 64/1732.4 = 0.03695$:

$$\tau_w = \frac{0.03695 \times 850 \times (1.019)^2}{8} = \frac{32.0}{8} = 4.00 \text{ Pa}$$

**Verification using pressure drop:**

$$\Delta p = \rho g h_f = 850 \times 9.81 \times 3.91 = 32{,}605 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta p \cdot D}{4 L} = \frac{32605 \times 0.1}{4 \times 200} = \frac{3260.5}{800} = 4.08 \text{ Pa} \checkmark$$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

The **Moody chart** (also called the **Moody diagram** or **Stanton diagram**) is a dimensionless logarithmic plot of the **Darcy friction factor $f$** (vertical axis) against the **Reynolds number $Re$** (horizontal axis), with a family of curves parameterized by the **relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$**. It was developed by Lewis F. Moody in 1944 and remains the standard graphical tool in fluid mechanics for determining the friction factor for any combination of flow regime and pipe roughness.

**Significance:**
- It unifies empirical and theoretical results for $f$ across all flow regimes.
- It encapsulates the Colebrook–White implicit equation graphically.
- It is essential for solving practical pipe flow problems in design offices worldwide.

**Reading the Moody chart:**

(i) **Laminar flow ($Re < 2000$):**
The friction factor is read from the single straight line $f = 64/Re$ (independent of roughness). For example, at $Re = 1000$, $f = 0.064$.

(ii) **Smooth-pipe turbulent flow ($Re > 4000$, $\varepsilon/D \to 0$):**
Use the lowest curve on the chart, labeled "smooth pipes." For example, at $Re = 10^5$, $f \approx 0.018$. The Blasius equation $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ is valid here.

(iii) **Fully-rough turbulent flow (high $Re$, $\varepsilon/D$ dominates):**
The friction factor curves become horizontal (independent of $Re$) — this is the **fully-rough zone**. The $f$ value is read from the appropriate $\varepsilon/D$ asymptote. For example, at $\varepsilon/D = 0.001$ in the fully-rough zone, $f \approx 0.020$. This is the constant-friction-factor limit used for very long rough pipes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing Darcy ($f$) vs Fanning ($4f$) friction factors:** They differ by a factor of 4. If the problem states "Fanning friction factor" or uses the symbol $4f$, the Darcy–Weisbach equation becomes $h_f = \frac{4f L V^2}{2gD}$. Mixing these is the **#1 cause of wrong answers** in this module.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute $Re$ first:** Many students directly use the Darcy–Weisbach equation with an assumed $f$ value. The examiner expects explicit identification of flow regime.
> 3. **Using Hagen–Poiseuille for turbulent flow:** This gives a drastically lower (and physically wrong) head loss. Always check $Re < 2000$ before applying.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** $\mu$ must be in Pa·s (or kg/m·s), $D$ in m, $V$ in m/s, $L$ in m. Mixing mm and m is a frequent error.
> 5. **Forgetting to use kinematic viscosity for $Re$:** $Re = VD/\nu$ requires $\nu$ in m²/s, not $\mu$ in Pa·s.
> 6. **Ignoring relative roughness in turbulent flow:** Students use $f = 64/Re$ even for turbulent flow. In the turbulent regime, the roughness $\varepsilon/D$ must be considered via the Moody chart or Colebrook equation.
> 7. **Pressure drop vs head loss confusion:** The question may ask for "pressure drop" (in Pa) or "head loss" (in m). They differ by a factor of $\rho g$. Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Head Loss Due to Friction (Module 4):**

- **Darcy–Weisbach Equation (Universal):** $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ — works for both laminar and turbulent flow; the only variable that changes is $f$.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Laminar only):** $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$ — derived analytically, no empirical input needed.
- **Laminar Darcy Friction Factor:** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ — independent of roughness, function of $Re$ only.
- **Blasius (Smooth Turbulent, $Re < 10^5$):** $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$.
- **Colebrook–White (Transition + Turbulent, all roughness):** $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ — implicit, requires iteration.
- **Swamee–Jain (Explicit Approximation):** $f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$ — preferred for direct numerical answer.
- **Reynolds Number Definition:** $Re = \dfrac{VD}{\nu} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$.
- **Regime Boundaries:** Laminar ($Re < 2000$), Transitional ($2000 \le Re \le 4000$), Turbulent ($Re > 4000$).
- **Major Loss (Friction) vs Minor Loss (Fittings):** $h_{total} = h_f + \sum h_m = h_f + \sum K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$.
- **Pressure Drop Conversion:** $\Delta p = \rho g h_f$.
- **Wall Shear Stress from $f$:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{8}$ (Darcy) or $\tau_w = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{2}$ (Fanning).
- **Friction Slope:** $S_f = \dfrac{h_f}{L} = f \dfrac{V^2}{2gD}$ — slope of the EGL/HGL.
- **Moody Chart = Universal Lookup Tool:** $f$ vs $Re$, parameterized by $\varepsilon/D$.
- **KTU Numerical Workflow:** (1) Compute $V$ from $Q$ and $A$, (2) Compute $Re$, (3) Identify regime, (4) Determine $f$, (5) Apply Darcy–Weisbach, (6) Report $h_f$ in m and $\Delta p$ in Pa.
- **Darcy vs Fanning Factor Trap:** $f_{Darcy} = 4 \times f_{Fanning}$ — always verify notation in the problem statement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hagen Poiseuille Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hagen Poiseuille Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation** is a fundamental physical law in fluid mechanics that quantifies the **steady, laminar, incompressible flow of a viscous Newtonian fluid** through a straight, horizontal circular pipe of constant cross-section. It expresses a direct mathematical relationship between the **volumetric flow rate** ($Q$) and the **pressure gradient** ($\Delta P / L$) driving the flow.

The standard canonical form of the equation is:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi D^{4} \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$$

where $R$ and $D$ are the pipe radius and diameter respectively, $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** of the fluid, and $L$ is the length of the pipe over which the pressure drop $\Delta P$ occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is classified as a **high-yield derivation topic** under Module 4 (Viscous Flow in Pipes). It is a **mandatory derivation** in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE), often tested for full 14 marks. Mastering the assumptions and step-by-step integration of the Navier-Stokes equation is non-negotiable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake through a **thin paper straw** versus a **wide bubble tea straw**. The wider straw delivers the milkshake effortlessly, but the thin straw requires you to suck much harder. Why? The Hagen-Poiseuille equation mathematically explains this everyday experience.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Straw Analogy Explained**
> - The flow rate $Q$ depends on the **fourth power of the radius** ($R^4$). Double the straw's diameter, and the flow rate increases by a factor of **16** ($2^4$).
> - The fluid's viscosity $\mu$ acts as internal friction; a thicker fluid (honey vs. water) resists flow proportionally.
> - A longer straw ($L$) means more wall friction, so the flow decreases.
> - A higher pressure difference $\Delta P$ (sucking harder) increases flow linearly.

The equation is also known as the **"Poiseuille's Law"** or the **"Hagen-Poiseuille Flow"** law, named after German engineer **Gotthilf Heinrich Ludwig Hagen** (1839) and French physiologist **Jean Léonard Marie Poiseuille** (1840), who independently arrived at the result while studying blood flow in capillaries and water flow in tubes.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** Measured in **Pascal-seconds (Pa·s)** or equivalently **N·s/m²** or **kg/(m·s)**. For water at 20°C, $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
- **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** $\nu = \mu / \rho$, measured in **m²/s**.
- **Standard Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- **Pipe Radius/Diameter:** Expressed in **meters (m)** for SI consistency.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Unit Consistency Rule:** If $D$ is given in mm, you **must** convert it to meters before substituting into the equation. A common KTU mark-deduction error arises from using mixed units such as mm and m in the same expression.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Assumptions (The Foundation of the Derivation)

The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is **valid only** when the following conditions are satisfied simultaneously. Any violation of these conditions invalidates the result, and KTU examiners frequently award marks for explicitly stating them.

1. **Steady Flow:** The flow parameters (velocity, pressure) at any point do not change with time: $\partial \vec{V} / \partial t = 0$.
2. **Laminar Flow Regime:** The Reynolds number must satisfy $Re < 2300$ for pipe flow. Streamlines are smooth, parallel, and non-intersecting.
3. **Incompressible Fluid:** The density $\rho$ is constant. This is valid for liquids and gases at low Mach numbers ($Ma < 0.3$).
4. **Newtonian Fluid:** The fluid obeys Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$. The viscosity is independent of shear rate.
5. **Horizontal Pipe:** The pipe is horizontal so that gravitational body forces do not create a pressure gradient along the flow direction. (Vertical pipe variants are extensions of this.)
6. **Constant Cross-Section:** The pipe has a uniform circular cross-section throughout the length $L$.
7. **Fully Developed Flow:** The velocity profile does not change along the axial direction, i.e., $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$.
8. **No-Slip Condition:** The fluid velocity is zero at the pipe wall ($v_z = 0$ at $r = R$).
9. **Negligible Entrance Effects:** The flow is observed far enough from the pipe entrance that entrance/exit disturbances are absent.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of the Derivation Strategy

The derivation proceeds through a structured logical pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Momentum Equation Reduction:** Begin with the **Navier-Stokes equation** in cylindrical coordinates. Apply the assumptions above to eliminate all terms, leaving a single ordinary differential equation.
- **Step 2 — First Integration:** Integrate the ODE once with respect to $r$. Apply the **boundary condition at the pipe axis** ($r = 0$) where the velocity gradient must be finite. This eliminates the integration constant.
- **Step 3 — Second Integration:** Integrate again. Apply the **no-slip condition at the wall** ($v_z = 0$ at $r = R$) to evaluate the second integration constant.
- **Step 4 — Velocity Profile:** The result is a **parabolic velocity profile** known as **Poiseuille flow**.
- **Step 5 — Flow Rate Calculation:** Integrate the velocity profile over the pipe's cross-sectional area to obtain the volumetric flow rate $Q$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every essential equation you must memorize for KTU examinations on this topic.

| **S. No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units (SI)** | **Description** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | m³/s | The canonical Hagen-Poiseuille equation |
| 2 | Flow Rate (Diameter form) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{4} \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Equivalent form using diameter |
| 3 | Maximum Velocity | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$ | m/s | Velocity at the pipe centerline ($r = 0$) |
| 4 | Average Velocity | $v_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{8 \mu L}$ | m/s | $v_{avg} = v_{max} / 2$ |
| 5 | Velocity Profile | $v_z(r) = v_{max} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$ | m/s | Parabolic distribution |
| 6 | Shear Stress Distribution | $\tau(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2 L}$ | Pa | Linear variation with radius |
| 7 | Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L}$ | Pa | Maximum shear stress at $r = R$ |
| 8 | Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | Pa | Useful for pump-power calculations |
| 9 | Fanning Friction Factor | $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ | dimensionless | For laminar flow in smooth pipes |
| 10 | Darcy Friction Factor | $f_D = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | dimensionless | Related to Fanning: $f_D = 4f$ |
| 11 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Determines flow regime |
| 12 | Friction Head Loss | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{\rho g D^{2}}$ | m | Head loss due to viscous friction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Reminder:** To convert between Fanning and Darcy friction factors, use $f_{Darcy} = 4 \, f_{Fanning}$. KTU questions may use either convention — always verify which one is being referenced.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is the cornerstone of several engineering and biomedical disciplines:

- **Biomedical Engineering & Hemodynamics:** Models blood flow through arteries and capillaries, though it requires modifications for non-Newtonian blood rheology.
- **Chemical Process Engineering:** Calculates pressure drops in pipelines carrying viscous fluids (oils, polymer solutions, etc.).
- **Microfluidics & Lab-on-a-Chip Devices:** Predicts flow in microchannels used in diagnostic devices and inkjet printer heads.
- **HVAC & Plumbing Design:** Estimates pumping power required to circulate water through long pipe networks.
- **Petroleum Engineering:** Calculates flow rates in oil pipelines and reservoir rocks.
- **Lubrication Theory:** Forms the basis of hydrodynamic bearing analysis (Reynolds lubrication equation).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

We begin with the **Navier-Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates** for the z-component (axial direction), assuming the velocity field has only one non-zero component: $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = 0$, $v_z = v_z(r, z)$ for an incompressible Newtonian fluid.

The full z-momentum equation in cylindrical coordinates is:

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r} \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial \theta^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial z^{2}}\right] + \rho g_z$$

Now we systematically apply the assumptions listed in Section 2.1:

**Assumption 1 — Steady Flow:** $\partial v_z / \partial t = 0$.

**Assumption 2 — Laminar, Axial Flow Only:** $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = 0$, so $v_r \cdot \partial v_z / \partial r = 0$ and $v_\theta \cdot \partial v_z / \partial \theta = 0$.

**Assumption 3 — Fully Developed Flow:** The velocity profile does not vary with $z$, so $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$, and therefore $v_z \cdot \partial v_z / \partial z = 0$. Also, $\partial^2 v_z / \partial z^2 = 0$.

**Assumption 4 — Axisymmetric Flow:** By symmetry, $v_z$ does not depend on $\theta$, so $\partial^2 v_z / \partial \theta^2 = 0$.

**Assumption 5 — Horizontal Pipe:** $g_z = 0$ (gravity has no component along the flow direction).

Substituting all these simplifications, the left-hand side becomes zero, and the right-hand side reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right)\right]$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z}$$

> Since $v_z = v_z(r)$ and $P = P(z)$ only, the partial derivatives become ordinary derivatives:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dz}$$

This is the **governing ordinary differential equation** for Hagen-Poiseuille flow.

---

### 3.2 First Integration

Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate with respect to $r$:

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2} + C_1$$

Divide by $r$:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \cdot r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Apply Boundary Condition 1 (Axis Symmetry):** At the centerline $r = 0$, the velocity gradient must be finite (otherwise the velocity would be infinite or undefined). The term $C_1 / r$ would blow up as $r \to 0$ unless $C_1 = 0$.

Therefore:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \cdot r$$

---

### 3.3 Second Integration

Integrate both sides with respect to $r$:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2} + C_2 = \frac{1}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \cdot r^{2} + C_2$$

**Apply Boundary Condition 2 (No-Slip at Wall):** At $r = R$, the fluid velocity is zero relative to the wall:

$$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \cdot R^{2} + C_2$$

$$C_2 = -\frac{1}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \cdot R^{2}$$

Substituting back:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \left(r^{2} - R^{2}\right) = -\frac{1}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dz} \left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

Since flow occurs in the direction of decreasing pressure, $dP/dz < 0$. We define the **pressure drop** $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 > 0$ over the length $L$, so $dP/dz = -\Delta P / L$:

$$\boxed{v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)}$$

This is the **parabolic velocity profile** characteristic of Poiseuille flow.

---

### 3.4 Maximum and Average Velocities

**Maximum Velocity (at centerline, $r = 0$):**

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^{2}}{4\mu L}$$

**Average Velocity (integral of profile divided by area):**

$$v_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2}{R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) r \, dr$$

$$v_{avg} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L R^{2}} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L R^{2}} \left[\frac{R^{2} \cdot r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R}$$

$$v_{avg} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L R^{2}} \left[\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right] = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L R^{2}} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^{2}}{8\mu L}$$

$$\boxed{v_{avg} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^{2}}{8\mu L} = \frac{v_{max}}{2}}$$

The ratio $v_{avg} = v_{max} / 2$ is a hallmark signature of fully developed laminar pipe flow.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of the Volumetric Flow Rate

The volumetric flow rate is obtained by integrating the velocity profile over the pipe's cross-sectional area:

$$Q = \int_{A} v_z \, dA = \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8\mu L}}$$

Expressing in terms of diameter $D = 2R$, so $R^4 = D^4/16$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi (D^{4}/16) \Delta P}{8\mu L} = \frac{\pi D^{4} \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** in its complete form.

---

### 3.6 Derivation of the Shear Stress Distribution

Using Newton's law of viscosity in cylindrical coordinates, the shear stress on a cylindrical fluid element is:

$$\tau_{rz} = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\mu \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot r = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

The negative sign indicates the stress direction. Taking the magnitude:

$$\boxed{\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}}$$

**At the wall ($r = R$):** The maximum wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$$

The shear stress varies **linearly** with radius — zero at the centerline and maximum at the wall.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Key Numerical Relationships

| **Quantity** | **Ratio to $v_{max}$** | **Ratio to $\tau_w$** | **Physical Meaning** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Centerline velocity ($r=0$) | $1.00 \, v_{max}$ | — | Maximum velocity at the pipe axis |
| Average velocity | $0.50 \, v_{max}$ | — | Cross-sectional mean velocity |
| Shear stress at center ($r=0$) | — | $0.00 \, \tau_w$ | Zero shear at the axis |
| Shear stress at wall ($r=R$) | — | $1.00 \, \tau_w$ | Maximum viscous friction |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: The Derivation Pipeline

The following Mermaid flow diagram illustrates the logical pipeline for deriving the Hagen-Poiseuille equation. Each block represents a transformation step, and arrows show the operational flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Start: Full Navier-Stokes in Cylindrical Coordinates] --> stepB[Apply 9 Governing Assumptions]
    stepB --> stepC[Reduce to ODE in r and z]
    stepC[Reduced ODE: 1 by r times d/dr of r dvz/dr equals 1 over mu dP/dz] --> stepD[First Integration with respect to r]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply BC1: Finite Gradient at r equals 0]
    stepE --> stepF[Second Integration with respect to r]
    stepF --> stepG[Apply BC2: No Slip at r equals R]
    stepG --> resultH[Parabolic Velocity Profile vz of r]
    resultH --> stepI[Integrate Profile over Cross Section]
    stepI --> resultJ[Hagen Poiseuille Equation]
    resultJ --> outK[Q equals pi R to the power 4 delta P over 8 mu L]

    style inputA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,stroke-width:2px
    style resultH fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px
    style outK fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c,stroke-width:2px
    style stepE fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:1px
    style stepG fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Real-World Application Mapping

The following diagram illustrates how the Hagen-Poiseuille equation serves as a central analytical block in various engineering application domains, with the flow of information clearly mapped between the input parameters, the core equation, and the derived outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pipe Geometry: R, D, L] --> E[Core Block: Hagen Poiseuille Equation]
    B[Fluid Properties: mu, rho] --> E
    C[Operating Condition: delta P] --> E
    D[Operating Condition: Q] --> E

    E --> F[Output 1: Flow Rate Q]
    E --> G[Output 2: Pressure Drop delta P]
    E --> H[Output 3: Velocity Profile]
    E --> I[Output 4: Wall Shear Stress]
    E --> J[Output 5: Pumping Power]

    F --> K1[Application 1: Pipeline Design]
    G --> K2[Application 2: Pump Sizing]
    H --> K3[Application 3: Microreactor Analysis]
    I --> K4[Application 4: Lubrication Bearing]
    J --> K5[Application 5: Energy Audit]

    style E fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:3px
    style F fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c
    style G fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c
    style H fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c
    style I fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c
    style J fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c
```

### 4.3 Velocity Profile Visualization (Schematic Block)

The following conceptual block represents the parabolic velocity profile $v_z(r) = v_{max}[1 - (r/R)^2]$ along the pipe cross-section.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0[Centerline r equals 0] --> V1[Velocity equals Vmax Maximum]
    R25[Radius r equals R over 4] --> V2[Velocity equals 15 over 16 Vmax]
    R50[Radius r equals R over 2] --> V3[Velocity equals 3 over 4 Vmax]
    R75[Radius r equals 3R over 4] --> V4[Velocity equals 7 over 16 Vmax]
    R100[Pipe Wall r equals R] --> V5[Velocity equals 0 No Slip]

    V1 --> M0[Parabolic Shape Mathematical Form]
    V2 --> M0
    V3 --> M0
    V4 --> M0
    V5 --> M0

    style V1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style V5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style M0 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
