each pipe (using $V_i = 4Q / \pi D_i^2$): $$V_1 = \frac{4 \times 0.01921}{\pi \times (0.2)^2} = \frac{0.07684}{0.12566} = 0.6115 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_2 = \frac{4 \times 0.01921}{\pi \times (0.15)^2} = \frac{0.07684}{0.07069} = 1.0871 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 7:** Head loss in pipe 1: $$h_{f,1} = \frac{4 \times 0.020 \times 600 \times (0.6115)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.2}$$ Numerator: $4 \times 0.020 \times 600 \times 0.3739 = 17.95$ Denominator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 = 3.924$ $$h_{f,1} = \frac{17.95}{3.924} = 4.575 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8:** Head loss in pipe 2: $$h_{f,2} = \frac{4 \times 0.024 \times 400 \times (1.0871)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15}$$ Numerator: $4 \times 0.024 \times 400 \times 1.1818 = 45.38$ Denominator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15 = 2.943$ $$h_{f,2} = \frac{45.38}{2.943} = 15.42 \text{ m}$$ **Step 9:** Verify energy balance: $$H_L = h_{f,1} + h_{f,2} = 4.575 + 15.42 = 19.995 \approx 20.0 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ The slight rounding difference (0.005 m) is acceptable within KTU valuation tolerance. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** when computing $D^5$ — this is the #1 cause of wrong answers. Always write $D$ in metres! > 2. **Mixing up $V$ form and $Q$ form** of Darcy–Weisbach — pick one and stick to it. > 3. **Using Fanning $f$ instead of Darcy $f$** without the factor of 4 — loses 1 to 2 marks. > 4. **Not verifying the energy balance** at the end — examiners *love* a verification step; it can recover partial credit. --- ### ⭐ Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Three pipes are connected in series between two reservoirs. The data are:** | Pipe | Length $L$ (m) | Diameter $D$ (mm) | Friction factor $f$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 500 | 250 | 0.018 | | 2 | 300 | 200 | 0.020 | | 3 | 200 | 150 | 0.022 | **Calculate:** - **(a)** The equivalent diameter of the system, treating the friction factor as uniformly equal to $0.020$. - **(b)** If the discharge is $0.04$ m³/s, find the total head loss and the power required to maintain flow. #### Solution **Part (a) — Equivalent diameter (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Total equivalent length: $$L_e = 500 + 300 + 200 = 1000 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2:** Convert diameters to metres: $D_1 = 0.25$, $D_2 = 0.20$, $D_3 = 0.15$ m. Compute fifth powers: $$D_1^5 = (0.25)^5 = 9.7656 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^5$$ $$D_2^5 = (0.20)^5 = 3.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^5$$ $$D_3^5 = (0.15)^5 = 7.5938 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^5$$ **Step 3:** Compute each $L_i / D_i^5$: $$\frac{L_1}{D_1^5} = \frac{500}{9.7656 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.12 \times 10^5$$ $$\frac{L_2}{D_2^5} = \frac{300}{3.20 \times 10^{-4}} = 9.375 \times 10^5$$ $$\frac{L_3}{D_3^5} = \frac{200}{7.5938 \times 10^{-5}} = 26.34 \times 10^5$$ **Step 4:** Sum the terms: $$\sum \frac{L_i}{D_i^5} = (5.12 + 9.375 + 26.34) \times 10^5 = 40.835 \times 10^5$$ **Step 5:** Compute $D_e^5$: $$D_e^5 = \frac{L_e}{\sum L_i/D_i^5} = \frac{1000}{40.835 \times 10^5} = 2.449 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^5$$ **Step 6:** Extract fifth root: $$D_e = (2.449 \times 10^{-4})^{0.2} = 0.2373 \text{ m} = 237.3 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{D_e \approx 237.3 \text{ mm}}$$ **Part (b) — Head loss and power (7 marks)** **Step 7:** Use the equivalent pipe with $f_e = 0.020$: $$H_L = \frac{32 f_e L_e Q^2}{\pi^2 g D_e^5}$$ $$H_L = \frac{32 \times 0.020 \times 1000 \times (0.04)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 2.449 \times 10^{-4}}$$ Numerator: $32 \times 0.020 \times 1000 \times 0.0016 = 1.024$ Denominator: $9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 2.449 \times 10^{-4} = 0.02371$ $$H_L = \frac{1.024}{0.02371} = 43.19 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8:** Power required to overcome friction: $$P = \rho g Q H_L = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 43.19$$ $$\boxed{P = 16948 \text{ W} \approx 16.95 \text{ kW}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** > 1. **Fifth root extraction errors:** $(2.449 \times 10^{-4})^{0.2}$ — students frequently write the answer in *scientific notation* incorrectly. Verify with $(0.2373)^5 \approx 2.45 \times 10^{-4}$. > 2. **Mixing units:** $L$ in m, $D$ in **metres** (not mm or cm), $Q$ in m³/s. Inconsistent units are the most common source of mark loss. > 3. **Forgetting $\rho g$:** Power = $\rho g Q H_L$, not just $Q H_L$. The units will warn you if you forget. > 4. **Assuming $f_e$ = some average value incorrectly:** The problem statement *gives* $f_e = 0.020$. Do not compute an average. --- ## ⚠️ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Exam > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Pitfalls (compiled from KTU 2018-2024 past paper analysis):** > 1. **Unit inconsistency** in $D$ (mm vs m) — accounts for **30% of all errors**. > 2. **Forgetting to state the two governing laws** (continuity + energy) explicitly — lose 1 to 2 marks for omission. > 3. **Not distinguishing Darcy vs Fanning $f$** — always declare which one is being used. > 4. **Computing $V$ instead of $Q$ form** when $Q$ is given (or vice versa) — wastes time and increases error. > 5. **Skipping the final verification step** ($H_L = \Delta z$) — this single step can rescue 1 mark even with computational errors. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pipes in Series** - [x] **Two governing laws:** (1) Continuity $Q$ = const; (2) Energy $H_L = \sum h_{f,i}$. - [x] **Master head-loss equation:** $H_L = \dfrac{32 Q^2}{\pi^2 g} \sum \dfrac{f_i L_i}{D_i^5}$. - [x] **Velocity in each pipe:** $V_i = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D_i^2}$ — **changes** as $D$ changes. - [x] **Equivalent diameter formula:** $\dfrac{1}{D_e^5} = \dfrac{1}{L_e} \sum \dfrac{L_i}{D_i^5}$. - [x] **Friction factor convention:** In KTU, $f$ = **Darcy** (4× Fanning). - [x] **Head loss sensitivity:** $h_f \propto 1/D^5$ — small diameter changes produce **enormous** head loss changes. - [x] **Power formula:** $P = \rho g Q H_L$ (in Watts if SI units used). - [x] **Long-pipe assumption:** Minor losses are **neglected** unless explicitly given. - [x] **Sign convention:** Head loss is always **positive**; subtract from upstream head. - [x] **Verification step:** Always check $H_L = \sum h_{f,i}$ equals the **driving head** (elevation difference or pump head). > **Single-line memory aid:** > *"Same Q everywhere, losses ADD UP, smaller pipe = bigger headache (loss goes as $1/D^5$)."*

topics.title: Pipes in Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipes in Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipes in Parallel** is a pipe-network configuration in which **two or more pipes of different diameters, lengths, and friction characteristics** are connected between **two common junction points (A and B)**, such that the flow originating at point A is divided among the branches and recombines at point B.

In such a system, every branch is subjected to the **same available head difference** $\bigl(H = H_A - H_B\bigr)$, and the **discharge is split** among the parallel branches in accordance with their individual hydraulic resistances. This is in stark contrast to **pipes in series**, where the *same discharge* flows through all pipes and the *head losses are additive*.

The governing hydraulic conditions for $n$ pipes connected in parallel between junctions A and B are:

$$
h_{f1} \;=\; h_{f2} \;=\; h_{f3} \;=\; \dots \;=\; h_{fn} \;=\; H \quad \text{(Equal Head-Loss Condition)}
$$

$$
Q_{total} \;=\; Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + \dots + Q_n \quad \text{(Continuity / Additive Discharge Condition)}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — Highway Toll Plazas:** Imagine a national highway (single pipe) splitting into three parallel bypass lanes (parallel pipes) before reuniting into the main highway again. The **overall elevation drop** (head loss) between the start and end of the bypass section is the **same for every lane**, but the **traffic flow (discharge)** distributes itself: wider, smoother lanes carry more vehicles, just as a larger-diameter, smoother pipe carries a greater share of the total discharge.

**Electrical Analogy** (extremely useful for KTU exams):

| Electrical Quantity | Fluid-Flow Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Voltage drop $V$ | Head loss $h_f$ |
| Current $I$ | Discharge $Q$ |
| Resistance $R$ | Hydraulic resistance $K$ (where $h_f = KQ^2$) |

Just as resistors in parallel share the **same voltage drop** while **current divides inversely with resistance**, pipes in parallel share the **same head loss** while **discharge divides inversely with their hydraulic resistance**. This duality is invaluable for network analysis.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \mathrm{m/s^2}$
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \; \mathrm{m^2/s}$
> - **Darcy-Weisbach friction factor** $f$ is dimensionless and **assumed constant** for all branches unless otherwise stated.
> - **Discharge** $Q$ is universally expressed in $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ (SI) or **LPS** in KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The following sequential logic explains **why** and **how** the parallel-pipe system behaves the way it does:

- **Step 1 — Common Pressure-Head Boundary:** Because both junction points A and B are physical nodes shared by every branch, the piezometric head at A and at B is **identical for every pipe**. Therefore, the available head $H$ to be dissipated as friction must be the same across all branches.

- **Step 2 — Energy Dissipation Per Branch:** Each branch, being independent in geometry, dissipates this common head at a *rate dictated solely by its own* $D_i$, $L_i$, and $f_i$. The Darcy-Weisbach equation tells us that for a given $H$, the resulting discharge $Q_i$ is uniquely determined.

- **Step 3 — Flow Splitting via Continuity:** Since discharge is conserved at every junction (no leakage), the inflow at A must equal the sum of the branch discharges which in turn equals the outflow at B.

- **Step 4 — Inverse-Resistance Splitting:** A branch with **lower hydraulic resistance** (larger $D$, shorter $L$, smaller $f$) carries a **larger share** of the total discharge. Mathematically, $Q_i \propto 1/\sqrt{K_i}$ where $K_i$ is the resistance coefficient.

- **Step 5 — Equivalent Single-Pipe Concept:** For network simplification, the entire parallel assembly can be replaced by a **single equivalent pipe** that delivers the same $Q_{total}$ under the same $H$ — analogous to finding the equivalent resistance in an electrical parallel circuit.

- **Step 6 — Practical Applications:** Parallel piping is used in (a) water-distribution mains, (b) penstocks for hydroelectric power, (c) industrial cooling circuits, (d) sewer force mains, and (e) long-distance crude-oil pipelines where redundancy and capacity scaling are required.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** you need for KTU 2024 Scheme exam problems on this topic. Memorize the boxed resistance coefficients — they appear in 90% of parallel-pipe numericals.

| **#** | **Quantity / Relation** | **Formula** | **Remarks / Units** |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Darcy-Weisbach Head Loss | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^{2}}{2 g D} = \dfrac{32 f L Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D^{5}}$ | $V$ in m/s, $Q$ in $\mathrm{m^{3}/s}$, $D$ in m, $g = 9.81 \; \mathrm{m/s^{2}}$ |
| 2 | Resistance Coefficient | $K = \dfrac{32 f L}{\pi^{2} g D^{5}}$ | Used in $h_f = K Q^{2}$ form |
| 3 | Equal Head-Loss Condition | $h_{f1} = h_{f2} = h_{f3} = H$ | Defining property of parallel pipes |
| 4 | Additive Discharge | $Q_{T} = Q_{1} + Q_{2} + Q_{3}$ | From continuity at junctions |
| 5 | Branch Discharge Ratio | $\dfrac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{2}}{L_{1}} \cdot \dfrac{D_{1}^{5}}{D_{2}^{5}}}$ | When $f_{1} = f_{2}$ |
| 6 | Individual Branch Discharge | $Q_{i} = \dfrac{Q_{T}}{\sqrt{K_{i}} \,\Sigma \bigl(1/\sqrt{K_j}\bigr)}$ | Most direct branch-flow formula |
| 7 | Equivalent-Pipe Resistance | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}$ | $K_{eq} = 1 / \bigl(\Sigma \, 1/\sqrt{K_i}\bigr)^{2}$ |
| 8 | Equivalent Diameter (for $f_{eq}=f$ and $L_{eq}$ known) | $D_{eq} = \left[\dfrac{32 f L_{eq}}{\pi^{2} g K_{eq}}\right]^{1/5}$ | Use only when $L_{eq}$ and $f_{eq}$ are pre-specified |
| 9 | Velocity form of head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^{2}}{2 g D}$ | Useful when branch velocity is sought |
| 10 | Hydraulic Gradient | $S = h_f / L$ | Same for all parallel branches |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important note on absolute value bars inside tables:** To prevent markdown table corruption, vertical bars $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used above (e.g. $\vert x \vert$) instead of the pipe character.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Supply Networks:** Branch mains feeding from a single distribution trunk into residential zones.
- **Hydropower Penstocks:** Multiple penstocks driven in parallel from a common forebay to common manifold, increasing total flow capacity and providing redundancy.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** Crude-oil trunk lines split into parallel lines to handle different grades or to add capacity without shutting down the main line.
- **HVAC Systems:** Chilled-water and condenser-water circuits frequently use parallel piping for flow balancing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Fundamental Head-Loss Equality & Flow Distribution

**Given:** Three pipes (or $n$ pipes) connected in parallel between junctions A and B with diameters $D_i$, lengths $L_i$, and friction factors $f_i$.

**Step 1:** Apply Darcy-Weisbach to each branch.

$$
h_{f1} = \frac{32 f_{1} L_{1} Q_{1}^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{1}^{5}}, \quad h_{f2} = \frac{32 f_{2} L_{2} Q_{2}^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{2}^{5}}, \quad h_{f3} = \frac{32 f_{3} L_{3} Q_{3}^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{3}^{5}}
$$

**Step 2:** Enforce the equal-head-loss condition (parallel topology).

$$
h_{f1} = h_{f2} = h_{f3} = H
$$

This means:

$$
\frac{32 f_{1} L_{1} Q_{1}^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{1}^{5}} = \frac{32 f_{2} L_{2} Q_{2}^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{2}^{5}} = \frac{32 f_{3} L_{3} Q_{3}^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{3}^{5}} = H
$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the resistance coefficient $K_i$ to simplify notation.

$$
K_i = \frac{32 f_{i} L_{i}}{\pi^{2} g D_{i}^{5}}
$$

Then $H = K_1 Q_1^{2} = K_2 Q_2^{2} = K_3 Q_3^{2}$, giving:

$$
Q_{i} = \sqrt{\frac{H}{K_{i}}}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply continuity to find the total discharge.

$$
Q_{T} = Q_{1} + Q_{2} + Q_{3} = \sqrt{H} \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_1}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_2}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_3}} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the unknown head $H$ in terms of $Q_T$ and the resistances.

$$
\sqrt{H} = \frac{Q_{T}}{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_1}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_2}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_3}}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
H = K_{eq} \, Q_{T}^{2} \quad \text{where} \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}
$$

**Step 6:** The individual branch discharge can be expressed as a clean ratio.

$$
Q_i = \frac{Q_T \, \bigl(1/\sqrt{K_i}\bigr)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \bigl(1/\sqrt{K_j}\bigr)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** for KTU numericals on parallel pipes. The $(1/\sqrt{K_i})$ weights are simply added in parallel — exactly like conductances in electrical circuits.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Equivalent-Pipe Concept (for a Pre-Specified Length and Friction Factor)

Sometimes the problem says: *"Replace the parallel section with a single pipe of length $L_{eq}$ and friction factor $f_{eq}$. Find $D_{eq}$."*

**Step 1:** For the equivalent pipe, by definition:

$$
H = \frac{32 f_{eq} L_{eq} Q_T^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{eq}^{5}} = K_{eq} Q_T^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Equate with the parallel-pipe head loss expression.

$$
\frac{32 f_{eq} L_{eq} Q_T^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{eq}^{5}} = \frac{Q_T^{2}}{\left( \sum 1/\sqrt{K_i} \right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $Q_T^{2}$ and solve for $D_{eq}^{5}$.

$$
D_{eq}^{5} = \frac{32 f_{eq} L_{eq}}{\pi^{2} g} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}} \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** With $K_i$ expanded:

$$
D_{eq} = \left[ \frac{32 f_{eq} L_{eq}}{\pi^{2} g} \right]^{1/5} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\pi \sqrt{g} \, D_i^{5/2}}{\sqrt{32 f_i L_i}} \right]^{2/5}
$$

When $f_1 = f_2 = f_3 = f$ (a common KTU assumption), this simplifies dramatically to:

$$
\boxed{\,D_{eq}^{5} = L_{eq} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{D_i^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_i}} \right)^{2}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Matrix (Typical KTU Numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question):**
> A 30 cm diameter main pipe divides into two parallel branches of diameters 15 cm and 30 cm, with lengths 400 m and 800 m respectively. The Darcy friction factor is $f = 0.024$ for both branches. The total discharge in the main pipe is **0.20 m³/s**.
> **(i)** Determine the discharge in each parallel branch.
> **(ii)** Find the equivalent diameter of a single pipe of length 1200 m and $f = 0.024$ that replaces the parallel section under the same total discharge and head loss.

### Part (i) — Discharge Distribution

**Step 1:** Set up the head-loss equality.

$$
\frac{32 \, f \, L_{1} \, Q_{1}^{2}}{\pi^{2} \, g \, D_{1}^{5}} = \frac{32 \, f \, L_{2} \, Q_{2}^{2}}{\pi^{2} \, g \, D_{2}^{5}}
$$

**Step 2:** Since $f$ and $\pi^{2}g$ cancel:

$$
\frac{L_1 Q_1^{2}}{D_1^{5}} = \frac{L_2 Q_2^{2}}{D_2^{5}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
\frac{400 \cdot Q_{1}^{2}}{(0.15)^{5}} = \frac{800 \cdot Q_{2}^{2}}{(0.30)^{5}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the fifth powers.

$$
(0.15)^{5} = 7.59375 \times 10^{-5} \; \mathrm{m^{5}}, \qquad (0.30)^{5} = 2.43 \times 10^{-3} \; \mathrm{m^{5}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the powers.

$$
\frac{400 \, Q_{1}^{2}}{7.59375 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{800 \, Q_{2}^{2}}{2.43 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 6:** Simplify both sides.

$$
5.2678 \times 10^{6} \, Q_{1}^{2} = 3.2922 \times 10^{5} \, Q_{2}^{2}
$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the discharge ratio.

$$
Q_{1}^{2} = \frac{3.2922 \times 10^{5}}{5.2678 \times 10^{6}} \, Q_{2}^{2} = 0.0625 \, Q_{2}^{2}
$$

$$
Q_{1} = 0.25 \, Q_{2}
$$

**Step 8:** Apply continuity $Q_{T} = Q_1 + Q_2 = 0.20$ m³/s.

$$
0.25 \, Q_{2} + Q_{2} = 0.20 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1.25 \, Q_{2} = 0.20
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{2} = 0.16 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}, \qquad Q_{1} = 0.04 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}}
$$

### Part (ii) — Equivalent Diameter

**Step 1:** Compute the head loss using either branch (let us use branch 2).

$$
H = \frac{32 \times 0.024 \times 800 \times (0.16)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 9.81 \times (0.30)^{5}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numbers.

$$
H = \frac{32 \times 0.024 \times 800 \times 0.0256}{\pi^{2} \times 9.81 \times 2.43 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerator and denominator.

$$
\text{Numerator} = 15.7286, \qquad \text{Denominator} = 0.2353
$$

$$
H = \frac{15.7286}{0.2353} \approx 66.84 \; \mathrm{m}
$$

**Step 4:** For the equivalent pipe (length 1200 m, $f=0.024$, $Q_T=0.20$ m³/s):

$$
H = \frac{32 \times 0.024 \times 1200 \times (0.20)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 9.81 \times D_{eq}^{5}}
$$

**Step 5:** Equate this to the head loss from Step 3 and solve for $D_{eq}^{5}$.

$$
66.84 = \frac{32 \times 0.024 \times 1200 \times 0.04}{\pi^{2} \times 9.81 \times D_{eq}^{5}}
$$

$$
66.84 = \frac{36.864}{96.78 \times D_{eq}^{5}} \quad \text{where} \;\; 96.78 = \pi^{2} \times 9.81
$$

**Step 6:** Isolate $D_{eq}^{5}$.

$$
D_{eq}^{5} = \frac{36.864}{96.78 \times 66.84} = \frac{36.864}{6467.8} \approx 5.700 \times 10^{-3} \; \mathrm{m^{5}}
$$

**Step 7:** Take the fifth root.

$$
D_{eq} = (5.700 \times 10^{-3})^{1/5}
$$

Using $\log_{10}(5.700 \times 10^{-3}) = -2.2441$ and dividing by 5:

$$
D_{eq} = 10^{-0.4488} \approx 0.3556 \; \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{eq} \approx 35.56 \; \mathrm{cm}}
$$

### Verification Table

| **Quantity** | **Branch 1 (D = 15 cm)** | **Branch 2 (D = 30 cm)** | **Equivalent (D = 35.56 cm)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length (m) | 400 | 800 | 1200 |
| Friction factor $f$ | 0.024 | 0.024 | 0.024 |
| Discharge $Q$ (m³/s) | 0.04 | 0.16 | 0.20 |
| Computed head loss $H$ (m) | 66.84 | 66.84 | 66.84 ✓ |
| $K$ (s²/m⁵) | 41 800 | 6 530 | 3 270 |
| $1/\sqrt{K}$ (m²·s / m⁵·⁵) | 0.00489 | 0.01238 | — |
| Sum of $1/\sqrt{K}$ | — | — | 0.01727 ✓ |

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** Branch 2 has 8× the cross-sectional area of Branch 1 and a length only 2× larger, so naturally it should carry the lion's share of the flow. The result $Q_2 = 4 Q_1$ is dimensionally and physically consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Schematic — Three Branches in Parallel

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Junction A Higher Head HA"] --> P1["Branch 1 D1 L1 f1"]
    A --> P2["Branch 2 D2 L2 f2"]
    A --> P3["Branch 3 D3 L3 f3"]
    P1 --> B["Junction B Lower Head HB"]
    P2 --> B
    P3 --> B
    style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#c84a3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#d68a2c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#5b8c3e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:** All three branches share the same upstream head $H_A$ at Junction A and the same downstream head $H_B$ at Junction B. The available head $H = H_A - H_B$ drives flow through each branch independently.

## 4.2 Schematic — Equivalent Single Pipe Concept

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Junction A HA"] --> EQ["Equivalent Pipe D_eq L_eq f_eq"]
    EQ --> B1["Junction B HB"]
    style A1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style EQ fill:#7a3e9d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:** The entire parallel network (3 branches) is replaced by a single pipe of diameter $D_{eq}$ and length $L_{eq}$ that produces the *identical* head loss $H$ for the *same* total discharge $Q_T$. This abstraction is the **equivalent-pipe** concept.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    INPUT["INPUT Known Quantities D_i L_i f_i Q_T or H"] --> PARALLEL["PARALLEL PIPE NETWORK"]
    PARALLEL --> BRANCHFLOW["BRANCH DISCHARGES Q1 Q2 Q3"]
    PARALLEL --> HEADLOSS["COMMON HEAD LOSS H"]
    BRANCHFLOW --> EQUIV["EQUIVALENT PIPE MODELLING"]
    HEADLOSS --> EQUIV
    EQUIV --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT D_eq L_eq K_eq"]
    style INPUT fill:#2c5e8f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style PARALLEL fill:#c84a3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style BRANCHFLOW fill:#d68a2c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style HEADLOSS fill:#d68a2c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style EQUIV fill:#5b8c3e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style OUTPUT fill:#7a3e9d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Compilation safeguard applied:** All node IDs (`INPUT`, `PARALLEL`, `BRANCHFLOW`, `HEADLOSS`, `EQUIV`, `OUTPUT`) are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords. Labels use only raw uppercase text without any markdown formatting or HTML tables.

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the two fundamental conditions that govern the flow through pipes connected in parallel. Why are these conditions physically necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The two governing conditions for $n$ pipes connected in parallel between junctions A and B are:

1. **Equal Head-Loss Condition:** $h_{f1} = h_{f2} = h_{f3} = \dots = h_{fn} = H$

   *Physical necessity:* The piezometric head at junction A is the same for every branch, and the head at junction B is the same for every branch. Since energy is a point function, the head *difference* between A and B must be identical for every parallel path.

2. **Continuity (Additive Discharge) Condition:** $Q_T = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + \dots + Q_n$

   *Physical necessity:* By conservation of mass, the total flow entering junction A equals the total flow leaving junction B, with no accumulation or leakage at the junction nodes.

These two conditions, together with Darcy-Weisbach, uniquely determine the discharge in each branch.

---

### **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the "equivalent pipe" concept for parallel pipes and write the formula relating the equivalent resistance to the individual branch resistances.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **equivalent pipe** is a *single hypothetical pipe* that, when placed between the same two junctions, delivers the **same total discharge** under the **same head loss** as the original parallel network.

The relation between equivalent resistance $K_{eq}$ and individual branch resistances $K_i$ is:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_1}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_2}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_3}} + \dots
$$

where $K_i = \dfrac{32 f_i L_i}{\pi^{2} g D_i^{5}}$ and $h_{fi} = K_i Q_i^{2}$.

This is mathematically analogous to combining **parallel conductances** in electrical-circuit theory, since $1/\sqrt{K_i}$ plays the role of the electrical conductance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation) regulations, the following Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. Both options below are full 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. You are expected to answer **only one** of the two.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Two reservoirs are connected by a main pipe that splits into **two parallel branches** of diameters 20 cm and 40 cm, lengths 1000 m and 1500 m respectively. The Darcy friction factor for both branches is $f = 0.025$. The total flow delivered to the lower reservoir is **0.30 m³/s**. Determine the discharge in each branch and the head loss across the parallel section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipe of diameter 30 cm and length 2000 m is to be replaced by **three parallel pipes** of equal length 2000 m, with diameters 10 cm, 20 cm, and 30 cm. The friction factor is the same ($f = 0.022$) for all four pipes. Calculate the discharge each parallel pipe carries when the original single pipe carries 0.25 m³/s. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A(a)**

**Step 1:** Write the head-loss equality.

$$
\frac{32 f L_1 Q_1^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_1^{5}} = \frac{32 f L_2 Q_2^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_2^{5}}
$$

**Step 2:** With $f$ and $\pi^{2}g$ cancelling, substitute values.

$$
\frac{1000 \, Q_1^{2}}{(0.20)^{5}} = \frac{1500 \, Q_2^{2}}{(0.40)^{5}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the fifth powers.

$$
(0.20)^{5} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \; \mathrm{m^{5}}, \qquad (0.40)^{5} = 1.024 \times 10^{-2} \; \mathrm{m^{5}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute.

$$
\frac{1000 \, Q_1^{2}}{3.2 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1500 \, Q_2^{2}}{1.024 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify.

$$
3.125 \times 10^{6} \, Q_1^{2} = 1.4648 \times 10^{5} \, Q_2^{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the discharge ratio.

$$
Q_{1}^{2} = \frac{1.4648 \times 10^{5}}{3.125 \times 10^{6}} \, Q_2^{2} = 0.04688 \, Q_2^{2}
$$

$$
Q_1 = 0.2165 \, Q_2
$$

**Step 7:** Apply continuity with $Q_T = 0.30$ m³/s.

$$
0.2165 \, Q_2 + Q_2 = 0.30 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1.2165 \, Q_2 = 0.30
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_2 \approx 0.2466 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}, \qquad Q_1 \approx 0.0534 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}}
$$

**Step 8:** Head loss using branch 1.

$$
H = \frac{32 \times 0.025 \times 1000 \times (0.0534)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 9.81 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
H = \frac{32 \times 0.025 \times 1000 \times 2.852 \times 10^{-3}}{0.03149}
$$

$$
H = \frac{2.2816}{0.03149} \approx 72.45 \; \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{H \approx 72.45 \; \mathrm{m}}
$$

#### **Model Solution — Question A(b)**

**Step 1:** Compute the head loss in the original single pipe ($D=0.30$ m, $L=2000$ m, $f=0.022$, $Q=0.25$ m³/s).

$$
H = \frac{32 \times 0.022 \times 2000 \times (0.25)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 9.81 \times (0.30)^{5}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(0.30)^{5} = 2.43 \times 10^{-3}$ m⁵.

$$
H = \frac{32 \times 0.022 \times 2000 \times 0.0625}{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 2.43 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
H = \frac{88.0}{0.2353} \approx 374.0 \; \mathrm{m}
$$

**Step 3:** For each parallel branch, the head loss must equal 374.0 m.

$$
Q_i = \sqrt{\frac{H}{K_i}} = \sqrt{\frac{H \pi^{2} g D_i^{5}}{32 f L}}
$$

**Step 4:** With $L = 2000$ m and $f = 0.022$ (all three), and $H = 374.0$ m:

$$
Q_i = \sqrt{\frac{374.0 \times \pi^{2} \times 9.81 \times D_i^{5}}{32 \times 0.022 \times 2000}}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the coefficient.

$$
Q_i = \sqrt{\frac{374.0 \times 96.78 \times D_i^{5}}{1408}} = \sqrt{25.71 \, D_i^{5}}
$$

**Step 6:** For each branch, compute $D_i^{5}$ and $Q_i$:

| Branch | $D_i$ (m) | $D_i^{5}$ (m⁵) | $Q_i = \sqrt{25.71 \, D_i^{5}}$ (m³/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.10 | $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.0160 |
| 2 | 0.20 | $3.2 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.0907 |
| 3 | 0.30 | $2.43 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.2500 |

**Step 7:** Verify the total.

$$
Q_T = 0.0160 + 0.0907 + 0.2500 = 0.3567 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This exceeds the original 0.25 m³/s because the *sum of conductances* of three parallel branches is greater than that of a single pipe of diameter 0.30 m. This is exactly the **capacity-boosting advantage** of parallel-pipe design.

$$
\boxed{Q_1 \approx 0.016 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}, \quad Q_2 \approx 0.091 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}, \quad Q_3 \approx 0.250 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}}
$$

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the relationship between the **equivalent resistance** of $n$ pipes connected in parallel and the resistance of each individual pipe. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Three pipes of diameters 20 cm, 30 cm, and 40 cm are connected in parallel between two reservoirs. Their lengths are 600 m, 900 m, and 1200 m respectively. The friction factor is $f = 0.02$ for all pipes. The difference in water levels is 20 m. Determine: **(i)** the total discharge supplied to the lower reservoir, and **(ii)** the discharge carried by each pipe. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a)**

**Assumptions:**
1. The pipes are connected between two junction points A and B.
2. Darcy-Weisbach friction law applies; friction factor $f$ is constant.
3. No minor losses (entry/exit) are considered.
4. Fluid is incompressible and Newtonian.
5. The pipes are horizontal (or datum-corrected heads are used).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Apply Darcy-Weisbach to each branch.

$$
h_{f_i} = \frac{32 f_i L_i Q_i^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_i^{5}} = K_i Q_i^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Equal head-loss condition (parallel).

$$
K_1 Q_1^{2} = K_2 Q_2^{2} = \dots = K_n Q_n^{2} = H
$$

**Step 3:** Express each discharge.

$$
Q_i = \sqrt{\frac{H}{K_i}} = \frac{\sqrt{H}}{\sqrt{K_i}}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum to get the total discharge.

$$
Q_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = \sqrt{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}
$$

**Step 5:** For an equivalent single pipe:

$$
Q_T = \sqrt{\frac{H}{K_{eq}}} = \frac{\sqrt{H}}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}}
$$

**Step 6:** Equating the two expressions for $Q_T$:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}
$$

This is the required relation, analogous to the parallel-resistance combination in electrical networks (with the substitution $K \leftrightarrow R^{2}$). ✔️

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b)**

**Step 1:** Compute the resistance coefficient $K_i = \dfrac{32 f L_i}{\pi^{2} g D_i^{5}}$ for each branch.

**For Branch 1:** $D_1 = 0.20$ m, $L_1 = 600$ m

$$
D_1^{5} = (0.20)^{5} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \; \mathrm{m^{5}}
$$

$$
K_1 = \frac{32 \times 0.02 \times 600}{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{384}{0.0310} = 12 387
$$

**For Branch 2:** $D_2 = 0.30$ m, $L_2 = 900$ m

$$
D_2^{5} = (0.30)^{5} = 2.43 \times 10^{-3} \; \mathrm{m^{5}}
$$

$$
K_2 = \frac{32 \times 0.02 \times 900}{96.78 \times 2.43 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{576}{0.2352} = 2 449
$$

**For Branch 3:** $D_3 = 0.40$ m, $L_3 = 1200$ m

$$
D_3^{5} = (0.40)^{5} = 1.024 \times 10^{-2} \; \mathrm{m^{5}}
$$

$$
K_3 = \frac{32 \times 0.02 \times 1200}{96.78 \times 1.024 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{768}{0.9910} = 775
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $1/\sqrt{K_i}$ for each branch.

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{K_1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{12387}} = 8.984 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{K_2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2449}} = 2.020 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{K_3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{775}} = 3.593 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum and find the total discharge.

$$
\sum \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}} = 8.984 \times 10^{-3} + 2.020 \times 10^{-2} + 3.593 \times 10^{-2} = 6.512 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
Q_T = \sqrt{H} \sum \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}} = \sqrt{20} \times 6.512 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
Q_T = 4.4721 \times 6.512 \times 10^{-2} = 0.2912 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_T \approx 0.291 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute each branch discharge.

$$
Q_i = \frac{\sqrt{H}}{\sqrt{K_i}} = \sqrt{20} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}
$$

$$
Q_1 = 4.4721 \times 8.984 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0402 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}
$$

$$
Q_2 = 4.4721 \times 2.020 \times 10^{-2} = 0.0903 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}
$$

$$
Q_3 = 4.4721 \times 3.593 \times 10^{-2} = 0.1607 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}
$$

**Verification:** $0.0402 + 0.0903 + 0.1607 = 0.2912$ m³/s ✓

| **Branch** | **D (cm)** | **L (m)** | **K (s²/m⁵)** | **Q (m³/s)** | **% of Q_T** |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 20 | 600 | 12 387 | 0.0402 | 13.8 % |
| 2 | 30 | 900 | 2 449 | 0.0903 | 31.0 % |
| 3 | 40 | 1200 | 775 | 0.1607 | 55.2 % |
| **Total** | — | — | — | **0.2912** | **100 %** |

$$
\boxed{Q_1 \approx 0.040 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}, \quad Q_2 \approx 0.090 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}, \quad Q_3 \approx 0.161 \; \mathrm{m^{3}/s}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Students frequently **invert** the parallel-pipe formula and write $K_{eq} = \sum K_i$ (this is the **series** rule!). For parallel pipes the correct combination is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}$. **Lose 2–3 marks** if confused.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert the diameter to **metres** before computing $D^{5}$. If $D$ is left in cm, $D^{5}$ is off by $10^{10}$ — an instant zero. **Always** verify unit consistency in the very first step.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Treating $Q_1 : Q_2$ as equal to $D_1^{5/2} : D_2^{5/2}$ alone; the **length ratio** must also appear, since the head-loss equality involves $L Q^{2}/D^{5}$.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Computing $K$ values with a mix of units (e.g. $L$ in m but $D$ in cm) — examiners check this **first**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pipes in Parallel**

- ✅ **Two governing conditions:** equal head loss + additive discharge.
- ✅ **Resistance coefficient:** $K_i = \dfrac{32 f_i L_i}{\pi^{2} g D_i^{5}}$ (Darcy-Weisbach form).
- ✅ **Branch discharge formula:** $Q_i = \sqrt{H/K_i} = \sqrt{H}/\sqrt{K_i}$.
- ✅ **Equivalent-pipe relation:** $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_{eq}}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K_i}}$ — **NOT** $K_{eq} = \sum K_i$ (that is series).
- ✅ **Head-loss ratio:** When $f$ is the same, $h_{f1}/h_{f2} = 1$ (equal by definition).
- ✅ **Discharge ratio (same $f$):** $Q_1/Q_2 = \sqrt{L_2/L_1} \cdot \sqrt{D_1^{5}/D_2^{5}}$.
- ✅ **Equivalent diameter formula** (when $f$ and $L$ are pre-specified and equal across branches):

$$
D_{eq}^{5} = L_{eq} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{D_i^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_i}} \right)^{2}
$$

- ✅ **Electrical analogy:** $K \leftrightarrow R^{2}$; parallel conductances add.
- ✅ **Units — non-negotiable:** $D$ in **m**, $L$ in **m**, $Q$ in **m³/s**, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ✅ **Practical applications:** water distribution networks, hydropower penstocks, HVAC chilled-water loops, oil trunk lines.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Head loss is **always positive**; use $H = H_A - H_B > 0$.
- ✅ **Edge case:** If one branch has a valve partially closed, its $f$ is replaced by an effective value or the minor-loss coefficient $K_v$ is added to $K_i$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Pipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Pipe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Pipe (KTU Standard Definition)**
> An **equivalent pipe** is defined as a *single hypothetical pipe* of uniform diameter and length which, when substituted for a compound pipe system (series, parallel, or series-parallel combination), carries the **same discharge** under the **same head loss** (or vice-versa), thereby producing identical hydraulic effects between the two upstream and downstream points.

In simpler terms, an equivalent pipe is a "mathematical replacement" for a complex network of pipes. The original system may have multiple pipes of varying diameters and lengths, but the engineer can replace the whole network with one imaginary pipe that behaves *hydraulically identical* to the original system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway vs. City Roads" Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine water flow as cars traveling from City A to City B.
> - In a **series** network, cars must traverse a sequence of narrow and wide roads **one after another** — the *total travel time* is the sum of times on each road.
> - In a **parallel** network, cars can choose between several alternative roads running side-by-side — the *total number of cars reaching* City B is the sum of cars using each route.
> - The **equivalent pipe** is like a single, perfectly designed *highway* that lets the same number of cars (discharge) reach the destination in the same total time (head loss).

## 1.3 Why Engineers Need This Concept

> [!TIP]
> - **Simplification:** A 10-pipe network can be reduced to ONE pipe for design calculations.
> - **Economy:** Helps select optimal pipe diameters for water distribution networks.
> - **Reservoir & Pump Design:** Allows quick estimation of total head loss in municipal systems.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Darcy-Weisbach friction factor: **f** (dimensionless)
- Standard Hazen-Williams roughness values are **not** used here — KTU 2024 uses **Darcy-Weisbach Equation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Configurations

> [!NOTE]
> Compound pipes in Fluid Mechanics exist in **two pure configurations**, and one combined form:
> 1. **Pipes in Series** — flow is *sequential* (one after another).
> 2. **Pipes in Parallel** — flow is *simultaneous* (split across branches).
> 3. **Series-Parallel** — networks that combine both.

## 2.2 Foundation: Darcy-Weisbach Head Loss

The governing equation for frictional head loss in a pipe is the **Darcy-Weisbach Equation**:

$$
h_f \;=\; \frac{4fLV^{2}}{2gD} \;=\; \frac{8fLQ^{2}}{\pi^{2}gD^{5}}
$$

Where:
- $h_f$ = head loss due to friction (m)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless)
- $L$ = length of pipe (m)
- $D$ = diameter of pipe (m)
- $Q$ = volumetric discharge (m³/s)
- $V$ = mean velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

## 2.3 Why These Two Equations Are Critical

| Property | Series Connection | Parallel Connection |
|---|---|---|
| **Discharge** | Same Q through every pipe | Q is **split** among branches |
| **Head Loss** | **Sums up**: $H = \Sigma h_{fi}$ | **Equal** across each branch |
| **Equivalent Pipe Idea** | Find $L_{eq}, D_{eq}$ for same $Q$ and total $H$ | Find $L_{eq}, D_{eq}$ for total $Q$ and same $H$ |
| **Driving Principle** | Conservation of **Mass** + Energy additivity | Conservation of **Mass** (sum of flows) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD EQUATIONS FOR EQUIVALENT PIPE (MEMORIZE THESE)**

| Configuration | Governing Relation | Special Case (Same f, Same D) | Special Case (Same f, Same L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Series** | $\dfrac{L_{eq}}{D_{eq}^{5}} \;=\; \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{L_{i}}{D_{i}^{5}}$ | $L_{eq} \;=\; \displaystyle\sum L_{i}\left(\dfrac{D}{D_{i}}\right)^{5}$ | — |
| **Parallel** | $\dfrac{D_{eq}^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_{eq}}} \;=\; \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{D_{i}^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_{i}}}$ | — | $D_{eq} \;=\; \left(\displaystyle\sum D_{i}^{5/2}\right)^{2/5}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** All lengths in **metres (m)**, diameters in **metres (m)**, $Q$ in **m³/s**, $h_f$ in **metres of fluid**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal Water Supply Networks** — sizing trunk mains and branch lines.
- **Hydraulic Design of Penstocks** — hydroelectric projects.
- **Industrial Piping** — refineries, cooling water systems, fire-fighting networks.
- **Sewer and Storm-water Networks** — flow capacity estimation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Pipe for Pipes in Series

### Physical Setup

> Consider $n$ pipes connected end-to-end in series between two reservoirs A and B. The discharge $Q$ is the **same** through every pipe (continuity), and the **total head loss** equals the algebraic sum of individual head losses.

### Step 1 — Head Loss in Pipe $i$ (Darcy-Weisbach)

$$
h_{fi} \;=\; \frac{8 f_{i}\, L_{i}\, Q^{2}}{\pi^{2}\, g\, D_{i}^{5}}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Head Loss Across the Series System

$$
H_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_{fi} \;=\; \frac{8Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{f_{i}\, L_{i}}{D_{i}^{5}}
$$

### Step 3 — Head Loss in the Equivalent Single Pipe

The equivalent pipe carries the same discharge $Q$ and produces the same total head loss $H_{total}$:

$$
H_{total} \;=\; \frac{8 f_{eq}\, L_{eq}\, Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g\, D_{eq}^{5}}
$$

### Step 4 — Equate Step 2 and Step 3

$$
\frac{8 f_{eq}\, L_{eq}\, Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g\, D_{eq}^{5}} \;=\; \frac{8Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{f_{i}\, L_{i}}{D_{i}^{5}}
$$

### Step 5 — Cancel Common Factors

Cancel $\dfrac{8Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g}$ from both sides:

$$
\frac{f_{eq}\, L_{eq}}{D_{eq}^{5}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{f_{i}\, L_{i}}{D_{i}^{5}}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Equivalent Pipe Relation (Series)

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{L_{eq}}{D_{eq}^{5}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_{i}}{D_{i}^{5}} \quad \text{(assuming same } f \text{ for all pipes)}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case:** If $D_{eq} = D$ (the equivalent diameter is *chosen* equal to a reference diameter $D$), then:
> $$L_{eq} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{i}\left(\frac{D}{D_{i}}\right)^{5}$$
> When all diameters are **equal**, $L_{eq} = \sum L_{i}$ (i.e., lengths simply add up — a powerful quick check).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Pipe for Pipes in Parallel

### Physical Setup

> Consider $n$ pipes connected in parallel between two junctions (or reservoirs). The **head loss** across each branch is the **same** (since they share the same upstream and downstream points), but the **discharge is divided** among them.

### Step 1 — Head Loss in Branch $i$

$$
H \;=\; h_{fi} \;=\; \frac{8 f_{i}\, L_{i}\, Q_{i}^{2}}{\pi^{2} g\, D_{i}^{5}}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $Q_{i}$ in Terms of $H$

$$
Q_{i}^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} g\, H\, D_{i}^{5}}{8 f_{i}\, L_{i}}
$$

$$
Q_{i} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g\, H\, D_{i}^{5}}{8 f_{i}\, L_{i}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g H}{8}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{i}^{5}}{f_{i}\, L_{i}}}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Discharge (Continuity)

$$
Q_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_{i} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g H}{8}} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{\frac{D_{i}^{5}}{f_{i}\, L_{i}}}
$$

### Step 4 — Equivalent Pipe Discharge

For the equivalent single pipe:

$$
Q_{total} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g H\, D_{eq}^{5}}{8 f_{eq}\, L_{eq}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Equate Step 3 and Step 4

$$
\sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g H\, D_{eq}^{5}}{8 f_{eq}\, L_{eq}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2} g H}{8}} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{\frac{D_{i}^{5}}{f_{i}\, L_{i}}}
$$

### Step 6 — Cancel Common Factors and Square Both Sides

Assuming same $f$ for all pipes:

$$
\frac{D_{eq}^{5}}{L_{eq}} \;=\; \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{\frac{D_{i}^{5}}{L_{i}}}\right)^{2}
$$

Equivalently, taking the square root before squaring:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{D_{eq}^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_{eq}}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{D_{i}^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_{i}}} \quad \text{(assuming same } f \text{)}\;}
$$

### Step 7 — Special Case: All Pipes Have Same Length $L$

If $L_{i} = L = L_{eq}$:

$$
D_{eq}^{5/2} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_{i}^{5/2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;D_{eq} \;=\; \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_{i}^{5/2}\right)^{2/5}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Parallel Pipes:**
> - The **5th power of diameter** dominates series networks.
> - The **2.5th power** governs parallel networks.
> - Therefore, **a small change in diameter has a HUGE effect on head loss** — a 10% diameter increase → ~61% reduction in $h_f$ for the same flow!

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Summary Table

| Quantity | Series | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Variable that is **conserved** | Discharge $Q$ | Head $H$ |
| Variable that **adds** | Head loss $H$ | Discharge $Q$ |
| Equivalent diameter exponent | $D^{5}$ | $D^{5/2}$ |
| When diameters equal | $L_{eq} = \Sigma L_i$ | $D_{eq} = n^{2/5} \cdot D$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic — Series Pipe System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reservoir A] --> B[Pipe 1: L1, D1]
    B --> C[Junction Node 1]
    C --> D[Pipe 2: L2, D2]
    D --> E[Junction Node 2]
    E --> F[Pipe n: Ln, Dn]
    F --> G[Reservoir B]
    H[Equivalent Pipe: Leq, Deq] -.->|Replaces entire chain| A
    H --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** Water flows from Reservoir A → Pipe 1 → Junction 1 → Pipe 2 → ... → Pipe $n$ → Reservoir B. The dashed arrow shows that the **equivalent pipe** spans directly from A to B, replacing the entire chain.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Parallel Pipe System

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Reservoir A] --> J1[Junction P]
    J1 --> B1[Pipe 1: L1, D1]
    J1 --> B2[Pipe 2: L2, D2]
    J1 --> B3[Pipe 3: L3, D3]
    B1 --> J2[Junction Q]
    B2 --> J2
    B3 --> J2
    J2 --> C[Reservoir B]
    EQ[Equivalent Pipe: Leq, Deq] -.->|Replaces 3 branches| A
    EQ --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The flow **splits at Junction P** into 3 branches (Pipes 1, 2, 3) running side-by-side, then **recombines at Junction Q** before reaching Reservoir B. The equivalent pipe replaces the three parallel branches as a single path.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solving an Equivalent Pipe Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Given Compound Pipe Network]) --> Step1[Step A: Identify Configuration: Series or Parallel]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step B: Write Continuity and Energy Equations]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step C: Substitute Darcy-Weisbach Formula]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step D: Apply the Correct Equivalent Pipe Relation]
    Step4 --> Series{Series?}
    Series -->|Yes| S1[Use Leq over Deq to the power 5 = Sum Li over Di to the power 5]
    Series -->|No| P1[Use Deq to the power 2.5 over sqrt Leq = Sum Di to the power 2.5 over sqrt Li]
    S1 --> StepE[Step E: Solve for the Unknown Leq or Deq]
    P1 --> StepE
    StepE --> Verify[Step F: Sanity Check Units and Limiting Cases]
    Verify --> Result([Final: Equivalent Pipe Dimensions Obtained])
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this flowchart during exams:** Follow arrows left-to-right, branch at the diamond, and stop at the final result. The "Verify" step is where you cross-check whether your numerical answer makes physical sense (e.g., $D_{eq}$ should lie between the smallest and largest pipe diameters in a series network when $L_{eq}$ is fixed).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

### Q1. Define an equivalent pipe and state the equivalent pipe relation for two pipes connected in **series**. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> An equivalent pipe is a single hypothetical pipe of uniform diameter and length that, when substituted for a compound pipe system, carries the same discharge under the same head loss.
>
> For **two pipes in series** with the same friction factor:
> $$\frac{L_{eq}}{D_{eq}^{5}} \;=\; \frac{L_{1}}{D_{1}^{5}} + \frac{L_{2}}{D_{2}^{5}}$$
> *(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: relation; 1 mark: statement of assumption on $f$)*

---

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

### Q2. State the equivalent pipe relation for **pipes in parallel** and explain why a small change in diameter has a disproportionately large effect on discharge. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> For pipes in parallel (same length and friction factor):
> $$D_{eq} \;=\; \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_{i}^{5/2}\right)^{2/5}$$
>
> Because discharge $Q \propto D^{5/2}$ (from $h_f = 8fLQ^{2}/\pi^{2}gD^{5}$), a small diameter change produces a much larger change in flow capacity.
> *(1 mark: relation; 2 marks: explanation of $D^{5/2}$ dependence)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 5th power on diameter** in series problems → 2–3 mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up series and parallel formulas** in series-parallel networks → major loss.
> 3. **Unit mismatches:** using cm in some places and m in others — KTU strictly expects **SI units** throughout.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of constant friction factor** $f$ → 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Algebraic errors** when taking the 2/5th power of $D_{eq}^{5}$ — show intermediate logs or step-by-step.

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Two pipes are connected in series between two reservoirs 1.2 km apart. Pipe 1 has diameter 30 cm and length 400 m. Pipe 2 has diameter 20 cm and length 600 m. The remaining 200 m is a uniform pipe of 25 cm diameter connecting them to an intermediate junction. Find the equivalent diameter of a single equivalent pipe of length 1200 m connecting the same two reservoirs. Take $f$ = 0.02 for all pipes.**

#### Part (a) — Series Equivalence (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Pipe 1: $D_1 = 0.30$ m, $L_1 = 400$ m
- Pipe 2: $D_2 = 0.20$ m, $L_2 = 600$ m
- Pipe 3: $D_3 = 0.25$ m, $L_3 = 200$ m
- Equivalent: $L_{eq} = 1200$ m, same $f$ throughout.

**Step 1 — Series Equivalent Formula**

$$
\frac{L_{eq}}{D_{eq}^{5}} \;=\; \frac{L_{1}}{D_{1}^{5}} + \frac{L_{2}}{D_{2}^{5}} + \frac{L_{3}}{D_{3}^{5}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Each Term**

$$
D_1^{5} \;=\; (0.30)^{5} \;=\; 0.00243 \;\text{m}^{5}
$$
$$
D_2^{5} \;=\; (0.20)^{5} \;=\; 0.00032 \;\text{m}^{5}
$$
$$
D_3^{5} \;=\; (0.25)^{5} \;=\; 0.0009766 \;\text{m}^{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Each Fraction**

$$
\frac{L_{1}}{D_{1}^{5}} \;=\; \frac{400}{0.00243} \;=\; 164,609.05
$$
$$
\frac{L_{2}}{D_{2}^{5}} \;=\; \frac{600}{0.00032} \;=\; 1,875,000.00
$$
$$
\frac{L_{3}}{D_{3}^{5}} \;=\; \frac{200}{0.0009766} \;=\; 204,800.00
$$

**Step 4 — Sum Them Up**

$$
\sum \frac{L_{i}}{D_{i}^{5}} \;=\; 164,609.05 + 1,875,000.00 + 204,800.00 \;=\; 2,244,409.05
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $D_{eq}^{5}$**

$$
D_{eq}^{5} \;=\; \frac{L_{eq}}{\sum (L_i / D_i^{5})} \;=\; \frac{1200}{2,244,409.05} \;=\; 5.346 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{5}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the 5th Root**

$$
D_{eq} \;=\; (5.346 \times 10^{-4})^{1/5}
$$
$$
\log(5.346 \times 10^{-4}) \;=\; -3.2720 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{-3.2720}{5} \;=\; -0.6544
$$
$$
D_{eq} \;=\; 10^{-0.6544} \;\approx\; 0.2219 \;\text{m} \;\approx\; 22.2 \;\text{cm}
$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** $\boxed{D_{eq} \;\approx\; 0.222\;\text{m} \;\approx\; 22.2\;\text{cm}}$

---

#### Part (b) — Cross-Verification Using Head Loss (7 marks)

**Given:** Discharge $Q = 0.10$ m³/s flows through the system. Verify the head loss is identical for both the original system and the equivalent pipe.

**Step 1 — Head Loss in Each Pipe of the Original System**

$$
h_{fi} \;=\; \frac{8 f L_i Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_{i}^{5}}
$$

$$
\frac{8 f Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} g} \;=\; \frac{8 \times 0.02 \times (0.10)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 9.81} \;=\; \frac{0.0016}{96.79} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Each Head Loss**

$$
h_{f1} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{400}{0.00243} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5} \times 164,609 \;=\; 2.722 \;\text{m}
$$
$$
h_{f2} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{600}{0.00032} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5} \times 1,875,000 \;=\; 30.99 \;\text{m}
$$
$$
h_{f3} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{200}{0.0009766} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5} \times 204,800 \;=\; 3.386 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Head Loss**

$$
H_{total} \;=\; 2.722 + 30.99 + 3.386 \;=\; 37.10 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Head Loss in the Equivalent Pipe**

$$
h_{f,eq} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{1200}{0.0005346}
$$
$$
h_{f,eq} \;=\; 1.653 \times 10^{-5} \times 2,244,409 \;=\; 37.10 \;\text{m} \;\checkmark
$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $h_{f,eq} = 37.10$ m, **matches exactly** the original system's total head loss. The equivalent diameter is verified.

---

### **Question B** *(Alternative to Question A — 14 Marks)*

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Three pipes of diameters 30 cm, 20 cm, and 15 cm with lengths 500 m, 400 m, and 300 m respectively are connected in parallel between two reservoirs.**

#### Part (a) — Derive the Equivalent Pipe Relation for Parallel Pipes (7 marks)

**Derivation Outline (with each step receiving marks):**

1. **Continuity:** $Q = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$ ... *(1 mark)*
2. **Energy Equation:** Head loss across each pipe is the same $= H$ ... *(1 mark)*
3. **Darcy-Weisbach:** $H = \dfrac{8fL_i Q_i^{2}}{\pi^{2} g D_i^{5}}$ ... *(1 mark)*
4. **Solve for $Q_i$:** $Q_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^{2} g H D_i^{5}}{8 f L_i}}$ ... *(1 mark)*
5. **Sum and equate to equivalent pipe:** ... *(2 marks)*
6. **Final Result:**
   $$\frac{D_{eq}^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_{eq}}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{D_{i}^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_{i}}}$$
   *(1 mark)*

---

#### Part (b) — Find the Equivalent Diameter for Length 400 m (7 marks)

**Given:** $D_1 = 0.30$ m, $D_2 = 0.20$ m, $D_3 = 0.15$ m; $L_1 = 500$ m, $L_2 = 400$ m, $L_3 = 300$ m; $L_{eq} = 400$ m.

**Step 1 — Apply the Formula**

$$
\frac{D_{eq}^{5/2}}{\sqrt{400}} \;=\; \frac{(0.30)^{5/2}}{\sqrt{500}} + \frac{(0.20)^{5/2}}{\sqrt{400}} + \frac{(0.15)^{5/2}}{\sqrt{300}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Each $D_i^{5/2}$**

$$
D_1^{5/2} \;=\; (0.30)^{2.5} \;=\; 0.04929 \;\text{m}^{5/2}
$$
$$
D_2^{5/2} \;=\; (0.20)^{2.5} \;=\; 0.01789 \;\text{m}^{5/2}
$$
$$
D_3^{5/2} \;=\; (0.15)^{2.5} \;=\; 0.008715 \;\text{m}^{5/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Each Fraction**

$$
\frac{D_1^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_1}} \;=\; \frac{0.04929}{\sqrt{500}} \;=\; \frac{0.04929}{22.361} \;=\; 0.002204
$$
$$
\frac{D_2^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_2}} \;=\; \frac{0.01789}{\sqrt{400}} \;=\; \frac{0.01789}{20.000} \;=\; 0.000895
$$
$$
\frac{D_3^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_3}} \;=\; \frac{0.008715}{\sqrt{300}} \;=\; \frac{0.008715}{17.321} \;=\; 0.000503
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Right Side**

$$
\sum \frac{D_i^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_i}} \;=\; 0.002204 + 0.000895 + 0.000503 \;=\; 0.003602
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $D_{eq}^{5/2}$**

$$
D_{eq}^{5/2} \;=\; 0.003602 \times \sqrt{400} \;=\; 0.003602 \times 20 \;=\; 0.07204
$$

**Step 6 — Take the 2/5th Power**

$$
D_{eq} \;=\; (0.07204)^{2/5}
$$
$$
\log(0.07204) \;=\; -1.1425 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{2}{5} \times (-1.1425) \;=\; -0.4570
$$
$$
D_{eq} \;=\; 10^{-0.4570} \;\approx\; 0.3493 \;\text{m} \;\approx\; 34.9 \;\text{cm}
$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $\boxed{D_{eq} \;\approx\; 0.349\;\text{m} \;\approx\; 34.9\;\text{cm}}$

**Sanity Check:** The equivalent diameter (34.9 cm) is **larger** than the largest branch diameter (30 cm) — this is correct for parallel pipes, because parallel branches offer *more flow capacity* than any single branch, so a *larger* equivalent pipe is needed to carry the combined discharge at the same head.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — EQUIVALENT PIPE**

- **Definition (verbatim):** *A single hypothetical pipe that gives the same discharge under the same head loss as a compound pipe system.*
- **Series key equation:** $\dfrac{L_{eq}}{D_{eq}^{5}} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{D_i^{5}}$
- **Parallel key equation:** $\dfrac{D_{eq}^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_{eq}}} = \sum \dfrac{D_i^{5/2}}{\sqrt{L_i}}$
- **Series special case (equal D):** $L_{eq} = \sum L_i$
- **Parallel special case (equal L):** $D_{eq} = \left(\sum D_i^{5/2}\right)^{2/5}$
- **Series → Lengths add when diameters are equal; diameters cannot be simply added.**
- **Parallel → Equivalent diameter is always ≥ the largest branch diameter.**
- **Discharge conservation** is the master principle: $Q$ constant in series; $Q$ splits in parallel.
- **Head loss conservation:** $H$ adds in series; $H$ equal in parallel.
- **Darcy-Weisbach Equation** is the foundation: $h_f = 8fLQ^{2}/(\pi^{2}gD^{5})$.
- **Always state assumption of constant $f$** in exam answers (KTU valuation mark).
- **Units check:** Use **SI units throughout** — convert cm to m before any computation.
- **Sanity check answer:** Series $D_{eq}$ should be between smallest and largest $D_i$ when $L_{eq}$ is fixed; parallel $D_{eq}$ should be ≥ largest $D_i$.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Mixing $D^{5}$ (series) with $D^{5/2}$ (parallel) formulas.
- **Real-world application:** Water distribution networks, penstocks, industrial pipelines.
- **Exam tip:** In Part B questions, always draw a clear schematic of the system before solving — KTU examiners award "method" marks generously for well-labelled diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission of Power Through Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission of Power Through Pipes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission of Power Through Pipes (Hydraulic Power Transmission):**
> The process of conveying mechanical energy from one location to another through a closed conduit carrying a fluid (usually water or oil) under pressure. The power is generated by a pump (or turbine) at the source, transmitted as pressure energy + kinetic energy of the flowing fluid, and extracted at the delivery end by a hydraulic motor or turbine. The power available at the delivery end equals the gross power supplied minus the power lost due to friction in the pipe.

Mathematically, the **Power Transmitted (P)** at the outlet of a pipe is given by:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{net}$$

where $H_{net} = H - h_f$ (Gross head supplied minus the head lost due to friction).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose with a Generator" Intuition

Imagine connecting a small hydroelectric generator to a long garden hose fed by an overhead tank. The water flowing in the hose carries energy. The **higher the tank**, the more energy each kilogram of water carries. The **faster the flow**, the more kilograms per second pass the generator. However, if you increase the flow too much by opening the tap wider, a large fraction of the tank's head is lost to friction along the hose walls — so less net energy reaches the generator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** There is a *sweet spot* of flow velocity at which the power reaching the end is **maximum**. Push the flow too slowly → little power (not enough mass). Push it too fast → huge friction losses eat up the head. The optimum lies in between.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Value (Water) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | **1000** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **m/s²** | **9.81** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water | $\mu$ | **N·s/m²** | **0.001** (at 20 °C) |
| Kinematic viscosity of water | $\nu$ | **m²/s** | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶** (at 20 °C) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The two classical results every KTU examiner expects are:
> 1. For **laminar flow**, maximum power is transmitted when $h_f = \dfrac{H}{2}$ (Efficiency $\eta = 50\%$).
> 2. For **turbulent flow**, maximum power is transmitted when $h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}$ (Efficiency $\eta = 66.67\%$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Setup

A pump (or an elevated reservoir) supplies fluid with a **gross head $H$** at the inlet of a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$. As the fluid flows, friction at the walls consumes some head $h_f$, which is given by the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**:

$$h_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot V^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot D}$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor. (Some textbooks use the Fanning factor $f'$ where $f = 4f'$, giving $h_f = \frac{4f' L V^2}{2gD}$.)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **The Gross Power Supplied** by the pump to the fluid at the inlet: $P_{gross} = \rho g Q H$.
2. **The Power Lost** to friction inside the pipe: $P_{loss} = \rho g Q h_f$.
3. **The Net Power Delivered** at the outlet (the useful transmitted power): $P = P_{gross} - P_{loss} = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$.
4. **The discharge** through a circular pipe of diameter $D$: $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 V$.
5. **The efficiency** of transmission (ratio of delivered power to gross power): $\eta = \dfrac{H - h_f}{H} = 1 - \dfrac{h_f}{H}$.

### 2.3 Expressing Friction Loss in Terms of Velocity

| Flow Regime | Friction Head Loss $h_f$ | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** ($Re < 2000$) | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} = \dfrac{32 \nu L V}{g D^2}$ | Derived from Hagen–Poiseuille flow profile |
| **Turbulent** ($Re > 4000$) | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \dfrac{2 f L V^2}{g D}$ | Darcy–Weisbach with empirical friction factor $f$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial observation:** In **laminar** flow, $h_f \propto V$, whereas in **turbulent** flow, $h_f \propto V^2$. This single fact changes the entire optimisation result — and is the most-tested KTU concept on this topic.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$ | Net power delivered | **W (watts)** |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 V$ | Continuity equation | **m³/s** |
| 3 | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$ | Laminar head loss (Hagen–Poiseuille) | **m** |
| 4 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Turbulent head loss (Darcy–Weisbach) | **m** |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{H - h_f}{H}$ | Transmission efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $h_f = \dfrac{H}{2}$ | Optimum condition for laminar flow | **m** |
| 7 | $h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}$ | Optimum condition for turbulent flow | **m** |
| 8 | $D_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{32 \mu L V_{opt}}{\rho g H}\right)^{1/2}$ | Optimum diameter (laminar) | **m** |
| 9 | $\eta_{max,lam} = 50\%$ | Max efficiency, laminar | **%** |
| 10 | $\eta_{max,turb} = 66.67\%$ | Max efficiency, turbulent | **%** |

> **Note on real-world engineering:** Hydraulic power transmission through long pipelines is used in **hydroelectric penstocks**, **district cooling water networks**, **oil pipelines**, and **ship hydraulic systems**. The efficiency numbers (50 %–66.67 %) are *upper bounds for ideal friction-only losses*; real systems add minor losses (bends, valves, entry/exit) which lower efficiency further.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Condition for Maximum Power Transmission in LAMINAR Flow

**Given:**
- Pipe length $L$, diameter $D$, gross head $H$.
- Laminar flow: $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$.

**Step 1 — Write Power in terms of $V$ (with $D$ and $L$ fixed).**

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 V \cdot \left(H - h_f\right)$$

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 V \cdot \left(H - \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the bracket.**

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H V - \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 V \cdot \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the second term.** The $\rho g$ in numerator and denominator cancel, and one $D^2$ cancels:

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H V - 8 \pi \mu L V^2$$

**Step 4 — Maximize with respect to velocity $V$.** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H - 16 \pi \mu L V = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the optimal velocity $V_{opt}$.**

$$V_{opt} = \frac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into $h_f$ to find the head-loss condition.**

$$h_{f,opt} = \frac{32 \mu L}{\rho g D^2} \cdot \frac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L} = \frac{H}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (Laminar):** Maximum power is transmitted when **head loss = half the gross head**, i.e. $h_f = H/2$.

**Step 7 — Efficiency at maximum power transmission.**

$$\eta_{max,lam} = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{H - H/2}{H} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Condition for Maximum Power Transmission in TURBULENT Flow

**Given:** Turbulent head loss $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \dfrac{2 f L V^2}{g D}$.

**Step 1 — Power expression.**

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 V \cdot \left(H - \frac{2 f L V^2}{g D}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Expand.**

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H V - \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 V \cdot \frac{2 f L V^2}{g D}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H V - \frac{\pi}{2} \rho f L V^3$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $V$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H - \frac{3\pi}{2} \rho f L V^2 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{opt}^2$.**

$$V_{opt}^2 = \frac{g D H}{6 f L}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into $h_f$.**

$$h_{f,opt} = \frac{2 f L}{g D} \cdot V_{opt}^2 = \frac{2 f L}{g D} \cdot \frac{g D H}{6 f L} = \frac{H}{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (Turbulent):** Maximum power is transmitted when **head loss = one-third of the gross head**, i.e. $h_f = H/3$.

**Step 7 — Efficiency at maximum power transmission.**

$$\eta_{max,turb} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Optimum Diameter (for a fixed discharge $Q$)

Sometimes the problem states the discharge $Q$ is fixed, not the velocity. In that case we treat $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ as a function of $D$, then maximize $P$.

**For Laminar Flow:**

$$P = \rho g Q H - \rho g Q \cdot \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} = \rho g Q H - \frac{32 \mu L Q^2}{\pi D^4} \cdot 4 = \rho g Q H - \frac{128 \mu L Q^2}{\pi D^4}$$

Setting $\dfrac{dP}{dD} = 0$:

$$\frac{dP}{dD} = 0 - \frac{128 \mu L Q^2}{\pi} \cdot \left(-\frac{4}{D^5}\right) = \frac{512 \mu L Q^2}{\pi D^5} = 0$$

This gives no finite optimum because $P \to \infty$ as $D \to \infty$. So we **maximize efficiency instead**:

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = 1 - \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi \rho g H D^4}$$

The efficiency **increases monotonically with $D$** — bigger pipe, less friction loss, higher efficiency. So for **fixed discharge**, the diameter is chosen by economic trade-off (capital cost vs pumping cost), giving an *economic optimum*, not a power-transmission optimum.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Friction head $h_f \propto$ | $V^1$ | $V^2$ |
| Condition for max power | $h_f = H/2$ | $h_f = H/3$ |
| Max efficiency $\eta_{max}$ | **50 %** | **66.67 %** |
| Velocity exponent in $P(V)$ | $V^2$ (parabolic) | $V^3$ (cubic) |
| Nature of curve | Inverted parabola, smooth | Inverted cubic, steeper falloff |
| Optimal velocity (laminar) | $V_{opt} = \dfrac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L}$ | $V_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{6 f L}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Hydraulic Power Transmission

The following block diagram maps the *functional flow* of energy from the source (reservoir/pump) through the pipe to the delivery point, identifying where losses occur. This serves as a substitute for a physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Reservoir Pump Station] -->|Gross Head H metres| B[Pipe Inlet Section]
    B --> C{Pipe Length L Diameter D Wall Roughness epsilon}
    C -->|Friction along wall| D[Head Loss hf per Darcy Weisbach]
    D -->|Remaining Net Head H minus hf| E[Pipe Outlet Section]
    E -->|Discharge Q cubic m per s at Velocity V| F[Hydraulic Motor Turbine Receiver]
    F -->|Mechanical Power Out| G[Load Generator Compressor Machine]
    C -->|Minor Losses| H[Entry Bends Valves Fittings]
    H --> D
    A -->|Efficiency eta equals H minus hf over H| I[Transmission Efficiency Indicator]
    D --> I
    G --> J[Output Shaft Power Watts minus Mechanical Losses]
    J --> K[Final Useful Work Output]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Source Reservoir** supplies a fixed gross head $H$.
- Along the pipe length, both **major (Darcy) losses** and **minor (fitting) losses** consume head.
- The remaining net head $(H - h_f)$ acts on the discharge $Q$ to deliver hydraulic power at the outlet.
- The hydraulic motor converts this to shaft power; further mechanical losses reduce the final output.

### 4.2 Optimisation Logic Flowchart (Max Power vs Max Efficiency)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Given Gross Head H Pipe Length L] --> B{Flow Regime Known?}
    B -->|Laminar Re below 2000| C[hf proportional to V]
    B -->|Turbulent Re above 4000| D[hf proportional to V squared]
    C --> E[dP by dV equals zero]
    D --> F[dP by dV equals zero]
    E --> G[Condition hf equals H by 2]
    F --> H[Condition hf equals H by 3]
    G --> I[Eta max equals 50 percent]
    H --> J[Eta max equals 66.67 percent]
    I --> K[Compute Vopt and Dopt]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output Maximum Transmitted Power]
```

### 4.3 Power-vs-Velocity Characteristic Curve (Conceptual)

The shape of $P$ vs $V$ differs between the two regimes — this is **the single most important visual** for KTU viva/practical exams:

| Flow Regime | Curve Shape | Peak Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | Symmetric inverted parabola, $P \propto V - aV^2$ | Peak at $V_{opt}$ where $h_f = H/2$ |
| **Turbulent** | Skewed inverted cubic, $P \propto V - bV^3$ | Peak at $V_{opt}$ where $h_f = H/3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Viva tip:** If an examiner asks "why is turbulent-flow efficiency higher than laminar-flow efficiency?", the answer is: *because in turbulent flow $h_f$ grows faster (as $V^2$ instead of $V$), so the optimal velocity point shifts to a regime where friction loss is a smaller fraction of the gross head (1/3 instead of 1/2), leaving more head available for useful work.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**State and explain the condition for maximum transmission of power through a pipe for laminar flow.**

**Model Answer:**
For laminar flow, the head loss is $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$, so the transmitted power is

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f) = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H V - 8 \pi \mu L V^2$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $V$ and setting $\dfrac{dP}{dV}=0$ gives $V_{opt} = \dfrac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L}$. Substituting back yields $h_f = \dfrac{H}{2}$. The transmission efficiency at this optimum is $\eta_{max} = 50\%$.

*(3 marks: 1 for expression of $P$, 1 for differentiation step, 1 for final condition + efficiency.)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between the conditions for maximum power transmission in laminar and turbulent flow regimes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Head loss proportionality | $h_f \propto V$ | $h_f \propto V^2$ |
| Optimum condition | $h_f = H/2$ | $h_f = H/3$ |
| Max efficiency | $50\%$ | $66.67\%$ |
| Reason | Friction grows slowly with $V$ | Friction grows faster, so less of $H$ is wasted |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each row of comparison + 1 for concluding remark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each (typically part (a) and part (b)). One sub-part is usually a derivation/proof and the other a numerical.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Derive the condition for maximum transmission of power through a pipe for **turbulent flow**. Also obtain the corresponding maximum transmission efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** For turbulent flow, Darcy–Weisbach equation gives:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{2 f L V^2}{g D}$$

**Step 2:** Power transmitted:

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f) = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 V \left(H - \frac{2 f L V^2}{g D}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Expanding:

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H V - \frac{\pi}{2} \rho f L V^3$$

**Step 4:** Differentiating with respect to $V$:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H - \frac{3 \pi}{2} \rho f L V^2$$

**Step 5:** Setting $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$:

$$V_{opt}^2 = \frac{g D H}{6 f L}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting back:

$$h_{f,opt} = \frac{2 f L}{g D} \cdot \frac{g D H}{6 f L} = \frac{H}{3}$$

**Step 7:** Maximum efficiency:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

**Result:** For maximum power transmission in turbulent flow, **head loss equals one-third of the gross head**, and the maximum efficiency is **66.67 %**.

---

**(b)** An oil of specific gravity $0.86$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.025\,\text{N·s/m}^2$ is pumped through a pipe of diameter $20\,\text{cm}$ and length $500\,\text{m}$. The gross head available is $30\,\text{m}$. If the flow is laminar, determine: **(i)** the velocity at which maximum power is transmitted, and **(ii)** the maximum power transmitted. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $\rho = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.025\,\text{N·s/m}^2$, $D = 0.20\,\text{m}$, $L = 500\,\text{m}$, $H = 30\,\text{m}$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

**(i) Optimal velocity:**

$$V_{opt} = \frac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L} = \frac{860 \times 9.81 \times (0.20)^2 \times 30}{64 \times 0.025 \times 500}$$

$$V_{opt} = \frac{860 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 30}{800} = \frac{10121.52}{800} \approx 12.65\,\text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Maximum power:**

At optimum, $h_f = H/2 = 15\,\text{m}$, so net head $= 15\,\text{m}$.

Discharge $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 (12.65) = 0.3974\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$P_{max} = \rho g Q (H - h_f) = 860 \times 9.81 \times 0.3974 \times 15$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 50{,}300\,\text{W} = 50.3\,\text{kW}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** Derive the condition for maximum transmission of power through a pipe for **laminar flow**. Also obtain the corresponding maximum transmission efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** For laminar flow:

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$$

**Step 2:** Power transmitted:

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 V \left(H - \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Expanding:

$$P = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H V - 8 \pi \mu L V^2$$

**Step 4:** Differentiating:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H - 16 \pi \mu L V$$

**Step 5:** Setting $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$:

$$V_{opt} = \frac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting back:

$$h_{f,opt} = \frac{32 \mu L}{\rho g D^2} \cdot \frac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L} = \frac{H}{2}$$

**Step 7:** Maximum efficiency:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{H - H/2}{H} = 50\%$$

**Result:** For maximum power transmission in laminar flow, **head loss equals half the gross head**, and the maximum efficiency is **50 %**.

---

**(b)** Water at $20\,\text{°C}$ ($\mu = 0.001\,\text{N·s/m}^2$) flows through a horizontal pipe of length $1000\,\text{m}$ and diameter $10\,\text{cm}$ under a gross head of $20\,\text{m}$. Determine the **maximum power** that can be transmitted assuming laminar flow, and the **corresponding efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.001\,\text{N·s/m}^2$, $D = 0.10\,\text{m}$, $L = 1000\,\text{m}$, $H = 20\,\text{m}$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Optimal velocity:

$$V_{opt} = \frac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.10)^2 \times 20}{64 \times 0.001 \times 1000}$$

$$V_{opt} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01 \times 20}{64} = \frac{1962}{64} \approx 30.66\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Check Reynolds number to confirm laminar regime:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 30.66 \times 0.10}{0.001} = 3{,}066{,}000$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** $Re = 3.06 \times 10^6 \gg 2000$ — the assumption of laminar flow is **physically invalid** at this velocity. The problem is mathematically solvable but practically unrealistic. In a real KTU answer, you must **flag this inconsistency** to the examiner to earn full marks.

**Step 3:** Mathematically (ignoring the regime mismatch):

Discharge $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 (30.66) = 0.2408\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

Net head $= H - h_f = 20 - 10 = 10\,\text{m}$.

$$P_{max} = \rho g Q (H - h_f) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.2408 \times 10 \approx 23{,}624\,\text{W} \approx 23.62\,\text{kW}$$

**Efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = 50\%$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Missing the condition $dP/dV = 0$** — students often write $P = \rho g Q H$ and forget to subtract $h_f$. Always show $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$.
> 2. **Wrong friction factor convention** — KTU textbooks (Modi & Seth, Rajput) use $f = 4f'$ (Fanning). Mixing the two conventions gives wrong $h_f$ and wrong optimum. **State which convention you are using.**
> 3. **Forgetting to express $Q$ in terms of $V$ and $D$** — examiners deduct 2 marks if you keep $Q$ and $h_f$ in mixed forms.
> 4. **Not converting units** — $\mu$ in poise = 0.1 N·s/m². Lengths in cm must be converted to m. Pressure heads in different units cause cascading errors.
> 5. **No final boxed answer** — KTU scripts without a clearly boxed final numerical answer lose 1–2 marks even if the derivation is correct.
> 6. **Ignoring physical validity** — if the optimal velocity produces $Re \gg 2000$ while assuming laminar flow, the answer is mathematically correct but practically meaningless. **Always check Re.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Power transmitted: $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$.
- ✅ Discharge: $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 V$.
- ✅ Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{H - h_f}{H}$.
- ✅ **Laminar** friction: $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$.
- ✅ **Turbulent** friction: $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ (Darcy form).
- ✅ **Max power (laminar):** $h_f = H/2 \Rightarrow \eta = 50\%$.
- ✅ **Max power (turbulent):** $h_f = H/3 \Rightarrow \eta = 66.67\%$.
- ✅ Optimal velocity (laminar): $V_{opt} = \dfrac{\rho g D^2 H}{64 \mu L}$.
- ✅ Optimal velocity (turbulent): $V_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{6 f L}}$.
- ✅ **Always verify flow regime** by computing $Re$ at $V_{opt}$.
- ✅ **Always state the friction-factor convention** used (Darcy vs Fanning).
