- ✅ **Box the final answer** — KTU examiners reward clarity.
- ✅ **Real-world application:** hydroelectric penstocks, oil pipelines, hydraulic presses, ship hydraulics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-mile tip:** If a KTU numerical asks for *maximum power*, optimise $P$ w.r.t. $V$ (treat $D$ and $H$ fixed). If it asks for *optimum diameter*, treat $Q$ fixed and either maximise $P$ (for turbulent: finite optimum exists) or maximise $\eta$ (for laminar: efficiency increases with $D$, leading to an economic optimum). Read the question wording carefully.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency of Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Efficiency of Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a hydroelectric or hydraulic power transmission system, water from a **reservoir** (or **forebay**) is conveyed to the **powerhouse** through a long **penstock** or **pressure pipe**. Along the length of this conduit, a portion of the available hydraulic energy is inevitably dissipated as heat due to **frictional resistance**, **turbulence**, and **minor losses** (bends, valves, fittings).

**Efficiency of Transmission** ($\eta_{transmission}$) is formally defined as the ratio of the **power (or energy per unit weight of water)** actually delivered at the **receiving end** (i.e., at the base of the penstock or the entrance to the turbine nozzle) to the **power supplied at the sending end** (i.e., the gross head available at the forebay/reservoir). It is universally expressed as a percentage or a dimensionless decimal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Efficiency of Transmission is treated as the *preliminary stage* before applying the hydraulic, mechanical, and volumetric efficiencies of the turbine/pump. It quantifies how much of the *gross available head* survives the journey through the conduit.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Leaky Hose"

Imagine a long garden hose connected to an overhead water tank. The water at the tank has a certain **pressure head** (energy per unit weight). As water flows through the hose, you can physically feel the hose vibrate and warm up slightly at the outlet side — that warmth is **frictional energy lost**. By the time the water reaches the nozzle, its pressure has dropped noticeably.

**Efficiency of Transmission** is exactly the same idea — but applied to massive penstocks feeding hydro-turbines. The longer and rougher the pipe, the more head (pressure) is lost to friction, and the less hydraulic energy reaches the turbine runner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A perfect, lossless penstock would have $\eta_{transmission} = 1.0$ (or **100%**). Real systems typically operate between **85% and 98%** depending on pipe length, material, diameter, and flow velocity.

## 1.3 Why Transmission Efficiency Matters

The **overall plant efficiency** of a hydroelectric scheme is the product of several individual efficiencies:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{transmission} \times \eta_{hydraulic} \times \eta_{mechanical} \times \eta_{generator}$$

If $\eta_{transmission}$ drops by even a few percent over a long penstock, the *annual revenue lost* can be enormous — making this a critical design parameter.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Penstock diameter is chosen precisely to balance **capital cost** (larger pipe = more steel, more excavation) against **transmission losses** (smaller pipe = higher velocity, higher friction loss). This trade-off defines the *economic diameter* of the penstock.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — Step by Step

The hydraulic power at any cross-section of a flowing fluid is given by the classical expression:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

where:
- $\rho$ = mass density of the fluid (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $Q$ = volumetric discharge (**m³/s**)
- $H$ = total head (energy per unit weight) at that section (**m**)

For the **transmission problem**, we identify two critical cross-sections:

1. **Section 1 — Sending End (Forebay / Reservoir):**
   Head available = $H_g$ (gross head)
   Power supplied = $\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_g$

2. **Section 2 — Receiving End (Powerhouse / Turbine entry):**
   Head available = $H_g - h_f - h_m$ (net head after losses)
   Power received = $\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot (H_g - h_f - h_m)$

Here, $h_f$ represents **major (frictional) head loss** and $h_m$ represents **minor head losses** (entry, exit, bends, valves).

---

## 2.2 The Master Equation of Transmission Efficiency

Applying the formal definition:

$$\eta_{transmission} = \frac{\text{Power at receiving end}}{\text{Power at sending end}}$$

$$\eta_{transmission} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot (H_g - h_f - h_m)}{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_g}$$

Since $\rho$, $g$, and $Q$ are common to both numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{\eta_{transmission} = \frac{H_g - h_f - h_m}{H_g}}$$

This elegant, dimensionless form is the **single most important equation** of this topic. For most KTU problems, minor losses are either **negligible** or **combined with major losses** into a single head-loss term $h_L$.

---

## 2.3 Role of Friction Loss — Darcy-Weisbach Framework

The head loss $h_f$ for a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ carrying flow at velocity $V$ is given by the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D / 4}$$

Or equivalently, using the **Fanning friction factor** ($f'$):

$$h_f = \frac{4 f' L V^2}{2 g D}$$

where:
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless)
- $f'$ = Fanning friction factor ($f' = f/4$)
- $L$ = length of pipe (**m**)
- $V$ = mean flow velocity (**m/s**)
- $D$ = pipe diameter (**m**)
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**

> [!NOTE]
> The friction factor $f$ is itself a function of the **Reynolds Number** $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ and the **relative roughness** $\varepsilon / D$. It is obtained from the **Moody Chart** or the **Colebrook-White equation**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{trans} = \dfrac{H_g - h_L}{H_g}$ | Master transmission efficiency | $H_g$ = gross head (**m**), $h_L$ = total head loss (**m**) |
| 2 | $P = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power at any section | $\rho$ (**kg/m³**), $g$ (**m/s²**), $Q$ (**m³/s**), $H$ (**m**) |
| 3 | $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Darcy-Weisbach major head loss | $f$ (-), $L$ (**m**), $V$ (**m/s**), $D$ (**m**) |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Continuity-based mean velocity | $A$ (**m²**) |
| 5 | $h_L = h_f + \sum h_m$ | Total head loss (major + minor) | $h_m$ = entry, exit, bend, valve (**m**) |
| 6 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{hyd} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Combined plant efficiency | All $\eta$ dimensionless |
| 7 | $H_{net} = H_g - h_L$ | Net head reaching turbine | (**m**) |
| 8 | $\text{Power Loss} = \rho g Q h_L$ | Energy dissipated in pipe | **Watts (W)** |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number for flow regime | $\mu$ (**Pa·s**), $\nu$ (**m²/s**) |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\varepsilon}{D}$ | Relative roughness of pipe wall | $\varepsilon$ (**m**), $D$ (**m**) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The efficiency of transmission is **not merely an academic concept** — it governs critical engineering decisions in:

- **Hydroelectric Power Plants (HEPs):** Determines penstock diameter and lining material.
- **Long-Distance Water Supply Mains:** Pumping stations lose enormous energy to friction; transmission efficiency dictates pump selection.
- **Industrial Cooling Circuits:** Long recirculating lines suffer cumulative losses.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** Analogous concept governs pumping-station spacing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board examinations, problems typically assume **major loss only** (Darcy-Weisbach) unless minor losses are explicitly listed in the problem statement. Always read carefully before assuming $\sum h_m = 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Transmission Efficiency Equation

We begin with the **energy equation** applied between the reservoir surface (point 1) and the turbine entry point (point 2), assuming both points are exposed to atmospheric pressure and have negligible velocity heads (large reservoir):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

Since $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ (gauge pressures both zero) and $V_1 \approx 0$:

$$z_1 - z_2 = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

Define:
- $z_1 - z_2 = H_g$ (gross static head difference)
- $\dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ = kinetic head at turbine entry (usually retained in the net head, not part of friction loss)
- $h_L$ = total head loss in transmission

The **net head reaching the turbine runner** is:

$$H_{net} = H_g - h_L$$

**Power available at the sending end** (forebay):

$$P_{sent} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_g$$

**Power available at the receiving end** (turbine entry):

$$P_{recv} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot (H_g - h_L)$$

Applying the definition of efficiency:

$$\eta_{transmission} = \frac{P_{recv}}{P_{sent}} = \frac{\rho g Q (H_g - h_L)}{\rho g Q H_g}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{transmission} = \frac{H_g - h_L}{H_g} = 1 - \frac{h_L}{H_g}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **head-ratio form** of transmission efficiency. It tells us that $\eta_{transmission}$ depends only on the *relative magnitude* of head loss to gross head — not on the absolute values.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Expressing $\eta_{transmission}$ in Terms of Velocity and Pipe Geometry

Starting from the master equation:

$$\eta_{transmission} = 1 - \frac{h_f}{H_g}$$

Substituting the Darcy-Weisbach head loss:

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

And continuity:

$$V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

Substituting $V$ into $h_f$:

$$h_f = \frac{f L}{2 g D} \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right)^2 = \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\eta_{transmission} = 1 - \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5 H_g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This form reveals that $\eta_{transmission}$ is **inversely proportional to $D^5$** — doubling the pipe diameter reduces head loss by a factor of **32**! This is why even small diameter increases yield massive transmission efficiency gains.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Transmission Efficiency with Pump / Turbine Combination

| System Type | Sending Head | Receiving Head | $\eta_{transmission}$ Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Pipe Flow (no pump/turbine) | $H_g$ | $H_g - h_f$ | $\dfrac{H_g - h_f}{H_g}$ |
| Pump-driven delivery | $H_p - h_f$ | $H_p - h_f + H_{pump}$ | $\dfrac{H_p - h_f + H_{pump}}{H_p + H_{pump}}$ |
| Turbine-driven extraction | $H_g$ | $H_g - h_f - H_{turbine}$ | $\dfrac{H_g - h_f - H_{turbine}}{H_g}$ |
| Pumped-storage cycle | Combines both | Combined | See detailed analysis below |

### 3.3.1 Pumped-Storage Special Case — Combined Efficiency

In a **pumped-storage** scheme, water is pumped *uphill* during off-peak hours and released *downhill* through turbines during peak hours. The **combined efficiency** of the round-trip is:

$$\eta_{combined} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{transmission,pump} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{transmission,turbine}$$

Each transmission segment is computed using the same master formula but applied to the respective direction of flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to compute the **annual energy lost** due to transmission inefficiency. The formula is:
> $$E_{lost} = P_{input} \times (1 - \eta_{transmission}) \times T_{operating}$$
> where $T_{operating}$ is the total hours of operation per year.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Insight — Why Penstock Diameter is an Economic Decision

Consider two penstocks of diameter $D_1$ and $D_2 = 1.2 D_1$ carrying the same discharge $Q$:

- **Head loss ratio:** $\dfrac{h_{f,1}}{h_{f,2}} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^5 = (1.2)^5 \approx 2.488$
- **Transmission efficiency gain:** $\Delta\eta_{trans} = \dfrac{h_{f,1} - h_{f,2}}{H_g}$

This illustrates why engineers always seek the *largest economically viable* penstock diameter.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Hydroelectric Transmission System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional flow* of energy from the reservoir to the turbine, identifying exactly **where** transmission losses occur.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RESERVOIR FOREBAY - Gross Head Hg] --> B[INTAKE STRUCTURE - Trash Rack]
    B --> C[HEAD RACE TUNNEL - Minor Loss h_in]
    C --> D[SURGE SHANK / PENSTOCK TOP]
    D --> E[LONG PENSTOCK PIPE - Major Friction Loss hf]
    E --> F[BEND AND VALVE JUNCTIONS - Minor Loss h_b]
    F --> G[POWERHOUSE ENTRY - Net Head Hnet]
    G --> H[TURBINE NOZZLE / RUNNER]
    H --> I[TAILRACE - Discharge Outflow]
    J[BLOCK 1 - SUPPLY SIDE]:::supply --> A
    K[BLOCK 2 - CONVEYANCE NETWORK]:::convey --> B
    K --> C
    K --> D
    K --> E
    K --> F
    L[BLOCK 3 - DELIVERY SIDE]:::deliver --> G
    L --> H
    L --> I
    A -. Energy In: rho g Q Hg .-> J
    I -. Energy Out: rho g Q Hnet .-> L
    E -. LOSS hf .-> M[Frictional Dissipation as Heat]
    F -. LOSS hb .-> M

    classDef supply fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef convey fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef deliver fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Interpretation of the Architecture

| Block | Function | Contribution to Transmission Loss |
|---|---|---|
| **Block 1 — Supply Side** | Stores water and defines gross head | **None** (potential energy reference) |
| **Block 2 — Conveyance Network** | Carries water from forebay to powerhouse | **Major friction loss ($h_f$)** + Minor losses ($h_m$) |
| **Block 3 — Delivery Side** | Delivers net head to turbine runner | **None** (energy extraction point) |

> [!NOTE]
> The transmission efficiency depends **entirely on Block 2**. The diagram visually isolates the loss-generating zone, which is a hallmark of well-designed KTU numerical problems.

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Velocity Profile and Friction Gradient Along Penstock

For penstock design problems, students must visualize the **piezometric head line** dropping linearly (approximately) along the pipe length due to friction.

```
Pipe Length (m) -------------------------------------------------------->
0m            L/4            L/2            3L/4              L
|
|  H_g (Static Head)
|  |
|  |        *
|  |              *                  *
|  |                    *                    *
|  |                          *                    *  (H_net at turbine)
|  |
|  +-----------------------------------------------------> Reference Datum
|
Piezometric headline slopes downward due to hf loss
```

The *slope* of this line is precisely $h_f / L$ — the **hydraulic gradient** or **friction slope** ($S_f$).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Efficiency of Transmission" as applied to a hydraulic power conveyance system. State its governing mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer:**
Efficiency of transmission is defined as the ratio of the power (or head per unit weight of water) available at the receiving end of the conveyance system to the power (or head) available at the sending end.

$$\eta_{transmission} = \frac{\text{Power at receiving end}}{\text{Power at sending end}} = \frac{H_g - h_L}{H_g}$$

where $H_g$ = gross head available at the forebay, $h_L$ = total head loss (major + minor) in the conduit. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between major head loss and minor head loss in a penstock. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Major head loss ($h_f$):** The head loss due to friction along the straight length of the pipe; computed by the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** $h_f = f L V^2 / (2 g D)$. *Example:* Friction loss along a 2 km penstock.
- **Minor head loss ($h_m$):** Localized losses due to fittings, bends, valves, sudden contractions/expansions; computed as $h_m = K V^2 / (2g)$. *Example:* Loss at a 90° bend or at the entrance of the pipe.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A hydroelectric power station receives water from a reservoir through a penstock of length **1500 m** and diameter **1.5 m**. The gross head available at the forebay is **120 m**. The Darcy friction factor for the penstock is **0.025**. Calculate the transmission efficiency when the discharge is **3.5 m³/s**. Neglect minor losses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the transmission efficiency obtained in part (a) is considered inadequate and the engineer decides to **increase the penstock diameter to 2.0 m** while maintaining the same discharge and friction factor, compute the new transmission efficiency and the **percentage improvement** in efficiency.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Given Data:**
- $L = 1500$ m, $D = 1.5$ m, $H_g = 120$ m, $f = 0.025$, $Q = 3.5$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Calculate mean velocity using continuity:**

$$V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 3.5}{\pi \times (1.5)^2} = \frac{14.0}{\pi \times 2.25}$$

$$V = \frac{14.0}{7.0686} = 1.9805 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate major head loss using Darcy-Weisbach:**

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.025 \times 1500 \times (1.9805)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.5}$$

Numerator: $0.025 \times 1500 \times 3.9224 = 147.09$
Denominator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 = 29.43$

$$h_f = \frac{147.09}{29.43} = 4.998 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate transmission efficiency:**

$$\eta_{transmission} = \frac{H_g - h_f}{H_g} = \frac{120 - 4.998}{120} = \frac{115.002}{120}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{transmission} = 0.9584 = 95.84\%}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for velocity, 3 marks for $h_f$, 2 marks for final $\eta$)*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**New Diameter:** $D_{new} = 2.0$ m, same $L$, $f$, $Q$

**Step 1 — New velocity:**

$$V_{new} = \frac{4 \times 3.5}{\pi \times (2.0)^2} = \frac{14.0}{\pi \times 4.0} = \frac{14.0}{12.566} = 1.1140 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — New head loss:**

$$h_{f,new} = \frac{0.025 \times 1500 \times (1.1140)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.0}$$

Numerator: $0.025 \times 1500 \times 1.2410 = 46.537$
Denominator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 = 39.24$

$$h_{f,new} = \frac{46.537}{39.24} = 1.186 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — New transmission efficiency:**

$$\eta_{new} = \frac{120 - 1.186}{120} = \frac{118.814}{120} = 0.9901 = 99.01\%$$

**Step 4 — Percentage improvement:**

$$\%\text{ Improvement} = \frac{99.01 - 95.84}{95.84} \times 100 = 3.31\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{new} = 99.01\%, \quad \text{Improvement} = 3.31\%}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for new $V$, 3 marks for new $h_f$, 2 marks for $\eta$ and improvement)*

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive, starting from first principles, the expression for the efficiency of transmission of power through a penstock. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A long pipeline of diameter **0.8 m** and length **3000 m** carries water at a velocity of **2.5 m/s**. The friction factor is **0.028**. If the gross head supplied at the inlet is **80 m**, calculate: (i) the head lost due to friction, (ii) the transmission efficiency, and (iii) the **annual energy loss** in kWh if the system operates **6000 hours/year**. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Incompressible, steady, uniform flow.
2. Reservoir surface has negligible velocity.
3. Atmospheric pressure acts at both reservoir surface and turbine entry.
4. Minor losses are either negligible or lumped with major losses.
5. Single conduit with constant diameter.

**Derivation:**

Apply the steady-flow energy equation between reservoir surface (1) and turbine entry (2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

With $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$, $V_1 \approx 0$, and $z_1 - z_2 = H_g$:

$$H_g = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

Define net head reaching turbine: $H_{net} = H_g - h_L$

Power supplied: $P_1 = \rho g Q H_g$

Power received: $P_2 = \rho g Q H_{net}$

$$\eta_{transmission} = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{\rho g Q (H_g - h_L)}{\rho g Q H_g}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{transmission} = \frac{H_g - h_L}{H_g} = 1 - \frac{h_L}{H_g}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 2 for final boxed equation)*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:** $D = 0.8$ m, $L = 3000$ m, $V = 2.5$ m/s, $f = 0.028$, $H_g = 80$ m, $T = 6000$ h/year

**(i) Frictional head loss:**

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.028 \times 3000 \times (2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.8}$$

Numerator: $0.028 \times 3000 \times 6.25 = 525$
Denominator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 = 15.696$

$$h_f = \frac{525}{15.696} = 33.45 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Transmission efficiency:**

$$\eta_{transmission} = \frac{80 - 33.45}{80} = \frac{46.55}{80} = 0.5819 = 58.19\%$$

**(iii) Annual energy loss:**

First, compute discharge:

$$Q = A \cdot V = \frac{\pi (0.8)^2}{4} \times 2.5 = 0.5027 \times 2.5 = 1.2566 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Power at inlet:

$$P_{in} = \rho g Q H_g = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.2566 \times 80 = 986,400 \text{ W} \approx 986.4 \text{ kW}$$

Power lost:

$$P_{lost} = P_{in} \times (1 - \eta) = 986.4 \times (1 - 0.5819) = 986.4 \times 0.4181 = 412.4 \text{ kW}$$

Annual energy loss:

$$E_{lost} = P_{lost} \times T = 412.4 \times 6000 = 2,474,400 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{lost} = 2.4744 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/year}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for $h_f$, 2 for $\eta$, 3 for energy loss)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Discharge in **L/s** instead of **m³/s** is a common error costing 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing Darcy and Fanning factors:** KTU uses **Darcy** ($f$). Using $f/4$ by mistake halves your head loss.
> 3. **Sign error in head loss equation:** $h_f$ is *subtracted* from $H_g$, not added.
> 4. **Ignoring velocity head at turbine entry:** In derivations, students often forget to mention where $V^2/2g$ is accounted for.
> 5. **Annual energy calculation:** Multiplying by **8760** hours (full year) instead of the *given operating hours* — always re-read the problem.
> 6. **Percentage improvement formula:** Some students use absolute difference; use *relative* improvement: $\dfrac{\eta_{new} - \eta_{old}}{\eta_{old}} \times 100$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation:** $\eta_{trans} = (H_g - h_L) / H_g$ — memorize this verbatim.
- **Darcy-Weisbach:** $h_f = f L V^2 / (2 g D)$ — KTU standard.
- **Velocity–Diameter Coupling:** $V = 4Q / (\pi D^2)$ — appears in **every** numerical.
- **Head Loss $\propto 1/D^5$:** A small diameter increase yields massive friction reduction.
- **Continuity:** Discharge is constant throughout a single pipe (no branches).
- **Overall Efficiency:** Product of *transmission*, *hydraulic*, *mechanical*, *generator* efficiencies — KTU loves this chain multiplication.
- **Annual Energy Loss Formula:** $E_{lost} = \rho g Q h_L \times T_{hours}$ in Joules; divide by $3.6 \times 10^6$ for kWh.
- **Assumptions to State:** Incompressible flow, steady state, atmospheric pressure at both ends, negligible velocity at reservoir, constant pipe diameter.
- **Moody Chart:** Used to obtain $f$ from $Re$ and $\varepsilon/D$ — important for open-ended design problems.
- **Minor Losses:** Often neglected unless the problem explicitly mentions bends, valves, or entry/exit.
- **Economic Diameter:** Always exists — penstocks are never sized purely for minimum loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Hammer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Hammer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Hammer** (also called *Hydraulic Transient*, *Hydraulic Shock*, or *Pressure Surge*) is a pressure surge or wave phenomenon that occurs in a closed conduit when a fluid (typically a liquid such as water) in motion is forced to stop or change direction abruptly — most commonly caused by the rapid closure of a valve, sudden stoppage of a pump, or rapid closure of a turbine wicket gate.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT416 - Fluid Mechanics)** syllabus terminology, water hammer belongs to the category of **unsteady, unsteady-flow transients** in pressurized pipe systems, governed by the **Joukowsky equation** and the **elastic waterhammer theory** (also called *compressible waterhammer theory*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Water hammer is a high-weightage topic in KTU Module 4 and is frequently asked as a 7-mark or 14-mark question in End Semester Examinations. The two cornerstone results are the **Joukowsky pressure-rise equation** and the **celerity (wave speed) equation** for elastic pipes.

### 1.2 Physical Constants Used in Water Hammer Analysis

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **1000** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Bulk modulus of water | $K$ | **2.1 × 10⁹** | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Wave speed in water (theoretical) | $a$ | ≈ 1450 | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Wave speed in steel pipe (typical) | $a$ | ≈ 1000–1200 | $\text{m/s}$ |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Train Braking Analogy"

Imagine a long freight train moving at high speed on a track. If the driver slams the emergency brakes, the entire train does **not** stop instantly. The couplings between the wagons compress sequentially, creating a chain of jolts that travels backward through the train until all the kinetic energy is dissipated.

**Water hammer behaves in exactly the same way:**

- The **moving water column** is like the moving train, possessing substantial **kinetic energy** (because of its mass and velocity).
- The **valve (or pump)** is like the brake.
- When the valve slams shut, the water cannot stop instantly because of its **momentum** (Newton's First Law).
- This momentum is converted into a **pressure shock wave** that propagates upstream through the pipe at the speed of sound in water.
- The pressure wave **travels** up the pipe, **reflects** at the reservoir or dead-end, and **oscillates** back and forth until friction gradually damps it out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the pressure spike?** When the valve closes, the water column must decelerate from velocity $V_0$ to zero. By Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$), a sudden deceleration requires a huge force, which manifests as a huge pressure rise at the valve. If the closure is *gradual* (closure time longer than the *critical time* $t_c = 2L/a$), the pressure rise is much smaller.

### 1.4 Two Sub-Categories of Water Hammer

1. **Sudden (Rigid) Water Hammer** — Valve closure time $T < t_c = 2L/a$. Full Joukowsky pressure rise occurs. Worst-case scenario.
2. **Gradual (Elastic) Water Hammer** — Valve closure time $T > t_c$. Pressure rise is smaller and depends on closure rate $dV/dt$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Water Hammer — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Initial Steady State**
Water flows steadily through a long pipe of length $L$, diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, with steady velocity $V_0$ and static pressure head $H_0$.

**Step 2 — Disturbance Initiation**
A valve at the downstream end is closed. If the closure is *sudden* (faster than the round-trip travel time of a pressure wave, $2L/a$), the fluid column is brought to rest almost instantaneously.

**Step 3 — Pressure Wave Generation**
The kinetic energy of the decelerating fluid is converted into potential energy (pressure). A *positive* pressure wave of magnitude $\Delta P = \rho \, a \, V_0$ travels upstream toward the reservoir at celerity $a$.

**Step 4 — Wave Reflection at the Reservoir**
At the reservoir (open boundary), the pressure must remain equal to hydrostatic. The pressure wave reflects as a *negative* (rarefaction) wave of equal magnitude, traveling back downstream.

**Step 5 — Wave Reflection at the Closed Valve**
At the closed valve, the velocity must remain zero. The negative wave reflects again as a positive wave, perpetuating the oscillation.

**Step 6 — Damping**
Friction (wall shear stress) and minor losses gradually dissipate the wave energy, and the system returns to a new steady state. The phenomenon typically decays in a few seconds to minutes.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why does the wave travel at the speed of sound in water?** Because the disturbance is propagated by **elastic compression** of both the fluid and the pipe wall, similar to how sound travels through a medium.
- **Why is the pressure rise proportional to $V_0$?** Because momentum must be destroyed ($\Delta P \cdot A \cdot dt = \rho \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \Delta V$), and the time available is the travel time $L/a$.
- **Why does an elastic pipe reduce the surge?** A more elastic pipe wall expands to absorb the pressure, increasing the effective "storage" of the system and **reducing** the wave speed $a$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations for KTU 2024 ESE.** Every formula below has appeared (or is a direct variant of a question) in past KTU examinations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \rho \, a \, V_0$ | **Joukowsky equation** (pressure rise) | Sudden closure surge |
| 2 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{a \, V_0}{g}$ | Pressure head rise due to surge | Head-based problems |
| 3 | $a = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K \, D}{E \, t}}}$ | **Celerity** in elastic pipe | Wave speed calculation |
| 4 | $a = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | Celerity in **rigid** pipe (theoretical max) | Water-only estimate |
| 5 | $t_c = \dfrac{2L}{a}$ | **Critical time** (round-trip time of wave) | Sudden vs. gradual classification |
| 6 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{2 L \, V_0}{g \, T}$ | Pressure rise for **gradual** closure (Michaud) | Slow valve closure |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{2 L \, V_0}{g \, \Delta H}$ | **Minimum closure time** to limit surge | Design of valves |
| 8 | $V_0 = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2$ | Pipeline velocity | Foundation equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall in Markdown:** The absolute value signs `|x|` and the field separator `|` in tables are syntactically identical in markdown. To preserve the KTU formula sheet table, we use `\vert` for the absolute value delimiter (e.g., $\vert \Delta P \vert$) and the formula $\tfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ instead of the pipe-character denominator. This is **not** a math error — it is a markdown rendering safeguard.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Water hammer analysis is **mandatory** in the design of:
- Hydroelectric penstocks (especially high-head stations)
- Long water supply mains (urban distribution networks)
- Oil & gas pipelines
- Nuclear power plant cooling systems
- Pumping stations with rapid-acting check valves

**Engineers use water hammer analysis to:**
1. Specify **surge tanks**, **air vessels**, or **pressure relief valves** to absorb shocks.
2. Determine the **minimum safe valve closure time** to prevent pipe rupture.
3. Design pipe wall thickness to withstand surge pressures.
4. Schedule pump start-up and shut-down sequences to avoid catastrophic transients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Joukowsky Equation (Pressure Rise on Sudden Closure)

**Statement to Prove:**

$$\Delta P = \rho \, a \, V_0$$

**Setup:** Consider a long pipe of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $L$ carrying water with steady velocity $V_0$. A valve at the downstream end is closed **instantaneously** at time $t = 0$.

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the decelerating fluid column.**

When the valve closes, the fluid column of length $L$ decelerates from $V_0$ to $0$. The unbalanced pressure $\Delta P$ acts on the cross-section $A$ to produce this deceleration.

$$F = m \cdot a_{decel}$$

**Step 2 — Express force in terms of pressure.**

The net force acting on the fluid column is the surge pressure times the cross-sectional area:

$$F = \Delta P \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Express mass of the fluid column.**

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Express the deceleration.**

The fluid column takes a finite time $t = L/a$ to come to rest (the time for the pressure wave to travel from valve back to the reservoir). Therefore:

$$a_{decel} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} = \frac{V_0 - 0}{L/a} = \frac{a \, V_0}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into Newton's Second Law.**

$$\Delta P \cdot A = (\rho \cdot A \cdot L) \cdot \left( \frac{a \, V_0}{L} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $A$ and $L$ from both sides.**

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot V_0 \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Step 7 — Convert to pressure head form (divide both sides by $\rho g$).**

$$\Delta H = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{a \, V_0}{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The Joukowsky pressure rise depends on the *initial velocity* $V_0$ (linearly) and the *wave speed* $a$ (linearly), but is **independent of the pipe length** $L$ for sudden closure. This is the most counter-intuitive result of water hammer theory.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Celerity (Wave Speed) in an Elastic Pipe

**Statement to Prove:**

$$a = \frac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K \, D}{E \, t}}}$$

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation for a Control Volume of Fluid.**

Consider a small element of fluid of length $dx$, cross-section $A$, in an elastic pipe. When pressure rises by $dP$, two things happen simultaneously:
- The fluid **compresses** (density increases by $d\rho$).
- The pipe wall **expands radially** (area increases by $dA$).

**Step 2 — Mass Conservation.**

The mass entering the element equals the mass leaving plus the increase in mass inside:

$$\rho A V - (\rho + d\rho)(A + dA)(V + dV) = 0$$

Neglecting second-order differentials and simplifying:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Wave Propagation Relationship.**

For a wave traveling at celerity $a$ in the negative $x$ direction, the method of characteristics gives:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{d\rho}{\rho} \cdot \frac{a}{V}$$

**Step 4 — Pipe Wall Expansion (Hoop Stress Relation).**

For a thin-walled pipe under internal pressure $dP$, the hoop stress is $\sigma = dP \cdot D / (2t)$. The corresponding circumferential strain is $\varepsilon = \sigma / E = dP \cdot D / (2 E t)$. This strain equals the relative change in pipe radius (and hence in pipe area):

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dR}{R} = 2 \varepsilon = \frac{dP \cdot D}{E \cdot t}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Bulk Modulus Definition.**

By definition of bulk modulus:

$$K = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

Hence:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{dP}{K}$$

**Step 6 — Combine All Three Equations.**

Substitute Step 4 and Step 5 into Step 2:

$$\frac{dP}{K} + \frac{dP \cdot D}{E \, t} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

Using Step 3 to eliminate $dV/V$:

$$\frac{dP}{K} + \frac{dP \cdot D}{E \, t} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} \cdot \frac{a}{V} = \frac{dP}{K} \cdot \frac{a}{V}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify.**

Divide both sides by $dP/K$:

$$1 + \frac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t} = \frac{a}{V}$$

But from Step 3, $a/V = d\rho/\rho \cdot (V / dV)^{-1}$. After eliminating $dV$ using the momentum equation $dP = -\rho \cdot a \cdot dV$, we get:

$$a^2 = \frac{K / \rho}{1 + \dfrac{K \, D}{E \, t}}$$

**Step 8 — Take the Square Root.**

$$a = \frac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K \, D}{E \, t}}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Cases (frequently asked in KTU):**
> - If the pipe is **rigid** ($E \to \infty$), the denominator $\to 1$ and $a = \sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1450 \text{ m/s}$.
> - If the pipe is **very thin** ($t \to 0$) or **very flexible** ($E \to 0$), the denominator $\to \infty$ and $a \to 0$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Michaud Equation (Gradual Closure)

For **gradual** valve closure (closure time $T > 2L/a$), the pressure rise is:

$$\Delta H = \frac{2 L \, V_0}{g \, T}$$

**Step 1:** For gradual closure, the deceleration is $a_{decel} = V_0 / T$ (not $a V_0 / L$).

**Step 2:** Newton's Second Law gives:

$$\Delta P \cdot A = \rho \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \frac{V_0}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\rho L V_0}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta H = \frac{L V_0}{g T}$$

**Step 4:** However, the pressure wave reflects and returns during the closure, so the *effective* decelerating length is $2L$. The correct result is:

$$\Delta H = \frac{2 L \, V_0}{g \, T} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Table

| Closure Type | Condition | Pressure Rise |
|---|---|---|
| Sudden (instant) | $T \le t_c = 2L/a$ | $\Delta H = a V_0 / g$ |
| Gradual | $T > 2L/a$ | $\Delta H = 2 L V_0 / (g T)$ |
| No closure (open) | $T \to \infty$ | $\Delta H \to 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Water Hammer Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[RESERVOIR] --> nodeB[STEADY INFLOW]
    nodeB[STEADY INFLOW] --> nodeC[LONG PIPE L]
    nodeC[LONG PIPE L] --> nodeD[VALVE CLOSURE TRIGGER]
    nodeD[VALVE CLOSURE TRIGGER] --> nodeE{SUDDEN OR GRADUAL}
    nodeE[SUDDEN OR GRADUAL] --> nodeF[SUDDEN CLOSURE T LESS THAN 2L OVER a]
    nodeE[SUDDEN OR GRADUAL] --> nodeG[GRADUAL CLOSURE T GREATER THAN 2L OVER a]
    nodeF[SUDDEN CLOSURE T LESS THAN 2L OVER a] --> nodeH[PRESSURE WAVE GENERATION]
    nodeG[GRADUAL CLOSURE T GREATER THAN 2L OVER a] --> nodeI[MODERATE SURGE]
    nodeH[PRESSURE WAVE GENERATION] --> nodeJ[WAVE PROPAGATION UPSTREAM AT CELERITY a]
    nodeJ[WAVE PROPAGATION UPSTREAM AT CELERITY a] --> nodeK[REFLECTION AT RESERVOIR]
    nodeK[REFLECTION AT RESERVOIR] --> nodeL[REFLECTION AT VALVE]
    nodeL[REFLECTION AT VALVE] --> nodeM[OSCILLATION AND DAMPING]
    nodeM[OSCILLATION AND DAMPING] --> nodeN[NEW STEADY STATE]
    nodeI[MODERATE SURGE] --> nodeN[NEW STEADY STATE]
    nodeN[NEW STEADY STATE] --> nodeO[DESIGN MITIGATION SURGE TANK AIR VESSEL RELIEF VALVE]
```

### 4.2 Joukowsky Surge — Decision Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START ANALYSIS] --> input[INPUT PIPE LENGTH L DIAMETER D THICKNESS t MATERIAL E FLUID K RHO VELOCITY V0]
    input --> calcCelerity[CALCULATE CELERITY a EQUALS SQRT K OVER RHO DIVIDED BY SQRT 1 PLUS K D OVER E t]
    calcCelerity --> calcCritical[CALCULATE CRITICAL TIME tc EQUALS 2L OVER a]
    calcCritical --> compare{COMPARE CLOSURE TIME T WITH tc}
    compare[T LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO tc] --> sudden[SUDDEN CLOSURE DELTA H EQUALS a V0 OVER g]
    compare[T GREATER THAN tc] --> gradual[GRADUAL CLOSURE DELTA H EQUALS 2L V0 OVER g T]
    sudden --> check1[CHECK PIPE STRESS VS ALLOWABLE]
    gradual --> check2[CHECK PIPE STRESS VS ALLOWABLE]
    check1 --> design[ADD SURGE PROTECTION IF NEEDED]
    check2 --> design[ADD SURGE PROTECTION IF NEEDED]
```

### 4.3 Pressure Wave Propagation — Phased Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph phase0[PHASE 0 STEADY STATE]
        dir0[VELOCITY V0 PRESSURE H0 EVERYWHERE]
    end
    subgraph phase1[PHASE 1 VALVE CLOSES AT t EQUALS 0]
        dir1[VALVE END V EQUALS 0 PRESSURE H0 PLUS a V0 OVER g UPSTREAM STILL V0]
    end
    subgraph phase2[PHASE 2 WAVE TRAVELS TO RESERVOIR]
        dir2[PRESSURE WAVE FRONT MOVES UPSTREAM AT CELERITY a]
    end
    subgraph phase3[PHASE 3 REFLECTION AT RESERVOIR]
        dir3[WAVE REFLECTS AS RAREFACTION TRAVELING DOWNSTREAM]
    end
    subgraph phase4[PHASE 4 SECOND REFLECTION AT VALVE]
        dir4[NEGATIVE WAVE REFLECTS AS POSITIVE WAVE CYCLE CONTINUES]
    end
    subgraph phase5[PHASE 5 DAMPING TO NEW STEADY STATE]
        dir5[FRICTION DAMPENS OSCILLATIONS SYSTEM REACHES STATIC HEAD]
    end
    phase0[PHASE 0 STEADY STATE] --> phase1[PHASE 1 VALVE CLOSES AT t EQUALS 0]
    phase1[PHASE 1 VALVE CLOSES AT t EQUALS 0] --> phase2[PHASE 2 WAVE TRAVELS TO RESERVOIR]
    phase2[PHASE 2 WAVE TRAVELS TO RESERVOIR] --> phase3[PHASE 3 REFLECTION AT RESERVOIR]
    phase3[PHASE 3 REFLECTION AT RESERVOIR] --> phase4[PHASE 4 SECOND REFLECTION AT VALVE]
    phase4[PHASE 4 SECOND REFLECTION AT VALVE] --> phase5[PHASE 5 DAMPING TO NEW STEADY STATE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** Water hammer is fundamentally a *time-dependent wave phenomenon*; a physical free-body diagram would not capture the propagation, reflection, and damping sequence. The above block diagrams map the **functional architecture** of the transient process and are the standard KTU-recommended teaching schematic.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define water hammer. What is the physical cause of the pressure surge?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Water hammer is the phenomenon of a sudden rise in pressure in a pipeline when a flowing fluid is brought to rest abruptly by the rapid closure of a valve, sudden stoppage of a pump, or similar disturbance.
>
> **Physical Cause (1 mark):** The pressure surge is caused by the **rapid conversion of the kinetic energy of the moving fluid into potential energy (pressure energy)** when the fluid is forced to decelerate almost instantaneously. By Newton's Second Law, this rapid deceleration requires a large force, manifesting as a large pressure rise.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Joukowsky equation. Define celerity and give its value for water in a rigid pipe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Joukowsky Equation (1.5 marks):**
> $$\Delta P = \rho \, a \, V_0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \Delta H = \frac{a \, V_0}{g}$$
> where $\Delta P$ is the pressure rise, $\rho$ is fluid density, $a$ is the celerity (wave speed), and $V_0$ is the initial steady-state velocity.
>
> **Celerity (1 mark):** Celerity is the speed at which a pressure disturbance (shock wave) travels through the fluid in the pipe. For water in a **rigid** pipe, $a = \sqrt{K/\rho} = \sqrt{2.1 \times 10^9 / 1000} \approx 1450 \text{ m/s}$.
>
> **Rigid Pipe Value (0.5 mark):** $a \approx 1450 \text{ m/s}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

## QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q.A (a) Derive the Joukowsky equation for pressure rise due to sudden closure of a valve in a pipe. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The pipe is horizontal (or the elevation change is negligible).
2. The fluid is slightly compressible (bulk modulus $K$ is constant).
3. The pipe wall is elastic (or rigid in the simplest case).
4. Closure is sudden (closure time $T \le 2L/a$).
5. Friction is neglected during the very short transient.
6. The flow is one-dimensional.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Consider a pipe of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ carrying water with steady velocity $V_0$. At time $t = 0$, the valve at the downstream end is closed **instantaneously**.

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the fluid column:**

$$\Delta P \cdot A = m \cdot a_{decel}$$

**Step 2 — Express mass of fluid column:**

$$m = \rho A L$$

**Step 3 — Express deceleration as the change in velocity over the wave travel time:**

The pressure wave travels at celerity $a$ and takes time $L/a$ to reach the upstream reservoir, after which the fluid column is fully decelerated. Therefore:

$$a_{decel} = \frac{V_0 - 0}{L/a} = \frac{a V_0}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Newton's Second Law:**

$$\Delta P \cdot A = \rho A L \cdot \frac{a V_0}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \rho \, a \, V_0}$$

**Step 6 — Pressure head form:**

$$\Delta H = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{a V_0}{g} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

**Q.A (b) A cast-iron pipe of diameter 30 cm and wall thickness 15 mm carries water at a velocity of 2.5 m/s. The pipe is 1500 m long. Calculate: (i) the celerity of the pressure wave, (ii) the critical time, and (iii) the pressure rise if the valve is closed in 4 seconds. Take $K = 2.1 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $E = 1.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $D = 0.30 \text{ m}, \; t = 0.015 \text{ m}, \; V_0 = 2.5 \text{ m/s}, \; L = 1500 \text{ m}$
- $K = 2.1 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2, \; E = 1.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3, \; g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $T = 4 \text{ s}$

**(i) Celerity (2.5 marks):**

$$a = \frac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K D}{E t}}}$$

Compute the denominator term:

$$\frac{K D}{E t} = \frac{(2.1 \times 10^9)(0.30)}{(1.0 \times 10^{11})(0.015)} = \frac{6.3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^9} = 0.42$$

Compute the numerator:

$$\sqrt{K/\rho} = \sqrt{2.1 \times 10^9 / 1000} = \sqrt{2.1 \times 10^6} = 1449.14 \text{ m/s}$$

Therefore:

$$a = \frac{1449.14}{\sqrt{1 + 0.42}} = \frac{1449.14}{\sqrt{1.42}} = \frac{1449.14}{1.1916} \approx 1216 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Critical Time (1.5 marks):**

$$t_c = \frac{2L}{a} = \frac{2 \times 1500}{1216} = \frac{3000}{1216} \approx 2.467 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Pressure Rise (2 marks):**

Since $T = 4 \text{ s} > t_c = 2.467 \text{ s}$, closure is **gradual**. Use Michaud equation:

$$\Delta H = \frac{2 L V_0}{g T} = \frac{2 \times 1500 \times 2.5}{9.81 \times 4} = \frac{7500}{39.24} \approx 191.13 \text{ m of water}$$

Convert to pressure:

$$\Delta P = \rho g \Delta H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 191.13 = 1.875 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 1.875 \text{ MPa}$$

---

## QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q.B (a) With a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of water hammer. Distinguish between **rigid water column theory** and **elastic water column theory**. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation with Sketch (3.5 marks):**

Water hammer is the phenomenon of a pressure surge occurring in a pipeline when a moving fluid column is suddenly stopped. Consider a long pipe AB of length $L$ connected to a reservoir at A and a valve at B.

When the valve at B is suddenly closed, the fluid column of length $L$ is brought to rest almost instantly. A positive pressure wave of magnitude $\Delta P = \rho a V_0$ travels upstream from B to A. On reaching the reservoir, the wave reflects as a negative (rarefaction) wave and travels back to B. The cycle continues, with friction gradually damping the oscillation.

*(Sketch: Show reservoir on left, pipe, valve on right; indicate pressure wave fronts traveling both ways.)*

**Distinction (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Rigid Water Column Theory | Elastic Water Column Theory |
|---|---|---|
| Pipe wall | Assumed **rigid** (does not expand) | Assumed **elastic** (expands under pressure) |
| Fluid | Assumed **incompressible** | Assumed **slightly compressible** |
| Celerity | $a = \infty$ (instantaneous) | $a = \sqrt{K/\rho} / \sqrt{1 + KD/(Et)}$ (finite) |
| Pressure rise | Michaud: $\Delta H = 2LV_0/(gT)$ for slow closure; unbounded for sudden closure | Joukowsky: $\Delta H = aV_0/g$ (bounded even for sudden closure) |
| Accuracy | Approximate; valid only for **gradual** closure | More accurate; valid for **both** sudden and gradual closure |
| Use | Quick preliminary estimate | Detailed design and analysis |

---

**Q.B (b) A steel penstock of diameter 1.2 m, wall thickness 20 mm, and length 800 m carries water at 4 m/s. The valve at the downstream end is closed suddenly. Determine: (i) the celerity of the pressure wave, (ii) the rise in pressure head, and (iii) the maximum surge pressure if the valve were closed gradually in 6 seconds. Take $K = 2.1 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
$D = 1.2 \text{ m}, \; t = 0.020 \text{ m}, \; L = 800 \text{ m}, \; V_0 = 4 \text{ m/s}, \; T_1 \to 0$ (sudden), $T_2 = 6 \text{ s}$ (gradual), $K = 2.1 \times 10^9, \; E = 2.0 \times 10^{11}$

**(i) Celerity (2 marks):**

$$\frac{KD}{Et} = \frac{(2.1 \times 10^9)(1.2)}{(2.0 \times 10^{11})(0.020)} = \frac{2.52 \times 10^9}{4.0 \times 10^9} = 0.63$$

$$\sqrt{K/\rho} = 1449.14 \text{ m/s}$$

$$a = \frac{1449.14}{\sqrt{1.63}} = \frac{1449.14}{1.2767} \approx 1135 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Pressure Head Rise on Sudden Closure (2 marks):**

$$\Delta H = \frac{a V_0}{g} = \frac{1135 \times 4}{9.81} = \frac{4540}{9.81} \approx 462.79 \text{ m of water}$$

**(iii) Maximum Surge Pressure on Gradual Closure (2 marks):**

Check $t_c$:

$$t_c = \frac{2L}{a} = \frac{2 \times 800}{1135} = \frac{1600}{1135} \approx 1.41 \text{ s}$$

Since $T_2 = 6 \text{ s} > t_c = 1.41 \text{ s}$, closure is gradual. Use Michaud:

$$\Delta H_{gradual} = \frac{2 L V_0}{g T_2} = \frac{2 \times 800 \times 4}{9.81 \times 6} = \frac{6400}{58.86} \approx 108.73 \text{ m of water}$$

Surge pressure:

$$\Delta P_{gradual} = \rho g \Delta H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 108.73 \approx 1.067 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 1.067 \text{ MPa}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check the critical time** $t_c = 2L/a$ before applying Joukowsky vs. Michaud. Always compare closure time $T$ with $t_c$. If $T \le t_c$, use Joukowsky; if $T > t_c$, use Michaud. **(−2 marks for wrong formula)**
>
> 2. **Using diameter $D$ instead of radius $R$** in the pipe wall expansion formula. The hoop strain formula is $\varepsilon = dP \cdot D / (2 E t)$. **(−1 mark per error)**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert pressure rise to head rise** (or vice versa). $\Delta H = \Delta P / (\rho g)$ and $\Delta P = \rho g \Delta H$. The question may ask for either form. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** $D$ in mm vs. m. **Always convert all lengths to metres** before substituting. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
>
> 5. **In derivation questions, students often skip the assumption list.** KTU examiner's key requires explicit statement of assumptions. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Confusing $t_c$ (critical time) with $T$ (closure time).** $t_c$ is a property of the *system* (pipe); $T$ is a property of the *valve operation*. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Water Hammer Cheat List:**

- [x] **Water Hammer** = sudden pressure surge in a pipe when flow is abruptly stopped.
- [x] **Joukowsky Equation** (sudden closure): $\Delta P = \rho a V_0$ or $\Delta H = a V_0 / g$.
- [x] **Celerity (elastic pipe):** $a = \sqrt{K/\rho} \, / \, \sqrt{1 + K D/(E t)}$.
- [x] **Celerity (rigid pipe limit):** $a = \sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1450 \text{ m/s}$ for water.
- [x] **Critical time:** $t_c = 2L / a$ — round-trip time of pressure wave.
- [x] **Sudden closure** criterion: $T \le t_c$ → use Joukowsky.
- [x] **Gradual closure** criterion: $T > t_c$ → use Michaud: $\Delta H = 2 L V_0 / (g T)$.
- [x] **Pressure rise is INDEPENDENT of pipe length** for sudden closure.
- [x] **Pressure rise is INDEPENDENT of wave speed** for gradual closure.
- [x] **Bulk modulus of water:** $K = 2.1 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$.
- [x] **Modulus of elasticity** of steel $\approx 2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$; cast iron $\approx 1.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$.
- [x] **Density of water:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- [x] **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- [x] **Mitigation devices:** surge tanks, air vessels, pressure relief valves, slow-closing valves.
- [x] **Always list assumptions** in derivation questions (rigid vs. elastic pipe, horizontal pipe, neglected friction, 1-D flow, constant bulk modulus).
- [x] **Always convert units** consistently (SI: m, kg, s, N, Pa).
- [x] **Remember to check** $T$ vs. $t_c$ **before** choosing the formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT416 - FLUID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cavitation** is a vaporization phenomenon in liquid flow that occurs when the **local static pressure** of a flowing liquid falls **below** the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. This causes the formation of transient vapor-filled cavities (or "bubbles") within the liquid. When these vapor bubbles are convected into regions of higher pressure, they collapse violently, producing high-energy micro-jets and shock waves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT416, Module 4):**
> *"Cavitation is the process of formation of vapor bubbles (or cavities) in a liquid when it is subjected to reduced pressure at constant ambient temperature. It is a critical phenomenon in hydraulic machines (pumps, turbines) and converging-diverging passages (orifices, venturis, nozzles)."*

The condition for incipient (beginning) cavitation is mathematically stated as:

$$P_{\text{local}} \le P_{\text{vapor}}$$

where:
- $P_{\text{local}}$ = local static (thermodynamic) pressure of the flowing liquid
- $P_{\text{vapor}}$ = saturation vapor pressure of the liquid at the bulk temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction — Cavitation vs. Boiling:**
> - **Boiling** occurs when the liquid's saturation temperature is raised (heat addition) at a given pressure.
> - **Cavitation** occurs when the local pressure is dropped (mechanical/dynamic action) at a given temperature.
> - Both phenomena convert liquid to vapor, but the driving mechanism is fundamentally different.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water in a covered pressure cooker. The water stays liquid even at 110 °C because the trapped steam raises the pressure above atmospheric. Now imagine a **prohibited trick** — placing that pressure cooker under a vacuum pump while still heating it. The water would violently boil at room temperature because the pressure dropped below the vapor pressure.

**Cavitation is precisely this — a "cold boiling"** induced by local pressure drop, NOT heat.

### Real-World Intuitive Analogy: The Boat Propeller

Picture a high-speed boat propeller spinning underwater:

1. The blade surfaces are curved (hydrofoils).
2. As the propeller spins, the **suction side** of each blade accelerates water to a very high velocity.
3. By Bernoulli's principle, high velocity ⇒ low pressure.
4. If this local pressure drops below the water's vapor pressure, tiny vapor cavities form.
5. These cavities are carried away with the flow into a high-pressure region (the wake).
6. They **implode** (collapse) with sharp "crackling" sounds — this is what mariners call the *"propeller cavitation noise."*

> [!TIP]
> **Everyday Observation:** You have heard cavitation many times! The *crackling/hissing* sound of a **kitchen faucet** when it is almost closed (high-velocity jet at the constriction) is a small-scale example of cavitation. The *kettling* noise in **overheating central heating pipes** is another familiar instance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Typical Value (Water @ 20 °C) | Units |
|----------|--------------------------------|-------|
| Vapor pressure of water ($P_v$) | **2.34** | kPa (absolute) |
| Density of water ($\rho$) | **998** | kg/m³ |
| Atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm}$) | **101.325** | kPa (absolute) |
| NPSH margin (typical pumps) | **0.5 – 3.0** | m |

> [!CAUTION]
> The **vapor pressure is strongly temperature-dependent**. At 100 °C, $P_v$ rises to **101.325 kPa** (equals atmospheric), so cavitation becomes trivially easy. This is why *hot* pumps cavitate more readily than *cold* pumps.

## 1.4 Where Cavitation Occurs (KTU Focus)

| Engineering System | Location of Cavitation | Reason for Low Pressure |
|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Centrifugal pump | Impeller eye (suction side of vanes) | High velocity, suction lift |
| Reaction turbine (Francis/Kaplan) | Runner blade trailing edges | High relative velocity at exit |
| Orifice / Venturi / Nozzle | Throat (minimum area) | Highest velocity ⇒ lowest pressure |
| Pipelines | Sudden contractions, valve throats | Local acceleration |
| Ship propellers | Suction side of blades | High rotational tip speed |
| Spillways / High-velocity chutes | Irregularities, joints | Local flow separation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Cavitation — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Pressure Variation in Flowing Liquids
By **Euler's / Bernoulli's equation**, when a fluid moves faster, its static (thermodynamic) pressure drops. The simplest form is:

$$P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$$

So, the static pressure at any point is reduced whenever the velocity $V$ increases (a phenomenon called the **Venturi effect**).

### Step 2: Reaching the Vapor Pressure Threshold
As the fluid accelerates through a constriction, $P$ falls. The instant $P$ reaches $P_v$, the liquid can no longer remain a single-phase fluid — dissolved gases and the liquid itself begin to vaporize, forming **cavities**.

### Step 3: Transport of Cavities
These cavities are swept along with the flow into regions of **higher** static pressure (further downstream or in a region of flow deceleration).

### Step 4: Violent Collapse (Implosion)
In the high-pressure region, the vapor inside the cavity is no longer in equilibrium. The cavity collapses **asymmetrically**, forming a high-speed micro-jet of liquid (~100–200 m/s) that strikes the adjacent solid boundary.

### Step 5: Material Damage and Side Effects
- **Pitting / Erosion:** Repeated micro-jet impacts fatigue and erode the metal surface.
- **Vibration & Noise:** Cavity collapse produces a broadband "hissing" or "crackling" sound and excites structural vibrations.
- **Performance Loss:** Vapor pockets displace real liquid, reducing mass flow rate and efficiency of pumps/turbines.
- **Efficiency Drop in Turbines:** Cavities on the suction side of turbine runner blades alter the lift/drag balance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does collapse cause damage?** During collapse, a vapor bubble concentrates the surrounding liquid's kinetic energy into a microscopic area. The resulting pressure spike can locally exceed **1000 MPa** — enough to pit even hardened steel.

## 2.2 The Cavitation Number — Thoma's Parameter ($\sigma$)

The **Thoma cavitation parameter** is the single most important dimensionless number used by hydraulic engineers to characterize cavitation susceptibility. It expresses the ratio of the pressure margin above vapor pressure to the dynamic (velocity) pressure of the flow.

### General (Velocity-Based) Form:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma = \dfrac{P_{\text{ref}} - P_{\text{vapor}}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{\text{ref}}^{\,2}}\;}$$

where $P_{\text{ref}}$ and $V_{\text{ref}}$ are appropriate reference pressure and velocity (chosen depending on the device — pump, turbine, or general flow).

### Critical / Incipient Cavitation Number
There is a specific value of $\sigma$ at which cavitation just begins, called the **critical cavitation number** $\sigma_c$. Cavitation occurs when:

$$\sigma < \sigma_c$$

and is **avoided** when:

$$\sigma \ge \sigma_c \quad \text{(with safety margin recommended)}$$

## 2.3 Cavitation in Pumps — Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

In **pumps**, cavitation is most commonly avoided by ensuring adequate pressure at the pump suction. The relevant quantity is the **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)**.

### NPSH Available (NPSHₐ) — Determined by the *system*:

$$\boxed{\;NPSH_{A} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm z_{s} - h_{f} - \dfrac{P_{v}}{\rho g}\;}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $P_{atm}$ | Absolute atmospheric pressure on the liquid surface |
| $z_s$ | Static suction head (positive if liquid is *above* pump; negative if below) |
| $h_f$ | Friction head loss in the suction piping |
| $P_v$ | Vapor pressure of the liquid (absolute) at the operating temperature |

### NPSH Required (NPSHᵣ) — Determined by the *pump manufacturer*:
A function of flow rate, rotational speed, and impeller geometry. Provided in the pump's performance curve.

### Cavitation Criterion for Pumps:

$$\boxed{\;NPSH_{A} > NPSH_{R} \quad \text{(by an adequate safety margin, typically } \ge 1\,\text{m)}\;}$$

## 2.4 Cavitation in Turbines — Head-Based Thoma Parameter

In **reaction turbines** (Francis, Kaplan), Thoma's parameter is expressed in terms of heads:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{T} = \dfrac{H_{sv} - H_{v}}{H}\;}$$

where:
- $H$ = net head across the turbine
- $H_{sv}$ = atmospheric pressure head at the tailrace (or setting) level
- $H_v$ = vapor pressure head at operating temperature
- $H_{sv} - H_v$ = **available suction head** (sometimes denoted $H_a$)

The criterion is:

$$\sigma_T \ge \sigma_{\text{critical}}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application | Key Variables |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P_{\text{local}} \le P_v$ | Incipient cavitation condition | Local static pressure, vapor pressure |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_{ref} - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{ref}^{2}}$ | General cavitation number (Thoma) | Reference $P$, $V$ |
| 3 | $NPSH_{A} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm z_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$ | Pump system analysis | Atmospheric, suction head, friction, vapor |
| 4 | $NPSH_{A} > NPSH_{R}$ | Pump cavitation criterion | Available vs. required NPSH |
| 5 | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{H_{sv} - H_v}{H}$ | Turbine cavitation number | Heads |
| 6 | $H_{sv} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - z_{\text{tailrace}}$ | Setting (suction) head calculation | Atmospheric, tailrace elevation |
| 7 | $\sigma \ge \sigma_c$ | Universal cavitation avoidance | Comparison with critical number |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Note for KTU Board Exams:** All **head** quantities must be in **metres (m)**. All **pressures** in $N/m^2$ or Pa. Use $\rho g = 9.81\,\text{kN/m}^3$ for water. Mixing units is the most common reason students lose 1–2 marks in numerical problems.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pump Selection & Piping Design:** Engineers compute $NPSH_A$ from the proposed piping layout and compare it with the manufacturer's $NPSH_R$ to select a pump that will operate without cavitation.
2. **Turbine Draft Tube Design:** The shape and length of the draft tube are designed so that pressure at the runner exit does not fall below vapor pressure.
3. **Ship Propeller Geometry:** Blade tip speed is limited to keep the suction-side pressure above vapor pressure.
4. **Spillway / Chute Design:** Aeration slots and surface smoothness reduce cavitation risk in high-velocity open-channel flow.
5. **Hydraulic System Reliability:** Cavitation prediction is central to MTBF (mean time between failures) of valves, hydraulic actuators, and process pumps.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the **full derivations** that KTU examiners expect for a 14-mark question. Every algebraic transition is justified.

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The General Thoma Cavitation Number from First Principles

We start with **Euler's equation of motion** (a differential form of Bernoulli's equation) along a streamline for an inviscid, incompressible fluid:

$$\dfrac{dP}{\rho} + V\,dV + g\,dz = 0$$

**Step 1 — Choose a Reference Point and the Incipient Cavitation Point:**

- Let point "1" be a location **upstream** of the constriction (high pressure, low velocity).
- Let point "2" be the location of **lowest static pressure** (typically at the throat, the point where cavitation begins).
- At the incipient cavitation threshold, $P_2 = P_v$ (local static pressure equals vapor pressure).

**Step 2 — Integrate Euler's Equation Between Points 1 and 2:**

Integrating between (1) and (2):

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{\rho} + \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V\,dV + \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g\,dz = 0$$

For an **incompressible** liquid ($\rho = \text{constant}$), this becomes:

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Isolate the Pressure Difference:**

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Cavitation Condition ($P_2 = P_v$):**

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_v}{\rho} = \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between the Same Two Points to Remove the Geometric Term:**

By Bernoulli's equation:

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$

Replacing $P_2 = P_v$ and rearranging:

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_v}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_1^{2} - V_2^{2}}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Compare Steps 4 and 5 to Eliminate the Velocity and Elevation Differences:**

From Step 4:
$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_v}{\rho} = \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2)$$

From Step 5 (rearranged):
$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_v}{\rho} = \dfrac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2)$$

Both are identical — the **two equations are the same** because we used the same Bernoulli principle.

**Step 7 — Recognize that the "Cavitation Number" is the Ratio of Pressure Margin to Dynamic Head:**

Thoma's brilliant insight was to **non-dimensionalize** the pressure margin above vapor pressure by the kinetic-energy (dynamic) pressure of the flow at the **reference velocity** $V_{ref}$:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P_1 - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{ref}^{\,2}}$$

For a free-stream approaching flow with velocity $V_\infty$:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_\infty = \dfrac{P_\infty - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{\,2}}\;}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation of $\sigma$:**

- **Large $\sigma$ (≫ 1):** Pressure margin is large relative to dynamic pressure — **no cavitation**.
- **Small $\sigma$ (≈ 0.1–1):** Marginal — **incipient cavitation**.
- **Very small $\sigma$ (≪ 0.1):** Dynamic pressure dominates — **fully developed cavitation / supercavitation**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question:** A common exam question asks, *"If Thoma's cavitation number is large, does cavitation occur?"* The correct answer is **NO** — a large $\sigma$ means a large pressure margin above vapor pressure, so cavitation is **suppressed**.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) for Pumps

Consider a pump installation: the liquid surface (reservoir) is at elevation $z_0$, the pump impeller eye is at elevation $z_p$, and the suction pipe has friction loss $h_f$.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation from the Reservoir Surface (Point 0) to the Pump Impeller Eye (Point P):**

$$\dfrac{P_0}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_0^{2}}{2g} + z_0 = \dfrac{P_p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_p^{2}}{2g} + z_p + h_f$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Boundary Conditions at the Reservoir Surface:**

At the free surface of an open reservoir, the pressure is atmospheric and the velocity is approximately zero:

$$P_0 = P_{atm}, \quad V_0 \approx 0$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + z_0 = \dfrac{P_p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_p^{2}}{2g} + z_p + h_f$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Pressure at the Impeller Eye:**

$$\dfrac{P_p}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + (z_0 - z_p) - \dfrac{V_p^{2}}{2g} - h_f$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Static Suction Head:**

Define the **static suction head** as $z_s = z_0 - z_p$:
- If the reservoir is **above** the pump → $z_s > 0$ (flooded suction).
- If the reservoir is **below** the pump → $z_s < 0$ (suction lift, common for shallow-well pumps).

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{P_p}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_s - \dfrac{V_p^{2}}{2g} - h_f$$

**Step 5 — Define NPSH Available as the Pressure Head at the Impeller Eye Above Vapor Pressure:**

NPSH is, by definition, the **excess of total head at the impeller inlet over the vapor-pressure head of the liquid**:

$$NPSH_A = \left(\dfrac{P_p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_p^{2}}{2g}\right) - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Expression for $P_p / \rho g$ from Step 4:**

$$NPSH_A = \left[\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_s - \dfrac{V_p^{2}}{2g} - h_f\right] + \dfrac{V_p^{2}}{2g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the Velocity Head Terms:**

The $-V_p^{2}/(2g)$ from Step 4 and $+V_p^{2}/(2g)$ from Step 5 cancel exactly:

$$NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

$$\boxed{\;NPSH_{A} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm z_{s} - h_{f} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}\;}$$

This matches the formula sheet entry (4).

**Step 8 — Engineering Insight:**
The velocity at the impeller eye **cancels out** — what matters is not the local velocity but rather the **total energy available** at the suction flange. The pump manufacturer accounts for $V_p$ when reporting $NPSH_R$, so the comparison $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ is a direct apples-to-apples test.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Thoma's Parameter in Head Form (for Turbines)

For a reaction turbine, define:
- $H$ = net head on the turbine (energy extracted per unit weight)
- $H_v$ = vapor pressure head of water at operating temperature
- $H_s$ = suction (or setting) head = atmospheric pressure head minus the elevation of the runner above tailrace
- $H_a = H_s - H_v$ = head available above vapor pressure at the runner outlet

**Step 1 — Begin with the Velocity-Based Thoma Parameter:**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P_{out} - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{out}^{\,2}}$$

where $P_{out}$ and $V_{out}$ refer to the runner outlet.

**Step 2 — Convert the Velocity Term to Head Using $V = \sqrt{2gH}$:**

For flow leaving the runner with velocity $V$ corresponding to the dynamic component of the net head $H$:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = \rho g H \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}/(\rho g) = H$$

**Step 3 — Convert the Pressure Term to Head:**

$$\dfrac{P_{out} - P_v}{\rho g} = H_s - H_v$$

where $H_s$ is the absolute pressure head at the runner outlet (above atmospheric datum) and $H_v$ is the vapor pressure head.

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio (Dividing Numerator by Denominator, both in head form):**

$$\sigma_T = \dfrac{(H_s - H_v)}{H} = \dfrac{H_a}{H}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{T} = \dfrac{H_{sv} - H_{v}}{H} = \dfrac{H_{a}}{H}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Cavitation Criterion for Turbines:**

The turbine is safe from cavitation if:

$$\sigma_T \ge \sigma_{\text{critical}}$$

In practice, the installation elevation (tailrace level relative to runner) is chosen to ensure this.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump handles water at 30 °C. The suction pipe is 50 mm in diameter and 6 m long. The pump takes water from an open sump whose water surface is 3 m below the pump centerline. The average velocity in the suction pipe is 2.5 m/s. The friction factor $f$ in the suction pipe is 0.025. Determine whether the pump will cavitate.

**Given:**
- Water at 30 °C → $P_v = 4.25$ kPa (absolute)
- $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
- $L = 6$ m, $D = 50$ mm = 0.05 m
- $V = 2.5$ m/s
- $z_s = -3$ m (sump is below pump)
- $f = 0.025$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 996$ kg/m³

**Solution:**

**Step A — Compute the Friction Head Loss:**

$$h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^{2}}{2gD} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.025 \times 6 \times (2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05}$$

Computing the numerator: $4 \times 0.025 \times 6 \times 6.25 = 3.75$

Computing the denominator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 0.981$

$$h_f = \dfrac{3.75}{0.981} = 3.822\;\text{m}$$

**Step B — Compute NPSH Available:**

$$NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{101325}{996 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{101325}{9770.76} = 10.37\;\text{m}$$

$$\dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} = \dfrac{4250}{9770.76} = 0.435\;\text{m}$$

$$NPSH_A = 10.37 + (-3) - 3.822 - 0.435 = 3.113\;\text{m}$$

**Step C — Compare with NPSH Required (assumed $NPSH_R = 2.0$ m from manufacturer's catalog):**

$$NPSH_A = 3.113\;\text{m} \;>\; NPSH_R = 2.0\;\text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The pump will **NOT cavitate** (with a healthy margin of ~1.1 m). The installation is safe.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| # | Concept | Driving Equation | Key Criterion | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Cavitation (general) | $P_{\text{local}} \le P_v$ | Yes if satisfied | 2–3 |
| 2 | Thoma parameter | $\sigma = (P - P_v)/(\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$ | $\sigma \ge \sigma_c$ | 3–4 |
| 3 | NPSH (pumps) | $NPSH_A = P_{atm}/(\rho g) \pm z_s - h_f - P_v/(\rho g)$ | $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ | 5–7 |
| 4 | Thoma (turbines) | $\sigma_T = (H_{sv} - H_v)/H$ | $\sigma_T \ge \sigma_c$ | 3–4 |
| 5 | Effects of cavitation | Pitting, noise, vibration, efficiency loss | Recognize | 2 |
| 6 | Remedies | Submerge pump, lower elevation, reduce friction, cool liquid | Application | 2–3 |

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Box Logic:** The diamond (A4) is the **decision node**. If the answer is YES, the pump is safe. If NO, the system must be modified — typically by:
> - Lowering the pump installation (decreasing suction lift / increasing suction head)
> - Increasing pipe diameter to reduce $h_f$
> - Reducing liquid temperature to lower $P_v$
> - Selecting a different pump with a lower $NPSH_R$
