# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cavitation. State the condition for incipient cavitation.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Cavitation** is a hydraulic phenomenon in which vapor cavities form within a flowing liquid when the **local static pressure** falls **below** the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the operating temperature. When these vapor bubbles are transported into regions of higher pressure, they collapse violently, producing noise, vibration, and erosion of adjacent solid surfaces.
>
> **Condition for incipient cavitation:**
> $$\boxed{P_{\text{local}} \le P_{\text{vapor}}}$$
>
> where $P_{\text{local}}$ is the local static pressure of the flowing liquid and $P_{\text{vapor}}$ is the saturation vapor pressure at the bulk liquid temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Allocation (Examiner Pattern):** Definition = 2 marks; Condition = 1 mark. Students commonly lose 1 mark by writing only the condition without the descriptive definition.

---

### Question 2: Define Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) Available and NPSH Required. State the cavitation criterion for a centrifugal pump.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **NPSH Available ($NPSH_A$):** It is the **total head available at the pump suction flange above the vapor pressure head of the liquid**. It is a property of the *piping system* and is given by:
> $$NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm z_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$$
>
> **NPSH Required ($NPSH_R$):** It is the **minimum NPSH needed by the pump at a given discharge and speed** to avoid cavitation. It is a property of the *pump* and is obtained from the manufacturer's performance curves.
>
> **Cavitation Criterion:**
> $$\boxed{NPSH_A > NPSH_R}$$
>
> The margin should be at least 0.5–1.0 m in practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $NPSH_A > NPSH$ (forgetting the subscript $R$) costs 1 mark. Always include the subscript notation exactly as in textbooks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 contains a 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): NPSH and Cavitation Analysis of a Centrifugal Pump Installation**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 4]`

A centrifugal pump draws water at 25 °C from a sump whose water surface is 2.5 m below the pump centerline. The suction pipe is 75 mm in diameter and 8 m long. The average velocity in the suction pipe is 3.0 m/s. The Darcy friction factor is 0.022. The pump manufacturer specifies the following at the design duty point:

- $NPSH_R = 2.5$ m
- Delivery head = 18 m at discharge 12 L/s
- Pump efficiency = 70 %

**Answer the following:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the **NPSH Available** at the pump suction and determine whether the pump will cavitate at this duty point.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the pump is relocated so that the sump water surface is **above** the pump centerline by 0.5 m, recalculate the NPSH Available. Comment on the change in cavitation safety margin. State **three** practical remedies if the original installation had been unsafe.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Given Data:**
- Water at 25 °C → $P_v = 3.17$ kPa, $\rho = 997$ kg/m³
- $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
- $D = 0.075$ m, $L = 8$ m, $V = 3.0$ m/s
- $f = 0.022$
- Case (a): $z_s = -2.5$ m (sump below pump)
- Case (b): $z_s = +0.5$ m (sump above pump)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

---

**(a) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Convert Pressures to Head:**

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{101325}{997 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{101325}{9780.57} = 10.36\;\text{m}$$

$$\dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} = \dfrac{3170}{9780.57} = 0.324\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Friction Head Loss:**

$$h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^{2}}{2gD} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.022 \times 8 \times 9}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075}$$

$$h_f = \dfrac{6.336}{1.4715} = 4.305\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply NPSH Formula with $z_s = -2.5$ m:**

$$NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

$$NPSH_A = 10.36 + (-2.5) - 4.305 - 0.324$$

$$NPSH_A = 3.231\;\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with NPSH Required:**

$$NPSH_A = 3.231\;\text{m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad NPSH_R = 2.5\;\text{m}$$

$$NPSH_A - NPSH_R = 0.731\;\text{m} > 0$$

**Conclusion:** $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ ⟹ **The pump will NOT cavitate** at this duty point, with a safety margin of approximately **0.73 m**.

---

**(b) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 5 — Recompute NPSH Available with $z_s = +0.5$ m:**

$$NPSH_A^{\text{new}} = 10.36 + 0.5 - 4.305 - 0.324 = 6.231\;\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the New Safety Margin:**

$$NPSH_A^{\text{new}} - NPSH_R = 6.231 - 2.5 = 3.731\;\text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The safety margin has increased from 0.73 m to 3.73 m — a **5-fold improvement** in cavitation safety.

**Step 7 — Three Practical Remedies if the Original Installation had been Unsafe** (i.e., if $NPSH_A < NPSH_R$):

1. **Increase static suction head** (raise sump level relative to pump, or place pump below sump) — directly adds to $z_s$.
2. **Reduce friction head loss** in suction piping — increase pipe diameter, shorten pipe length, use smoother bends.
3. **Reduce liquid temperature** to lower $P_v$ (coolers/chillers) — directly reduces the vapor pressure head term.
4. *(Alternative)* Use a **different pump model with a lower $NPSH_R$** at the design duty.
5. *(Alternative)* Reduce the **rotational speed** of the pump (variable frequency drive) — this typically lowers $NPSH_R$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion of $P_v$ to head** (kPa → m). Always convert.
> 2. **Sign convention of $z_s$**: positive when sump is *above* pump (flooded suction), negative when *below* (suction lift). Many students reverse this and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing friction factors**: Some textbooks use $f$ (Darcy) and some use $f'$ (Fanning). The formula $h_f = 4 f' L V^2 / (2gD)$ is for *Fanning*. If the problem gives $f$ = Darcy, use $h_f = f L V^2/(2gD)$. **Read the question carefully.**
> 4. **Not stating the comparison** explicitly. Writing only the $NPSH_A$ value without comparing to $NPSH_R$ is **incomplete** (loses 1 mark).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Thoma's Cavitation Number and Reaction Turbine Setting**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]`

A reaction turbine operates under a **net head** of 30 m and discharges water at 2 m³/s. The runner is set **4.5 m above the tailrace water level**. The atmospheric pressure at the site is **98 kPa** and the water temperature is **20 °C** (vapor pressure of water = 2.34 kPa).

**Answer the following:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the **Thoma cavitation number** $\sigma_T$ for the turbine installation. Given that the **critical cavitation number** for this turbine at the operating duty is $\sigma_c = 0.10$, determine whether cavitation will occur.

**(b) [7 marks]** If cavitation is predicted (or to provide a safety margin of 1 m in available head), determine the **maximum permissible elevation** of the runner above the tailrace. State **two** design modifications that could prevent cavitation in turbines.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Given Data:**
- $H = 30$ m
- $Q = 2$ m³/s (not needed for $\sigma$ computation)
- $z_{\text{runner-tailrace}} = 4.5$ m (runner is above tailrace)
- $P_{atm} = 98$ kPa
- $P_v = 2.34$ kPa
- Water at 20 °C → $\rho = 998$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\sigma_c = 0.10$

---

**(a) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute Atmospheric Pressure Head:**

$$H_{atm} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{98000}{998 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{98000}{9790.38} = 10.01\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Vapor Pressure Head:**

$$H_v = \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} = \dfrac{2340}{9790.38} = 0.239\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Suction (Setting) Head at the Runner Outlet:**

The pressure head at the runner outlet is the **atmospheric head minus the elevation above tailrace** (since tailrace is at atmospheric pressure):

$$H_{s} = H_{atm} - z_{\text{runner-tailrace}} = 10.01 - 4.5 = 5.51\;\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Available Head Above Vapor Pressure:**

$$H_{a} = H_s - H_v = 5.51 - 0.239 = 5.271\;\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Thoma's Cavitation Parameter:**

$$\sigma_T = \dfrac{H_a}{H} = \dfrac{5.271}{30} = 0.1757$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_T \approx 0.176}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with Critical Cavitation Number:**

$$\sigma_T = 0.176 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sigma_c = 0.10$$

Since $\sigma_T > \sigma_c$ (i.e., $0.176 > 0.10$), **cavitation will NOT occur** at the operating duty point.

**Safety margin:**

$$\sigma_T - \sigma_c = 0.076 \quad \text{or} \quad 76\% \text{ above critical}$$

---

**(b) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 7 — Determine Maximum Permissible Runner Elevation:**

For the cavitation criterion to be just satisfied *with* a 1 m head safety margin, the *available head* must be at least:

$$H_a^{\text{required}} = (\sigma_c + 0.05) \times H = 0.10 \times 30 = 3.0\;\text{m (minimum)}$$

Wait — the question specifies a 1 m safety margin in **head**. So the available head must exceed the minimum required by 1 m:

$$H_a^{\text{min}} = \sigma_c \cdot H + 1 = 0.10 \times 30 + 1 = 4.0\;\text{m}$$

**Step 8 — Express $H_a$ in Terms of Runner Elevation:**

$$H_a = H_{atm} - z - H_v = 10.01 - z - 0.239 = 9.771 - z$$

where $z$ is the runner elevation above tailrace.

**Step 9 — Solve for $z$:**

$$H_a = 4.0\;\text{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 9.771 - z = 4.0$$

$$z_{\max} = 9.771 - 4.0 = 5.771\;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{z_{\max} \approx 5.77\;\text{m}}$$

**Conclusion:** The runner can be placed up to **5.77 m above the tailrace** while maintaining the 1 m safety margin against cavitation.

**Step 10 — Two Design Modifications to Prevent Turbine Cavitation:**

1. **Lower the runner elevation** (i.e., reduce $z$): Place the runner closer to the tailrace, or even below it (creating a "submerged" installation). This increases $H_a$ (more pressure head at the runner exit) and thereby increases $\sigma_T$.

2. **Use a draft tube (conical or elbow type):** A properly designed draft tube recovers kinetic energy at the runner exit and creates a **vacuum (sub-atmospheric pressure) at the runner exit**, which effectively raises the available head above vapor pressure. This is the standard method for Francis and Kaplan turbines.

3. *(Alternative)* **Lower the operating temperature** of the water to reduce $P_v$ (relevant for thermal power plant cooling water circuits).

4. *(Alternative)* **Operate at a lower head** (e.g., reduce guide vane opening) — but this changes the duty point and is usually undesirable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Errors:**
> 1. **Confusing $H_{atm}$ with $P_{atm}$**: Students write $H_{atm} = 98$ kPa instead of converting to head (~10 m). Always convert pressure to head via $\rho g$ before using in head-based Thoma formula.
> 2. **Sign of $z$ in $H_s$ formula**: $H_s = H_{atm} - z$ is correct when the runner is *above* tailrace. If the runner were *below* tailrace, the sign flips: $H_s = H_{atm} + z$.
> 3. **Forgetting $H_v$**: The vapor pressure head is small (~0.24 m) but it MUST be subtracted. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not interpreting $\sigma_T$ vs $\sigma_c$**: A bare numerical value of $\sigma_T$ without the comparison and conclusion is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision summary — read this 1 hour before the exam:**

### A. Core Concepts (2-marks level)
- ✅ Cavitation = vapor bubble formation when **$P_{\text{local}} \le P_v$**.
- ✅ Bubbles form at **low-pressure zones** (throats, impeller eyes, blade suction sides).
- ✅ Bubbles **collapse** in **high-pressure regions** (wake, downstream).
- ✅ **Vapor pressure $P_v$ increases sharply with temperature** (water: 2.34 kPa at 20 °C, 101.3 kPa at 100 °C).

### B. Key Formulae (Memorize!)
- ✅ **Incipient cavitation condition:** $P_{\text{local}} \le P_v$.
- ✅ **Thoma's parameter (general):** $\sigma = (P_{\text{ref}} - P_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\text{ref}}^2)$.
- ✅ **Thoma's parameter (turbines, head form):** $\sigma_T = (H_{sv} - H_v) / H$.
- ✅ **NPSH Available:** $NPSH_A = P_{atm}/(\rho g) \pm z_s - h_f - P_v/(\rho g)$.
- ✅ **Pump cavitation criterion:** $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$.
- ✅ **Turbine cavitation criterion:** $\sigma_T \ge \sigma_c$.

### C. Critical Distinctions
- ✅ **Cavitation ≠ Boiling.** Cavitation is pressure-driven; boiling is temperature-driven.
- ✅ **NPSHₐ is from the system (piping); NPSHᵣ is from the pump (manufacturer).** Don't mix up sources.
- ✅ **$\sigma$ large = safe; $\sigma$ small = dangerous.** Inverse relationship to cavitation risk.
- ✅ **Flooded suction** ($z_s > 0$) is always safer than **suction lift** ($z_s < 0$).

### D. Effects of Cavitation (Memorize 4)
- ✅ **Pitting / erosion** of metal surfaces.
- ✅ **Noise and vibration** (characteristic crackling / hissing).
- ✅ **Reduced efficiency** and performance of pumps / turbines.
- ✅ **Structural damage** to runner blades, impellers, valve seats.

### E. Remedies (Memorize 5)
- ✅ **For pumps:** lower pump elevation (or raise sump), increase suction pipe diameter, shorten suction pipe, reduce liquid temperature, use pump with lower $NPSH_R$.
- ✅ **For turbines:** use draft tube, lower runner setting, reduce $P_v$ by cooling water, use cavitation-resistant blade materials (e.g., stainless steel), polish blade surfaces.

### F. Common KTU Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ❌ Forgetting to convert $P_v$ (in kPa) to **head** (in m) before using in head-based formulas.
- ❌ Mixing Fanning and Darcy friction factors — the formula $h_f = 4fLV^2/(2gD)$ is for **Fanning $f$**; for **Darcy $f$**, use $h_f = fLV^2/(2gD)$.
- ❌ Wrong sign of $z_s$: positive above pump, negative below.
- ❌ Writing $NPSH_A > NPSH$ instead of $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$.
- ❌ Not stating the conclusion (safe / unsafe) explicitly in numerical answers.

### G. Typical KTU Question Patterns
- 📝 **3-mark question:** Define cavitation + condition, or define NPSH + criterion.
- 📝 **14-mark question:** Numerical NPSH calculation with two sub-parts (compute & modify), OR Thoma number analysis with installation design.
- 📝 **Common twist:** "What if the temperature changes?" or "What if the pump is moved to a higher elevation?" — both require recomputation using the same formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra for Cavitation (PEPOT416):** *"Pressure falls below vapor pressure → bubbles form → bubbles move to high pressure → bubbles collapse → pitting + noise + vibration. Avoid it by ensuring NPSHₐ > NPSHᵣ (pumps) or σₜ > σc (turbines)."*
